services.name: GST Registration
services.shortDescription: Mandatory and voluntary GST registration support with ongoing compliance options.
services.content:
# Gst Registration

## Overview

GST Registration is the process by which a business obtains a unique GSTIN (Goods and Services Tax Identification Number),  a 15-digit alphanumeric code,  from the GST portal, authorizing it to collect GST from customers, claim Input Tax Credit, and file GST returns. GST Registration is the foundation of GST compliance in India. Under the GST law (CGST Act, 2017), registration is mandatory for every supplier whose aggregate annual turnover exceeds the prescribed threshold, or who is otherwise required to register (regardless of turnover) under specific categories. The GSTIN structure is: State code (2 digits) + PAN (10 digits) + Entity number in state (2 digits) + Check digit (1 digit). Registration Type Description Regular Taxpayer Standard GST registration for ongoing business Composition Scheme Simplified compliance for small businesses (turnover limits apply) Casual Taxable Person For temporary/occasional taxable supply in a state Non-Resident Taxable Person For foreign entities supplying in India TDS Deductor Government entities deducting TDS under GST TCS Collector E-commerce operators collecting TCS SEZ Unit / Developer Suppliers or developers in SEZs Input Service Distributor For distributing ITC from head office to branches

## Applicability

GST Registration is mandatory for: Threshold / Category Threshold Goods suppliers (non-special category states) Annual turnover above ₹40 lakh Service providers (non-special category states) Annual turnover above ₹20 lakh Special category states (NE states, Uttarakhand, etc.) Goods: ₹20 lakh; Services: ₹10 lakh Interstate supply (goods) Mandatory regardless of turnover E-commerce sellers Mandatory regardless of turnover E-commerce operators (TCS) Mandatory regardless of turnover Importers and exporters Mandatory regardless of turnover Reverse charge mechanism recipients Mandatory if RCM liability exists Casual taxable persons Mandatory before commencing supply Non-resident taxable persons Mandatory before commencing supply

Applicable pan-India,  37 jurisdictions (28 states + 8 UTs + 1 central jurisdiction for central taxpayers).

## Features

15-digit GSTIN: Unique identifier for every registered taxpayer,  state-wise PAN-based: GST registration is linked to PAN,  one PAN can have multiple GSTINs (one per state/UT where business is conducted) Online application: Entirely online on gst.gov.in,  Form GST REG-01 ARN-based tracking: Application Reference Number generated on submission,  allows tracking of application status Deemed approval: If the GST officer does not act on an application within the prescribed period (7 working days for Aadhaar-authenticated applications), registration is deemed granted State-wise registration: A separate GSTIN is required for each state/UT where the business has operations or makes taxable supplies Multiple registrations possible: A business with operations in 5 states needs 5 separate GSTINs Composition option: Small businesses (goods up to ₹1.5 crore turnover, restaurants up to ₹1.5 crore, service providers up to ₹50 lakh) can opt for simplified composition scheme at registration

## Benefits

Legal compliance: Mandatory for applicable businesses,  operating without GST registration attracts a penalty of ₹10,000 or the tax amount evaded, whichever is higher ITC eligibility: Only GST-registered businesses can claim Input Tax Credit,  unregistered businesses bear GST as a cost B2B business enablement: Most businesses transact only with GST-registered vendors,  registration is a commercial prerequisite for B2B operations Credibility: A GSTIN-displaying business is perceived as formal, compliant, and credible,  essential for enterprise clients, government contracts, and institutional buyers E-commerce marketplace access: Amazon, Flipkart, Meesho, and other platforms require GST registration before onboarding sellers,  registration is a gating condition Export benefits: GST registration is required for LUT filing, IGST refund, and ITC refund,  all critical for exporters Interstate supply: Registered businesses can make inter-state supplies freely; unregistered businesses cannot make inter-state supplies of goods at all

## Documents

Document Proprietorship Partnership / LLP Company PAN Proprietor's PAN Firm's PAN Company's PAN Aadhaar Proprietor's Aadhaar All partners' Aadhaar Directors' Aadhaar Photograph Proprietor All partners Directors Address proof of business ✓ ✓ ✓ Electricity bill / rent agreement ✓ ✓ ✓ NOC from owner (if rented) ✓ ✓ ✓ Bank statement / cancelled cheque ✓ ✓ ✓ Partnership deed Not applicable ✓ Not applicable LLP agreement / CoI Not applicable LLP: ✓ Not applicable Certificate of Incorporation Not applicable Not applicable ✓ MoA / AoA Not applicable Not applicable ✓ Board resolution for signatory Not applicable Not applicable ✓ Digital Signature Certificate Not required Not required for partnership Required for company

## Process

1. Determine Registration Need Confirm whether mandatory registration is required or voluntary registration is being sought. Identify all states where registration is needed.

2. Aadhaar Authentication The primary signatory's Aadhaar must be linked with the mobile number for OTP-based authentication during the registration application.

3. Form GST REG-01 Filing Visit gst.gov.in. Click on "New Registration." Select taxpayer type, state, and district. Fill Part A, PAN, mobile, and email verification. Then complete Part B, business details, promoter/partner details, business address, bank details, and upload documents.

4. Application Submission Submit Form GST REG-01 using DSC (for companies/LLPs) or EVC (OTP-based for proprietors and partnerships). The system generates an ARN.

5. Aadhaar Authentication / Biometric Verification For Aadhaar-authenticated applications: complete the Aadhaar OTP verification. For states requiring biometric verification (or for applicants who flag Aadhaar authentication): complete biometric verification at the GST Suvidha Kendra.

6. GST Officer Review The GST officer reviews the application within 7 working days. For applications where Aadhaar authentication is complete, if no action in 7 working days, registration is deemed granted. For non-Aadhaar/biometric cases, 30 working days.

7. GSTIN Issuance Upon approval, Form GST REG-06 (Certificate of Registration) is issued, containing the 15-digit GSTIN. Download the certificate from the GST portal.

8. Post-Registration Setup Set up the GST return filing schedule, configure accounting software with GSTIN, update invoicing templates with GSTIN, and link bank account for refund credits. Fees Service Government Fee GST Registration Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee GST Registration,  proprietorship ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 GST Registration,  company/LLP ₹2,500 – ₹5,000 GST Registration,  multiple states (per state) ₹2,000 – ₹4,000 GST Registration + advisory package ₹5,000 – ₹10,000 Rejected application resubmission ₹1,500 – ₹3,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance File GST returns (GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B) from the first return period after registration Mandatory to display GSTIN on signboard at principal place of business GSTIN must be mentioned on all tax invoices issued File NIL returns even if no business activity in a month,  penalties apply for non-filing Apply for additional place of business registration or new state GSTIN if business expands geographically

## Common Mistakes

Registering under wrong business type: Selecting wrong entity type (e.g., "Proprietor" instead of "Private Limited Company") causes significant compliance complications Wrong address on application: Using a virtual office or shared workspace address without proper NOC,  leads to rejection or subsequent cancellation Not registering in all relevant states: Operating in a state without a GSTIN for that state is a violation,  particularly common for service companies with clients across states Delayed registration: Crossing the threshold and not registering within 30 days of exceeding it,  the 30-day window is the legal requirement

## Frequently asked questions

**We are a freelancer providing design services with an annual income of ₹12 lakh. Do we need GST registration?** No,  not mandatory. Service providers with annual turnover below ₹20 lakh (₹10 lakh in special category states) are below the GST registration threshold. However, voluntary GST registration may be beneficial if your clients are GST-registered businesses,  they can then claim ITC on your invoices, making your services more cost-effective for them and potentially allowing you to charge more. Weigh the compliance burden against the commercial benefit before deciding.

**I have an e-commerce business selling handmade products on Flipkart. My revenue is only ₹8 lakh. Do I need GST?** Yes. E-commerce sellers are mandatorily required to register for GST regardless of their turnover,  the ₹20/40 lakh threshold exemption does not apply to e-commerce sellers. Both Amazon and Flipkart require a GSTIN before you can list products. Additionally, e-commerce operators (Flipkart, Amazon) collect TCS (Tax Collected at Source) at 1% from registered sellers' payments,  which can be claimed back in GSTR-3B. Register for GST before starting your marketplace selling journey.

**We are a Bengaluru-based company providing IT services to a client in Mumbai. Do we need a Maharashtra GST registration?** Not necessarily. For supply of services, the place of supply is generally the location of the service recipient,  i.e., Maharashtra. However, a service provider does not need a GST registration in every state where their clients are located. Your Bengaluru GSTIN can be used to supply services interstate,  charge IGST on the invoice. A Maharashtra GSTIN would only be needed if you have a physical office/branch in Maharashtra that constitutes a "fixed establishment." For pure remote service delivery, your Karnataka GSTIN suffices for pan-India service clients.

**My GST registration application was rejected. What are the common reasons and how do I reapply?** Common rejection reasons include: mismatch between PAN name and business name; address proof not matching the declared principal place of business; NOC from property owner not submitted or not properly executed; Aadhaar authentication failure; and incomplete or unclear document uploads. Review the rejection order carefully,  it specifies the grounds. Address each ground in the reapplication. File a fresh Form GST REG-01 with corrected details and proper documentation. Alternatively, file a reply to the Show Cause Notice (GST REG-23) if the rejection was preceded by an SCN, which allows you to clarify without a fresh application.

**We want to register for GST voluntarily even though our turnover is below the threshold. Is voluntary registration advisable?** Voluntary GST registration makes commercial sense if: (a) you supply to GST-registered B2B clients who need ITC on your invoices; (b) you want to be eligible for ITC on your own purchases; (c) you plan to export and need GST registration for LUT and refund claims; or (d) your turnover is approaching the threshold and you want to avoid the disruption of a mid-year mandatory registration. The downside is the ongoing compliance burden,  monthly/quarterly GST returns. Weigh these factors against your specific business situation before opting for voluntary registration.

services.name: Professional Tax Registration
services.shortDescription: State‑specific Professional Tax registration including PTEC and PTRC where applicable.
services.content:
# Professional Tax Registration

## Short overview

Professional Tax (PT) is a state-level tax levied on income earned by professionals, traders, employees, and businesses. Registration for Professional Tax is mandatory in states that have implemented PT under their respective State Acts (passed under Article 276 of the Constitution, which allows states to levy professional tax up to ₹2,500 per annum per person).

There are two types of registration: **PTRC** (Professional Tax Registration Certificate) for employers who deduct PT from employees, and **PTEC** (Professional Tax Enrolment Certificate) for self-employed professionals and businesses.

## Key features

- State-level tax (not central)
- PTRC for employers, PTEC for self-employed
- Slab-based, varies by income
- Maximum ₹2,500 per annum per person

## Benefits

- Legal compliance with state tax laws
- Required for business and employer compliance
- Deductible expense for employers (paid for employees)
- Required for certain professional licensings
- Avoids penalty for non-registration

## Limitations

- Recurring monthly or annual liability
- Multiple state registrations for businesses operating in multiple states
- Inconsistent across states (some states don't have PT)
- Manual reconciliation effort

## Applicability

| State | PT applicable | Maximum annual PT |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| Karnataka | Yes | ₹2,400 |
| Tamil Nadu | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| West Bengal | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| Andhra Pradesh / Telangana | Yes | ₹2,400 |
| Gujarat | Yes | ₹2,400 |
| Madhya Pradesh | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| Kerala | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| Odisha | Yes | ₹2,500 |
| Delhi | No |,  |
| UP | No |,  |
| Rajasthan | No |,  |
| Punjab | No |,  |
| Haryana | No |,  |

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State-specific |
| PAN card | Of entity or individual |
| Address proof | Business address |
| Identity proof | Of proprietor or directors |
| Employee details | For PTRC |
| GST registration | If applicable |
| Salary register | For PTRC |
| Bank account details | |
| Photograph | |

## Process

1. Apply on state PT portal (e.g. mahagst.gov.in for Maharashtra).
2. Submit documents online.
3. Department verification.
4. Certificate issuance (typically within 7 to 15 days).
5. Begin monthly or annual PT deductions and payments.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| --- | --- |
| Registration fee | Generally Nil to ₹500 |
| Annual PT (per individual) | Up to ₹2,500 (slab-based) |

## Validity and renewal

Permanent registration (no expiry). Annual return filing required in most states. Monthly or quarterly PT payment depending on state.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is PT registration mandatory for a one-person business?** Yes, if the state has PT. Self-employed individuals need PTEC and pay annual PT based on income slab.

**2. Do remote workers need PT registration in their location state?** PT applies based on the state where the employer operates and pays the employee. Remote workers in non-PT states employed by PT-state companies typically have PT deducted.

**3. What if I have employees in multiple states?** Separate PTRC registration in each state where you have employees is required.

**4. Are partners in a partnership firm liable for PT?** Yes, partners are typically liable for PT under PTEC based on their share of income.

**5. What's the penalty for non-registration?** States impose penalties of 10% of PT due plus interest, with additional penalty for delayed payment.

All service content is for informational purposes. State PT rules, slabs, and timelines differ. Consult state commercial tax or GST portals and qualified professionals for your case.

services.name: TAN Registration
services.shortDescription: TAN registration for TDS/TCS compliance on salaries, professional fees, rent and other payments.
services.content:
# TAN registration

## Overview

TAN (Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number) is a unique 10-character alphanumeric code issued by the Income Tax Department of India to every person or entity that is required to deduct tax at source (TDS, Tax Deducted at Source) or collect tax at source (TCS, Tax Collected at Source) under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. TAN is issued under Section 203A of the Income Tax Act and is mandatory for all tax deductors.

Like PAN, TAN is issued through the authorised agencies NSDL and UTIITSL. The TAN format is: AAAA12345A, 4 letters (first indicates city code, next 3 are random), 5 numbers (sequential), and 1 check digit.

Every employer deducting TDS from employees' salaries, every business making payments to contractors (TDS under Section 194C), every company paying rent above ₹50,000/month (TDS under Section 194-I), and every entity collecting TCS on specified goods and services must obtain a TAN and use it for all TDS/TCS compliance, including TDS payments, TDS returns (24Q, 26Q, 27Q, 27EQ), and TDS certificates (Form 16, 16A, 27D).

Quoting an incorrect or false TAN, or making TDS payments without a TAN, attracts a penalty of ₹10,000 under Section 272BB of the Income Tax Act.

## Applicability

**TAN is mandatory for any person/entity who:**

| TDS/TCS obligation | Who needs TAN |
| --- | --- |
| Deducts TDS on salary | All employers |
| Deducts TDS on contractor payments | All businesses making payments to contractors |
| Deducts TDS on rent | Individuals/HUFs paying rent above ₹50,000/month |
| Deducts TDS on professional/technical fees | Businesses paying professional fees |
| Deducts TDS on interest, dividends | Banks, NBFCs, companies |
| Deducts TDS on property purchase | Individual buyers of property above ₹50 lakh |
| Collects TCS on sale of goods | Sellers of specified goods (Section 206C) |
| Deducts TDS on foreign remittances | Businesses making payments to non-residents |

**Not required for:**

- Individuals deducting TDS on property purchase, they use PAN (Form 26QB) without TAN
- Individuals deducting TDS on rent payment under Section 194-IB, Form 26QC, no TAN needed

## Key features

- Unique 10-digit TAN: Separate from PAN, used exclusively for TDS/TCS compliance
- City-specific first character: The first letter of TAN indicates the city/region of the assessing officer's jurisdiction
- Mandatory quoting: TAN must be quoted on all TDS payment challans (Challan 281), TDS returns, and TDS certificates, failure attracts ₹10,000 penalty
- One TAN per PAN: Generally, one TAN is issued per entity. Large organizations with multiple branches can obtain a separate TAN for each branch, but all TDS returns for a PAN can be filed under a single TAN
- Online application: TAN application (Form 49B) can be filed online through NSDL or UTIITSL portals
- No Aadhaar linkage required: Unlike PAN, TAN does not require Aadhaar linking
- TAN verification: TAN details can be verified on the Income Tax portal, valid TANs show the entity name and jurisdiction
- Traces portal: TDSCPC.gov.in (Tax Deduction and Collection System) is the portal where TDS returns are filed, TDS certificates are generated, and TAN-based compliance is managed

## Benefits

- **Legal TDS compliance:** TAN is the legal identifier for all TDS/TCS activities, without TAN, TDS payments cannot be made through the banking system (banks require TAN for Challan 281)
- **TDS certificate issuance:** Form 16, Form 16A, Form 27D (TCS certificates) can only be generated by entities with a valid TAN, deductees need these certificates to claim TDS credit
- **TRACES portal access:** TAN registration enables access to the TRACES portal for downloading TDS certificates, reconciling Form 26AS, and managing TDS compliance
- **Avoid higher TDS:** Deductees receiving payments without TAN-backed TDS deduction face higher TDS rates, proper TAN compliance benefits both deductor and deductee
- **Payroll compliance:** Employers must have TAN for monthly salary TDS (Section 192), Form 24Q filing, and Form 16 issuance, TAN is the backbone of payroll tax compliance
- **Bank mandate:** Banks process TDS payment through Challan 281 only against a valid TAN, a TAN is operationally essential for any business making significant vendor or employee payments

## Documents

| Document | Individual / HUF | Company / firm |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Form 49B (TAN application) | ✓ | ✓ |
| PAN of the applicant | ✓ | ✓ |
| Name and address of deductor | ✓ | ✓ |
| Aadhaar (for individual applicants) | ✓ | Not required |
| Certificate of Incorporation | Not applicable | ✓ |
| Office address proof | ✓ | ✓ |
| Authorised signatory details | Not required | ✓ |
| Contact details | ✓ | ✓ |

## Process

1. **Apply online (Form 49B)** Visit tin.nsdl.com or utiitsl.com. Click on "Apply for New TAN", "Online Application for TAN (Form 49B)." Select the deductor type (company, firm, individual, etc.).
2. **Fill application details** Complete Form 49B, deductor's name, address, PAN, contact details, and nature of business. For companies, enter CIN and director details.
3. **Pay TAN application fee** Pay the application fee online (₹65 + GST = approximately ₹77 total) via net banking, debit card, or credit card.
4. **Acknowledgement** Upon submission, a 14-digit acknowledgement number is generated. The form can be submitted online (for most cases) or a physical copy sent to NSDL/UTIITSL for cases requiring physical verification.
5. **Processing and TAN issuance** NSDL/UTIITSL processes the application and verifies details. TAN is issued within 7–15 working days. The TAN allotment letter is sent to the registered address.
6. **Register on TRACES portal** After TAN receipt, register on the TRACES portal (tdscpc.gov.in) using the TAN, PAN, and a token number from a filed TDS statement (or for new deductors, use the Conso file of the first return). TRACES registration enables TDS return filing, TDS certificate generation, and TAN-linked compliance management.
7. **Link with banking for Challan 281** Update the TAN with your bank for TDS payment, the bank registers the TAN in their system to process Challan 281 payments.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Government fee |
| --- | --- |
| TAN Application (Form 49B) | ₹65 + GST = ₹77 approx. |
| TAN reprint | ₹50 + GST |
| TAN correction | ₹65 + GST |

| Professional service | Estimated fee |
| --- | --- |
| TAN application assistance | ₹500 – ₹2,000 |
| TAN + TRACES registration setup | ₹1,500 – ₹4,000 |
| TAN correction filing | ₹500 – ₹1,500 |
| Bulk TAN applications (10+) | ₹400 – ₹800 per TAN |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Activity | Deadline |
| --- | --- |
| TAN application | Before first TDS deduction/payment |
| TDS deposit (monthly) | 7th of following month (30th April for March) |
| TDS return Q1 (April–June) | 31st July |
| TDS return Q2 (July–September) | 31st October |
| TDS return Q3 (October–December) | 31st January |
| TDS return Q4 (January–March) | 31st May |

## Common errors

- Applying for TAN without PAN: TAN application requires the applicant's PAN, applying without PAN leads to rejection
- Using wrong TAN on challans: Quoting an incorrect or a previously surrendered TAN on Challan 281, the TDS credit does not reflect in the deductee's Form 26AS, causing disputes
- Not registering on TRACES after TAN allotment: Many new deductors receive TAN but don't register on TRACES, inability to file TDS returns and generate certificates follows
- Multiple TANs for the same entity: Inadvertently applying for multiple TANs for the same entity, surrender duplicates immediately to avoid compliance complications

## Frequently asked questions

**We are a startup that just hired our first employee at ₹8 lakh annual salary. Do we need a TAN?** Yes. As soon as your company has an employee whose salary income exceeds the basic exemption limit (₹2.5 lakh under the old regime or ₹3 lakh under the new regime), TDS on salary (Section 192) becomes applicable. Even if TDS is zero (because the employee's income after deductions doesn't result in taxable income), the employer must obtain a TAN and file a quarterly TDS return (Form 24Q), even if it is a NIL return. Apply for TAN immediately upon hiring your first employee, before the first salary payment is due.

**We are a freelancer (individual) who makes payments to other freelancers above ₹30,000 per contract. Do we need a TAN?** TDS under Section 194J (professional fees) at 10% is applicable when a person (other than an individual or HUF not subject to tax audit) makes professional payments above ₹30,000 per contract or ₹1 lakh per year to a single contractor. Individual freelancers who are NOT subject to tax audit are generally not required to deduct TDS on professional payments. However, if your professional receipts triggered a tax audit in the preceding year (above ₹50 lakh for professionals), you must deduct TDS and therefore need a TAN. Individual buyers of property above ₹50 lakh and individuals paying rent above ₹50,000/month have specific TDS obligations under Sections 194-IA and 194-IB respectively, but these use PAN-based forms (26QB, 26QC), not TAN.

**Our company received a notice saying we deducted TDS but didn't quote TAN on the challan. What is the consequence and how do we fix it?** Failure to quote TAN (or quoting an incorrect TAN) on TDS payment challans attracts a penalty of ₹10,000 under Section 272BB. To fix the specific challan: file a "Challan Correction" request with the bank or through the OLTAS (Online Tax Accounting System) on the NSDL portal, to update the TAN on the challan. Once corrected, the TDS credit will reflect correctly in deductees' Form 26AS. For the forward-looking compliance, immediately update all payment mandates and accounting systems with the correct TAN. Respond to the notice with the correction evidence and request for penalty waiver based on bona fide mistake.

**Can we use the same TAN for all our offices across different states?** Yes, one TAN can be used for TDS compliance across all offices and branches of the same legal entity, regardless of how many states they are in. However, large organizations sometimes prefer separate TANs for each branch/location for internal tracking purposes, this is permissible. If separate TANs are used for different branches, each branch files its own TDS returns under its respective TAN, and TDS certificates are issued from the respective branch TAN. Ensure all deductors' addresses in TDS returns correctly reflect the office address for the specific TAN to avoid jurisdictional confusion.

**We obtained a TAN 3 years ago but never used it (no TDS obligations materialised). Do we need to do anything to keep it active?** Unlike PAN, TAN does not become "inoperative" from non-use. However, if you have a TAN and made no TDS deductions in any quarter, you should still file NIL TDS returns for those quarters, technically, the obligation to file TDS returns exists for every entity with a TAN, and non-filing (even NIL) can trigger notices. In practice, the TDSCPC system generates notices for TANs that filed returns in previous quarters but stopped filing, assume the deductor has active TDS obligations. If you genuinely have no TDS obligations and won't have any in the foreseeable future, you can apply for TAN deactivation/surrender at your jurisdictional TDS officer.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: PF Registration
services.shortDescription: EPF registration for establishments with 20 or more employees or voluntary coverage.
services.content:
The Employees’ Provident Fund (EPF) framework is governed by the Employees’ Provident Funds & Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952 and administered by the Employees’ Provident Fund Organisation (EPFO) under the Ministry of Labour and Employment, Government of India.

PF Registration is compulsory for establishments employing 20 or more persons, though voluntary registration is also permitted. Once registered, all statutory compliances under the Act must be followed. PF contributions are calculated on the basic salary (including DA and retaining allowance). Employers and employees each contribute 12% in aggregate, which is deposited into the 12‑digit Universal Account Number (UAN) of each employee.

We provide expert support for assessing applicability and obtaining PF Registration for your establishment.

services.name: ESI Registration
services.shortDescription: ESI registration for eligible establishments and employees under the ESI Act, 1948.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

ESI Registration is a mandatory statutory process through which eligible employers enroll their establishment under the Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme. This scheme is governed by the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948 and administered by the Employees' State Insurance Corporation. The ESI framework is designed as a comprehensive social security system, providing medical care and financial benefits to employees in cases of sickness, maternity, disability, and employment injury. Once registered, the employer is required to contribute towards the ESI fund for eligible employees and ensure continuous compliance through contributions and return filings. ESI Registration becomes applicable when:

- An establishment employs 10 or more employees (in most states; threshold may vary)
- Employees earn wages up to ₹21,000 per month (subject to revision)
- The establishment operates within a notified ESI area

Upon successful registration, the employer receives a 17-digit ESI registration number, and each employee is allotted an Insurance Number (IP Number) linked to their ESI records. This ensures seamless access to medical and financial benefits under the scheme. ESI Registration is not merely a procedural requirement, it establishes a structured mechanism for employee welfare, compliance discipline, and long-term workforce security.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Comprehensive Employee Welfare

Provides access to medical treatment, hospitalization, and insurance benefits for employees and their dependents.

### Statutory Compliance Assurance

Ensures adherence to labour laws and avoids legal complications.

### Financial Protection for Employees

Offers monetary benefits during sickness, maternity, or disability.

### Employer Credibility

Enhances reputation as a compliant and responsible employer.

### Access to ESI Infrastructure

Employees can utilize ESI hospitals, dispensaries, and medical facilities.

### Reduced Workforce Risk

Minimizes financial burden on employer in case of employee medical emergencies.

### Employee Retention Support

Improves job satisfaction and long-term retention.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Mandatory Compliance Burden

Requires continuous tracking of contributions and filings.

### Limited Applicability

Applies only to employees within prescribed wage limits.

### Operational Complexity

Requires accurate payroll integration and employee data management.

### Financial Outflow

Employer must contribute a fixed percentage of wages.

### Penalties for Non-Compliance

Delay or non-registration can lead to fines and legal action.

## 4) Applicability Across India (Coverage)

ESI Registration is governed by a central law, but its applicability depends on whether the area is notified under the ESI scheme.

### Applicable in Notified Areas Across India

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, etc.

### Administering Authority

- Employees' State Insurance Corporation

Who Must Register?

- Factories and establishments with 10 or more employees
- Shops, hotels, restaurants, cinemas, and service entities
- Businesses operating in notified ESI zones

## 5) Key Features of ESI Registration

### Statutory Registration Requirement

Mandatory once eligibility criteria are met.

### Unique Employer Code

17-digit registration number issued to employer.

### Employee Insurance Number

Each employee receives a unique IP number.

### Wage-Based Coverage

Applicable to employees earning up to threshold limit.

### Integrated Digital System

Registration and compliance managed online via ESIC portal.

### Contribution-Based Scheme

Employer and employee contribute monthly.

### Continuous Compliance Obligation

Requires ongoing filing and payment after registration.

## 6) Documents & Information Required

ESI Registration is primarily driven by accurate business and employee data rather than extensive paperwork.

### Employer Details

- PAN of business/entity
- Address proof of establishment
- Certificate of incorporation/registration

### Business Information

- Nature of business activity
- Date of commencement
- Employee strength

### Employee Details

- Employee names and details
- Salary/wage structure
- Aadhaar and bank details

### Supporting Records

- Salary registers
- Attendance records

### Additional Requirements

- Digital signature (for companies/LLPs)
- Bank account details

Accurate information ensures smooth registration and avoids future compliance issues.

## 7) Registration Workflow (Practical Process)

1. Determine Applicability,  Check employee count, wage limits, and location eligibility.
2. Access ESIC Portal,  Visit ESIC online registration system.
3. Employer Sign-Up,  Create login credentials and initiate registration.
4. Fill Registration Form,  Enter business, employer, and employee details.
5. Upload Required Information,  Provide necessary documents and data.
6. Verification by Authority,  System validates details submitted.
7. Generation of ESI Code,  17-digit registration number is issued.
8. Employee Enrollment,  Register employees and generate IP numbers.
9. Begin Compliance Cycle,  Start monthly contributions and periodic return filings.

## 8) Government Cost / Financial Implications

### Registration Cost

- No government fee for ESI Registration

### Contribution Rates

- Employee Contribution: 0.75% of wages
- Employer Contribution: 3.25% of wages

Total Contribution: 4% of wages

### Penalty & Interest

- Interest on delayed payment: ~12% per annum
- Penalties for non-registration or late compliance

### Additional Costs

- Payroll and compliance management systems
- Professional/consultant fees

### Important Notes

- Registration must be completed within prescribed time after applicability
- Contributions are mandatory once registered
- No renewal required, but ongoing compliance is essential

## Conclusion

ESI Registration is a foundational compliance requirement that integrates businesses into India's social security ecosystem. By ensuring access to healthcare and financial protection for employees, it plays a crucial role in building a secure and sustainable workforce. While the process is streamlined through digital systems, the responsibility of maintaining accurate data, timely contributions, and continuous compliance remains critical. For employers, ESI Registration is not just about meeting legal obligations, it reflects a commitment to employee welfare, organizational integrity, and long-term operational stability.

services.name: Import Export Code (IEC)
services.shortDescription: IEC code registration for businesses engaged in import and export of goods.
services.content:
# Import Export Code (iec) Registration

## Overview

The Import Export Code (IEC) is a unique 10-digit identification number issued by the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India. The IEC is the primary and foundational authorisation required by any Indian entity or individual to conduct import or export of goods and services. It is, in essence, the trade licence for international commerce in India. The IEC was introduced under the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 and the Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) issued thereunder. Since its integration with PAN in 2021, the IEC is now directly derived from the entity's PAN,  the 10-digit IEC number is the same as the entity's 10-digit PAN, making the system more streamlined and reducing duplication. Key characteristics of the IEC: Feature Detail Issuing authority DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) Legal basis Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 Format 10-digit alphanumeric (same as PAN)

## Validity

Lifetime,  no expiry (annual update required)

## Applicability

Mandatory for all imports/exports of goods Fees Nil (Free) Processing time 2–5 working days Portal dgft.gov.in

The IEC is checked at every stage of the import/export transaction,  by Customs at the port, by the bank for foreign exchange remittances, by DGFT for scheme applications, and by Export Promotion Councils for RCMC issuance. No import or export can legally proceed without a valid, active IEC. Important exceptions: IEC is not required for: Imports/exports for personal use (non-commercial) Central and state government departments and ministries Goods exported under baggage rules Specific exemptions under the FTP (e.g., defence, security-related)

## Applicability

IEC registration is mandatory for: Entity / Activity IEC Requirement Indian companies importing goods Mandatory Indian companies exporting goods Mandatory Indian individuals / proprietors importing/exporting commercially Mandatory Partnership firms and LLPs in import/export Mandatory Trusts and societies importing/exporting Mandatory E-commerce sellers shipping products internationally Mandatory Service exporters above SOFTEX threshold Mandatory (for SOFTEX filing) Importers of services paying in foreign exchange Mandatory Indian companies receiving FDI and making ODI Indirectly required (IEC linked to FEMA compliance) Companies applying for RCMC with EPCs Mandatory prerequisite Advance Authorisation, EPCG, MEIS/RoDTEP applicants Mandatory prerequisite

Not required for: Persons importing/exporting for personal use (not connected with trade or commerce) Government departments (they use a special exemption code) Imports/exports of specific goods exempted under the FTP notifications Applicable pan-India,  one IEC covers all of India. A single IEC enables imports and exports from any port across all states,  Mumbai (JNCH), Chennai, Kolkata, Delhi, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Ahmedabad, Mundra, Kandla, Cochin, Vizag, and all other ports and ICDs.

## Benefits

Gateway to global trade: IEC is the single authorisation that opens the door to all international commerce,  without it, no import or export of goods can be legally completed in India Lifetime validity: Unlike many business licences that require annual renewal, IEC is a one-time registration with lifetime validity (subject to annual update confirmations),  no reapplication required as the business grows Free of cost: The DGFT charges no fee for IEC registration,  it is one of the very few government business licences that is completely free Single IEC for all transactions: One IEC covers all import/export activities of the entity across all ports, all products, and all countries,  no product-specific or port-specific licensing Customs clearance facilitation: IEC is referenced in every Bill of Entry (import) and Shipping Bill (export),  a valid IEC ensures smooth customs processing; an invalid or absent IEC causes immediate clearance holds Bank foreign exchange facilitation: Banks require IEC for processing foreign exchange payments for imports (advance payments, LCs) and for crediting export proceeds,  IEC is the FEMA identification for trade transactions Export promotion scheme eligibility: All DGFT export incentive schemes,  RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation, EPCG, Duty Drawback,  require an active IEC. The IEC is the entity's identity in the export scheme ecosystem RCMC prerequisite: RCMC (Registration-cum-Membership Certificate) from Export Promotion Councils (EPCs),  required for sector-specific export benefits,  can only be obtained after IEC GST LUT prerequisite: Filing LUT for zero-rated exports requires a valid IEC,  particularly for goods exporters E-commerce export enablement: International marketplaces (Amazon Global Selling, eBay, Etsy, Alibaba) require an active IEC for Indian sellers listing on their international platforms Professional and service credibility: An IEC on a consultancy or service firm's profile signals global commercial readiness,  useful for winning international contracts and project bids

## Limitations

Annual update mandatory: Despite lifetime validity, IEC holders must complete the Annual IEC Update on the DGFT portal between April 1 and June 30 every year,  missed updates lead to automatic deactivation PAN-based,  PAN errors propagate: Since IEC is now PAN-derived, errors in the entity's PAN records (wrong name, wrong date of birth, wrong address) directly appear in the IEC,  correcting IEC requires first correcting the underlying PAN, which can be a slow process Not a permit for restricted goods: IEC enables import/export in general,  but certain products (arms and ammunition, narcotics, wildlife products, certain chemicals) require additional import/export licences beyond the IEC. IEC alone is not sufficient for restricted, prohibited, or canalised goods Sector-specific restrictions: For certain import/export activities (e.g., defence items, dual-use goods under SCOMET,  Special Chemicals, Organisms, Materials, Equipment, and Technologies), additional DGFT authorisations beyond the IEC are mandatory No protection against misuse: The IEC number is semi-public,  it appears on shipping documents and customs records. Fraudulent use of IEC by third parties (impersonation) is a known risk, though mitigated by Aadhaar and DSC authentication in the new system Deactivation impact: An IEC deactivated due to missed annual update immediately blocks all import/export transactions at customs and banks,  the business impact is significant for active traders, making the annual update a critical compliance deadline

## Documents

Document Proprietorship Partnership Firm Private Limited / LLP PAN of entity Proprietor's PAN Firm's PAN Company's/LLP's PAN Aadhaar Proprietor's Aadhaar Managing partner's Aadhaar Director's/DP's Aadhaar Photograph Proprietor Managing partner Director Address proof of business ✓ ✓ ✓ Bank account proof Cancelled cheque / bank certificate ✓ ✓ Partnership deed Not applicable ✓ Not applicable LLP Agreement / Certificate of Incorporation Not applicable LLP: ✓ ✓ MoA/AoA Not applicable Not applicable ✓ DSC of authorized signatory Not required Not required Not required (EVC works) Board resolution (for company) Not applicable Not applicable ✓ GST registration certificate ✓ (if registered) ✓ ✓ Mobile number and email ID ✓ ✓ ✓

## Process

1. DGFT Portal Account Creation Visit dgft.gov.in. Click on "Login" → "Register." Create a user account using your business email ID and mobile number. Verify the account through OTP verification.

2. Aadhaar Verification Link the authorized signatory's Aadhaar to the DGFT portal account using OTP verification sent to the Aadhaar-registered mobile number.

3. IEC Application, Form ANF 2A After login, navigate to Services → IEC Profile Management → Apply for IEC. Form ANF 2A is the IEC application form. The portal auto-populates PAN-linked data (name, address, date of incorporation) from the Income Tax database.

4. Fill Application Details Complete all sections, entity details, principal business address, director/partner details, bank account details (cancelled cheque or bank certificate), and GST details.

5. Upload Documents Upload the required documents, address proof, bank certificate, entity registration documents (CoI, partnership deed), and other applicable documents. Documents must be in PDF or JPEG format within the size limits.

6. Fee Payment IEC registration is free, the DGFT portal confirms nil fee. Proceed to submit without any payment gateway step.

7. Aadhaar OTP Signing The application is submitted with Aadhaar OTP authentication of the authorized signatory (Aadhaar-linked mobile OTP). This replaces the earlier DSC requirement for most applicants.

8. Application Reference Number (ARN) Upon submission, the DGFT portal generates an ARN. The ARN can be used to track the application status on the portal.

9. DGFT Processing The DGFT system validates the application against PAN, Aadhaar, and GST databases. For clean applications (no data mismatches), IEC is generated automatically, often within minutes to a few hours. Where manual review is required (data mismatches, document clarity issues), processing takes 2–5 working days.

10. IEC Certificate Download Upon successful processing, the IEC Certificate (e-IEC) is generated and available for download from the DGFT portal under "Print IEC." The 10-digit IEC number is printed on the certificate along with the entity details and issue date.

11. Post-Registration Steps After IEC issuance: Register on ICEGATE (for customs e-filing) Obtain RCMC from relevant EPC (for export scheme eligibility) File LUT for exports without IGST payment Register with STPI (for software/service exporters) Update IEC details with AD bank for FEMA compliance Fees Service Government Fee IEC Registration (new) Nil (Free) IEC Annual Update Nil (Free) IEC Modification Nil (Free) IEC Surrender/Cancellation Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee IEC Registration,  proprietorship ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 IEC Registration,  partnership/LLP ₹2,000 – ₹4,000 IEC Registration,  private limited company ₹2,500 – ₹5,000 IEC + RCMC package ₹4,000 – ₹10,000 IEC + ICEGATE registration package ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 IEC + GST LUT + RCMC full export setup ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 IEC error/rejection resolution ₹2,000 – ₹5,000

IEC registration is completely free on the DGFT portal. Professional fees are for application preparation, documentation, and filing assistance, exclusive of GST @18%.

## Timeline

Stage

## Timeline

Document preparation 1–2 working days DGFT portal account creation and Aadhaar verification Same day IEC application submission Same day DGFT processing (auto-approval,  clean application) Immediate to 4 hours DGFT processing (manual review,  data mismatch) 2–5 working days IEC certificate download Immediately upon approval ICEGATE registration (next step) 3–7 working days Total end-to-end (IEC only) 1–5 working days Total end-to-end (IEC + ICEGATE + RCMC) 10–20 working days

Use Cases 1. First-Time Garment Exporter A Tiruppur textile manufacturer who has been selling domestically decides to explore export markets in Europe. Before approaching any overseas buyer, attending an international trade fair, or listing on export marketplaces, they need an IEC. With the IEC in place, they can apply for RCMC with AEPC (Apparel Export Promotion Council), register on ICEGATE for customs filings, file LUT with GST for zero-rated exports, and begin fulfilling international purchase orders with proper documentation. 2. E-Commerce Seller Going Global A Jaipur-based handicraft seller on domestic marketplaces wants to expand to Amazon's Global Selling programme (selling on Amazon US, UK, and EU). Amazon requires a valid IEC as part of its international seller verification. The seller registers for an IEC, uploads it to Amazon's global seller account, and can now list products for international buyers with proper export documentation support. 3. Import of Raw Materials A Pune-based specialty chemicals manufacturer wants to import specific chemical precursors from Germany and South Korea that are not available domestically. Without an IEC, their customs broker cannot file a Bill of Entry on their behalf. They obtain an IEC, register on ICEGATE, and work with their CHA to clear the chemical imports through customs,  with proper REACH, SCOMET, and hazmat clearances where applicable. 4. IT Company Exporting Software Services A Hyderabad-based SaaS company with US and European clients is technically an exporter of services. While digital service exports don't pass through physical customs, an IEC is required for SOFTEX filing with STPI (for software exports above USD 25,000 per transaction), for FEMA compliance (export proceeds must be realised against an active IEC), and for GST LUT filing to export services without IGST payment. The IEC is their foundational EXIM compliance document even in the digital services context. 5. NGO Importing Donated Medical Equipment A Chennai-based charitable trust receives a donation of medical equipment from a US-based foundation. The equipment must be imported and customs-cleared,  requiring an IEC for the trust, even for a non-commercial import. The trust registers for an IEC and applies for the relevant customs exemption (under notification for charitable imports) using their IEC as the importer identifier.

## Frequently asked questions

**I am a freelancer/consultant providing services to clients in the US and Europe. Do I need an IEC even though I'm not exporting physical goods?** For purely digital service exports where you are an individual (not a registered business entity) and your earnings are below the SOFTEX threshold of USD 25,000 per transaction, the IEC requirement is less clear-cut,  many individual service exporters operate without an IEC by receiving foreign payments directly into their savings accounts with AD bank oversight. However, if you want to formally register as a service exporter, claim DGFT export benefits, file SOFTEX with STPI, or your transactions exceed SOFTEX thresholds, an IEC is necessary. If you have a registered business entity (proprietorship, LLP, or Pvt Ltd) providing the services, an IEC for that entity is strongly advisable for complete FEMA compliance and future export incentive eligibility.

**My IEC application was rejected due to a "name mismatch." What does this mean and how do I fix it?** The most common cause of IEC name mismatch rejection is a difference between the entity name in the PAN database (Income Tax department records) and the entity name as typed in the IEC application. Since IEC data is auto-validated against PAN records, even a minor difference,  an extra space, an abbreviation (Pvt vs Private), or a spelling variation,  triggers a mismatch. Fix by: (a) downloading your entity's PAN card and noting the exact name as it appears on PAN; (b) ensuring the IEC application uses the exact same name letter-for-letter. If the PAN itself has an incorrect name (different from your ROC incorporation certificate), first correct the PAN at the Income Tax department,  IEC modification can only follow after PAN correction.

**Can a company have multiple IECs for different divisions or business units?** No. Under the DGFT framework, one PAN = one IEC. A company can have only one IEC regardless of the number of divisions, subsidiaries, or business units it operates. All divisions of the same company must use the same IEC for their import/export transactions. If you want to track import/export activities separately for different divisions, this must be done through internal accounting systems,  not by obtaining separate IECs. However, if the divisions are separate legal entities (separate companies with separate PANs), each entity gets its own separate IEC.

**We exported goods 3 years ago and have never updated our IEC since then. Can we still use it?** If you have not completed the mandatory Annual IEC Update between April 1 and June 30 for each year since the update requirement was introduced (FY 2021-22 onwards), your IEC may have been automatically deactivated. Check the IEC status on the DGFT portal (Services → IEC Profile Management → Check IEC Status). If deactivated, complete the overdue Annual Update,  the portal allows backdated completion of the update even after June 30. Once the update is submitted and verified, the IEC is reactivated. For any specific years where the update was missed, the IEC was technically inactive during that period,  review whether any imports/exports during those periods need to be regularised.

**Is an IEC required for importing goods for personal use,  say, I want to import a vintage motorcycle from the UK as a collector's item?** Personal imports,  goods for personal use that are not connected to any trade or business,  are generally exempt from the IEC requirement. Customs clears personal imports under the Baggage Rules or as personal imports against the individual's PAN without an IEC. However, "personal use" has a specific meaning in customs law,  the item must genuinely be for personal consumption or use and not for resale or commercial purpose. A vintage motorcycle imported once as a collector's personal item would typically qualify as personal import. If you plan to import multiple vehicles, deal in imported collectibles commercially, or if customs questions the personal use nature, an IEC may be required. Consult a CHA for the specific HS code and customs classification of your intended import before proceeding.

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific import-export compliance advice should be obtained from qualified DGFT/customs practitioners and CHAs based on your specific trade activities, product categories, and business structure.

services.name: FSSAI Registration
services.shortDescription: Food business registration and licensing (Registration, State and Central License).
services.content:
# Fssai Registration

## Overview

FSSAI Registration is the foundational compliance requirement for any food business operating in India. Governed by the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 and administered by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), this registration is mandatory for every entity involved in the manufacture, processing, storage, distribution, sale, or import of food products. FSSAI operates under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India, and functions as the apex regulatory body for food safety standards across the country. Every food business operator (FBO),  from a small home baker or street food vendor to a mid-sized catering company or grocery retailer,  must obtain the appropriate level of FSSAI authorisation before commencing operations. FSSAI authorisation exists in three tiers: Basic Registration (for petty food businesses), State License (for mid-size operators), and Central License (for large-scale or multi-state operators). This page covers Basic FSSAI Registration,  the entry-level authorisation for petty food business operators (PFBOs) with an annual turnover of up to ₹12 lakh. Operating without FSSAI Registration is a punishable offence under the FSS Act, attracting fines of up to ₹5 lakh and imprisonment in severe cases. Getting registered is not just a legal requirement,  it is your food business's licence to operate legitimately and build consumer trust.

## Applicability

FSSAI Basic Registration applies to: Petty food manufacturers with an annual turnover not exceeding ₹12 lakh Small retailers, hawkers, itinerant vendors, and temporary stall holders Home-based food businesses (cloud kitchens, tiffin services, home bakers) Cottage industry food producers Dhaba operators and small roadside eateries with limited turnover Small-scale food storage operators Distributor/supplier/retailer of food products with low turnover Who needs State License instead of Registration: Operators with annual turnover between ₹12 lakh and ₹20 crore Mid-size manufacturers, processors, restaurants, hotels (up to 3-star), caterers (up to 100 persons) Who needs Central License instead: Operators with annual turnover above ₹20 crore Importers, exporters of food products Food businesses operating in more than one state FCI-approved warehouses and large cold chain operators State-wise applicability: FSSAI Registration is applicable pan-India,  across all 28 states and 8 union territories including Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Kerala, Telangana, Punjab, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, Odisha, and all others. The State Food Safety Departments in each state are the licensing authorities for State Licenses; Basic Registration is processed centrally through the FSSAI FoSCoS portal.

## Benefits

Legal authorisation to operate: FSSAI Registration is a prerequisite to legally run any food business; prevents fines, raids, and closure orders from food safety officers Consumer trust and credibility: Displaying the 14-digit FSSAI Registration number on food products and packaging builds consumer confidence and brand legitimacy E-commerce marketplace access: Platforms like Zomato, Swiggy, Amazon Fresh, BigBasket, and Flipkart Grocery mandate valid FSSAI Registration before onboarding any food seller Business expansion readiness: A registered food business can more easily upgrade to a State or Central License as it scales,  maintaining compliance continuity Bank finance eligibility: Banks and NBFCs require FSSAI Registration for food business loan applications and working capital financing FSSAI logo usage: Registered FBOs can use the FSSAI logo on their products and marketing material, enhancing brand perception Government scheme access: Access to government food processing schemes (PM-FME Scheme, PMKVY food processing modules) often requires valid FSSAI Registration Reduced legal liability: Compliance with FSSAI standards reduces the risk of product liability claims and consumer complaints

## Limitations

Turnover cap: Basic Registration is only for businesses with annual turnover up to ₹12 lakh,  exceeding this requires upgrade to a State License Limited scope of activities: Some food business activities (e.g., food import, large-scale manufacturing) are not eligible for Basic Registration regardless of turnover No multi-state coverage: Basic Registration is location-specific and covers only the registered premises; each additional location requires separate registration/licensing Mandatory upgrade: If the business grows beyond the ₹12 lakh threshold, the FBO must proactively apply for a State License,  failure to upgrade is a violation Product restrictions: Certain high-risk food categories (proprietary foods, nutraceuticals, novel food) cannot be manufactured under Basic Registration,  a State or Central License is required No import authorisation: Importing food products requires a Central License; Basic Registration does not authorise food imports

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Photo of the applicant | Recent passport-size photograph |
| Photo ID proof | Aadhaar card, voter ID, passport, or driving licence |
| Address proof of business premises | Electricity bill, rent agreement, NOC from owner |
| Proof of business activity | Declaration of food business type and products |
| Form B (FSSAI Registration Form) | Filled application on the FoSCoS portal |
| Bank account details | Cancelled cheque or passbook copy |
| List of food products to be handled | Category-wise list of food articles |
| NOC from municipality/panchayat | For certain types of food businesses |
| Partnership deed / MoA (if applicable) | For firms and companies (not mandatory for individuals) |

## Process

1. Determine Registration Category Assess annual turnover and nature of food business to confirm eligibility for Basic Registration vs. State/Central License.

2. FoSCoS Portal Registration Create an account on FSSAI's FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System) portal at foscos.fssai.gov.in. This is the single-window portal for all FSSAI applications.

3. Application Submission (Form B) Fill in Form B with business details, food product categories, premises address, and proprietor/partner/director information. Upload required documents.

4. Fee Payment Pay the prescribed registration fee online through the FoSCoS portal via net banking, UPI, or debit/credit card.

5. Application Processing The Registering Authority reviews the application. For Basic Registration, there is typically no physical inspection requirement, the application is processed based on submitted documents.

6. Grant of Registration Certificate Upon approval, the FSSAI Registration Certificate with the unique 14-digit Registration Number is issued digitally and can be downloaded from the FoSCoS portal.

7. Display and Compliance Display the FSSAI Registration Certificate at the business premises. Print the 14-digit Registration Number on all food products, packaging, menus (for restaurants), and marketing materials. Fees Registration Type Annual Turnover Government Fee


## Validity

Basic Registration Up to ₹12 lakh ₹100 per year 1–5 years State License ₹12 lakh – ₹20 crore ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 per year 1–5 years Central License Above ₹20 crore / importers ₹7,500 per year 1–5 years

## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Basic FSSAI Registration (end-to-end) ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 State License application support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Central License application support ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 Document preparation and review ₹1,000 – ₹2,500 Upgrade from Registration to License ₹2,500 – ₹5,000

Government fees are paid directly to FSSAI via the FoSCoS portal. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

Registration/License Type Minimum Validity Maximum Validity Basic Registration 1 year 5 years State License 1 year 5 years Central License 1 year 5 years

The FBO chooses the validity period (1 to 5 years) at the time of application. Fees are charged proportionally for the chosen period. Opting for a longer validity period reduces administrative burden and renewal compliance risk. The certificate expires on the end date specified on it and must be renewed before expiry to avoid lapsing.

Renewal FSSAI Registration must be renewed before the expiry date to maintain continuity of authorisation Renewal applications should be filed at least 30 days before the expiry date Renewal is done through the FoSCoS portal under the "Renewal" section using the existing Registration Number A late fee of ₹100 per day is applicable for delayed renewal applications (filed after expiry) If the registration lapses without renewal, the FBO must apply for a fresh registration,  this may involve re-inspection and delays All documents must be re-verified at the time of renewal; updated documents (new premises lease, updated product list) must be submitted if there are any changes Renewals are processed within 30 days from the date of application submission under the deemed approval mechanism

## Frequently asked questions

**I sell homemade pickles and chutneys online through Instagram. Do I need FSSAI Registration?** Yes, without exception. Any commercial food business,  including home-based operations selling through social media, WhatsApp, or online marketplaces,  requires valid FSSAI authorisation. If your annual turnover is below ₹12 lakh, Basic Registration suffices. Online food sales platforms also require you to display a valid FSSAI number before listing your products. Operating without it exposes you to penalties and delisting from platforms.

**Can I operate multiple food businesses from the same premises under one FSSAI Registration?** Yes, if all food activities are conducted from the same physical address and the combined turnover falls within the Basic Registration threshold. However, the FSSAI Registration Certificate is premises-specific. If you operate from multiple locations,  even within the same city,  each location requires its own FSSAI Registration or License. You cannot use one Registration number across different business premises.

**How long does it take to get FSSAI Basic Registration?** For Basic Registration, the FoSCoS portal processes applications within 7–10 working days from the date of complete application submission. In practice, if the application is complete and documents are in order, approvals are often granted within 3–5 working days. If the Registering Authority does not grant or reject the application within 7 days, it is deemed approved. State Licenses and Central Licenses have longer timelines of 30–60 days.

**Is FSSAI Registration mandatory for a cloud kitchen that only does delivery,  no dine-in?** Absolutely yes. Cloud kitchens are food businesses under FSSAI's framework regardless of whether they have dine-in or takeaway service. In fact, FSSAI has issued specific guidelines for cloud kitchens requiring registration, hygiene compliance, and mandatory display of FSSAI numbers on delivery packaging and on aggregator platforms like Swiggy and Zomato. Cloud kitchens with revenues above ₹12 lakh need to upgrade to a State License.

**What food categories are covered under Basic FSSAI Registration?** Basic FSSAI Registration covers a wide range of food products including cereals and cereal products, pulses and pulse products, milk and milk products (small-scale), fruits and vegetables (fresh and processed), meat and meat products (small scale), bakery products, condiments, spices, edible oils, beverages (non-alcoholic), and packaged drinking water (small scale). However, certain high-risk and specialised categories,  infant foods, nutraceuticals, proprietary foods, health supplements, and food additives,  require a State or Central License even at low turnover levels.

services.name: Trade License
services.shortDescription: Municipal trade/shop license (e.g. Gumasta) for local business operations.
services.content:
# Trade License

## Short overview

A Trade License is a municipal authorisation issued by the local municipal corporation or municipality permitting a person or entity to carry out a specified business or trade at a specified location. It is governed by state Municipal Corporation Acts and is one of the foundational compliance requirements for any business operating from a fixed premises within municipal limits.

The license ensures that businesses operate in zones permitted under municipal planning, comply with local trade norms, and contribute to municipal revenue. Different categories of trades have different license requirements, hazardous, non-hazardous, food, manufacturing, services.

## Key features

- Municipal-level license
- Premises-specific
- Annual validity (typically)
- Trade category-specific fees

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation to operate business
- Required for various downstream registrations (GST, FSSAI, etc.)
- Required for bank account opening (current account)
- Tendering eligibility (government or PSU)
- Consumer trust
- Legal protection

## Limitations

- Multiple municipality registrations for multi-location operations
- Annual renewal
- Inspection-based renewal
- State and municipal variations
- Categorisation complexity for diverse businesses

## Applicability

Required pan-India in all municipal areas: BMC Mumbai (Trade License), MCD Delhi (Trade License), BBMP Bangalore (Trade License), Chennai Corporation (Trade License), all other municipalities.

Common applicants: shops, stores, retail outlets, restaurants, cafes, service businesses, manufacturing units, professional offices.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | Municipality-specific |
| Business address proof | Lease or ownership |
| Identity of proprietor | PAN, Aadhaar |
| Premises plan | Layout |
| NOC from owner | If leased |
| Building completion certificate | If applicable |
| Fire NOC | For specific categories |
| Environment NOC | For polluting trades |
| Other category-specific permissions | |

## Process

1. Apply at municipal office (online in most metros).
2. Documentation submission.
3. Inspection (if required).
4. Fee payment.
5. License issuance.

Timeline: 7 to 60 days.

## Government fees

| Business category | Approximate annual fee |
| --- | --- |
| Small retail or services | ₹500 to ₹5,000 |
| Restaurants | ₹2,000 to ₹15,000 |
| Manufacturing units | ₹5,000 to ₹50,000 |
| Hazardous trades | ₹10,000 to ₹1,00,000 |

Fees vary substantially by municipality and category.

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity (April to March in most municipalities). Renewal 30 days before expiry.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is Trade License needed for online businesses with no physical premises?** If you have a registered business address (even a small office), Trade License is typically required. Pure home-based online businesses below thresholds may be exempt in some municipalities.

**2. Is Trade License same as Shops and Establishments License?** Different licenses with overlap. Shops Act covers labour aspects; Trade License covers business permission. Both often required.

**3. Can multiple businesses share one Trade License at same address?** Generally no. Each distinct trade typically requires its own license.

**4. What categories are typically prohibited from Trade Licenses in residential areas?** Hazardous trades, manufacturing units, large retail, and certain service businesses are typically prohibited in residential zones.

**5. What's the renewal grace period after expiry?** Most municipalities allow 30 to 60 days grace with late fee. Beyond this, fresh application may be needed.

All service content is for informational purposes. Municipal rules and fees vary. Consult your local body and advisors.
