services.name: Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)
services.shortDescription: Class II and Class III Digital Signature Certificates with USB tokens.
services.content:
# Digital Signature Certificate (dsc)

## Overview

A Digital Signature Certificate (DSC) is an electronic equivalent of a physical signature, issued by a Certifying Authority (CA) licensed by the Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA) under the Information Technology Act, 2000. A DSC authenticates the identity of the holder and enables secure, legally valid electronic signing of documents, forms, and transactions. In India, DSCs are mandatory for a wide range of government filings and electronic transactions,  including MCA (Ministry of Corporate Affairs) filings, Income Tax Return filings, GST filings, EPFO submissions, Customs/ICEGATE filings, FSSAI applications, DGFT EXIM filings, e-tendering on government procurement portals, and many more. They are also used for internal corporate governance,  digitally signing board resolutions, agreements, and official correspondence. DSCs are issued in three classes: Class 1 DSC: Validates email address and username. Used for minimal-security applications. Class 2 DSC: (Discontinued from January 2021) Previously used for IT and MCA filings. Class 3 DSC: The highest class,  validates identity by verifying identity documents. Now the only class issued for signing purposes. Mandatory for MCA filings, e-tendering, GST registration, Customs, FSSAI, and all other regulatory e-filings. DSCs are issued on USB cryptographic tokens (pen drive-like devices) that securely store the private key, preventing extraction or misuse.

## Features

Legally valid: DSCs issued by CCA-licensed Certifying Authorities (eMudhra, Sify, NeSL, (n)Code, NSDL, etc.) are legally valid under the IT Act, 2000,  digitally signed documents are admissible as evidence Class 3 for all signings: Since January 2021, only Class 3 DSC is issued for signing purposes in India,  ensuring the highest identity verification standard USB cryptographic token: DSC is stored on a FIPS 140-2 certified USB cryptographic token,  the private key cannot be extracted, ensuring security Individual and organisation DSCs: DSCs are issued to individuals (directors, professionals, authorised signatories) and can be tagged to organisations for specific filings Document DSC and eSign: DSC is available as a physical token for document signing; eSign (Aadhaar-based online signature) is a cloud-based alternative for individuals Validity up to 2 years: DSCs are issued with a validity of 1 or 2 years, renewable thereafter Multiple CA options: Several CCA-licensed Certifying Authorities in India,  providing competitive pricing and service options

## Benefits

Mandatory for government filings: DSC is mandatory for MCA director filings, IT returns for companies/LLPs, GST registration and returns, EPFO ECR submissions, ICEGATE customs filings, FSSAI applications, e-tender submissions,  no DSC means no filing Legal validity: Digitally signed documents have full legal standing under the IT Act,  equivalent to physical signatures for most purposes Faster processing: Electronic filing with DSC eliminates the need for physical document submission and wet signatures,  speeding up regulatory processing Tamper-evident: Digitally signed documents carry a cryptographic seal,  any tampering after signing is detectable, protecting document integrity Cost efficiency: Eliminates courier, printing, and notarisation costs for documents that can be digitally signed and submitted Remote signing: DSC enables directors, partners, and authorised signatories to sign documents and file forms from anywhere,  no physical presence required Audit trail: All DSC-signed transactions create an immutable audit trail,  valuable for compliance, legal, and forensic purposes

## Limitations

USB token dependency: The DSC resides on a physical USB token,  if lost, stolen, or damaged, a fresh DSC must be obtained; no remote backup of the private key is possible Device compatibility: The USB token requires driver installation and is compatible with Windows and specific browser configurations,  Mac and Linux compatibility can be limited Renewal required: DSC expires after 1–2 years; all systems and filings using the DSC must be updated with the new DSC on renewal,  sometimes causing disruption if renewal is delayed Not transferable: A DSC is issued to an individual; it cannot be transferred to another person,  when a director resigns, the new director must obtain their own DSC Token damage risk: Physical tokens can be damaged,  rough handling, static electricity, or water exposure can render the token inoperable, requiring emergency DSC replacement eSign limitations: Aadhaar-based eSign (cloud alternative to DSC token) has transaction volume limits and may not be accepted for all regulatory filings

## Applicability

Directors and designated partners: All directors of companies and designated partners of LLPs filing forms on the MCA portal Chartered Accountants, Company Secretaries, and other professionals: For filing returns, certificates, and attestations on MCA, Income Tax, and GST portals Authorised signatories: For GST registration, EPFO filings, ICEGATE customs submissions, FSSAI applications Individual taxpayers: For Income Tax e-filings where digital signature is required (companies, LLPs, firms above threshold) E-tendering participants: All participants in government e-procurement (GeM portal, CPPP, state e-tender portals) require Class 3 DSC EXIM businesses: For DGFT applications, Customs filings, and ECGC applications Applicable pan-India,  DSC is obtained online with video KYC; no geographical limitation.

## Documents Required

Document Individual DSC Organisation/Director DSC PAN card ✓ ✓ Aadhaar card ✓ ✓ Passport-size photograph ✓ ✓ Email ID and mobile number ✓ ✓ Video KYC (live verification) ✓ ✓ Certificate of Incorporation (for company) Not required ✓ Board resolution authorising DSC Not required ✓ (for designated DSC) GST certificate (for GST-related DSC) Not required ✓ (if applicable)

## Process

1. Choose Certifying Authority Select a CCA-licensed Certifying Authority (eMudhra, Sify, NeSL, (n)Code, NSDL, Capricorn, etc.) based on pricing, service, and portal compatibility.

2. Online Application Visit the CA's website and fill the DSC application form, select DSC type (Signing Only / Signing and Encryption), class (Class 3), validity (1 or 2 years), and token requirement.

3. Document Upload Upload PAN card, Aadhaar card, photograph, and other required documents on the CA portal.

4. Video KYC Complete the video KYC process, a live video call with the CA representative for identity verification. The applicant must present their PAN and Aadhaar to the camera.

5. Fee Payment Pay the DSC fee online via net banking, UPI, or debit/credit card.

6. USB Token Delivery The CA ships the pre-installed USB cryptographic token (with DSC loaded) to the applicant's registered address, typically within 3–5 working days.

7. Installation and Testing Install the token driver on the computer. Test the DSC by signing a sample document or logging in to the MCA portal.

8. Registration on Portals Register/update the DSC on all relevant portals, MCA, IT e-filing, GST portal, EPFO, ICEGATE, etc. Fees DSC Type


## Validity

Government/CA Fee Class 3 DSC (Signing Only) 1 year ₹800 – ₹1,500 Class 3 DSC (Signing Only) 2 years ₹1,200 – ₹2,500 Class 3 DSC (Signing + Encryption) 2 years ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 USB Cryptographic Token One-time ₹500 – ₹1,000 DSC Renewal 1–2 years ₹800 – ₹2,000

## Professional Service

Estimated Fee DSC procurement and setup assistance ₹500 – ₹1,500 Portal registration of DSC ₹500 – ₹1,000 Bulk DSC procurement (10+ DSCs) ₹400 – ₹800 per DSC

Fees vary by Certifying Authority. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

DSC Option

## Validity

Standard 1 year from date of issue Extended 2 years from date of issue

DSC must be renewed before expiry. On renewal, a new certificate is issued on the same or a new USB token. If the DSC expires during a filing, the filing will fail,  proactive renewal (30–60 days before expiry) is recommended. The USB token hardware can be reused for renewal; only the digital certificate is updated.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can one USB token hold DSCs of multiple persons?** Standard USB cryptographic tokens are designed to hold only one DSC (for one individual) for security purposes. Some advanced multi-slot tokens can hold multiple certificates,  but this is not standard practice for regulatory filings and raises security concerns. Each director, partner, or authorised signatory should have their own dedicated USB token with their individual DSC. Sharing tokens between individuals compromises the non-repudiation principle of digital signatures.

**What happens if my USB DSC token is lost or stolen?** Immediately contact your Certifying Authority to revoke the compromised DSC. Revoking the certificate invalidates it and prevents misuse by whoever possesses the token. After revocation, you must apply for a fresh DSC on a new token. Revocation is critical and time-sensitive,  delay in reporting a lost token could allow misuse for fraudulent filings. Some CAs provide a lost token replacement service with expedited processing.

**I am a foreign national serving as a director of an Indian company. Can I get an Indian DSC?** Yes. Foreign nationals serving as directors of Indian companies can obtain Indian Class 3 DSCs. Since foreign nationals may not have Aadhaar, the video KYC process uses passport-based identity verification. The CA requires a certified copy of the passport (pages with photo, address, and validity), overseas address proof, and PAN (which foreign national directors are required to obtain). Some CAs provide dedicated services for overseas DSC applications.

**Is an Aadhaar-based eSign a valid substitute for a USB token DSC?** Aadhaar-based eSign is valid and legally recognised under the IT Act for many purposes, including signing documents, agreements, and certain government forms. However, not all regulatory portals accept eSign in place of a USB token DSC,  specifically, the MCA portal for company director filings requires a USB token Class 3 DSC and does not accept eSign. GSTN and Income Tax portals have broader eSign acceptance. Check the specific portal's requirements before relying on eSign instead of a token DSC.

**My DSC expired two weeks ago and I missed my MCA filing deadline. What should I do?** First, apply for a fresh/renewed DSC immediately,  processing takes 3–5 working days for token delivery. For the missed MCA filing, file as soon as the new DSC is operational and pay the applicable late fee (MCA late fees vary by form type and duration of delay). If the form is time-sensitive (like a board resolution filing or charge registration), additional steps may be required. In future, set reminders 60 days before DSC expiry and renew proactively to prevent filing disruptions.

services.name: Health License
services.shortDescription: Municipal Health License for specified trades and activities in addition to Trade License.
services.content:
# Health License

## Short overview

A Health License (also called Health Trade License or Sanitary License) is a state or municipal license required for businesses dealing with food, health-affecting products and services, or operations that have public health implications. The license is issued by municipal corporations, health departments, or local bodies under state-specific Public Health Acts and Municipal Corporation Acts.

While FSSAI handles food businesses centrally, the Health License is a separate municipal-level authorisation often required in addition to FSSAI for food businesses, hospitals, clinics, beauty parlours, gyms, swimming pools, salons, and similar establishments.

## Key features

- Municipal-level license
- Premises-specific
- Annual validity
- Inspection-based grant

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation for health-related businesses
- Demonstrates compliance with public health norms
- Required for various municipal permits
- Builds consumer confidence
- Required for business loans and insurance

## Limitations

- Multiple municipal permissions sometimes required
- Annual renewal
- Inspection-based approval
- State and municipality-specific variations
- Overlaps with FSSAI for food businesses (dual compliance)

## Applicability

Required pan-India with municipal-specific frameworks: Mumbai (BMC Health License), Delhi (MCD Health Trade License), Bangalore (BBMP Trade License, health category), Chennai (Chennai Corporation Health License), other municipalities, similar local frameworks.

Common applicants: restaurants, eateries, food shops, hospitals, clinics, nursing homes, beauty parlours, salons, gyms, swimming pools, spas, massage parlours, pharmacy stores, meat shops, dairy outlets.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | Municipal-specific |
| Business address proof | Lease or ownership |
| Identity of proprietor | PAN, Aadhaar |
| Trade license | Existing if any |
| FSSAI license | For food businesses |
| Drug license | For pharmacies |
| Premises plan | Layout |
| Water source proof | Water connection details |
| Drainage approval | |
| NOC from neighbours | In some municipalities |
| Medical fitness certificates | For staff handling food or health |

## Process

1. Apply at municipal office with documents.
2. Inspection by Health Officer or Sanitary Inspector.
3. Compliance verification (water, drainage, hygiene).
4. Fee payment.
5. License issuance.

Timeline: 30 to 90 days.

## Government fees

| Business type | Approximate annual fee |
| --- | --- |
| Small food shops | ₹2,000 to ₹10,000 |
| Restaurants | ₹5,000 to ₹25,000 |
| Salons, gyms | ₹3,000 to ₹15,000 |
| Hospitals | ₹15,000 to ₹1,00,000+ |
| Spas | ₹5,000 to ₹25,000 |

Fees vary by municipality and category.

## Validity and renewal

Annual (April to March in most municipalities). Renew 30 days before expiry. Reinspection at renewal.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. If I have an FSSAI license, do I also need a Health License?** In most states, yes. FSSAI is central food safety; Health License is municipal public health authorisation. Both are typically required.

**2. Are massage parlours required to have Health Licenses?** Yes, especially in metros. Beauty parlours, spas, and massage centres require Health Licenses for hygiene compliance.

**3. Can a pharmacy operate without a Health License if it has a Drug License?** Drug License is for drug retail; Health License covers premises hygiene. Both are typically required in major municipalities.

**4. What is the typical inspection focus during Health License grant?** Hygiene, water source, drainage, ventilation, pest control, staff health, premises cleanliness, and category-specific compliance.

**5. Can a Health License be transferred when business is sold?** Generally no. New owner must apply for fresh license in their name.

All service content is for informational purposes. Procedures, fees, and requirements vary by municipality. Consult local authorities and qualified professionals for current rules.

Proprietorship is the simplest form of business in India, owned and managed by a single individual (the proprietor) who retains all profits. It is typically suited for micro, small and medium enterprises in the unorganised sector.

To establish business identity, a proprietorship should obtain Udyog Aadhaar / MSME Registration; without it, banks and other stakeholders may not recognise the entity as a formal business. Trade License (where applicable) and TAN Registration for TDS/TCS on payments may also be required.

We provide expert support and guidance for forming a proprietorship in your desired business name and obtaining the necessary registrations.

We provide comprehensive support in drafting a robust Partnership Deed and registering your partnership firm with the concerned authorities, along with optional GST setup and compliance.

services.name: One Person Company (OPC)
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of One Person Company with DSC, DIN, PAN, TAN, GST and more.
services.content:
# ONE Person Company Registration

## Overview

A One Person Company (OPC) is a distinctive business structure introduced under the Companies Act, 2013 that enables a single entrepreneur to operate a corporate entity with limited liability. OPC combines the benefits of sole proprietorship and private limited company, offering solo entrepreneurs the privileges of a separate legal entity, perpetual succession, and easier access to credit.

## Features

An OPC has only one shareholder (who is also typically the sole director). A nominee must be appointed at the time of incorporation, who takes over if the sole member dies or becomes incapacitated. The suffix "OPC Private Limited" is used. There is no requirement for AGM. Only Indian resident citizens can form an OPC.

## Advantages

OPC provides limited liability protection to the single owner, a separate legal identity, perpetual existence, easier access to bank loans, better credibility than proprietorship, and fewer compliance requirements than private limited companies. It allows a solo entrepreneur to participate in tenders and scale up professionally.

## Limitations

Only Indian residents (staying 120+ days in the previous FY) can form an OPC. A person can be a member of only one OPC at a time. Conversion to private limited is mandatory if paid-up capital exceeds Rs. 50 lakh or turnover exceeds Rs. 2 crore for 3 consecutive years (this threshold restriction was later relaxed but conversion triggers still apply in certain cases). FDI is not allowed in an OPC. NRIs can now form an OPC, but certain restrictions apply.

## Applicability

OPC registration is available across all states and UTs including Maharashtra, Karnataka, Delhi, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Punjab, Bihar, Odisha, and others. Registration is centralized through the MCA portal.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of member & nominee | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar of member & nominee | ID proof |
| Passport-size photograph | Member & nominee |
| Address proof (utility bill/bank statement) | Recent |
| Registered office proof | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |
| DSC of director | Class 3 |
| DIN application | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA and AOA | Drafted |
| Consent of nominee (INC-3) | Signed |
| Declaration of director (INC-9) | Signed |

## Process

Obtain DSC for the sole director. Apply for name reservation via SPICe+ Part A. File SPICe+ Part B along with MOA, AOA, INC-3 (nominee consent), INC-9 (declaration), and AGILE-PRO (for PAN, TAN, GST, EPFO, ESIC, bank account). Post-approval, the Certificate of Incorporation, PAN, TAN, and other registrations are issued. Open a bank account to commence operations.

## Government Fees

| Authorized Capital | Fee | Up to Rs. 10 lakh | Rs. 2,000  -  Rs. 6,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 10-25 lakh | Rs. 6,000  -  Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 25-50 lakh | Rs. 15,000  -  Rs. 30,000 |
| Name Reservation | Rs. 1,000 | DSC | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 |

Stamp duty (state-based) Rs. 500  -  Rs. 10,000

## Timeline

OPC registration typically takes 10 - 15 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ filing and incorporation: 7-10 days. Bank account and post-incorporation setup: 3-5 days.

## Comparison

| Parameter | OPC | Sole Proprietorship | Pvt Ltd |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Min. Members | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| Liability | Limited | Unlimited | Limited |
| Legal Entity | Separate | Same as owner | Separate |
| Compliance | Moderate | Low | High |
| AGM | Not required | N/A | Required |
| FDI | Not allowed | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Tax | 25%/22% | Individual slab | 25%/22% |

## FAQs

**Q1. Can an NRI form an OPC?** Yes, as per the 2021 amendment, NRIs can form an OPC in India. Prior to this, only resident Indians could form OPCs. The residency requirement is now 120 days in the preceding financial year.

**Q2. Who is a nominee in an OPC and what role does the nominee play?** A nominee is a person who takes over the OPC in the event of the member's death or incapacity. The nominee must be an Indian citizen and must consent in writing (Form INC-3).

**Q3. Is OPC required to hold AGM?** No, OPCs are exempt from holding Annual General Meetings. However, they must file annual returns (MGT-7A) and financial statements (AOC-4) with the MCA.

**Q4. When must an OPC convert into a Pvt Ltd Company?** The mandatory conversion threshold (based on paid-up capital of Rs. 50 lakh or average turnover of Rs. 2 crore) was removed in 2021, allowing OPCs to grow without mandatory conversion. Voluntary conversion is allowed anytime after 2 years.

**Q5. Can one person form multiple OPCs?** No, a person can be a member of only one OPC at a time. The same restriction applies to being a nominee in more than one OPC.

services.content:
A Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) is a hybrid business structure combining features of a company and a traditional partnership. It provides limited liability to partners while allowing operational flexibility through an LLP Agreement.

An LLP is a separate legal entity with perpetual succession. Partners’ liability is generally limited to their agreed contribution, and they are insulated from liabilities arising out of other partners’ wrongful acts or misconduct. Compared to companies, LLPs enjoy more contractual flexibility, a simpler governance structure and lower compliance burden.

We provide end‑to‑end support in forming LLPs, drafting LLP Agreements, obtaining approvals and setting up tax registrations and compliance.

services.name: Private Limited Company
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of private limited companies with DSCs, DINs, PAN, TAN, GST and MSME.
services.content:
# Private Limited Company Registration

## Overview

A Private Limited Company is the most preferred business structure in India for startups, SMEs, and growing businesses. Governed by the Companies Act, 2013, it offers a separate legal identity, limited liability, perpetual succession, and ease of raising capital. It's the go-to structure for venture capital, angel investment, and structured growth. Over 10 lakh private limited companies are registered in India.

## Features

Minimum 2 shareholders and 2 directors are required, with at least one director being an Indian resident. Maximum 200 shareholders are allowed. There is no minimum capital requirement. Shares are privately held and cannot be publicly traded. The word "Private Limited" must appear at the end of the name. It cannot issue prospectus to the public.

## Advantages

Private limited companies offer limited liability protection, separate legal entity status, perpetual succession, ease of fundraising from investors, enhanced credibility with banks and vendors, tax benefits under Section 115BAA (22%) or 115BAB (15% for new manufacturers), and easy transferability of shares. Startups enjoy additional incentives under DPIIT recognition.

## Limitations

Compliance is stringent,  including mandatory board meetings, AGM, annual filings (AOC-4, MGT-7), statutory audit regardless of turnover, and DIR-3 KYC. Shares cannot be freely transferred without board approval. Cannot invite public subscription. Operational costs are higher than proprietorship/partnership/LLP. Dissolution requires formal winding-up process.

## Applicability

Private limited company registration is available across all Indian states and UTs. Maharashtra, Karnataka, Delhi NCR, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Punjab, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, and Bihar see the highest volume of registrations. Incorporation is centralized through the MCA portal.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of all directors & shareholders | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar of all directors & shareholders | ID proof |
| Passport (for foreign nationals) | Apostilled |
| Address proof (recent utility bill/bank statement) | ≤ 2 months old |
| Passport-size photographs | All directors/shareholders |
| Registered office proof | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |
| DIN application | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA & AOA | Drafted |
| INC-9 declaration | Signed by all |

## Process

Obtain DSC for all directors. Reserve the name through SPICe+ Part A (2 name options, up to 1 resubmission). File SPICe+ Part B along with MOA, AOA, INC-9, AGILE-PRO (for PAN, TAN, GST, EPFO, ESIC, bank account), and other declarations. On approval, the Certificate of Incorporation is issued along with PAN, TAN, and other registrations. Open a bank account to commence business.

## Government Fees

| Authorized Capital | Fee (approx.) | Up to Rs. 1 lakh | Rs. 2,000  -  Rs. 6,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 1-5 lakh | Rs. 5,000  -  Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 5-10 lakh | Rs. 10,000  -  Rs. 25,000 |
| Rs. 10-25 lakh | Rs. 20,000  -  Rs. 40,000 | Above Rs. 25 lakh | Rs. 40,000+ |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 | Stamp duty | Varies by state |

## Timeline

Private limited company registration typically takes 10 - 15 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ filing and incorporation: 7-10 days. Bank account activation: 3-7 days post-incorporation.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Pvt Ltd | OPC | LLP |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Public Ltd | Min. Members | 2 | 1 |
| 2 | 7 | Max. Members | 200 |
| 1 | No limit | No limit | Compliance |
| High | Moderate | Moderate | Very High |
| Fundraising | Easy (PE/VC) | Limited | Limited |
| Public + PE/VC | FDI | Allowed | Not allowed |
| Limited | Allowed | Audit | Mandatory |
| Mandatory | If > Rs. 40L | Mandatory | Transfer of Shares |
| Restricted | N/A | Restricted | Freely |

## FAQs

**Q1. What is the minimum capital required?** There is no minimum paid-up capital requirement for a private limited company. You can incorporate with as low as Rs. 1 of paid-up capital, though authorized capital carries associated stamp duty.

**Q2. Can a private limited company be converted into a public limited company?** Yes, a private limited company can be converted into a public limited company by altering its articles of association, passing a special resolution, and filing Form MGT-14 and INC-27 with the MCA.

**Q3. Is GST registration automatic with incorporation?** GST can be applied for through the AGILE-PRO form at the time of incorporation. It is optional and becomes mandatory only when the turnover threshold is crossed or interstate supply is made.

**Q4. Can a foreign national be a director of an Indian private limited company?** Yes, a foreign national can be a director, subject to obtaining DIN. However, at least one director on the board must be a resident of India (having stayed at least 182 days in the previous calendar year).

**Q5. What is the difference between authorized capital and paid-up capital?** Authorized capital is the maximum capital a company is legally permitted to issue to shareholders. Paid-up capital is the actual capital received from shareholders against issued shares.

services.name: Public Limited Company
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of public limited companies with higher capital and compliance needs.
services.content:
# Public Limited Company Registration

## Overview

A Public Limited Company is a company that can raise capital from the public by issuing shares, which may be listed on a stock exchange. Governed by the Companies Act, 2013, public limited companies require higher minimum members, directors, and have stricter compliance norms. They are suited for large-scale businesses seeking public investment, listing on stock exchanges like BSE or NSE, or broader ownership.

## Features

Minimum 7 shareholders and 3 directors are required, with at least one resident director. There is no maximum limit on shareholders. The name ends with "Limited" (not "Private Limited"). Shares are freely transferable. A public limited company can issue a prospectus to the public, undertake IPOs, and list on stock exchanges. It must hold statutory, AGM, and board meetings regularly.

## Advantages

Public limited companies can raise massive capital through public offerings. Shares are freely transferable, enhancing liquidity. The company enjoys enhanced credibility, institutional trust, and brand recognition. Listing provides exit opportunities for early investors. Expansion and M&A activities are easier. Limited liability protection for shareholders remains intact.

## Limitations

Compliance is very high,  including SEBI LODR (if listed), secretarial audit, independent directors, audit committee, CSR (if thresholds are met), quarterly disclosures, and corporate governance reports. Formation costs are higher. Takeover risks exist as shares are freely transferable. Detailed public disclosures are mandatory. Decision-making is slower due to larger boards and shareholder approvals.

## Applicability

Public limited company registration is available pan-India. Listed and unlisted public companies operate across Maharashtra (Mumbai being the financial capital), Karnataka, Delhi NCR, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, and other states. Listed entities also comply with BSE/NSE and SEBI regulations.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of all directors & subscribers | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar/Passport | ID proof |
| Address proof | Recent utility bill/bank statement |
| Passport-size photographs | All |
| Registered office proof | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |
| DIN for directors | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA & AOA | Drafted |
| INC-9 declaration | Signed |
| Consent of directors (DIR-2) | Signed |

## Process

Obtain DSC for all proposed directors. Apply for name approval via SPICe+ Part A. File SPICe+ Part B with MOA, AOA, INC-9, DIR-2, AGILE-PRO, and other declarations. Once incorporated, obtain Certificate of Commencement of Business (INC-20A) before commencing operations. Comply with regulatory formalities including appointing auditors, holding statutory meeting, and optionally preparing for IPO.

## Government Fees

| Authorized Capital | Fee | Up to Rs. 5 lakh | Rs. 5,000  -  Rs. 15,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 5-10 lakh | Rs. 15,000  -  Rs. 25,000 | Rs. 10-50 lakh | Rs. 25,000  -  Rs. 75,000 |
| Rs. 50 lakh-1 cr | Rs. 75,000  -  Rs. 1,50,000 | Above Rs. 1 cr | Rs. 1,50,000+ |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 | Stamp duty | Varies by state |

## Timeline

Public limited company registration typically takes 15 - 25 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ filing and incorporation: 10-15 days. Commencement of Business Certificate (INC-20A): within 180 days of incorporation. Listing (if planned) takes several months of additional SEBI process.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Public Ltd | Pvt Ltd | LLP |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Min. Members | 7 | 2 | 2 |
| Min. Directors | 3 | 2 | 2 designated partners |
| Max. Members | Unlimited | 200 | No limit |
| Prospectus | Can issue | Cannot issue | Cannot |
| Share Transfer | Free | Restricted | Restricted |
| Listing | Possible | Not possible | Not possible |
| Compliance | Very High | High | Moderate |
| AGM | Mandatory | Mandatory | Not required |

## FAQs

**Q1. What is the minimum paid-up capital for a public limited company?** Under the Companies (Amendment) Act, 2015, the minimum paid-up capital requirement was removed. A public limited company can now be incorporated with any paid-up capital.

**Q2. Is INC-20A required for public limited companies?** Yes, every company (both public and private) must file INC-20A (Declaration for Commencement of Business) within 180 days of incorporation. Failure attracts Rs. 50,000 penalty on the company.

**Q3. Is a public limited company always listed?** No, public limited companies can be listed or unlisted. Listing on a stock exchange is optional and subject to SEBI listing requirements and eligibility.

**Q4. What is the role of independent directors?** Listed public companies must have at least 1/3rd of the board as independent directors. Specified unlisted public companies (with paid-up capital ≥ Rs. 10 crore, turnover ≥ Rs. 100 crore, or loans ≥ Rs. 50 crore) must have at least 2 independent directors.

**Q5. Can a public limited company be converted back to private?** Yes, through a special resolution, NCLT approval, and compliance with Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013. The conversion triggers changes in the MOA, AOA, and name.

services.name: Nidhi Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of Nidhi companies for mutual benefit deposits and loans among members.
services.content:
# Nidhi Company Registration

## Overview

A Nidhi Company is a type of Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC) recognized under Section 406 of the Companies Act, 2013. It is formed for the purpose of borrowing and lending money among its members (known as "mutual benefit society"). Nidhi Companies promote thrift and savings habit among members and are governed by the Nidhi Rules, 2014. They do not require an RBI license but must comply with MCA regulations.

## Features

A Nidhi Company operates only with its members,  deposits and loans are exclusively member-to-member. It requires a minimum of 7 shareholders and 3 directors at the time of incorporation. Minimum paid-up equity share capital of Rs. 10 lakh is mandatory. Within 120 days of incorporation, it must have at least 200 members, Rs. 20 lakh Net Owned Funds, and file Form NDH-1.

## Advantages

Nidhi Companies offer a simple structure for mutual savings and lending without needing RBI registration. They promote financial inclusion in rural and semi-urban areas. Interest rates for loans are typically lower than banks for members. Limited liability, separate legal entity, and perpetual succession are assured. Operational costs are lower than NBFCs.

## Limitations

Activities are restricted to members only,  no business with non-members is permitted. They cannot deal in chit funds, hire purchase, insurance, or acquisition of securities. Advertising for deposits is prohibited. Branch opening is restricted. Interest rates on deposits are capped. Compliance is strict, and non-compliance can lead to license cancellation.

## Applicability

Nidhi Companies are popular in South India, especially Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Telangana. They are also present in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and other states. Nidhi concept does not apply to Jammu & Kashmir. They operate at local and district levels to promote thrift among specific communities.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of directors & shareholders | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar/Voter ID/Passport | ID proof |
| Address proof (bank statement/utility bill) | Recent (2 months) |
| Photographs | Passport-size |
| Registered office proof | Rent agreement + NOC + utility bill |
| DSC of directors | Class 3 |
| DIN of directors | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA and AOA | With "Nidhi" in objects |
| Declaration by promoters | In INC-9 |

## Process

Obtain DSCs for all directors. Apply for name approval (name must end with "Nidhi Limited"). File SPICe+ with MOA, AOA, and declarations. Upon incorporation, receive COI, PAN, TAN. Within 120 days, achieve minimum criteria (200 members, Rs. 20 lakh NOF, 10% unencumbered deposits). File Form NDH-1 within 90 days of the end of the first financial year. Thereafter, file Form NDH-3 half-yearly.

## Government Fees

| Item | Fee | Name Reservation | Rs. 1,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| DSC (per director) | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 | SPICe+ filing fee | Rs. 5,000  -  Rs. 15,000 |
| Stamp duty (state-dependent) | Rs. 2,000  -  Rs. 15,000 | NDH-1 filing | Rs. 300  -  Rs. 600 |
| NDH-3 (half-yearly) | Rs. 300  -  Rs. 600 | PAN & TAN | Rs. 170 |

## Timeline

Nidhi Company incorporation takes 15 - 20 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ approval: 7-10 days. Post-incorporation compliance (NDH-1) must be met within 120 days and filed within 90 days after the end of the first FY.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Nidhi | NBFC | Cooperative Society |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Governing Law | Companies Act | RBI + Companies Act | Coop. Societies Act |
| Min. Capital | Rs. 10 lakh | Rs. 2 crore (NBFC) | Rs. 25 lakh (Credit) |
| RBI License | Not required | Required | Not required |
| Lending to | Members only | Public | Members |
| Regulator | MCA | RBI | State Registrar |
| Members | Min 200 in 120 days | N/A | Min 10 |

## FAQs

**Q1. Does a Nidhi Company require RBI approval?** No, Nidhi Companies are specifically exempted from RBI regulation as they deal only with their members. However, they are notified under Section 406 of the Companies Act.

**Q2. Can a Nidhi Company open branches anywhere?** A Nidhi Company can open up to 3 branches within the district only after earning profit for 3 consecutive financial years. Additional branches require Regional Director's approval.

**Q3. What is Net Owned Fund (NOF)?** NOF is the paid-up equity share capital plus free reserves minus accumulated losses and intangible assets. A minimum NOF of Rs. 20 lakh is mandatory within 120 days of incorporation.

**Q4. Can a Nidhi Company accept deposits from non-members?** No, a Nidhi Company can only accept deposits from its members and lend only to its members. Dealing with non-members is strictly prohibited.

**Q5. What is the interest rate limit for Nidhi deposits and loans?** The maximum interest rate on deposits cannot exceed the rate prescribed by RBI for NBFCs. Loan interest cannot exceed 7.5% above the highest deposit rate offered.

services.name: Producer Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of producer companies for primary producers and agribusinesses.
services.content:
# Producer Company Registration

## Overview

A Producer Company is a corporate form specifically designed for primary producers such as farmers, agriculturists, dairy farmers, fishermen, horticulturists, and artisans. Introduced under Part IXA of the Companies Act, 1956 (now reflected in Chapter XXIA of the Companies Act, 2013), it combines the benefits of a cooperative society with the efficiency and governance of a private limited company. Its primary objective is to facilitate production, harvesting, procurement, marketing, and sale of produce by its members.

## Features

Minimum 10 individual producers OR 2 producer institutions OR a combination of both are required. Minimum 5 directors are required, with a maximum of 15. There is no maximum limit on members. Minimum authorized capital is Rs. 5 lakh. The name must end with "Producer Company Limited". Only primary producers can be members. Operates on the principle of "one member, one vote" irrespective of shareholding.

## Advantages

Producer Companies enjoy the benefits of limited liability, separate legal identity, perpetual succession, and democratic governance. Members receive patronage bonus based on their business with the company. Income from agricultural activities is eligible for tax exemptions under Section 10(1). They can avail government subsidies, NABARD funding, and other agricultural schemes. Surplus is distributed as bonus and limited return on share capital.

## Limitations

Only primary producers can be members,  non-producers are excluded. Business activities are restricted to those listed in Section 581B (production, harvesting, processing, marketing of members' produce). Shares are not freely transferable,  only among members. Voting rights are capped at one vote per member. Raising external equity is limited. Complex governance structure compared to private limited companies.

## Applicability

Producer Companies are popular in agricultural states like Maharashtra, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Rajasthan, West Bengal, Kerala, Bihar, and Odisha. Government of India and NABARD actively promote Producer Companies through FPO (Farmer Producer Organization) schemes.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of all directors & members | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar of all directors & members | ID proof |
| Proof of being a primary producer | Land records, certificates |
| Address proof of members | Utility bill/bank statement |
| Photographs | Passport-size |
| Registered office proof | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |
| DIN application | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA and AOA | Producer Co. specific |
| INC-9 declaration | Signed |

## Process

Obtain DSCs for all directors. Apply for name approval via SPICe+ Part A (name must end with "Producer Company Limited"). File SPICe+ Part B with MOA (with producer-specific objects), AOA, INC-9, AGILE-PRO, and proof of members being primary producers. On approval, Certificate of Incorporation, PAN, TAN are issued. Open bank account and commence activities.

## Government Fees

| Authorized Capital | Fee | Rs. 5 lakh (min) | Rs. 5,000  -  Rs. 10,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 5-10 lakh | Rs. 10,000  -  Rs. 20,000 | Rs. 10-25 lakh | Rs. 20,000  -  Rs. 40,000 |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 | Name Reservation | Rs. 1,000 |

Stamp duty Varies by state

## Timeline

Producer Company registration takes 15 - 25 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ filing (with producer proof verification): 10-15 days. Bank account and NABARD/FPO empanelment can take additional 15-30 days.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Producer Co. | Cooperative Society | Pvt Ltd |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Governing Law | Companies Act | Coop. Act (state) | Companies Act |
| Min. Members | 10 producers/2 institutions | 10 | 2 |
| Max. Members | Unlimited | Unlimited | 200 |
| Voting | One member one vote | One member one vote | Per shareholding |
| Regulator | MCA (central) | State Registrar | MCA |
| Tax Benefit | Agri exemptions | Limited | Standard |
| Transferability | Restricted | Restricted | Restricted |

## FAQs

**Q1. Who can be a member of a Producer Company?** Only primary producers such as farmers, agriculturists, horticulturists, fishermen, artisans, beekeepers, and similar individuals, or institutions of primary producers, can be members of a Producer Company.

**Q2. What tax benefits are available to Producer Companies?** Income derived from agricultural activities is 100% exempt under Section 10(1). Additionally, Section 80PA allows 100% deduction on profits from specified activities (marketing of agricultural produce) for FPOs with turnover up to Rs. 100 crore.

**Q3. Can a Producer Company raise FDI?** No, FDI is not allowed in Producer Companies. However, they can raise funds through NABARD, SFAC, government schemes, and grants under FPO (Farmer Producer Organization) initiatives.

**Q4. What is the minimum capital requirement?** Minimum authorized capital is Rs. 5 lakh for a Producer Company. There is no minimum paid-up capital prescribed, but sufficient capital should be subscribed to meet operational needs.

**Q5. How is a Producer Company different from a cooperative?** Producer Companies are governed by the Companies Act and MCA (central regulation), offering corporate-style governance, while cooperatives are state-registered under respective Cooperative Societies Acts with significant state control and restrictions.

services.name: Section 8 Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of not‑for‑profit Section 8 companies for charitable and social objectives.
services.content:
# Section 8 Company Registration

## Overview

A Section 8 Company is a company incorporated under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013 for promoting charitable objects such as commerce, art, science, sports, education, research, social welfare, religion, charity, or protection of the environment. It operates as a non-profit entity,  profits, if any, are applied towards promoting its objectives and cannot be distributed as dividends to members. It enjoys the credibility of a corporate structure combined with the purpose of a charitable organization.

## Features

Section 8 companies have no minimum capital requirement. Minimum 2 members and 2 directors (for private) or 7 members and 3 directors (for public) are required. The name can be without "Limited" or "Private Limited" suffix. Central Government license is mandatory before incorporation. Profits must be used for charitable purposes only. It enjoys higher credibility than trusts or societies.

## Advantages

Section 8 companies offer higher credibility and public trust compared to trusts and societies. Eligible for 12A and 80G tax exemptions under the Income Tax Act. Can receive CSR funds from corporates and foreign donations (with FCRA registration). Enjoys stamp duty exemptions in some states. Has a corporate structure for better governance and transparency. No minimum capital requirement.

## Limitations

Cannot distribute profits or pay dividends. Strict compliance with MCA, Income Tax, and possibly FCRA (for foreign donations). Alteration of MOA requires Central Government approval. Section 8 license can be revoked for misuse. Conversion into a regular company requires stringent approvals. Members do not receive any personal financial benefit from the company.

## Applicability

Section 8 companies can be registered across all Indian states and UTs. Major concentrations are in Maharashtra, Delhi, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Telangana, West Bengal, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala, and Rajasthan. Registration is centralized through the MCA portal.

## Documents

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| PAN of all directors & subscribers | Mandatory |
| Aadhaar of Indian directors | ID proof |
| Passport (for foreigners) | Apostilled |
| Address proof | Recent utility bill |
| Photographs | Passport-size |
| Registered office proof | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |
| DIN for directors | Through SPICe+ |
| MOA (Form INC-13) | Specific for Section 8 |
| AOA | Drafted |
| Declaration (INC-14 by CA/CS/CWA) | Signed |
| Declaration (INC-15 by subscribers) | Signed |
| Projected statements (3 years) | Income-expenditure |
| Grounds for application | Detailed note |

## Process

Obtain DSC for all directors. Apply for name approval via SPICe+ Part A (name should reflect charitable purpose). File SPICe+ Part B along with MOA (INC-13), AOA, INC-14 (declaration by professional), INC-15 (declaration by subscribers), projected income-expenditure statement for 3 years, and grounds for application. The Registrar grants the Section 8 license and Certificate of Incorporation together. Apply for PAN, TAN, and subsequently 12A/80G registration.

## Government Fees

| Item | Fee | Name Reservation | Rs. 1,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| License Fee | Rs. 0 (exempted) | Stamp duty on MOA/AOA | Varies by state |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 | SPICe+ filing fee | Rs. 1,000  -  Rs. 5,000 |
| 12A/80G Registration | Nil | PAN & TAN | Rs. 170 |

## Timeline

Section 8 company registration takes 15 - 25 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. SPICe+ filing and license issuance: 10-15 days. 12A/80G application post-incorporation: 1-3 months. FCRA registration (if required): 3-6 months.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Section 8 Co. | Trust | Society |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Governing Law | Companies Act, 2013 | Indian Trusts Act, 1882 | Societies Reg. Act, 1860 |
| Regulator | MCA (central) | State Registrar | State Registrar |
| Min. Members | 2 (private) / 7 (public) | 2 (minimum trustees) | 7 |
| Credibility | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| CSR Funds | Eligible | Eligible (with approval) | Eligible (with approval) |
| Compliance | High | Low-Moderate | Moderate |
| Name Suffix | None required | None | None |

## FAQs

**Q1. Can a Section 8 company distribute profits?** No, Section 8 companies are strictly prohibited from distributing profits or paying dividends. All income must be used to promote the company's charitable objectives.

**Q2. Is 12A/80G registration mandatory for Section 8 companies?** While not mandatory for incorporation, 12A registration is essential for income tax exemption, and 80G registration is essential for donors to claim tax deductions. Both are highly recommended.

**Q3. Can foreign directors be appointed in a Section 8 company?** Yes, foreign nationals can be directors subject to obtaining DIN. However, at least one director must be an Indian resident. FCRA registration is required to accept foreign donations.

**Q4. Can a Section 8 company be converted into a regular company?** Yes, subject to strict conditions under Section 8(4)(ii),  including surrender of accumulated profits to another Section 8 entity, Central Government approval, and compliance with procedural requirements.

**Q5. How is a Section 8 company different from a trust?** A Section 8 company has higher credibility, structured corporate governance, and centralized MCA regulation, while a trust is regulated at the state level with less compliance but lower institutional credibility.

services.name: Indian Subsidiary of Foreign Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of Indian subsidiaries and wholly owned subsidiaries of foreign companies.
services.content:
# Indian Subsidiary OF Foreign Company Registration

## Overview

An Indian Subsidiary is a company incorporated in India where a foreign entity holds more than 50% of the paid-up share capital. It is governed by the Companies Act, 2013, FEMA, 1999, and RBI regulations. This is the most popular mode for foreign companies to establish operations in India, as it offers limited liability, 100% FDI under the automatic route (in most sectors), and perpetual succession.

## Features

The subsidiary has a separate legal identity from its parent company. It can be a Wholly-Owned Subsidiary (100% foreign ownership) or partly owned. Minimum of 2 shareholders and 2 directors are required, with at least one director being an Indian resident. There is no minimum paid-up capital requirement. FDI is permitted under automatic or approval routes depending on the sector.

## Advantages

Foreign parents enjoy limited liability and easy repatriation of profits. India offers a large consumer market, skilled workforce, and competitive costs. The subsidiary can independently enter into contracts, own property, sue and be sued. It enables localization of operations, tax efficiency through DTAA benefits, and easier access to Indian banking and labour.

## Limitations

Compliance is extensive,  including MCA filings, RBI reporting, transfer pricing, and tax audits. Sectors like retail multi-brand, defence, and atomic energy have FDI restrictions. FEMA compliance for inward remittances and share allotments is strict. Repatriation requires proper documentation and compliance with transfer pricing norms.

## Applicability

Indian subsidiaries can be incorporated across all Indian states,  Maharashtra, Karnataka, Delhi NCR, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, and UTs. States like Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Telangana are top FDI destinations due to their IT infrastructure and pro-business policies.

## Documents

| Document | Provider | Certificate of Incorporation of parent company | Foreign parent (apostilled) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Board resolution of parent company | Foreign parent | Passport of foreign directors | Foreign directors (apostilled) |
| Address proof of foreign directors | Foreign directors | PAN & Aadhaar of Indian director | Indian director |
| DSC of all directors | DSC authority | DIN application (Form DIR-3) | All directors |
| Registered office proof in India | Owner/landlord | NOC from property owner | Owner |

MOA & AOA Drafted by consultant

## Process

The process begins with obtaining DSCs for all proposed directors. Name approval is sought through SPICe+ Part A. Incorporation documents (SPICe+ Part B, AGILE-PRO, INC-9, MOA, AOA) are filed along with declarations. Post-approval, the Certificate of Incorporation, PAN, TAN, and bank account are issued. RBI reporting through FC-GPR is done within 30 days of share allotment.

## Government Fees

| Authorized Capital | Stamp Duty & MCA Fees (approx.) | Up to Rs. 1 lakh | Rs. 2,000  -  Rs. 5,000 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 1 lakh  -  Rs. 5 lakh | Rs. 5,000  -  Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 5 lakh  -  Rs. 10 lakh | Rs. 15,000  -  Rs. 30,000 |
| Above Rs. 10 lakh | Rs. 30,000+ (varies by state) | DSC per director | Rs. 1,500  -  Rs. 2,500 |

Name reservation Rs. 1,000

## Timeline

Overall incorporation typically takes 15 - 25 working days. DSC: 1-2 days. Name approval: 2-3 days. Apostille of foreign documents: 7-15 days. SPICe+ filing and incorporation: 7-10 days. PAN, TAN, bank account: 3-5 days post-incorporation.

## Comparison

| Parameter | Indian Subsidiary | Branch Office | Liaison Office |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Legal Entity | Separate | Extension of parent | Extension of parent |
| Business Allowed | Yes (full) | Limited activities | No commercial activity |
| FDI Route | Automatic (mostly) | RBI approval | RBI approval |
| Profit Repatriation | Yes, after tax | Yes | Not applicable |
| Setup Time | 15-25 days | 30-45 days | 30-45 days |
| Compliance | High | Medium | Low |

## FAQs

**Q1. Can a foreign company own 100% of an Indian subsidiary?** Yes, in most sectors under the automatic FDI route. Certain sectors like multi-brand retail, defence, and insurance have caps or require government approval.

**Q2. Is it necessary to have an Indian resident director?** Yes, at least one director on the board must be a resident of India, having stayed at least 182 days in the previous calendar year.

**Q3. How are profits of an Indian subsidiary repatriated?** Profits can be repatriated as dividends (after payment of Dividend Distribution Tax or TDS), royalty, technical fees, or buyback of shares, subject to FEMA compliance.

**Q4. What is FC-GPR and when is it filed?** FC-GPR is a form filed with RBI within 30 days of allotting shares to foreign investors, reporting the inward remittance and share allotment.

**Q5. Are foreign directors required to visit India for incorporation?** No, foreign directors can complete the entire process remotely using apostilled documents and Digital Signature Certificates obtained using their passport.

services.name: ITR‑1 (SAHAJ) Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑1 filing for resident individuals with income from salary, one house property and other sources.
services.content:
# ITR-1 form filing (Sahaj)

## Overview

ITR-1, popularly known as Sahaj (meaning "simple" in Hindi), is the simplest income tax return form designed for ordinary resident individuals with straightforward income profiles. It is prescribed by the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) under the Income Tax Act, 1961, and is applicable for individual taxpayers whose income arises primarily from salary, one house property, and other sources like interest, without the complexity of business income, capital gains, or foreign assets.

ITR-1 is a single-page form that has been continuously simplified over the years, with extensive pre-filling from employer data (Form 16), bank TDS data (Form 26AS), Annual Information Statement (AIS), and Taxpayer Information Summary (TIS). For most salaried individuals with standard incomes, filing ITR-1 involves primarily verifying the pre-filled data and making necessary corrections, before submitting the return online.

Despite its simplicity, ITR-1 filing requires careful attention, errors in claiming deductions (Section 80C, 80D, HRA exemption, LTA), incorrect selection of the tax regime (old vs. new), or failure to declare all income sources (bank interest, savings account interest, rental income) are common mistakes that lead to income tax notices.

## Applicability

ITR-1 can be filed by individuals who meet **ALL** of the following:

| Criterion | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| Residency | Ordinarily Resident in India |
| Total Income | Up to ₹50 lakh |
| Income from Salary | One employer salary (including pension) |
| Income from House Property | Maximum one house property (self-occupied or let-out) |
| Income from Other Sources | Interest income, family pension |
| Agricultural Income | Up to ₹5,000 |

**ITR-1 CANNOT be used if:**

- Total income exceeds ₹50 lakh
- There is income from business or profession
- Capital gains exist (short-term or long-term)
- More than one house property
- Foreign income or foreign assets are held
- Income from lottery, horse races, or speculative activity
- The individual is a Director in a company
- Unlisted equity shares are held
- TDS deducted under Section 194N (cash withdrawal above threshold)
- The individual is a Non-Resident (NR) or Resident but Not Ordinarily Resident (RNOR)

## Key features

- Pre-filled return: Income Tax portal pre-fills salary (from Form 16), TDS (from Form 26AS), interest income (from AIS/TIS), and deductions, reducing data entry effort
- Old vs. new tax regime choice: Salaried individuals must choose between the Old Tax Regime (with deductions) and the New Tax Regime (lower rates, no most deductions) when filing ITR-1
- Single online form: Available in online mode directly on incometax.gov.in, no software download needed
- JSON upload option: Also available as offline JSON file for those preferring offline preparation
- Instant processing: Simple ITR-1 filings are often processed within a few hours to a few days, refunds credited directly to pre-validated bank accounts
- EVC verification: Can be verified through Aadhaar OTP, net banking EVC, or bank ATM EVC, physical ITR-V posting optional
- Section 80C to 80U deductions: Under the old regime, all standard deductions (80C, 80D, 80CCD, HRA, LTA, standard deduction of ₹50,000) can be claimed in ITR-1

## Benefits

- **Simplicity:** The form's straightforward structure makes it accessible to most salaried taxpayers without professional assistance, though professional review improves accuracy
- **Fast refunds:** Simple ITR-1 returns are processed quickly, refunds are often credited within 7–30 days of filing and verification
- **Pre-filling reduces errors:** Auto-populated data from employer and bank records reduces manual entry errors
- **Free filing option:** The Income Tax portal provides free online filing of ITR-1, no cost to file independently
- **Compliance record:** Annual ITR-1 filing creates an income proof document widely used for home loans, vehicle loans, visa applications, and insurance policies
- **Regime optimisation:** Comparing old vs. new regime during ITR-1 preparation helps identify the tax-efficient choice for each financial year

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form 16 (Part A and Part B) | From employer, salary breakdown and TDS deducted |
| Form 26AS | Tax Credit Statement, all TDS, advance tax, refunds |
| Annual Information Statement (AIS) | Comprehensive income information from ITD |
| Bank statements (all accounts) | For interest income verification |
| Savings account interest certificates | From all banks, FD interest, savings interest |
| HRA rent receipts and rent agreement | If claiming HRA exemption under old regime |
| Home loan interest certificate | If claiming deduction under Section 24(b) for housing loan |
| LIC premium receipts | For Section 80C deduction |
| PPF / ELSS / NPS contribution proof | For Section 80C / 80CCD deductions |
| Health insurance premium receipt | For Section 80D deduction |
| Education loan interest certificate | For Section 80E deduction |
| Donation receipts | For Section 80G deductions |
| PAN and Aadhaar | Identity |
| Pre-validated bank account details | For refund credit |

## Process

1. **Gather documents** Collect Form 16 from employer, download Form 26AS and AIS from the IT portal, and compile bank statements and deduction proof documents.
2. **Log in to Income Tax portal** Log in to incometax.gov.in using PAN and password. Navigate to e-File → Income Tax Returns → File Income Tax Return.
3. **Select assessment year and ITR-1** Select the Assessment Year (e.g., AY 2025-26 for FY 2024-25). Select "ITR-1, Sahaj" as the applicable form.
4. **Review pre-filled data** Review all pre-filled sections, Personal Information, Gross Total Income (salary, house property, other sources), and TDS. Verify against Form 16 and AIS, correct any discrepancies.
5. **Choose tax regime** Select Old Tax Regime or New Tax Regime. Under old regime, enter all eligible deductions (80C, 80D, HRA, etc.). Under new regime, most deductions are not available, only standard deduction of ₹50,000 for salaried.
6. **Enter additional income** Add any income not pre-filled, savings account interest, FD interest, family pension, rental income from one house property.
7. **Verify tax computation** Review the auto-computed tax liability. Compare with TDS already deducted. If self-assessment tax is payable, pay via Challan 280 before filing.
8. **Submit ITR** Submit the return after review. The portal generates an acknowledgement (ITR-V).
9. **Verify return** Verify within 30 days, using Aadhaar OTP, net banking EVC, or by posting signed ITR-V to CPC Bengaluru. An unverified return is not treated as a filed return.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-1 self-filing (portal) | Free |
| ITR-1 filing assistance (CA/tax consultant) | ₹500 – ₹2,000 |
| ITR-1 with HRA / deduction optimisation | ₹1,500 – ₹4,000 |
| ITR-1 with regime comparison and advice | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 |

Government filing fee: Nil. Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Non-audit individuals (salaried) | July 31 of the Assessment Year |
| Belated return filing | December 31 of the Assessment Year |
| Revised return filing | December 31 of the Assessment Year |
| ITR-U (updated return) | Within 4 years of end of Assessment Year |

Late filing fee: ₹1,000 (if total income ≤ ₹5 lakh) or ₹5,000 (if income > ₹5 lakh) under Section 234F.

## Common errors

- Not reporting savings account interest: Interest on savings accounts (including the ₹10,000 exemption under Section 80TTA for non-seniors) must still be declared as income, many taxpayers omit this
- Wrong ITR form selection: Using ITR-1 when capital gains exist (even small amounts from mutual fund redemptions), requires ITR-2
- Incorrect HRA exemption computation: Manual HRA computation errors, using metro city limits for non-metro locations, using annual rent instead of monthly
- Not reconciling Form 26AS with Form 16: TDS claimed must match Form 26AS, discrepancies cause demand notices after processing
- Filing under old regime without opting correctly: Not explicitly selecting the old regime in the portal, in the absence of a proper selection, the new regime is applied by default in some cases

## Frequently asked questions

**I redeemed some mutual funds this year. Can I still use ITR-1?** No. Any capital gains, including from mutual fund redemptions, whether short-term or long-term, whether gain or loss, disqualify you from using ITR-1. You must use ITR-2 (for individuals and HUFs without business income) when capital gains are involved. Even a ₹500 long-term capital gain from a small SIP redemption technically requires ITR-2. Most mutual fund platforms provide capital gains statements in formats compatible with ITR-2 pre-fill, making the switch to ITR-2 less complex than it appears.

**My employer has two branches and I received two Form 16s for the year. Can I still use ITR-1?** Yes, receiving two Form 16s (from the same or different employers during different periods of the year) does not disqualify you from ITR-1, provided all other ITR-1 eligibility criteria are met (total income under ₹50 lakh, no capital gains, no business income, etc.). You must combine the salary income from both Form 16s and report the total in ITR-1. Be careful about TDS, the second employer may have applied the full basic exemption again, resulting in under-deduction. Compute the net tax liability on the combined income and pay any self-assessment tax before filing.

**I am a salaried employee but also earn ₹3,000 per month from YouTube content creation. Which ITR form should I use?** YouTube income (from Google's AdSense or brand deals) is typically treated as income from business/profession under the Income Tax Act, since it arises from a commercial activity, even if small. Income from business or profession disqualifies ITR-1. You should file ITR-3 (for individuals with business/profession income alongside salary) or ITR-4 if you opt for presumptive taxation under Section 44ADA (for professionals) or Section 44AD (for business income up to ₹2 crore turnover). The appropriate form depends on the quantum of YouTube income and whether you want to maintain regular books of accounts.

**I have a home loan on my self-occupied property. How do I claim this deduction in ITR-1?** In ITR-1, under the "Income from House Property" section, select "Self-Occupied." The annual value of a self-occupied property is NIL, so no rental income is added. However, you can claim a deduction for interest paid on the home loan under Section 24(b), up to ₹2 lakh per year for a self-occupied property (under the old tax regime). Enter the actual interest paid (from the bank's interest certificate for the financial year) subject to the ₹2 lakh limit. Note: home loan interest deduction is NOT available under the new tax regime (except under Section 24(b) for let-out property).

**I missed the July 31 deadline for ITR-1 filing. What should I do now?** File a Belated Return under Section 139(4), available until December 31 of the Assessment Year. A belated ITR-1 attracts: (a) a late filing fee of ₹1,000 (income ≤ ₹5 lakh) or ₹5,000 (income > ₹5 lakh) under Section 234F; (b) interest under Section 234A at 1% per month on the unpaid tax from July 31 to the date of filing; and (c) inability to carry forward certain losses (like house property loss). After December 31, you can only file ITR-U (updated return), which attracts additional tax of 25%–70%. File immediately to minimise interest accumulation.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: ITR‑2 Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑2 filing for individuals and HUFs not having business/professional income.
services.content:
# ITR-2 form filing

## Overview

ITR-2 is the Income Tax Return form prescribed for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) who have income from multiple sources, particularly salary, house property, and capital gains, but do not have income from business or profession. It is more comprehensive than ITR-1 (Sahaj) and is designed to capture the complexity of investors, high-income salaried employees, NRIs, and individuals with foreign income or assets.

ITR-2 is the most commonly misunderstood form, many taxpayers who file ITR-1 should actually be filing ITR-2 (because they have mutual fund capital gains, equity trading profits, rental income from multiple properties, or foreign assets). Using an incorrect form can result in income tax notices, defective return classification, and potential scrutiny.

The form captures all income heads except business/profession, making it ideal for salaried professionals who also invest actively in equities, mutual funds, and real estate.

## Applicability

**ITR-2 is applicable for:**

| Income source | ITR-2 applicable |
| --- | --- |
| Salary / Pension | ✓ |
| Income from house property (one or more) | ✓ |
| Capital gains (short-term and long-term) | ✓ |
| Income from other sources (interest, dividends, winnings) | ✓ |
| Foreign income or foreign assets | ✓ |
| NRI / RNOR tax returns | ✓ |
| Director in a company (without business income) | ✓ |
| Holder of unlisted shares | ✓ |
| Total income above ₹50 lakh | ✓ |
| Agricultural income above ₹5,000 | ✓ |

**ITR-2 CANNOT be used if:**

- There is income from business or profession (even a minor freelance/consulting income), use ITR-3
- The individual opts for presumptive taxation, use ITR-4

## Key features

- Capital gains schedules: Detailed Schedule CG captures STCG (Short-Term Capital Gains) and LTCG (Long-Term Capital Gains), equity shares, mutual funds, real estate, debt instruments, separately classified
- Schedule AL (Assets and Liabilities): Mandatory for individuals with income exceeding ₹50 lakh, captures movable and immovable assets, investments, and liabilities
- Schedule FSI (Foreign Source Income): For individuals with foreign income, salary abroad, foreign dividends, foreign interest, foreign rental income
- Schedule TR (Tax Relief): For claiming DTAA relief on doubly-taxed foreign income
- Schedule FA (Foreign Assets): Mandatory disclosure of foreign bank accounts, foreign immovable property, foreign financial interests
- Schedule TPSA: Transfer pricing disclosure for specified domestic transactions
- Multiple house properties: Unlike ITR-1, ITR-2 captures income from multiple house properties, including deemed let-out annual value computation for the second property
- NRI-specific fields: Non-resident individuals use ITR-2 with specific schedules for FEMA residency status, repatriation status, and NRI-specific deductions

## Benefits

- **Captures full income complexity:** One form covers salary, multiple properties, investments, capital gains, and foreign income, no need for multiple forms
- **LTCG set-off:** Capital loss carry-forward and set-off opportunities are captured in ITR-2, reducing future tax liability
- **NRI compliance:** Provides the complete framework for NRI tax compliance, foreign income disclosure, DTAA relief, and repatriation documentation
- **Foreign asset compliance:** Schedule FA in ITR-2 ensures compliance with the Black Money Act, mandatory disclosure of foreign assets; non-disclosure can attract penalty of ₹10 lakh per foreign asset
- **Loan eligibility:** ITR-2 with capital gains and investment disclosures strengthens loan applications, particularly for high-value home loans and business loans
- **Regime optimisation:** For high-income individuals, ITR-2 allows comparison of old vs. new tax regime, with capital gains and multiple deductions in play, the optimal regime choice significantly impacts net tax outflow

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form 16 | From employer(s) |
| Capital gain statements | From brokers (Zerodha, ICICI Direct, etc.) and mutual fund houses |
| Form 64B | For mutual fund dividend income |
| Property details | For house property income, rental agreements, municipal tax receipts |
| Home loan interest certificate | For all properties with loans |
| Foreign income documents | Employer letter, foreign bank statements |
| Foreign bank account details | Account number, SWIFT, country, opening/closing balance |
| Passport | For NRIs, for residency status determination |
| TRC and Form 10F | For DTAA relief claims |
| Form 26AS and AIS | Tax credit and information statement |
| Asset details | For Schedule AL, property, investments, vehicles, jewellery |

## Process

1. **Determine capital gains** Download capital gains statements from all brokers and mutual fund platforms. Classify as STCG/LTCG and by asset type (equity, debt, real estate).
2. **Compile all income sources** Gather salary from Form 16, house property income (rental income minus municipal taxes minus 30% standard deduction minus loan interest), and other sources income.
3. **Compute foreign income and DTAA relief** If foreign income exists, compute tax payable in India and the DTAA relief available to avoid double taxation.
4. **Prepare Schedule FA** List all foreign assets, bank accounts, immovable property, investments, with acquisition cost, opening and closing values.
5. **File ITR-2 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in. Select ITR-2. Review pre-filled data and enter all additional information, capital gains schedules, house property details, foreign income, and assets. Select tax regime.
6. **Tax computation and payment** Review the computed tax, surcharge, and cess. Pay any balance self-assessment tax via Challan 280.
7. **Verify and submit** Submit and verify using Aadhaar OTP or EVC within 30 days.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-2, salary + simple capital gains | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 |
| ITR-2, multiple properties + capital gains | ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 |
| ITR-2, NRI with foreign income/assets | ₹10,000 – ₹30,000 |
| ITR-2, high income + Schedule AL | ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 |
| ITR-2, complex (foreign assets + DTAA) | ₹15,000 – ₹50,000 |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Non-audit individuals | July 31 of the Assessment Year |
| Belated return | December 31 of the Assessment Year |
| Revised return | December 31 of the Assessment Year |

Late filing fee: ₹5,000 (income > ₹5 lakh) under Section 234F.

## Common errors

- Using ITR-1 instead of ITR-2 when capital gains exist: One of the most common errors, even ₹1 of capital gain from mutual fund redemption requires ITR-2
- Not disclosing all foreign assets in Schedule FA: Under-disclosure in Schedule FA can attract ₹10 lakh penalty per undisclosed asset under the Black Money Act, this is a critical compliance requirement
- Incorrect LTCG computation for equity: Not applying the grandfathering provision for equity LTCG (cost of acquisition = higher of actual cost or market value as on January 31, 2018), leads to over-computation of LTCG
- Missing interest on tax-free bonds in Schedule OS: Interest on tax-free bonds is exempt but must still be disclosed, omission creates AIS mismatch notices
- Not filing Schedule AL when income exceeds ₹50 lakh: Mandatory asset and liability disclosure often omitted, leading to defective return notices

## Frequently asked questions

**I am an NRI living in the US with Indian rental income. Which ITR should I file and do I need to declare my US income in India?** As an NRI, your India taxable income includes only income sourced in India, Indian rental income, Indian capital gains, Indian bank interest (subject to NRI-specific tax rates). Your US income is not taxable in India. File ITR-2 for NRI filers (select NRI residency status in the personal details section). Disclose Indian rental income, compute tax after applicable deductions (30% standard deduction, municipal taxes, loan interest), and claim TDS credit. You must disclose foreign assets (US bank accounts, US property, US investments) in Schedule FA even as an NRI, though this is for information purposes and these assets are not taxed in India.

**I sold my house for ₹80 lakh after owning it for 6 years. Which ITR form do I use and how is the capital gain computed?** Use ITR-2, long-term capital gain on immovable property is reported in Schedule CG of ITR-2. LTCG on property held for more than 2 years is taxed at 20% with indexation benefit (or 12.5% without indexation as per the Finance Act 2024 amendments, choose the more beneficial option). Compute indexed cost of acquisition using the Cost Inflation Index (CII) for the purchase year and sale year. If the LTCG is reinvested in a new residential property within 2 years (or construction within 3 years), the gain is exempt under Section 54. Alternatively, invest in Capital Gains Bonds under Section 54EC within 6 months for exemption up to ₹50 lakh.

**I have both stock trading profits (F&O) and salary income. Can I use ITR-2?** No. F&O (Futures and Options) trading income is classified as non-speculative business income under the Income Tax Act, regardless of whether the trading is occasional or frequent. Business income disqualifies you from ITR-2. You must file ITR-3 (for individuals/HUFs with business/profession income). If your F&O turnover is within the presumptive taxation limits, you could potentially use ITR-4, but F&O under presumptive taxation requires turnover to be calculated correctly (absolute sum of profits and losses on all F&O contracts). Misreporting F&O as capital gains in ITR-2 is a common error that attracts scrutiny.

**How does the grandfathering provision work for Long-Term Capital Gains on equity shares and equity mutual funds?** LTCG on equity shares and equity-oriented mutual funds held for more than 12 months, in excess of ₹1 lakh per year, is taxed at 10% (12.5% from AY 2024-25 as per Finance Act 2024 amendments). The grandfathering provision applies to assets purchased before February 1, 2018, the cost of acquisition for these assets is deemed to be the HIGHER of: (a) actual purchase price; or (b) Fair Market Value (FMV) as on January 31, 2018. This effectively exempts gains accrued before February 1, 2018. Your broker's capital gains statement typically accounts for this grandfathering automatically, verify the statement before entering data in Schedule CG of ITR-2.

**My spouse and I jointly own 3 residential properties. We have no business income. Which form do we each file and how is rental income from the joint property split?** Both you and your spouse, being individuals without business income (assuming no other disqualifying factors), would file ITR-2 (if you have capital gains or multiple properties) or ITR-1 (only if you meet all ITR-1 criteria, which seems unlikely with 3 properties). Rental income from a jointly owned property is split between co-owners in proportion to their ownership share (typically 50:50 for spouses unless specified otherwise in the property documents). Each co-owner declares their share of rental income in their individual ITR. The 30% standard deduction, municipal taxes, and loan interest are also split in the same ownership proportion. If there is no rental agreement and both are co-owners, the income is generally split equally.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.
