services.name: ITR‑3 Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑3 filing for individuals and HUFs having business or professional income.
services.content:
# ITR-3 form filing

## Overview

ITR-3 is the Income Tax Return form for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) who have income from business or profession, in addition to any other income they may have (salary, house property, capital gains, other sources). It is the most comprehensive ITR form for individuals, capturing the full complexity of a business-running individual's tax position.

ITR-3 is applicable for freelancers, consultants, doctors, lawyers, architects, chartered accountants, stock traders with F&O positions, YouTubers, small business owners, and any individual who earns income from a business or profession alongside (or instead of) a salary. Unlike ITR-4 (which uses a simplified presumptive taxation system), ITR-3 requires the taxpayer to maintain regular books of accounts under Section 44AA and report detailed profit and loss, balance sheet, and trading account data.

The form has expanded significantly over recent years, capturing trading account data, partner's income from firms, speculative and non-speculative business income, and complete financial statements, making it effectively a mini-audit-quality return for individual business income earners.

## Applicability

| Income source | ITR-3 |
| --- | --- |
| Income from business (regular, speculative) | ✓ |
| Income from profession (doctor, lawyer, CA, architect, engineer) | ✓ |
| F&O (Futures and Options) trading income | ✓ |
| Intraday equity trading (speculative business income) | ✓ |
| Partner's income from a firm (share of profit) | ✓ |
| Any business/profession income PLUS salary | ✓ |
| Any business income PLUS capital gains | ✓ |
| Proprietary business with turnover above presumptive limit | ✓ |
| Professionals with gross receipts above ₹50 lakh (above 44ADA limit) | ✓ |

**ITR-3 vs. ITR-4:** ITR-4 (Sugam) uses presumptive taxation under Sections 44AD/44ADA/44AE. If total turnover from business exceeds ₹2 crore (44AD) or gross professional receipts exceed ₹50 lakh (44ADA), or if the taxpayer opts out of presumptive taxation (even below the threshold), ITR-3 must be used with regular books of accounts.

## Key features

- Part A, General Information and P&L: Trading Account, Manufacturing Account, and Profit & Loss Account, for businesses maintaining books
- Balance sheet disclosure: Complete balance sheet of the proprietor's business as on March 31, assets, liabilities, capital, loans
- Quantitative details: For manufacturing businesses, quantity of goods produced, purchased, and sold
- Schedule BP (Business/Profession): Detailed schedule capturing taxable business income, disallowances, and adjustments
- Schedule CYLA, BFLA, CFL: Current year loss adjustment, brought forward loss adjustment, and loss carry-forward schedules
- Speculative and non-speculative income separation: F&O income (non-speculative) and intraday trading (speculative) are reported separately with different loss set-off rules
- Partner's income schedule: For individuals who are partners in a firm, share of profit, remuneration, interest from firm
- Tax audit report reference: If tax audit under Section 44AB is applicable, the audit report number and CA details must be reported in ITR-3
- Complete set-off of losses: Business losses from one source can be set off against income from other business sources (with restrictions for speculative losses)

## Benefits

- **Complete loss capture and carry-forward:** Businesses with losses can carry forward non-speculative business losses for 8 years, reducing future tax liability, ITR-3 is the mechanism to preserve and utilise these losses
- **Tax audit alignment:** For businesses under tax audit, ITR-3 directly references the audit report, ensuring consistency between the return and the audit findings
- **F&O tax compliance:** F&O trading losses (non-speculative) can be set off against salary, house property, and other income in the same year (with certain restrictions), ITR-3 is the correct form to capture this set-off
- **Professional income flexibility:** Doctors, lawyers, CAs, and other professionals not opting for presumptive taxation can claim all actual expenses (depreciation, rent, salaries, subscriptions) against their professional income in ITR-3
- **Partner's exempt income:** Share of profit from a partnership firm is exempt from tax in the hands of the partner, ITR-3 correctly captures this as exempt income, with remuneration and interest from the firm as taxable income

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Profit and Loss statement | For the financial year, business/professional income |
| Balance Sheet | As on March 31 |
| Books of accounts | Cash book, ledger, day book (for tax audit cases) |
| Form 26AS and AIS | Tax credit statement |
| Form 16 / 16A | If salary income also exists |
| Capital gains statements | From brokers, F&O, equity trading |
| Partner's capital account (if any) | From firm's books |
| Tax audit report (Form 3CA/3CB + 3CD) | If turnover triggers mandatory tax audit |
| Depreciation workings | Asset register, depreciation schedule |
| GST returns | For reconciliation of business turnover |
| Bank statements (business account) | For turnover and expense verification |
| TDS challans | For TDS deducted on business payments |

## Process

1. **Prepare financial statements** Compile the P&L account and Balance Sheet for the financial year. Ensure GST turnover reconciles with income tax turnover. Compute depreciation as per Income Tax Act (not Companies Act) rates.
2. **Tax audit (if applicable)** If turnover from business exceeds ₹1 crore (or ₹10 crore for digital transactions) or professional receipts exceed ₹50 lakh, get tax audit done under Section 44AB. The auditor files Form 3CA/3CB and 3CD on the Income Tax portal.
3. **Compute business income** Compute net business income, net profit from P&L account adjusted for disallowances (Section 40A, 43B), additions (deemed income), depreciation adjustments.
4. **Compile all income heads** Add salary (from Form 16), house property, capital gains (from broker statements), and other income to business income for total income computation.
5. **Loss set-off** Apply loss set-off rules, business loss against other business income, speculative loss only against speculative income, capital loss only against capital gains.
6. **File ITR-3 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in. Select ITR-3. Fill all schedules, BP (business income), P&L, Balance Sheet, Capital Gains, other income, deductions, and tax computation.
7. **Pay self-assessment tax** Pay any balance tax via Challan 280. Enter challan details in the return.
8. **Submit and verify** Submit and verify using DSC (mandatory for companies and LLPs; optional but recommended for individuals under audit) or Aadhaar OTP / EVC.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-3, freelancer/consultant (simple) | ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 |
| ITR-3, F&O trader + salary | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 |
| ITR-3, proprietary business with P&L | ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 |
| ITR-3, with tax audit (Section 44AB) | ₹20,000 – ₹75,000 |
| ITR-3, professional + multiple income sources | ₹10,000 – ₹30,000 |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%. Tax audit fees are typically separate from return filing fees.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Non-audit individuals (business income) | July 31 |
| Individuals under tax audit (Section 44AB) | October 31 |
| Revised return | December 31 |

## Common errors

- Using ITR-4 (presumptive) when books are maintained: Once regular books of accounts are maintained, the taxpayer cannot use presumptive taxation in ITR-4, must use ITR-3
- Not separating speculative and non-speculative income: Combining intraday trading (speculative) with F&O (non-speculative) in the same schedule, incorrect loss set-off rules result
- Missing tax audit requirement: Not getting tax audit done when turnover exceeds the Section 44AB threshold, attracts penalty of 0.5% of turnover (max ₹1.5 lakh) under Section 271B
- Not reconciling GST turnover with IT turnover: Differences between GSTR-3B turnover and ITR-3 business income trigger income tax scrutiny, document and explain all differences
- Depreciation rate errors: Using Companies Act depreciation rates instead of Income Tax Act rates (Schedule II vs. Appendix I of Income Tax Rules), common for small business owners

## Frequently asked questions

**I am a doctor with a private clinic and also a salaried employee at a hospital. Which ITR form should I file?** You should file ITR-3, combining your hospital salary (reportable under "Income from Salary") and your private clinic income (reportable under "Income from Business or Profession"). Since you have professional income from the clinic, ITR-1 and ITR-2 are both inapplicable. If your gross professional receipts from the clinic are below ₹50 lakh and you want to opt for simplified filing, you can consider ITR-4 with presumptive taxation under Section 44ADA (presuming 50% of gross receipts as profit, with no need to maintain detailed books). However, if your actual expenses exceed 50% of receipts (making actual profit less than 50%), ITR-3 with regular books is more tax-efficient.

**My F&O trading resulted in a loss of ₹2 lakh this year. How do I report this and can I set it off against my salary?** F&O loss is classified as non-speculative business loss, it cannot be set off against salary income in the same year (salary is a separate head). However, it can be set off against other business/profession income in the same year if you have any. If the loss cannot be fully set off in the current year, it can be carried forward for 8 subsequent assessment years and set off against business income in those years. The F&O loss must be reported in Schedule CFL (Carry Forward Losses) in ITR-3 to preserve the carry-forward benefit. Missing this schedule means the loss is permanently forfeited.

**I run a small grocery store with annual sales of ₹40 lakh. Do I need to maintain books of accounts and file ITR-3?** For a business with turnover of ₹40 lakh, you have the option of presumptive taxation under Section 44AD in ITR-4, declaring 8% of turnover (6% for digital transactions) as profit without maintaining detailed books. This is simpler and suitable for small traders. Alternatively, if your actual profit is less than 8%, you can opt for ITR-3 with regular books, maintaining records and claiming lower actual profit. However, opting for ITR-3 below the presumptive threshold (₹2 crore for 44AD) triggers mandatory book-keeping and, if net income is below the presumptive rate, requires tax audit if income is below the exemption limit. Choose based on your actual profitability versus the presumptive rate.

**I am a partner in two partnerships, a law firm and a consultancy. How do I report this in ITR-3?** As a partner, your share of profit from each firm is exempt from income tax (since the firm pays tax on its profits). However, any salary, commission, or interest received from the firms as a partner is taxable as business/profession income in your hands. In ITR-3, report the exempt share of profit in Schedule EI (Exempt Income) for each firm separately. Report the taxable remuneration and interest from the firms in Schedule BP (Business/Profession). You will also need to fill Schedule Part B1, "Part B, Statements of partners of firms" for each partnership. Ensure the remuneration claimed matches the partnership deed provisions and the firm's computation of income.

**My turnover crossed ₹1 crore this year (mainly from e-commerce sales). Is a tax audit mandatory?** Under Section 44AB, a tax audit is mandatory if total sales/turnover/gross receipts from business exceed ₹1 crore in a financial year (with an exception, if more than 95% of transactions are digital, the threshold is ₹10 crore). For most e-commerce sellers receiving payments through digital channels (UPI, credit cards, netbanking), the higher ₹10 crore threshold may apply, verify the exact proportion of digital vs. cash transactions. If the ₹1 crore threshold applies and is breached, engage a CA for tax audit under Section 44AB, file Form 3CD (the audit report) before October 31, and file ITR-3 (also before October 31 for audit cases). Non-compliance with tax audit attracts a penalty of 0.5% of turnover (up to ₹1.5 lakh) under Section 271B.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: ITR‑4 (SUGAM) Filing
services.shortDescription: Presumptive taxation return filing for eligible small businesses and professionals.
services.content:
# ITR-4 form filing (Sugam)

## Overview

ITR-4, popularly known as Sugam (meaning "easy" in Hindi), is the simplified Income Tax Return form for individuals, HUFs (Hindu Undivided Families), and firms (other than LLPs) who have opted for the presumptive taxation scheme under Sections 44AD, 44ADA, or 44AE of the Income Tax Act. It is designed to reduce the compliance burden for small business owners, freelancers, and transport operators by allowing them to declare a percentage of turnover as profit, without maintaining detailed books of accounts.

The presumptive taxation scheme provides three tracks:

| Section | Applicable to | Presumptive income |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 44AD | Business persons with turnover up to ₹2 crore | 8% of turnover (6% for digital receipts) |
| 44ADA | Specified professionals (doctors, lawyers, CAs, architects, etc.) with gross receipts up to ₹50 lakh | 50% of gross receipts |
| 44AE | Goods transport operators with up to 10 vehicles | Fixed amount per vehicle per month |

ITR-4 is one of the most widely filed return forms in India, making it accessible to lakhs of small shopkeepers, traders, consultants, and professionals who otherwise would need to maintain extensive accounting records.

## Applicability

**ITR-4 (Sugam) is applicable for:**

Individuals, HUFs, and Partnership Firms (not LLP) with:

- Business income eligible for Section 44AD (turnover up to ₹2 crore)
- Professional income eligible for Section 44ADA (gross receipts up to ₹50 lakh)
- Income from goods transport vehicles under Section 44AE
- Additionally having income from salary/pension, one house property, and other sources (interest, family pension)
- Total income up to ₹50 lakh

**ITR-4 CANNOT be used if:**

- The person is a director in a company
- Unlisted equity shares are held
- Income from capital gains exists
- The person has foreign assets or foreign income
- Agricultural income exceeds ₹5,000
- Income from speculative business exists
- The person is an NRI or RNOR
- The taxpayer opts out of presumptive scheme (must use ITR-3 instead)

## Key features

- No books of accounts required: The prime benefit of ITR-4, taxpayers under presumptive scheme need not maintain day-to-day accounts, cash books, or ledgers
- Simplified income computation: Income is computed as a fixed percentage of turnover/receipts, no complex P&L preparation
- Pre-filled data: Bank interest, TDS, and other pre-filled data from AIS/TIS is auto-populated
- Five-year commitment: Taxpayers opting for 44AD must continue for 5 consecutive years; opting out in any year requires switching to ITR-3 and paying regular tax for the next 5 years
- Advance tax simplification: Under Section 44AD/44ADA, advance tax is payable in a single installment of 100% by March 15 (instead of four quarterly installments)
- Suitable for GST composition scheme dealers: Businesses under GST composition scheme (which also has turnover limits) typically align well with the presumptive taxation track in ITR-4

## Benefits

- **No tax audit:** Businesses under 44AD/44ADA are exempt from tax audit under Section 44AB as long as they declare income at or above the presumptive rate
- **Simplified compliance:** No need for professional accountants to maintain detailed books, reducing accounting costs significantly for small businesses
- **Time efficiency:** ITR-4 preparation takes a fraction of the time compared to ITR-3 with full financial statements
- **Lower effective tax:** For professionals with low actual expenses (where actual profit > 50% of receipts), 44ADA reduces the declared profit to exactly 50%, lowering the tax base
- **Digital transaction incentive:** 44AD offers a lower presumptive rate of 6% (vs. 8%) for digital receipts, incentivising cashless transactions
- **Advance tax simplicity:** Single-installment advance tax by March 15 is simpler to manage than four quarterly installments

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Business/profession turnover summary | Annual sales/receipts figure, from GST returns, bank statements |
| Bank statements | All business accounts, for turnover and income verification |
| Form 26AS and AIS | Tax credit and income information |
| Form 16 / 16A | If salary income also exists |
| GST returns (GSTR-3B) | For turnover reconciliation |
| Transport vehicle details (for 44AE) | Vehicle registration number, ownership period |
| Advance tax challans | If advance tax was paid during the year |
| PAN and Aadhaar | Identity |
| Deduction proof | 80C, 80D investments and premiums |

## Process

1. **Compute presumptive income** Calculate total business turnover or professional gross receipts. Apply the applicable percentage (8%/6% for 44AD; 50% for 44ADA; fixed per vehicle for 44AE) to arrive at presumptive income.
2. **Add other income** Add salary (from Form 16), house property income, and other sources income to the presumptive business income.
3. **Apply deductions** Under old regime: claim eligible deductions, 80C, 80D, 80CCD, etc. Note: business deductions are not separately claimed under presumptive scheme, the percentage already accounts for all expenses.
4. **File ITR-4 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in. Select ITR-4. Review pre-filled data. Enter presumptive income details, select the applicable section (44AD/44ADA/44AE), enter turnover, and the system computes presumptive income automatically.
5. **Pay self-assessment tax** Compute and pay any balance tax. Enter challan details.
6. **Submit and verify** Submit and verify using Aadhaar OTP or EVC.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-4, simple (single profession/business) | ₹1,500 – ₹4,000 |
| ITR-4, with salary + business | ₹2,500 – ₹6,000 |
| ITR-4, with multiple deductions | ₹3,000 – ₹7,000 |
| ITR-4, transport operator (44AE) | ₹3,000 – ₹6,000 |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-4 filers (non-audit) | July 31 of Assessment Year |
| Belated return | December 31 |
| Revised return | December 31 |

## Common errors

- Opting for ITR-4 when capital gains exist: Even ₹1 of capital gain disqualifies ITR-4, use ITR-3
- Turnover exceeding the limit: Filing ITR-4 with business turnover above ₹2 crore (44AD limit) or professional receipts above ₹50 lakh (44ADA), invalid filing; ITR-3 is required
- Declaring income below presumptive rate without audit: Declaring business income below 8%/6% (44AD) or 50% (44ADA) without getting a tax audit done, attracts penalty under Section 271B
- Not maintaining the 5-year continuity for 44AD: Opting out of 44AD before completing 5 years, triggers loss of presumptive benefits and mandatory tax audit for the following years

## Frequently asked questions

**I am a freelance graphic designer earning ₹18 lakh annually. Can I use ITR-4?** Graphic design is not specifically listed among the eligible professions under Section 44ADA (which covers medicine, law, engineering, architecture, accountancy, technical consultancy, interior decoration, and other notified professions). The CBDT has expanded the list of eligible professions under 44ADA over time, verify whether graphic design/creative services fall under "other notified professions." If your profession qualifies under 44ADA, you can file ITR-4 declaring 50% of ₹18 lakh (₹9 lakh) as income. If not qualified, file ITR-3 with actual P&L. Many digital professionals (content creators, designers, developers) face this ambiguity, consulting a CA is advisable.

**My shop's annual sales are ₹95 lakh. Can I use ITR-4 under Section 44AD and declare 8% as income (₹7.6 lakh) even if my actual profit is ₹15 lakh?** Technically, yes, under Section 44AD, declaring 8% of turnover as income is permissible regardless of actual profit. The section provides a "deemed" income computation, no obligation to prove actual accounts. If your actual profit is ₹15 lakh but you declare ₹7.6 lakh (8% of ₹95 lakh) under 44AD, the difference is your benefit of the presumptive scheme, you pay tax only on ₹7.6 lakh. This is legally valid. However, if the Income Tax department has reason to believe your actual profit is significantly higher and you have suppressed income (not just using the presumptive scheme), there is a risk of scrutiny. Using the presumptive scheme appropriately, with legitimate business activity at a reasonable profit margin, is the intended use.

**Can a CA (Chartered Accountant) file under ITR-4 using Section 44ADA?** Yes, Chartered Accountants are specifically listed as eligible professionals under Section 44ADA. A CA with gross professional receipts up to ₹50 lakh can file ITR-4, declaring 50% of gross receipts as professional income, without maintaining detailed books. This is widely used by sole-practitioner CAs and small CA firms (where the firm itself is not an LLP, LLPs file ITR-5). Note that the 44ADA limit of ₹50 lakh applies to gross receipts, if you receive referral fees, consulting income, or any other professional income, all must be included in the ₹50 lakh computation.

**If I declare presumptive income under 44AD this year but want to switch to regular taxation next year (because I want to claim more expenses), can I?** Opting out of Section 44AD before completing 5 consecutive years has consequences. If you opt for 44AD for one year and then opt out (i.e., declare income below 8% of turnover) in any of the 5 subsequent years, you lose the right to claim presumptive taxation under Section 44AD for the following 5 years. During those 5 years, you must file ITR-3 with regular books and may require a tax audit if income falls below the basic exemption limit. This 5-year lock-in is a key consideration before initially opting for 44AD, plan your tax strategy carefully.

**I have both a small retail shop (turnover ₹45 lakh) and a professional practice as an engineer (gross receipts ₹18 lakh). Can I combine both in ITR-4?** This is an interesting dual-income scenario. Section 44AD applies to business income and Section 44ADA applies to professional income, they are separate provisions. In principle, you can claim both in a single ITR-4, reporting your shop's income under 44AD (6%/8% of ₹45 lakh) and your engineering fees under 44ADA (50% of ₹18 lakh). However, the total income (combined business + professional + other sources) must remain within the ₹50 lakh overall income limit for ITR-4 eligibility. Verify the aggregate income, if it exceeds ₹50 lakh, you must file ITR-3 instead.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: ITR‑5 Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑5 filing for firms, LLPs, AOPs, BOIs, co‑operative societies and certain other entities.
services.content:
# ITR-5 form filing

## Overview

ITR-5 is the Income Tax Return form prescribed for entities other than individuals, HUFs, and companies, specifically Partnership Firms, Limited Liability Partnerships (LLPs), Association of Persons (AOPs), Body of Individuals (BOIs), Artificial Juridical Persons, Cooperative Societies, Local Authorities, and registered societies. It is a comprehensive return form that captures the full income profile of these entities, business income, capital gains, house property income, and other sources, along with complete financial statements.

For partnership firms and LLPs, which represent a large proportion of India's professional services, manufacturing, and trading businesses, ITR-5 is the annual tax filing form that consolidates all income, deductions, TDS credits, and tax payments into a verified, legally filed return.

ITR-5 requires financial statements (P&L account and Balance Sheet), depreciation schedules, partner/member details, and profit sharing ratio information. For firms and LLPs with annual turnover above ₹1 crore (or professional receipts above ₹50 lakh), a tax audit under Section 44AB is also mandatory, and the audit report must be filed before the ITR-5 is submitted.

## Applicability

| Entity type | ITR-5 |
| --- | --- |
| Partnership firm | ✓ |
| Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) | ✓ |
| Association of Persons (AOP) | ✓ |
| Body of Individuals (BOI) | ✓ |
| Artificial Juridical Person | ✓ |
| Cooperative society | ✓ |
| Local authority | ✓ |
| Registered society (non-Section 12A) | ✓ |
| Estate of deceased person | ✓ |
| Estate of insolvent | ✓ |

**Not applicable for:** Individuals, HUFs, companies (use ITR-6), entities filing under Section 139(4A), charitable/religious trusts (use ITR-7).

## Key features

- Profit sharing schedule: Detailed capture of each partner's / member's name, PAN, profit share ratio, salary, and interest from the firm
- Separate books compliance: ITR-5 requires full P&L and Balance Sheet, trading account data is required for manufacturing/trading firms
- Tax audit integration: For audit cases, Form 3CA (for firms with statutory audit) or 3CB (for firms without statutory audit) and Form 3CD are filed by the auditing CA and referenced in ITR-5
- Carry-forward losses: Full set-off and carry-forward schedules, business loss, capital loss, unabsorbed depreciation
- TDS credit for firm: TDS deducted on firm's income (rent received, professional fees, etc.) is claimed as credit in the firm's ITR-5
- Advance tax reporting: Advance tax paid in four installments is reported with challan details
- Complete MAT applicability for LLPs: LLPs are not subject to MAT, important distinction from companies
- AMT (Alternate Minimum Tax): LLPs with adjusted total income above ₹20 lakh are subject to AMT at 18.5%, Form 29C (AMT computation) must be filed if AMT liability arises

## Benefits

- **Entity-level tax compliance:** ITR-5 ensures the firm/LLP meets its own tax obligations, separate from the partners' individual returns
- **Partner income legitimisation:** A properly filed ITR-5 establishes the firm's income and the partners' shares, essential for partners to correctly report their salary/interest in individual returns
- **Business credibility:** Firms with consistent ITR-5 filing history demonstrate financial accountability, important for bank credit, government contracts, and enterprise client onboarding
- **Loss carry-forward:** Firm-level business losses can be carried forward for 8 years, ITR-5 captures and preserves these loss carry-forwards
- **TDS refund:** Excess TDS on firm income can be claimed as a refund in the firm's ITR-5, improving the firm's working capital
- **Statutory audit alignment:** For firms requiring statutory audit (LLPs with turnover above ₹40 lakh or contribution above ₹25 lakh), the statutory audit and tax audit together provide a comprehensive financial compliance framework

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Profit and Loss account | For the financial year, firm's business income |
| Balance Sheet | As on March 31 |
| Partner's capital accounts | Individual partner-wise capital movement |
| Partnership deed / LLP agreement | Profit sharing ratio, salary, interest terms |
| Form 3CA/3CB + 3CD | Tax audit report, if audit applicable |
| Form 26AS and AIS | TDS credits on firm's income |
| GST returns (GSTR-9) | Turnover reconciliation |
| Bank statements | All firm bank accounts |
| Advance tax challans | Quarterly advance tax payments |
| Depreciation schedule | As per Income Tax Act rates |
| Capital gain statements | If firm has investment income |

## Process

1. **Prepare financial statements** Prepare firm's P&L account and Balance Sheet. Compute income under each head. Prepare depreciation schedule as per Income Tax Act rules.
2. **Tax audit (if applicable)** Engage a CA for tax audit if turnover exceeds ₹1 crore. File Form 3CA/3CB and 3CD on the Income Tax portal before October 31.
3. **Compute firm's total income** Business income (after audit adjustments), capital gains, house property income, and other sources income are totaled. Apply partner's salary and interest deduction under Section 40(b).
4. **File ITR-5 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in using the firm's PAN credentials. Select ITR-5. Fill all schedules, financial statements, partner details, capital gains, deductions, tax computation.
5. **Pay tax** Firms pay tax at 30% (plus surcharge and cess on applicable income). Pay any balance tax via Challan 280.
6. **Verify using DSC** ITR-5 must be verified using the DSC of any one partner/designated partner, Aadhaar OTP is not available for firm-level returns.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-5, small firm/LLP (simple) | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 |
| ITR-5, medium firm with tax audit | ₹20,000 – ₹60,000 |
| ITR-5, LLP with AMT computation | ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 |
| ITR-5, complex (capital gains + multiple activities) | ₹25,000 – ₹75,000 |

Tax audit fees are charged separately. Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Firms not requiring audit | July 31 |
| Firms requiring tax audit under 44AB | October 31 |
| Revised return | December 31 |

## Common errors

- Partners' salary exceeding Section 40(b) limits: Deducting partners' salary in excess of the limits prescribed under Section 40(b) of the Income Tax Act, leads to disallowance and additional tax
- Not filing ITR-5 even if the firm had no business activity: A dormant firm with a PAN is still required to file ITR-5 (NIL return), missing this triggers notice and penalty
- Incorrect partner PAN entry: Wrong PAN of any partner leads to incorrect TDS credit and potential notices to both the firm and the partner
- Not accounting for AMT on LLP: LLPs with significant exemptions (Section 10AA, etc.) may attract AMT at 18.5% on adjusted total income, missing AMT computation is a common oversight

## Frequently asked questions

**Our LLP had no income this year, it was dormant. Do we still need to file ITR-5?** Yes. Even a dormant LLP with nil income must file ITR-5 (as a NIL return). The LLP's PAN remains active, and the Income Tax department expects an annual return, non-filing is treated as failure to file, attracting late fee and potential notice. Filing a NIL ITR-5 takes minimal effort and keeps the LLP's compliance record clean. Similarly, the LLP must file its MCA Annual Return (Form 11 and Form 8) and maintain a bank account, all of which are obligations regardless of activity level.

**What is the Section 40(b) limit for partner's salary deduction in a firm's ITR-5?** Section 40(b) of the Income Tax Act prescribes the maximum salary payable to working partners that is deductible as a business expense in the firm's computation. The limits are: (a) on the first ₹3 lakh of book profit (or where book profit is a loss): ₹1.5 lakh or 90% of book profit, whichever is higher; (b) on the balance of book profit: 60% of the remaining book profit. "Book profit" is the net profit of the firm as per P&L, adjusted for specific additions and deductions. Salary to partners beyond these limits is disallowed and added back to the firm's income. The partnership deed must specifically authorise the salary, salary not authorised by the deed is entirely disallowed.

**Our LLP is required to file a statutory audit under the LLP Act. Does this satisfy the tax audit requirement under Section 44AB?** No. The statutory audit under the LLP Act (required by MCA for LLPs with turnover above ₹40 lakh or contribution above ₹25 lakh) and the tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act are separate requirements. The statutory audit examines financial statements for true and fair view; the tax audit examines income computation and tax compliance under the Income Tax Act. Both are typically conducted by the same CA but produce different reports, the statutory auditor's report (under LLP Act) and Form 3CA/3CD (under Income Tax Act). If turnover exceeds ₹1 crore, both must be conducted and filed separately.

**Can a firm claim House Rent Allowance (HRA) or personal deductions in ITR-5?** No. HRA, personal deductions under Chapter VIA (80C, 80D, etc.), and other personal allowances are individual-specific deductions under the Income Tax Act, they cannot be claimed by firms, LLPs, or other non-individual entities. A firm pays tax on its net income at the flat rate of 30% plus surcharge. The only deductions available to a firm are business deductions under Section 30–37 (rent, salaries, depreciation, etc.) and specific deductions like Section 80G (donations). Partners can claim personal deductions in their individual ITR-3 returns for their individual tax computations.

**We are converting our partnership firm to an LLP. What are the ITR filing implications during the conversion year?** In the conversion year: (a) the partnership firm must file ITR-5 for the period it operated as a partnership, from April 1 to the date of conversion to LLP; (b) the LLP must file a separate ITR-5 for the period after conversion, from the date of LLP incorporation to March 31. The conversion itself, if done under the LLP Act provisions (by registering the firm as an LLP with the MCA), is generally tax-neutral, no capital gains arise on the transfer of assets from the firm to the LLP (subject to continuity conditions). However, ensure the tax audit (if applicable) covers both periods and the PAN of the firm and LLP are separately managed.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: ITR‑6 Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑6 filing for companies not claiming exemption under section 11.
services.content:
# ITR-6 form filing

## Overview

ITR-6 is the Income Tax Return form prescribed for all companies registered in India under the Companies Act, 2013 (or the earlier Companies Act, 1956), except those companies claiming exemption under Section 11 (income of religious or charitable trusts), which file ITR-7. Every company incorporated in India, whether private limited, public limited, one person company, Section 8 company (unless Section 11 exemption is claimed), producer company, or Nidhi company, is mandatorily required to file ITR-6 every assessment year.

ITR-6 is the most comprehensive and data-intensive return form, capturing complete financial statements, detailed tax computation, Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) computation, transfer pricing data, international transactions, Schedule AL-2 (assets and liabilities), and multi-year depreciation schedules. For large companies with complex operations, ITR-6 preparation involves multiple teams, accounts, tax, legal, and statutory audit, working in coordination.

The filing of ITR-6 is invariably preceded by the statutory audit under the Companies Act, the tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act (mandatory for all companies regardless of turnover), and for larger companies, the transfer pricing audit under Section 92E.

ITR-6 must be filed using the authorised signatory's DSC, electronic verification is mandatory; physical ITR-V is not accepted for company returns.

## Applicability

| Entity type | ITR-6 |
| --- | --- |
| Private limited company | ✓ |
| Public limited company | ✓ |
| One Person Company (OPC) | ✓ |
| Section 8 company (without Section 11 exemption) | ✓ |
| Producer company | ✓ |
| Nidhi company | ✓ |
| Foreign company with Indian operations | ✓ (as resident company) |

**Not applicable for:** Individuals, HUFs, firms, LLPs, they use other forms. Companies claiming exemption under Section 11 (charitable/religious), they use ITR-7.

## Key features

- MAT computation: All companies (except eligible start-ups and some others) are subject to Minimum Alternate Tax at 15% of book profits under Section 115JB, ITR-6 captures the MAT computation and MAT credit carry-forward
- Transfer pricing schedule: Schedule for International Related Party Transactions and Specified Domestic Transactions, mandatory for companies with cross-border transactions above ₹1 crore per transaction
- Detailed depreciation schedule: Block-wise depreciation as per Income Tax Act rates, separate from the Companies Act depreciation used in financial statements
- Tax audit mandatory: Unlike other entities, ALL companies are required to undergo tax audit under Section 44AB regardless of turnover, Form 3CA and 3CD must be filed before ITR-6
- ESOP deduction: Companies providing ESOPs to employees can claim deduction at the time of exercise, captured in ITR-6
- Expenditure on scientific research: R&D expenditure deduction under Section 35, weighted deduction for approved R&D companies
- DSC mandatory: ITR-6 must be digitally signed, no physical or OTP-based verification accepted
- Comprehensive disclosure: Directors' details, PAN of directors, major transactions (loans, investments, guarantees) are disclosed
- Form 29B for MAT: A CA certificate in Form 29B certifying the book profit computation must be filed where MAT applies

## Benefits

- **Statutory compliance:** Companies are legally obligated to file ITR-6, non-filing attracts fees, penalties, and prosecution for directors under Section 276CC
- **MAT credit carry-forward:** Excess MAT paid over regular tax can be carried forward as MAT credit for 15 years, ITR-6 captures and preserves this credit
- **TDS refund processing:** Companies with excess TDS on income (rent received, interest income, professional fees) can claim refunds in ITR-6
- **Loss carry-forward for companies:** Business losses, capital losses, and unabsorbed depreciation are carried forward, for companies, unabsorbed depreciation can be carried forward indefinitely
- **80-IAC startup exemption:** DPIIT-recognised startups with IMB approval claim the Section 80-IAC income tax holiday in ITR-6, three consecutive years of 100% profit deduction
- **Transparent financials:** A well-filed ITR-6 aligned with audited accounts demonstrates financial transparency, essential for fundraising, DRHP filing, and M&A transactions

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Audited P&L account and Balance Sheet | Statutory audit completed financial statements |
| Tax audit report (Form 3CA + 3CD) | Filed by auditing CA on IT portal |
| Form 29B (if MAT applicable) | CA-certified MAT computation |
| Depreciation schedule (IT Act rates) | Block-wise depreciation workings |
| Transfer pricing report (Form 3CEB, if applicable) | For international/domestic related party transactions |
| Form 26AS | TDS credits on company income |
| Advance tax challans | All four quarters |
| TDS challans (tax deposited by company) | TDS deducted and deposited |
| Director details | PAN, DIN, Aadhaar of all directors |
| DSC of authorised director | For mandatory digital signing of ITR-6 |
| MAT credit schedule | Prior years' MAT credit carry-forward |
| Capital gain computation | Property, investments, shares sold |

## Process

1. **Complete statutory audit** Ensure the statutory audit under the Companies Act is completed and the auditor's report (including CARO) is signed off.
2. **Complete tax audit** Engage a CA for tax audit under Section 44AB. File Form 3CA and 3CD on the IT portal before October 31.
3. **Transfer pricing audit (if applicable)** For companies with international related party transactions above ₹1 crore, complete the transfer pricing audit. File Form 3CEB before November 30 (in transfer pricing cases, ITR-6 due date is November 30).
4. **Prepare tax computation** Compute company's total income, starting from net profit per books, adding back disallowed expenses, applying depreciation as per IT Act, claiming deductions (80C, 80G, 80-IAC for startups, etc.), computing MAT.
5. **File ITR-6 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in using company credentials. Select ITR-6. Fill all schedules, P&L, Balance Sheet, capital gains, deductions, MAT, taxes paid, and director details.
6. **Pay remaining tax** Pay any balance self-assessment tax via Challan 280. Enter challan details in the return.
7. **Sign with DSC and submit** Submit the return. Digitally sign using the authorised director's DSC. The return is instantly processed upon DSC-based submission.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-6, small company (turnover < ₹1 crore) | ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 |
| ITR-6, medium company (₹1–₹50 crore) | ₹25,000 – ₹75,000 |
| ITR-6, large company (₹50 crore+) | ₹75,000 – ₹3,00,000+ |
| ITR-6, startup with 80-IAC exemption | ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 |
| ITR-6, with transfer pricing | ₹50,000 – ₹2,00,000+ |
| Tax audit (Form 3CA/3CD) | ₹15,000 – ₹2,00,000+ |

Tax audit and transfer pricing fees are typically separate from ITR-6 preparation fees. Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Companies not requiring TP audit | October 31 |
| Companies with transfer pricing audit | November 30 |
| Revised return | December 31 |

## Common errors

- Not computing MAT correctly: Using book profit from the wrong version of financial statements (draft vs. audited), MAT must be computed on audited book profit
- Incorrect depreciation rate application: Using Companies Act depreciation rates in the tax computation instead of Income Tax Act rates, leads to incorrect taxable income
- Forgetting Section 43B disallowances: PF/ESI contributions, statutory dues, and other items payable but not paid by March 31 are disallowable under Section 43B, commonly missed
- Not referencing Form 3CA/3CD before filing: ITR-6 must reference the correct UDIN of the filed tax audit report, a common mismatch that creates processing errors
- Missing transfer pricing compliance: Not filing Form 3CEB when international related party transactions exceed the ₹1 crore threshold, attracts penalty of 2% of the transaction value

## Frequently asked questions

**Our startup has not made any profit in the last 3 years. Are we still required to file ITR-6?** Yes. Every company incorporated in India must file ITR-6 for every assessment year, regardless of whether it made a profit, a loss, or had no activity at all. A loss-making startup still has a filing obligation. In fact, filing ITR-6 for loss years is particularly important for carrying forward business losses and unabsorbed depreciation, which can be set off against future profits. Unabsorbed depreciation can be carried forward indefinitely for companies; business losses for 8 years. Non-filing deprives the company of these carry-forward benefits.

**Our company paid advance tax in all four quarters but our final tax liability is lower than what we paid. Can we claim a refund in ITR-6?** Yes. Excess advance tax paid over the final tax liability (after TDS credit) is refundable and can be claimed in ITR-6. The refund is credited to the company's pre-validated bank account linked to the PAN. Additionally, if TDS was deducted on the company's income (e.g., TDS on rental income, interest income, professional fees received) and the cumulative TDS exceeds the final tax liability, the entire excess is refundable. Income Tax refunds for companies are typically processed within 30–90 days of return processing.

**We are a private limited company with only one director/shareholder (OPC, One Person Company). Do we have the same filing requirements as a regular Pvt Ltd company?** Yes, an OPC (One Person Company) is a company under the Companies Act, 2013 and has identical income tax filing obligations as any other private limited company. ITR-6 with DSC-based verification is mandatory, tax audit under Section 44AB is mandatory, and all company-level tax obligations (MAT, advance tax, etc.) apply. The simplified nature of an OPC for corporate law purposes does not translate to simplified income tax compliance. The sole director's DSC is used for ITR-6 filing.

**Our company is a subsidiary of a US company and pays a management fee to the US parent. What are the transfer pricing and ITR-6 implications?** Payment of management fees to the US parent constitutes an international related party transaction, subject to India's Transfer Pricing regulations under Sections 92 to 92F of the Income Tax Act. The management fee must be at arm's length, documented in a Transfer Pricing study prepared by an accountant. A Transfer Pricing auditor must file Form 3CEB before November 30 certifying the arm's length nature of the transaction. ITR-6 must be filed by November 30 (the extended deadline for TP cases). The management fee is also subject to TDS under Section 195 (with 15CA/15CB compliance), making it a multi-regulatory transaction requiring coordinated compliance.

**Our company received a demand notice after ITR-6 was processed, saying there is a mismatch between TDS credit claimed and Form 26AS. How do we resolve this?** TDS mismatch notices are among the most common post-processing issues. Steps to resolve: (a) download Form 26AS and compare TDS credited against TDS claimed in ITR-6, identify the specific TDS entry causing the mismatch; (b) if TDS appears in ITR-6 but not in Form 26AS, it means the deductor (your client/tenant/bank) hasn't filed their TDS return or has used a wrong PAN, follow up with them to file a correction; (c) once the deductor files a correction and the Form 26AS updates, apply for rectification under Section 154 on the income tax portal to get the demand revised; (d) if the demand is otherwise incorrect, file a response under "Response to Outstanding Demand" on the portal, citing the correction application. Paying the demand without resolving the root cause leaves the excess TDS credit permanently lost.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: ITR‑7 Filing
services.shortDescription: ITR‑7 filing for entities required to file under sections 139(4A) to 139(4F).
services.content:
# ITR-7 form filing

## Overview

ITR-7 is the Income Tax Return form prescribed for entities required to file returns under specific provisions of the Income Tax Act, primarily persons including companies who are required to furnish a return under Sections 139(4A), 139(4B), 139(4C), or 139(4D). In practice, ITR-7 is filed by charitable and religious trusts, political parties, scientific research institutions, universities and educational institutions, news agencies, mutual funds, hospital trusts, and other specified entities that claim income exemption under Sections 11, 12, or 10 of the Income Tax Act.

**The most common ITR-7 filers are:**

| Entity type | Applicable section |
| --- | --- |
| Charitable / religious trusts | Section 139(4A), income exempt under Section 11/12 |
| Political parties | Section 139(4B) |
| Scientific research associations, news agencies | Section 139(4C) |
| Universities, colleges, educational institutions | Section 139(4C) |
| Mutual funds | Section 139(4D) |
| Medical institutions and hospitals (trust-based) | Section 139(4A) |
| Infrastructure debt funds | Section 139(4D) |

ITR-7 is distinct from ITR-5 (for firms/LLPs) and ITR-6 (for companies), it specifically applies to entities claiming exemption under the charitable/religious or specified institutional categories. The form captures detailed information about the trust's or institution's activities, income, application of income, accumulation, and compliance with conditions for exemption.

## Applicability

**ITR-7 is applicable for:**

- Trusts registered under Section 12AA or Section 12AB of the Income Tax Act (charitable/religious trusts)
- Institutions approved under Section 10(23C), universities, educational institutions, hospitals
- Political parties claiming exemption under Section 13A
- Scientific research associations approved under Section 35(1)(ii)/(iii)
- News agencies approved under Section 10(22B)
- Trade unions, employers' associations under Section 10(24)
- Notified institutions under Section 10(23A) to 10(23F)
- Mutual funds under Section 10(23D), through SEBI-registered AMCs
- Infrastructure debt funds under Section 10(47)
- Any other entity specifically required to file under Sections 139(4A) to 139(4D)

**Not applicable for:** Individuals, HUFs, firms, LLPs, or regular companies not claiming charitable/institutional exemption.

## Key features

- Schedule ER (Application of Income): Detailed schedule of how the trust/institution applied its income, towards charitable objects, accumulation under Section 11(2), religious activities
- Schedule AI (Accumulation of Income): For trusts that accumulate income under Section 11(2), requires investment in specified modes within the prescribed period
- Audit report under Section 12A/12AB: Trust registration and audit under Section 12A/12AB is a prerequisite, auditor's report in Form 10B or 10BB must be filed
- Foreign contribution reporting: Trusts receiving foreign contributions under FCRA must disclose such amounts separately
- Section 13 violations: If any part of trust income has been used for the benefit of specified persons (trustees, donors), Schedule 13 captures these violations which can lead to denial of exemption
- Anonymous donations: Anonymous donations above the prescribed threshold are taxable at 30%, captured in ITR-7
- Corpus donations: Corpus donations (to the permanent fund) are not income and are separately reported
- Section 80G certification: Trusts with 80G approval must disclose donations received, for donors' deduction compliance

## Benefits

- **Exemption preservation:** Correct ITR-7 filing with all required disclosures preserves the trust's income exemption under Section 11/12, incorrect or missing filings can lead to denial of exemption for the year
- **Donor deduction facilitation:** Properly filed ITR-7 with Section 80G certification details enables donors to claim deductions, critical for NGO fundraising
- **Regulatory transparency:** Detailed disclosures in ITR-7 (income, expenditure, beneficiaries, governance) demonstrate accountability to donors, government, and the public
- **Carry-forward of excess application:** If a charitable trust applies more than 85% of income in a year, the excess application can be carried forward as a set-off against future years' mandatory 85% application, ITR-7 preserves this benefit
- **Audit compliance:** ITR-7 requires Form 10B/10BB audit, ensuring annual professional review of the trust's activities and finances, strengthening governance

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Trust deed / instrument of creation | Constitutional document of the trust |
| Section 12A/12AB registration certificate | Mandatory for exemption claim |
| Section 80G certificate (if applicable) | For donors' deduction |
| Auditor's report in Form 10B or 10BB | Mandatory audit for trusts under 12A/12AB |
| Income and expenditure account | For the financial year |
| Balance sheet of the trust | As on March 31 |
| Donation receipt register | Donor-wise donations received |
| Application of income details | Object-wise expenditure during the year |
| Investment register | For corpus and accumulated income investments in specified modes |
| Form 10 filing (Section 11(2) accumulation) | If income is being accumulated beyond the year |
| Foreign contribution details | If FCRA registration exists |
| PAN of the trust | Trust PAN |

## Process

1. **Complete trust audit** Engage a CA to conduct the audit of the trust and file Form 10B (for trusts with income above ₹5 crore or with foreign contributions or accumulation under Section 11(2)) or Form 10BB (for others) on the Income Tax portal. The UDIN of the filed audit form is referenced in ITR-7.
2. **Compute trust's income** Compute income from all sources, voluntary contributions (excluding corpus), interest, rental income, business income (from incidental business). Compute 85% mandatory application requirement and verify actual application.
3. **Prepare application and accumulation schedules** Prepare detailed Schedule ER (income application) and Schedule AI (accumulation under Section 11(2), if any). Prepare Form 10 for accumulation if income is being set aside for future years.
4. **File ITR-7 online** Log in to incometax.gov.in using trust PAN credentials. Select ITR-7. Complete all schedules, trust details, registration details, income computation, application of income, investment details, and trustee information.
5. **Verify and submit** ITR-7 can be verified through DSC of the managing trustee or by Aadhaar OTP of the authorised signatory. Submit the return.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Estimated Professional Fee |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-7, small trust (income < ₹50 lakh) | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 |
| ITR-7, medium trust (₹50 lakh – ₹5 crore) | ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 |
| ITR-7, large trust (above ₹5 crore) | ₹40,000 – ₹1,50,000 |
| Form 10B / 10BB audit | ₹10,000 – ₹75,000 |
| Section 12AB registration support | ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Trusts requiring audit under Section 12A/12AB | October 31 |
| Non-audit charitable entities | July 31 |
| Revised return | December 31 |

## Common errors

- Not applying 85% of income: Failing to apply at least 85% of income towards charitable/religious objects in the year, the unapplied income becomes taxable at 30%
- Anonymous donation limit: Receiving anonymous donations above ₹1 lakh per donor or 5% of total donations (whichever is higher), the excess is taxable at 30%
- Section 13 violations: Using trust income for the benefit of trustees, founders, or their relatives, leads to complete denial of Section 11 exemption for the year
- Not filing Form 10B/10BB before ITR-7: ITR-7 requires the audit form UDIN, filing ITR-7 without completing the audit first makes the return defective

## Frequently asked questions

**Our NGO received ₹25 lakh in grants from a foreign foundation. Do we need to disclose this in ITR-7?** Yes, foreign contributions received by an NGO must be disclosed in ITR-7 under the foreign contribution schedule. Additionally, receiving foreign contributions without a valid FCRA registration is a violation of the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010, ensure FCRA registration is active before accepting foreign grants. The income tax treatment of FCRA grants depends on whether they are corpus donations (not income) or programme grants (income requiring 85% application). Proper documentation and characterisation of the grant is essential for correct ITR-7 reporting and FCRA annual returns.

**A charitable trust received ₹1.5 crore in donations but spent only ₹1 crore on charitable activities (67%). What are the tax implications?** The trust must apply at least 85% of its income (₹1.5 crore × 85% = ₹1.275 crore) towards its charitable objects. Since only ₹1 crore was applied (67%), the shortfall of ₹27.5 lakh (₹1.275 crore – ₹1 crore) is subject to tax at 30%. However, before paying tax, the trust has options: (a) set aside the shortfall as "accumulation" under Section 11(2), file Form 10 with the Assessing Officer, specifying the purpose and timeline for utilising the accumulated amount (maximum 5 years); (b) deposit the unapplied amount in a specified investment mode. If neither option is exercised, the ₹27.5 lakh shortfall is taxable. ITR-7 must correctly reflect this position.

**We are a Section 8 Company (non-profit company) under the Companies Act. Should we file ITR-6 or ITR-7?** The answer depends on whether the Section 8 Company has obtained Section 12A/12AB registration under the Income Tax Act. If yes, the Section 8 Company files ITR-7 (like any other registered charitable institution). If the Section 8 Company has NOT obtained Section 12A/12AB registration, it files ITR-6 (like any regular company) and pays tax on its net income at the normal corporate tax rate. Obtaining Section 12A/12AB registration is strongly advisable for all genuine non-profit Section 8 Companies, it provides income exemption and significantly reduces the tax burden.

**Our trust's Section 12AB registration was recently approved. Which year's ITR-7 do we file first?** Once Section 12AB registration is granted, the exemption is generally effective from the assessment year specified in the registration order. File ITR-7 for all years from the effective date of registration. For years before registration where ITR-5 or ITR-7 (without valid registration) may have been filed, the registration order may specify retroactive applicability (for new registrations, the exemption typically applies prospectively). Consult a CA on whether any prior-year returns need to be revised and whether the exemption can be availed for prior years based on the specific registration order language.

**Is a management committee member of a trust personally liable if the trust fails to file ITR-7?** The primary obligation for ITR-7 filing rests with the trust itself, but under Section 161 and related provisions of the Income Tax Act, the trustee(s) are representative assessees and are personally liable (in their capacity as trustees, not in their personal capacity) for the trust's tax dues and compliance obligations. The Income Tax department can proceed against the managing trustee for the trust's defaults, including non-filing penalties. In cases of willful non-filing or tax evasion by a trust, prosecution under Section 276CC (for willful failure to furnish return) can also be initiated against the responsible trustee(s). Maintaining a strict compliance culture within the trust, including annual ITR-7 filing, is therefore a governance imperative for trustees.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: TDS Return Filing
services.shortDescription: TDS return preparation and e‑filing for all quarters and sections.
services.content:
# TDS return filing

## Overview

TDS Return Filing is the quarterly statutory compliance requirement for all persons who have deducted Tax at Source (TDS) or collected Tax at Source (TCS) under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Every TAN-holder (Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number holder) who has deducted TDS from payments made during the quarter must file the applicable TDS return statement with the Income Tax department through the TRACES/TIN portal by the prescribed due date.

TDS Returns are statements that consolidate all TDS deductions made during the quarter, payment-wise, deductee-wise, along with the corresponding tax deposits made via Challan 281. These returns form the basis for the automatic generation of Form 26AS for each deductee, which deductees use to claim TDS credit against their tax liability when filing ITRs.

**Types of TDS return forms:**

| Form | Nature of TDS/TCS | Filed by |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Form 24Q | TDS on Salary (Section 192) | Employers |
| Form 26Q | TDS on non-salary payments (domestic) | All deductors |
| Form 27Q | TDS on payments to non-residents | Persons making foreign payments |
| Form 27EQ | TCS (Tax Collected at Source) returns | TCS collectors |

The TDS return ecosystem has been significantly streamlined through the TRACES portal (TDSCPC.gov.in) and the RPU (Return Preparation Utility) provided free by the Income Tax department, enabling accurate TDS return preparation and online submission.

## Applicability

**TDS return filing is mandatory for:**

- All employers who deduct TDS on employee salaries (Form 24Q)
- All businesses and individuals (under tax audit) who deduct TDS on contractor payments, professional fees, rent, interest, commission, etc. (Form 26Q)
- All persons making payments to non-residents, foreign payments subject to TDS under Section 195 (Form 27Q)
- E-commerce operators, sellers of specified goods, lottery operators, and others collecting TCS (Form 27EQ)
- Banks and NBFCs deducting TDS on interest payments
- Companies deducting TDS on dividends paid

**NIL TDS returns:** If a TAN-holder made no TDS deductions in a quarter but has previously filed returns, a NIL return must still be filed to maintain compliance status.

Applicable pan-India, all states and UTs; TDS return filing is a central compliance on the TRACES portal.

## Key features

- Quarterly filing: TDS returns are filed four times a year, Q1 (April-June), Q2 (July-September), Q3 (October-December), Q4 (January-March)
- Deductee-wise data: Each TDS return contains transaction-level data, deductee PAN, payment amount, TDS rate, TDS amount, and challan number for each payment
- RPU (Return Preparation Utility): Free software from NSDL for preparing TDS returns in the .fvu (file validation utility) format required for submission
- TRACES portal filing: Online submission of prepared TDS return files on the TRACES portal
- Digital signature requirement: Companies and government deductors must submit TDS returns using DSC; others can use a physical signature on the verification form
- Form 26AS auto-update: Filed TDS returns directly feed into deductees' Form 26AS, enabling them to claim TDS credit in their ITR
- Correction statements: TDS returns can be corrected for PAN errors, amount errors, or challan mismatches through correction statements on TRACES
- Centralised Processing Cell (CPC-TDS): TRACES processes TDS returns centrally, generating demand notices for short deduction, late filing, or mismatch
- Form 16 and 16A generation: After filing TDS returns, Form 16 (for salary TDS) and Form 16A (for non-salary TDS) certificates are generated on TRACES and downloaded for issuance to deductees

## Benefits

- **Deductee tax credit facilitation:** TDS returns are the mechanism through which deductees receive credit for TDS deducted, without a filed return, the TDS doesn't appear in the deductee's Form 26AS and cannot be claimed
- **Employer compliance:** Timely Form 24Q filing and Form 16 issuance are legal obligations of every employer, compliance demonstrates responsible payroll management
- **Avoid interest and penalties:** Late filing attracts ₹200/day late fee under Section 234E and penalties up to ₹1 lakh under Section 271H, timely filing eliminates these costs
- **Vendor relationship management:** Vendors and service providers receiving TDS-deducted payments need TDS certificates (Form 16A), timely return filing enables timely certificate issuance, strengthening business relationships
- **TRACES analytics:** TRACES provides deductors with analytics on TDS credit matching, defaults, and pending corrections, enabling proactive compliance management
- **Regulatory credibility:** Companies with consistent TDS return filing history demonstrate tax compliance credibility, important during audits, due diligence, and government tenders

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| TAN of deductor | For return identification |
| PAN of deductees | Correct PAN of all persons from whom TDS was deducted |
| Payment details | Nature of payment, payment date, amount, TDS rate |
| Challan details | BSR code, challan date, challan serial number, amount deposited |
| Challan 281 acknowledgements | For each TDS payment made during the quarter |
| Form 16 / 16A (previous) | For reference on prior deductions |
| Salary details (for Form 24Q) | Gross salary, exemptions, deductions for each employee |
| Form 12B from new employees | For employees joining mid-year, prior employer salary details |
| Non-resident payment details (for 27Q) | Country, nature of payment, DTAA provisions |

## Process

1. **Compile TDS data** Gather all payment data for the quarter, payments made to each deductee, TDS rate applied, TDS amount deducted, and the corresponding Challan 281 details (BSR code, date, serial number).
2. **Download and install RPU** Download the latest Return Preparation Utility (RPU) from the NSDL/TRACES website. Install on the computer. The RPU generates the .fvu file required for return submission.
3. **Prepare return in RPU** Open the RPU. Enter deductor details (TAN, PAN, name, address, financial year, quarter). Enter challan details (one row per challan). Enter deductee details (one row per deductee payment, PAN, name, payment amount, TDS rate, TDS amount). Validate the data, the RPU performs data validation.
4. **Generate FVU file** Run the FVU (File Validation Utility) on the prepared return. The FVU checks for errors, PAN validity, challan matching, rate correctness. Correct all errors. Generate the final .fvu file.
5. **Upload on TRACES portal** Log in to TRACES (tdscpc.gov.in) using TAN credentials. Navigate to Statements / Payments → Upload TDS. Upload the .fvu file along with the signed verification form (or DSC for electronic verification).
6. **Token number generation** TRACES generates a token number upon successful upload. Track the return processing status using this token.
7. **Return processing** TRACES processes the return, matching challans, validating PANs, and updating Form 26AS for all deductees. Any defaults (short deduction, PAN errors, challan mismatch) are flagged as demands.
8. **Generate and issue TDS certificates** After processing, log in to TRACES and download Form 16A for non-salary TDS or request Form 16 generation for salary TDS. Issue certificates to deductees within the prescribed deadline.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Government fee |
| --- | --- |
| TDS return filing | Nil |
| Late filing fee (Section 234E) | ₹200 per day per return |
| Penalty for non-filing (Section 271H) | ₹10,000 – ₹1,00,000 |

| Professional service | Estimated fee |
| --- | --- |
| TDS return, Form 24Q (salary, small org) | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000/quarter |
| TDS return, Form 26Q (non-salary, small) | ₹2,000 – ₹4,000/quarter |
| TDS return, Form 24Q + 26Q (medium org) | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000/quarter |
| TDS return, Form 27Q (non-resident) | ₹3,000 – ₹8,000/quarter |
| Form 16 / 16A generation and distribution | ₹1,000 – ₹5,000 |
| TDS correction statement | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 |
| Annual TDS compliance retainer | ₹15,000 – ₹1,00,000 p.a. |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Quarter | Period | TDS return due date |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Q1 | April – June | July 31 |
| Q2 | July – September | October 31 |
| Q3 | October – December | January 31 |
| Q4 | January – March | May 31 |

**TDS certificate issuance**

| Due date | |
| --- | --- |
| Form 16 (salary), annual | June 15 (15 days after Q4 return due) |
| Form 16A (non-salary), quarterly | 15 days after due date of each quarterly return |

## Common errors

- Incorrect deductee PAN: The most common and costly error, TDS returns with wrong PANs result in TDS credit not reflecting in deductees' Form 26AS, leading to complaints, demand notices, and correction statement obligations. Always verify PANs before filing
- Challan mismatch: Entering wrong BSR code, challan date, or serial number in the TDS return, causes challan mismatch defaults on TRACES. Cross-verify challan details from the bank's Challan 281 acknowledgement before filing
- Short deduction: Deducting TDS at a lower rate than prescribed, attracts short deduction demand (deductor must pay the shortfall with interest)
- Late deposit of TDS after deduction: Deducting TDS in March but depositing in May, interest under Section 201(1A) at 1.5% per month for delay in deposit
- Not filing correction for PAN errors: Knowing that a deductee's PAN in the original return was wrong but not filing a correction, the deductee continues to not see the TDS credit, potentially filing complaints with the IT department against the deductor

## Frequently asked questions

**We deducted TDS from a vendor payment but the vendor says they don't see the credit in Form 26AS. What could be wrong?** The most likely reasons: (a) the TDS return has not been filed yet for that quarter, the vendor's Form 26AS updates only after the return is filed; (b) the PAN used in the TDS return for this vendor is incorrect, TDS credit is mapped to PAN; a wrong PAN means credit goes to someone else's account; (c) there is a challan mismatch in the filed return, the system couldn't reconcile the deduction with the payment, holding up Form 26AS update. Check the TRACES portal for the TDS return's processing status and any defaults. File a correction statement if there is a PAN error or challan mismatch.

**We missed the TDS return due date by 10 days. What is the exact penalty and can it be waived?** Under Section 234E, a late fee of ₹200 per day for the duration of delay is levied, for 10 days, this is ₹2,000 per return. This is a mandatory late fee, not a penalty, and typically cannot be waived. For egregious delays (beyond one year) or willful non-filing, an additional penalty of ₹10,000 to ₹1 lakh under Section 271H can be levied by the AO, but this requires a formal proceeding. The ₹200/day late fee is automatically assessed when the return is filed late. Pay the late fee before filing the delayed return, the TRACES system will prompt you for the fee payment.

**One of our employees left mid-year and later joined another company. How do we handle TDS for the period they worked with us?** For an employee who left mid-year, your TDS obligation (Form 24Q) covers the period they were in your payroll, from April 1 (or joining date if mid-year) to the date of leaving. File Form 24Q for Q4 including their salary for the portion of the year they were with you. Issue Form 16 (Part A and Part B) covering only the period of employment with your company. The new employer will collect their Form 12B (declaring prior employer salary) and consolidate the total salary for the year to compute the correct TDS from their salary. The employee then files their ITR combining salary from both employers and claiming TDS deducted by each under their respective Form 16s.

**We have deducted TDS at 10% from a contractor payment, but the contractor provided a lower TDS certificate from the IT department (Section 197). Can we revise the deduction retrospectively?** Section 197 certificates allow deductors to deduct TDS at a lower rate as specified in the certificate, but only from the date the certificate is received. If TDS was already deducted at the standard rate (10%) before the certificate was received, that deduction stands. Going forward (after receiving the certificate), deduct at the reduced rate specified. The contractor can claim a refund of the excess TDS in their ITR, the excess appears in their Form 26AS as deducted, and their lower final tax liability results in a refund. There is no mechanism to retrospectively revise the TDS deducted before the Section 197 certificate was obtained.

**We are a small company with only 3 employees, all with annual salary below ₹3 lakh (below tax threshold). Do we still need to file Form 24Q?** If no employee's salary is taxable (i.e., all three have total income below the basic exemption limit under the regime they chose), no TDS needs to be deducted under Section 192, and Form 24Q can be a NIL return. However, if you have previously filed Form 24Q for earlier quarters where TDS was deducted (from higher-earning employees), continue filing NIL returns to maintain continuity. If you have never filed Form 24Q before and have no TDS obligation, you may not technically be required to file, but obtaining a TAN and being prepared to file if salaries increase is advisable. Note: Other TDS obligations (contractor payments, professional fees, rent) may independently require Form 26Q filings even if salary TDS is NIL.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.
