services.name: Incorporation in Malaysia
services.shortDescription: Company registration and local regulatory setup in Malaysia.
services.content:
# Company incorporation in Malaysia

## Overview

Malaysia is an increasingly popular destination for Indian entrepreneurs seeking a Southeast Asian base. With a majority English-speaking business environment, strong digital infrastructure, MSC Malaysia status benefits for tech companies, and competitive operating costs, Malaysia offers a compelling mid-tier option between Hong Kong/Singapore and lower-cost ASEAN markets. The primary incorporation structure for foreign investors is the Sendirian Berhad (Sdn Bhd), the equivalent of a private limited company.

Malaysia is also a popular destination for Indian IT firms, BPO/KPO services, and manufacturing operations targeting ASEAN markets, thanks to its ASEAN membership, bilateral trade ties with India, and Malaysia-India DTAA.

## Key benefits

- Competitive corporate tax rate: 24% (standard) with SME concessions
- Pioneer Status and Investment Tax Allowance (ITA) for qualifying tech and manufacturing sectors
- MSC Malaysia (Multimedia Super Corridor) status for digital/tech companies, significant tax incentives
- 100% foreign ownership allowed in most sectors (subject to equity guidelines in regulated industries)
- English widely used in business and government
- Strong ASEAN supply chain access, Thailand, Vietnam, Indonesia
- Lower operational costs than Singapore
- India-Malaysia DTAA and India-ASEAN FTA
- Labuan IBFC (International Business and Financial Centre), offshore business structure available

## Limitations

- Some sectors (retail, agriculture, certain services) require local Bumiputera (indigenous Malay) equity participation
- At least one locally resident director required
- Not as globally prestigious as Singapore for international investors
- Banking for non-resident Indian founders can be slow and documentation-heavy
- Bumiputera equity policies can limit 100% foreign control in certain sectors
- Company Secretary must be a Malaysian resident member of MAICSA or a licensed company secretary

## Comparison: Malaysia vs India (Incorporation)

| Parameter | Malaysia (Sdn Bhd) | India (Private Ltd) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Corporate Tax | 24% / 17% for SMEs (first MYR 600K) | 22% / 25% |
| Minimum Capital | MYR 1 | None |
| Local Director Required | Yes (at least 1) | No |
| Foreign Ownership | Up to 100% (most sectors) | 100% (most sectors) |
| Annual Audit | Mandatory | Mandatory (above threshold) |
| ASEAN Market Access | Yes | No |
| Time to Incorporate | 1–5 days | 7–15 days |
| GST / VAT | SST 6–10% (Sales and Service Tax) | GST 5%–28% |
| DTAA with India | Yes | - |
| Language | English / Malay | English |

## Documents required

For Indian founders incorporating a Sdn Bhd in Malaysia:

- Passport copy (certified true copy) of all shareholders and directors
- Residential address proof
- Email address and contact details of all directors and shareholders
- Proposed company name (3 options recommended)
- Company objectives / nature of business
- Registered office address in Malaysia
- Consent to Act as Director (Form 49 equivalent)
- For Indian corporate shareholder: apostilled Certificate of Incorporation, Board Resolution, MoA/AoA

## Step-by-step process

1. **Step 1 - Name Reservation:** Search and reserve company name on MyCoID portal (Companies Commission of Malaysia, SSM). Name availability confirmed in 1–2 days.
2. **Step 2 - Prepare Incorporation Documents:** Constitution (if adopting custom), director/shareholder consent forms, registered agent documents.
3. **Step 3 - File with SSM (Suruhanjaya Syarikat Malaysia):** Submit incorporation application online through MyCoID 2016. All directors must have a MyCoID account (foreigners can register online).
4. **Step 4 - Certificate of Incorporation:** Issued digitally. In Malaysia, the Certificate of Incorporation is now replaced by the Notice of Registration upon incorporation under the Companies Act 2016.
5. **Step 5 - Appoint Company Secretary:** Must be appointed within 30 days of incorporation. Must be a licensed company secretary under MAICSA.
6. **Step 6 - Open Bank Account:** Major banks include Maybank, CIMB, RHB, Public Bank, HSBC Malaysia.
7. **Step 7 - Tax Registration:** Register with LHDN (Inland Revenue Board) for Income Tax and IRBM for SST (if applicable).
8. **Step 8 - Work Passes & Employment:** Malaysia Employment Pass required for foreign directors/employees. MM2H (Malaysia My Second Home) visa for longer-term residency.

## Cost overview

| Item | Approximate Cost (INR) |
| --- | --- |
| SSM Incorporation Fee | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 |
| Registered Address (annual) | ₹30,000 – ₹80,000 |
| Company Secretary (annual) | ₹25,000 – ₹80,000 |
| Bank Account Setup | ₹0 – ₹20,000 |
| Annual Audit + Tax | ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000 |
| Total Year 1 | ₹1,20,000 – ₹3,50,000 |

## Tax implications for Indian founders

Malaysia's standard corporate tax is 24%. SMEs with paid-up capital ≤ MYR 2.5 million pay 17% on the first MYR 600,000 of chargeable income.

Pioneer Status (up to 10 years tax exemption) and Investment Tax Allowance are available for qualifying manufacturing and tech companies through MIDA (Malaysian Investment Development Authority).

MSC Malaysia status companies enjoy 10 years of full tax exemption on statutory income from qualifying MSC activities.

India-Malaysia DTAA: Dividends, 5% withholding (if ≥25% holding); Royalties, 10%.

Indian residents must report Malaysian company ownership under Schedule FA in ITR and comply with FEMA ODI regulations.

Labuan IBFC companies (offshore Malaysia) pay only 3% tax on net audited profits, an alternative for holding and trading structures.

## Typical timeline

| Stage | Time Required |
| --- | --- |
| Name reservation | 1–2 days |
| SSM incorporation | 1–3 days |
| Company Secretary appointment | 1–2 weeks |
| Bank account opening | 2–6 weeks |
| Tax registration | 1–2 weeks |
| Total (fully operational) | 4–8 weeks |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Is Malaysia's Sdn Bhd suitable for Indian IT companies expanding to ASEAN?** Yes. Malaysia is one of the most cost-effective and English-friendly bases for Indian IT companies expanding into ASEAN. MSC Malaysia status offers 10-year tax exemption and access to world-class tech infrastructure (Cyberjaya corridor). It also qualifies for Malaysia Digital Economy initiatives.

**Q2. What is Labuan IBFC and how is it different from a regular Malaysian company?** Labuan IBFC is Malaysia's offshore financial jurisdiction (similar in concept to Dubai's free zones). A Labuan company pays only 3% corporate tax on net audited profits for trading activities, or MYR 20,000 flat for non-trading (holding) activities. It is used primarily for international trading, holding, and financial services, not for conducting business within Malaysia.

**Q3. Can an Indian national be the sole shareholder of a Malaysian Sdn Bhd?** Yes. A foreigner (including Indian nationals) can be the 100% shareholder of a Malaysian Sdn Bhd in most sectors. However, certain sectors, retail, agriculture, construction, education, require local Malaysian equity participation. Always verify the sector's equity policy with the relevant ministry or MIDA before incorporating.

**Q4. What is the minimum number of directors required for a Malaysian Sdn Bhd?** At least one director who is ordinarily resident in Malaysia (i.e., has their principal place of residence in Malaysia). A foreign founder who is not resident in Malaysia must appoint a locally resident nominee director or establish their own residency through an Employment Pass or MM2H visa.

**Q5. Which Indian industries are most active in Malaysian incorporation?** IT and software services (Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai), gloves and rubber manufacturing (due to supply chains), palm oil and agri-commodity trading, BPO/KPO firms, and textile companies (Tamil Nadu, Gujarat) are the major Indian sectors with Malaysian entities.

## Note

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: Incorporation in UK
services.shortDescription: UK limited company incorporation with Companies House.
services.content:
# Company incorporation in UK

## Overview

The United Kingdom remains one of the most accessible and globally respected jurisdictions for company incorporation. A UK Private Limited Company (Ltd) can be registered in under 24 hours, has no minimum share capital, is fully accessible to non-residents, and carries immense brand credibility with global clients. Companies House (the UK's registrar) maintains a transparent public register that serves as a trust signal for Indian businesses serving UK and European clients.

Post-Brexit, the UK has accelerated its bilateral trade agenda, the India-UK Free Trade Agreement (FTA) is under negotiation and expected to significantly open market access for Indian services, IT, and pharmaceutical companies. The UK is also home to the world's largest Indian diaspora (~1.8 million), making it an ideal destination for Indian entrepreneurs.

## Key benefits

- Fastest incorporation globally, 24 hours or less, fully online
- No minimum share capital
- No requirement for UK-resident directors or shareholders
- High global credibility, UK Ltd is trusted by global clients and investors
- Potential FTA benefits under India-UK FTA (under negotiation)
- UK-India DTAA in place, efficient tax structure
- Straightforward banking with fintechs (Wise Business, Revolut Business, Monzo Business)
- No requirement for physical UK office to incorporate
- Access to UK SME finance, R&D tax credits (25% credit for SMEs)
- Strong IP protection under UK IP law

## Limitations

- Corporate Tax increased to 25% (April 2023) for profits above GBP 250,000
- Annual accounts, confirmation statements, and tax returns are publicly accessible
- VAT registration mandatory once turnover exceeds GBP 90,000, complex compliance
- Full HMRC tax and payroll compliance required if UK employees are hired
- Post-Brexit loss of EU market access, separate EU entity needed for EU operations
- UK banking for non-resident Indian directors can face KYC challenges (use fintech alternatives)

## Comparison: UK vs India (Incorporation)

| Parameter | UK (Private Ltd) | India (Private Ltd) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Corporate Tax | 19% (up to GBP 50K) / 25% (above GBP 250K) | 22% / 25% |
| Minimum Share Capital | None (GBP 1) | None |
| Resident Director Required | No | No |
| Annual Audit | Required above thresholds | Required above thresholds |
| Public Disclosure | High (Companies House public) | Moderate (MCA public) |
| VAT Threshold | GBP 90,000 | ₹20–40 lakh |
| Time to Incorporate | Same day to 24 hours | 7–15 days |
| R&D Tax Credits | Yes (25% for SMEs) | Yes (weighted deduction) |
| DTAA with India | Yes | - |
| FTA with India | Under negotiation | - |

## Documents required

For Indian nationals incorporating a UK Ltd:

- Valid passport (scan/copy, no notarisation required for online registration)
- Residential address (any country)
- Proposed company name
- Registered office address in the UK (can use agent address)
- Director and shareholder details (name, date of birth, nationality, service address)
- Service address for directors (UK address can be used)
- Share capital and shareholder structure
- Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code for business activity
- PSC (Person with Significant Control) declaration

## Step-by-step process

1. **Step 1 - Choose Company Name:** Check availability at Companies House online register. Must be unique and comply with naming rules.
2. **Step 2 - Register on Companies House Portal:** File online via Companies House WebFiling or through a registered agent.
3. **Step 3 - Submit Form IN01:** This is the application for incorporation, includes director details, shareholder structure, share capital, and registered office.
4. **Step 4 - Receive Certificate of Incorporation:** Issued digitally by Companies House, typically within hours.
5. **Step 5 - Register for Corporation Tax:** Register with HMRC within 3 months of starting business activity.
6. **Step 6 - Apply for UTR (Unique Taxpayer Reference):** Issued by HMRC after CT registration.
7. **Step 7 - Open Business Bank Account:** Neobanks like Wise Business, Revolut Business, Monzo Business allow non-resident directors to open accounts online. Traditional banks (Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds) require UK residency or in-branch visits.
8. **Step 8 - Register for VAT:** Mandatory above GBP 90,000 annual turnover. Voluntary registration below this threshold is beneficial if your clients are VAT-registered businesses.
9. **Step 9 - Payroll / PAYE:** Register with HMRC if UK employees are hired.
10. **Step 10 - Annual Confirmation Statement + Accounts:** File annually with Companies House. File Corporation Tax return with HMRC annually.

## Cost overview

| Item | Approximate Cost (INR) |
| --- | --- |
| Companies House Registration Fee | ₹1,200 – ₹2,500 |
| Registered Address (annual) | ₹15,000 – ₹60,000 |
| Accountant / Tax Filing (annual) | ₹40,000 – ₹1,50,000 |
| VAT Registration & Returns | ₹30,000 – ₹80,000/year |
| Bank Account (Wise / Revolut) | ₹0 – ₹5,000 |
| Total Year 1 | ₹90,000 – ₹3,00,000 |

## Tax implications for Indian founders

UK Corporate Tax: 19% for profits up to GBP 50,000 (small profits rate); 25% for profits above GBP 250,000 (main rate). Marginal relief applies between GBP 50,000–250,000.

R&D Tax Credits: SMEs can claim 25% of qualifying R&D expenditure as a tax credit, very valuable for Indian-owned UK tech or pharma companies.

Dividends paid from UK company to Indian shareholders: 10% withholding tax under India-UK DTAA (if beneficial owner holds < 25%); 15% otherwise. UK domestic law imposes no withholding on dividends in most cases.

Indian founders must declare UK company shareholding in Schedule FA of Indian ITR.

FEMA ODI compliance required for Indian residents investing in UK entities.

Transfer pricing rules apply to related-party transactions between UK company and Indian group entities.

Place of Effective Management (POEM) risk: If UK company management and decision-making occurs from India, CBDT may treat it as an Indian resident company and tax at 40% (foreign company rate).

## Typical timeline

| Stage | Time Required |
| --- | --- |
| Companies House registration | Same day to 24 hours |
| HMRC CT registration | 2–4 weeks (UTR issuance) |
| Bank account (Wise/Revolut) | 1–5 business days |
| Bank account (traditional UK bank) | 4–8 weeks |
| VAT registration | 4–8 weeks |
| Total (fully operational) | 4–10 weeks |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Can an Indian national be the sole director and shareholder of a UK Ltd company?** Yes. There is no requirement for UK residency or citizenship for directors or shareholders. An Indian national living in India can be the sole director and sole shareholder of a UK Ltd company, with no local director required. This is one of the UK's most attractive features for Indian founders.

**Q2. Do I need a UK bank account to run a UK Ltd company?** No legal requirement, but practically necessary for UK client invoicing, HMRC tax payments, and VAT. Fintech neobanks (Wise Business, Revolut Business, Airwallex UK) have significantly simplified non-resident business banking in the UK and can be opened entirely online without UK residency.

**Q3. What is the impact of Brexit on Indian businesses incorporating in the UK?** Post-Brexit, UK companies no longer have automatic access to the EU single market. Indian businesses targeting both UK and EU customers typically now need two entities, a UK Ltd and an EU entity (often an Irish Ltd or Dutch BV). However, for businesses focused purely on UK clients or using the UK as a global hub, Brexit has limited impact on the company structure itself.

**Q4. What is the PSC register and what are Indian founders' obligations?** Every UK company must maintain a Register of People with Significant Control (PSC), individuals who own 25%+ shares, voting rights, or right to appoint directors. This information is public on Companies House. Indian founders are required to register as PSCs, provide their personal details, and keep the register updated. Non-compliance is a criminal offence.

**Q5. Can Indian IT companies use a UK Ltd to invoice UK and US clients and then remit profits to India?** Yes. An Indian IT company can own a UK Ltd subsidiary that invoices UK/global clients. The UK entity pays UK Corporation Tax on profits, and remaining profits can be remitted to India as dividends (subject to India-UK DTAA rates). This structure is common for IT service companies in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, and Chennai targeting British enterprises.

## Note

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: Incorporation in Australia
services.shortDescription: Company registration with ASIC and basic tax setup in Australia.
services.content:
# Company incorporation in Australia

## Overview

Australia is one of the most business-friendly and transparent economies in the world, consistently ranking in the top tier of global ease-of-doing-business indices. For Indian entrepreneurs, exporters, IT firms, and consultants, incorporating in Australia offers access to a stable English-speaking market, strong IP protection laws, and preferential trade ties with Asia-Pacific countries. The most common structure for foreign founders is the Proprietary Limited Company (Pty Ltd), which offers limited liability and is straightforward to set up.

Australia has a strong rule of law, a highly skilled workforce, and a market of 26+ million with high per capita income, making it attractive for B2B services, fintech, education technology, and professional services.

## Key benefits

- Stable, transparent legal and regulatory framework
- English-speaking market, minimal language/legal barrier for Indian founders
- Access to CPTPP and Australia-India ECTA (free trade agreement) benefits
- Strong IP and trademark protection under Australian law
- Easy banking and financial infrastructure
- No requirement for a local director in some structures (though recommended)
- Australia-India Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA) in place
- Path to business migration visas for founders

## Limitations

- Annual compliance costs are relatively high (ASIC fees, audits, tax filings)
- Australian market is smaller than USA or China in absolute size
- Payroll tax and Goods and Services Tax (GST) apply with strict compliance
- Directors must meet fit-and-proper person requirements
- Distance and time zone difference from India may create operational challenges
- Minimum one local director required for most Pty Ltd companies

## Comparison: Australia vs India (Incorporation)

| Parameter | Australia (Pty Ltd) | India (Private Ltd) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Corporate Tax Rate | 25% (small business) / 30% | 22% (domestic) / 25% (new mfg.) |
| Minimum Directors | 1 (local) | 2 |
| Minimum Share Capital | None | ₹1 (no minimum) |
| Annual Compliance | ASIC + ATO filings | MCA + ROC filings |
| Time to Incorporate | 1–3 days | 7–15 days |
| GST Threshold | AUD 75,000 | ₹20–40 lakh |
| Foreign Ownership | 100% allowed | 100% allowed (in most sectors) |
| DTAA with India | Yes | - |
| Public Disclosure | Moderate (ASIC register) | High (MCA public) |

## Documents required

For the company registration process with ASIC (Australian Securities and Investments Commission):

- Proposed company name (check ASIC availability)
- Details of directors and shareholders (passport copies for foreign nationals)
- Registered office address in Australia
- Constitution or adoption of replaceable rules
- ACN (Australian Company Number) application
- TFN (Tax File Number) application post-registration
- ABN (Australian Business Number) registration
- For Indian residents: Indian address proof, PAN card, notarised passport copy

## Step-by-step process

1. **Step 1 - Name Search & Reservation:** Check name availability on the ASIC register. Reserve if needed.
2. **Step 2 - Choose Company Structure:** Most foreign founders choose Pty Ltd (Proprietary Limited). Public companies have more disclosure obligations.
3. **Step 3 - Appoint Directors & Shareholders:** Minimum one director must ordinarily reside in Australia. Foreign founders can be shareholders but typically need a local director.
4. **Step 4 - Register with ASIC:** Submit Form 201 (Application for Registration as an Australian Company) online or through a registered agent.
5. **Step 5 - Obtain ACN:** Issued immediately upon approval by ASIC.
6. **Step 6 - Apply for ABN and TFN:** Through the Australian Business Register (ABR), required for tax and GST.
7. **Step 7 - Open Business Bank Account:** Major banks include Commonwealth Bank, ANZ, Westpac, NAB. Many support non-resident business accounts.
8. **Step 8 - Register for GST:** Mandatory if turnover exceeds AUD 75,000.
9. **Step 9 - PAYG and Payroll Setup:** Required if hiring Australian employees.
10. **Step 10 - Annual Compliance:** Annual review with ASIC, tax returns with ATO, financial statements if required.

## Cost overview

| Item | Approximate Cost (INR) |
| --- | --- |
| ASIC Registration Fee | ₹7,000 – ₹10,000 |
| Registered Agent / Local Director Fee (annual) | ₹80,000 – ₹2,00,000 |
| Company Secretary / Compliance | ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000/year |
| Bank Account Opening | ₹0 – ₹10,000 (varies by bank) |
| Total Setup Cost (Year 1) | ₹1,50,000 – ₹3,50,000 |
| Annual Ongoing Compliance | ₹1,00,000 – ₹2,50,000/year |

## Tax implications for Indian founders

Australia levies 25% corporate tax on small businesses (turnover below AUD 50 million) and 30% on larger entities.

Dividend repatriation to India attracts withholding tax, typically 15% under the India-Australia DTAA (reduced from 30%).

Royalties and interest also benefit from reduced DTAA rates.

Indian founders must report foreign company ownership to the RBI under FEMA (Overseas Direct Investment, ODI route) if their Indian entity holds shares.

Transfer pricing rules apply if transactions occur between the Australian company and the Indian parent/associate.

Controlled Foreign Company (CFC) rules do not currently apply in India but CBDT reporting of foreign assets is mandatory for resident Indians under Schedule FA in ITR.

## Typical timeline

| Stage | Time Required |
| --- | --- |
| Name check and reservation | 1 day |
| ASIC registration | 1–3 business days |
| ABN / TFN registration | 1–5 business days |
| Bank account opening | 2–4 weeks |
| GST registration | 1–5 business days |
| Total (fully operational) | 4–6 weeks |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Can an Indian resident incorporate a company in Australia without visiting?** Yes. You can register remotely through a registered agent or business formation service. However, you must appoint at least one director who ordinarily resides in Australia. Many services provide a nominee director facility.

**Q2. Is there a minimum capital requirement to start an Australian Pty Ltd?** No. There is no minimum paid-up capital requirement. You can start with as little as AUD 1 in share capital, making it accessible for bootstrapped startups.

**Q3. What is the India-Australia ECTA and how does it benefit Indian businesses?** The India-Australia Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement (ECTA), effective from December 2022, reduces or eliminates tariffs on goods and improves market access for services. Indian IT and professional services companies incorporated in Australia benefit from reduced trade friction.

**Q4. Do I need to file tax returns in both India and Australia?** If you are an Indian tax resident holding shares in an Australian company, you must report the foreign asset in your Indian ITR (Schedule FA). The Australian company files its own tax returns with the ATO. Taxes paid in Australia can be claimed as foreign tax credit in India under DTAA provisions.

**Q5. Which Indian states commonly have businesses expanding to Australia?** Businesses from Maharashtra (Mumbai), Karnataka (Bengaluru), Tamil Nadu (Chennai), Delhi NCR, and Telangana (Hyderabad), particularly in IT, pharma, and professional services, are the most active in Australia incorporations.

## Note

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: Incorporation in Hong Kong
services.shortDescription: Hong Kong private company formation for trade and regional HQs.
services.content:
# Company incorporation in Hong Kong

## Overview

Hong Kong remains one of Asia's premier business and financial hubs despite political changes since 2019. For Indian businesses, Hong Kong offers a common law legal system, zero capital gains tax, territorial taxation, a convertible currency (HKD pegged to USD), and deep integration with mainland China's economy. The standard structure is a Private Limited Company, which can be formed quickly, has no minimum share capital, and imposes no restrictions on foreign ownership.

Hong Kong is ideal for trading companies, regional holding structures, fintech businesses, professional services firms, and any Indian company seeking a credible Asia-Pacific base with mainland China access.

## Key benefits

- Territorial tax, only Hong Kong-sourced profits taxed (16.5% standard, 8.25% for first HKD 2 million)
- Zero capital gains tax, no inheritance tax, no VAT/GST
- 100% foreign ownership, no local shareholder required
- Common law legal system, familiar and predictable for Indian businesses
- No minimum share capital requirement
- Quick incorporation, 1–5 days
- Gateway to mainland China market
- Strong banking sector, HSBC, Standard Chartered, DBS, OCBC
- Over 40 DTAA treaties globally
- India-Hong Kong DTAA signed (2018)

## Limitations

- Recent political changes have reduced Hong Kong's autonomy and predictability
- Onshore profits taxed at 16.5%, not zero tax for local operations
- Banks have become more stringent on KYC for non-resident Indian founders
- Must maintain local registered address and company secretary
- Annual government fees and audit requirements
- Not ideal for businesses needing EU market access
- India-Hong Kong DTAA has anti-avoidance provisions (PPT, Principal Purpose Test)

## Comparison: Hong Kong vs India (Incorporation)

| Parameter | Hong Kong (Pvt Ltd) | India (Private Ltd) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Corporate Tax | 8.25% (first HKD 2M) / 16.5% | 22% / 25% |
| Capital Gains Tax | 0% | 10%–20% (LTCG / STCG) |
| GST / VAT | None | 18% (standard GST) |
| Minimum Capital | None | None |
| Foreign Ownership | 100% | 100% (most sectors) |
| Annual Audit | Mandatory | Mandatory (above threshold) |
| Time to Incorporate | 1–5 business days | 7–15 days |
| Language of Filings | English / Chinese | English |
| DTAA with India | Yes (2018) | - |
| Common Law System | Yes | Yes |

## Documents required

For Indian nationals incorporating in Hong Kong:

- Passport copy (notarised) of all shareholders and directors
- Residential address proof (utility bill or bank statement, within 3 months)
- Proposed company name (in English and/or Chinese)
- Particulars of directors and company secretary
- Registered office address in Hong Kong (can use registered agent's address)
- Signed consent to act as director/company secretary
- For Indian corporate shareholder: apostilled Certificate of Incorporation, Board Resolution, MoA/AoA

## Step-by-step process

1. **Step 1 - Name Search and Approval:** Check name availability on Companies Registry database (WebSearch system). Names must be unique and not misleading.
2. **Step 2 - Prepare Incorporation Documents:** Prepare the Articles of Association, NNC1 (Notice of Incorporation) form, and director/shareholder details.
3. **Step 3 - File with the Companies Registry:** Submit NNC1, Articles, and identification documents. Can be filed online or in person.
4. **Step 4 - Receive Certificate of Incorporation:** Typically issued within 1–5 business days. The Business Registration Certificate from the IRD (Inland Revenue Department) is also required.
5. **Step 5 - Appoint Company Secretary:** Every Hong Kong company must have a locally resident company secretary (individual or corporate).
6. **Step 6 - Open Bank Account:** Major banks include HSBC, Standard Chartered, Hang Seng, DBS (HK). Non-resident account opening now requires strong business substance documentation.
7. **Step 7 - Register for Profits Tax:** No separate registration required, automatic under the IRD.
8. **Step 8 - File Annual Return and Audited Accounts:** Required annually. Financial year can be chosen by the company.

## Cost overview

| Item | Approximate Cost (INR) |
| --- | --- |
| Companies Registry fee | ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 |
| Business Registration Certificate | ₹6,000 – ₹8,000/year |
| Company Secretary (annual) | ₹25,000 – ₹80,000 |
| Registered Address (annual) | ₹20,000 – ₹60,000 |
| Audit + Tax Filing (annual) | ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000 |
| Bank Account Setup | ₹0 – ₹30,000 |
| Total Year 1 | ₹1,20,000 – ₹3,50,000 |

## Tax implications for Indian founders

Hong Kong Profits Tax is territorial, only profits arising from or derived in Hong Kong are taxed. Offshore profits (e.g., a Hong Kong trading company billing international clients with no Hong Kong-based operations) may be exempt from Hong Kong Profits Tax on application.

Rate: 8.25% on first HKD 2 million of assessable profits; 16.5% above that (for corporations).

India-Hong Kong DTAA (effective 2018): Dividends, 5% withholding (if Indian company holds ≥25%); otherwise 10%. Royalties, 10%.

Indian residents holding Hong Kong companies must report under Schedule FA in ITR and comply with FEMA ODI regulations.

Economic substance regulations in Hong Kong now require genuine local activity for offshore tax claim. Pure letterbox companies face scrutiny.

## Typical timeline

| Stage | Time Required |
| --- | --- |
| Name search and approval | 1 day |
| Incorporation with Companies Registry | 1–5 business days |
| Business Registration Certificate | Concurrent or 1–3 days |
| Bank account opening | 4–8 weeks |
| Total (fully operational) | 5–10 weeks |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Is Hong Kong still a reliable jurisdiction for Indian businesses given the 2019–2020 political changes?** Hong Kong retains its common law legal system, separate tax regime, independent courts, and convertible currency under the "One Country, Two Systems" framework (until 2047). However, the National Security Law (2020) and electoral changes have reduced political autonomy. For commercial activity, Hong Kong remains functional and credible, but Indian businesses with sensitive IP or politically sensitive operations may prefer Singapore as an alternative.

**Q2. Can a Hong Kong company claim offshore tax exemption on profits earned entirely outside Hong Kong?** Yes, in principle. If a company can demonstrate that all profit-generating activities occurred outside Hong Kong (e.g., overseas buyers, overseas suppliers, no Hong Kong employees involved in the transaction), it may apply for an Offshore Claim with the IRD. Post-BEPS reforms have made this scrutiny more rigorous, genuine economic substance must be demonstrated.

**Q3. Is a physical office mandatory in Hong Kong?** No. A registered address (provided by a company secretary firm or service provider) is sufficient for incorporation. However, for bank account opening and offshore tax claims, a genuine business address with demonstrated local activity is increasingly important.

**Q4. How does the India-Hong Kong DTAA benefit Indian businesses?** The India-Hong Kong DTAA (effective 2018) reduces withholding tax on dividends (5% if holding ≥25%, else 10%), interest (10%), and royalties (10%). It also provides clarity on Permanent Establishment rules and eliminates double taxation, making dividend repatriation and royalty flows from Hong Kong to India more tax-efficient.

**Q5. Which Indian businesses use Hong Kong as their Asia base?** Diamond and precious metals traders (Surat, Mumbai), textile export companies (Gujarat, Tamil Nadu), IT and fintech firms, pharma companies (Hyderabad, Ahmedabad), and commodity trading groups are the dominant Indian users of Hong Kong structures.

## Note

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: Incorporation in Germany
services.shortDescription: Company formation in Germany for EU market access.
services.content:
# Company incorporation in Germany

## Overview

Germany is the largest economy in Europe and a global hub for engineering, manufacturing, automotive, chemicals, and industrial technology. For Indian businesses targeting European markets, Germany offers unmatched infrastructure, a highly skilled workforce, and the credibility of a European Union member state. The most common structures for foreign investors are the GmbH (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung, Private Limited Company) and the UG (Unternehmergesellschaft, a low-capital startup vehicle).

Access to the EU single market (450 million consumers), EU harmonised regulations, and German engineering reputation make this an attractive destination for Indian exporters, manufacturing firms, tech companies, and professional service providers.

## Key benefits

- Full access to EU single market, 27 countries, 450 million consumers
- Gateway to European supply chains, R&D infrastructure, and talent
- Stable rule of law and predictable regulatory environment
- India-EU bilateral trade growth, India-EU FTA negotiations underway
- Strong IP protection under EU frameworks
- Germany's Mittelstand ecosystem, ideal for B2B manufacturing and services
- German credibility, strong brand value for Indian companies selling to EU clients
- Skilled workforce and engineering talent

## Limitations

- High labour costs and strict employee protection laws
- Corporate and trade tax combined effective rate of 28%–33%
- GmbH requires minimum share capital of EUR 25,000
- German language mandatory for statutory filings, contracts, and court proceedings
- Bureaucratic and complex registration process
- Commercial register (Handelsregister) process can take 2–4 weeks
- Mandatory social security contributions for employees
- Complex VAT regime across EU member states

## Comparison: Germany vs India (Incorporation)

| Parameter | Germany (GmbH) | India (Private Ltd) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Corporate Tax (effective) | ~28%–33% (CIT + trade tax) | 22% / 25% |
| Minimum Share Capital | EUR 25,000 (~₹22 lakh) | None |
| Minimum Directors | 1 (can be foreign) | 2 |
| Local Director Required | No | No |
| Annual Audit | Required above thresholds | Required above thresholds |
| EU Market Access | Yes (full) | No |
| Language of Filings | German | English |
| DTAA with India | Yes | - |
| Time to Register | 2–5 weeks | 7–15 days |

## Documents required

For Indian founders registering a GmbH in Germany:

- Notarised and apostilled copies of passport (all shareholders/directors)
- Specimen signatures (notarised)
- Articles of Association (Gesellschaftsvertrag), must be notarised by a German notary
- List of shareholders and their addresses
- Declaration of shareholder list
- Proof of registered address in Germany (office lease or address service agreement)
- Bank confirmation of share capital deposit (EUR 12,500 minimum half of minimum capital)
- Managing director appointment letter
- For Indian corporate shareholder: apostilled Certificate of Incorporation, Board Resolution, MoA/AoA

## Step-by-step process

1. **Step 1 - Draft Articles of Association:** Must be drafted in German and notarised by a German notary (Notar). Articles define shareholders, management, and share structure.
2. **Step 2 - Open a Blocked Bank Account:** Open a German business bank account in blocked status. Transfer minimum share capital (EUR 25,000 for GmbH; EUR 1 for UG). Obtain bank confirmation.
3. **Step 3 - Notarial Authentication:** All founding documents, AoA, shareholder list, managing director appointment, must be certified by a German notary.
4. **Step 4 - Register with the Commercial Court (Handelsregister):** The notary files registration application with the local Amtsgericht (District Court). This is mandatory for GmbH.
5. **Step 5 - Tax Registration with Finanzamt:** Register for trade tax (Gewerbesteuer) and VAT (Umsatzsteuer/Mehrwertsteuer) with the local tax office.
6. **Step 6 - Register for Trade License (Gewerbeanmeldung):** Required for commercial activities in Germany.
7. **Step 7 - Obtain VAT ID:** Apply for EU VAT registration number for cross-border EU transactions.
8. **Step 8 - Unblock Bank Account:** After Commercial Register entry, the company can unblock the bank account for operations.
9. **Step 9 - Register with Social Security & Employment Bodies:** If hiring German employees.
10. **Step 10 - Annual Compliance:** Statutory accounts, trade tax return, CIT return, VAT filings.

## Cost overview

| Item | Approximate Cost (INR) |
| --- | --- |
| Notary fees (GmbH formation) | ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000 |
| Commercial Register fees | ₹10,000 – ₹20,000 |
| Minimum share capital (GmbH) | ₹22,00,000 (EUR 25,000),  refundable equity |
| Registered address (annual) | ₹1,20,000 – ₹3,00,000 |
| Legal / accounting setup | ₹1,50,000 – ₹4,00,000 |
| Apostille / notarisation (India) | ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 |
| Total Year 1 (excl. capital) | ₹3,50,000 – ₹9,00,000 |

## Tax implications for Indian founders

Germany levies Corporate Income Tax (CIT) at 15% + 5.5% solidarity surcharge + municipal Trade Tax (Gewerbesteuer) of ~14%–17%, making the effective combined rate approximately 28%–33%.

Dividends distributed to Indian shareholders attract 10% withholding tax under the India-Germany DTAA.

Interest and royalties are also covered under the DTAA with reduced rates.

Indian residents investing in Germany must comply with FEMA ODI regulations and report to RBI.

German VAT must be charged on goods/services sold within Germany and the EU.

Transfer pricing rules (both Indian and German) apply on related-party transactions.

## Typical timeline

| Stage | Time Required |
| --- | --- |
| Articles of Association + Notary | 1–2 weeks |
| Bank account + capital deposit | 1–2 weeks |
| Commercial Register (Handelsregister) entry | 2–4 weeks |
| Tax registration | 1–2 weeks |
| Total (fully operational) | 6–10 weeks |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Can an Indian national be the sole director (managing director) of a German GmbH?** Yes. There is no requirement for a German or EU-resident director. An Indian national can be the sole managing director (Geschäftsführer) of a GmbH. However, having a local German representative for day-to-day matters is practically advisable, especially for dealing with banks and government bodies.

**Q2. What is the difference between a GmbH and a UG in Germany?** A GmbH requires a minimum share capital of EUR 25,000 and is treated as a fully-fledged private limited company. A UG (Unternehmergesellschaft haftungsbeschränkt) is the startup-friendly variant that can be formed with as little as EUR 1 in share capital. However, a UG must retain 25% of profits each year until accumulated reserves reach EUR 25,000, at which point it can be converted to a GmbH.

**Q3. Is German language mandatory for running the GmbH?** All statutory documents, Commercial Register filings, tax returns, and legal contracts in Germany must be in German. Most government interactions also happen in German. It is strongly recommended to engage a German-speaking accountant (Steuerberater) and legal counsel for compliance management.

**Q4. Does a German company get automatic EU VAT registration?** No. You must separately register for German VAT (Umsatzsteuer) with the Finanzamt. Once registered, you receive a German VAT number (Steuernummer) and can apply for an EU VAT ID number (Umsatzsteuer-Identifikationsnummer) for intra-EU transactions.

**Q5. Which Indian sectors are most active in German incorporation?** Automotive components (Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu), specialty chemicals (Gujarat), IT and engineering consulting (Bengaluru, Pune, Hyderabad), and pharma companies (Hyderabad, Ahmedabad) are the most active in establishing German entities, primarily to support EU sales and supply chain operations.

## Note

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: Temporary GST Registration
services.shortDescription: Temporary GST registrations for short‑term events and projects.
services.content:
# Temporary Gst Registration (casual Taxable Person)

## Overview

Temporary GST Registration refers to the registration obtained by a Casual Taxable Person (CTP) under Section 24 of the CGST Act, 2017. A Casual Taxable Person is defined under Section 2(20) of the CGST Act as a person who occasionally undertakes transactions involving supply of goods or services in a state or Union Territory where they do not have a fixed place of business. Common situations requiring Casual Taxable Person registration include: Businesses participating in trade fairs or exhibitions in a state where they are not otherwise registered Seasonal businesses operating in a specific state only during a festival or tourist season (e.g., a handicraft seller from Rajasthan selling at a Delhi craft fair) Contract workers and freelancers executing a short-term project in a different state from where they are registered Event management companies managing events in states outside their registration state Film production companies shooting in a state where they don't have a permanent establishment Unlike the regular taxpayer who obtains permanent GST registration, the CTP registration is temporary,  valid for a maximum of 90 days (extendable by another 90 days). The CTP cannot claim ITC from inter-state purchases in most cases, and must make an advance deposit of estimated GST liability before registration is granted.

## Applicability

Casual Taxable Person registration is required for: Any person making occasional taxable supplies of goods or services in a state where they do not have a fixed place of business Person attending trade exhibitions, fairs, or expos in a different state Seasonal business operators entering a new state temporarily Businesses executing a one-time project or contract in a state outside their normal registration CTP is NOT applicable if: The person has a permanent business establishment in the state,  they must register as a regular taxpayer The supply is entirely exempt from GST,  no registration required for exempt supplies The person is a Non-Resident (foreign national/entity) making supplies in India,  they register as NRTP, not CTP Applicable pan-India,  CTP registration is available in all states and UTs.

## Features

Registration in Form GST REG-01: CTP applies using the same form as regular registration, but selects "Casual Taxable Person" as the taxpayer type Mandatory advance deposit: Before the CTP GSTIN is issued, the applicant must make an advance tax deposit equal to the estimated GST liability for the period of supply 90-day validity: CTP registration is valid for up to 90 days from the effective date,  extendable by another 90 days maximum GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B filing: CTPs file regular GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns during their registration period,  these must be filed before applying for extension Final GSTR-3B: CTPs file a final return after the supply period ends Refund of excess advance deposit: If actual GST liability is less than the advance deposit, the excess is refundable No ITC on inter-state purchases for CTPs in many situations: This limitation makes the advance deposit model more conservative Existing regular GSTIN in home state: A CTP can simultaneously hold their regular GSTIN in their home state,  the CTP GSTIN in the new state is separate and temporary

## Benefits

Legal supply across state boundaries: Enables businesses to make taxable supplies in states where they are not permanently registered,  without having to go through the full permanent registration process Short-term and hassle-free: For genuinely occasional supplies, CTP registration is less burdensome than permanent multi-state registration Refund mechanism: Excess advance deposit is refundable,  the advance deposit does not represent a cost, only a temporary cash outflow Tax invoice issuance: CTPs can issue valid GST tax invoices,  enabling their B2B buyers to claim ITC Compliance simplicity: For a defined short-term supply period, CTP returns are straightforward compared to managing permanent registrations in multiple states

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form GST REG-01 | With taxpayer type selected as "Casual Taxable Person" |
| PAN of the entity | Company/firm/individual PAN |
| Aadhaar of primary signatory | For authentication |
| Photo of primary signatory |  |
| Address proof of the principal place of business in the state | Even a temporary address,  event hall, exhibition stall, hotel address |
| Details of supply | Duration, nature of goods/services, estimated turnover |
| Estimated advance deposit calculation | GST liability computation for the supply period |
| Home state GSTIN (if already registered) | Reference to existing registration |
| Bank account details | Preferably an account to which the advance deposit refund can be credited |

## Process

1. Estimate GST Liability Estimate the total turnover expected during the supply period and compute the expected GST liability (applying the correct GST rate to the estimated turnover).

2. File GST REG-01 as CTP File Form GST REG-01 on the GST portal, select the state where the temporary supply will be made and choose "Casual Taxable Person" as the registration type.

3. Make Advance Deposit Make the advance tax deposit on the GST portal (the system requests the deposit before granting the GSTIN). Deposit into the Electronic Cash Ledger via net banking/UPI.

4. Receive CTP GSTIN Upon deposit confirmation and document verification, the GST officer grants the CTP GSTIN. The certificate specifies the validity period (up to 90 days).

5. Conduct Business Use the CTP GSTIN for all supplies made during the registration period. Issue tax invoices with the CTP GSTIN.

6. File GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B File returns for each return period within the CTP registration validity. All returns must be filed before the registration expires.

7. Apply for Extension (if needed) If the supply period needs to be extended beyond 90 days, apply for extension before the CTP registration expires, with an additional advance deposit for the extended period. Maximum total period: 180 days.

8. File Final Returns and Claim Refund After the supply period, file the final GSTR-3B. Apply for a refund of any excess advance deposit through Form RFD-01. Fees Service Government Fee CTP Registration Nil (Advance deposit is required but refundable)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee CTP registration application support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Advance deposit computation ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B filing (per month) ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Extension application ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Refund of excess advance deposit ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 End-to-end CTP compliance package ₹10,000 – ₹25,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance Apply for CTP registration and make advance deposit before commencing supply,  not on the day of the event File all GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns within the registration period Do not exceed the 90-day (or 180-day with extension) validity without a fresh registration Maintain all tax invoices, stock records, and return copies for 6 years even for the CTP period Refund applications must be filed after the final GSTR-3B,  within 2 years of the relevant date

## Common Mistakes

Starting supply without CTP registration: Beginning to sell at an exhibition without a CTP GSTIN,  all sales are non-compliant and GST collected (if any) cannot be legally retained Underestimating advance deposit: If actual GST exceeds the advance deposit, the shortfall must be paid immediately,  compute conservatively to avoid cash crunch Not filing returns within the validity period: CTP returns must be filed within the 90-day period,  expired CTP registrations cannot be reactivated; a fresh registration must be obtained Using home state GSTIN for supplies in another state: Issuing invoices from a home-state GSTIN for supplies made at an event in another state,  this is an incorrect place of supply treatment and creates ITC issues for buyers in the other state Forgetting to claim refund: The advance deposit refund is not automatic,  businesses often forget to file RFD-01 after completing the supply period

## Frequently asked questions

**We participate in 4 trade fairs per year in different states. Do we need a fresh CTP registration for each fair?** Yes,  each state requires a separate CTP registration for the duration of the fair in that state. If you attend a fair in Delhi, another in Mumbai, one in Chennai, and one in Kolkata, you need 4 separate CTP registrations,  one for each state, covering only the duration of the fair in that state. Each CTP registration requires a separate advance deposit for the estimated GST liability in that state for that period. If you regularly attend trade fairs in the same states every year, it may be more efficient to obtain permanent GST registrations in those states instead.

**The trade fair where I'm participating starts in 3 days. Is it too late to get a CTP GST registration?** The standard GST registration processing timeline is 7 working days (for Aadhaar-authenticated applications),  which may be too long for a fair starting in 3 days. However, the GST portal processes CTP applications with some urgency given the time-bound nature. Additionally, since the portal has a deemed approval mechanism (if the officer does not act within 7 working days, registration is deemed granted), you can often commence operations with the ARN pending. That said, waiting until 3 days before is inadvisable,  apply for CTP registration at least 10–15 days before the event to ensure the GSTIN is active and the advance deposit is processed.

**I am registered in Karnataka. I am providing professional consulting services for a 2-month project in Hyderabad (Telangana). Do I need a CTP registration in Telangana?** This depends on whether you have a fixed place of business in Telangana. If you are working remotely from Karnataka or staying in a hotel in Hyderabad (temporary accommodation is not a fixed establishment), the place of supply for services is typically the location of the service recipient (Telangana). You would charge IGST using your Karnataka GSTIN,  no CTP registration in Telangana is needed for most service supplies. However, if the nature of your engagement requires physical presence and operations from Telangana over an extended period with significant infrastructure, CTP registration may be more appropriate. Get a professional opinion on the specific facts.

**What is the difference between CTP (Casual Taxable Person) and NRTP (Non-Resident Taxable Person)?** Both are temporary GST registrations, but they differ in applicability: CTP is for Indian residents (individuals or businesses) making occasional supplies in a state outside their home registration. NRTP is for foreign nationals or foreign entities (without a fixed establishment in India) making occasional supplies anywhere in India. The application forms differ,  CTP uses GST REG-01 (same form as regular taxpayers, with CTP type selected); NRTP uses GST REG-09 (a separate form designed for non-residents). Both require advance deposits and have 90-day maximum validity. The return form also differs,  CTPs file GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B; NRTPs file GSTR-5.

**Our CTP registration for a 2-month fair in Delhi has expired but we still have 3 weeks of the fair remaining. What do we do?** If the CTP registration expires before the supply period ends, you have two options: (a) if you are still within the first 90-day period and haven't yet applied for extension,  apply immediately for the 90-day extension (maximum total period is 180 days); (b) if the maximum 180-day period has already been exhausted,  apply for a fresh CTP registration for the remaining period, with a new advance deposit. In the interim period between expiry and fresh registration, technically you cannot make taxable supplies. Minimise this gap by tracking expiry dates carefully and applying for extension or fresh registration well before the current registration expires.

packages.description: Quote covers registration, return filing for the period and cancellation.

services.name: GST Registration for Foreigners
services.shortDescription: GST registration for non‑resident taxable persons in India.
services.content:
# Gst Registration For Foreigners

## Overview

Foreign nationals, foreign companies, and entities resident outside India that make taxable supplies of goods or services within India,  even temporarily,  are required to obtain GST registration in India. This registration is governed by specific provisions under the CGST Act, 2017 that cater to two primary categories of foreign suppliers: Non-Resident Taxable Persons (NRTP) and suppliers of OIDAR (Online Information and Database Access and Retrieval) services. Non-Resident Taxable Person (NRTP): A person who occasionally undertakes taxable transactions involving supply of goods or services in India, but who does not have a fixed place of business in India. Examples include foreign artists performing in India, foreign traders selling at Indian trade fairs, foreign film production companies shooting in India, or foreign consultants on short-term India assignments. OIDAR Service Providers: Foreign companies providing online services to Indian consumers,  including streaming services (Netflix, Spotify), cloud software, e-books, online gaming, online advertising, and data analytics services,  are required to register and pay GST in India. This was introduced to create a level playing field between Indian and foreign digital service providers. Foreign companies with a fixed establishment in India (branch office, liaison office, project office) are treated as Indian residents for GST purposes and must register as regular taxpayers,  not as NRTPs.

## Applicability

Foreign Entity Type GST Registration Type Required Foreign person/company making occasional taxable supplies in India Non-Resident Taxable Person (NRTP) Foreign company providing OIDAR services to Indian non-business consumers OIDAR provider registration Foreign company with a branch/project office in India Regular GST registration Foreign company with Liaison Office (no business activity) No GST registration required Foreign e-commerce operator with Indian customers May require NRTP or OIDAR registration depending on model Foreign artist/performer at events in India NRTP registration Foreign exhibitor at India trade fairs NRTP registration

Applicable pan-India,  a single NRTP or OIDAR registration covers all-India operations; state-wise multiple registrations are generally not required for NRTPs.

## Features

Advance registration for NRTP: NRTPs must register at least 5 days before commencing taxable supplies in India,  Form GST REG-09 Advance deposit mandatory: NRTPs must make an advance tax deposit equal to the estimated GST liability for the period of supply,  deposited into the Electronic Cash Ledger at the time of registration Temporary nature: NRTP registration is granted for up to 90 days (extendable by another 90 days),  it is not a permanent registration No ITC for NRTPs: NRTPs cannot claim Input Tax Credit,  they pay GST only on output supplies OIDAR registration,  Simplified: OIDAR providers register in Form GST REG-10 (simplified registration for cross-border digital service providers) through a single jurisdiction Authorised representative in India: NRTPs and OIDAR providers must appoint an authorised representative in India for compliance purposes PAN not required for NRTPs: Unlike regular taxpayers, NRTPs are not required to have a PAN,  they use their passport or tax identification number from their home country Monthly GSTR-5 filing: NRTPs file monthly returns in Form GSTR-5; OIDAR providers file GSTR-5A

## Benefits

Legal supply in India: Foreign entities can legally supply goods and services in India for short periods,  exhibitions, events, concerts, film shoots, and professional engagements Compliance simplicity: The NRTP framework is designed for occasional suppliers,  simpler than the regular GST registration process and without permanent establishment implications Clear liability computation: Advance deposit mechanism ensures GST liability is pre-funded,  no surprise tax demands after the supply period OIDAR compliance avoids penalties: Foreign digital service providers who register and pay GST avoid the risk of GST department action, blocking of websites/services in India, and reputational risk Refund of excess deposit: If the actual GST liability is less than the advance deposit, the NRTP can claim a refund of the excess after filing the final return Level playing field: OIDAR registration ensures foreign digital companies pay the same GST as Indian digital service providers,  maintaining competitive equity

## Documents

Document NRTP OIDAR Provider GST REG-09 / REG-10 form REG-09 REG-10 Passport of the foreign individual ✓ Not required (entity-level) Tax Identification Number from home country ✓ ✓ Certificate of Incorporation (for foreign company) ✓ ✓ Details of authorised representative in India ✓ ✓ Aadhaar/PAN of Indian authorised representative ✓ ✓ Bank account details (Indian bank account) ✓ ✓ Description of supplies to be made in India ✓ ✓ Estimated tax liability (for advance deposit) ✓ Not required Details of event/engagement in India ✓ Not applicable

## Process

1. Engage Indian Authorised Representative Appoint an Indian resident as the authorised representative who will sign and file GST applications and returns on behalf of the NRTP.

2. File GST REG-09 at least 5 days before supply Complete and file Form GST REG-09 on the GST portal, entity details, supply details, period of supply, and authorised representative information.

3. Make Advance Deposit Compute the estimated GST liability for the supply period. Make an advance deposit into the Electronic Cash Ledger at the time of or before registration.

4. Receive GSTIN A temporary GSTIN is issued for the period specified in the application (up to 90 days).

5. File GSTR-5 File monthly GSTR-5 returns for each month (or part thereof) of taxable supply in India. GSTR-5 is due by the 20th of the following month.

6. File Final GSTR-5 After the supply period ends, file the final GSTR-5 within 7 days of the expiry of registration or the last day of the supply period, whichever is earlier.

7. Claim Refund of Excess Deposit If actual liability is less than the advance deposit, claim a refund of the excess through Form RFD-01 after the final return is filed. For OIDAR Provider: File Form GST REG-10 on the GST portal. Appoint an Indian representative. File monthly GSTR-5A returns by the 20th of the following month. Fees Service Government Fee NRTP / OIDAR GST Registration Nil Advance deposit (NRTP) Based on estimated liability (refundable)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee NRTP registration support ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 OIDAR registration support ₹15,000 – ₹35,000 GSTR-5 filing (per month) ₹5,000 – ₹10,000 GSTR-5A filing (OIDAR, per month) ₹5,000 – ₹10,000 Authorised representative services (annual) ₹25,000 – ₹75,000 Refund of excess advance deposit ₹5,000 – ₹15,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance NRTP must register at least 5 days before commencing supplies,  not on the day of supply Advance deposit must be maintained,  if actual liability exceeds the deposit, pay the additional amount promptly File GSTR-5 by the 20th of each month,  or within 7 days of end of registration for the final return OIDAR providers must file GSTR-5A monthly by the 20th,  regardless of whether any supply was made in that month The authorised representative is legally responsible for all GST compliance on behalf of the NRTP/OIDAR provider in India

## Common Mistakes

Registering too late: NRTPs who register on the day of supply (instead of 5 days before) are technically in violation,  pre-registration with advance deposit is mandatory Underestimating advance deposit: If the actual GST exceeds the deposit, the excess is a demand,  compute conservatively with a margin Not appointing a knowledgeable Indian representative: The Indian authorised representative bears legal compliance responsibility,  choosing an uninformed or unavailable representative creates compliance gaps OIDAR providers not registering: Foreign digital service providers incorrectly assuming that physical absence from India exempts them from GST,  the law specifically covers cross-border digital services to Indian consumers Missing the final return deadline: NRTPs must file the final GSTR-5 within 7 days of the end of registration,  this short window is frequently missed

## Frequently asked questions

**A foreign musician is coming to India for a 3-concert tour over 2 weeks. Does the promoter or the musician need NRTP registration?** The liability to register and pay GST depends on who is making the taxable supply. If the foreign musician is directly contracted with the Indian event organiser (as the supplier of entertainment services), the musician (or their management entity) is the NRTP and must register. If the Indian event organiser is contracted with the venue and audience, and the musician is contracted as a performer with the organiser, the supply is between the foreign musician and the Indian organiser,  the organiser pays GST under Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM) and the musician may not need NRTP registration. The contractual structure determines the GST treatment,  professional advice before the tour is essential.

**Does a foreign company with a Liaison Office in India need to register for GST?** Generally no,  a Liaison Office (LO) is permitted only to undertake liaison activities (market research, information gathering, promotion of the parent company's products) and is not permitted to undertake commercial activities. Since an LO makes no taxable supply, there is typically no GST liability and no registration requirement. However, if the LO is found to be performing activities beyond its permitted scope (which constitutes a supply), GST registration would be required and the LO would be treated as a fixed establishment of the foreign company in India.

**Netflix is a US company charging Indian subscribers for streaming services. How does their India GST compliance work?** Netflix and similar foreign streaming services fall under the OIDAR (Online Information and Database Access and Retrieval) category. OIDAR services provided by foreign suppliers to Indian non-business consumers are taxable in India at 18% GST. Netflix (or any OIDAR provider) must register under Form GST REG-10 on the GST portal, collect GST from Indian subscribers (or absorb it in pricing), and file monthly GSTR-5A returns. The CBIC has been actively enforcing OIDAR compliance for foreign digital platforms serving Indian consumers.

**We are a UK-based consulting firm providing advisory services to Indian corporate clients. Are we subject to Indian GST?** Services provided by a foreign entity to an Indian business entity are treated as import of services by the Indian recipient. The Indian business client pays GST under the Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM) on such imported services. The UK consulting firm itself does not need to register for GST in India for this model,  the tax compliance burden falls on the Indian recipient, not the UK supplier. However, if the UK firm has a fixed establishment (branch office, project office) in India, that establishment is treated as an Indian supplier and regular GST registration is required.

**A foreign company wants to participate as an exhibitor at an Indian trade fair for 10 days. What is the GST registration process and timeline?** The foreign exhibitor must register as a Non-Resident Taxable Person (NRTP) before the trade fair. The process: (a) appoint an Indian authorised representative immediately; (b) file Form GST REG-09 on the GST portal at least 5 days before the first day of the trade fair; (c) make an advance deposit into the Electronic Cash Ledger estimated to cover GST on all sales expected at the fair; (d) receive the temporary GSTIN; (e) charge GST on all sales at the fair; and (f) file the final GSTR-5 within 7 days of the last day of the fair. Begin the process at least 10–15 days before the fair date to allow time for registration processing.
