services.name: GST Annual Return
services.shortDescription: Preparation and filing of annual GST returns and reconciliations.
services.content:
# Gst Annual Return Filing (gstr-9)

## Overview

GSTR-9 is the Annual Return that every regular GST-registered taxpayer is required to file for each financial year. It consolidates all the monthly/quarterly GST returns (GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B) filed during the year into a comprehensive annual summary,  providing a complete picture of the taxpayer's outward supplies, inward supplies, ITC availed, taxes paid, and adjustments made during the financial year. GSTR-9 is filed on the GST portal (gst.gov.in) and is due by December 31 of the year following the financial year for which it is filed. For example, GSTR-9 for FY 2024-25 is due by December 31, 2025. The GST Council has provided various exemptions and simplifications for GSTR-9 over the years: Taxpayer Category GSTR-9 Status Turnover up to ₹2 crore Exempt from GSTR-9 (optional filing) Turnover above ₹2 crore Mandatory GSTR-9 Turnover above ₹5 crore GSTR-9 + GSTR-9C (Self-Certified Reconciliation Statement) mandatory Composition taxpayers File GSTR-9A (separate annual return for composition dealers) Input Service Distributors GSTR-9 not applicable Non-resident taxable persons GSTR-9 not applicable

GSTR-9C (previously requiring CA/CMA certification, now self-certified for turnover up to ₹5 crore, and requiring reconciliation for above ₹5 crore) is a reconciliation statement that compares the GSTR-9 data with the audited annual accounts.

## Applicability

GSTR-9 annual return is mandatory for: All regular GST-registered taxpayers with annual aggregate turnover above ₹2 crore Taxpayers registered as regular taxable persons throughout the financial year Taxpayers who were registered for part of the year (pro-rata period covered) GSTR-9C (Reconciliation Statement) is additionally mandatory for: Taxpayers with annual aggregate turnover above ₹5 crore GSTR-9 is NOT applicable for: Taxpayers with turnover up to ₹2 crore (optional) Composition scheme dealers (they file GSTR-9A) Input Service Distributors Casual taxable persons Non-resident taxable persons TDS deductors and TCS collectors Applicable pan-India,  all GST-registered businesses across all states and UTs must comply with GSTR-9 filing requirements.

## Features

Consolidated annual summary: Aggregates all GSTR-1 (outward supply) and GSTR-3B (tax payment) data filed during the year into one comprehensive return ITC reconciliation: Part of GSTR-9 specifically captures ITC availed, ITC reversed, and ineligible ITC,  providing a full-year ITC picture Amendment window: GSTR-9 provides an opportunity to declare and pay tax on any amendments, corrections, or additional liabilities identified after the regular returns were filed GSTR-9C reconciliation: The reconciliation statement compares GSTR-9 data with audited financials,  surfacing discrepancies between accounting records and GST returns Pre-populated data: The GST portal pre-populates GSTR-9 with data from GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and GSTR-2B,  reducing data entry effort; pre-populated data can be edited where required Late fee structure: Late filing attracts ₹200 per day (₹100 CGST + ₹100 SGST), subject to a maximum of 0.25% of turnover in the state/UT Financial year based: One GSTR-9 per GSTIN per financial year,  businesses with multiple GSTINs must file separate returns for each

## Benefits

Statutory compliance: Avoids late fees, notices, and potential scrutiny from GST authorities for non-filing Error correction opportunity: Discrepancies identified between GSTR-1/3B and actual business data can be corrected in GSTR-9 by paying additional tax,  preventing future demand notices ITC health check: The process of preparing GSTR-9 forces a comprehensive review of the year's ITC position,  helping identify over-claimed or under-claimed credits Financial statement alignment: GSTR-9C preparation aligns GST returns with audited financials,  improving overall accounting accuracy and audit readiness Litigation prevention: Accurate GSTR-9 filings reduce the scope for GST department to raise annual turnover or ITC discrepancy notices Banking and lending: Lenders increasingly cross-verify GSTR-9 data with ITR and financial statements for loan sanctions,  a clean GSTR-9 supports creditworthiness Clean compliance record: A complete, accurate GSTR-9 filing history supports the business during M&A due diligence, IPO readiness, and large contract bidding

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| GSTR-1 filed (all months/quarters) | For FY being reported,  outward supply data |
| GSTR-3B filed (all months/quarters) | Tax payment data |
| GSTR-2B (all months) | Auto-populated ITC statement |
| ITC register / purchase register | Internal ITC records for reconciliation |
| Sales register / ledger | Outward supply reconciliation |
| Audited financial statements | Balance sheet and P&L for GSTR-9C preparation |
| Audit report (statutory auditor) | For GSTR-9C reconciliation reference |
| Debit notes and credit notes issued/received | For adjustment reporting |
| RCM payment records | Reverse charge mechanism payments made during the year |
| Advance received / adjusted details | For supply declared in advance receipt period |

## Process

1. Data Compilation Compile all GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B filed during the FY. Download GSTR-2B for all months. Extract data from the accounting system, sales, purchases, ITC register.

2. Reconciliation Reconcile GSTR-1 data with the sales register; GSTR-3B ITC data with GSTR-2B and purchase register. Identify and explain all differences.

3. GSTR-9 Preparation Prepare the GSTR-9 return table-by-table, outward supplies (Tables 4 & 5), ITC availed (Table 6), ITC reversed (Table 7), other ITC (Table 8), tax paid (Table 9), and previous year adjustments (Tables 10 & 11).

4. GSTR-9C Preparation (if turnover above ₹5 crore) Prepare the GSTR-9C reconciliation statement, comparing GSTR-9 turnover with audited P&L turnover, identifying and explaining differences (deductions, non-GST supplies, advances, etc.).

5. Review and Finalisation Review the GSTR-9 (and GSTR-9C) for accuracy. Identify any additional tax liability and compute the interest (if applicable).

6. Additional Tax Payment (if required) Pay any additional tax liability identified during GSTR-9 preparation, through DRC-03 (voluntary payment form) or directly in GSTR-9.

7. Filing on GST Portal File GSTR-9 (and GSTR-9C) on the GST portal before December 31. Download and archive the ARN (Acknowledgement Reference Number). Fees Service Estimated Professional Fee GSTR-9 filing (turnover up to ₹2 crore) ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 GSTR-9 filing (₹2 crore – ₹5 crore turnover) ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 GSTR-9 + GSTR-9C (above ₹5 crore) ₹20,000 – ₹75,000 GSTR-9 + GSTR-9C (above ₹50 crore) ₹50,000 – ₹2,00,000 Data reconciliation support ₹5,000 – ₹20,000 Late filing with penalty computation ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 Government Late Fee Amount GSTR-9 late fee ₹200/day (max 0.25% of state turnover) Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%. Compliance File GSTR-9 by December 31 of the succeeding financial year Turnover above ₹5 crore must also file GSTR-9C All GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B for the year must be filed before GSTR-9 can be submitted Pay any additional liability identified during GSTR-9 preparation to avoid interest Maintain all reconciliation workings and supporting data for at least 6 years GSTR-9 once filed cannot be revised, accuracy before submission is critical


## Common Mistakes

Not reconciling GSTR-1 with sales register before GSTR-9 filing: Leads to incorrect turnover reporting in GSTR-9 that conflicts with audited financials Incorrect ITC reversal reporting: Missing ITC reversals under Rule 42 or Section 17(5) in GSTR-9 creates exposure for demand notices Ignoring previous year's adjustments: Amendments made in the current year relating to the previous year must be separately reported in Tables 10 and 11,  commonly omitted Filing GSTR-9 before all GSTR-3Bs are filed: GSTR-9 cannot capture accurate data if all monthly returns haven't been filed,  always file all pending GSTR-3Bs first Not paying additional liability before filing: Reporting additional liability in GSTR-9 without paying it immediately attracts interest from the original due date

## Frequently asked questions

**Our turnover is ₹1.8 crore. Is GSTR-9 optional for us and should we still file it?** Yes, GSTR-9 is optional for taxpayers with annual aggregate turnover up to ₹2 crore. However, we recommend filing voluntarily for several reasons: (a) it creates a complete compliance record; (b) banks and NBFC lenders increasingly cross-check GSTR-9 data; (c) it provides an opportunity to identify and correct discrepancies between monthly returns and actual business data before they surface in departmental scrutiny; (d) as your business grows beyond ₹2 crore, a prior filing history avoids the first-year complexity of filing GSTR-9 without practice.

**We discovered during GSTR-9 preparation that we over-claimed ₹3 lakh in ITC last year. How do we handle this in GSTR-9?** The over-claimed ITC must be reversed. Report the reversal in Table 7 of GSTR-9 (ITC Reversed). The additional tax liability arising from the reversal (plus interest at 18% p.a. from the date of original incorrect claim) should be paid through DRC-03 before or at the time of filing GSTR-9. Proactive disclosure and payment in GSTR-9 demonstrates good faith compliance and typically results in lower exposure than waiting for the department to detect and demand the reversal.

**Is GSTR-9C still required to be certified by a Chartered Accountant?** For taxpayers with turnover above ₹5 crore, GSTR-9C is required as a self-certified reconciliation statement,  meaning the taxpayer or their authorised signatory certifies the statement. CA/CMA certification is no longer mandatory for GSTR-9C (this change was made effective from FY 2020-21 onwards). However, engaging a CA or GST practitioner to prepare GSTR-9C is strongly advisable,  the reconciliation between GST returns and audited accounts requires accounting expertise and errors in GSTR-9C can attract notices.

**We have 5 GSTINs across 5 states. How many GSTR-9 returns do we need to file?** One GSTR-9 per GSTIN per financial year. If your business has 5 GSTINs (registered in 5 states), you must file 5 separate GSTR-9 returns,  one for each GSTIN, reporting only the transactions of that GSTIN. The turnover threshold (₹2 crore for mandatory filing, ₹5 crore for GSTR-9C) is based on the aggregate turnover across all GSTINs of the PAN,  not the individual GSTIN's turnover. So even if one GSTIN has a turnover of ₹80 lakh, if the total PAN-level turnover exceeds ₹2 crore, all GSTINs must file GSTR-9.

**Can we amend or revise GSTR-9 after filing?** No. Once GSTR-9 is submitted and filed, it cannot be revised or amended. This is why thorough review before filing is absolutely critical. If you discover an error after filing, the recourse is limited,  material errors (particularly underreporting of tax liability) should be disclosed and paid through DRC-03 proactively before the GST department detects them through scrutiny. For genuine errors, a representation can be made to the GST officer with supporting documentation during any subsequent scrutiny or audit.

packages.name: Custom
packages.description: Price depends on turnover and complexity of reconciliations required.

services.name: GST E‑Way Bill
services.shortDescription: Assistance with e‑way bill generation and compliance.
services.content:
# Gst E-way Bill

## Overview

The E-Way Bill (Electronic Way Bill) is a compliance document generated on the GST E-Way Bill portal (ewaybillgst.gov.in) for the movement of goods valued above ₹50,000 within India. Introduced under Rule 138 of the CGST Rules, 2017, the E-Way Bill is a digital document that must accompany the physical movement of goods,  whether by road, rail, air, or ship,  from the point of supply to the point of delivery. An E-Way Bill consists of two parts: Part A containing supply details (GSTIN of supplier/recipient, place of supply, invoice number, value, HSN code) and Part B containing vehicle details (transporter ID, vehicle number, mode of transport). The bill contains a unique E-Way Bill Number (EBN) that can be verified by GST enforcement officers at checkposts and during transit. The E-Way Bill system was introduced to replace the paper-based way bill/transit pass system that existed under the pre-GST state VAT regimes, and to create a real-time digital trail of goods movement across India,  enabling enforcement authorities to cross-verify consignments with GST returns and detect tax evasion in the supply chain. Failure to generate an E-Way Bill for applicable consignments,  or transportation of goods with an expired, incorrect, or invalid E-Way Bill,  attracts seizure of goods and vehicles plus a penalty of ₹10,000 or tax payable (whichever is higher) under Section 129 of the CGST Act.

## Applicability

E-Way Bill is required for: Movement of goods valued above ₹50,000 (consignment value including GST) across or within India,  inter-state or intra-state All modes of transport: Road (own vehicle, hired vehicle), rail, air, ship Both taxable and zero-rated supplies: Exports, SEZ supplies, job work, and bill-to-ship-to scenarios all require E-Way Bill if value exceeds ₹50,000 Unregistered persons: Even if the supplier is unregistered, E-Way Bill is required for consignments above ₹50,000 Exemptions from E-Way Bill: Exempted Category Details Exempted goods 153 categories of goods exempted by CBIC notification Movement within same state below state threshold Some states have higher intra-state thresholds Movement by non-motorised conveyance Handcarts, animal carts Customs-sealed containers Under customs supervision Movement of goods up to 50 km Between job worker and principal (intra-state) Defence ministry supplies Consignments for Ministry of Defence Empty cargo containers

Movement under Customs Clearance for export/import under customs supervision

State-specific thresholds: Some states have set higher intra-state E-Way Bill thresholds (e.g., ₹1 lakh for certain states). Check state-specific notifications.

## Features

Single portal: ewaybillgst.gov.in,  centralized E-Way Bill generation for all of India with real-time validation Unique EBN: Each E-Way Bill has a unique 12-digit Electronic Way Bill Number Validity period: Based on distance,  1 day for every 200 km (road), with extension possible for exceptional circumstances Part A and Part B: Part A is supply details; Part B is transporter/vehicle details. Part A can be generated without Part B,  Part B must be updated before goods move Multiple generation methods: Web interface, SMS, mobile app, API integration with ERP/billing systems Consolidated E-Way Bill: For multiple invoices in a single vehicle, a consolidated E-Way Bill (CEWB) covering multiple EBNs can be generated E-Way Bill cancellation: Can be cancelled within 24 hours of generation if goods are not transported RFID integration: Vehicles registered on the E-Way Bill portal with RFID tags can be tracked through RFID scanners at checkposts,  reducing manual verification delays Alert system: SMS and email alerts to supplier, recipient, and transporter when E-Way Bill is generated, updated, or expires

## Benefits

Legal movement of goods: E-Way Bill is the legal authorisation for goods movement,  without it, vehicles can be intercepted, goods seized, and penalties imposed Digital compliance trail: E-Way Bill data is cross-referenced with GSTR-1 filings,  consistent data reduces the risk of scrutiny and demand notices Reduced checkpost delays: The E-Way Bill system eliminated the multi-state checkpost system that caused significant transit delays,  goods now move more freely with digital verification Real-time tracking: Businesses can track their own consignments using the E-Way Bill portal,  improving supply chain visibility Dispute prevention: A valid E-Way Bill with correct consignment details protects against unjustified detention by enforcement officers,  the document is the legal proof of compliance GSTIN verification: The E-Way Bill system validates GSTIN of supplier and recipient,  catching registration issues before goods move Business intelligence: E-Way Bill data provides real-time intelligence on goods movement patterns,  useful for supply chain analysis

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Tax invoice / bill of supply | For supply of goods by registered person |
| Delivery challan | For non-supply movement (job work, returns, branch transfer) |
| Bill of entry | For imported goods |
| GSTIN of supplier and recipient | For validation on E-Way Bill portal |
| Transporter ID / vehicle number | For Part B of E-Way Bill |
| HSN code of goods | For correct goods classification |
| Consignment value | Invoice value including GST |

## Process

1. E-Way Bill Portal Registration Register on ewaybillgst.gov.in using GSTIN and OTP verification. Suppliers, recipients, and transporters can all register.

2. Generate E-Way Bill (Part A) Log in to the portal. Select "Generate E-Way Bill." Enter: transaction type (outward/inward/job work/etc.), document type (invoice/delivery challan), document details, supplier/recipient GSTIN, consignment value, HSN code, and quantity.

3. Enter Transporter Details (Part B) Enter the vehicle number and transporter GSTIN (or transporter ID). Part B can be updated by the transporter if the supplier has not entered it.

4. Generate and Download EBN The portal generates a unique 12-digit EBN. Download and print (or share digitally) the E-Way Bill document. The driver/transporter must carry a copy (physical or digital).

5. Monitor Validity Track E-Way Bill validity, 1 day per 200 km. For long-distance shipments, plan movements to ensure delivery within validity. In case of exceptional delay (accident, natural calamity, transhipment), extend validity on the portal before expiry.

6. Update Part B (if vehicle changes mid-route) If goods are transhipped from one vehicle to another, the transporter must update Part B on the portal with the new vehicle number.

7. Cancellation (if required) If goods are not transported after E-Way Bill generation, cancel the EBN within 24 hours on the portal. Fees Service Government Fee E-Way Bill generation Nil (Free) Professional / Compliance Service Estimated Fee E-Way Bill portal setup and training ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 E-Way Bill API integration support ₹15,000 – ₹50,000 E-Way Bill compliance audit ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 Penalty response / seizure case support ₹10,000 – ₹40,000 Government Penalties Amount No E-Way Bill / invalid E-Way Bill ₹10,000 or tax due, whichever is higher Goods seizure and detention Tax + penalty + release order required Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%. Compliance Generate E-Way Bill before goods commence movement, not after Ensure Part B (vehicle details) is filled before the goods physically move Monitor expiry and extend validity if goods are delayed in transit Cancel E-Way Bills for consignments that don't move within 24 hours of generation Maintain all E-Way Bill records for 6 years Cross-verify E-Way Bill data with GSTR-1 before filing, EBN data is cross-checked by the system


## Common Mistakes

Generating E-Way Bill after goods have moved: EBN must precede movement,  generating it post-movement is a violation even if the value is correct Incorrect HSN code in E-Way Bill: Mismatch between invoice HSN and E-Way Bill HSN attracts detention,  use the same HSN in both documents Not updating vehicle details (Part B): Moving goods with only Part A filled and no vehicle number is non-compliant,  enforcement officers will detain the consignment Allowing E-Way Bill to expire during transit: Without a valid extension, an expired E-Way Bill is treated the same as no E-Way Bill,  goods are subject to detention and penalty Not generating E-Way Bill for branch transfers: Transfers between two GST registrations of the same company are taxable supplies,  E-Way Bill is required above ₹50,000 even for intra-company movements

## Frequently asked questions

**Our goods are valued at ₹45,000 on the invoice. Do we still need an E-Way Bill if we add GST, making the total ₹53,100?** The consignment value for E-Way Bill threshold purposes is the invoice value including GST (taxable value + CGST + SGST/IGST + cess). If the total invoice value including GST exceeds ₹50,000, an E-Way Bill is required regardless of whether the pre-tax value was below ₹50,000. In your example, ₹53,100 (total with GST) exceeds the ₹50,000 threshold,  an E-Way Bill is required.

**We sell through a marketplace model (seller ships to customer directly). Who generates the E-Way Bill,  us, the marketplace, or the customer?** In a marketplace model, E-Way Bill can be generated by the supplier (seller), the recipient, or the transporter,  whoever initiates the movement. In bill-to-ship-to scenarios (where the marketplace invoices the end customer but the supplier ships to the customer), both the supplier's invoice and the marketplace's invoice are involved. CBIC has clarified that a single E-Way Bill referencing either invoice is acceptable. In practice, most marketplace platforms have integrated E-Way Bill generation into their systems,  sellers should confirm the specific arrangement with the marketplace.

**What happens if our driver is stopped at a check-point and the E-Way Bill is valid but the vehicle number is different from what's on the E-Way Bill?** This is a common situation during transhipment or vehicle breakdown. If the vehicle number on the E-Way Bill doesn't match the actual vehicle, the officer can detain the goods. To avoid this, update Part B on the E-Way Bill portal with the new vehicle number before the replacement vehicle commences the journey. In case of an unplanned vehicle change (breakdown), update the vehicle number on the portal as soon as possible and show the update to the officer,  this demonstrates compliance and typically resolves the issue without penalty.

**Is an E-Way Bill required for goods sent for job work?** Yes. Movement of goods for job work (from principal to job worker and back) requires an E-Way Bill if the value of goods exceeds ₹50,000. The document used is a delivery challan (not a tax invoice, since this is not a supply). The E-Way Bill is generated based on the delivery challan. One exception: for intra-state movement between a principal and a registered job worker within 50 km, some states have exempted the E-Way Bill requirement,  check your specific state's notification.

**Our E-Way Bill expired while the goods were in transit due to a flood in the route. What do we do?** Exceptional circumstances like natural calamities, floods, and accidents are grounds for E-Way Bill extension. The generator or transporter can extend the E-Way Bill validity through the portal,  the extension must be initiated within 8 hours of expiry in case of force majeure circumstances. In practice, if the portal extension was not done in time due to the emergency, the transporter should carry evidence of the natural calamity (news reports, government advisories, route blockage certificates) and present them to the enforcement officer. The officer has discretion to consider such circumstances,  and a DRC-01 representation can be filed if goods are detained unfairly.

packages.name: Custom
packages.description: Ongoing support packages available for regular logistics‑heavy businesses.

services.name: GST Return Filing
services.shortDescription: Regular monthly/quarterly GST return preparation and filing.
services.content:
# Gst Return Filing

## Overview

GST Return Filing is the periodic submission of transaction data by GST-registered taxpayers to the GST portal, declaring their outward supplies (sales), inward supplies (purchases), Input Tax Credit claimed, and tax paid for each return period. GST returns form the backbone of the GST compliance system,  they are the primary mechanism through which the government tracks tax liability, ITC claims, and tax payments across the entire economy. The GST return filing ecosystem comprises multiple forms, each serving a specific purpose: Return Form Purpose Filed By Frequency GSTR-1 Outward supply details Regular taxpayers Monthly (if turnover > ₹5 crore or opted out of QRMP) / Quarterly (QRMP scheme) GSTR-3B Summary return,  tax payment Regular taxpayers Monthly / Quarterly (QRMP) GSTR-2B Auto-populated ITC statement System-generated Monthly GSTR-4 Annual return (composition) Composition scheme dealers Annually GSTR-5 Return for NRTP Non-resident taxable persons Monthly GSTR-5A Return for OIDAR providers Foreign OIDAR suppliers Monthly GSTR-6 ISD return Input Service Distributors Monthly GSTR-7 TDS return TDS deductors Monthly GSTR-8 TCS return E-commerce operators Monthly GSTR-9 Annual return Regular taxpayers Annually GSTR-10 Final return Cancelled registrations Within 3 months of cancellation GSTR-11 Return for UIN holders Entities with Unique Identity Number Monthly (on purchase) IFF Invoice Furnishing Facility QRMP quarterly filers Optional, monthly

## Applicability

GST Return Filing is mandatory for every GST-registered entity: Taxpayer Type Applicable Returns Regular taxpayer (monthly) GSTR-1 (monthly) + GSTR-3B (monthly) Regular taxpayer (QRMP scheme) GSTR-1 (quarterly) + GSTR-3B (quarterly) + IFF (optional monthly) Composition scheme dealer CMP-08 (quarterly payment) + GSTR-4 (annual) Non-resident taxable person GSTR-5 (monthly) OIDAR provider GSTR-5A (monthly) Input Service Distributor GSTR-6 (monthly) TDS deductor GSTR-7 (monthly) E-commerce operator (TCS) GSTR-8 (monthly) All regular taxpayers (turnover above ₹2 crore) GSTR-9 (annual)

NIL Return Filing: Even if there is no business activity in a month, NIL returns must be filed for GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B,  failure to file NIL returns attracts the same late fee as non-filing of regular returns.

## Features

QRMP scheme: Quarterly Return Monthly Payment,  eligible taxpayers with turnover up to ₹5 crore can file GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B quarterly while making monthly tax payments GSTR-2B auto-population: ITC available to the taxpayer is auto-populated in GSTR-3B based on GSTR-2B,  reducing manual data entry Invoice matching: The GST system automatically reconciles GSTR-1 filed by suppliers with GSTR-2B of recipients,  mismatches flag potential ITC discrepancies E-invoicing integration: For businesses under e-invoicing mandate, invoice data flows directly from the IRP (Invoice Registration Portal) to GSTR-1,  reducing errors Late fee structure: Late filing attracts ₹50/day for regular returns (₹20/day for NIL returns), subject to caps based on turnover Interest on late payment: Interest at 18% p.a. is levied on the net tax liability unpaid by the due date,  applies automatically Blocked ITC under Rule 86B: Taxpayers with taxable turnover above ₹50 lakh in a month cannot use more than 99% of ITC for tax payment,  1% must be paid in cash HSN summary mandatory in GSTR-1: HSN-wise summary of outward supplies is mandatory in GSTR-1 based on turnover-linked thresholds

## Benefits

ITC claim mechanism: GSTR-3B is the vehicle through which ITC is claimed,  accurate and timely filing ensures ITC is accessed and utilized for tax payment Clean compliance record: Regular, timely GST return filing builds a clean compliance rating,  important for bank loans, government tenders, and large enterprise contracts Avoid late fees and interest: Returns filed by due dates avoid late fees (₹50/day) and the significant interest burden (18% p.a.) on unpaid tax Supplier relationship management: GSTR-1 filed by suppliers directly impacts recipients' GSTR-2B,  timely filing helps recipients' ITC and business relationships GST portal-based reconciliation tools: The portal's ITC comparison between GSTR-2B and GSTR-3B helps identify supplier non-compliance early Audit readiness: Consistent, accurate GST return filing creates a clean audit trail that simplifies statutory audits, bank audits, and GST department audits

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Sales invoices / tax invoices | All outward supply invoices for the period |
| Purchase invoices | All inward supply invoices for ITC claim |
| GSTR-2B (auto-populated) | ITC available as per supplier filings |
| Credit notes and debit notes | Adjustments to supply values |
| RCM invoices | For reverse charge mechanism payments |
| Export documents | Shipping bills, FIRC (for services) |
| E-Way Bill data | Cross-reference with invoice data |
| Advance receipts | GST on advances received must be declared |
| ITC register | Running ledger of ITC claimed and utilized |
| Previous month GSTR-3B | For reference and continuity |

## Process

1. Data Compilation Compile all sales and purchase invoices for the return period. Reconcile with GSTR-2B for ITC verification.

2. Prepare GSTR-1 Enter all outward supply details in GSTR-1, B2B invoices, B2C invoices, exports, credit notes, debit notes, and HSN summary. File GSTR-1 by the due date (11th of following month for monthly; 13th of following quarter for QRMP).

3. Reconcile ITC with GSTR-2B Compare ITC available in GSTR-2B with actual purchase invoices. Chase up suppliers whose invoices are missing from GSTR-2B. Claim only GSTR-2B-reflected ITC in GSTR-3B (to avoid notices under Rule 36(4)).

4. Prepare GSTR-3B Summarise all outward supplies (Table 3.1), eligible ITC (Table 4), and ineligible/reversed ITC (Table 4D). Compute net tax liability.

5. Pay Tax Pay the net tax liability (after setting off ITC) through the Electronic Cash Ledger, CGST, SGST/IGST, and cess separately. Pay any interest on late payment.

6. File GSTR-3B Submit GSTR-3B on the GST portal using DSC or EVC. GSTR-3B is due by the 20th of the following month (for monthly filers); 22nd/24th for quarterly QRMP filers.

7. Download Filed Returns Download the ARN-confirmed copies of filed GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B. Archive for compliance records. Fees


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B (monthly, small business) ₹1,500 – ₹3,000/month GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B (monthly, medium business) ₹3,000 – ₹8,000/month GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B (monthly, large business) ₹8,000 – ₹25,000/month QRMP scheme filing (quarterly) ₹3,000 – ₹10,000/quarter NIL return filing ₹500 – ₹1,000/month Annual GST compliance retainer ₹20,000 – ₹2,00,000 p.a.

Government Late Fees Amount GSTR-1 late fee ₹50/day (₹20/day for NIL),  max varies by turnover GSTR-3B late fee ₹50/day (₹20/day for NIL),  max varies by turnover

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance GSTR-1 must be filed before GSTR-3B for the same period,  GSTR-3B without corresponding GSTR-1 creates data mismatches ITC in GSTR-3B must not exceed ITC in GSTR-2B as a rule,  excess claims attract notices File NIL returns for inactive months,  non-filing blocks GSTR-1 filing for subsequent periods E-invoicing compliance is mandatory above prescribed thresholds,  e-invoice IRN must be generated before the invoice is issued RCM liability must be self-assessed and paid in GSTR-3B,  RCM ITC is available only after payment

## Common Mistakes

Claiming ITC without GSTR-2B reflection: Claiming ITC for invoices not reflected in GSTR-2B,  creates discrepancy notices under Rule 36(4) and possible ITC reversal demand Not declaring all outward supplies in GSTR-1: Missing invoices in GSTR-1,  recipients cannot claim ITC, damaging business relationships and potentially attracting GST audit Incorrectly applying IGST vs. CGST+SGST: Wrong tax head applied,  e.g., inter-state supply taxed as intra-state,  requires rectification in subsequent returns Treating advance receipts as not taxable: GST is payable on advances received for goods (where applicable),  not declaring advances is a common compliance gap Filing GSTR-3B before paying tax: GSTR-3B should be filed only after the cash balance in the Electronic Cash Ledger is sufficient to cover the net tax liability

## Frequently asked questions

**We are under the QRMP scheme. Do we still need to pay GST monthly?** Yes. Under the QRMP (Quarterly Return Monthly Payment) scheme, GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B are filed quarterly,  but tax must be paid monthly for the first two months of each quarter (using the Fixed Sum Method,  35% of the previous quarter's tax,  or the Self-Assessment Method based on actual transactions). In the third month of the quarter, the full GSTR-3B is filed with the balance tax paid. Monthly payment is mandatory regardless of quarterly filing,  delaying payment until the quarterly filing attracts interest at 18% p.a.

**Our vendor hasn't filed their GSTR-1 for 3 months. Can we still claim ITC on their invoices?** As per Rule 36(4) of the CGST Rules, ITC can only be claimed on invoices reflected in GSTR-2B,  which depends on the supplier filing their GSTR-1. If the supplier hasn't filed GSTR-1, their invoices won't appear in your GSTR-2B, and you cannot claim the ITC (without risk of reversal). You should follow up with the supplier to file their GSTR-1 and reflect your invoices. If the supplier consistently fails to file, consider it a significant vendor compliance risk,  such suppliers create ITC exposure for their buyers. Switching to compliant vendors is the most effective long-term solution.

**We accidentally filed GSTR-3B with incorrect figures for last month. Can we revise it?** GSTR-3B cannot be revised once filed. However, corrections can be made in the next month's GSTR-3B,  by adjusting the figures in the relevant tables to account for the correction. For example, if you over-claimed ITC last month, reduce the ITC claim in the current month's GSTR-3B by the excess amount and pay interest on the over-claimed ITC from the date of the incorrect filing. For material errors, it is advisable to use DRC-03 to voluntarily pay any additional tax liability,  demonstrating good faith compliance before any notice is received.

**Is GST return filing required even if we have no sales for the month?** Yes. Every GST-registered taxpayer must file all applicable returns,  GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B,  even if there is zero business activity for the month. These are called NIL returns. Non-filing of NIL returns attracts late fees (₹20/day for NIL returns,  ₹10 CGST + ₹10 SGST). Additionally, not filing GSTR-1 blocks the filing of the next GSTR-3B. Persistent non-filing leads to system-generated notices and can trigger compulsory cancellation of GST registration by the officer.

**How do we handle a credit note issued to a B2C customer?** Where is it reported? Credit notes issued to unregistered (B2C) customers are reported in GSTR-1 in Table 9B (Credit and Debit Notes for Unregistered Persons). The credit note reduces the taxable value and the GST previously declared for the relevant invoice period. Note that unlike B2B credit notes (where the recipient reverses ITC), B2C credit notes don't trigger ITC reversal on the recipient's end (since they are unregistered). However, the supplier can reduce the output tax liability by the GST amount on the credit note,  this adjustment is reflected in GSTR-3B for the month in which the credit note is issued.

services.name: Input Tax Credit Reconciliation
services.shortDescription: GST input tax credit reconciliation between books and portal.
services.content:
# Input Tax Credit Reconciliation

## Overview

Input Tax Credit (ITC) Reconciliation is the process of matching and verifying the ITC claimed in GSTR-3B returns with the ITC available in the auto-populated GSTR-2B statement, and further reconciling both with the actual purchase register (books of accounts). ITC reconciliation is one of the most critical and compliance-intensive aspects of GST management,  errors in ITC claims are the single largest source of GST demand notices, interest liabilities, and penalties across India. Under Section 16 of the CGST Act, ITC can be availed only if: The taxpayer holds a valid tax invoice Goods/services have been received The supplier has filed their GSTR-1 (and the invoice appears in GSTR-2B) The tax has actually been paid by the supplier The annual return (GSTR-9) has been filed Rule 36(4) of the CGST Rules restricts ITC to what is reflected in GSTR-2B,  any excess claim is subject to automatic interest and reversal. Rule 42 and Rule 43 govern proportionate reversal of common ITC used for both taxable and exempt supplies. ITC reconciliation is required at multiple levels: Monthly: GSTR-2B vs. purchase register vs. GSTR-3B ITC claimed Annual: Full-year ITC reconciliation for GSTR-9 preparation Special: Reconciliation for ITC refund claims, GST audits, and M&A due diligence

## Applicability

ITC Reconciliation is relevant for: All regular GST-registered businesses that claim ITC Businesses with significant purchase volumes and multiple suppliers Companies with both taxable and exempt supplies (requiring proportionate ITC reversal) Exporters claiming ITC refunds Companies undergoing GST audits or scrutiny Businesses preparing GSTR-9 and GSTR-9C annual returns Companies involved in M&A transactions,  as buyers conduct ITC due diligence on acquisition targets Applicable pan-India,  all states and UTs.

## Features

Three-way reconciliation: Purchase register (books) vs. GSTR-2B (portal) vs. GSTR-3B claimed (returns),  the three must align Supplier-level tracking: Reconciliation identifies specific suppliers whose invoices are missing from GSTR-2B,  enabling targeted follow-up Automated tools available: Reconciliation software (like ClearTax, Zoho Books, Tally GST modules) can automate matching of large invoice volumes,  reducing manual effort ITC reversal computation: Where ITC has been over-claimed, reconciliation quantifies the reversal amount and the applicable interest Section 17(5) blocked ITC identification: Reconciliation identifies ITC on blocked categories (motor vehicles, entertainment, personal expenses) that should never have been claimed GSTR-2B timing: GSTR-2B is generated on the 14th of each month,  the cutoff for inclusion is supplier filing by the 11th; invoices filed after the 11th appear in the next month's GSTR-2B

## Benefits

Interest and penalty prevention: Proactive monthly reconciliation prevents excess ITC claims that automatically attract 18% p.a. interest,  catching differences early is far cheaper than addressing them after receiving a demand notice Supplier compliance management: ITC reconciliation identifies non-compliant suppliers (who don't file GSTR-1 on time),  enabling businesses to switch to compliant vendors and protect their ITC GSTR-9 preparation readiness: Businesses that reconcile monthly face minimal surprises during annual GSTR-9 preparation,  the year-end work is lighter and faster Cash flow optimisation: Maximising legitimate ITC claims (without excess) reduces effective cash out-of-pocket GST costs,  every ₹1 of ITC left unclaimed increases cash GST outflow Audit defense: Documented ITC reconciliation workings are the primary defense during GST audits,  showing that ITC was claimed only on the basis of GSTR-2B and with proper verification Refund claim accuracy: For ITC refund claims (LUT route for exporters, inverted duty), accurate ITC reconciliation ensures refund calculations are correct,  preventing deficiency memos

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| GSTR-2B (monthly, all periods) | Auto-populated ITC statement from portal |
| Purchase register / ledger | Internal book records of all purchases with GST |
| GSTR-3B filed copies | ITC claimed in each month's return |
| Supplier-wise GSTR-1 filing status | For identifying non-filing suppliers |
| Tax invoices from suppliers | Original invoices for ITC eligibility verification |
| ITC register (maintained internally) | Running ledger of ITC claimed, reversed, and balance |
| Rule 42/43 computation | Proportionate reversal workings (for mixed supply businesses) |
| Credit notes received | For ITC reversal adjustments |
| Import of services invoices | For RCM-paid ITC claims |

## Process

1. Download GSTR-2B Download GSTR-2B for the reconciliation period (month/quarter/year) from the GST portal. This contains all ITC reflected based on supplier filings.

2. Extract Purchase Register Export the purchase register from your accounting software, with invoice number, supplier GSTIN, invoice date, taxable value, and tax amounts.

3. Three-Way Matching Match each invoice in the purchase register against GSTR-2B. Categorize as: (a) Matched, invoice in both books and GSTR-2B; (b) In books but not in GSTR-2B, supplier hasn't filed; (c) In GSTR-2B but not in books, duplicate or unrecorded purchase.

4. Identify Discrepancies Quantify the ITC difference: invoices in books but not in GSTR-2B represent ITC that cannot be claimed without risk. Invoices in GSTR-2B but not in books need to be verified and recorded.

5. Supplier Follow-Up Contact suppliers whose invoices are missing from GSTR-2B, request them to file their GSTR-1 and include the missing invoices. Set a timeline (before the next GSTR-2B cutoff).

6. ITC Eligibility Review Review all matched invoices for ITC eligibility, confirm goods/services received, invoice is valid, and ITC is not blocked under Section 17(5). Remove blocked ITC from the claim.

7. Rule 42/43 Reversal (if applicable) For businesses with exempt supplies, compute the proportionate ITC reversal under Rule 42/43 and apply to the ITC claim.

8. Reconcile with GSTR-3B Compare the final eligible ITC figure (from steps 1–7) with the ITC claimed in GSTR-3B. Quantify excess or deficit ITC claimed. Adjust in the next GSTR-3B if excess was claimed.

9. Document Reconciliation Working Maintain a documented reconciliation worksheet (Excel or software-generated) for each period, this is the audit trail for ITC claims. Fees


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Monthly ITC reconciliation (up to 100 invoices) ₹3,000 – ₹6,000/month Monthly ITC reconciliation (100–500 invoices) ₹6,000 – ₹15,000/month Annual ITC reconciliation (for GSTR-9) ₹15,000 – ₹50,000 ITC reconciliation for refund claim ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 ITC reconciliation for GST audit defense ₹20,000 – ₹75,000 ITC software setup and training ₹10,000 – ₹30,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance Reconcile GSTR-2B with purchase register before filing each month's GSTR-3B Do not claim ITC exceeding GSTR-2B limits without documented justification Apply Rule 42/43 reversal at least quarterly (monthly is best practice) Maintain ITC reconciliation workings for at least 6 years Follow up with non-compliant suppliers regularly,  persistent non-filers should be replaced with compliant vendors

## Common Mistakes

Claiming ITC on blocked categories: ITC on motor vehicles (with exceptions), foods and beverages, health club memberships, club memberships, and personal expenses is blocked under Section 17(5),  claiming it invites reversal plus interest Not following up with non-filing suppliers: Assuming that GSTR-2B will eventually catch up,  if the supplier never files GSTR-2B, the ITC never materialises and may be reversed with interest Not reversing ITC on credit notes: When a supplier issues a credit note and it reflects in GSTR-2B as a negative ITC, businesses must reduce the ITC claim accordingly,  missing this results in excess ITC Claiming GSTR-2B ITC without receiving goods: GST law requires actual receipt of goods/services as a condition for ITC,  claiming ITC on advance payment invoices (before receipt) is a violation Forgetting RCM ITC: ITC on RCM (Reverse Charge Mechanism) payments is available only in the same period the RCM GST is paid,  not on a cash basis or accrual basis from earlier periods

## Frequently asked questions

**Our supplier has gone out of business and will never file their GSTR-1. We have paid them and have their invoice. Can we claim ITC?** This is a challenging situation. Technically, under Rule 36(4), ITC can only be claimed to the extent reflected in GSTR-2B,  and if the supplier never files GSTR-1, the invoice will never appear. Some businesses have contested this in High Courts, arguing that the denial of ITC to a bona fide buyer due to supplier default violates fundamental rights,  and some courts have given relief in such cases. In practice, claim the ITC with proper documentation (invoice, payment proof), disclose the situation in GSTR-9, and be prepared to contest any reversal demand legally. Document the legitimate nature of the purchase thoroughly.

**GSTR-2B shows ₹5 lakh ITC from a supplier, but we only have invoices worth ₹4.5 lakh in our books. What should we do with the extra ₹50,000 in GSTR-2B?** The ₹50,000 surplus in GSTR-2B could be a duplicate invoice filing by the supplier, an invoice for a different purchase period, or an error in the supplier's GSTR-1. Investigate by contacting the supplier and reviewing your purchase records. Do not claim the extra ₹50,000 unless you can match it to a specific eligible invoice in your books. Claiming ITC without a corresponding valid invoice is a violation under Section 16,  the GSTR-2B reflection is necessary but not sufficient; you also need a valid invoice and actual receipt of goods/services.

**We forgot to claim ITC for some invoices from 3 months ago. Can we still claim it?** Yes, subject to time limits. ITC can be claimed up to the earlier of: (a) the due date of filing GSTR-3B for September of the financial year following the year in which the invoice was issued; or (b) the date of filing of annual return (GSTR-9) for the relevant financial year. So ITC on invoices from FY 2024-25 can be claimed up to September 2025's GSTR-3B (filed October 20, 2025) or the date of filing GSTR-9 for FY 2024-25,  whichever is earlier. Beyond this deadline, the ITC lapses,  making timely monthly reconciliation essential.

**We have a mix of taxable and GST-exempt exports. How do we calculate the ITC reversal for exempt supplies?** Rule 42 of the CGST Rules governs ITC reversal for common inputs/input services used for both taxable and exempt supplies. The formula: Reversal = (Exempt supply turnover / Total turnover) × Common ITC for the period. "Exempt supply" for this purpose includes both GST-exempt supplies AND nil-rated supplies but does not include zero-rated supplies (exports),  exports are zero-rated and do not trigger Rule 42 reversal. This is a critical distinction,  ITC used for exports is fully available, while ITC used for exempt domestic supplies must be reversed. The computation is done monthly with an annual true-up.

**Our company was recently acquired. The new parent wants to verify our ITC history. What documents should we prepare?** For ITC due diligence in an M&A context, prepare: (a) month-wise ITC reconciliation workings for the past 3–5 years,  GSTR-2B vs. GSTR-3B vs. purchase register; (b) summary of blocked ITC categories and proof that none was claimed; (c) Rule 42/43 reversal computations for all years; (d) list of suppliers with consistently missing GSTR-2B data,  and status of follow-up; (e) GSTR-9 and GSTR-9C for past filed years; (f) any ITC reversal demands received and their resolution status; and (g) pending ITC claims not yet reflected in GSTR-2B. Well-maintained ITC records signal compliance maturity and reduce the risk of post-acquisition surprises.

services.name: GST Registration Cancellation
services.shortDescription: Closure and cancellation of GST registrations.
services.content:
# Gst Registration Cancellation

## Overview

GST Registration Cancellation is the formal process of surrendering a GSTIN,  either voluntarily by the taxpayer or compulsorily by the GST officer. Upon cancellation, the taxpayer is no longer required to file GST returns or pay GST going forward. However, the cancellation process involves important closing obligations,  including filing pending returns, reversing ITC on remaining stock, and paying the tax on closing inventory. Voluntary Cancellation (initiated by the taxpayer) is done in Form GST REG-16 and is applicable when: The business is closed, shut down, or transferred The business has been deregistered or amalgamated with another entity The taxpayer's turnover has permanently fallen below the registration threshold The business has changed its constitution (e.g., sole proprietor converting to company,  old GSTIN is cancelled, new one registered) Compulsory Cancellation (initiated by the GST officer) under Section 29(2) of the CGST Act occurs when: The taxpayer has not filed returns for 6 consecutive months (regular taxpayer) The taxpayer has not filed returns for 3 consecutive quarters (composition taxpayer) The taxpayer obtained registration by fraud, misrepresentation, or suppression of facts The taxpayer does not conduct business from the declared place of business After cancellation, if the taxpayer wishes to resume business and GST compliance, they can apply for Revocation of Cancellation (covered in a separate service page).

## Applicability

GST Registration Cancellation applies to: Businesses shutting down or winding up operations Businesses transferred through slump sale, merger, or demerger (seller's GSTIN is cancelled) Proprietorships where the proprietor has died and business is not being continued Businesses whose turnover has permanently fallen below the GST threshold Entities changing their legal form (e.g., proprietor to company,  old GSTIN cancelled, new registered) Businesses that registered voluntarily but no longer wish to remain registered Applicable pan-India,  all GST-registered entities across all states and UTs.

## Features

Voluntary cancellation via GST REG-16: Taxpayer-initiated cancellation application filed on the GST portal 30-day processing: GST officer must process the cancellation application within 30 days of receipt Final return filing: Upon cancellation, the taxpayer must file GSTR-10 (Final Return) within 3 months of the cancellation effective date or order date ITC reversal on closing stock: At the time of cancellation, the taxpayer must pay an amount equal to ITC on closing stock of inputs, semi-finished goods, and finished goods (or output tax thereon,  whichever is higher),  this is a critical and often overlooked obligation Cancellation effective date: Voluntary cancellations can be effective from a future date chosen by the taxpayer or the date of filing the application,  the officer determines the effective date Suspension during proceedings: The GST officer may first suspend the registration (GST REG-17) and issue a show-cause notice before compulsory cancellation Interest on pending liability: Any GST liability pending at the time of cancellation must be paid with interest before or at the time of filing the final return

## Benefits

End of compliance obligations: Once cancelled, no further GST returns need to be filed,  reducing administrative burden and professional fees for businesses that have ceased operations Clean closure: A formally cancelled GSTIN creates a clean regulatory record,  important for legal closure of the business entity Avoid penalties for non-filing: If a business has ceased operations but the GSTIN remains active, non-filing of returns attracts penalties,  cancellation stops this penalty accumulation Financial clarity: The ITC reversal and final return process creates a clear accounting of all GST obligations at closure,  preventing future demands No GST liability post-cancellation: After effective cancellation date, GST is neither charged on supplies nor is return filing required,  legal and financial certainty

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form GST REG-16 (online) | Voluntary cancellation application |
| Reason for cancellation | Business closure, deregistration, constitution change, etc. |
| Address of cancellation reason entity | For transfers,  details of the entity to which business was transferred |
| Details of closing stock | Quantity and value of inputs, semi-finished and finished goods |
| Stock as on date of cancellation | For ITC reversal computation |
| All pending GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B | All pending returns must be filed before cancellation application |
| Proof of business closure (if applicable) | Dissolution deed, winding up order, merger documentation |

## Process

1. File All Pending Returns Before applying for cancellation, ensure all pending GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns are filed up to the period immediately before the desired cancellation date.

2. Compute ITC Reversal on Closing Stock Calculate the ITC reversal obligation on the closing stock of inputs, semi-finished goods, and finished goods as on the cancellation date. The reversal amount is the ITC attributable to closing stock or output tax thereon, whichever is higher.

3. File GST REG-16 Log in to the GST portal. Go to Registration → Application for Cancellation. Complete Form GST REG-16, select reason, enter cancellation effective date, provide closing stock details, and the ITC reversal amount.

4. Pay ITC Reversal Amount If the ITC reversal amount is payable (i.e., credit ledger balance is insufficient), pay the amount through DRC-03 or in GSTR-3B for the final period.

5. GST Officer Processing The officer reviews the cancellation application within 30 days. If satisfied, issues Form GST REG-19 (Cancellation Order) specifying the effective date of cancellation.

6. File GSTR-10 (Final Return) After cancellation order is issued, file GSTR-10 (Final Return) within 3 months of the cancellation date or order date. GSTR-10 confirms the ITC reversal on closing stock and finalises all GST obligations.

7. Archive GSTIN Records Maintain all GST records (returns, invoices, ITC registers) for 6 years from the date of cancellation, GST authorities can still audit past periods. Fees Service Government Fee GST Cancellation Application Nil


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee GST cancellation application filing ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Pending returns filing before cancellation ₹500 – ₹2,000 per return ITC reversal computation ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 GSTR-10 (Final Return) filing ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 End-to-end GST closure package ₹8,000 – ₹20,000

Government Penalties Amount Late filing of GSTR-10 ₹200/day (₹100 CGST + ₹100 SGST), max ₹10,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance File GSTR-10 (Final Return) within 3 months of the effective date of cancellation,  non-filing attracts penalties All GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B must be filed before the cancellation application ITC reversal on closing stock is a mandatory obligation,  not optional After cancellation, do not collect GST from customers,  issuing a tax invoice after the effective date of cancellation and collecting GST is an offence Maintain all records for 6 years from the date of the last return filed

## Common Mistakes

Applying for cancellation without filing all pending returns: The GST portal will not process the cancellation application if there are pending returns Not computing ITC reversal on closing stock: Cancelling without reversing ITC on closing stock leaves a pending liability that the department can demand with interest later Forgetting to file GSTR-10: The final return (GSTR-10) is separate from the regular returns and is a mandatory post-cancellation filing,  widely overlooked by businesses Collecting GST after the effective cancellation date: After cancellation, any GST collected from customers must still be deposited with the government,  but continuing to charge GST without a valid GSTIN is an offence Not maintaining records after cancellation: GST records must be maintained for 6 years even after the GSTIN is cancelled,  audits of past periods are possible

## Frequently asked questions

**Our company is being liquidated under the Companies Act. When and how should we cancel our GSTIN?** In a voluntary liquidation, the company's GST registration should be cancelled once the decision to wind up is taken and all business operations have ceased. The liquidator is responsible for filing the cancellation application (GST REG-16) on behalf of the company, filing all pending GST returns, paying all outstanding GST liabilities, reversing ITC on closing stock, and filing GSTR-10. The GST cancellation should ideally be coordinated with the final settlement of assets and liabilities under the liquidation,  as the ITC reversal on closing stock depends on the inventory position at the time of cancellation.

**We have ₹8 lakh in the Electronic Credit Ledger. Can we get a refund of this ITC credit before cancellation?** Yes. If you have excess ITC in the Electronic Credit Ledger that exceeds your outstanding GST liabilities, you can apply for a refund of the excess credit under Section 54(1) of the CGST Act,  "refund of balance in the electronic credit ledger." File Form RFD-01 for this refund before applying for cancellation. Alternatively, apply for refund and cancellation simultaneously,  the refund will be processed as part of the cancellation settlement. Do not cancel first and then try to claim the refund,  it becomes significantly more complex after cancellation.

**We transferred our business to a new company. Do we need to cancel the old GSTIN?** Yes. When a business is transferred,  through slump sale, demerger, or any other mechanism,  the transferor's GSTIN must be cancelled as the business entity ceases to operate under the old structure. The transferee (new company) must either register for a new GSTIN or include the transferred business in its existing GST registration. In the cancellation application (GST REG-16), the reason cited would be "Transfer of business" and the details of the transferee entity should be provided. ITC balances can be transferred to the transferee under the GST ITC transfer provisions on merger/demerger.

**Can I cancel GST registration on a specific future date?** Yes. In Form GST REG-16, the applicant can specify the desired date of cancellation,  which can be a future date. The GST officer, upon processing, may accept this date or fix a different effective date. Common scenarios for future-dated cancellation include: businesses that want to complete all current orders/contracts under the existing GSTIN before closure; businesses that want to ensure all annual returns (GSTR-9) are filed before the GSTIN is cancelled; and businesses timing the cancellation to align with their financial year-end for cleaner accounting.

**If my GSTIN is cancelled, can I still claim ITC on invoices received before the cancellation date?** The right to claim ITC on invoices received before the cancellation date depends on whether those invoices were reflected in GSTR-3B before cancellation. Generally, ITC must be claimed in the GSTR-3B for the period in which the supply was received,  so ITC on pre-cancellation invoices should have been claimed in the regular GSTR-3Bs filed before the cancellation. After the final return (GSTR-10) is filed, there is no mechanism to claim additional ITC. This is another reason to ensure all pending ITC is captured in the last GSTR-3B before the cancellation effective date.

services.name: GST Registration Revocation
services.shortDescription: Revocation of compulsory GST cancellation, Form REG-21 and restoration of GSTIN.
services.content:
# Gst Registration Revocation

## Overview

GST Registration Revocation is the process of reinstating a GST registration that has been cancelled by the GST officer on a suo motu basis (compulsory cancellation). When a GST officer cancels a taxpayer's registration,  typically due to non-filing of returns for 6 or more consecutive months (regular taxpayer) or 3 consecutive quarters (composition dealer),  the taxpayer can apply for revocation of cancellation within 90 days of the cancellation order, to resume their GST registration and compliance obligations. Revocation is specifically a remedy against officer-initiated (compulsory) cancellation,  it is not available for voluntary cancellation (where the taxpayer themselves applied for cancellation in Form GST REG-16). If a taxpayer voluntarily cancelled their registration and wants to re-register, they must apply for fresh GST registration. The revocation process under Section 30 of the CGST Act requires the taxpayer to: (a) file all pending GST returns up to the date of cancellation order; (b) pay all pending tax, interest, and late fees; and (c) file the revocation application in Form GST REG-21 within the prescribed period. The GST Council has periodically announced amnesty schemes extending the revocation deadline and waiving late fees,  businesses with cancelled GSTINs should monitor such schemes to take advantage of relaxed conditions.

## Applicability

GST Registration Revocation is applicable for: Taxpayers whose GST registration was cancelled by the officer under Section 29(2) of the CGST Act for: Non-filing of returns for 6 consecutive months (regular taxpayers) Non-filing of returns for 3 consecutive quarters (composition scheme) Failure to commence business within 6 months of registration (for voluntary registrants) Non-existence at the declared principal place of business Obtaining registration through fraud or misrepresentation Not available for: Voluntary cancellations (taxpayer applied for REG-16),  must apply for fresh registration Cancellations pursuant to court/tribunal orders without specific revocation provisions Applicable pan-India,  all states and UTs.

## Features

90-day revocation window: The application for revocation must be filed within 90 days of the service of the cancellation order,  this is the standard statutory period Extended timelines (GST Council discretion): CBIC/GST Council has extended revocation timelines on multiple occasions through special amnesty schemes,  monitor CBIC notifications Form GST REG-21: The revocation application is filed online on the GST portal using Form GST REG-21 Prior compliance mandatory: All pending GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns (up to the effective date of cancellation) must be filed before the revocation application can be submitted on the portal Tax, interest, and late fee payment: All outstanding tax liability, interest (18% p.a.), and late fees must be paid before or at the time of revocation application 30-day officer processing: The GST officer must process the revocation application within 30 working days and either revoke the cancellation or issue a show-cause notice GSTIN restoration: Upon revocation, the original GSTIN is restored,  the taxpayer can resume operations, file returns, and claim ITC from the period after revocation Commissioner's discretion: In certain cases (after the initial 90-day window), the GST Commissioner can allow revocation on application,  subject to satisfactory explanation for the delay

## Benefits

GSTIN restoration without fresh registration: Revocation restores the original GSTIN,  the taxpayer does not lose their registration history, ITC balance in the credit ledger, or B2B invoice trail Business continuity: Revocation enables the taxpayer to resume legal GST-compliant business operations,  issue tax invoices, claim ITC, and file returns Avoidance of fresh registration hassles: A fresh registration application involves re-registering with all documents,  revocation avoids this effort by restoring the existing registration Supplier relationship restoration: B2B buyers require a valid GSTIN from their supplier,  revocation restores the GSTIN and enables buyers to continue claiming ITC on the supplier's invoices E-commerce platform reinstatement: E-commerce platforms (Amazon, Flipkart) suspend sellers with cancelled GSTINs,  revocation enables reinstatement of the seller account GST Council amnesty schemes: During amnesty periods, late fees are often waived,  making revocation even more cost-effective for non-compliant taxpayers

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form GST REG-21 (online) | Revocation application on GST portal |
| Cancellation order (GST REG-19) | The officer's cancellation order,  contains the effective cancellation date |
| All pending GSTR-1 returns (filed) | Must be filed before revocation application |
| All pending GSTR-3B returns (filed) | Must be filed and tax/interest paid before revocation application |
| Payment challans for all pending tax and interest | Evidence of payment of outstanding dues |
| Late fee payment proof | All late fees paid |
| Reason for non-filing (explanation) | To be stated in the revocation application |
| Supporting documentation (if applicable) | For illness, litigation, or other genuine hardship,  supporting documents |

## Process

1. Identify the Cancellation Order Log in to the GST portal and locate the cancellation order (GST REG-19), note the effective date of cancellation and the date the order was served (as the 90-day clock runs from the date of service).

2. Compile Pending Returns Identify all unfiled GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns from the last filed period to the effective date of cancellation. This is often the primary obstacle, compiling months of pending data.

3. File All Pending GSTR-1 Returns File all pending GSTR-1 returns in chronological order (earliest period first). The portal requires sequential filing, a later period cannot be filed if an earlier period is pending.

4. File All Pending GSTR-3B Returns File all pending GSTR-3B returns, paying all tax liability, interest (18% p.a.), and late fees for each period. The portal calculates late fees automatically.

5. Pay Any Additional Dues Ensure the Electronic Cash Ledger has sufficient balance for all payments. Pay any shortfall through net banking/UPI.

6. File GST REG-21 Once all pending returns are filed and all dues are paid, navigate to Registration → Application for Revocation of Cancellation on the GST portal. File Form GST REG-21 with the reason for revocation.

7. Officer Processing The GST officer reviews the revocation application within 30 working days. The officer may: (a) revoke the cancellation (Form GST REG-22); or (b) issue a show-cause notice (Form GST REG-23) if not satisfied.

8. Respond to SCN (if issued) If GST REG-23 is issued, respond within the specified timeframe explaining the reasons for past non-compliance and providing assurance of future compliance. The officer then issues the final order.

9. Revocation Order (GST REG-22) Upon satisfactory processing, the officer issues GST REG-22 restoring the GSTIN. Update all business systems with the restored GSTIN. Fees Service Government Fee Revocation Application (GST REG-21) Nil Pending returns late fee (government) ₹20–₹50 per day per return (subject to amnesty)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee GST revocation application filing ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Pending returns filing (per return) ₹500 – ₹2,000 per return Bulk pending returns filing package ₹8,000 – ₹25,000 SCN response drafting ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 End-to-end revocation package ₹15,000 – ₹40,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%. Late fees paid to the government are additional.

Compliance File the revocation application within 90 days of the cancellation order service date All pending returns must be filed before the revocation application Pay all outstanding tax, interest (18% p.a.), and late fees before applying Monitor CBIC notifications for amnesty schemes that may waive late fees or extend the revocation timeline After revocation, maintain strict return filing compliance to prevent repeat cancellation

## Common Mistakes

Filing revocation application without filing all pending returns: The GST portal will not allow REG-21 submission if any GSTR-3B returns are pending,  the portal enforces this sequentially Missing the 90-day deadline: Not tracking the 90-day window from the cancellation order service date,  after 90 days, revocation requires Commissioner-level approval, which is more complex Not paying interest on pending tax: Paying only the principal tax without the 18% p.a. interest,  the returns cannot be filed (and hence revocation cannot proceed) without paying interest Confusing voluntary cancellation with compulsory cancellation: Attempting to file REG-21 for a voluntarily cancelled GSTIN,  revocation is only for compulsory (officer-initiated) cancellation Assuming revocation is automatic after filing returns: Filing all pending returns does not automatically restore the GSTIN,  the REG-21 revocation application must be separately filed after returns are filed

## Frequently asked questions

**Our GSTIN was cancelled 6 months ago and we missed the 90-day revocation window. Can we still get revocation?** After the initial 90-day window has passed, revocation requires approval from the Commissioner of GST (or a designated officer at the Commissionerate level). File a formal application to the Commissioner explaining the reasons for the delay in seeking revocation and the steps taken to file pending returns and pay all dues. The Commissioner has discretion to allow revocation beyond the 90-day period,  particularly where the taxpayer has genuine hardship reasons and has now brought their compliance fully current. Additionally, watch for CBIC amnesty schemes,  the GST Council has periodically announced special revocation windows with extended timelines and waived late fees.

**How much would the total cost be to revoke a GSTIN cancelled for non-filing of 12 months of returns?** The total cost depends on: (a) tax liability for the 12 months of unfiled returns,  this is the biggest variable and depends on actual business volumes; (b) interest at 18% p.a. on the unpaid tax from the due date of each return,  12 months of non-filing can result in substantial interest; (c) late fees per return,  approximately ₹50/day per return (capped at ₹10,000 per return under normal provisions, often reduced under amnesty schemes); (d) professional fees for filing 24 returns (12 GSTR-1 + 12 GSTR-3B) and the revocation application. For a business with ₹5 lakh/month in taxable supplies, the total outflow could easily be ₹10–₹20 lakh,  primarily from tax + interest + late fees.

**Can we continue operating our business using our PAN (without GSTIN) while the revocation is being processed?** Technically, a business that is required to be GST-registered (i.e., meets the mandatory registration criteria) cannot legally make taxable supplies without a valid GSTIN,  even while awaiting revocation. However, on a practical level, many businesses continue operations during the revocation period while keeping records of all supplies for retroactive reporting. If customers require a GSTIN for ITC purposes, they will not be able to claim ITC on your invoices during the period your GSTIN is cancelled,  which may affect B2B relationships significantly.

**After GSTIN revocation, will the ITC balance in my Electronic Credit Ledger be restored?** Yes. Upon revocation of cancellation (GST REG-22), the GSTIN is fully restored,  including the Electronic Credit Ledger balance that existed at the time of cancellation (subject to any reversals or adjustments made during the cancellation period). The ITC balance is not forfeited during compulsory cancellation,  it is effectively "frozen" and is restored upon revocation. However, any ITC that was incorrectly claimed before cancellation and was identified during the pending return filing process must be reversed,  reducing the effective balance.

**Our GSTIN was cancelled, but we have ongoing contracts with government clients who require a valid GSTIN. Can we get emergency/expedited revocation?** There is no specific "expedited" revocation mechanism under the CGST Act,  the standard processing time is 30 working days from the revocation application. However, where government contracts are at risk, a representation can be made to the jurisdictional Commissioner with documentation of the specific urgency (contract details, government client correspondence) to request priority processing. In parallel, file all pending returns and the revocation application immediately,  minimising the processing time by ensuring the application is complete and all dues are paid without any delays on your side.

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific GST advice should be obtained from qualified GST practitioners based on your business's specific facts, transactions, and state of operations.
