services.name: Private to Public Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Converting private limited companies into public limited companies.
services.content:
# Private to public limited company conversion

## Overview

A **private limited** company becomes a **public limited** company by altering its **MOA** and **AOA** under **Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013**. The change is used when the board plans a wider shareholder base, a future **IPO**, or contracts that need **"Limited"** without **"Private"** in the name.

> [!TIP] **When to use it**  
> You expect **more than 200** shareholders, want to issue a **prospectus** or build toward **BSE/NSE** listing, or need a **public** company profile for **large** tenders and **institutional** **comfort**.

## Key features

- **EGM** and **special resolution** (at least **75%** votes) for **conversion** and **M&A** change.
- **MGT-14** and **INC-27** (or the applicable current **MCA** e-form) with public-company **MOA/AOA**.
- **Minimum 7** shareholders and **3** directors before or when the new certificate is issued. **"Private"** is **dropped** from the legal name in the **normal** case.

| Area | Public company note |
|------|---------------------|
| Transfer of shares | Freer transfer; still read **class** and **agreements** for any **restriction** on a **set** of **securities** |
| Governance | Heavier: independent directors, secretarial audit and CSR at **stated** **thresholds** |
| Listing | **Converting** to public and **listing** are different; SEBI and exchanges **govern** an **IPO** when you **choose** that path |

## Benefits

- A **larger** shareholder base and a path to **prospectus**-based and **public** **capital** when you choose.
- **Same PAN and CIN** in the standard conversion, so **continuity** of **books** and **credits** on one **entity** id.
- **Name** line drops **"Private"**; rest of the **name** is unchanged unless you run a **separate** **name** change.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> Ongoing **compliance** is **higher** than for a pvt ltd. **SEBI/ exchange** and **DRHP** work apply **only** if you **list**; a **public** CIN without listing still has a **higher** **governance** bar than a **pvt** **ltd** at similar scale.

## Process

1. **Board** then **EGM** pass a **special resolution** for conversion to **public** and **M&A** amendment.
2. **File MGT-14** within the statutory window (commonly **30 days**; confirm the live form).
3. **File INC-27** with **amended** **MOA/AOA** for a **public** company.
4. Ensure at least **7** shareholders and **3** directors; use **DIR-2/DSC** and **DIR-12** as applicable for new appointees.
5. **RoC** issues an amended **COI**; **"Private"** is **removed** from the **registered** name (e.g. **ABC Private Limited** becomes **ABC Limited**).
6. **INC-20A** and other post-incorporation steps as applicable; **update** **GST**, **lenders**, and **KYC** with the new **M&A** and **name** line.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (pvt ltd) | Existing |
| MOA & AOA (pvt ltd) | To be replaced by public version |
| Altered MOA & AOA (public) | Drafted |
| Special resolution (EGM) | Passed |
| MGT-14 | MCA filing |
| INC-27 | Application for conversion |
| List of shareholders (min. 7) | Updated cap table |
| Consent of new directors (DIR-2) | As needed |
| DIR-12 (new directors) | If appointing new directors |
| Audited financial statements | Latest year |
| PAN, Aadhaar of new directors | KYC |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| INC-27 | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| MOA/AOA stamp duty | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 15,000 (state) |
| DIR-12 per new director | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| DSC per new director | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| Professional fees (CA/CS) | Rs. 15,000 to Rs. 50,000 |

## Tax impact (summary)

**Same** **entity**: **no** new **CIN** in a **typical** private to public conversion. **PAN** and **CIN** **continue**; unabsorbed **losses**, **depreciation**, and **credits** roll on if the **income** **tax** **continuity** **conditions** for that company are met. A simple **M&A** restyle to **"Limited"** is **broadly** **tax**-**neutral** at the **entity** level. **STT** and **securities**-driven **TDS** follow **securities** **trading** and **listing** work, not the **act** of **dropping** **"Private"** from the **name** alone. **ESOPs** and **securities** **perquisites** are taxed when they **arise** under the **IT** **Act**; reconfirm **115BAA** / **115BAB** and **DPIIT**-linked **positions** with your **CA** on the **filing** **date**.

## Applicability

**Private** **limited** **companies** under the **Companies Act, 2013** in **all** **states** and **UTs**, filed with the **jurisdictional** **RoC** on the **current** **MCA** **portal** **journey** **in** **force** when you file.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can a public company exist without an IPO?** **Yes**. **Listing** and **converting** to **public** are **independent** steps.

**How many** **shareholders and directors?** **At least** **7** **shareholders** and **3** **directors** for a public **limited** company (listed companies have **extra** **board** **and** **SEBI** **rules** when **applicable**).

**Must the name change?** The word **"Private"** is **removed** (for example, **ABC Private Limited** to **ABC Limited**). A **further** **name** **change** needs its **own** **MCA** process.

**Is shareholder approval** **required?** **Yes**, a **special** **resolution** (typically **75%** **voting** **power**) at an **EGM** is **required** to **authorise** **conversion** and **M&A** **amendment**.

**Can a public** **limited** return to **private?** A **listed** company may be **delisted** under **SEBI** rules and may **later** **convert** to **private** with **NCLT**-backed or **simplified** **paths** as **applicable**; **treat** it as a **separate** **governance** project with your **CS**.

services.name: Public to Private Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Re‑conversion of public limited companies into private companies.
services.content:
# Public to private limited company conversion

## Overview

A public limited company can reclassify as a private limited company by altering the M&A under Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013. A proviso in Section 14(1) can require NCLT approval if the company has made a public issue of shares or has more than 200 shareholders. Listed companies usually complete a SEBI-regulated delisting before the RoC re-styles the name to add "Private" on the same CIN.

> [!TIP] **When to use**  
> After a de-listing, a take-private, or when you need pvt ltd governance, transfer restrictions, and a "Private" in the legal name on the same CIN.

## Key features

- MGT-14 and INC-27-class e-forms (V3 e-form set in force on the filing date) with M&A for a private company.
- NCLT if the Section 14(1) proviso matches your cap table and history; confirm the live proviso and rules with your company secretary.
- If BSE or NSE-listed, SEBI delisting and settlement with public holders typically comes before the pvt M&A and COI re-style on the CIN-continued file.

| Track | What to expect |
|-------|-----------------|
| Unlisted, 200 or fewer shareholders, no public offer | Simpler EGM, MGT-14, and INC-27-class path with the RoC, subject to the live MCA user manual. |
| Proviso facts or a listed name | NCLT order and/or SEBI delisting, then the pvt re-style; sequence and notices are part of a tight CS file. |

## Benefits

- The word "Private" in the name and a M&A that matches pvt ltd rules, usually on the same CIN.
- Share transfer restrictions in the AOA, which a pvt ltd can use as the Act and your deed read on the filing date.
- Lower public-disclosure and SEBI-LODR load after a de-list, versus a main-board listed CIN, subject to the facts in your re-style file.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> NCLT time and cost if the proviso applies. SEBI delisting is long and data-heavy for listed names. Public deposits, if any, must be repaid or squared before you lock the pvt re-style. Minority rights in NCLT and SEBI paths need a fair process.

## Process

1. With your CS, read whether the Section 14(1) proviso applies: public offer of shares or a cap table with more than 200 shareholders, as the Act in force on the filing date states.
2. If listed, run SEBI delisting in the order the regulations require; pay fair exit to public holders in the SEBI path you choose.
3. EGM: pass a special resolution for the pvt re-style and M&A change. File MGT-14 in the time the Act and form text give you.
4. If NCLT: petition, notices to shareholders and creditors, and a bench order, then file the order with the RoC as the manual requires.
5. File INC-27-class and M&A in the pvt set; the RoC re-issues or amends the COI so the name has "Private Limited" in the pvt class on the CIN-continued line, in the normal CIN-continued pvt re-style.
6. Update GST, banks, and CERSAI- and counterparty KYC; if you de-listed, close STT- and-listed-KYC on the bourses as the bourse and SEBI ask.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (public) | In hand |
| M&A, existing and altered | AOA with pvt set |
| Special resolution (EGM / postal ballot) | As required |
| MGT-14 | Filed in time |
| INC-27 | Or the current V3 e-form in force |
| NCLT order | If proviso or court route applies |
| SEBI / exchange delisting proof | If listed first |
| List of shareholders (max 200 for pvt) | Updated if proviso and fact pattern need it |
| NOC from secured creditors | If term debt and lenders ask for it in your file set |
| Audited financials | Current year, as the form asks |
| DSC of directors | Class 3 |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| INC-27 | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| NCLT filing (if any) | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000 |
| Advocate / CS for NCLT (if any) | Rs. 25,000 to Rs. 2,00,000+ |
| AOA stamp duty | Rs. 500 to Rs. 5,000 (state) |
| Delisting (SEBI process) if listed | Rs. 2,00,000 to Rs. 10,00,000+ |
| CS / CA professional (full file) | Rs. 20,000 to Rs. 75,000 |

## Tax impact (summary)

A CIN-continued pvt re-style of a CIN-continued public, where no re-allocation of the whole balance sheet to a new CIN happens in your fact pattern, is often tax-neutral at entity level: same PAN and CIN, no fresh deemed transfer in the pvt re-style on the same CIN-continued CIN, subject to a CA read of your M&A, delisting, and buyback facts. A delisting buyback can attract buyback tax under Section 115QA in the company and Section 10(34A) read with capital gains in hands of holders; map each leg with a CA. STT and securities-line TDS fall away on delisted and off-bourse tranches as the year and the facts in your bourse/SEBI and IT read show.

## Applicability

Public limited companies in all states and UTs under the 2013 Act, filed on the V3 e-form set with the jurisdictional RoC, and, when the proviso applies, at the NCLT bench the manual routes you to, and, when the name is listed, on the SEBI and exchange set first.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is NCLT always required?** No. It is required when the proviso in Section 14(1) is met, for example a past public offer of shares or more than 200 shareholders, per the text in force on the filing date and your cap table. Your CS confirms.

**Can a listed company go private in one day?** No. Delisting and public-holder settlement are long; the pvt re-style and COI re-issue follow in a second wave after a clean bourse/SEBI line.

**How long does it take?** Unlisted, at most 200 shareholders, no public offer: often 2 to 4 months. Listed: 6 to 12+ months with delisting, then 2 to 4 months for the pvt re-style, as a very rough range.

**What about minority holders?** In listed paths, SEBI reverse book-building and fair price rules protect retail and non-promoter holders. In NCLT, minority rights and creditor notice are in the NCLT order text.

**Can we go public again later?** Yes. A pvt can convert back to a public and then list under the pvt to public and SEBI path when you are ready, subject to fresh resolutions and a new growth story in law.

services.name: Temporary One Day Function License
services.shortDescription: One‑day liquor service licence for functions and events.
services.content:
# Temporary One Day Function License

## Short overview

A Temporary One Day Function License is a comprehensive single-day event license that combines liquor service permission with other event-related approvals (food service, entertainment, public assembly) for organised functions like weddings, corporate events, large social gatherings, and public functions. While "One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor" focuses specifically on liquor service, the One Day Function License is often a broader composite permission covering multiple regulatory aspects of large events.

The license enables event hosts and organisers to legally conduct large-scale events with full compliance, covering liquor service, food service, fire safety, public assembly, and entertainment, through a streamlined single-window approval (in states with such systems) or coordinated multi-department approvals.

## Key features

- Single-day comprehensive permission
- Multi-department coordination
- Event-specific conditions
- Quantity-based liquor permission
- Capacity-based fire and safety compliance

## Benefits

- Single-window approval for multiple event-related permissions in some states
- Comprehensive coverage for liquor, food, entertainment
- Reduces multiple agency follow-ups
- Suitable for large weddings (500+ guests)
- Corporate events, product launches, conferences

## Limitations

- Single day only
- Higher fees than basic one-day liquor permission
- Strict event-specific conditions
- Heavy compliance requirements
- Last-minute approvals difficult

## Applicability

Available in most states with varying nomenclature: Maharashtra (One Day Function License, composite), Karnataka (Special Event Permit), Delhi (Composite Event Permission), Tamil Nadu (Special Function License), Telangana (Event Function License).

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Composite application form | |
| Event details | Date, venue, type, duration |
| Guest list or estimate | Attendance numbers |
| Venue plan | Layout with bar, food, entry and exit points |
| Venue NOC | |
| Fire NOC for venue | |
| Police clearance | |
| Local body NOC | |
| Liquor source confirmation | From authorised wholesaler |
| Caterer FSSAI license | For food service |
| Insurance | Event insurance recommended |

## Process

Apply 30 to 45 days before event with comprehensive documentation. Multi-department clearance and approval. Pay consolidated fees. Receive license. Conduct event with all conditions met.

## Government fees

| Event scale | Composite fee |
| --- | --- |
| Small (under 200 guests) | ₹10,000 to ₹25,000 |
| Medium (200 to 800 guests) | ₹25,000 to ₹75,000 |
| Large (800 to 2000 guests) | ₹75,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Mega events | ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000+ |

## Validity and renewal

Single day only. No renewal, fresh application for each event.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is one composite license enough or do I still need separate permissions?** In some states (Maharashtra, Delhi), composite licenses cover most needs. In other states, separate permissions for liquor, food, entertainment may still be needed.

**2. Can the function be held in a residential building?** Possible but more complex, requires building society NOC, parking arrangements, and noise compliance. Better to use commercial venues.

**3. What if attendance exceeds the declared number?** This is a violation. Apply for a higher capacity in the original application or seek upward revision before the event.

**4. Is event insurance mandatory?** Not legally mandatory in most states, but strongly recommended. Some venues require it.

**5. Can DJ or live performance be conducted under this license?** Entertainment elements require additional Public Performance License (PPL) and Police Entertainment License. The function license alone may not cover music or performance.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Event‑based permission to serve foreign liquor.
services.content:
# One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor

## Short overview

A One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor is a temporary license authorising the service of IMFL (Indian Made Foreign Liquor), including whisky, rum, vodka, gin, brandy, beer, wine, at a specific event for a single day. This permission is the most commonly sought temporary liquor license in India for weddings, corporate events, parties, conferences, and private functions held at venues that don't have their own permanent liquor license.

The permission allows the host or event organiser to legally serve liquor procured from authorised state-licensed wholesalers or retailers at a specified venue for the duration of the event. It is governed by state excise rules and requires advance application typically 15 to 30 days before the event.

## Key features

- Single-day validity
- Specific venue and event
- Quantity cap based on attendees
- Source restricted to authorised wholesalers
- Time-restricted (typically 11 AM to 11 PM, extendable on payment)

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation for liquor service at private functions
- Suitable for weddings, corporate events, conferences, parties
- Quick processing (1 to 4 weeks)
- Allows full range of IMFL, beer, wine, spirits
- Avoids legal complications of unauthorised liquor service

## Limitations

- Single day validity only
- Quantity restrictions based on guest count
- Source restriction, must procure from authorised licensee
- Heavy penalties for non-compliance
- Cannot resell or transfer unused liquor

## Applicability

| State | One day permission process |
| --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | FL-IV (One Day License) |
| Karnataka | KSBCL temporary permit |
| Tamil Nadu | TASMAC One Day Permit |
| Delhi | L-49 / L-50 |
| Telangana | One-day function license |
| Goa | Foreign Liquor One Day |
| Rajasthan | Temporary permit |

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State-specific |
| Event details | Date, time, venue, type of event |
| Guest list / count estimate | Expected attendance |
| Venue NOC | From property owner |
| Local body permission | Municipal NOC |
| Police clearance | Security arrangement details |
| Applicant ID | PAN, Aadhaar |
| Source license details | Of liquor wholesaler or retailer from whom liquor will be procured |
| Fire NOC | If venue requires |

## Process

1. Apply 15 to 30 days in advance with documents and fees.
2. Excise officer verifies application.
3. Coordinate with police for clearance.
4. Pay license fee and obtain permit.
5. Procure liquor from authorised source on event day.
6. Maintain consumption records and surrender unused stock.

## Government fees

| Event size | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Small (under 100 guests) | ₹3,000 to ₹10,000 |
| Medium (100 to 500 guests) | ₹10,000 to ₹30,000 |
| Large (500 to 1500 guests) | ₹30,000 to ₹75,000 |
| Mega events (1500+) | ₹75,000 to ₹2,00,000+ |

## Validity and renewal

Single day, no renewal. Fresh application for each event. Some states allow 3-day or 7-day permissions for extended events.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. How far in advance should I apply for a wedding liquor permission?** Apply at least 30 days in advance. Wedding season (Nov to Feb) sees heavy application volumes, early application avoids last-minute rejections.

**2. Can I purchase liquor from outside the state for a one-day permission?** No. Liquor must be procured from authorised licensees within the state. Inter-state procurement requires separate import permits.

**3. What happens to leftover liquor after the event?** Most states require the licensee to either return unopened stock to the source vendor (with refund as per arrangement) or destroy it under excise supervision. Personal retention is not permitted.

**4. Can I serve liquor at my home for a private party under this permission?** Yes, residential premises permission is granted in many states for weddings and family functions. Address and capacity must be properly disclosed.

**5. Is a one-day permission required for serving liquor only to family members?** Most states exempt small private gatherings (under 25 to 50 persons). Larger family functions or events with non-family guests typically require permission.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: One Year & Lifelong Permission for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Long‑term foreign liquor serving permissions for eligible premises.
services.content:
# One Year and Lifelong Permission for Foreign Liquor

## Short overview

The One Year and Lifelong Personal Permission for Foreign Liquor (often called "FL-XII" in Maharashtra or similar codes in other states) is an individual-level liquor possession and consumption permit granted by state excise departments authorising a person to legally possess and consume IMFL within prescribed limits. This is a personal license for the individual, not a commercial license, and does not authorise commercial sale or service.

The license is most relevant in states like Maharashtra, where the Bombay Prohibition Act, 1949 still technically requires individuals to have a permit to consume alcohol. While enforcement varies, the license provides legal protection for the holder against possession and consumption charges.

## Key features

- Individual permit (not entity-based)
- Personal consumption only
- One-year or lifelong validity options
- State-specific quantity limits

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation for personal liquor possession
- Avoids penalties under prohibition acts (in states like Maharashtra)
- Permits possession within home for personal consumption
- Lifelong permission is one-time and never needs renewal
- Enables purchase from authorised retailers without legal questions

## Limitations

- Personal use only, cannot be used for commercial purposes
- Possession limits apply (typically 9 litres of IMFL or equivalent at home)
- License must be carried or produced when purchasing from retailers in some states
- Lifelong permission has higher one-time fee
- Not transferable

## Applicability

| State | Personal permit | Authority |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | FL-XII (One year or Lifelong) | State Excise |
| Gujarat | Liquor permit (medical or special) | Excise Department |
| Bihar | Special permits (limited) | Excise |
| Other states | Generally not required |,  |

In most states (Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, etc.), personal consumption doesn't require an individual permit, the law allows possession within reasonable limits without specific permission.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State excise form |
| Identity proof | PAN, Aadhaar |
| Address proof | Residence proof |
| Photographs | 2 to 3 passport size |
| Age proof | Confirming above legal drinking age |
| Income certificate | Some states require this |
| Affidavit | Stating personal use only |

## Process

1. Apply online or at Collector or Excise office.
2. Submit documents and pay fees.
3. Verification by excise officer.
4. Permit issued (typically within 7 to 30 days).

## Government fees

| License type | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| One Year permission (Maharashtra) | ₹100 to ₹1,000 |
| Lifelong permission (Maharashtra) | ₹1,000 to ₹2,000 |

Fees vary by state and are nominal.

## Validity and renewal

One-year permission: renewable annually. Lifelong permission: valid for lifetime of holder.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is the FL-XII permit mandatory in Maharashtra to drink alcohol?** Technically yes under the Bombay Prohibition Act, but enforcement is selective. For legal protection during possession, transport, or any law enforcement encounter, holding a permit is advisable.

**2. Can I drink at a bar without an individual permit?** At licensed bars and restaurants, the establishment's license covers your consumption. The personal permit primarily applies to home possession and consumption.

**3. What is the home possession limit under this permit?** In Maharashtra, the limit is typically 9 litres of IMFL (12 bottles of 750ml). Beer limit may differ. Exceeding limits attracts excise penalties.

**4. Do tourists visiting Maharashtra need this permit?** Tourists can obtain temporary permits (one-day or one-month) at airports or major hotels in Maharashtra. The license helps avoid issues during stay.

**5. Can I transfer my lifelong permit to a family member?** No. Personal permits are non-transferable. Each individual must obtain their own permit.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: One Day Permission for Country Liquor
services.shortDescription: Single‑day permit to serve country liquor at events.
services.content:
# One Day Permission for Country Liquor

## Short overview

A One Day Permission for Country Liquor is a temporary liquor license issued by state excise departments authorising the sale or service of country liquor (locally produced traditional liquor, often referred to as "desi daru" or "deshi sharab") at a specific event or function on a single day. This permission is typically sought for cultural festivals, community gatherings, fairs, agricultural events, and traditional ceremonies in regions where country liquor consumption is prevalent.

Country liquor is significantly cheaper than IMFL and is regulated under different sections of state excise acts, with separate manufacturing, distribution, and retail frameworks. The One Day Permission allows event organisers to legally serve country liquor at a controlled, time-bound event without obtaining a permanent country liquor license.

## Key features

- Single-day validity
- Specific event or location designated
- Quantity capped (typically based on guest count and excise norms)
- Source restriction to government-authorised distilleries

## Benefits

- Time-bound, low-commitment liquor service authorisation
- Lower fees compared to permanent licenses
- Enables legal service at cultural and traditional events
- Quick approval (1 to 2 weeks typically)
- No need for permanent premises licensing

## Limitations

- Limited to one specific day
- Strict quantity limits (based on attendee count)
- Heavy penalties for unauthorised quantities or extended hours
- Source restriction, must purchase from authorised state corporation or distillery
- Many urban states heavily restrict or prohibit country liquor

## Applicability

Country liquor licensing is more common in rural areas of: Maharashtra (rural districts), Karnataka (rural areas, certain districts), Madhya Pradesh (rural), Rajasthan (specific districts), Andhra Pradesh and Telangana (rural), Punjab and Haryana (limited), Uttar Pradesh (rural belts). States like Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and metro Mumbai have heavily restricted country liquor.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State excise prescribed form |
| Event details | Date, venue, expected attendance |
| Premises permission | NOC from venue owner |
| Local body permission | Municipal or panchayat NOC |
| Police NOC | Event security clearance |
| Applicant ID | PAN, Aadhaar |
| Liquor procurement plan | From authorised source |

## Process

Submit application 15 to 30 days before event with documents and fees. Excise officer verifies and grants permission. Procure liquor only from designated source on event day.

## Government fees

| Event size | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| Small event (under 100 guests) | ₹2,000 to ₹5,000 |
| Medium event (100 to 500 guests) | ₹5,000 to ₹15,000 |
| Large event (500+ guests) | ₹15,000 to ₹50,000+ |

## Validity and renewal

Single day only, no renewal. Fresh application required for each event.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can country liquor be served in a city like Mumbai or Pune under this permission?** Country liquor service in major urban centers is heavily restricted. Most metro and Tier 1 city licenses cover only IMFL events. Check state-specific rules.

**2. Is country liquor cheaper than IMFL for event purposes?** Yes, country liquor costs significantly less per litre than IMFL, making it viable for budget events. However, the procurement quota and permitted brands may be restricted.

**3. What happens if attendees consume more than the permitted quantity?** The licensee is responsible for compliance. Excessive quantities can result in license cancellation, fines, and potential prosecution.

**4. Can foreign nationals or tourists consume country liquor at such events?** Generally yes, but the licensee must ensure all consumers meet legal drinking age and follow state guidelines. Some states restrict foreign nationals to specific liquor categories.

**5. Do I need a separate FSSAI license for serving country liquor at an event?** If food is also served, FSSAI license or temporary food permission is required separately. The liquor permission doesn't cover food service.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Wholesale License for Wine
services.shortDescription: Excise licensing for wholesale wine traders.
services.content:
# Wholesale License for Wine

## Short overview

A Wholesale License for Wine specifically authorises wholesale distribution of wines (still, sparkling, fortified) from licensed wineries and importers to retailers, hotels, and restaurants. With India's wine market growing rapidly (15 to 20% CAGR), this license category has gained importance, especially with the boom of Indian wine production in Maharashtra (Nashik, Pune), Karnataka (Bangalore), and import-driven markets.

Some states have separate wine wholesale categories with lower fees than IMFL wholesale, recognising wine as a low-alcohol, premium product distinct from spirits.

## Key features

- B2B wine distribution only
- Lower entry threshold than IMFL wholesale in some states
- Source-restricted procurement
- Cold storage requirements

## Benefits

- Growing wine consumption in India
- Premium pricing and margins
- Lower license fees than IMFL wholesale in some states
- Cleaner regulatory framework
- Aligns with wine tourism (Nashik wine country)
- Educated, premium customer base

## Limitations

- Niche market, smaller than beer or spirits
- High capital requirements for premium wine inventory
- Cold chain logistics needed
- Limited retail base for wine-specific sales
- Competing with beer and spirits in same outlets

## Applicability

| State | Wine wholesale framework |
| --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | Wine wholesale license (lower fee than IMFL) |
| Karnataka | KSBCL handles wine + private licenses |
| Goa | Wine wholesale |
| Telangana | TSBCL |
| Delhi | L-9 (wine specific) |
| Rajasthan | Wine wholesale |

## Documents required

Similar to IMFL wholesale with additional cold chain infrastructure documentation.

## Process

Standard wholesale license process. Timeline: 4 to 9 months.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual license fee | ₹3,00,000 to ₹15,00,000 |
| Security deposit | ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual with renewal compliance.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can I wholesale only Indian wines under this license?** Most wine wholesale licenses cover both Indian and imported wines, provided imports come through licensed importers.

**2. Are champagne and sparkling wines covered?** Yes, sparkling wines, champagne, and prosecco are all covered under wine wholesale licenses.

**3. What are special storage requirements?** Temperature-controlled storage (12 to 18°C ideal), humidity control, and protection from sunlight. Premium wines need specialised cellar conditions.

**4. Can I supply wine to events directly?** Wholesale licenses are B2B only. Event supply requires either retail purchase by event organisers or specific event-licensee arrangements.

**5. Is wine wholesale more profitable than IMFL wholesale?** Per-bottle margins are higher, but absolute volumes are smaller. Profitability depends on portfolio mix and market access.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Wholesale License for Country Liquor
services.shortDescription: Wholesale licences for country liquor operations.
services.content:
# Wholesale License for Country Liquor

## Short overview

A Wholesale License for Country Liquor authorises an entity to procure country liquor from licensed manufacturers or distilleries and supply it to retail country liquor shops, ON licensees, and authorised dealers within a specified geographic area. This is a B2B license, the wholesaler does not sell directly to consumers.

Country liquor wholesale is typically managed by state government corporations (KSBCL in Karnataka, MSBCL in Maharashtra for IMFL, BSBCL in Bihar prohibition state) or by private wholesale licensees in some states. The wholesale tier is critical to the country liquor distribution system.

## Key features

- B2B-only license
- Geographic territory specified
- Source-restricted procurement
- Daily reporting obligations

## Benefits

- Stable B2B business with regular institutional clients
- Higher transaction volumes than retail
- Lower per-unit margins but higher absolute returns
- Long-term contracts with retailers
- Government-supported pricing structure

## Limitations

- Very high license fees and security deposits
- Heavy compliance, daily stock movement reporting
- State government often has monopoly or near-monopoly
- Limited number of licenses per region
- Strict source restrictions (only from licensed distilleries)

## Applicability

Country liquor wholesale is more relevant in: Maharashtra (MSWC handles much of it), Karnataka (KSBCL monopoly), Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh and Telangana, Bihar (prohibition state, limited).

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State excise prescribed |
| Entity registration | Partnership, LLP, Company documents |
| PAN, GST, IEC | All applicable registrations |
| Premises documents | Warehouse or godown title or lease |
| Storage facility plan | Layout of warehouse with security features |
| Bank guarantee | High-value bank guarantee |
| ITR (3 years) | Demonstrating financial capacity |
| Police verification | Of all directors or partners |
| Net worth certificate | From CA |
| Insurance | Stock insurance |

## Process

Multi-stage application with detailed financial scrutiny, premises verification, and high-level excise officer approvals. Timeline: 6 to 12 months.

## Government fees

| Component | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual license fee | ₹10,00,000 to ₹50,00,000+ |
| Security deposit or bank guarantee | ₹25,00,000 to ₹2,00,00,000+ |

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity with stringent renewal compliance verification.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can a private entity hold a country liquor wholesale license in all states?** No. Many states (Karnataka, Tamil Nadu) have state corporation monopolies. Private wholesale licenses are limited to specific states with private wholesale frameworks.

**2. What is the difference between IMFL wholesale and country liquor wholesale?** Country liquor wholesale handles locally produced traditional liquors at lower price points; IMFL wholesale handles "foreign" branded liquors at higher price points. Different license categories with different fee structures.

**3. Can a wholesaler operate in multiple districts under one license?** Generally one wholesale license covers one designated territory (district or zone). Multi-territory operations require multiple licenses.

**4. What is the minimum storage capacity required?** Typically 5,000 to 25,000 cases minimum, varying by state. Premises must have proper security, ventilation, and fire safety.

**5. Can the wholesaler sell directly to consumers?** No. The license restricts sales to licensed retailers only. Direct consumer sales are prohibited and attract heavy penalties.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Wholesale excise licence for foreign liquor.
services.content:
# Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor

## Short overview

A Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor (IMFL Wholesale License) authorises an entity to procure IMFL, including whisky, rum, vodka, gin, brandy, beer, wine, from licensed manufacturers, importers, and bonded warehouses, and supply to authorised retail licensees, hotels, restaurants, clubs, and bar permit holders within a specified state or territory.

This is one of the most lucrative B2B alcohol licenses, often controlled by state corporations (KSBCL in Karnataka, MSWC in Maharashtra, TASMAC in Tamil Nadu) or held by major private players like United Spirits, Pernod Ricard, AB InBev distributors, etc.

## Key features

- B2B only
- State-wide territory typically
- Source-restricted procurement
- Daily stock movement and tax reporting
- High-volume business model

## Benefits

- Strategic position in liquor supply chain
- High revenue potential (₹100+ crores in major states)
- Long-term institutional client relationships
- Access to premium brand portfolios
- Significant economic moat once established
- Government-aligned business model

## Limitations

- Extremely high entry barriers
- Massive license fees and bank guarantees
- Heavy regulatory scrutiny
- Strict source-and-destination tracking
- State monopolies in several states
- Significant working capital requirements (₹50+ crore)

## Applicability

| State | Wholesale framework |
| --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | Private wholesale licenses + MSWC |
| Karnataka | KSBCL (state monopoly) |
| Tamil Nadu | TASMAC (state monopoly) |
| Delhi | Mix of private and government |
| Telangana | TSBCL + private |
| West Bengal | WBSBCL + private |
| Punjab | Private wholesale |
| Rajasthan | RSBCL |
| Goa | Private wholesale |

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Detailed application | Comprehensive business plan |
| Entity registration | Company or LLP documents |
| Net worth certificate | Minimum ₹5 to 10 crore typically |
| Premises documents | Bonded warehouse facility |
| Fire NOC for warehouse | |
| Bank guarantee | ₹50 lakh to ₹5 crore |
| Director or partner verification | Police clearance |
| ITR, 5 years | High-value applicants |
| Source agreements | With manufacturers or importers |
| GST, PAN, IEC | All registrations |

## Process

Highly involved process with multi-tier approval. Timeline: 6 to 18 months.

## Government fees

| Component | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000 |
| Annual license fee | ₹50,00,000 to ₹5,00,00,000 |
| Bank guarantee | ₹50,00,000 to ₹5,00,00,000 |
| Security deposit | ₹25,00,000 to ₹1,00,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual renewal with extensive compliance verification.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can a foreign liquor company directly hold an Indian wholesale license?** Foreign companies typically operate through Indian subsidiaries or Indian wholesale partners. Direct foreign holding is restricted under FDI rules.

**2. What's the minimum working capital for IMFL wholesale operations?** Realistically ₹25 to 100 crore depending on state and territory. Stock holding alone can be ₹10+ crore.

**3. Can wholesalers also export liquor?** Export requires separate IEC and DGFT permissions. Wholesale license alone doesn't authorise exports.

**4. How is wholesale pricing regulated?** States typically fix maximum margins between distillery price and wholesale price. Wholesalers cannot exceed regulated margins.

**5. Can a wholesale license be transferred?** Transfer requires state government approval and is rarely granted. Usually entire entity must be acquired (not just license).

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Permit Room License
services.shortDescription: Licensing of permit rooms attached to hotels and restaurants.
services.content:
# Permit Room License

## Short overview

A Permit Room License (often called a "Permit Room" in Maharashtra and similar designations in other states) is a state excise license authorising a restaurant, hotel, or eatery to serve all categories of liquor (beer, wine, IMFL spirits) for on-premises consumption with food. The "Permit Room" is essentially the licensed area within the restaurant where liquor service is permitted.

This is the most common type of bar and restaurant liquor license in India, allowing the establishment to operate as a full-service food and beverage outlet with comprehensive liquor offerings. It is typically held by mid-to-upscale restaurants, hotels, lounges, pubs, and dining establishments.

## Key features

- On-premises service with food
- All liquor categories covered
- Specific licensed area (Permit Room) within restaurant
- Annual validity
- Mandatory food sales requirement (typically minimum 30 to 40% of revenue)

## Benefits

- Full range of liquor service (beer, wine, spirits, cocktails)
- Higher revenue potential, liquor sales typically have 200 to 400% margins
- Attracts diverse customer demographics
- Enables differentiated dining experience
- Premium brand positioning
- Supports cocktail innovation and mixology

## Limitations

- High annual license fees (₹5 to 25 lakh typically)
- Strict compliance, surprise inspections, age verification, dry days
- Heavy security deposits
- Operating hour restrictions
- Strict bookkeeping and stock register maintenance
- Heavy penalties for violations (license cancellation, prosecution)

## Applicability

| State | Equivalent license | Fee range (annual) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | FL-III (Permit Room) | ₹5 to 25 lakh |
| Karnataka | CL-9 (Bar and Restaurant) | ₹5 to 20 lakh |
| Delhi | L-17 / L-19 | ₹10 to 30 lakh |
| Tamil Nadu | FL-3 (Bar) | TASMAC governed |
| Telangana | TS Excise Bar | ₹8 to 25 lakh |
| Goa | Foreign Liquor Bar | ₹3 to 10 lakh |

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State-specific |
| Restaurant trade license | Municipal trade license |
| FSSAI License | Food safety registration |
| Premises ownership or lease | Title or lease |
| Site plan | Marking permit room area |
| Fire NOC | Mandatory |
| Police NOC | |
| Local body NOC | Municipal approval |
| Distance certificate | Compliance with distance norms |
| GST registration | |
| ITR (3 years) | Of entity or proprietor |
| Partnership deed or MoA | Constitutional documents |
| Bank solvency certificate | |

## Process

1. Submit application with all documents.
2. Site inspection by Excise officer.
3. Police verification of proprietor or partners.
4. Public notice for objections (in some states).
5. License fee and security deposit payment.
6. License issuance.
7. Stock procurement and operations start.

Timeline: 60 to 180 days.

## Government fees

| Category | Fee range (annual) |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹10,000 to ₹50,000 |
| License fee, Tier 3 cities | ₹3,00,000 to ₹8,00,000 |
| License fee, Tier 2 cities | ₹8,00,000 to ₹15,00,000 |
| License fee, Tier 1 metros | ₹15,00,000 to ₹30,00,000 |
| License fee, premium hotels | Up to ₹50,00,000+ |
| Security deposit | ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity (financial year). Renewal 30 to 60 days before expiry. Compliance audit during renewal. Late renewal penalty: 25 to 100% additional.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is FSSAI License mandatory for a Permit Room License?** Yes. Without a valid FSSAI license, a permit room license cannot be granted as food service is integral to the license requirements.

**2. What is the minimum food sales requirement?** Most states require 30 to 40% of revenue from food sales. Pure bars without food don't qualify for permit room licenses.

**3. Can I have a separate "ladies-only" section in my permit room?** Yes, this is permissible and even recommended in some states. It must be clearly demarcated and noted in the license.

**4. Are happy hours and discounted liquor offers permitted?** Yes, but pricing must comply with state-mandated minimum prices. Selling below MRP attracts penalties.

**5. Can I have live music or DJ in the permit room?** Yes, but additional licenses (Public Performance License from PPL or IPRS, Police Entertainment License, dance floor license if applicable) are needed.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Club License
services.shortDescription: Liquor licence for private and membership‑based clubs.
services.content:
# Club License

## Short overview

A Club License is a special category liquor license issued by state excise departments to registered private members' clubs, recreational clubs, sports clubs, social clubs, professional clubs (Rotary, Lions), and similar member-only establishments, authorising them to serve liquor (beer, wine, and IMFL) to bona fide members and their guests within the club premises.

The license is distinct from a regular bar or restaurant license because of the members-only nature, service is restricted to registered members and their accompanying guests, not to the general public. The club must be registered as a society, trust, or Section 8 Company under applicable laws and have a defined membership structure with admission criteria, membership fees, and proper governance.

Club Licenses are governed by state-specific excise acts and rules, with significant variations in eligibility, license fees, conditions, and operational restrictions. Premium clubs in major cities (Bombay Gymkhana, Madras Club, Bangalore Club, Delhi Gymkhana, etc.) operate under such licenses.

## Key features

- Issued only to registered clubs (not individual proprietorships)
- Members-only service (with limited guest privileges)
- Annual validity with renewal requirements
- Proper member register and guest register maintenance mandatory
- Service limited to club premises
- Typically permits all categories of liquor (beer, wine, IMFL)

## Benefits

- Captive customer base, registered members are loyal recurring customers
- Higher margins, clubs typically charge premium prices for liquor service
- Less competition, limited number of club licenses issued
- Opportunity to build prestigious membership base
- License fees generally lower than commercial bar licenses
- Less stringent operating hour restrictions in some states

## Limitations

- Restricted to members and guests, cannot serve walk-in public
- Requires established club infrastructure with adequate facilities
- Significant initial investment in club premises and amenities
- Member admission processes can be slow and selective
- Subject to excise audits and member register scrutiny
- Limited revenue scalability compared to public bars
- Compliance with club registration laws (Societies Act, Trusts Act, Companies Act)

## Applicability

| State | Club License Type | Authority |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | Form CL-IV / Club License | State Excise Department |
| Karnataka | CL-4 (Club License) | KSBCL / Excise Department |
| Tamil Nadu | FL-3 (Club License) | TASMAC / Excise |
| Telangana | Club License | State Excise Department |
| Delhi | L-15 (Club License) | Delhi Excise Department |
| Rajasthan | Club License | RSBCL |
| West Bengal | FL Club | WBSBCL |
| Punjab | Club License | Punjab Excise |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State-specific club license form |
| Club registration certificate | Society, Trust, or Section 8 Company registration |
| Memorandum and Rules | Club's MoA, AoA, or Rules and Regulations |
| Member list | Current registered membership list with details |
| Premises ownership / lease | Title deed or registered lease |
| Premises plan | Layout showing dining, bar, and storage areas |
| FSSAI License | For food service in the club |
| Fire NOC | Mandatory safety clearance |
| Local body NOC | Municipal approval |
| GST Registration | Tax compliance |
| Income Tax Returns | Club's last 3 years' ITRs |
| Bank Statement | Demonstrating financial capacity |
| Office bearer details | Names, PAN, Aadhaar of governing committee |

## Process

1. **Application submission:** Submit application with all documents to State Excise Commissioner.
2. **Documentary verification:** Excise department verifies club registration, membership structure, and premises documents.
3. **Premises inspection:** Inspector verifies premises, dining facilities, bar area, storage, and overall infrastructure.
4. **Public notice (in some states):** Publication of notice for objections.
5. **License fee payment:** Upon provisional approval, pay annual license fee and security deposit.
6. **License issuance:** Club License issued by Excise Commissioner or authorised officer.
7. **Stock procurement:** Obtain liquor stock from authorised wholesalers or state corporations.
8. **Operations commencement:** Begin liquor service to members.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹5,000 to ₹25,000 |
| Annual license fee, small clubs | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, medium clubs | ₹2,00,000 to ₹10,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, premium clubs in metros | ₹10,00,000 to ₹50,00,000 |
| Security deposit | ₹1,00,000 to ₹10,00,000 |
| Stock label fees | Variable, per bottle |

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity (April 1 to March 31). Renewal application 30 to 60 days before expiry. Compliance audit and member register verification during renewal. Late renewal fee: typically 50 to 100% additional.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can a club admit non-members for special events?** Most states allow guests of members but prohibit non-member entry for liquor service. For special public events, separate "One Day Function License" is typically required.

**2. Is there a minimum membership requirement for a Club License?** Most states require minimum 100 to 200 active members for a Club License application. Some states have higher thresholds for premium club categories.

**3. Can corporate entities purchase club memberships?** Corporate memberships are permitted in most clubs and most state laws. Each corporate member typically gets nominee privileges as defined in club rules.

**4. Can the club host wedding receptions and serve liquor to wedding guests?** For wedding receptions where non-members attend, an additional One Day Function License is required for liquor service. The Club License alone doesn't cover such public events.

**5. Are office bearers personally liable for club license violations?** Yes. Office bearers (President, Secretary, Treasurer) named in the license can be held personally liable for excise violations, including underage service, after-hours service, or sale to non-members.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Beer Shoppee License
services.shortDescription: Retail beer shop licensing under state excise laws.
services.content:
# Beer Shoppee License

## Short overview

A Beer Shoppee License is a state-issued retail liquor license that authorises the licensee to sell beer (and in some states, mild liquor with low alcohol content) in sealed bottles or cans for off-premises consumption. The license is granted under the respective state Excise Act and Excise Rules, liquor being a State Subject under Entry 8 of the State List in the Constitution of India, making this a state-specific license with significantly different procedures, fees, and conditions across states.

A Beer Shoppee (often referred to as a Beer Shop, Beer Outlet, or Mild Liquor Shop in different states) is distinct from a full-fledged liquor shop (which sells the entire range of IMFL, Indian Made Foreign Liquor, including spirits like whisky, rum, vodka, gin), it is restricted to beer and sometimes wine and mild alcoholic beverages with low alcohol content (typically below 8.75% alcohol by volume).

The license is highly regulated, limited number of licenses are issued in each district (often through auction or lottery), the location must comply with distance restrictions from schools, religious places, and hospitals, and the licensee must comply with operating hours, age verification, and quota purchase requirements from authorised state-licensed wholesalers or government corporations.

## Key features

- Premises-specific license, tied to a specific location
- Annual or multi-year validity (typically 1 year)
- Renewable subject to compliance and timely fee payment
- Subject to quota purchase from state-authorised sources
- Mandatory display of license certificate at premises
- Age verification mandatory (no sale to persons below legal drinking age, varies by state from 21 to 25 years)

## Benefits

- Lower investment compared to full IMFL retail license, lower license fee, smaller minimum stock requirement
- Wider customer base, beer is the most consumed alcoholic beverage in India, with growing demand from young consumers
- Stable monthly revenue once established, repeat customers and consistent demand patterns
- Beer is less price-sensitive than spirits, margins are more predictable
- Bulk procurement discounts from authorised wholesalers
- Eligibility for promotional support from beer brand owners (point-of-sale displays, refrigeration units, signage)

## Limitations

- Limited product range, restricted to beer (and sometimes mild liquor and wine) only, cannot sell spirits
- High regulatory scrutiny, frequent excise inspector visits, compliance audits, and stock verification
- Operating hour restrictions, typically 11 AM to 11 PM, with state-specific dry days (national holidays, election days, religious festivals)
- Quota-based purchases, must purchase from designated wholesalers or state corporations at fixed rates
- High annual license fees in metros and Tier 1 cities
- License cannot be transferred without state government approval
- Heavy penalties for selling to minors, selling above MRP, or stocking unauthorised brands

## Applicability

State-wise Beer License Framework:

| State | Authority | License Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | State Excise Department | FL/BR-II (Beer Shoppee) |
| Karnataka | KSBCL | CL-2/CL-7 |
| Tamil Nadu | TASMAC (state monopoly) | Limited private licenses |
| Telangana | Excise Department | Beer Wine Shop license |
| Delhi | Delhi Excise | L-9 / L-10 |
| Rajasthan | RSBCL | Beer/Wine Shop |
| Uttar Pradesh | Excise Department | Beer Shop license |
| West Bengal | WBSBCL | FL Beer Off |
| Goa | Excise Department | Wholesale and Retail Beer |

Note: States like Gujarat, Bihar, Nagaland, and Mizoram are dry or prohibition states with severe restrictions on alcohol licensing.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State excise department prescribed form |
| Identity proof | PAN, Aadhaar of applicant |
| Address proof of premises | Ownership documents or registered lease deed |
| NOC from property owner | If premises is leased |
| Site plan / layout | Of proposed beer shop premises |
| Distance certificate | Confirming compliance with distance norms from schools, religious places |
| Local body NOC | From municipality or panchayat |
| Police clearance certificate | Verification of applicant's antecedents |
| Income tax returns | Last 3 years' ITR of applicant |
| Bank solvency certificate | Demonstrating financial capacity |
| Affidavit | Stating applicant has no prior excise violations |
| Photographs of premises | Front, interior, signboard area |
| GST registration (if applicable) | For tax compliance |

## Process

1. **Application submission:** Submit application in prescribed form along with documents and application fee to the state excise department.
2. **Site inspection:** Excise inspector visits the proposed premises to verify compliance with distance norms, premises specifications, and zonal restrictions.
3. **Police verification:** Police verification of applicant's character, antecedents, and any prior involvement in offences.
4. **Public notice / objections:** In some states, a public notice is published inviting objections to the proposed license.
5. **License fee payment:** Upon provisional approval, pay the prescribed annual license fee.
6. **License issuance:** Excise Commissioner or Collector issues the Beer Shoppee License.
7. **Premises setup and stock procurement:** Set up the premises as per specifications and procure initial beer stock from authorised wholesalers.
8. **Commencement of operations:** Begin retail beer sales after displaying the license at premises.

## Government fees

| Component | Approximate fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹1,000 to ₹10,000 |
| License fee, small towns | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 per annum |
| License fee, Tier 2 cities | ₹2,00,000 to ₹5,00,000 per annum |
| License fee, Tier 1 cities or metros | ₹5,00,000 to ₹15,00,000 per annum |
| Security deposit | ₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000 |
| Renewal fee | Same as annual license fee |

Fees vary significantly by state, location category, and type of beer shop. Auction-based licenses (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka in some districts) can run into crores.

## Validity and renewal

**Validity:** Typically 1 year (April 1 to March 31 in most states)

**Renewal:** Apply 30 to 60 days before expiry with renewal fee, compliance affidavit, and fresh inspection (in some states). Late renewal attracts heavy penalty. License lapse means cessation of business; reapplication required if lapse exceeds prescribed period.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can I sell wine and other low-alcohol beverages along with beer under a Beer Shoppee License?** This depends on the state. In Maharashtra, the FL/BR-II license covers beer and wine. In Karnataka, the CL-7 license specifically covers beer and wine. In some states, separate wine shop licenses are required. Always check the specific scope of license under your state's excise rules.

**2. What is the minimum age requirement for a Beer Shoppee License applicant?** The applicant must typically be at least 21 years old, of Indian nationality, and a resident of the state where the license is sought. Some states require minimum educational qualifications.

**3. Can I operate the Beer Shoppee from a residential building?** Generally no. Most state excise rules require the premises to be in commercial zones with proper municipal approval. Operating from residential premises violates zoning laws and the license terms.

**4. What is the distance restriction from schools and religious places?** Most states require minimum 100 meters distance from schools, religious places, and hospitals. Some states (like Karnataka) require 100 meters in urban and 200 meters in rural areas. State highway proximity also has restrictions following Supreme Court rulings.

**5. Can a single applicant hold multiple Beer Shoppee Licenses?** Most states restrict one applicant (or one immediate family) to one or two licenses to prevent monopolisation. Specific limits vary by state and are typically enforced strictly.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Mild Liquor & Wine Bar License
services.shortDescription: Licensing for establishments serving mild liquor and wine.
services.content:
# Mild Liquor and Wine Bar License

## Short overview

A Mild Liquor and Wine Bar License is a state-issued license authorising restaurants, hotels, and bars to serve mild liquor (beer with alcohol content typically below 8.75% ABV) and wine for on-premises consumption with food. This is a restricted form of liquor license, distinct from a full bar license that permits all categories of IMFL (Indian Made Foreign Liquor including whisky, rum, vodka, gin).

The license is popular among casual dining restaurants, cafes, microbreweries, and lounge bars that wish to serve beer and wine alongside food but don't want the operational complexity and higher fees of a full IMFL bar license. In states like Maharashtra and Karnataka, this license category has gained significant traction due to the growing pub and microbrewery culture.

## Key features

- On-premises service only with food
- Restricted alcoholic beverage range
- Annual license validity
- Premises must include kitchen and dining facility
- FSSAI License mandatory

## Benefits

- Lower license fees compared to full IMFL bar license
- Suitable for casual dining and pub-style establishments
- Wider customer appeal, beer and wine attract diverse demographics
- Lower compliance burden compared to spirits
- Attractive for women patrons and corporate diners
- Aligns with food-pairing concepts (wine with cuisine)

## Limitations

- Cannot serve spirits (whisky, rum, vodka, gin, brandy)
- Limited to mild liquor and wine only
- Same operational restrictions as full bar (timing, dry days, age verification)
- Customers seeking spirits will go elsewhere, competitive disadvantage in some markets
- License upgrade to full bar requires fresh application and higher fees

## Applicability

| State | License type | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Maharashtra | FL/BR-IV | Beer and Wine Bar License |
| Karnataka | CL-9 | Restaurant Bar (limited) |
| Goa | Restaurant Bar | Beer and Wine permitted |
| Telangana | TS Excise | Beer and Wine restaurant |
| Delhi | L-17 | Restricted bar license |
| Tamil Nadu | TASMAC governed | Restricted private licenses |

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State-specific |
| Restaurant registration | Trade license, FSSAI license |
| Premises ownership or lease | Title or lease document |
| Site plan | Dining and bar area marked |
| Fire NOC | Mandatory |
| Local body NOC | Municipal approval |
| Police verification | Of proprietor or partners |
| GST Registration | |
| ITR, last 3 years | Of the entity or proprietor |
| Distance certificate | Compliance with distance norms |

## Process

Similar to Beer Shoppee License process, with additional verification of restaurant infrastructure, kitchen facility, and dining area capacity. Typical timeline: 60 to 120 days.

1. Prepare documents and application as per state excise rules.
2. Submit to excise with restaurant and FSSAI proof.
3. Undergo site inspection of dining, kitchen, and bar areas.
4. Clear police and local body requirements where applicable.
5. Pay fees and receive license.

## Government fees

| Component | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹5,000 to ₹15,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 3 cities | ₹1,00,000 to ₹3,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 2 cities | ₹3,00,000 to ₹8,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 1 metros | ₹8,00,000 to ₹20,00,000 |
| Security deposit | ₹1,00,000 to ₹5,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity (financial year basis). Renewal 30 to 60 days before expiry. Compliance with operating conditions verified at renewal.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can I serve cocktails using beer or wine as base under this license?** Yes, cocktails using only beer or wine (without spirits) are permitted. Sangria, beer cocktails, wine spritzers are allowed.

**2. Can I upgrade from Mild Liquor and Wine Bar to Full Bar License later?** Yes. Upgrade requires fresh application, additional license fee, and possibly additional premises modifications. The upgrade is not automatic.

**3. Are imported beers and wines allowed under this license?** Yes, provided they are procured from authorised state wholesalers or importers and have valid customs clearance and label approval.

**4. What are typical operating hours allowed?** Most states permit 11 AM to 11 PM (some allow till midnight or 1 AM with extension fee). Dry days apply.

**5. Can I have a dance floor or DJ in my Mild Liquor and Wine Bar?** This requires additional permissions, dance floor license and Public Performance License. The liquor license alone doesn't cover entertainment.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Wine Bar License
services.shortDescription: Specialised excise licence for wine bars.
services.content:
# Wine Bar License

## Short overview

A Wine Bar License is a specialised liquor license authorising restaurants and dedicated wine bars to serve wine (and sometimes mild liquor) for on-premises consumption with food. This license is distinct from a full bar license and caters to wine-focused establishments, wine bars, wine lounges, wine retail with tasting, vineyards' tasting rooms, and dedicated wine restaurants.

The license has gained popularity in metros (Mumbai, Bangalore, Pune, Delhi) with the growth of wine culture, wine tasting events, and premium dining.

## Key features

- On-premises wine service with food
- Specific premises designation
- Annual license validity
- FSSAI license mandatory

## Benefits

- Niche premium positioning
- Lower license fees than full bar in some states
- Aligns with sophisticated dining and tourism
- Less restrictive regulatory framework
- Supports wine tourism and vineyard tasting rooms
- Premium pricing power

## Limitations

- Limited to wine, narrower customer base
- Cannot serve beer or spirits (in pure wine bar license)
- Higher inventory costs (premium wines)
- Requires sommelier or wine expertise
- Staff training costs

## Applicability

Particularly relevant in Maharashtra (wine country, Nashik, Sula, Fratelli vineyards), Karnataka, Delhi NCR, Bangalore, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad.

## Documents required

Similar to Permit Room or Wine Bar combination: restaurant trade license, FSSAI, fire NOC, premises documents.

## Process

Standard liquor license process. Timeline: 60 to 120 days.

## Government fees

| Category | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Application fee | ₹5,000 to ₹15,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 3 | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 2 | ₹2,00,000 to ₹5,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 1 | ₹5,00,000 to ₹15,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual validity, renewable.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can I have wine tasting events under a wine bar license?** Yes, wine tasting events are typically permitted within the licensed premises. Off-premises tasting events may require additional permissions.

**2. Can I sell wine bottles for takeaway?** Wine bar licenses are typically for on-premises consumption only. Takeaway requires a wine retail license (Wine Shoppee).

**3. Are wine cocktails (sangria, spritzers) permitted?** Yes, cocktails using only wine as base are permitted.

**4. Can I serve cheese and small bites without full menu?** Most wine bar licenses require food service, but cheese boards, charcuterie, and tapas-style food typically qualify.

**5. Are wine education classes permitted at the wine bar?** Yes, wine education and certification programs (WSET, etc.) are commonly held at wine bars and don't require additional licenses if conducted within licensed premises.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Wine Shoppee License
services.shortDescription: Retail licence for wine shops.
services.content:
# Wine Shoppee License

## Short overview

A Wine Shoppee License is a state-issued retail license authorising the sale of wine in sealed bottles for off-premises consumption. Similar to Beer Shoppee but exclusive to wine, this license caters to wine retail outlets, vineyard cellar door sales, wine boutique stores, and gourmet wine retailers.

## Key features

- Off-premises wine retail
- Sealed bottle sales only
- Annual validity
- Source restricted to authorised wholesalers or wineries

## Benefits

- Premium product positioning
- Lower license fees than IMFL retail in some states
- Educated, repeat customer base
- Supports vineyard direct-to-consumer sales
- Aligns with wine tourism

## Limitations

- Limited to wine only (narrower than IMFL or beer)
- Smaller customer base than mass-market liquor
- Requires wine knowledge for staff
- Higher per-unit inventory cost

## Applicability

Most active in Maharashtra (especially Nashik, Pune wine country), Karnataka (Bangalore), Goa, Delhi NCR.

## Documents required

Standard retail license documents: premises proof, NOCs, financial documents, applicant verification.

## Process

Standard retail liquor license process. Timeline: 60 to 120 days.

## Government fees

| Category | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| Annual license fee, small towns | ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, Tier 2 | ₹2,00,000 to ₹5,00,000 |
| Annual license fee, metros | ₹5,00,000 to ₹15,00,000 |

## Validity and renewal

Annual renewable.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can vineyards directly sell wine from their estate?** Yes, vineyards can have a Wine Shoppee License at their estate (cellar door sales). Often called "Estate Sales License."

**2. Can imported wines be sold under this license?** Yes, provided procured from authorised importers and proper customs clearance.

**3. Is online wine sale permitted?** Online wine sale rules vary by state, some allow with delivery licenses (Maharashtra, Goa), others restrict.

**4. What about wine accessories and books?** Selling wine accessories, books, and related merchandise alongside wine is typically permitted and doesn't require separate licenses.

**5. Can wine tasting be conducted at a wine shop?** This depends on state rules, some allow limited in-shop tasting, others require separate tasting permissions.

All service content is for informational purposes. License-specific procedures, fees, and compliance requirements vary significantly by state and may change over time. Consult qualified legal and regulatory professionals or the respective state authorities for the most current and applicable requirements.

services.name: Solvency Certificate
services.shortDescription: Assistance in obtaining bank or authority‑issued solvency certificates.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Solvency Certificate is an official financial document issued by a bank or a competent revenue authority certifying that an individual, firm, or company is financially sound and capable of meeting its obligations. It serves as a formal declaration of the applicant's net worth and creditworthiness based on assets, liabilities, and banking history. Typically issued by scheduled banks or authorities like the District Collector/Tehsildar, a Solvency Certificate is widely used in:

- Government tenders and contracts
- Infrastructure and EPC projects
- Visa and immigration processes
- Court matters and legal proceedings
- Business credibility verification

The certificate reflects that the applicant possesses sufficient assets or financial backing up to a specified amount. Banks assess solvency based on factors such as account balance, fixed deposits, property valuation, credit profile, and financial track record before issuing the certificate. Although not governed by a single centralized statute, solvency certification is recognized across institutions and is often a mandatory requirement in tender eligibility and financial qualification criteria.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Proof of Financial Stability

Demonstrates strong financial position to authorities and institutions.

### Essential for Government Tenders

Often mandatory for bidding in contracts and projects.

### Enhances Business Credibility

Builds trust with clients, partners, and financial institutions.

### Facilitates Loan Processing

Supports creditworthiness assessment for loans and funding.

### Legal & Procedural Requirement

Used in court cases, guarantees, and financial declarations.

### Supports International Applications

Useful for visa and immigration processes.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Dependent on Financial Strength

Only issued if sufficient assets or balances are maintained.

### Time-Consuming Verification

Banks or authorities may take time to verify documents.

### Validity Restrictions

Certificate is valid only for a limited period (often 6–12 months).

### Documentation Intensive

Requires detailed financial and asset-related documents.

### Discretionary Issuance

Issuance depends on the satisfaction of issuing authority.

## 4) Applicability Across India (Coverage)

Solvency Certificates are universally accepted across India, though issuance depends on local authorities and banking institutions.

### Applicable in All Regions

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Ladakh, etc.

### Issuing Authorities

- Scheduled commercial banks
- Cooperative banks (in some cases)
- District Collector / Revenue Authorities

Who Can Apply?

- Individuals
- Proprietors and professionals

- Partnership firms
- Companies and contractors

## 5) Key Features of Solvency Certificate

### Financial Credibility Certification

Confirms applicant's ability to meet financial commitments.

### Amount-Specific Document

Issued for a defined solvency value (e.g., ₹10 lakhs, ₹1 crore).

### Time-Bound Validity

Typically valid for 6–12 months.

### Institution-Based Issuance

Provided by banks or government authorities.

### Based on Asset Evaluation

Considers bank balance, deposits, property, and financial records.

### Widely Accepted Document

Recognized by government departments, courts, and institutions.

## 6) Documents Required

### Basic Documents

- PAN card
- Identity proof (Aadhaar, Passport, etc.)
- Address proof

### Financial Documents

- Bank statements (last 6–12 months)
- Fixed deposit receipts
- Income tax returns

### Asset Documents

- Property ownership documents
- Valuation reports (if required)

### Business Documents (if applicable)

- GST registration
- Company incorporation documents

- Financial statements

### Additional Documents

- Application form (bank/authority specific)
- Passport-size photographs

Requirements may vary depending on issuing authority.

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step-by-Step Solvency Certificate Process

1. Identify Issuing Authority,  Choose bank or revenue authority based on requirement.
2. Prepare Documentation,  Collect financial, asset, and identity documents.
3. Submit Application,  Apply with required forms and supporting documents.
4. Verification Process,  Authority verifies financial position and documents.
5. Asset Evaluation (if required),  Valuation of property or assets may be conducted.
6. Approval by Authority,  Certificate is approved based on financial assessment.
7. Issuance of Certificate,  Solvency Certificate is issued with specified value.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Bank Charges

- ₹1,000 to ₹10,000 (varies by bank and solvency amount)

### Government Authority Fees

- Nominal charges (varies by state and authority)

### Additional Costs

- Valuation charges (for property assessment)

- Professional/consultant fees
- Documentation expenses

### Important Notes

- Fees vary significantly depending on issuing authority
- Higher solvency amounts may involve higher scrutiny and cost
- Charges are non-refundable in most cases

## Conclusion

A Solvency Certificate is a crucial financial document that establishes the credibility and financial strength of an individual or business entity. It plays a vital role in securing government contracts, financial approvals, and legal clearances. While the process involves detailed verification and documentation, obtaining a solvency certificate significantly enhances trust and eligibility in high-value transactions. For businesses and professionals, it serves as a strong indicator of financial reliability and operational capability in both domestic and international contexts.
