services.name: FSSAI Return Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FSSAI Return Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Fssai Return Filing

## Overview

FSSAI Return Filing is a periodic statutory compliance requirement for specific categories of Food Business Operators (FBOs) under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and the Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011. Certain FBOs,  primarily manufacturers and importers,  are required to file annual returns with FSSAI detailing the food products manufactured, imported, and sold during the financial year. There are two types of FSSAI returns relevant to most FBOs: Form D-1 (Annual Return): Filed annually by all food manufacturers and importers holding a State License or Central License. It captures details of food products manufactured/imported during the financial year. Form D-2 (Half-yearly Return): Filed half-yearly (April–September and October–March) specifically by milk and milk product manufacturers. This is a specialised return for dairy FBOs. FSSAI returns serve as a key data source for the food safety authority to monitor production volumes, product categories, and compliance levels across India's food industry. Non-filing or incorrect filing attracts penalties and can affect the license renewal process. While FSSAI Return Filing is often overlooked by small and medium food businesses, it is a non-negotiable statutory obligation for all licensed manufacturers and importers,  and increasing enforcement action by FSSAI makes timely and accurate return filing more important than ever.

## Applicability

Form D-1 (Annual Return),  Mandatory for: All food manufacturers holding a State License or Central License All food importers holding a Central License FBOs engaged in both manufacturing and trading/distribution who manufacture any food product Form D-1 is NOT required for: Basic Registration holders (petty food business operators) Pure traders, distributors, and retailers who do not manufacture any food product Storage operators, transporters, and restaurants/caterers (unless they also manufacture) Form D-2 (Half-yearly Return),  Mandatory for: Licensed manufacturers of milk and milk products (dairy processors, chilling centres, processing plants) Applicable under both State and Central Licenses Filing Deadlines: Return Type Period Filing Deadline Form D-1 (Annual) April 1 – March 31 May 31 of the following year Form D-2 (Half-yearly),  H1 April – September October 31 Form D-2 (Half-yearly),  H2 October – March April 30

Pan-India applicability: Applies uniformly across all states and UTs to all licensed manufacturers and importers,  Karnataka, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, Rajasthan, West Bengal, Telangana, Kerala, Punjab, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, Haryana, Andhra Pradesh, and all others.

## Benefits

Legal compliance: Avoids penalties for non-filing which can range from ₹1 lakh to ₹5 lakh under the FSS Act Smooth license renewal: FSSAI licensing authorities verify return filing compliance at the time of license renewal,  pending returns can stall or block renewals Data-driven credibility: Accurate return filing builds a verified production track record with FSSAI,  useful for government procurement, export registrations, and B2B contracts Audit readiness: Filed FSSAI returns corroborate the production volumes and product categories declared to GST, income tax, and excise authorities,  ensuring cross-compliance Investment and M&A readiness: Investors and acquirers verify FSSAI return compliance during due diligence,  clean filing history is a mark of compliance maturity Export facilitation: APEDA, MPEDA, and other export promotion bodies often seek FSSAI compliance certificates; regular return filing supports export authorisation processes Market access: Certain organised retail chains, hypermarkets, and institutional buyers ask for FSSAI return filing compliance as part of supplier onboarding criteria

## Limitations

Manufacturing-only obligation: Pure traders and retailers are exempt from return filing,  this creates confusion for businesses that have both manufacturing and trading activities (all must file for the manufacturing component) Data accuracy burden: Accurate compilation of product-wise manufacturing/import volumes, pack sizes, and sales figures across 12 months requires robust internal record-keeping systems that many SME food manufacturers lack No correction mechanism post-filing: Once filed, FSSAI returns cannot be easily amended. Errors in product volumes or category descriptions may create discrepancies with other regulatory filings Penalty for non-filing is significant: Fines of ₹1 lakh to ₹5 lakh for non-compliance with licensing conditions (which includes return filing) are disproportionate to the filing effort,  making it a high-risk oversight Portal-based filing dependencies: The FoSCoS portal has historically been subject to technical issues around deadlines,  last-minute filers often encounter portal downtime or login issues Awareness gap: A large proportion of State License holders in Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities are unaware of the return filing obligation, leading to widespread non-compliance that is now attracting increased regulatory attention

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| FSSAI License certificate | Current valid State/Central License |
| Product-wise manufacturing data | Category, product name, quantity manufactured (MT/KL/Units) for the FY |
| Import records (for importers) | Bill of entry, quantity imported, CIF value, product details |
| Sales/dispatch records | Product-wise sales volumes and values for the FY |
| GST returns (GSTR-1/3B) | For cross-referencing sales and turnover data |
| List of food products manufactured | With FSSAI product categories and codes |
| Recall/complaint records (if any) | Any product recalls or consumer complaints during the year |
| Previous year's FSSAI Return | For comparative data and opening balance verification |
| Milk procurement and processing data | For dairy FBOs filing Form D-2 |

## Process

1. Determine Applicability Confirm whether your FSSAI License category (State or Central) and business activity (manufacturing/importing) requires Form D-1 and/or Form D-2 filing.

2. Data Collection and Compilation Gather product-wise manufacturing/import data for the entire financial year. This includes quantity produced, quantity imported, quantity sold, and closing stock, broken down by product category and product name.

3. Cross-Reference with GST and Financial Records Reconcile FSSAI return data with GST returns (GSTR-1, GSTR-3B) and audited financials to ensure consistency. Discrepancies between FSSAI returns and GST/income tax filings can attract scrutiny from multiple regulators.

4. FoSCoS Portal Login Log in to the FoSCoS portal and navigate to the "Annual Return" or "Half-Yearly Return" section under your License.

5. Fill Return Form Online Complete the return form (D-1 or D-2) on the FoSCoS portal with accurate data for each product category. The portal auto-populates the FSSAI License details.

6. Review and Validation Review all entries for accuracy before submission. Check that product categories match the categories mentioned in the FSSAI License. Validate totals and ensure no mandatory fields are blank.

7. Submit Return Submit the return on the FoSCoS portal before the deadline. Download and save the submission acknowledgement with the unique submission reference number.

8. Post-Filing Archival Maintain filed returns and supporting data for at least 2 years (for routine inspections) or 5 years (for legal/M&A purposes). FSSAI inspectors may request copies of filed returns during premises inspections. Fees Return Type Government Filing Fee Remarks Form D-1 (Annual Return) Nil No fee for return filing Form D-2 (Half-yearly Return) Nil No fee for return filing Late filing penalty ₹100 per day (license condition violation) Enforced at renewal or inspection


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Form D-1 Annual Return filing (simple,  single product category) ₹2,000 – ₹4,000 Form D-1 Annual Return filing (moderate,  multiple categories) ₹4,000 – ₹8,000 Form D-2 Half-yearly Return filing (per return) ₹2,500 – ₹5,000 Data compilation and reconciliation support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Retrospective filing (multiple prior years) ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 Annual FSSAI compliance retainer (returns + advisory) ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 p.a.

No government fee is payable for FSSAI return filing. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

FSSAI Returns, once filed, are permanent statutory records. They do not have a "validity period",  they are historical compliance records for the period covered. However, their relevance is highest during: License renewal proceedings (authority checks for pending unfiled returns) Food Safety Officer inspections (inspector may request last 2 years' returns) M&A and investment due diligence (last 3–5 years' returns reviewed) Export registration and institutional buyer onboarding (last 1–2 years' returns may be required)

Renewal FSSAI Returns are a condition of license compliance. At the time of FSSAI License Renewal, the FoSCoS portal and the licensing authority verify whether all pending returns for the license validity period have been filed. If any annual or half-yearly returns are pending, the renewal application may be put on hold until the outstanding returns are filed. Best practice is to file all pending returns before initiating the renewal application to avoid delays in renewal approval.

## Frequently asked questions

**I am a restaurant with a State License,  do I need to file FSSAI Annual Returns?** No. Restaurants, hotels, caterers, dhabas, and food service businesses that do not manufacture packaged food products are exempt from Form D-1 Annual Return filing. The return obligation applies specifically to licensed food manufacturers and importers. If, however, your restaurant also manufactures and sells packaged food products (e.g., bottled sauces, packed namkeen, branded pickles) under its FSSAI License, the manufacturing component would attract return filing obligations.

**We manufacture in one state but sell across India. Do we file the FSSAI return with the Central authority or State authority?** If you hold a State License (single manufacturing unit), the Annual Return is filed with the State Food Safety Department that issued your license,  regardless of where your products are sold. If you hold a Central License (because you sell across states or your turnover exceeds ₹20 crore), the return is filed with FSSAI's Central Licensing Authority through the FoSCoS portal. The key factor is your license type, not your sales geography.

**What details are required in the FSSAI Annual Return Form D-1?** Form D-1 requires product-wise details including: the name of the food product, FSSAI food category code, quantity manufactured during the year (in MT, KL, or relevant unit), quantity imported (for importers), quantity sold during the year, and closing stock. The form also captures information about any product recalls during the year. The data must be broken down by food product category as per FSSAI's food categorisation system,  making internal record-keeping in an organised, category-wise format essential throughout the year.

**We are a small-scale manufacturer of organic jams and fruit preserves with a State License. Our accountant says we don't need to file FSSAI returns. Is this correct?** Your accountant is incorrect. Any food manufacturer holding a State License or Central License,  regardless of the size of the business or the nature of the product,  is required to file Form D-1 Annual Return by May 31. The exemption from return filing applies only to Basic Registration holders and to non-manufacturing license holders (pure traders, retailers, transporters). As a licensed manufacturer, you must file the Annual Return every year.

**Can FSSAI returns be revised or corrected after submission?** The FoSCoS portal does not currently provide a straightforward revision mechanism for submitted FSSAI returns,  once submitted, the return is treated as final. If you have made a material error (e.g., wrong quantity figures, wrong product category), you should immediately notify the licensing authority in writing with the correct data and seek guidance on the correction process. In practice, authorities have some discretion to accept corrigendum submissions in cases of bona fide errors. This underscores the importance of thorough review before submission,  rushing to file at the last minute increases the risk of errors that cannot be easily corrected.

All content is for informational purposes. Specific FSSAI compliance advice should be sought from qualified professionals based on your food business category, scale, and state of operations.

services.name: FSSAI Modification
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FSSAI Modification — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Fssai Modification

## Overview

FSSAI Modification refers to the process of amending an existing FSSAI Registration or License to reflect changes in the food business's details,  such as a change in premises, addition of new food product categories, change in business name, change in proprietor/partners/directors, addition of a new manufacturing unit, or change in the installed capacity of machinery. Food Business Operators (FBOs) are legally required to inform FSSAI of any significant change in business details within the prescribed timeline. Operating under an FSSAI certificate that no longer accurately reflects the actual business,  different address, expanded product range, or changed ownership,  constitutes non-compliance and can result in cancellation of the license and penalties. FSSAI Modification is processed through the FoSCoS portal and requires submission of the modification application along with supporting documents evidencing the change. Depending on the nature of the modification, it may be approved without inspection or may trigger a physical inspection of the premises by a Food Safety Officer. Proactive modification management is a best practice for all scaling food businesses,  it keeps the FSSAI certificate current and avoids regulatory complications during audits, marketplace verifications, or due diligence for investment.

## Applicability

FSSAI Modification is applicable to all existing FSSAI certificate holders,  Basic Registration, State License, and Central License,  when any of the following changes occur: Change in business name,  rebranding or legal name change of the FBO Change in business address,  shifting of manufacturing unit, warehouse, or retail outlet Addition of new food product categories,  expanding into a new food segment not covered in the original certificate Removal of food product categories,  discontinuing certain product lines Change in proprietor/partners/directors,  due to retirement, death, resignation, or induction of new partners/directors Change in installed capacity,  upgrading manufacturing machinery or increasing production capacity Addition of new manufacturing premises,  adding a new factory or production facility Change in nature of business,  e.g., from retail to manufacturing Change in contact details,  mobile number, email, or authorised signatory Upgrade of license category,  from Basic Registration to State License, or from State License to Central License Across India: Applicable in all states and UTs. State-level modifications are processed by the respective State Food Safety Department; Central License modifications by FSSAI's Central Licensing Authority. The FoSCoS portal is the unified application platform across all jurisdictions.

## Benefits

Legal accuracy: Keeps the FSSAI certificate current and legally valid,  prevents cancellation due to mismatched details Audit protection: Food Safety Officers conducting inspections verify premises, products, and capacity against the FSSAI certificate; mismatches result in notices and penalties Marketplace compliance: E-commerce platforms verify FSSAI certificate details against the seller's profile; outdated certificates trigger delisting Business continuity: Addition of new product categories through modification allows the FBO to expand legally without interruption or fresh licensing Investor and buyer confidence: In M&A, franchise, or investment transactions, buyers conduct FSSAI due diligence; updated certificates signal good compliance culture Avoids double registration: Timely modification (e.g., for address change) avoids the need to apply for a fresh registration/license, saving time and money Seamless renewal: Renewals are smoother when all details on the existing certificate are accurate and current

## Limitations

Inspection risk: Modifications involving change of premises or addition of manufacturing units trigger mandatory physical inspection by a Food Safety Officer, which can cause delays Timeline uncertainty: While the FoSCoS portal has deemed approval mechanisms, modifications involving inspections can take 30–60 days or longer Retrospective modification not available: FSSAI does not allow backdating of modifications; if a change was made without modifying the certificate, the FBO is technically in violation for the intervening period Capacity upgrade limitations: Certain capacity expansions may require the business to upgrade from State to Central License rather than simply modifying the existing State License Category additions,  risk of downgrade trigger: Adding regulated food categories (e.g., meat, infant foods) may trigger enhanced compliance requirements or license category change Multiple changes in one application: While multiple changes can be clubbed in a single modification application, it increases document complexity and processing time

## Documents Required

| Document | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Existing FSSAI Certificate (copy) | Reference for current certificate details |
| Application Form (Modification) on FoSCoS | Filled modification application with changed details |
| Proof of new address | Electricity bill, rent agreement, or property documents (for address change) |
| NOC from new premises owner | If the new premises is rented |
| Updated list of food products | For addition/removal of food categories |
| New partnership deed / MoA / board resolution | For change in partners/directors |
| Affidavit from outgoing/incoming partner/director | Confirming change in management |
| Updated layout plan of premises | For new premises or capacity addition |
| List and details of machinery | For capacity enhancement modifications |
| CA certificate for turnover | If modification triggers license category upgrade |
| Photo of new authorised signatory | For change in authorised signatory |
| Government ID proof of new partner/director | PAN, Aadhaar of incoming management personnel |

## Process

1. Identify Nature of Modification Determine the specific change(s) to be incorporated into the FSSAI certificate and assess whether the modification requires inspection or is document-based only.

2. FoSCoS Portal Login Log in to the FoSCoS portal using existing credentials. Navigate to the "Modification" section against the relevant FSSAI Registration/License number.

3. Fill Modification Application Complete the modification application online with accurate details of the proposed changes. Ensure all fields are filled correctly, errors will cause rejection or delays.

4. Document Upload Upload all supporting documents in the prescribed format (PDF, JPEG). Ensure document quality is clear and legible.

5. Fee Payment Pay the applicable modification fee online. The fee varies based on the nature and type of modification and the category of FSSAI authorisation.

6. Application Review by Authority The Registering/Licensing Authority reviews the application. For document-based modifications (e.g., contact details, name change), approval is typically granted within 30 days. For inspection-required modifications, a Food Safety Officer conducts a premises inspection.

7. Grant of Modified Certificate Upon approval, the modified FSSAI Registration/License Certificate is generated digitally on the FoSCoS portal. The FBO downloads and uses the updated certificate.

8. Update Business Materials Update the FSSAI number and certificate details on food packaging, signage, e-commerce profiles, and marketing materials to reflect the modified certificate. Fees Modification Type Government Fee (Approx.) Basic Registration modification ₹100 – ₹200 State License modification (minor) ₹1,000 – ₹2,000 State License modification (major, new premises/capacity) ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Central License modification (minor) ₹2,500 – ₹5,000 Central License modification (major) ₹5,000 – ₹7,500


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Basic Registration modification (end-to-end) ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 State License modification support ₹3,000 – ₹7,000 Central License modification support ₹6,000 – ₹15,000 Consultation for modification eligibility ₹1,000 – ₹2,500 Inspection support and preparation ₹2,000 – ₹5,000

Government fees are paid via FoSCoS portal. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

A modification does not reset or extend the validity of the existing FSSAI Registration or License. The modified certificate retains the same expiry date as the original certificate. The FBO must apply for renewal separately before the expiry date. In cases where the modification results in a license category upgrade (e.g., Basic to State), the new license is issued with a fresh validity period chosen at the time of the upgrade application.

Renewal Renewal of an FSSAI certificate with pending modifications is discouraged. Best practice is to: Complete all modifications first and ensure the certificate is current Then file the renewal application on FoSCoS with the updated details reflected in the modified certificate Filing renewal with outdated details (i.e., applying for renewal of a certificate that already has changes not yet modified) creates inconsistencies in the FSSAI database and can complicate future compliance If the certificate is close to expiry and a modification is pending, it is advisable to submit both the modification and renewal applications simultaneously to avoid any gap in authorisation.

## Frequently asked questions

**I have renamed my food brand. Do I need to modify my FSSAI License or get a fresh one?** If only your brand/trade name has changed but your legal business entity name remains the same, you may not need a formal modification for the FSSAI license (since FSSAI licenses are issued to the legal entity, not the brand). However, if your legal entity name has also changed,  for example, your company was renamed through MCA,  you must file a modification application with FSSAI with the updated MoA and certificate of incorporation. Operating under a certificate with a different legal name than your current registered name is a compliance risk.

**We've started manufacturing a new product category (e.g., we're adding dairy products to our existing bakery license). Do we need a fresh license or a modification?** You need a modification to add the new food category to your existing FSSAI License. The modification application should specify the new category of food products being added. Depending on the risk level of the new category, this may trigger a physical inspection by a Food Safety Officer. You should not manufacture the new category before the modified license reflecting the addition is issued,  doing so constitutes unauthorised manufacturing under FSSAI.

**Our food manufacturing unit has shifted to a new location within the same city. Is FSSAI modification sufficient or do we need a fresh license?** A modification application for change of address/premises is the correct approach,  not a fresh license. You must file the modification before shifting operations to the new premises (or immediately upon shifting). The modification will typically trigger a physical inspection of the new premises to verify food safety compliance before the updated certificate is issued. Manufacturing from the new premises without an updated FSSAI certificate reflecting the new address is a violation.

**How long does FSSAI take to process a modification application?** Under the FoSCoS system, document-based modifications (name change, contact details, partner change) are typically processed within 30 days. Modifications requiring physical inspection (new premises, capacity enhancement) can take 30–60 days depending on the workload of the Food Safety Officer in your jurisdiction. If the authority does not respond within 30 days for non-inspection modifications, deemed approval applies. Tracking the application status on FoSCoS is recommended.

**Can I modify my Basic FSSAI Registration to add manufacturing activities that were not part of the original application?** Yes, provided the added manufacturing activity and the overall business still falls within the Basic Registration eligibility criteria (annual turnover below ₹12 lakh and no restricted food categories). If the new manufacturing activity places you in a restricted or high-risk food category (e.g., proprietary foods, infant foods, meat processing), you will need to upgrade to a State License,  modification of the Basic Registration alone will not be sufficient. Our team can advise on whether a modification or a fresh license application is the appropriate path.

services.content:
# Gst Annual Return Gstr-10 Filing (final Return)

## Overview

GSTR-10, commonly known as the Final Return, is a one-time GST return that must be filed by every taxpayer whose GST registration has been cancelled or surrendered. It is the last formal compliance requirement under a cancelled GSTIN,  and it finalises all GST obligations, particularly the reversal of Input Tax Credit on closing stock of goods held as on the effective date of cancellation. GSTR-10 is filed on the GST portal and must be submitted within 3 months from the date of the cancellation order or the date of cancellation,  whichever is earlier. For example, if GST registration is cancelled with effective date October 31 and the cancellation order is issued on November 10, the GSTR-10 must be filed by February 10 (3 months from November 10). GSTR-10 is applicable for: All regular taxpayers whose registration has been cancelled voluntarily (Form GST REG-16) or suo motu by the GST officer All composition scheme dealers whose registration has been cancelled GSTR-10 is NOT applicable for: Input Service Distributors Non-Resident Taxable Persons (they file final GSTR-5) Casual Taxable Persons The GSTR-10 captures the closing stock details, ITC attributable to the closing stock (which must be reversed and paid), and the total tax payable. It is a critical compliance document,  non-filing or late filing attracts significant penalties.

## Applicability

GSTR-10 (Final Return) is mandatory for all regular and composition GST-registered taxpayers who have: Voluntarily applied for cancellation of GST registration (Form GST REG-16) Had their GST registration cancelled by the GST officer (suo motu cancellation under Section 29(2)) Had their registration cancelled pursuant to a court or tribunal order Undergone business transfer, merger, or dissolution requiring GSTIN cancellation Applicable pan-India,  all states and UTs.

## Features

One-time filing: GSTR-10 is filed exactly once,  after the GST registration is cancelled; it is not a periodic return Closing stock ITC reversal: The core of GSTR-10,  declaring the value of closing stock (inputs, semi-finished, finished goods, capital goods) as on the date of cancellation and reversing the ITC thereon ITC reversal computation: The amount to be paid = ITC in respect of inputs held in stock, or the output tax payable on such goods (at the applicable GST rate),  whichever is higher Late fee structure: If GSTR-10 is not filed within 3 months of the cancellation date, CBIC issues a notice in Form GSTR-10 directing filing within 15 days. Failure leads to assessment and best judgment determination of ITC reversal. Late fee: ₹200/day (₹100 CGST + ₹100 SGST), subject to a maximum of ₹10,000 Pre-populated with cancellation data: The GSTR-10 form on the GST portal pre-populates the taxpayer's details and effective cancellation date from the cancellation order All pending GSTR-3Bs must be filed first: GSTR-10 cannot be filed if there are pending GSTR-3B returns,  all regular returns up to the cancellation date must be filed before GSTR-10

## Benefits

Clean GST closure: Filing GSTR-10 formally closes the taxpayer's GST obligations,  no further liability or compliance requirement under the cancelled GSTIN Prevents officer-initiated assessment: If GSTR-10 is not filed within 3 months, the GST officer may make a best-judgment assessment of the ITC reversal liability,  which could be higher than the actual liability computed by the taxpayer Legal finality: GSTR-10 creates a legal record of all closing stock and ITC reversal,  protecting against future demands on the cancelled GSTIN Refund eligibility: If after ITC reversal there is still an excess balance in the Electronic Credit Ledger, it can be claimed as a refund after filing GSTR-10 Unblocks portal for related entities: In group company structures, a cancelled GSTIN with unfiled GSTR-10 can sometimes cause compliance complications for related GSTINs,  filing GSTR-10 resolves this

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Cancellation order (GST REG-19) | Effective date of cancellation |
| Closing stock details | List of all inputs, semi-finished goods, finished goods, and capital goods held as on the date of cancellation |
| Purchase invoices for closing stock | To compute ITC attributable to closing stock |
| ITC register | To identify ITC availed on goods now in closing stock |
| All pending GSTR-3B filings | Must be completed before GSTR-10 |
| Stock valuation report | For determining the higher of ITC vs. output tax on closing stock |

## Process

1. Complete All Pending GSTR-3B Filings Ensure GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B are filed for all return periods up to the effective date of GST registration cancellation. GSTR-10 cannot be submitted if there are pending GSTR-3Bs.

2. Identify Closing Stock Prepare a detailed inventory of all goods (inputs, semi-finished, finished, capital goods) held as on the effective date of cancellation. Include quantity, value, and HSN code.

3. Compute ITC Reversal For each item in closing stock: compute the ITC availed when the goods were purchased (from purchase invoices). Also compute the output tax payable on the closing stock at the applicable GST rate. The ITC reversal = higher of ITC on closing stock OR output tax on closing stock.

4. Log In and Access GSTR-10 Log in to the GST portal. Navigate to Returns → Final Return → GSTR-10.

5. Fill GSTR-10 The system auto-populates ARN of the cancellation order and the effective date. Enter details of closing stock (Table 7A, goods, Table 7B, capital goods), the ITC to be reversed, and the output tax payable.

6. Pay the ITC Reversal Amount If the Electronic Credit Ledger has sufficient balance, the reversal is debited from the credit ledger. If insufficient, pay the balance through the Electronic Cash Ledger (NEFT/RTGS/online payment on GST portal).

7. Submit and File GSTR-10 Submit the GSTR-10. The system requires DSC or EVC for filing. An ARN is generated on successful submission.

8. Claim Refund (if applicable) If post-reversal there is an excess credit balance in the Electronic Credit Ledger, file Form RFD-01 for refund of the excess. Fees Service Government Fee GSTR-10 Filing Nil


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee GSTR-10 filing support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Closing stock and ITC reversal computation ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Pending GSTR-3B filing before GSTR-10 ₹500 – ₹2,000 per return End-to-end GST cancellation + GSTR-10 package ₹8,000 – ₹20,000

Government Late Fees Amount Late filing of GSTR-10 ₹200/day (max ₹10,000)

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

Compliance File GSTR-10 within 3 months of the cancellation date or order,  whichever is earlier File all pending GSTR-3Bs before submitting GSTR-10 Pay the ITC reversal amount before or at the time of filing GSTR-10 Respond to GSTR-10 notices (issued by officer if not filed within 3 months) within the 15-day response window Archive all GSTR-10 records and supporting stock valuation documents for 6 years

## Common Mistakes

Filing GSTR-10 before all GSTR-3Bs are filed: The portal will not allow GSTR-10 submission if there are pending GSTR-3B returns Ignoring GSTR-10 entirely: The most common mistake,  businesses that cancel their GSTIN consider compliance done after the REG-16 application and completely overlook the mandatory GSTR-10 filing Incorrect closing stock valuation: Under-declaring closing stock to reduce ITC reversal liability,  this is a compliance risk and can be detected in a GST audit Not claiming excess credit ledger refund: After ITC reversal in GSTR-10, if there's still excess credit balance, businesses often leave it unclaimed,  this money is a legitimate refund Missing the 3-month deadline: The 3-month window from the cancellation date or order runs quickly,  businesses that don't track this deadline end up with late fees of ₹200/day

## Frequently asked questions

**Our company's GST was cancelled by the officer (compulsory cancellation) because we didn't file returns for 6 months. We want to revoke the cancellation. Do we still need to file GSTR-10?** No,  if you are applying for revocation of cancellation (to resume your GST registration), you should NOT file GSTR-10. GSTR-10 is the final return filed when you permanently close the GSTIN. To revoke a compulsorily cancelled registration, file all pending returns first and then file the revocation application (Form GST REG-21). GSTR-10 is only for when the cancellation is permanent and intended. Filing GSTR-10 when seeking revocation would permanently close the GSTIN, defeating the purpose.

**We have no closing stock on the date of cancellation. Do we still need to file GSTR-10?** Yes. GSTR-10 is mandatory for all cancellation situations,  even if you have zero closing stock. In this case, GSTR-10 is filed with NIL closing stock details,  the ITC reversal will be zero. The filing is still a required legal compliance step to formally close the GST obligations under the cancelled GSTIN. Many businesses with zero stock skip GSTR-10, leading to late fee notices from the GST department.

**We cancelled our GST registration 5 months ago and completely forgot to file GSTR-10. What should we do now?** File GSTR-10 immediately. The late fee will be ₹200/day for the days beyond the 3-month deadline (₹100 CGST + ₹100 SGST),  up to a maximum of ₹10,000. Also check if the GST officer has issued a best-judgment assessment notice (under Section 62 of the CGST Act),  if a notice has been received, respond and file GSTR-10 within the notice period. Continuing to ignore GSTR-10 after the 3-month deadline makes the officer's best-judgment assessment final,  which may result in a higher ITC reversal demand than your actual liability.

**Our GST was cancelled because we merged with another company. The merged entity's GSTIN is active. Who files GSTR-10 for the old entity?** The old entity (the merged/absorbed company) must file GSTR-10 for its cancelled GSTIN. This is typically the responsibility of the authorised signatory of the old entity,  who continues to have access to the old GSTIN's GST portal credentials until GSTR-10 is filed and the cancellation is formally completed. In practice, the CFO or Company Secretary of the merged company's post-merger management handles this closure filing. Ensure GSTR-10 is filed as part of the merger completion checklist,  it is often overlooked in M&A integration.

**What happens to the unclaimed ITC in my Electronic Credit Ledger after GST cancellation?** The ITC balance in the Electronic Credit Ledger at the time of cancellation is first used to offset the ITC reversal liability (on closing stock). If the ITC reversal amount is less than the credit ledger balance, the remaining credit balance can be claimed as a refund through Form RFD-01 after filing GSTR-10. If the credit ledger balance is insufficient to cover the ITC reversal, the shortfall must be paid through the Electronic Cash Ledger. In no circumstance does the remaining ITC "lapse" without either being used for reversal or refunded,  but you must actively file the refund claim; it is not automatically credited.

Tax Audit,  Overview

Our consultants support Tax Audit under INCOME TAX, from applicability review through filings, department queries and final acknowledgement.

Applicability

This service is intended for clients who need structured, compliant handling with clear documentation and realistic timelines.

Benefits

Fewer rejections from incomplete paperwork, coordinated submissions, and a single point of contact for follow-ups.

Process

Requirement capture → document checklist → preparation and validation → submission to authority → tracking and query handling → closure.

Documents required

Entity KYC, address proof, category-specific forms and annexures. A tailored checklist is shared after the first consultation.

services.name: ICEGATE Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for ICEGATE Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Icegate Registration

## Overview

ICEGATE (Indian Customs Electronic Gateway) is the national portal of the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC), Government of India, that serves as the single electronic interface between trade, cargo, and the Indian Customs department. It is the digital backbone of India's customs clearance system,  enabling the electronic filing of import and export documents, online payment of customs duties, tracking of consignments, and access to a wide range of customs-related services. ICEGATE was developed and is maintained by the National Informatics Centre (NIC) in collaboration with CBIC, and handles millions of customs transactions every year. The portal integrates with other critical trade systems including the Indian Customs EDI System (ICES), the DGFT portal for EXIM policy, the Reserve Bank of India for FEMA reporting, the GST portal for e-way bill and refund linkages, and various Port Community Systems (PCS) at major seaports and airports. ICEGATE Registration is the process by which an importer, exporter, customs broker (CHA,  Customs House Agent), freight forwarder, shipping line, airline, port operator, or any other trade facilitation entity registers on the ICEGATE portal to gain access to its digital customs services. Without a valid ICEGATE registration, importers and exporters cannot electronically file Bills of Entry (for imports) or Shipping Bills (for exports),  the foundational documents of customs clearance. Key services accessible through ICEGATE after registration include: Electronic filing of Bills of Entry and Shipping Bills Online payment of customs duty, IGST, and other levies through ICEPAY Real-time tracking of consignment status and customs clearance progress Access to customs duty drawback status and refund tracking e-Sanchit,  electronic document upload system (paperless customs) SWIFT (Single Window Interface for Facilitating Trade),  for importing of goods requiring NOC from multiple government agencies Export Promotion Schemes,  MEIS, RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation tracking Risk Management System (RMS),  automated customs assessment and examination IGST Refund for exporters,  automated refund linked to GSTN ICEGATE registration is distinct from IEC (Import Export Code) registration,  IEC is the DGFT-issued authorisation to conduct import/export; ICEGATE is the customs portal through which the actual transactions are processed.

## Applicability

ICEGATE registration is required by: Importers: All entities importing goods into India who file or cause to be filed Bills of Entry Exporters: All entities exporting goods from India who file or cause to be filed Shipping Bills Customs House Agents (CHAs) / Customs Brokers: Licensed intermediaries who file customs documents on behalf of importers and exporters Freight Forwarders: Companies coordinating international shipment and customs clearance Shipping Lines and Airlines: Carriers who file Import General Manifest (IGM) and Export General Manifest (EGM) with customs Port Operators and ICDs (Inland Container Depots): For cargo management and customs coordination Steamer Agents: Representing shipping lines for port operations Air Cargo Agents: Filing cargo manifests with customs authorities Banks: Participating in online customs duty payment Export Promotion Councils: For RCMC and export data reporting Geographical applicability: ICEGATE is a pan-India system covering all major customs ports,  JNCH (Nhava Sheva, Mumbai), Chennai Seaport, Kolkata Port, Mundra, Kandla, Hazira, IGIA (Delhi airport), CSIA (Mumbai airport), Bengaluru airport, Hyderabad airport, and all other designated customs ports, ICDs, and CFSs (Container Freight Stations) across India.

## Benefits

Paperless customs clearance: ICEGATE enables the complete digitisation of customs documentation,  Bills of Entry, Shipping Bills, manifests, and annexures are filed electronically, eliminating physical document submission at customs houses 24x7 operations: ICEGATE is available round-the-clock,  importers and exporters can file customs documents, pay duty, and track consignments at any time, from anywhere Faster customs clearance: Electronic filing through ICEGATE feeds directly into the ICES (Indian Customs EDI System),  enabling automated risk profiling under the Risk Management System (RMS), which allows compliant consignments to be cleared with minimal or no physical examination e-Sanchit document repository: Supporting documents (invoices, packing lists, certificates of origin, licenses) are uploaded on e-Sanchit and referenced in customs filings,  eliminating physical submission of paper documents at the customs house SWIFT,  single window clearance: For imports requiring NOC/clearance from agencies like FSSAI, Plant Quarantine, Animal Quarantine, Drug Controller, BIS, etc., the SWIFT module on ICEGATE enables single-window electronic application,  avoiding separate applications to each agency Real-time duty payment (ICEPAY): Customs duty can be paid online 24x7 through net banking, NEFT, RTGS, or duty credit scrip,  immediate duty credit confirmation speeds up clearance IGST refund automation: Exporters' IGST refund claims are processed automatically through the ICEGATE-GSTN data linkage,  no separate refund application needed for most categories Duty drawback tracking: ICEGATE provides real-time tracking of duty drawback claims,  giving exporters visibility on claim status and credit timelines RoDTEP and MEIS/FPS tracking: Export incentive scheme credits are tracked and credited through ICEGATE,  enabling exporters to monitor and utilise their EXIM scheme benefits Business intelligence: Consignment-level import and export data accessible through ICEGATE enables businesses to analyse trade volumes, duty costs, and clearance timelines

## Limitations

Technical complexity: ICEGATE has a steep learning curve,  first-time users often require assistance to navigate the portal, register correctly, and file documents in the correct format IEC prerequisite: IEC from DGFT is mandatory before ICEGATE registration,  importers/exporters without an active IEC cannot complete the ICEGATE registration process DSC mandatory for certain users: CHAs, Shipping Lines, and Airline Agents require a Class 3 Digital Signature Certificate (DSC) for ICEGATE registration,  adding to setup cost and time Port-specific codes: ICEGATE uses port-specific ICEGATE codes,  the same entity may need to register separately for different customs ports if they operate from multiple ports System downtime: ICEGATE maintenance windows and technical issues can disrupt time-sensitive customs filings,  backup procedures exist but cause delays Customs broker dependency: Most first-time importers/exporters are not registered directly on ICEGATE and rely on CHAs,  direct ICEGATE access requires technical capability that many SMEs lack Data accuracy pressure: Errors in electronically filed documents (BoE/SB) are difficult to amend post-submission,  the electronic system requires higher data accuracy than paper-based systems e-Sanchit document size restrictions: Document upload size limits on e-Sanchit can cause issues with large multi-document consignments

## Documents

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| IEC (Import Export Code) | Active DGFT-issued IEC,  mandatory prerequisite |
| PAN of the entity | Business PAN matching IEC records |
| GSTIN | Active GST registration |
| Certificate of Incorporation / Partnership Deed | Legal entity proof |
| Bank account details | Account linked to IEC and ICEGATE for duty payments |
| Email ID and mobile number | For ICEGATE account creation and OTP verification |
| Digital Signature Certificate (Class 3) | For CHA/Shipping Line/Airline registrations,  individual DSC |
| CHA License number | For Customs House Agents,  CBIC-issued CHA license |
| ICEGATE registration form | Online form with entity and user details |
| Authorisation letter | For multi-user accounts,  authorising specific individuals as ICEGATE users |
| Port of operation | Specific customs port for which registration is sought |
| KYC documents of key personnel | PAN, Aadhaar, photos of directors/partners |

## Process

1. Obtain IEC from DGFT Before ICEGATE registration, ensure your IEC (Import Export Code) is active and updated on the DGFT portal (dgft.gov.in). ICEGATE validates IEC details during registration, outdated IEC records will cause ICEGATE registration errors.

2. Visit ICEGATE Portal Go to icegate.gov.in. Navigate to the "Register" or "New User Registration" section. ICEGATE has separate registration pathways for different user types, Importer/Exporter, Customs Broker/CHA, Shipping Line, Airline, Port Operator, and Bank.

3. Select User Type Choose the appropriate user type, "IE" (Importer/Exporter), "CHA" (Customs Broker), "SL" (Shipping Line), "AIR" (Airline Agent), etc. The registration form and required documents differ by user type.

4. Fill Registration Form Complete the ICEGATE registration form, entity details (name, IEC, GSTIN, PAN), contact details (email, mobile), bank account details, and port of operation. For CHA: enter CHA license number, GSTIN, and DSC details.

5. Document Upload Upload all required documents, IEC certificate, PAN, GSTIN certificate, Incorporation certificate, and authorisation letter (for CHA filings on behalf of importers/exporters).

6. DSC Registration (for CHAs and carriers) CHAs, Shipping Lines, and Airline Agents must register their Class 3 DSC on ICEGATE, the DSC is used for digital signing of customs filings. Connect the DSC token to the computer and register the certificate on the ICEGATE portal.

7. ICEGATE Verification and Approval ICEGATE authorities verify the submitted information against IEC, PAN, and GST databases. For Importer/Exporter registrations, the process is largely automated, ICEGATE code is generated upon successful data validation.

8. Receive ICEGATE Code Upon successful registration, an ICEGATE code (user ID) is issued to the entity, this is the identifier used for all future customs filings and transactions. Email confirmation and ICEGATE login credentials are provided.

9. ICEPAY Bank Account Linkage Link your bank account to ICEPAY (ICEGATE's payment gateway) for online customs duty payment. This involves a net banking setup verification with your bank.

10. e-Sanchit Registration (if needed) For importers who upload supporting documents electronically, complete the e-Sanchit module setup, enabling document upload and reference number generation for customs clearance. Fees Registration Type Government Fee Remarks Importer/Exporter ICEGATE registration Nil Free registration CHA (Customs Broker) ICEGATE registration Nil CHA License fee separate Shipping Line ICEGATE registration Nil Airline Agent ICEGATE registration Nil ICEGATE DSC registration Nil DSC itself costs ₹800–₹3,000


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee ICEGATE registration (importer/exporter) ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 ICEGATE registration (CHA/freight forwarder) ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 DSC procurement and ICEGATE setup ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 ICEPAY bank linkage setup ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 e-Sanchit module setup and training ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Full ICEGATE onboarding package ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 ICEGATE error resolution / troubleshooting ₹3,000 – ₹8,000

Government registration on ICEGATE is free. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Timeline

Step Typical Timeline IEC obtention (prerequisite) 1–3 working days (if not already held) ICEGATE registration form submission 1 working day ICEGATE data validation and code issuance 2–5 working days DSC procurement and registration (for CHAs) 3–7 working days (token delivery) ICEPAY bank account linkage 1–3 working days e-Sanchit module activation Same day or next working day Total end-to-end (first-time setup) 5–15 working days

For importers/exporters who already have an IEC, ICEGATE registration is often completed within 3–7 working days. CHAs with DSC requirements may take up to 15 working days.

Use Cases 1. First-Time Importer of Industrial Machinery A manufacturing company importing CNC machines from Germany for the first time needs to register on ICEGATE to file their Bill of Entry electronically, pay customs duty online, and track their cargo through the ICES system. Without ICEGATE registration, they cannot directly file or even monitor their customs clearance,  they would be entirely dependent on their CHA without any direct access to their shipment status. 2. Garment Exporter Using RoDTEP Scheme A Tiruppur-based garment exporter shipping to Europe uses ICEGATE to file Shipping Bills electronically, reference their RCMC and LUT details, and receive automatic credit of RoDTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products) scrips. The RoDTEP credit is directly linked to the Shipping Bill data on ICEGATE and credited to the exporter's Electronic Credit Ledger,  making ICEGATE the operational backbone of their export incentive claim. 3. Customs House Agent (CHA) Onboarding A logistics company in Chennai that has obtained a CHA license from CBIC registers on ICEGATE with their DSC to begin filing customs documents on behalf of their importer and exporter clients. The CHA's ICEGATE registration enables them to act as the legal agent for customs filings,  without it, the CHA cannot operate digitally even with a valid CHA license. 4. Pharmaceutical Importer Using SWIFT A pharmaceutical company importing Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) from China uses ICEGATE's SWIFT (Single Window Interface) module to simultaneously apply for clearance from the Drug Controller (CDSCO) as part of the Bill of Entry filing,  avoiding separate manual applications to CDSCO and integrating the NOC approval directly into the customs clearance workflow. 5. E-Commerce Exporter Tracking IGST Refund A Bengaluru-based e-commerce company exporting handicrafts to US consumers (with IGST paid on export) uses ICEGATE to track the automated processing of their IGST refund,  the Shipping Bill data on ICEGATE is matched with their GSTR-1 on the GSTN portal, and the refund is processed automatically and credited to their bank account. ICEGATE provides real-time status of refund processing at each stage.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is ICEGATE registration the same as IEC registration?** Can one be used in place of the other? No,  they are distinct registrations from different government bodies serving different purposes. IEC (Import Export Code) is a 10-digit authorisation code issued by the DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) under the Ministry of Commerce,  it is the fundamental licence to conduct import/export transactions in India. ICEGATE registration is a portal account on the Indian Customs Electronic Gateway,  it gives the entity access to file customs documents (Bills of Entry and Shipping Bills) electronically and use customs-related digital services. IEC is the prerequisite for ICEGATE registration (ICEGATE validates your IEC during registration), but having an IEC without an ICEGATE account means you can still conduct imports/exports but only through a CHA who is registered on ICEGATE,  you wouldn't have direct access to your customs transactions.

**Do small importers and exporters who use a Customs House Agent (CHA) also need their own ICEGATE registration?** Not necessarily,  if you are a small importer/exporter who exclusively uses a licensed CHA for all your customs filings, the CHA files all documents on ICEGATE using their own registration and acts on your behalf. You don't need your own ICEGATE account for this model. However, having your own ICEGATE account provides significant operational benefits: direct real-time visibility into your consignment's customs status, direct customs duty payment capability, direct access to drawback and RoDTEP credit tracking, and the ability to communicate directly with customs authorities,  particularly valuable as your import/export volumes grow.

**We are a startup that recently got our IEC and want to start exporting software services. Do we need ICEGATE registration for software/service exports?** ICEGATE is primarily relevant for physical goods,  it processes Bills of Entry (for goods imports) and Shipping Bills (for goods exports) at customs ports. Software and services exported digitally (via electronic transfer, cloud, or internet) do not pass through physical customs and do not require ICEGATE filing. For software service exports, your primary compliance platforms are the DGFT portal (for IEC), the GSTN portal (for LUT filing and GSTR-1 reporting of exports), STPI (Software Technology Parks of India,  for SOFTEX filing for software exports above USD 25,000), and your AD bank (for FEMA compliance on foreign remittances). ICEGATE would only be relevant if you are exporting physical goods like packaged software CDs, hardware, or other tangible items.

**Our ICEGATE registration was done by our previous CHA and we've lost access to it. How do we recover or re-register?** This is a common challenge when companies change their CHA. ICEGATE accounts registered by a CHA on behalf of an importer/exporter are generally in the entity's name (using the entity's IEC and PAN) even if the CHA set them up. To recover access: contact ICEGATE helpdesk (helpdesk@icegate.gov.in or 1800-3010-1000) with your IEC, PAN, and entity details,  they can assist with password reset and user credential recovery. If the original account cannot be recovered, you may need to register a fresh ICEGATE account,  ICEGATE generally allows re-registration when the original account credentials are entirely lost and the entity's IEC/PAN is verified.

**How does ICEGATE's e-Sanchit system work and is it mandatory?** e-Sanchit (Electronic Storage and Computerised Handling of Indirect Tax Documents) is a document management system on ICEGATE where importers/exporters upload supporting documents digitally,  commercial invoices, packing lists, bills of lading, certificates of origin, licenses, and permits. These uploaded documents are assigned a unique IRN (Image Reference Number) that is referenced in the Bill of Entry or Shipping Bill,  replacing the physical submission of documents at the customs house. e-Sanchit is effectively mandatory for importers filing Bills of Entry electronically, as customs officers are increasingly expecting document references rather than physical paper copies. For exporters, Shipping Bills typically reference fewer documents, but e-Sanchit upload of licenses, RCMC, and certificates is increasingly expected for smooth clearance.

services.name: IEC Modification
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for IEC Modification — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Iec Modification

## Overview

IEC Modification is the process of updating or amending the details of an existing Import Export Code (IEC) registered on the DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) portal. The IEC is the fundamental authorisation for conducting import and export activities in India,  issued once and valid for the lifetime of the entity. However, businesses undergo changes over time,  change of address, change in bank account, addition of new partners or directors, change in business name, addition of export/import branches,  all of which must be reflected in the IEC by filing a modification application with DGFT. IEC Modification became particularly important after DGFT's migration to the new, Aadhaar and PAN-integrated IEC system in April 2021. Under the updated framework: IEC details are now directly linked with PAN data from the Income Tax database and GST data from the GSTN portal,  any mismatch between IEC records and PAN/GST data triggers alerts and can block customs transactions All IEC holders must mandatorily update (confirm or modify) their IEC details every year between April 1 and June 30,  the Annual IEC Updation,  failing which the IEC is deactivated IEC Modification is required for both material changes (where actual business information has changed) and for the mandatory annual update (where details are confirmed unchanged). Both are processed online through the DGFT portal at dgft.gov.in, are free of cost, and are processed within a few working days. Outdated or incorrect IEC details create direct operational problems,  customs port authorities cross-check IEC details for every consignment, and mismatches between the IEC record and the shipping documents cause clearance delays, Bill of Entry rejections, and in some cases, consignment holds at the port.

## Applicability

IEC Modification is applicable to: All IEC holders,  for the mandatory Annual IEC Updation (April 1 to June 30 every year) IEC holders with changed registered address,  whenever the principal place of business shifts IEC holders with changed bank account details,  whenever the bank account linked to the IEC changes Companies with changed directors,  addition or removal of directors due to resignation, appointment, or retirement Partnerships where partners have changed,  induction or exit of partners IEC holders with changed business name,  after a legal name change of the entity IEC holders adding new branch offices,  for exporters/importers adding new export/import branches or warehouses IEC holders whose GST details have changed,  new GSTIN, GSTIN cancellation, or GSTIN amendment Proprietorships where the proprietor has changed,  succession, marriage name change, or legal change Annual IEC Update (mandatory for all): Every IEC holder,  regardless of whether any details have changed,  must log in to the DGFT portal between April 1 and June 30 and either confirm that all details are current (no change update) or make the required modifications. Failure to complete this Annual Update results in automatic deactivation of the IEC. A deactivated IEC blocks all customs clearances and DGFT scheme applications. Applicable pan-India,  IEC and IEC Modification are central (DGFT) processes applicable uniformly across all states and UTs.

## Benefits

Operational continuity: Updated IEC details prevent customs clearance rejections caused by document mismatches,  particularly for address, bank account, and director/partner name fields that are routinely checked during port operations Annual reactivation: The Annual IEC Update (even if no changes are needed) prevents the IEC from being automatically deactivated,  maintaining uninterrupted import/export capability GSTN alignment: IEC details must align with GSTIN records,  modification ensures consistency between DGFT, Customs, and GST databases, preventing cross-system reconciliation issues Scheme benefit continuity: DGFT schemes (RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation, EPCG) require an active, accurate IEC,  modifications maintain eligibility for these export promotion benefits Banking compliance: Banks process export/import transactions against the IEC,  bank account changes must be reflected to prevent FEMA compliance issues in export proceeds realization RCMC alignment: RCMC (Registration-cum-Membership Certificate) with EPCs is linked to IEC details,  IEC modifications may trigger corresponding RCMC updates for continued scheme eligibility Legal accuracy: The IEC is a legal document issued by the Government of India,  inaccurate information creates regulatory risk, particularly in FEMA compliance and customs valuation contexts

## Limitations

Annual update mandatory regardless of changes: Even if nothing has changed, the annual April–June update is mandatory,  failure to complete it deactivates the IEC regardless of the entity's compliance history Deactivation if annual update is missed: An IEC deactivated due to missed annual update cannot be used for any import/export transaction,  reactivation requires completing the delayed update and a waiting period PAN-linked constraints: Since IEC is now PAN-linked, any mismatch between the entity's PAN data and IEC data requires PAN correction first,  DGFT cannot override PAN database records Director/partner changes require DSC of exiting/incoming persons: Modifications involving change of signatories require DSC of the new authorised signatory,  which takes time to procure if the new director/partner doesn't have one No modification during dispute: If the IEC is under suspension or adjudication for a DGFT/customs violation, modifications may be restricted until the dispute is resolved ICEGATE update required separately: Modifying IEC on the DGFT portal does not automatically update ICEGATE,  the importer/exporter must also update their ICEGATE profile separately, particularly for bank account changes linked to ICEPAY

## Documents

Document Required Modification Type Online modification form on DGFT portal (ANF-2A) All modification types PAN of the entity All modification types Aadhaar and DSC of authorised signatory All modification types New address proof Address change (electricity bill, rent agreement, property document) New bank certificate / cancelled cheque Bank account change Updated Certificate of Incorporation Company name change MoA amendment / ROC order Company name change Board resolution for new authorized signatory Director change or new signatory New director's PAN and Aadhaar Director addition/change Updated partnership deed Partner addition/change in partnership GST registration certificate (updated) If GSTIN has changed Trade licence / MSME certificate (if any) Supporting business existence documents

## Process

1. Log In to DGFT Portal Go to dgft.gov.in. Log in using your IEC credentials (IEC number, registered email, and password). If it is the annual update period (April 1 to June 30), a mandatory update prompt will appear on the dashboard.

2. Navigate to IEC Modification Go to Services → IEC Profile Management → Update/Modify IEC Details. The current IEC details are displayed pre-populated from the DGFT database.

3. Identify Fields to Modify Review all fields, entity details, address, bank account, authorised signatory, directors/partners, and GSTIN. Identify all fields that need to be updated. For Annual Update with no changes: simply confirm all details are current.

4. Make Required Changes Update the relevant fields, address, bank account number and IFSC code, director/partner details, GSTIN, etc. The portal validates each field against PAN, GST, and other government databases in real time.

5. Upload Supporting Documents Upload the supporting documents for each changed field, address proof for address change, new bank certificate for bank account change, ROC documents for name change, etc. Documents must be in PDF format and within the specified file size limit.

6. Aadhaar Authentication / DSC Signing The modification application must be authenticated by the authorised signatory using either Aadhaar OTP (for proprietors and individuals) or DSC (for companies and LLPs). Sign the application digitally.

7. Submit the Modification Application Submit the completed modification application on the DGFT portal. An Application Reference Number (ARN) is generated immediately upon submission.

8. DGFT Processing and Approval DGFT processes the modification. For most standard modifications, approval is granted within 1–3 working days on a deemed basis (if PAN/GST validation is successful). For modifications requiring physical document verification or where there is a data mismatch, the processing may take longer.

9. Download Updated IEC Certificate Upon approval, the updated IEC certificate with the new details is available for download from the DGFT portal. The 10-digit IEC number remains the same, only the details change.

10. Update ICEGATE and Other Portals After IEC modification, update the new details on ICEGATE, RCMC with relevant EPCs, and any other portals where IEC details are registered. Fees Modification Type Government Fee IEC Modification (all types) Nil (Free) Annual IEC Update (April–June) Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee IEC Modification,  simple (address/bank) ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 IEC Modification,  moderate (director/name change) ₹2,500 – ₹6,000 IEC Modification,  complex (entity restructuring) ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 Annual IEC Update (no changes) ₹500 – ₹1,500 Annual IEC Update + modifications ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 ICEGATE profile update post-IEC modification ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 IEC reactivation after deactivation ₹2,000 – ₹5,000

Government fees for IEC modification are nil. Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%.

## Timeline

Stage Typical Timeline Document preparation 1–3 working days DGFT portal submission Same day DGFT approval (standard modification) 1–3 working days DGFT approval (name/entity change,  document review) 3–7 working days ICEGATE profile update (post-IEC modification) 1–2 working days Total end-to-end (standard modification) 2–5 working days Total end-to-end (complex/name change) 5–10 working days

Annual IEC Update during April–June (with no changes) is typically completed instantly on the DGFT portal upon login and confirmation.

Use Cases 1. Company Relocated Its Office A Pune-based pharmaceutical exporter shifts its registered office from Aundh to Wakad. The IEC still shows the old Aundh address. Before the next export shipment, the company must modify the IEC to reflect the new address,  as customs filings reference the IEC address, and a mismatch between the Shipping Bill and IEC record can cause clearance queries at the port. 2. Annual IEC Update (No Changes) A Delhi-based importer of electronic components has not changed any business details since last year. Between April 1 and June 30, they must log in to the DGFT portal and confirm all IEC details are unchanged,  completing the mandatory Annual IEC Update. Missing this window results in IEC deactivation, which would block their imports at customs until the update is completed and the IEC is reactivated. 3. Director Change After Funding Round A Bengaluru-based tech startup that imports server hardware received Series A funding and onboarded two new investor-nominated directors, while one founding director exited. The IEC modification is required to update the director list,  adding two new directors and removing the exiting director,  along with the DSC of the new authorized signatory for future IEC-related filings. 4. Bank Account Change A Chennai-based seafood exporter changed their primary banking relationship from Canara Bank to HDFC Bank for better trade finance facilities. The IEC must be modified to reflect the new HDFC Bank account,  as export proceeds realized in the HDFC account are reconciled against the IEC-linked bank account for FEMA compliance and DGFT scheme purposes. 5. Addition of New Export Branch A Surat-based textile exporter that primarily exports from Mumbai port opens a new manufacturing and export unit in Silvassa with its own dispatch and shipping operations. The IEC modification adds the Silvassa unit as an additional branch under the same IEC,  enabling customs filings from Silvassa to reference the same IEC while correctly identifying the Silvassa facility as the place of export.

## Frequently asked questions

**My company's name changed after RoC approval. Do I need a new IEC or just an IEC modification?** You need an IEC modification,  not a new IEC. The IEC number (10 digits, linked to the company's PAN) remains the same even after a company name change. The modification updates the entity name on the IEC to reflect the new legal name as per the updated Certificate of Incorporation. Submit the IEC modification application on the DGFT portal with the MCA-issued Certificate of Incorporation showing the new name, the ROC approval order for the name change, and the company's updated PAN card (if the name on PAN has also been updated). Operating under the old IEC name after a legal name change creates compliance risk in customs and FEMA contexts.

**We missed the Annual IEC Update window (April 1 to June 30). Our IEC has been deactivated. What do we do?** An IEC deactivated due to a missed annual update can be reactivated by completing the overdue Annual IEC Update,  even after the June 30 deadline. Log in to the DGFT portal and complete the update (confirming or modifying details as applicable). Upon submission, the IEC is typically reactivated within 1–3 working days. There is no penalty fee for late annual update,  but the period of deactivation means all imports and exports during that period were technically not covered by a valid IEC. Ensure any shipments during the deactivation period are documented carefully. Going forward, set a calendar reminder for April 1 each year.

**Our proprietor got married and her name has legally changed. Does the IEC need to be modified?** Yes. Since the IEC for a proprietorship is issued in the name of the proprietor (for proprietorships) or the entity (for companies/firms), a legal name change of the proprietor must be reflected in the IEC. Submit a modification application with the name change proof,  typically a gazette notification of name change, updated Aadhaar in the new name, and updated PAN in the new name (if already updated). All three identity documents should reflect the same name for the modification to be processed smoothly. The IEC number remains the same,  only the proprietor name field is updated.

**We want to add a new bank account to our IEC without removing the existing one. Is that possible?** The DGFT IEC system currently allows only one primary bank account to be linked to the IEC at any time,  not multiple simultaneous bank accounts. If you want to use a new bank account for a specific transaction (e.g., export proceeds realization), you would need to modify the IEC to update the bank account to the new one. Export proceeds must be realized in the bank account linked to the IEC. If you anticipate switching between accounts frequently, discuss with your banker,  some situations (e.g., different currencies in different accounts) may require specific advice on FEMA compliance alongside the IEC bank account registration.

**We are merging with another company. The merged entity will use our IEC. How does IEC modification work in an M&A scenario?** Merger and amalgamation scenarios are among the most complex IEC situations. If your company (the surviving entity) is absorbing the other company, your existing IEC continues,  but the IEC must be modified to reflect any changes in the surviving company's details post-merger (director changes, address changes, etc.). The absorbed company's IEC must be surrendered/cancelled separately. If the other company's IEC has more valuable export history (for scheme eligibility or licensing purposes), discuss with a DGFT consultant whether there is any mechanism to transfer scheme history,  IEC numbers themselves are not transferable, but export turnover data may be consolidated for certain purposes. Post-merger, coordinate with DGFT, ICEGATE, EPCs (for RCMC), and the AD bank to ensure all systems reflect the post-merger entity structure.
