services.name: RCMC Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for RCMC Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Rcmc Registration

## Overview

RCMC (Registration-cum-Membership Certificate) is a certificate issued by Export Promotion Councils (EPCs) or Commodity Boards to Indian exporters. It serves the dual purpose of registering the exporter with the relevant EPC/Commodity Board and granting them membership,  hence "Registration-cum-Membership Certificate." RCMC is mandatory for Indian exporters who wish to claim benefits under India's Foreign Trade Policy (FTP),  including export incentive schemes like the Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products (RoDTEP), Advance Authorisation, EPCG (Export Promotion Capital Goods) Scheme, and duty drawback. The RCMC is issued by the EPC or Commodity Board whose mandate covers the product category being exported by the applicant. India has approximately 26 Export Promotion Councils and 10 Commodity Boards, each covering specific product categories: EPC / Commodity Board Product Category APEDA Agriculture and processed food MPEDA Marine products EEPC India Engineering products FIEO General merchandise, services CLE Leather and leather products AEPC Apparel / garments GJEPC Gems and jewellery SPICES Board Spices COFFEE Board Coffee TEA Board Tea RUBBER Board Rubber COIR Board Coir PHARMEXCIL Pharmaceuticals CHEMEXCIL Chemicals and allied products SHEFEXIL Shellac and forest products STPI Software Technology Parks (IT exports)

## Features

Dual-purpose certificate: Simultaneously registers the exporter with the EPC and grants membership,  all in one certificate Product-specific: RCMC is issued for specific product categories (HS codes) covered by the issuing EPC/Commodity Board; exporters dealing in multiple product categories may need RCMCs from multiple bodies DGFT integration: RCMC must be linked to the exporter's IEC (Import Export Code) on the DGFT portal,  this linkage enables claim of FTP benefits 5-year validity: RCMC is typically valid for 5 years (April 1 to March 31 of the 5th year) with annual renewal of membership EPC services access: RCMC holders get access to EPC services,  buyer-seller meets, export fairs, market development assistance, trade delegations, and export intelligence Digital RCMC: DGFT has introduced e-RCMC,  digital RCMC integrated with the DGFT portal for seamless FTP benefit claims Mandatory for FTP schemes: RCMC is a prerequisite for filing applications for RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation, EPCG, and other FTP schemes with DGFT

## Benefits

FTP incentive scheme access: Without RCMC, exporters cannot claim benefits under RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation, EPCG, Merchandise Exports from India Scheme (if still applicable), and other DGFT-administered export promotion schemes APEDA/MPEDA certification for food exports: For agricultural and marine product exports, APEDA and MPEDA RCMC is mandatory for customs clearance,  not just for incentive claims EPC networking: EPC membership provides access to government-supported international trade fairs, buyer-seller meets, and market intelligence,  helping exporters identify new markets Brand India promotion: EPCs collectively promote Indian products in global markets,  RCMC holders benefit from these promotional efforts as representatives of Indian export industries Market development assistance: Many EPCs provide Market Development Assistance (MDA) grants to RCMC holders for international trade fair participation and export market development Legal standing in export disputes: RCMC validates the exporter's registration status,  useful in commercial and legal disputes with overseas buyers Certificate of Origin facilitation: Some EPCs assist RCMC holders in obtaining Certificates of Origin for preferential trade under FTAs,  relevant for exports to ASEAN, Japan, Korea, UAE, etc.

## Limitations

Multiple RCMCs may be needed: Exporters dealing in diverse product categories (e.g., both engineering goods and chemicals) may need RCMCs from multiple EPCs,  adding administrative and fee burden Annual membership fee: While the RCMC itself has 5-year validity, EPC membership fees are typically annual,  non-payment of annual fees can affect RCMC status IEC prerequisite: RCMC can only be obtained after obtaining an IEC (Import Export Code) from DGFT,  IEC is therefore the first step Product category accuracy: Exporters must correctly identify and apply under the appropriate EPC for their product HS code,  misclassification can cause FTP benefit rejections Renewal complexity: When RCMC expires after 5 years, renewal requires fresh application with updated documents APEDA registration separate: For certain food product categories, APEDA registration (separate from RCMC) may be required for specific export certifications

## Applicability

RCMC is required for any Indian entity that: Exports goods or services and wishes to claim benefits under India's Foreign Trade Policy Exports agricultural, processed food, marine, or horticultural products (APEDA/MPEDA RCMC mandatory for customs clearance) Exports under an Advance Authorisation or EPCG scheme Is a manufacturer-exporter or merchant-exporter with an IEC Applicable pan-India,  exporters across all states (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Rajasthan, UP, West Bengal, Telangana, Kerala, Punjab, etc.) can obtain RCMC from the relevant EPC.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| IEC (Import Export Code) | Mandatory prerequisite,  from DGFT |
| Application form of the relevant EPC | EPC-specific application |
| Certificate of Incorporation / Partnership Deed | Legal entity documents |
| PAN card | Company/individual PAN |
| GST Registration Certificate | GSTIN |
| Bank certificate / Banker's letter | Certifying banking relationship |
| Export performance data (if any) | Prior year's export turnover (for renewal or some EPCs) |
| MSME/Udyam Registration (if applicable) | For MSME fee category |
| DSC of authorised signatory | For digital/online application |
| Photograph of authorised signatory | For EPC records |

## Process

1. Obtain IEC Apply for and obtain Import Export Code (IEC) from DGFT (dgft.gov.in), mandatory before applying for RCMC.

2. Identify Relevant EPC Based on your export product category and HS codes, identify the appropriate Export Promotion Council or Commodity Board.

3. Application Submission Apply on the EPC's online portal (most major EPCs now have online application systems) or submit a physical application to the EPC's regional office.

4. Document Submission and Fee Payment Submit all required documents and pay the membership/RCMC fee online or via demand draft.

5. EPC Verification The EPC verifies documents and IEC details. Some EPCs may request additional information.

6. RCMC Issuance Upon verification, the EPC issues the RCMC, typically within 7–15 working days of complete application.

7. DGFT Portal Linkage Link the issued RCMC with the IEC on the DGFT portal to enable FTP scheme benefit claims.

8. Annual Membership Renewal Renew EPC membership annually by paying the annual membership fee, maintains RCMC validity throughout the 5-year period. Fees EPC / Board Membership / RCMC Fee (Approx.) FIEO ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 per year APEDA ₹5,900 (one-time for 5 years) EEPC India ₹5,000 – ₹25,000 per year PHARMEXCIL ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 per year AEPC (apparel) ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 per year GJEPC (gems & jewellery) ₹10,000 – ₹30,000 per year CHEMEXCIL ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 per year


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee IEC + RCMC package ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 RCMC application support ₹2,000 – ₹6,000 Multi-EPC RCMC management ₹5,000 – ₹15,000

EPC fees are paid directly to the EPC. Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

RCMC is typically valid for 5 years (April 1 of the year of issue to March 31 of the 5th year). Annual EPC membership fees must be paid within the 5-year period to maintain active RCMC status. At the end of 5 years, a fresh RCMC application is required. Some EPCs issue certificates co-terminus with the calendar year,  verify the specific EPC's validity structure at the time of application.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can I export without RCMC if I'm not claiming any FTP incentives?** For most product categories, yes,  exporters who are not claiming any DGFT-administered FTP benefits are not legally required to hold RCMC. However, for specific categories like agricultural and marine products, APEDA and MPEDA RCMC is mandatory for customs clearance regardless of whether FTP incentives are claimed. Additionally, many overseas buyers, particularly institutional and government buyers, require proof of EPC membership (RCMC) as part of supplier qualification.

**My company exports both garments and engineering goods. Do I need two separate RCMCs?** Yes. Garments fall under AEPC (Apparel Export Promotion Council) and engineering goods fall under EEPC India. Since different product categories are governed by different EPCs, you need separate RCMC from each relevant EPC. Some exporters with highly diverse product portfolios hold 3–4 RCMCs from different EPCs. The IEC is common across all RCMCs,  it is the single identifier on the DGFT portal that links all your RCMCs.

**Is RCMC the same as IEC?** What's the difference? No, they are different. IEC (Import Export Code) is a 10-digit code issued by DGFT that is the basic licence to import or export,  every exporter/importer must have it. RCMC is a registration with a specific Export Promotion Council that is required to claim FTP scheme benefits. IEC is always needed first; RCMC is a second-layer registration built on top of IEC. IEC is from DGFT (government); RCMC is from private-sector EPCs/Commodity Boards (though they operate under the Ministry of Commerce framework).

**How do I link my RCMC with my IEC on the DGFT portal?** Log in to the DGFT portal (dgft.gov.in) with your IEC credentials. Navigate to the "Services" section and find "RCMC Management." Enter your RCMC details,  issuing EPC, certificate number, validity dates, and product HS codes covered. The portal validates the RCMC details against the EPC's records (if the EPC is integrated with DGFT's e-RCMC system). Once linked, your RCMC is visible in your DGFT profile and FTP scheme applications automatically reference it.

**Our RCMC expired 2 months ago and we didn't renew it. Can we still claim RoDTEP on exports done during the valid period?** For exports made during the period when the RCMC was valid, you can claim RoDTEP provided all other eligibility conditions are met and the claim is filed within the permissible filing window. DGFT's RoDTEP claim portal checks RCMC validity as of the export date (shipping bill date),  not the date of claim filing. If your RCMC was valid when the exports were made, the claim should be processable. However, you must renew the RCMC immediately for future exports to continue being eligible for FTP benefits.

services.name: One Person Company Annual Compliance
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for One Person Company Annual Compliance — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# ONE Person Company (opc) Annual Compliance

## Overview

A One Person Company (OPC), introduced under the Companies Act, 2013, allows a single entrepreneur to operate a corporate entity with limited liability. While it enjoys several relaxations compared to private limited companies, OPCs must still comply with various annual filing requirements under the MCA, Income Tax Department, and GST laws.

## Applicability

OPC annual compliance applies uniformly across India including Maharashtra, Karnataka, Delhi, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Kerala, Rajasthan, Punjab, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, and Union Territories. Every OPC registered under the Companies Act, 2013 must comply, regardless of whether it has conducted business.

## Compliance Requirements

| Compliance | Form | Authority | Due Date |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Financial Statements | AOC-4 | MCA | Within 180 days of FY end |
| Annual Return | MGT-7A | MCA | Within 60 days of AGM equivalent |
| Director KYC | DIR-3 KYC | MCA | September 30 |
| Statutory Audit | Auditor Report | ICAI | Annual (mandatory) |
| Income Tax Return | ITR-6 | IT Dept | October 31 |
| Tax Audit | Form 3CA-3CD | IT Dept | If applicable |
| Board Meetings | Min 2 per year | Internal | 90-day gap |
| GST Returns | GSTR-1, 3B, 9 | GST | Monthly/Annual |

## Benefits

OPC compliance ensures continuity of limited liability protection, maintains the separate legal entity status, enables easy conversion to a private limited company once thresholds are crossed, and preserves the credibility required for bank loans and B2B contracts. Compliant OPCs also find it easier to attract investors when scaling up.

## Penalties / Risks

| Non-Compliance | Penalty |
| --- | --- |
| AOC-4 delay | Rs. 100 per day |
| MGT-7A delay | Rs. 100 per day |
| Director KYC default | Rs. 5,000 + DIN deactivation |
| Audit default | Rs. 25,000 to Rs. 5 lakh |
| ITR non-filing | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 10,000 + interest |
| Continued default | Director disqualification, company strike off |

## Documents Required

Certificate of Incorporation, MOA and AOA, PAN and TAN, bank statements, invoices and purchase records, salary records, TDS challans, GST returns, DSC of director, financial statements, nominee details, and previous ITR.

## Process / Timeline

OPCs are exempt from holding AGMs. Financial statements must be signed by the director and submitted to the auditor. Once the auditor signs, AOC-4 must be filed within 180 days from the end of the financial year (i.e., by September 27). MGT-7A is filed within 60 days thereafter. ITR-6 is filed by October 31.

## Government Fees

| Form | Fee Range | AOC-4 | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 (based on capital) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MGT-7A | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 | DIR-3 KYC | Nil if on time |
| ADT-1 | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 | Late filing | Rs. 100/day |

## Compliance Calendar

| Month | Activity |
| --- | --- |
| April-May | Book closure, audit preparation |
| June-August | Statutory audit |
| September 27 | AOC-4 filing |
| September 30 | DIR-3 KYC |
| October 31 | ITR-6 |
| November 28 | MGT-7A filing |
| Quarterly | TDS returns, board meeting |

## FAQs

**Q1. Is an AGM required for an OPC?** No, OPCs are specifically exempted from holding Annual General Meetings. However, financial statements must still be prepared and filed.

**Q2. What is MGT-7A and how is it different from MGT-7?** MGT-7A is an abridged form of the annual return specifically designed for OPCs and small companies, requiring less information than MGT-7.

**Q3. Can an OPC skip audit if it has no revenue?** No, statutory audit is mandatory for every OPC regardless of turnover or business activity. Even zero-revenue OPCs must get books audited.

**Q4. When must an OPC convert to a private limited company?** An OPC must convert to a private limited company when its paid-up capital exceeds Rs. 50 lakh or average annual turnover exceeds Rs. 2 crore for three consecutive years.

**Q5. How many board meetings must an OPC conduct?** An OPC with more than one director must hold at least one board meeting in each half of the calendar year, with a minimum gap of 90 days between the two meetings.

services.content:
ROC Notice Reply,  Overview

Our consultants support ROC Notice Reply under LEGAL COMPLIANCE, from applicability review through filings, department queries and final acknowledgement.

Applicability

This service is intended for clients who need structured, compliant handling with clear documentation and realistic timelines.

Benefits

Fewer rejections from incomplete paperwork, coordinated submissions, and a single point of contact for follow-ups.

Process

Requirement capture → document checklist → preparation and validation → submission to authority → tracking and query handling → closure.

Documents required

Entity KYC, address proof, category-specific forms and annexures. A tailored checklist is shared after the first consultation.

services.name: Auditor Appointment (Form ADT-1 Filing)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Auditor Appointment (Form ADT-1 Filing) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## Auditor appointment (Form ADT-1 filing)

### Detailed overview

The appointment of an auditor is a mandatory annual compliance under Section 139 of the Companies Act, 2013, requiring every company to appoint an auditor for conducting the statutory audit of its financial statements. The first auditor is appointed by the Board within 30 days of incorporation, subsequent auditors are appointed by shareholders at each AGM for a 5-year term (typically).

Form ADT-1 is the prescribed MCA form used to intimate the auditor's appointment to the Registrar of Companies. Filing ADT-1 is the formal acknowledgement of the audit engagement and is a foundational compliance for every company.

### Key features

- ADT-2 consent and eligibility, ADT-1 intimation with RoC
- First auditor by board, subsequent cycles at AGM subject to rotation rules where applicable
- Without a valid auditor, AOC-4 and audit trail break down

## Applicability

Applicable to all companies, private limited, public limited, Section 8, OPC, producer company. Pan-India.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| First auditor | Appointed by board within 30 days of incorporation |
| Subsequent auditors | Appointed at AGM for 5 years |
| Form ADT-1 | Filed within 15 days of appointment |
| Auditor consent | Form ADT-2 (auditor's consent) |
| Auditor eligibility | Section 141, independence, qualifications |
| Rotation | Mandatory for listed companies and large unlisted (5/10 year cycle) |

## Benefits

- Statutory compliance with audit requirements
- Independent verification of financial statements
- Lender and investor confidence
- Detection and prevention of frauds
- Tax compliance and credibility

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Late ADT-1 filing | ₹100 per day |
| No auditor appointed | Company in default, financial statements unaudited, cannot file AOC-4 |
| Wrong appointment (ineligible auditor) | Appointment void, reappointment required |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Auditor's consent (ADT-2) | Auditor's willingness to accept appointment |
| Auditor's eligibility certificate | Confirming Section 141 compliance |
| Board resolution or shareholder resolution | Approving appointment |
| AGM minutes | For shareholder appointments |
| Auditor's PAN and FRN | Firm Registration Number for CA firms |
| DSC of director | For ADT-1 signing |

## Process

1. **Auditor identification:** Identify CA or CA firm meeting Section 141 eligibility (independence, no conflicts).
2. **Auditor consent:** Obtain Form ADT-2 from auditor, consent and eligibility certificate.
3. **Board or shareholder approval:** First auditor by board (within 30 days of incorporation), subsequent at AGM by shareholders.
4. **File ADT-1:** Within 15 days of appointment, file ADT-1 with the MCA.
5. **Audit engagement:** Auditor commences audit work.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| ADT-1 | ₹200 to ₹600 (based on authorized capital) |
| Late fee | ₹100 per day |

## Timeline

| Stage | Timeline |
| Board or AGM approval | Day 1 |
| ADT-1 filing | Within 15 days |

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can the same auditor be reappointed indefinitely?** For listed companies and certain unlisted public companies, mandatory rotation applies, individual auditor maximum 5 years, firm maximum 10 years. After this, a cooling-off period of 5 years applies. For other companies, reappointment without rotation is permitted.

**2. What is Form ADT-2 vs ADT-1?** ADT-2 is the auditor's written consent and eligibility certificate (auditor's document). ADT-1 is the company's intimation of appointment to the MCA. Both are required.

**3. Can a company appoint the same auditor for both statutory audit and tax audit?** Yes. There is no statutory bar on the same CA or firm conducting both statutory audit (under Companies Act) and tax audit (under Income Tax Act). However, independence considerations should be evaluated.

**4. What happens if a company does not appoint an auditor at AGM?** The Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) for government companies, or the Tribunal in some cases, may appoint an auditor. The company is in default. Practical resolution is to convene EGM and appoint promptly.

**5. Can an auditor resign mid-term and what is the process?** Yes. Auditor can resign by filing Form ADT-3 within 30 days of resignation. The company must appoint a new auditor within 30 days of receiving the resignation notice, through a board meeting and confirmation at the next general meeting.

services.name: Demat of Shares
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Demat of Shares — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## Demat of shares

### Detailed overview

Dematerialization (Demat) of shares is the process of converting physical share certificates into electronic format held in a demat account with a Depository Participant (DP), registered with NSDL or CDSL. The Companies (Prospectus and Allotment of Securities) Rules mandate that certain categories of companies issue and hold their shares only in demat form.

- All listed companies
- All public companies (mandatory)
- All unlisted public companies (mandatory)
- Specified private companies above certain thresholds

For private companies, demat is increasingly mandated for holding subsidiaries of listed companies and for certain large unlisted companies.

### Key features

- ISIN, depository, RTA, and shareholder communication for conversion
- Eliminates loss or damage of physical certificates where mandatory or adopted
- Supports faster transfers and market readiness

## Applicability

| Company type | Demat mandatory |
| Listed companies | Yes (since 1996) |
| Unlisted public companies | Yes (mandatory since 2018) |
| Private companies (large) | Yes (specified) |
| Other private companies | Optional (but increasingly common) |

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| ISIN | International Securities Identification Number from depository |
| Depository agreement | With NSDL or CDSL |
| RTA appointment | Registrar and Transfer Agent |
| Communication to shareholders | About demat process |
| Physical share certificate cancellation | After demat |
| MCA filing | INC-22A (if applicable) |

## Benefits

- Eliminates physical certificate risks (loss, damage, theft)
- Faster share transfers (T+2 settlement)
- Reduced paperwork
- Online portfolio management
- Required for IPO or listing
- Mandatory compliance for certain companies

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Non-compliance with demat mandate | ₹10,000 per day penalty |
| Continued non-compliance | Restrictions on share transfers |
| Failure to appoint RTA | Operational disruption |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Application to depository | NSDL or CDSL membership |
| Depository agreement | Standard agreement |
| RTA appointment letter | |
| Shareholder list | For ISIN application |
| MoA, AoA | Constitutional documents |
| ISIN application | Per security |

## Process

1. Engage Depository Participant (DP), bank or broker.
2. Open Beneficial Owner Account (Demat account) at company level.
3. Appoint Registrar and Transfer Agent (RTA).
4. Apply for ISIN through RTA.
5. Communicate to shareholders about demat.
6. Shareholders open individual demat accounts.
7. Submit physical certificates for dematerialization.
8. RTA cancels physical certificates and credits demat accounts.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| ISIN application | ₹15,000 (NSDL and CDSL) |
| RTA fees | ₹25,000 to ₹1,00,000 annually |
| DP charges | Variable |

## Timeline

30 to 90 days for company-level demat setup, share-by-share conversion ongoing.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is demat mandatory for all private companies?** Currently mandatory for unlisted public companies and certain large private companies (particularly those that are subsidiaries of listed companies, or with ≥ ₹50 crore paid-up capital). Most other private companies have demat as optional.

**2. What is ISIN and why is it needed?** ISIN (International Securities Identification Number) is a unique 12-character code identifying each security globally. Required for trading and for demat purposes. Each class of shares has its own ISIN.

**3. Can shareholders refuse to convert their physical shares to demat?** For mandatory demat companies, transfer of physical shares is restricted, shareholders are essentially compelled to demat to enable any transfer. They retain ownership but in electronic form.

**4. What is the difference between NSDL and CDSL?** NSDL (National Securities Depository) and CDSL (Central Depository Services) are India's two depositories. Both serve similar functions. Companies typically have ISINs with both for shareholder convenience.

**5. Do small private companies benefit from voluntary demat?** Yes, easier share transfers (especially in fundraising), reduced paperwork in M&A, modernized governance, IPO readiness, and investor preference. Many startups voluntarily demat shares before Series A funding.

services.name: Share Transfer
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Share Transfer — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## Share transfer

### Detailed overview

Share transfer is the legal process of transferring ownership of shares from one shareholder (transferor) to another (transferee). Governed by Section 56 of the Companies Act, 2013, share transfers can be private (unlisted companies, through Form SH-4) or through stock exchange (listed companies, automated via depository system).

For private companies, AOA may impose restrictions on share transfer (right of first refusal, board approval, etc.), common for protecting shareholding integrity.

### Key features

- SH-4 and stamp for unlisted, demat and exchange settlement for listed
- Board can refuse in private companies if AOA allows, with reasons
- FEMA and pricing rules for non-resident leg of transfers

## Applicability

All companies (private and public). Process varies based on listing status. Pan-India.

## Compliance requirements

| Type | Process |
| Unlisted private company | Form SH-4 and share certificate, board approval if AOA requires, stamp duty |
| Unlisted public company | Form SH-4 and share certificate, stamp duty |
| Listed company | Demat transfer through depository, broker-mediated |

## Benefits

- Liquidity for shareholders
- Capital exit mechanism
- Strategic equity restructuring
- Investor exit and entry

## Risks and penalties

| Risk | Detail |
| Improper SH-4 execution | Transfer void |
| Inadequate stamp duty | Transfer not registrable |
| AOA restriction violation | Board may refuse registration |
| Undisclosed beneficial ownership | Penalties under the Companies Act |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Form SH-4 | Share transfer instrument |
| Share certificate | Original (for cancellation or reissue) |
| Stamp duty paid (0.25% of consideration) | |
| Transfer deed witnessing | |
| Board resolution | For approval of transfer (if AOA requires) |
| Notice to transferee or transferor | Of decision |
| Updated members register | Internal record |

## Process

1. Transferor and transferee execute Form SH-4 with transferee's particulars
2. Pay stamp duty (0.25% of consideration value)
3. Submit share certificate and SH-4 to company
4. Board considers (within 30 days for private, immediate for public)
5. If approved, name updated in register, new certificate issued
6. If refused, written reasons within 30 days, appeal to NCLT possible

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| Stamp duty on SH-4 | 0.25% of consideration (state-specific variations) |
| No MCA fee | Internal company action |

## Timeline

7 to 30 days for unlisted, immediate for listed (T+2 settlement).

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can private company share transfer be refused by the board?** Yes, if AOA grants such power. Board can refuse on stipulated grounds (e.g. transferee not approved). Refusal must be communicated within 30 days, transferor can appeal to NCLT.

**2. What is the stamp duty on share transfer?** 0.25% of consideration value (Indian Stamp Act). State-specific variations apply. Adhesive stamps or e-stamping accepted.

**3. Are gifts of shares treated as transfers?** Yes. Gift of shares is a transfer requiring SH-4, stamp duty (on market value of shares), and procedure. Tax implications under the Income Tax Act also apply.

**4. Can shares of a private company be transferred to non-residents?** Subject to FEMA compliance, particularly Section 6 of FEMA (capital account transactions). Pricing, sectoral caps, and FC-TRS filing may apply for transfers to non-residents.

**5. What happens if SH-4 is lost before company processing?** Reissue with proper indemnity from transferor. Stamp duty already paid is not refundable, new SH-4 with fresh stamp may be required (state-specific).

services.name: DPT-3 Form Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for DPT-3 Form Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## DPT-3 form filing

### Detailed overview

Form DPT-3 is the prescribed form under Section 73 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 16 of the Companies (Acceptance of Deposits) Rules, 2014, for filing the annual return of deposits (and exempt deposits) by every company. The form captures details of deposits, money received but not considered as deposit (loans from directors, share application money pending allotment, advances from customers, etc.), and outstanding amounts.

DPT-3 is a critical compliance for tracking unsecured loans, deposits, and other monies received by companies, preventing illegal deposit acceptance and ensuring transparency in company finances.

### Key features

- Annual return by June 30 covering receipts and outstandings
- Auditor certificate typically integral to filing
- Captures both deposits and many exempt categories to improve transparency

## Applicability

Mandatory for all companies (except government companies) that have received money from any source other than issue of shares or bonds and loans from banks or NBFCs, including loans from directors, related parties, customers (advances), employees, etc.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form | DPT-3 |
| Due date | June 30 each year (annual return) |
| Information period | Money received during the FY |
| Outstanding amounts | As on March 31 |
| Auditor certification | Required |
| Director DSC | For form signing |

## Benefits

- Statutory compliance with deposit regulations
- Transparency in financial dealings
- Prevention of disguised illegal deposit acceptance
- Required for a clean compliance record

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Non-filing | ₹100 per day late fee |
| Inaccurate information | Penalty on company and officers in default |
| Continued default | Up to ₹5 crore on company, ₹2 lakh on officers |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Form DPT-3 | MCA prescribed |
| Auditor's certificate | On deposit information |
| Statement of deposits | Detail-wise breakdown |
| Loan agreements | For loans from directors, related parties |
| Customer advance details | If significant |
| Employee deposit details | If applicable |

## Process

1. Compile all amounts received during the year (other than from share issue and bank loans)
2. Categorise, deposits, exempt deposits, loans from directors, customer advances
3. Get auditor's certificate
4. Prepare Form DPT-3
5. Director DSC signing
6. File with MCA by June 30

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| DPT-3 | ₹200 to ₹600 |
| Late fee | ₹100 per day |

## Timeline

By June 30 each year.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Are loans from directors considered deposits requiring DPT-3?** Loans from directors are exempted deposits, but still must be reported in DPT-3. They are listed under money received not considered as deposits.

**2. Are customer advances reported in DPT-3?** Yes, customer advances received that have been outstanding beyond 365 days (or specific timelines) are reported. Short-term advances tied to specific contracts are typically exempt.

**3. Does an inactive company need to file DPT-3?** If the company has any outstanding loans or deposits as on March 31, yes. NIL DPT-3 is required if no such amounts exist but the company is otherwise active.

**4. Can DPT-3 be filed without an auditor's certificate?** No. The auditor's certificate confirming the deposit information is mandatory for DPT-3 filing.

**5. What happens if a company illegally accepts deposits?** Penalty up to ₹5 crore, repayment of deposits with interest, and possible criminal proceedings. Director's personal liability can also arise. Strict compliance with deposit rules is essential.

services.name: LLP Form 11 Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for LLP Form 11 Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## LLP Form 11 filing

### Detailed overview

Form 11 is the Annual Return of an LLP filed with the Registrar of Companies (RoC) under the LLP Act, 2008. The form captures the LLP's structural information, partner details, contributions, business activities, and any changes during the financial year, analogous to MGT-7 for companies.

Form 11 is one of the two foundational annual MCA filings for LLPs (the other being Form 8 for financial statements). All LLPs must file Form 11 annually regardless of business activity.

### Key features

- Annual structural return by May 30 for March year-end
- Designated partner DSC, agreement and partner data reconciled
- Complements Form 8 (accounts and solvency)

## Applicability

Mandatory for all LLPs registered in India. Pan-India applicability.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form | LLP Form 11 |
| Due date | May 30 each year (FY ending March 31) |
| Filing period | Within 60 days of FY end |
| Designated Partner DSC | Mandatory |
| Pre-conditions | LLP Agreement up to date, partner records current |

## Benefits

- Statutory annual MCA compliance
- Maintains LLP active status
- Public record of LLP structure
- Prevents non-compliance penalties

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Late filing | ₹100 per day (no upper cap historically, recent caps may apply) |
| Continued non-filing | LLP marked inactive, restrictions on operations |
| Strike-off risk | After persistent non-compliance |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| LLP agreement | Reference |
| Updated partner list | With contributions |
| List of designated partners | |
| Form 11 | MCA prescribed |
| DSC of designated partner | For signing |

## Process

1. Compile partner data and contributions
2. Verify against existing LLP Agreement
3. Prepare Form 11
4. DSC signing by designated partner
5. File on the MCA portal by May 30

## Government fees

| Capital contribution | Fee |
| Up to ₹1 lakh | ₹50 |
| ₹1 to 5 lakh | ₹100 |
| ₹5 to 10 lakh | ₹150 |
| Above ₹10 lakh | ₹200 |

Late fee: ₹100 per day.

## Timeline

By May 30 each year.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is Form 11 required for inactive LLPs?** Yes. Even inactive LLPs must file Form 11 annually until formal closure. Non-filing accumulates significant late fees over time.

**2. What is the difference between Form 11 and Form 8?** Form 11 = Annual Return (structural information). Form 8 = Statement of Account and Solvency (financial information). Both mandatory annually.

**3. Can Form 11 be filed without finalising LLP accounts?** Form 11 is structural and non-financial, does not require finalised accounts. Form 8 requires finalised financials.

**4. What is the consequence of multi-year Form 11 non-filing?** Significant accumulated late fees (₹100 per day per year), MCA marking LLP as defaulter, restrictions on filings, eventual strike-off risk.

**5. Are partners personally liable for Form 11 non-compliance?** Designated Partners bear primary responsibility. Penalties under LLP Act can be imposed on Designated Partners for non-compliance.

services.name: AOC-4 Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for AOC-4 Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## AOC-4 filing

### Detailed overview

Form AOC-4 is the prescribed form under Section 137 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 12 of the Companies (Accounts) Rules, 2014, for filing the financial statements of a company with the Registrar of Companies (RoC). Every company incorporated in India, whether private limited, public limited, OPC, Section 8, or producer company, is required to file Form AOC-4 annually with the MCA, capturing its audited Balance Sheet, Profit and Loss Account, Cash Flow Statement, Notes to Accounts, and Directors' Report.

AOC-4 is one of the two foundational annual MCA filings (the other being MGT-7) and represents a company's compliance with statutory financial disclosure obligations. The financial statements filed via AOC-4 become part of the public record on the MCA portal, accessible to investors, lenders, vendors, and regulators.

The form has variants depending on the type of the company.

- AOC-4, for unlisted companies with non-XBRL filings
- AOC-4 XBRL, for companies required to file in XBRL format (listed companies, large unlisted companies)
- AOC-4 NBFC, for NBFCs
- AOC-4 CFS, for consolidated financial statements

### Key features

- Prescribed form for audited balance sheet, profit and loss, cash flow (where applicable), notes, and Directors' Report with RoC
- One of the two foundational annual MCA filings with MGT-7
- Public record disclosure on the MCA portal for investors, lenders, vendors, and regulators
- Pan-India filing across all states and UTs

## Applicability

AOC-4 is applicable to all companies incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013 (or earlier Companies Act, 1956), including private limited companies, public limited companies (listed and unlisted), one person companies (OPCs), Section 8 companies (non-profit), producer companies, and Nidhi companies. The form must be filed pan-India regardless of the state of incorporation, including Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, and all other states and UTs.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form filing | AOC-4, AOC-4 XBRL, or AOC-4 NBFC as applicable |
| Due date | Within 30 days of the AGM |
| AGM cutoff | September 30 (for FY ending March 31) |
| Standard AOC-4 due date | October 30 |
| Documents to attach | Audited financials, board report, auditor report, CSR report (if applicable) |
| Digital signing | DSC of one director and DSC of a practising professional (CA, CS, or CMA) |
| Pre-condition | Audit completion and AGM held |

## Benefits

- Statutory MCA compliance maintenance
- Public disclosure of financials supports lender and vendor due diligence
- Enables annual return filing (MGT-7 follow-on filing)
- Required for company status as Active on the MCA portal
- Critical for due diligence in M&A, fundraising, and IPO
- Establishes a track record of good corporate governance

## Risks and penalties

| Default type | Penalty |
| Late filing, first 15 days | ₹100 per day (after due date) |
| Continued delay | ₹100 per day until filing |
| Maximum late fee | No statutory cap, accumulates indefinitely |
| Company in default | Company status moves to default, which restricts further filings |
| Director disqualification | If default exceeds 3 years (with MGT-7), directors disqualified under Section 164(2) |
| Strike-off risk | Continued non-filing leads to strike-off of the company by RoC |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Audited balance sheet | Mandatory financial statement |
| Audited profit and loss account | Mandatory financial statement |
| Cash flow statement | If applicable to the company |
| Notes to accounts | Detailed accounting notes |
| Auditor's report | Independent auditor's opinion |
| Directors' report | Board's discussion of operations |
| CSR report | If CSR applicable (Section 135) |
| Secretarial audit report | For applicable companies (MR-3) |
| Form AOC-2 | Related party transaction disclosure |
| AGM notice and minutes | Confirming financial approval at AGM |
| DSC of director and CA, CS, or CMA | For digital signing |

## Process

1. **Audit completion:** Statutory auditor completes audit of financial statements.
2. **Board approval:** Board approves audited financials and the Directors' Report.
3. **AGM convening:** Annual General Meeting is held by September 30 (for FY ending March 31), where shareholders approve the financials.
4. **Form preparation:** AOC-4 is prepared with all attachments, financials, reports, and supporting documents.
5. **Professional certification:** A practising CA, CS, or CMA verifies the form.
6. **DSC signing:** The form is digitally signed by a director and the certifying professional.
7. **MCA portal filing:** The form is uploaded on mca.gov.in within 30 days of the AGM.
8. **SRN generation:** The system generates a Service Request Number as confirmation of filing.

## Government fees

| Authorized capital | MCA filing fee |
| Up to ₹1,00,000 | ₹200 |
| ₹1,00,001 – ₹4,99,999 | ₹300 |
| ₹5,00,000 – ₹24,99,999 | ₹400 |
| ₹25,00,000 – ₹99,99,999 | ₹500 |
| ₹1 crore and above | ₹600 |

Late filing additional fee: ₹100 per day (no upper cap).

## Timeline

| Activity | Timeline |
| FY end | March 31 |
| Audit completion | Within 6 months (by September 30) |
| AGM | By September 30 |
| AOC-4 filing | Within 30 days of AGM (typically October 30) |
| Total annual cycle | 7 months from FY end |

## Frequently asked questions

**1. What happens if a company misses the AOC-4 filing deadline by 6 months?** The company accrues a late fee of ₹100 per day from the due date, for 6 months of delay, this works out to approximately ₹18,000 in late fees. Beyond financial penalty, the company's compliance status is flagged as in default on the MCA portal, which can affect its ability to file other statutory forms, raise loans, or enter into business contracts that require compliance verification.

**2. Can AOC-4 be revised after filing?** Yes, AOC-4 can be revised through a fresh filing. If any error or omission is discovered after the original filing, the company can file a revised AOC-4 with the corrected information. The revised filing must be approved by the board and shareholders (in case of material changes) and filed with the MCA.

**3. Is AOC-4 mandatory for an OPC (One Person Company)?** Yes, OPCs must file AOC-4 annually like any other company. However, OPCs file AOC-4 within 180 days from the end of the financial year (i.e., by September 27 for FY ending March 31), and they do not need to hold an AGM, financial statements need only be approved by the sole member.

**4. What is AOC-4 XBRL and which companies must file it?** AOC-4 XBRL is the XBRL (eXtensible Business Reporting Language) format of AOC-4, mandatory for: (a) all listed companies and their subsidiaries, (b) companies with paid-up capital of ₹5 crore or more, (c) companies with turnover of ₹100 crore or more, (d) all companies required to prepare consolidated financial statements. XBRL filing requires specialised software and CA or CS expertise.

**5. Does AOC-4 need to be filed even if a company had no business activity during the year?** Yes. Even dormant or non-operational companies must file AOC-4 with NIL or minimal financial statements. The only exception is companies that have obtained Dormant Company status under Section 455, they have separate, simplified filing requirements.

All service content is for informational purposes. MCA compliance requirements, fees, and procedures are subject to change. Consult qualified company secretaries or chartered accountants for case-specific guidance based on your company's structure, status, and specific transaction needs.

services.name: MGT-7A Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for MGT-7A Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## MGT-7A filing

### Detailed overview

Form MGT-7A is the simplified Annual Return form prescribed for small companies and One Person Companies (OPCs) under the Companies Act, 2013. Introduced through MCA notification on March 5, 2021, MGT-7A replaced the earlier MGT-7 filing requirement for these specific categories of companies, significantly reducing the compliance burden for small businesses.

The form captures essential annual information, shareholders, directors, capital, and key changes, but with fewer mandatory schedules and disclosures compared to the full MGT-7. This recognition of the smaller scale of operations of these companies makes MCA compliance more accessible for startups, SMEs, and one-person businesses.

### Key features

- Simplified annual return for eligible small companies and all OPCs
- Fewer schedules than MGT-7, no MGT-8 in the same way as full MGT-7
- Director DSC pathway without mandatory practising CS in the same manner as MGT-7 for many filers

## Applicability

MGT-7A applies to small companies, where paid-up capital ≤ ₹4 crore and turnover ≤ ₹40 crore, and to one person companies (OPCs), all OPCs regardless of size. A "Small Company" under Section 2(85) of the Companies Act must satisfy both conditions on paid-up capital and turnover. If either threshold is breached, the company must file regular MGT-7. It is applicable pan-India.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form | MGT-7A |
| Due date | Within 60 days of AGM (regular companies) |
| Due date for OPC | Within 60 days from completion of 6 months from FY end (no AGM) |
| Documents | Shareholding pattern, director details, debt particulars |
| Certification | DSC of director (CS certification not mandatory) |
| Simplified vs MGT-7 | No MGT-8 required as described for the large-company MGT-7 track |

## Benefits

- Simplified compliance, fewer disclosures
- Lower professional fees (CS certification not mandatory in the same way)
- Faster preparation
- Recognition of small company and OPC status
- Same legal validity as MGT-7 for eligible filers

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Late filing | ₹100 per day |
| Continued non-filing (3 years) | Director disqualification |
| Strike-off risk | After persistent non-compliance |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Shareholding pattern | Current shareholders with shares held |
| Director particulars | DIN, dates of appointment or cessation |
| Debt and charges | If any |
| AGM or board approval | Confirming the annual return |
| Director's DSC | For form signing |

## Process

1. Confirm small company or OPC eligibility (verify paid-up capital and turnover thresholds).
2. Compile annual data.
3. Hold AGM (for small companies) or use board resolution (for OPC).
4. Prepare MGT-7A.
5. Director DSC signing.
6. File on the MCA portal.

## Government fees

| Authorized capital | Fee |
| Up to ₹1 lakh | ₹200 |
| ₹1 lakh – ₹5 lakh | ₹300 |
| ₹5 lakh – ₹25 lakh | ₹400 |
| ₹25 lakh – ₹1 crore | ₹500 |
| Above ₹1 crore | ₹600 |

Late fee: ₹100 per day.

## Timeline

60 days from AGM date for small companies, for OPCs, 60 days after 6 months from FY end (i.e., by November 27 for FY ending March 31).

## Frequently asked questions

**1. If our company crosses the small company threshold mid-year, do we file MGT-7A or MGT-7?** Eligibility is determined based on the data as on the AGM date or relevant balance sheet date. If the company crossed thresholds during the year, MGT-7 (regular) applies for that year onwards.

**2. Does MGT-7A require Practising Company Secretary certification?** No, unlike MGT-7 which requires CS certification for many companies, MGT-7A does not require mandatory CS certification, only director's DSC is sufficient.

**3. Can a startup that grew rapidly continue filing MGT-7A?** Only if both paid-up capital remains ≤ ₹4 crore and turnover remains ≤ ₹40 crore. Crossing either threshold mandates MGT-7 filing.

**4. What is the OPC-specific MGT-7A timeline?** OPCs do not hold AGMs. MGT-7A for OPCs is due 60 days after the expiry of 6 months from FY end, typically by November 27 for FY ending March 31.

**5. Is MGT-7A filing applicable to dormant small companies?** Yes. Small companies, even if dormant, must file MGT-7A annually unless they obtain formal Dormant Company status under Section 455.

services.name: MSME-1 Form Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for MSME-1 Form Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## MSME-1 form filing

### Detailed overview

Form MSME-1 is the half-yearly return required to be filed by every specified company (defined as a company that receives goods or services from a Micro or Small Enterprise) under Section 405 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Companies (Furnishing of Information about Payment to Micro and Small Enterprises) Order, 2019.

The form captures details of payments due to MSME suppliers exceeding 45 days from acceptance, protecting MSMEs from delayed payments by larger companies and ensuring compliance with the MSMED Act, 2006.

### Key features

- Half-yearly filing for payments outstanding beyond 45 days to micro and small enterprises
- NIL return if no such dues, still mandatory
- Udyam-based supplier identification in practice

## Applicability

Filer: all companies receiving supplies from MSMEs, particularly applicable to companies with significant procurement from small vendors. Companies must file even if there are no outstanding dues to MSMEs (NIL return).

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form | MSME-1 |
| Frequency | Half-yearly |
| Due dates | April 30 (for October to March period), October 31 (for April to September period) |
| Information | Outstanding dues to MSMEs greater than 45 days as on the period-end |
| Director DSC | For signing |

## Benefits

- Compliance with MSME protection regulations
- Avoids penalties under the Companies Act
- Demonstrates ethical procurement practices
- Aligns with government MSME support agenda

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Non-filing | Penalty up to ₹25,000 on company |
| Continued default | Daily penalty plus officer-in-default penalty |
| False information | Higher penalties |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| List of MSME suppliers | With invoices greater than 45 days outstanding |
| Reasons for delay | If specified |
| Form MSME-1 | MCA prescribed |
| Director DSC | For signing |

## Process

1. Identify all MSME suppliers (verify Udyam registrations)
2. Identify outstanding payments greater than 45 days as on period end
3. Compile list with invoice details and outstanding amounts
4. Prepare Form MSME-1
5. File on the MCA portal by deadline

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| MSME-1 | ₹200 to ₹600 |
| Late fee | ₹100 per day |

## Timeline

Half-yearly, April 30 and October 31.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. How do we identify MSME suppliers?** Suppliers should provide their Udyam Registration Number. Without Udyam registration, suppliers do not qualify as MSMEs and are not tracked in MSME-1.

**2. What if there are no outstanding payments to MSMEs?** A NIL Form MSME-1 should be filed. Filing is mandatory regardless of whether dues exist.

**3. Are dues to medium enterprises (above small) tracked?** No. MSME-1 is specifically for Micro and Small enterprise dues. Medium enterprise dues are not tracked.

**4. What if an MSME supplier has not claimed Udyam registration but qualifies?** The MSMED Act protections apply only to registered MSMEs. Without Udyam, supplier does not get statutory protections, but commercial best practice is to track and pay all suppliers timely.

**5. Can interest be charged on MSME dues greater than 45 days?** Yes, the MSMED Act allows MSMEs to claim interest at 3x the bank rate (compounding) on overdue payments. Significant cost to defaulting buyers.

services.name: Aadhar PAN Linking
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Aadhar PAN Linking — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Aadhaar–PAN Linking is a mandatory compliance requirement that integrates an individual's Permanent Account Number (PAN) with their Aadhaar number to ensure identity authentication and tax transparency. This linkage is mandated under Section 139AA of the Income-tax Act, 1961 and implemented by the Income Tax Department in coordination with the Unique Identification Authority of India. The objective of linking Aadhaar with PAN is to:

- Eliminate duplicate or fake PANs
- Strengthen tax compliance
- Enable seamless e-verification of returns
- Create a unified identity system for financial transactions

Failure to link Aadhaar with PAN within the prescribed timeline may lead to inoperative PAN status, restricting its usability for filing income tax returns, financial transactions, and KYC processes. The process is fully digital and can be completed through the Income Tax e-filing portal, making it accessible, fast, and user-friendly.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Ensures PAN Validity

Prevents PAN from becoming inoperative.

### Simplifies Income Tax Filing

Enables seamless e-verification of returns.

### Eliminates Duplicate PANs

Helps government curb tax evasion.

### Faster Processing of Refunds

Improves efficiency in tax refunds and assessments.

### Unified Identity System

Creates a single verified identity for financial transactions.

### Mandatory for Financial Compliance

Required for banking, investments, and high-value transactions.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Mandatory Compliance Requirement

Non-linking leads to PAN becoming inoperative.

### Data Mismatch Issues

Differences in name, DOB, or gender may cause rejection.

### Late Fee Applicable

Penalty is levied for delayed linking.

### Dependency on Aadhaar Availability

Requires valid Aadhaar enrollment.

### Limited Flexibility

Certain exempt categories (like NRIs) must still verify eligibility.

## 4) Applicability Across India (Coverage)

Aadhaar–PAN Linking is governed by a central law, making it applicable across India.

### Applicable in All Regions

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Ladakh, etc.

### Administering Authorities

- Income Tax Department
- Unique Identification Authority of India

Who is Required to Link?

- All individuals holding PAN and Aadhaar
- Residents as per Income Tax Act

### Exemptions (subject to conditions)

- Non-Resident Indians (NRIs)
- Certain categories as notified by government

## 5) Key Features of Aadhaar–PAN Linking

### Mandatory Integration

Required under Income Tax provisions.

### Digital Process

Completed entirely online via e-filing portal.

### One-Time Linking

Once linked, no renewal required.

### OTP-Based Verification

Authentication through Aadhaar-linked mobile number.

### Immediate Status Check

Users can verify linking status online.

### Penalty for Delay

Late fee applicable if not linked within due date.

## 6) Documents & Information Required

Aadhaar–PAN Linking requires minimal documentation but accurate details.

### Essential Information

- PAN number
- Aadhaar number

### Personal Details

- Name (as per Aadhaar and PAN)
- Date of birth
- Gender

### Authentication Requirement

- Mobile number linked with Aadhaar for OTP

Optional

- Proof for correction (if mismatch exists)

Accuracy of details is critical for successful linking.

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step-by-Step Aadhaar–PAN Linking Process

1. Visit Income Tax Portal,  Access e-filing website.
2. Login or Use Quick Link,  Use PAN credentials or "Link Aadhaar" option.
3. Enter Details,  Provide PAN, Aadhaar number, and personal details.
4. Validate Information,  System verifies data with Aadhaar database.
5. OTP Verification,  Enter OTP received on Aadhaar-linked mobile number.
6. Submit Request,  Complete linking process.
7. Check Status,  Verify whether PAN is successfully linked with Aadhaar.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Late Fee for Linking

- ₹1,000 (as per current provisions)

### Other Costs

- No fee if linked within prescribed timelines (earlier phases)
- No recurring charges

### Additional Expenses

- Correction charges (if updating Aadhaar or PAN details)
- Professional assistance (optional)

### Important Notes

- Fee is subject to change as per government notification
- PAN may become inoperative if not linked
- Linking is a one-time compliance

Conclusion

Aadhaar–PAN Linking is a critical compliance measure aimed at strengthening India's tax ecosystem by ensuring transparency and eliminating duplication. It simplifies tax processes, enhances identity verification, and supports seamless financial operations. With a straightforward digital process and minimal documentation, linking Aadhaar with PAN is both efficient and essential. For individuals, timely compliance ensures uninterrupted use of PAN for financial transactions and tax filings, making it a foundational requirement in today's regulatory environment.

services.name: Aadhar Address Update
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Aadhar Address Update — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Aadhaar Address Update is the process of modifying or correcting the residential address linked to an individual's Aadhaar number to ensure that demographic records remain accurate, current, and legally valid. This process is governed by the Unique Identification Authority of India under the provisions of the Aadhaar Act, 2016. An updated Aadhaar address is essential because Aadhaar serves as a primary identity and address proof across various financial, governmental, and regulatory frameworks. Any mismatch or outdated address can lead to issues in KYC verification, banking services, subsidy benefits, and official communications. The update can be carried out through:

- Online Self-Service Update Portal (SSUP) (with OTP authentication)
- Offline/Physical update at Aadhaar Enrolment/Update Centers

UIDAI allows address updates through valid supporting documents or via Address Validation Letter (in specific cases), ensuring flexibility for individuals who may not have standard proof in their name. Aadhaar Address Update is not just a correction process, it is a critical compliance step to maintain seamless identity authentication across India's digital and financial ecosystem.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Accurate Identity Records

Ensures correct residential information in Aadhaar database.

### Seamless KYC Compliance

Facilitates smooth verification for banking, telecom, and financial services.

### Access to Government Benefits

Ensures proper delivery of subsidies and welfare schemes.

### Improved Communication Reliability

Enables receipt of official documents and correspondence.

### Essential for Financial Transactions

Required for account opening, loans, and investments.

### Supports Digital Authentication

Enhances Aadhaar-based OTP and e-KYC processes.

## 3) Challenges / Limitations

### Document Dependency

Requires valid proof of address for update.

### Data Mismatch Risks

Incorrect entries may lead to rejection.

### Processing Time

Offline updates may take longer for verification.

### Limited Update Frequency

Frequent changes may trigger additional verification.

### Mobile Number Requirement

Online update requires Aadhaar-linked mobile number.

## 4) Applicability Across India (Coverage)

Aadhaar Address Update is governed by a central authority, making it uniformly applicable across India.

### Applicable in All Regions

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Ladakh, etc.

### Administering Authority

- Unique Identification Authority of India

Who Can Apply?

- Any Aadhaar holder requiring address correction or update
- Residents shifting to a new location
- Individuals with incorrect address details in Aadhaar

## 5) Key Features of Aadhaar Address Update

### Multiple Update Modes

Online and offline options available.

### OTP-Based Authentication

Online updates verified via registered mobile number.

### Document-Based Verification

Requires valid proof of address.

### Centralized Database Update

Changes reflect across Aadhaar-linked services.

### Tracking Facility

Users can track update status using URN (Update Request Number).

### Secure and Regulated Process

Managed under UIDAI guidelines.

## 6) Documents & Information Required

Aadhaar Address Update relies on accurate supporting documents.

### Essential Details

- Aadhaar number
- Current and updated address

### Proof of Address (Any One)

- Utility bills (electricity, water, gas)
- Bank statement/passbook
- Rent agreement
- Passport
- Driving license

### Additional Inputs

- Mobile number linked with Aadhaar (for OTP)

### Alternative Option

- Address Validation Letter (if no document in applicant's name)

### Important Note

Documents must be valid, legible, and match applicant details.

## 7) Update Workflow (User Journey Perspective)

1. Choose Update Mode,  Select online portal or visit Aadhaar center.
2. Enter Aadhaar Details,  Provide Aadhaar number and authenticate via OTP.
3. Submit Address Details,  Enter new address accurately.
4. Upload/Provide Proof,  Attach valid address proof document.
5. Verification Process,  UIDAI verifies submitted details.
6. Acknowledgment Generation,  Receive Update Request Number (URN).
7. Status Tracking,  Track update progress online.
8. Confirmation of Update,  Download updated Aadhaar once approved.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Online Update Fee

- ₹50 (as per UIDAI guidelines)

### Offline Update Fee

- ₹50 at Aadhaar Enrolment/Update Centers

### Additional Costs

- Document preparation (if required)
- Professional assistance (optional)

### Important Notes

- Fees are subject to revision by UIDAI
- Charges are nominal and standardized across India
- Payment is required per update request

## Conclusion

Aadhaar Address Update is a fundamental process that ensures the accuracy and reliability of one of India's most widely used identity systems. With Aadhaar being integral to banking, taxation, and government services, maintaining updated address information is essential for uninterrupted access to benefits and compliance requirements. The process is designed to be user-friendly, secure, and accessible both online and offline. By keeping Aadhaar details current, individuals can ensure seamless identity verification and avoid complications in financial and administrative transactions, making it an essential aspect of modern digital compliance.

services.name: MSME Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for MSME Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Msme Registration (udyam Registration)

## Overview

MSME Registration,  officially known as Udyam Registration,  is the government registration for Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in India under the Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act, 2006 (MSMED Act). Since July 1, 2020, the registration is done online through the Udyam Registration portal (udyamregistration.gov.in),  entirely paperless, free of cost, and based on self-declaration. The Udyam Registration portal is integrated with the Income Tax and GST databases,  turnover and investment figures are verified directly from PAN and GSTIN data, eliminating the need for manual document submission. The output is the Udyam Registration Certificate with a unique 19-digit Udyam Registration Number (URN). MSMEs are classified as follows under the revised MSMED Act criteria (effective since June 2020): Category Investment in Plant & Machinery/Equipment Annual Turnover Micro Up to ₹1 crore Up to ₹5 crore Small Up to ₹10 crore Up to ₹50 crore Medium Up to ₹50 crore Up to ₹250 crore

Udyam Registration replaced the earlier EM Part-I & II system and the Udyog Aadhaar Memorandum (UAM) system. Businesses registered under UAM were required to migrate to Udyam by June 30, 2022. Udyam Registration is free,  no government fee is charged.

## Features

Completely online and paperless: No document upload required,  data pulled from PAN and GST systems automatically Aadhaar-based verification: Proprietors/managing partners/directors verify identity through Aadhaar OTP,  no physical presence required Unique Udyam Registration Number: A permanent 19-digit Udyam number (format: UDYAM-XX-00-0000000) issued on registration Udyam Registration Certificate: Downloadable digital certificate with QR code for instant verification Classification auto-revised: UDYAM system auto-updates MSME classification annually based on tax return data,  no need to manually revise as business grows Free of cost: Government does not charge any fee for Udyam Registration Self-declaration based: The registration is based on the applicant's self-declaration,  incorrect declarations attract cancellation and penalties NIC code-based classification: Businesses are classified by their National Industry Classification (NIC) code,  covering manufacturing and service activities

## Benefits

Priority Sector Lending: Banks are directed by RBI to provide priority sector lending to MSMEs at lower interest rates,  Udyam Registration is proof of MSME status for bank loan applications Credit Guarantee: Access to CGTMSE (Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises),  collateral-free loans up to ₹5 crore for micro and small enterprises; Udyam Registration is mandatory for this Government tenders: Public procurement policy mandates that 25% of government procurement must come from MSMEs,  with 4% reserved for SC/ST-owned MSMEs; Udyam Registration enables participation GeM portal MSME benefits: MSMEs on the Government e-Marketplace (GeM) get preferential treatment, special seller categories, and access to MSME-specific tenders Delayed payment protection: The MSMED Act mandates that buyers pay MSMEs within 45 days; registered MSMEs can file complaints for delayed payment through the MSME Samadhaan portal Subsidies and incentive schemes: Udyam Registration is a prerequisite for MSME-specific subsidies,  technology upgradation (CLCSS), cluster development (CDP), quality certification (ZED), ISO reimbursement, and export promotion Tax benefits: MSMEs registered under Udyam may be eligible for certain state-level tax exemptions, electricity tariff concessions, and stamp duty exemptions,  varying by state Trademark and patent fee concession: MSMEs with Udyam Registration get 50% concession on trademark filing fees and rebates on patent fees

## Limitations

Aadhaar mandatory: The registration process requires Aadhaar of the proprietor/managing partner/director,  entities without Aadhaar-linked proprietors face registration challenges Turnover-based auto-reclassification: As the business grows, the Udyam system auto-upgrades classification based on GST/IT return data,  a business may suddenly find itself classified as "Medium" or even beyond MSME limits without awareness, losing certain MSME benefits PAN and GSTIN mandatory: GST registration is required for Udyam Registration (for businesses above GST threshold); businesses below GST threshold can register with a self-declaration on turnover Single registration per Aadhaar: An individual can hold only one Udyam Registration,  businesses with diverse activities must consolidate under a single registration Not for large enterprises: Once turnover exceeds ₹250 crore or investment exceeds ₹50 crore, the entity exits MSME classification and all MSME benefits cease No retrospective benefit: MSME benefits apply from the date of Udyam Registration,  delayed registration means delayed access to benefits

## Applicability

Udyam Registration is available to: Entity Type

## Eligibility

Proprietorship Individual proprietors running business Hindu Undivided Family (HUF) Karta of HUF Partnership Firm Managing partner Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) Designated Partner Private Limited Company Director/Authorised Signatory Public Limited Company Director/Authorised Signatory Cooperative Society Authorised representative Trust (for business activities) Trustee/Authorised representative Self Help Groups (SHGs) Authorised representative

Activities covered: Manufacturing enterprises (producing goods) and Service enterprises (rendering services). Both are eligible for Udyam Registration under the MSME classification. Applicable pan-India,  all states and UTs. State governments also run state-level MSME schemes that reference Udyam Registration as the qualifying credential.

## Documents Required

Note: Udyam Registration is paperless,  no document upload is required. However, the following information/credentials must be available: Information/Credential Details Aadhaar number Of proprietor / managing partner / director PAN card Of the business entity GSTIN (if applicable) GST registration number Bank account details For verification NIC code of business activity National Industry Classification code Business address Registered/principal place of business Number of employees Approximate headcount Investment details Plant and machinery / equipment investment amount

## Process

1. Visit Udyam Registration Portal Go to udyamregistration.gov.in. Click on "For New Entrepreneurs who are not Registered yet as MSME."

2. Aadhaar Verification Enter Aadhaar number of the proprietor/managing partner/director. Verify via OTP sent to Aadhaar-linked mobile number.

3. PAN Verification Enter the PAN of the individual and the business entity. The system auto-populates investment and turnover data from the IT database.

4. GSTIN Entry Enter the GSTIN (for GST-registered businesses). Turnover data is auto-populated from the GST database.

5. Business Details Enter the business name, registered address, bank details, NIC activity code, number of employees, and other required details.

6. Self-Declaration and Submission Review all pre-filled and manually entered details. Submit the self-declaration and final application.

7. Udyam Registration Certificate The Udyam Registration Certificate with unique Udyam Registration Number is generated instantly and can be downloaded from the portal. Fees Service Government Fee Udyam Registration Nil (Free) Udyam Certificate download Nil


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Udyam Registration assistance ₹500 – ₹2,000 UAM to Udyam migration support ₹1,000 – ₹3,000 NIC code identification and classification advisory ₹1,000 – ₹2,500 MSME benefit utilisation consulting ₹3,000 – ₹10,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

Udyam Registration is permanent,  it does not expire and does not require periodic renewal. The registration remains valid as long as the enterprise continues to meet MSME classification criteria. The Udyam system auto-updates classification annually based on ITR and GST return data. If turnover or investment crosses MSME thresholds, the enterprise is automatically reclassified or exits MSME status,  but the registration record itself is permanent.

## Frequently asked questions

**I already have Udyog Aadhaar (UAM). Do I need to re-register on Udyam?** Yes. The Government of India mandated migration from Udyog Aadhaar Memorandum (UAM) to Udyam Registration by June 30, 2022. Businesses still operating only with UAM (without Udyam Registration) are no longer officially recognised as MSMEs for benefit purposes, bank lending, and government procurement. If you haven't migrated yet, you should do so immediately on the Udyam Registration portal under the "For those already having registration as UAM" option.

**Does a startup with DPIIT Recognition also need Udyam Registration separately?** Yes,  DPIIT Recognition and Udyam Registration are separate registrations with different benefits. DPIIT Recognition unlocks Startup India benefits (tax exemption, IPR concessions, regulatory relaxations). Udyam Registration unlocks MSME benefits (priority sector lending, CGTMSE, government procurement preference, delayed payment protection). If your startup meets the MSME classification criteria (turnover below ₹250 crore and investment below ₹50 crore), obtaining both DPIIT Recognition and Udyam Registration maximises the benefits available to you.

**Can my company have different Udyam Registrations for different branches or activities?** No. Udyam Registration is a single registration covering all activities and all plants/offices/branches of the enterprise. You cannot have separate Udyam Registrations for different branches or business activities of the same legal entity. All activities (including multiple NIC codes) and all locations are covered under one Udyam Registration. The registration reflects the enterprise as a whole,  not individual units.

**We are a manufacturing company that has grown significantly and now have a turnover of ₹260 crore. Do we lose MSME status automatically?** Under the Udyam system, MSME classification is auto-updated annually based on ITR and GST return data. If your annual turnover crosses ₹250 crore (the Medium Enterprise threshold), your enterprise will be reclassified as "beyond MSME",  losing MSME benefits prospectively. The reclassification happens at the beginning of the new financial year following the one in which the threshold was crossed. Existing benefits and contracts (like ongoing CGTMSE-guaranteed loans) continue under the original terms,  but new benefit applications would not be available after reclassification.

**I'm a freelancer providing IT services. Can I register on Udyam?** Yes. Service enterprises,  including independent professionals and freelancers providing IT services, consulting, design, or other services,  are eligible for Udyam Registration as service MSMEs, provided their annual turnover is below ₹250 crore. A freelancer can register as a proprietorship on the Udyam portal using their Aadhaar and PAN. Udyam Registration as a service MSME gives access to priority sector lending benefits, delayed payment protection (if you invoice corporate clients), and potential eligibility for government service contracts on GeM.
