services.name: BIS Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for BIS Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Bis Registration

## Overview

BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) Registration is a mandatory certification requirement for specified categories of products sold in India. The Bureau of Indian Standards, established under the Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016, is India's national standards body operating under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution. BIS certification ensures that products conform to relevant Indian Standards (IS standards),  covering quality, safety, performance, and reliability parameters. BIS offers three main product certification schemes: ISI Mark Scheme (Product Certification): For domestically manufactured products across categories like steel, cement, electrical goods, food products, and more. Products bearing the ISI Mark (IS certification mark) signal conformity to the relevant Indian Standard. Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS): For electronics and IT products (mobile phones, laptops, LED lights, power banks, set-top boxes, etc.) under the Electronics and Information Technology Goods (Requirement for Compulsory Registration) Order, 2012. CRS registration is mandatory and enforced by the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY). Foreign Manufacturer Certification Scheme (FMCS): For overseas manufacturers exporting products to India that fall under mandatory BIS certification categories. BIS registration/certification is legally mandatory for over 400 product categories. Selling non-certified products in these categories is a cognisable offence attracting prosecution, product seizure, and heavy penalties.

## Features

IS Mark (ISI Mark): The IS Certification Mark,  commonly called the ISI Mark,  is one of India's most recognised quality certification symbols; it is mandatory for over 100 product categories under the ISI Mark Scheme Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS): Electronic products must be registered under CRS before being manufactured in or imported into India; covers 75+ product categories including smartphones, tablets, laptops, LED products Hallmarking: BIS administers the gold and silver hallmarking scheme,  mandatory for gold jewellery sold in India (HUID-based hallmarking since 2021) Indian Standards: BIS publishes and maintains over 20,000 Indian Standards (IS standards) across sectors,  the technical backbone of the certification scheme BIS Care App: Consumers can verify the authenticity of BIS-certified products (ISI mark, hallmark) through the BIS Care mobile app and BIS website FMCS for importers: Foreign manufacturers exporting BIS-mandatory categories to India must obtain FMCS certification before shipping; Indian importers must verify this compliance

## Benefits

Legal market access: Without mandatory BIS certification (ISI Mark or CRS), products in covered categories cannot be legally sold in India,  certification is the licence to sell Consumer safety assurance: BIS-certified products meet defined safety and quality benchmarks,  reducing product failure rates, consumer hazard risks, and product liability exposure Competitive advantage: The ISI Mark is widely trusted by Indian consumers,  products bearing it command higher consumer confidence, better shelf placement, and pricing power E-commerce compliance: Amazon, Flipkart, and other platforms require BIS-CRS registration numbers for applicable electronics categories before listing approval Government procurement: Central and state government tenders for electrical goods, building materials, and other BIS-covered products require certified products,  certification unlocks this massive procurement market Export support: BIS certification under MRAs (Mutual Recognition Agreements) with foreign standards bodies can facilitate market access in certain export markets Hallmarking for jewellery: Mandatory BIS hallmarking enables jewellers to demonstrate purity compliance,  a significant trust factor in the Indian jewellery market

## Limitations

Factory inspection mandatory: BIS ISI Mark certification requires factory inspection by BIS officers,  this involves preparation, potential disruptions, and additional costs for factory upgrades to meet IS standard requirements Ongoing surveillance: BIS conducts periodic (quarterly/half-yearly) surveillance inspections of certified factories and may conduct market surveillance (purchasing products from retail and testing them),  non-conformance during surveillance leads to license suspension Test report requirement: Products must be tested at BIS-recognised/NABL-accredited laboratories and must pass before certification is granted,  testing costs and timelines add to the compliance burden High penalty for violations: Misuse of BIS certification mark or selling uncertified products in mandatory categories can result in imprisonment up to 2 years and fines up to ₹2 lakh (or both) under the BIS Act, 2016 Country-of-origin complexities: For CRS and FMCS, Chinese manufacturers face additional scrutiny and documentation requirements post 2020 trade tensions Category-specific challenges: Some IS standards are stringent and require significant product design or manufacturing process changes,  especially for SME manufacturers

## Applicability

ISI Mark: Mandatory for products in categories including electrical wires and cables, switches, plugs, sockets, cement, steel (TMT bars), LPG cylinders, helmets, baby food, packaged drinking water, and many more CRS (Compulsory Registration): Mandatory for electronics/IT products including mobile phones, laptops, tablets, LED lights, power banks, smart watches, set-top boxes, printers, UPS, and 75+ more categories FMCS: Foreign manufacturers of products falling under ISI/CRS mandatory categories who export to India Hallmarking: Mandatory for gold jewellery manufacturers and retailers (HUID-based since June 2021) Voluntary certification: Manufacturers of non-mandatory products can voluntarily obtain BIS certification to signal quality,  common in sectors like furniture, textiles, and toys State-wide applicability: BIS is a central government body with jurisdiction across all states and UTs. BIS has regional offices in New Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Chandigarh, and other cities, serving manufacturers across India.

## Documents Required

Document ISI Mark Scheme CRS Scheme Application form Form V (BIS portal) Online CRS application Certificate of Incorporation / GST certificate ✓ ✓ PAN of the company ✓ ✓ Factory/premises address proof ✓ ✓ List of products and models ✓ ✓ Test report from BIS-recognised/NABL lab ✓ ✓ IS Standard compliance declaration ✓ ✓ Factory layout plan ✓ Not required List of manufacturing machinery ✓ Not required Quality control procedures documentation ✓ Not required Import documents (for FMCS) Not applicable Not applicable (separate scheme) Bank guarantee / security deposit ✓ (varies) ✓ (varies) Agreement with BIS (signed) ✓ ✓

## Process

1. Identify Applicable IS Standard Identify the relevant Indian Standard (IS number) for your product category on the BIS website (bis.gov.in).

2. Application on BIS MIS Portal Register and submit Form V application on the BIS MIS portal with all company and product details.

3. Product Testing Get products tested at a BIS-recognised or NABL-accredited laboratory. Submit test reports (must pass all parameters of the relevant IS standard).

4. BIS Factory Inspection BIS officers conduct an inspection of the manufacturing premises to verify production processes, quality control procedures, and infrastructure conformance with IS standard requirements.

5. Grant of License Upon satisfactory inspection and test reports, BIS grants the IS Certification License with a unique CM/L number (Certification Mark/License number). The ISI mark can now be used on products.

6. Ongoing Surveillance Maintain quality control procedures and cooperate with BIS for periodic surveillance inspections and market surveillance testing. CRS Registration Process:

1. Product Testing Get the electronics product tested at a BIS-empanelled laboratory for conformance to applicable Indian Standard (safety/EMI-EMC parameters).

2. Online CRS Application Apply on the BIS CRS portal (crsbis.in) with product details, test reports, and applicant information.

3. BIS Review and Approval BIS reviews the application and test reports. Upon approval, the CRS Registration Number is issued within 25–45 days.

4. Mark products with CRS number The CRS Registration Number must be marked on each unit of the product, physically on the device or its packaging. Fees Scheme Type Government Fee (Approx.) ISI Mark Application fee ₹1,000 – ₹5,000 ISI Mark Marking fee (annual, per product) ₹500 – ₹5,000 + royalty on production ISI Mark Factory inspection charges ₹15,000 – ₹50,000+ (travel + per diem) CRS Registration Per product/model ₹1,000 – ₹10,000 NABL lab testing Per product category ₹10,000 – ₹1,00,000+


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee BIS ISI Mark application support ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 CRS registration support (per model) ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 FMCS application support ₹30,000 – ₹75,000 Factory audit preparation consulting ₹10,000 – ₹30,000

Professional fees are exclusive of GST @18%. Lab testing fees are charged directly by the testing laboratory.

## Validity

Scheme License Validity Renewal ISI Mark License 1–2 years Annual renewal with surveillance compliance CRS Registration 2 years Renewal before expiry on BIS portal FMCS License 1–2 years Annual renewal Hallmarking License 1–5 years Renewal before expiry

## Frequently asked questions

**My product is an LED bulb manufactured in China and I want to import and sell it in India. What BIS compliance do I need?** LED lamps fall under the Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS). The Chinese manufacturer must either: (a) obtain FMCS (Foreign Manufacturer Certification Scheme) certification from BIS for the specific LED product models, or (b) the Indian importer can apply for CRS registration on behalf of the manufacturer with a test report from a BIS-empanelled lab. Without valid BIS-CRS registration, LED bulbs cannot be legally imported into or sold in India. Customs authorities check BIS compliance at the port of entry.

**I'm a small manufacturer of steel water bottles. Is BIS certification mandatory?** Steel water bottles are currently under voluntary BIS certification,  they are not in the mandatory ISI Mark or CRS list. However, if you wish to supply to government institutions, large organised retailers, or export to certain markets, voluntary BIS certification (IS 14846 for stainless steel cookware/utensils) significantly boosts credibility. Monitor BIS notifications,  product categories are regularly added to the mandatory list; having voluntary certification puts you ahead of compliance requirements.

**How long does it typically take to get BIS ISI Mark certification?** The timeline for ISI Mark certification varies: laboratory testing takes 4–8 weeks; BIS factory inspection scheduling takes 4–8 weeks after application; BIS review and grant of license takes an additional 4–6 weeks. In total, end-to-end ISI Mark certification typically takes 4–6 months for a well-prepared applicant. Delays in document submission, factory non-conformance during inspection, or product test failures extend this significantly. CRS registration for electronics is faster,  typically 25–45 days from complete application submission.

**Can I use the ISI Mark on my products before receiving the BIS license?** Absolutely not. Using the ISI Mark without a valid BIS certification license is a cognisable offence under the Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016, punishable with imprisonment of up to 2 years and/or a fine of up to ₹2 lakh. BIS conducts regular market surveillance and can initiate prosecution for unauthorised mark usage. Manufacturers must wait for the official license grant before printing the ISI Mark (with the CM/L license number) on their products.

**Our electronics product model has been updated with minor component changes. Do we need a fresh CRS registration?** Whether a product update requires fresh CRS registration depends on the nature of the change. Minor changes (firmware updates, colour changes, cosmetic alterations) that do not affect electrical safety, EMI-EMC compliance, or the core specifications covered in the original test report may not require fresh registration,  but a declaration of conformity for the modified model should be maintained. Significant changes (different chipset, changed power supply, altered circuit design) require fresh product testing and a new or amended CRS registration. BIS has issued guidance on this,  consulting a BIS compliance expert is advisable before releasing an updated model.

Certificate of Origin (COO),  Overview

Our consultants support Certificate of Origin (COO) under START NEW BUSINESS, from applicability review through filings, department queries and final acknowledgement.

Applicability

This service is intended for clients who need structured, compliant handling with clear documentation and realistic timelines.

Benefits

Fewer rejections from incomplete paperwork, coordinated submissions, and a single point of contact for follow-ups.

Process

Requirement capture → document checklist → preparation and validation → submission to authority → tracking and query handling → closure.

Documents required

Entity KYC, address proof, category-specific forms and annexures. A tailored checklist is shared after the first consultation.

services.name: STARTUP INDIA REGISTRATION
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for STARTUP INDIA REGISTRATION — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Startup India Registration

## Overview

Startup India Registration refers to the process of getting your startup officially recognised by DPIIT (Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade) under the Startup India initiative. This is the same DPIIT Recognition covered in our DPIIT Registration service,  however, many users specifically search for "Startup India Registration" as the entry point to the Startup India ecosystem. The Startup India initiative was launched on January 16, 2016 by the Government of India with the vision of building a strong ecosystem for nurturing innovation and startups in India that will drive sustainable economic growth and generate large-scale employment opportunities. Startup India Registration (DPIIT Recognition) is the foundational step in accessing the full suite of Startup India benefits,  ranging from tax exemptions and self-certification of labour law compliance to IPR fee concessions, government procurement preference, and access to the Fund of Funds. The registration is done on the Startup India portal (startupindia.gov.in), is completely free, and is processed within a few working days for eligible entities. For a complete coverage of eligibility, features, process, and FAQs, please refer to our DPIIT Registration service page. This page focuses on the Startup India programme context and the specific benefits ecosystem.

## Features

Free and online: Startup India Registration is completely free and done online at startupindia.gov.in DPIIT Recognition Certificate: The output is the DPIIT Recognition Certificate with unique recognition number Access to Startup India Hub: Registered startups get access to the Startup India Hub,  a knowledge, mentoring, and networking platform Tax exemption gateway: Gateway to applying for Section 80-IAC income tax exemption and Section 54GB capital gains exemption Self-certification of compliance: 5-year self-certification under 6 labour laws and 3 environmental laws IPR fee concessions: 80% rebate on patent fees and 50% on trademark fees Startup India showcase: Profile on Startup India portal,  discoverable by investors, incubators, and government stakeholders Access to government schemes: Access to Startup India-specific schemes,  Seed Fund Scheme (up to ₹20 lakh for prototype development), Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS,  up to ₹50 lakh), and AIM (Atal Innovation Mission) programmes Incubator network: Access to DPIIT-recognised incubators and accelerators across India

## Benefits

Seed funding access: The Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) provides up to ₹50 lakh to DPIIT-recognised startups for proof of concept, prototype development, product trials, market entry, and commercialisation,  disbursed through DPIIT-recognised incubators Income tax holiday: After IMB approval, 100% income tax exemption for 3 years out of the first 10 years,  significantly improving early-stage unit economics Capital gains exemption: Exemption from capital gains tax under Section 54GB when proceeds from residential property sale are invested in eligible startup equity Angel tax exemption: DPIIT-recognised startups are exempt from Section 56(2)(viib),  the "angel tax",  on issue of shares at a premium (with conditions and investment value limits) Government procurement: Participation in government procurement without turnover and prior experience requirements,  access to a ₹70,000+ crore annual government procurement market Regulatory sandbox: DPIIT facilitates access to regulatory sandboxes with SEBI, RBI, IRDAI, and other regulators,  allowing startups to test innovative financial, fintech, and insurance products under relaxed regulatory conditions Easy exit: IBC fast-track winding up (within 90 days) for registered startups classified as fast-track companies

## Limitations

10-year and ₹100 crore cap: Recognition lapses if the startup crosses 10 years from incorporation or ₹100 crore in annual turnover Innovation requirement: Generic or conventional businesses do not qualify; innovation, technology, or IP must be demonstrable Tax exemption requires IMB approval: DPIIT Recognition alone does not grant income tax exemption,  a separate Inter-Ministerial Board (IMB) application is needed Angel tax exemption,  investment cap: Angel tax exemption for DPIIT-recognised startups applies only for investments up to the fair market value of shares,  investments above FMV are still taxable SISFS fund is limited: The seed fund is channelled through DPIIT-recognised incubators who have their own selection criteria,  recognition does not guarantee seed fund access Not all benefits are central: State-specific startup benefits (state seed funds, state innovation schemes) are separate from Startup India and have their own eligibility criteria

## Applicability

Criterion Requirement Entity type Private Limited Company, LLP, or Registered Partnership Firm Age Incorporated/registered within the past 10 years Annual turnover Below ₹100 crore in any year since incorporation Nature Working towards innovation/development or scalable business model Restriction Not formed by splitting/restructuring existing business

Applicable across all states and UTs,  Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi NCR, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune, Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Kolkata, Kochi, and all Tier 2 and Tier 3 startup ecosystems.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Certificate of Incorporation / Registration | For Pvt Ltd, LLP, or registered partnership |
| PAN of the entity | Business PAN |
| Innovation description | 500-word description of product/service and innovation |
| Pitch deck or demo link (optional) | Supports innovation description |
| Founders' details | Name, DIN/DPIN, PAN, Aadhaar |
| Revenue details (if any) | Confirming sub-₹100 crore threshold |

## Process

1. startupindia.gov.in Portal Register on the Startup India portal with the company's email. Create an entity profile.

2. Application Details Fill in incorporation details, founders' information, entity type, and industry sector.

3. Innovation Description Write a compelling description of the startup's innovation or scalable business model. This is the most reviewed section.

4. Document Upload Upload the Certificate of Incorporation.

5. Self-Declaration Submit the self-declaration of eligibility.

6. DPIIT Review Review and recognition within 2–10 working days typically.

7. Recognition Certificate Download the DPIIT Recognition Certificate from the Startup India portal. Fees Service Government Fee Startup India Registration Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Startup India registration support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Innovation write-up drafting ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 IMB tax exemption application (Section 80-IAC) ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 Comprehensive Startup India benefits advisory ₹10,000 – ₹25,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

DPIIT Recognition (Startup India Registration) is valid until the entity crosses 10 years from incorporation date or ₹100 crore in annual turnover,  whichever occurs first. No periodic renewal is required. The certificate does not expire within the 10-year eligibility window.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can a Partnership Firm (not LLP) get Startup India Registration?** Yes. Registered Partnership Firms are eligible for DPIIT Recognition under the Startup India framework,  provided they meet all other eligibility criteria (age, turnover, and innovation requirement). The partner applying for recognition must be a registered partner of the firm. Note that Registered Partnership Firms have certain limitations compared to Pvt Ltd companies and LLPs,  particularly in raising equity investment and issuing shares,  which are important considerations for startups planning to raise external funding.

**We are a 4-year-old edtech startup with ₹15 crore revenue. Are we eligible for Startup India Registration?** Yes, based on the stated facts. You meet the age criterion (within 10 years) and the turnover criterion (below ₹100 crore). Eligibility depends additionally on demonstrating the innovation/technology/IP element. An edtech startup developing proprietary learning technology, adaptive AI, or innovative pedagogy would typically qualify. A pure content aggregation or tutoring marketplace business model without a technology-driven innovation element might face more scrutiny in the recognition review.

**Our startup was rejected for DPIIT Recognition. What should we do?** If DPIIT rejects a recognition application, the applicant can reapply with a strengthened innovation description. The most common reason for rejection is an insufficient or unclear description of the innovation element. Revise the innovation write-up to clearly articulate: (a) what the product/service is; (b) what specific problem it solves; (c) how it uses technology, IP, or an innovative business model; (d) its scalability potential and employment/wealth creation impact. If the rejection reason is entity-type or eligibility-related, address the structural issue first before reapplying.

**After getting DPIIT Recognition, how do we apply for the Section 80-IAC income tax exemption?** Section 80-IAC income tax exemption requires a separate application to the Inter-Ministerial Board (IMB),  constituted by DPIIT,  through the Startup India portal. The IMB evaluates whether the startup is engaged in genuine innovation and meets additional criteria for tax exemption. The process involves submitting detailed information about the business model, technology, product, team, financials, and innovation. IMB applications are reviewed rigorously,  not all DPIIT-recognised startups are granted 80-IAC exemption. Professional support for the IMB application is highly advisable given its complexity and financial impact.

**Can an existing profitable business spin off a startup subsidiary and get DPIIT Recognition for the subsidiary?** Yes, provided the subsidiary is independently incorporated (as a new Pvt Ltd or LLP), meets the age criterion, has its own turnover below ₹100 crore, and is genuinely working on innovation. However, the restriction that the entity should "not be formed by splitting up or reconstruction of an existing business" must be carefully navigated,  if the subsidiary is merely taking over an existing business segment (rather than developing a new, innovative product/service), DPIIT may deny recognition. The subsidiary must demonstrate independent innovation, not just a restructured version of the parent's existing operations.

services.name: ISO Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for ISO Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Iso Certification

## Overview

ISO Certification refers to the certification that an organisation's management systems, products, services, or processes conform to one or more International Standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO),  an independent, non-governmental international body headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, with 167 national member bodies including the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) representing India. ISO standards are developed through international consensus among technical experts and cover virtually every industry,  from quality management and environmental management to information security, food safety, occupational health, energy management, and medical devices. ISO Certification is granted by accredited third-party Certification Bodies (CBs),  not by ISO directly. In India, the accreditation body for ISO certification bodies is the National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB), operating under the Quality Council of India (QCI). The most commonly sought ISO certifications in India include: ISO Standard Focus Area ISO 9001:2015 Quality Management Systems (QMS) ISO 14001:2015 Environmental Management Systems (EMS) ISO 45001:2018 Occupational Health and Safety (OH&S) ISO 27001:2022 Information Security Management Systems (ISMS) ISO 22000:2018 Food Safety Management Systems (FSMS) ISO 13485:2016 Medical Devices Quality Management ISO 50001:2018 Energy Management Systems ISO/IEC 17025:2017 Testing and Calibration Laboratories ISO 20000-1:2018 IT Service Management ISO 26000:2010 Social Responsibility (guidance, not certifiable)

## Features

Third-party certification: ISO certification is issued by accredited third-party Certification Bodies,  not by the government or ISO directly System-based, not product-based: ISO certification (e.g., ISO 9001) certifies the management system, not individual products,  though ISO standards for testing labs and medical devices are more product-specific NABCB/IAF accreditation: Certification Bodies operating in India should be accredited by NABCB (India) or by members of the International Accreditation Forum (IAF) for international recognition Three-year certification cycle: ISO certificates are typically valid for 3 years with annual surveillance audits in years 1 and 2, and a full recertification audit in year 3 Gap analysis to certification: The certification process begins with a gap analysis of current practices against the ISO standard requirements, followed by system implementation, internal audit, and certification audit Scope-defined: The ISO certificate specifies the exact scope of certification,  which products, services, locations, and processes are covered International recognition: ISO certification from an IAF-accredited body is globally recognised,  no need for country-specific certification in most cases

## Benefits

Market access and tender eligibility: Many government and corporate tenders mandate ISO 9001 certification,  especially for IT services (ISO 27001 for data handling), manufacturing (ISO 9001), and food production (ISO 22000) Export facilitation: International buyers (European, US, Japanese) routinely require ISO certification as a supplier qualification criterion,  certification opens export market doors Process improvement: Implementing ISO standards forces systematic process documentation, measurement, and continuous improvement,  resulting in operational efficiency gains beyond the certification itself Customer confidence: ISO certification signals commitment to quality and continuous improvement,  a powerful marketing tool for B2B sales, enterprise client acquisition, and institutional buyer trust IT and data contracts: ISO 27001 (information security) is increasingly mandatory for IT companies handling sensitive client data,  especially for BPO, ITES, and SaaS companies serving US, EU, and UK clients Regulatory compliance alignment: ISO 22000 (food safety) aligns closely with FSSAI's Food Safety Management System requirements,  dual compliance is easier for ISO 22000-certified food companies Reduced defect rates and waste: ISO 9001 implementation typically results in measurable reductions in product defects, customer complaints, and production waste Insurance premium reduction: Some insurers offer premium discounts for ISO-certified organisations, recognising the systematic risk management processes that ISO implementation entails

## Limitations

Cost of implementation: Implementing an ISO management system (documentation, training, process changes, internal audit) can be resource-intensive,  especially for SMEs and first-time implementers Annual surveillance costs: Certification Bodies charge annual surveillance audit fees in addition to the initial certification fee,  making ISO an ongoing cost, not a one-time expense Risk of certificate mills: The ISO certification market has unaccredited "certificate mills" that issue certificates without rigorous audits,  these certificates lack credibility and are rejected by discerning buyers; choosing a NABCB-accredited CB is essential Implementation vs. compliance gap: Many organisations obtain ISO certification through documentation without genuinely implementing the system,  auditors from reputable CBs detect this, but it undermines the operational benefits of ISO 3-year recertification: Major recertification audits every 3 years require significant preparation time and effort Scope limitations: The ISO certificate covers only the defined scope,  processes, products, or locations outside the scope are not certified and using the certificate to imply broader coverage is misleading

## Applicability

ISO 9001: Manufacturing companies, service firms, IT companies, educational institutions,  virtually all sectors ISO 14001: Manufacturing, mining, energy, construction,  industries with significant environmental impact ISO 45001: All industries with workplace safety risks,  construction, manufacturing, chemicals, logistics ISO 27001: IT companies, BPOs, fintech, healthtech, e-commerce,  any business handling sensitive digital data ISO 22000: Food manufacturers, food processors, restaurant chains, catering companies, cold chain operators ISO 13485: Medical device manufacturers, distributors,  mandatory alignment for CE marking and US FDA approvals ISO/IEC 17025: Testing and calibration laboratories seeking NABL accreditation Applicable pan-India,  ISO certification is available to businesses across all states and UTs through accredited Certification Bodies with offices and auditors across India.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Company profile | Overview of business, processes, and locations |
| Scope of certification | Proposed scope statement for the ISO certificate |
| Existing QMS/management system documentation | Quality manual, procedures, work instructions (if any) |
| Organisational chart | Management structure and responsibilities |
| List of key processes | Process map of the organisation |
| Previous audit reports (if any) | Internal audit, management review records |
| Legal and regulatory compliance list | Applicable laws and regulations |
| Certificate of Incorporation | Legal entity documents |
| Previous ISO certificate (for recertification) | If applying for recertification or CB change |
| Interested parties analysis | Documented assessment of stakeholders |

## Process

1. Gap Analysis Assess current practices against the requirements of the applicable ISO standard. Identify gaps between existing systems and standard requirements.

2. System Implementation Develop and implement the required documentation (Quality Manual, SOPs, procedures), processes, and controls. Train employees on the new system.

3. Internal Audit Conduct a full internal audit of the implemented management system against the ISO standard requirements.

4. Management Review Hold a formal Management Review Meeting to review audit findings, objectives, and system performance, as required by the ISO standard.

5. Select Certification Body Choose a NABCB-accredited (or IAF member-accredited) Certification Body. Obtain quotes and select based on CB's sector expertise, accreditation scope, and pricing.

6. Stage 1 Audit (Document Review) The CB conducts a Stage 1 (Document Review) audit, reviewing the management system documentation and assessing readiness for Stage 2.

7. Stage 2 Audit (Certification Audit) The CB conducts the Stage 2 (Certification) audit, on-site assessment of system implementation, process effectiveness, and standard conformance.

8. Corrective Action and Certification Address any non-conformances identified in Stage 2. Upon satisfactory closure, the CB issues the ISO certification certificate.

9. Surveillance Audits Annual surveillance audits in Year 1 and Year 2. Recertification audit in Year 3 to renew the certificate. Fees ISO Standard Implementation Cost (Consultancy) Certification Body Fee (3-year cycle) ISO 9001:2015 (small org) ₹50,000 – ₹1,50,000 ₹40,000 – ₹1,00,000 ISO 9001:2015 (medium org) ₹1,50,000 – ₹4,00,000 ₹80,000 – ₹2,00,000 ISO 27001:2022 ₹3,00,000 – ₹10,00,000 ₹1,00,000 – ₹3,00,000 ISO 14001:2015 ₹1,00,000 – ₹3,00,000 ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000 ISO 22000:2018 ₹1,50,000 – ₹4,00,000 ₹80,000 – ₹2,00,000 ISO 45001:2018 ₹1,00,000 – ₹3,00,000 ₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000 All fees exclusive of GST @18%. CB fees vary based on organisation size (employee count), number of sites, and complexity of scope.


## Validity

ISO certificates are valid for 3 years from the date of certification. Year 1 and Year 2 involve annual surveillance audits (shorter than the initial certification audit). Year 3 involves a full recertification audit to renew the certificate for another 3-year cycle. Failure to complete surveillance audits on time leads to certificate suspension; continued non-compliance leads to withdrawal.

## Frequently asked questions

**Does ISO 9001 certification guarantee product quality?** ISO 9001 certifies the Quality Management System,  the processes, controls, and systematic approach the organisation uses to deliver products and services. It does not guarantee that every individual product is defect-free. What it does guarantee is that the organisation has systematic processes for preventing defects, monitoring quality, addressing customer complaints, and continually improving. A well-implemented ISO 9001 system typically results in significantly lower defect rates, but the certification is a system certification, not a product guarantee.

**Can a startup or very small business (5–10 employees) get ISO 9001 certification?** Yes. ISO 9001 has no minimum size requirement,  it applies to organisations of any size. For very small businesses, the documentation and implementation requirements are proportional to the organisation's complexity. A 5-person company does not need the same level of documentation and process formality as a 500-person factory. ISO 9001's "risk-based thinking" and "proportionality" principles allow small organisations to implement the standard in a practical, right-sized manner. Many SMEs and startups have successfully obtained ISO 9001 certification.

**We received an ISO certificate from an online provider for ₹5,000. Is it valid?** Almost certainly not. ISO certificates issued by unaccredited bodies,  often marketed online at suspiciously low prices,  are not recognised by government authorities, serious corporate buyers, or export market partners. Valid ISO certification requires accreditation of the Certification Body by NABCB (India) or an IAF member accreditation body. Always verify the CB's accreditation status on the NABCB website (nabcb.qci.org.in) or the IAF CertSearch database before engaging them. Unaccredited certificates have zero value and can damage your credibility.

**Is ISO 27001 mandatory for IT companies in India?** ISO 27001 is not legally mandated by Indian law for IT companies (unlike some sectors where specific regulations may reference it). However, it is commercially mandatory for many IT companies,  especially those serving US, European, or UK clients, working on government IT projects, handling sensitive personal data under DPDPA 2023 compliance frameworks, or seeking enterprise client contracts. Many RFPs for IT outsourcing, cloud services, and data processing contracts specifically require ISO 27001 certification. Companies without it face commercial exclusion from significant market segments.

**Can we get multiple ISO certifications (e.g., ISO 9001 + ISO 14001 + ISO 45001) in one integrated audit?** Yes,  and this is increasingly common practice. The three management system standards ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001 share a common High-Level Structure (HLS/Annex SL), making integration relatively straightforward. Organisations can implement an Integrated Management System (IMS) covering quality, environment, and OH&S, and undergo a single integrated certification audit from a CB. Integrated audits are more efficient, cost-effective, and demonstrate a holistic approach to management,  something sophisticated buyers and regulators appreciate. Ask your certification body about integrated audit programmes when seeking multiple certifications.

services.name: DOT OSP License
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for DOT OSP License — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Dot/osp License

## Overview

A DOT (Department of Telecommunications) OSP (Other Service Provider) License is a registration required by companies that provide certain telecom-based services in India using the public switched telephone network (PSTN), ISDN, or similar telecom resources,  without owning their own telecom infrastructure. OSP registration is regulated by the Department of Telecommunications, Ministry of Communications, Government of India. OSPs typically use telecom infrastructure (telephone connections, internet bandwidth) provided by licensed telecom operators (TSPs,  Telecom Service Providers) to deliver services to customers. Common OSP services include: Call centre services (domestic and international BPOs) IT-enabled services (ITES) that use voice/data telecom connections Tele-banking, tele-medicine, and tele-education services E-commerce helpdesk and customer support services Network Operation Centres (NOCs) IT/ITES companies with home-based agents (work from home) The DoT significantly liberalised the OSP framework in November 2020,  removing many restrictions on interconnection between domestic and international OSPs, relaxing infrastructure requirements, and expanding the scope for work-from-home agents. Understanding the revised OSP framework is critical for IT/BPO companies structuring their operations and telecom infrastructure.

## Features

Two categories: Domestic OSP (serves only Indian customers) and International OSP (serves overseas customers) Online registration: OSP registration is done online through the DoT's Saral Sanchar portal (dot.gov.in) No license fee: DoT does not charge any license fee for OSP registration,  it is a free registration Multi-location registration: A single OSP company can register multiple centres under the same OSP registration Work-from-home agents allowed: Post-2020 liberalisation, OSPs can deploy agents working from home without specific DoT approval,  with some compliance requirements EPABX/auto-dialler regulation: OSP registered companies must ensure their EPABX (Electronic Private Automatic Branch Exchange) systems are compliant with DoT specifications Interconnection flexibility: Post-2020, restrictions on interconnection between domestic OSPs have been significantly relaxed Annual compliance: OSPs must submit an annual self-declaration of compliance to DoT

## Benefits

Legal authorisation for BPO/call centre operations: OSP registration is a legal prerequisite for operating a call centre or BPO in India,  without it, the company risks DoT enforcement action, including disconnection of telecom services Credibility with telecom operators: TSPs (BSNL, Airtel, Jio, etc.) require OSP registration proof before providing bulk telephone lines, leased circuits, and internet bandwidth to BPO/call centre companies Client confidence: International clients (US, UK, Australian companies) outsourcing to Indian BPOs require proof of OSP registration as part of their vendor due diligence and compliance requirements Work-from-home flexibility: Post-2020, OSP companies can deploy home-based agents legally,  enabling hybrid work models, business continuity, and talent access beyond metro cities Access to international calling infrastructure: International OSPs can provide international calling services legally,  without OSP registration, using international VOIP in a commercial call centre setting is a violation Audit-ready compliance: Proper OSP registration and annual compliance submissions protect companies against DoT audits and enforcement actions

## Limitations

Penalties for non-compliance: Operating as an OSP without registration or violating OSP conditions (e.g., unauthorised interconnections) can result in disconnection of all telecom services and financial penalties Telecom infrastructure constraints: OSPs must use telecom services from licensed TSPs,  they cannot use unlicensed VOIP or grey market telephone connections Work-from-home compliance requirements: While WFH agents are permitted, OSPs must maintain logs of agent connectivity, VPN usage, and data security compliance,  adding operational overhead No resale of telecom capacity: OSPs cannot resell excess telecom capacity to other businesses,  the telecom connections must be used only for the OSP's own service delivery International OSP,  routing restrictions: International OSPs must route international calls through licensed ISPs and TSPs,  using unauthorised international gateways is a serious violation attracting criminal prosecution EPABX compliance: OSPs must use DoT-approved EPABX configurations,  non-compliant EPABX setups (common in older BPO infrastructure) must be upgraded

## Applicability

OSP registration is required by: Domestic and international call centres and BPOs IT/ITES companies with customer-facing voice operations Tele-medicine platforms using patient voice/video consultation at scale Online education platforms with tutoring call centre operations E-commerce and fintech companies with large customer support voice operations Insurance companies and banks operating tele-sales call centres (through third-party BPOs) Companies providing home-based call centre agent services Applicable pan-India,  OSP registration is with the DoT's Central office; the registration covers operations across all states. However, companies with centres in multiple states must register each centre under the same OSP license.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Certificate of Incorporation | Company's registration with RoC |
| PAN card of the company | Corporate PAN |
| GST registration certificate | GSTIN of the OSP entity |
| List of directors/partners | With DIN/PAN details |
| Address proof of OSP centre(s) | Lease agreement or ownership documents for each centre |
| Network diagram | EPABX configuration, network topology showing TSP connectivity |
| Declaration on OSP compliance | Self-declaration regarding non-usage of grey routes |
| Board resolution | Authorising OSP registration and naming authorised signatory |
| Details of TSP connections | Telecom service provider, type of connection, number of lines |
| Site plan of OSP centre | Floor plan showing agent workstations, EPABX location |

## Process

1. Determine OSP Category Identify whether registration is required as a Domestic OSP, International OSP, or both, based on the customer geography of the services being delivered.

2. Saral Sanchar Portal Registration Register on the DoT's Saral Sanchar portal (dot.gov.in/saral-sanchar) as an OSP applicant.

3. Application Submission Complete the OSP registration application with company details, centre address(es), nature of OSP services, network/EPABX details, and TSP information.

4. Document Upload Upload all required documents, Incorporation certificate, PAN, GST certificate, address proof of centre(s), network diagram, and board resolution.

5. Self-Declaration Submission Submit the self-declaration of compliance with DoT OSP conditions, confirming that grey routes will not be used and that EPABX infrastructure meets DoT specifications.

6. DoT Review and Grant of Registration DoT reviews the application. OSP registration is typically granted within 15–30 working days for complete applications.

7. Obtain Registration Certificate Download the OSP Registration Certificate from the Saral Sanchar portal. Share with TSPs as proof of OSP status for procuring bulk telecom connections.

8. Annual Compliance File the annual self-declaration of compliance on the Saral Sanchar portal by March 31 each year. Fees Service Government Fee OSP Registration (Domestic or International) Nil (Free) Annual compliance self-declaration Nil


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee OSP registration application support ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 Network diagram preparation ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 Annual compliance filing ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 OSP compliance audit ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 Multi-centre OSP registration ₹15,000 – ₹40,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

OSP registration under the revised 2020 framework does not have a fixed expiry period,  it is a perpetual registration subject to annual compliance filings and adherence to DoT conditions. Non-filing of annual self-declaration or violation of OSP conditions can lead to suspension or cancellation of the registration.

## Frequently asked questions

**Does a software company with a small internal help desk need OSP registration?** Not necessarily. OSP registration is required only when a company uses PSTN/ISDN-based telecom resources to provide services to external clients or customers. An internal IT help desk serving only the company's own employees using standard corporate telephone connections does not require OSP registration. However, if the company operates a customer-facing call centre (even if small) and uses bulk telephone lines or EPABX systems to handle customer calls as a commercial service, OSP registration is required.

**Can home-based call centre agents be deployed legally after the 2020 OSP liberalisation?** Yes. The DoT's November 2020 OSP liberalisation explicitly permits OSPs to deploy agents working from home (WFH/remote agents) without requiring specific DoT approval. WFH agents must connect to the OSP's systems via VPN, maintain data security as per the company's policy, and use only TSP-provided connections (not grey market VOIP). The OSP must maintain logs of WFH agent connectivity and ensure compliance with the OSP conditions. This change has been a significant enabler for India's BPO industry's hybrid work models.

**What is a "grey route" in the context of OSP compliance and why is it illegal?** A grey route refers to the use of unauthorized or unlicensed channels to route international telephone calls,  typically using illegal VOIP gateways that bypass licensed TSPs and evade call charges and regulatory oversight. Using grey routes for international calls is a serious violation under both the Indian Telegraph Act and DoT OSP conditions,  it deprives the government of revenues, undermines licensed TSPs, and exposes the OSP to criminal prosecution, disconnection of all telecom services, and heavy penalties. All international calls from OSP centres must be routed exclusively through DoT-licensed international gateways operated by TSPs.

**We have call centres in 5 cities. Do we need separate OSP registrations for each?** No. A single OSP registration covers all of the company's OSP centres across India. However, the registration application must list all centres (with addresses and infrastructure details for each) under the same company. If a new centre is added after the initial registration, the company must update its OSP registration to include the new centre before commencing OSP operations from it. Each centre's EPABX configuration and TSP connections must comply with DoT OSP conditions.

**Our BPO client (a US company) is asking for our OSP license. Is OSP registration the same as a license?** In common parlance (and in many international client contracts), "OSP License" and "OSP Registration" are used interchangeably to refer to the DoT OSP registration certificate. Technically, OSP is a "registration" under the current DoT framework (not a license that requires approval in the traditional sense). The Registration Certificate issued by DoT upon successful OSP registration is the document you provide to clients and TSPs as proof of your authorised OSP status. Most international BPO clients understand this and accept the DoT OSP Registration Certificate as the required "OSP license."

services.name: STARTUP INDIA TAX EXEMPTION
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for STARTUP INDIA TAX EXEMPTION — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Startup India Tax Exemption

## Overview

Startup India Tax Exemption refers primarily to the income tax exemption under Section 80-IAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961, available exclusively to eligible startups recognised by DPIIT under the Startup India initiative. This exemption allows eligible startups to claim a 100% deduction on profits and gains from business for any 3 consecutive assessment years out of the first 10 years from the year of incorporation. In addition to Section 80-IAC, the Startup India framework includes several other tax-related benefits: Tax Benefit Section / Provision Beneficiary 3-year income tax holiday Section 80-IAC DPIIT-recognised startups (with IMB approval) Angel tax exemption Section 56(2)(viib) exemption DPIIT-recognised startups on share issuance Capital gains exemption on residential property Section 54GB Individual investors investing in eligible startups Capital gains exemption on sale of LTCG assets Section 54EE Individuals investing in Startup India Fund of Funds Carry forward of losses Section 79 relaxation DPIIT-recognised startups,  losses can be carried forward even on shareholding change

The Section 80-IAC exemption is the most significant of these benefits,  for a startup with ₹5 crore annual profit, three tax-free years save approximately ₹3.9 crore in income tax (at 26% effective rate),  a massive saving that directly improves runway and reinvestment capacity.

## Features

100% profit deduction: Complete income tax exemption on profits for 3 chosen years,  zero tax liability on business income for those years Choice of 3 years: The startup can choose any 3 consecutive assessment years out of the first 10 years to avail the exemption,  allowing strategic timing to maximise benefit IMB approval required: The Income Tax Department does not automatically grant 80-IAC exemption to all DPIIT-recognised startups,  a separate application to the Inter-Ministerial Board (IMB) is required Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) applicability: Section 80-IAC exemption does not exempt startups from MAT,  MAT at 15% (plus surcharge and cess) may still apply; for startups incorporated after March 31, 2024, the amended provisions may affect MAT applicability Angel tax exemption (Section 56(2)(viib)): DPIIT-recognised startups are exempt from the "angel tax" on shares issued at a premium,  provided conditions are met (including aggregate paid-up share capital and premium not exceeding ₹25 crore as per recent amendments) Section 79 relaxation: Normally, a company cannot carry forward and set-off losses if 51% of shareholders change. DPIIT-recognised startups are exempt from this restriction,  allowing loss carry-forward even through multiple funding rounds that change shareholder composition

## Benefits

Direct tax saving: 3 years of 100% income tax exemption translates to direct cash conservation equal to 25–26% of profits in each exempt year,  money that can be reinvested into growth Improved unit economics for investors: Tax-exempt profits during the growth phase make startup investments more attractive,  supporting better valuation and easier fundraising Angel investment facilitation: Exemption from angel tax (Section 56(2)(viib)) removes a significant friction point that has historically deterred angel investors from investing in early-stage Indian startups Flexible year selection: Choosing the optimal 3 years for exemption (typically the years with the highest profitability) allows tax-efficient planning,  startups can use the loss years without exemption and the profit years with exemption Loss carry-forward protection: Section 79 relaxation ensures that early-stage losses (common in the first few years) are not forfeited due to shareholding changes in funding rounds,  protecting the tax asset for future set-off Capital gains benefits for investors: Section 54GB and 54EE exemptions make startup investment more attractive for individual investors by providing capital gains tax relief,  indirectly improving the startup's access to capital

## Limitations

IMB approval is rigorous: Not all DPIIT-recognised startups receive 80-IAC approval,  the IMB evaluates the innovation, technology, and scalability of the business model carefully; generically described businesses face rejection MAT is not exempt: Even with 80-IAC exemption, startups may be subject to Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) at 15% of book profits,  reducing (but not eliminating) the tax benefit for some startups Revenue-stage dependency: The 80-IAC exemption is only valuable for startups that are actually generating profits,  pre-revenue or deep-loss-making startups gain no immediate benefit Angel tax exemption conditions: The exemption from Section 56(2)(viib) is subject to conditions including investment value limits,  DPIIT-recognised startups receiving investment from non-registered investors above prescribed limits may still face angel tax complications 10-year and ₹100 crore window: Both the DPIIT Recognition and the 80-IAC benefit are time-limited,  startups that don't reach profitability within the 10-year window lose the exemption opportunity Retroactive risk: If a startup is later found to have obtained DPIIT Recognition on the basis of incorrect information, the income tax benefits may be reversed with interest and penalties

## Applicability

The Section 80-IAC exemption applies to eligible startups meeting ALL of the following: Criterion Requirement DPIIT Recognition Must have valid DPIIT Recognition Certificate Entity type Private Limited Company or LLP (partnership firms are NOT eligible for 80-IAC) Incorporation date On or after April 1, 2016 (for IMB applications under current provisions) Annual turnover Below ₹100 crore in any year Nature Engaged in innovation, development, deployment of new products/processes/services IMB approval Must obtain specific IMB (Inter-Ministerial Board) approval for 80-IAC

Applicable pan-India,  all states and UTs. Eligible startups anywhere in India can apply for IMB approval.

## Documents Required

For Section 80-IAC IMB Application:

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| DPIIT Recognition Certificate | Mandatory,  must be valid |
| Certificate of Incorporation | Company/LLP incorporation certificate |
| PAN of the entity | Business PAN |
| Memorandum of Association / LLP Agreement | Constitutional documents |
| Audited financial statements | For all years of operation since incorporation |
| Business plan / pitch deck | Detailed description of business, technology, innovation |
| Detailed description of innovation | Technology used, IP owned/licensed, R&D activities |
| Product/service documentation | Technical specifications, screenshots, patent details |
| Revenue and growth metrics | User base, revenue growth, market size |
| Details of key team members | Founders and key management team profiles |
| Shareholding pattern | Current cap table |
| Any third-party validation | Awards, accelerator acceptance, media coverage, customer testimonials |

## Process

1. Ensure DPIIT Recognition Obtain DPIIT Recognition Certificate from the Startup India portal (startupindia.gov.in), mandatory prerequisite.

2. IMB Application on Startup India Portal Log in to the Startup India portal. Navigate to "Tax Exemption, 80IAC" under the Benefits section. Fill and submit the detailed IMB application form.

3. Detailed Business Documentation Prepare a comprehensive document package, audited financials, business plan, innovation description, product documentation, and team profiles. This is the most document-intensive step.

4. IMB Review The Inter-Ministerial Board, constituted by DPIIT with members from DPIIT, CBDT, and other relevant ministries, reviews the application. The IMB may seek clarifications or additional information.

5. IMB Approval If satisfied, the IMB approves the application and issues an acknowledgement. DPIIT notifies the applicant of the IMB's decision. Approval timelines can range from 2–6 months.

6. Filing Income Tax Return with 80-IAC Deduction After IMB approval, the startup claims the 80-IAC deduction in its Income Tax Return (ITR) for the chosen assessment years. The IMB approval reference number is cited in the ITR.

7. Angel Tax Exemption (if applicable) For angel tax exemption under Section 56(2)(viib), DPIIT Recognition alone is sufficient (subject to conditions), no separate IMB approval needed for angel tax exemption. Fees Service Government Fee 80-IAC IMB Application Nil (Free) DPIIT Recognition Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee DPIIT Recognition application ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 80-IAC IMB application preparation ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 Innovation documentation and business plan ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 Tax planning for 80-IAC year selection ₹8,000 – ₹20,000 Angel tax exemption compliance advisory ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 End-to-end Startup India tax benefits package ₹25,000 – ₹60,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

The 80-IAC exemption applies for any 3 consecutive assessment years out of the first 10 years from the year of incorporation. The DPIIT Recognition must be valid at the time of claiming the exemption. The IMB approval, once granted, remains effective for the 3 chosen years,  it does not need to be renewed annually. The exemption years must be chosen strategically,  once a year is chosen as a "non-exempt" year, it cannot be changed retroactively.

## Frequently asked questions

**Our startup is DPIIT-recognised but we haven't applied for 80-IAC exemption and we're in our 5th year. Is it too late?** No, it is not too late,  but act quickly. The 80-IAC exemption is available for any 3 consecutive years within the first 10 years. You still have 5 years of the eligibility window remaining. Apply for IMB approval immediately. The exemption can be claimed for future profitable years,  even if you've already filed ITRs for past years without the exemption, you cannot revise closed assessments retroactively to add 80-IAC (subject to specific circumstances). Planning the remaining exempt years carefully with a CA is strongly advisable.

**Our startup has never made a profit. Can we "bank" the 80-IAC exemption for future profitable years?** Yes,  and this is precisely the intent of the flexible year selection mechanism. If your startup is loss-making in the early years, you don't need to use any of your exempt years during those years. Once the startup reaches profitability (within the 10-year window), you select your 3 consecutive exempt years starting from that point. You choose WHEN to start the 3-year clock,  the key is that (a) your DPIIT Recognition must be active, and (b) the IMB approval must be obtained before the years you wish to claim.

**What is "angel tax" and how does DPIIT Recognition help avoid it?** Section 56(2)(viib) of the Income Tax Act (popularly called "angel tax") taxes the amount received by a company from an investor above the Fair Market Value (FMV) of shares as "income from other sources",  effectively taxing the startup on the premium it receives over FMV. DPIIT-recognised startups are exempt from Section 56(2)(viib) subject to conditions,  primarily that the aggregate paid-up share capital and securities premium after the proposed issue does not exceed ₹25 crore (excluding certain investor categories). This exemption is critical because most early-stage startups issue shares at significant premiums over FMV (based on future growth expectations), and angel tax would otherwise impose a heavy and disproportionate tax burden on capital raising.

**Can a startup claim 80-IAC exemption for a year in which it also claimed carry-forward of losses?** The 80-IAC exemption covers profits,  in a loss year, there are no profits to exempt. Section 80-IAC and loss carry-forward work at different stages: loss years generate tax losses that are carried forward to be set off against future profits; when those future profits arise, the startup can choose to claim 80-IAC exemption (effectively exempting those profits) rather than set off the brought-forward losses. Setting off losses first and then applying 80-IAC optimisation requires careful multi-year tax planning with a CA,  the sequencing significantly affects total tax paid over the startup's life.

**Our startup was incorporated in 2018. Can we still apply for IMB approval in 2026?** Yes. As of 2026, a startup incorporated in 2018 is in its 8th year,  within the 10-year eligibility window. You can apply for IMB approval now. If approved, you can claim 80-IAC exemption for any 3 consecutive assessment years within the remaining window (i.e., from AY 2026-27 onwards, you have approximately 2–3 assessment years of eligibility remaining depending on the exact incorporation date). Delaying further would narrow the window for claiming the benefit,  apply for IMB approval immediately to preserve your ability to use the exemption.

All content is for informational purposes. Specific government registration, tax, and compliance advice should be obtained from qualified professionals based on your entity's specific circumstances, state of incorporation, and business activities.

services.name: Professional Tax Return Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Professional Tax Return Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Professional Tax Return Filing is a statutory compliance requirement imposed by state governments in India on individuals earning income through employment, profession, or trade. It is governed by respective state-specific legislations such as the Karnataka Tax on Professions, Trades, Callings and Employments Act, 1976 and similar laws enacted by other states. Professional Tax is a direct tax levied by state authorities, unlike income tax which is governed by the central government. Employers are responsible for deducting Professional Tax (PT) from employees' salaries and remitting it to the state government. Additionally, businesses, freelancers, and professionals must obtain Professional Tax Registration (PTR) or Enrollment (PTE) and file periodic returns. Professional Tax Return Filing involves:

- Reporting tax deducted or payable
- Submitting employee-wise details (for employers)
- Ensuring timely payment and compliance

The tax amount is generally capped at ₹2,500 per year per individual, as per constitutional limits. Filing frequency (monthly, quarterly, or annually) depends on the state and the number of employees. This compliance is essential for:

- Employers with salaried staff
- Self-employed professionals (CA, lawyer, doctor, etc.)
- Businesses and firms operating in applicable states

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Compliance

Ensures adherence to state tax laws and avoids penalties.

### Smooth Business Operations

Maintains proper statutory compliance for employers and businesses.

### Employee Trust and Transparency

Ensures correct deduction and reporting of taxes.

### Eligibility for Government Registrations

Required for obtaining certain licenses and registrations.

### Avoids Legal Penalties

Prevents fines, interest, and prosecution by authorities.

### Supports Financial Documentation

Helps in audits, loan processing, and statutory filings.

### Simplified State-Level Taxation

Structured and predictable tax system with capped liability.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### State-Specific Complexity

Different rules, rates, and procedures across states.

### Compliance Burden

Requires periodic filing and record maintenance.

### Penalties for Non-Compliance

Late filing or payment attracts fines and interest.

### Limited Applicability

Not applicable in all states (some states do not levy PT).

### Administrative Effort

Requires tracking employee salary slabs and deductions.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State-wise Coverage)

Professional Tax is state-specific, and not all states impose it.

### States Where Professional Tax is Applicable

- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha
- Central/Other Regions: Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Assam

### States/UTs Where It is Not Levied

- Delhi
- Uttar Pradesh
- Haryana
- Rajasthan
- Punjab
- Himachal Pradesh
- Union Territories (most)

### Issuing Authorities

- State Commercial Tax Departments
- Local Municipal Authorities (in some states)

### Example Authority

- In Karnataka: Professional Tax is administered under the Karnataka Tax on

### Professions, Trades, Callings and Employments Act, 1976

Who is Liable?

- Employers (for employee deductions)
- Self-employed individuals
- Companies, LLPs, partnerships
- Freelancers and consultants

## 5) Key Features of Professional Tax Return Filing

### State-Level Tax

Levied and administered by individual state governments.

### Dual Responsibility

- Employer deducts and files (PTR)
- Individual pays (PTE)

### Capped Tax Liability

Maximum ₹2,500 per year per person.

### Periodic Filing Requirement

Monthly, quarterly, or annual returns based on state rules.

### Online Filing Systems

Most states offer digital filing portals.

### Penalty for Non-Compliance

Late payment or filing attracts penalties.

### Employee-Based Slabs

Tax calculated based on salary ranges.

## 6) Documents Required

### Basic Documents

- PAN card of business/individual
- Address proof

### Registration Documents

- Professional Tax Registration Certificate (PTR)
- Professional Tax Enrollment Certificate (PTE)

### Employee-Related Documents (for employers)

- Employee salary details
- Employee PAN and details
- Salary registers and payroll records

### Filing Documents

- Challan/payment proof
- Previous returns (if applicable)

### Additional Documents

- Bank details
- Authorization letter (if filed through consultant)

Documentation may vary depending on state requirements.

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step-by-Step Professional Tax Return Filing Process

1. Obtain Registration/Enrollment,  Apply for PTR (employer) or PTE (self-employed).
2. Determine Applicable Tax Slab,  Calculate tax based on employee salary or professional category.
3. Deduct Professional Tax,  Employer deducts tax from employee salaries.
4. Payment of Tax,  Deposit tax with state government through online portal.
5. Preparation of Return,  Compile employee details and tax payment information.
6. Filing of Return,  Submit return online/offline as per state rules.
7. Acknowledgment Generation,  Receive confirmation of filing.
8. Maintain Records,  Keep records for audit and compliance purposes.
9. Periodic Compliance,  Repeat filing as per monthly/quarterly/annual cycle.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

Professional Tax itself is the primary cost; filing fees are generally not applicable.

### Tax Amount

- Based on state-specific slabs
- Maximum ₹2,500 per year per individual

### Registration Fees

- Nominal fees (varies by state)

### Penalty and Interest

- Late payment: Interest (varies by state, e.g., 1–2% per month)
- Late filing: Fixed penalties

### Additional Costs

- Professional/consultant fees
- Compliance management costs

### Important Notes

- Tax rates and slabs differ across states
- Timely filing avoids penalties
- No uniform national fee structure

## Conclusion

Professional Tax Return Filing is a crucial compliance requirement for businesses, employers, and professionals operating in states where the tax is applicable. It ensures adherence to state tax laws while maintaining transparency in employee taxation and business operations.

Although the tax amount is relatively small, non-compliance can lead to penalties and legal complications. With increasing digitization of state tax systems, filing has become more streamlined, but careful tracking of deadlines, employee data, and state-specific rules remains essential. For businesses aiming for seamless operations and regulatory compliance, timely and accurate Professional Tax Return Filing is a fundamental aspect of financial and statutory management.

services.name: Professional Tax Payment
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Professional Tax Payment — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Professional Tax Payment refers to the actual remittance of tax liability imposed by state governments on individuals earning income through employment, profession, or trade. Unlike return filing (which is declaratory), payment is the financial compliance component governed by state-specific laws such as the Karnataka Tax on Professions, Trades, Callings and Employments Act, 1976. Professional Tax (PT) is deducted by employers from employee salaries or paid directly by self-employed individuals. The responsibility lies in:

- Deduction (for employers)
- Calculation (based on slab rates)
- Timely deposit with the state government

Each state provides an online treasury or commercial tax portal for seamless payment through net banking, challan generation, or authorized banks. The tax is capped at ₹2,500 annually per individual, but payment frequency varies:

- Monthly (for employers with larger workforce)
- Quarterly (in some states)
- Annually (for self-employed or enrolled individuals)

Professional Tax Payment is a critical statutory obligation, and failure to comply may result in interest, penalties, or recovery proceedings by state authorities.

## 2) Why Professional Tax Payment Matters (Benefits & Practical Value)

### Regulatory Compliance Backbone

Ensures adherence to state tax laws and avoids legal exposure.

### Operational Continuity

Smooth functioning of business without compliance interruptions.

### Employee Salary Integrity

Accurate deduction and deposit builds trust and avoids disputes.

### Audit & Financial Readiness

Essential for statutory audits, due diligence, and funding processes.

### Government Recognition

Supports eligibility for licenses, tenders, and registrations.

### Penalty Avoidance

Timely payment prevents interest accumulation and fines.

## 3) Challenges / Limitations

### State-Wise Variations

Different rules, portals, and payment cycles across states.

### Recurring Compliance Burden

Requires regular tracking of due dates and liabilities.

### Interest on Delays

Late payments attract monthly interest penalties.

### Administrative Dependency

Payroll accuracy directly impacts correct tax payment.

### Not Universally Applicable

Some states do not levy Professional Tax, creating inconsistency.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State Coverage)

Professional Tax Payment is not a uniform national levy; it depends on state laws.

### States Where Payment is Mandatory

- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha
- Central/Other States: Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Assam

### States/UTs Without Professional Tax

- Delhi
- Uttar Pradesh
- Haryana
- Rajasthan
- Punjab
- Himachal Pradesh
- Most Union Territories

### Administering Authorities

- State Commercial Tax Departments
- State Finance Departments
- Municipal bodies (in select states)

Illustration: In Karnataka, payments are governed under the Karnataka Tax on Professions, Trades, Callings and Employments Act, 1976.

## 5) Core Characteristics of Professional Tax Payment

### Direct Tax at State Level

Collected and retained by respective state governments.

### Dual Mode of Payment

- Employer (on behalf of employees – PTR)
- Individual (self-payment – PTE)

### Slab-Based Calculation

Tax varies depending on income brackets.

### Digitally Enabled System

Most states provide online payment gateways.

### Strict Due Date Compliance

Fixed timelines for each payment cycle.

### Linked with Return Filing

Payment must align with filed returns.

## 6) Information & Records Required

Instead of heavy documentation, Professional Tax Payment relies on accurate financial data inputs:

### For Employers

- Employee salary register
- Monthly PT deduction sheet
- PTR number

### For Individuals (PTE)

- Enrollment certificate
- Applicable slab category

### For Payment Processing

- Challan details
- Bank/payment credentials

- Previous payment references (if any)

### Supportive Records

- Payroll reports
- Tax computation sheets

## 7) Payment Workflow (Practical Process Flow)

1. Identify Liability,  Determine applicable PT based on salary slabs or professional category.
2. Deduct or Compute Tax

- Employers deduct from salaries
- Individuals calculate own liability

1. Access State Portal,  Login to the respective state PT portal.
2. Generate Challan,  Enter details and generate payment challan.
3. Execute Payment,  Pay via:

- Net banking
- Debit/credit card
- Authorized bank branches

1. Obtain Acknowledgment,  Download receipt or challan confirmation.
2. Record & Reconcile,  Match payment with payroll and return filing data.
3. Maintain Compliance Cycle,  Repeat as per monthly/quarterly/annual schedule.

## 8) Government Cost / Amount Payable

### Tax Liability

- Based on income slabs defined by state
- Maximum limit: ₹2,500 per year per individual

### Payment Frequency Impact

- Monthly payments: Smaller recurring amounts
- Annual payments: Lump sum (for enrolled individuals)

### Additional Financial Implications

- Interest on Delay: Typically 1–2% per month
- Late Payment Penalty: Fixed or percentage-based (state-specific)

### Other Costs

- Professional/compliance service fees
- Administrative overheads

### Important Note

There is no separate "fee" for payment, only the tax amount and penalties (if applicable).

## Conclusion

Professional Tax Payment is the execution layer of compliance, translating calculated liability into actual statutory fulfillment. While the tax itself is minimal, its importance lies in maintaining uninterrupted compliance, financial discipline, and regulatory credibility. For businesses, it directly ties into payroll accuracy and statutory reporting. For professionals, it ensures lawful operation within state jurisdictions. With increasing digitization, the process has become more streamlined, but consistency in tracking deadlines and maintaining records remains critical. In practical terms, timely and accurate Professional Tax Payment is not just a routine obligation, it is a reflection of a business's compliance maturity and operational efficiency.
