services.name: Expedite Trademark Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Expedite Trademark Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Expedited Trademark Registration is a fast-track mechanism that enables applicants to accelerate the examination and processing of their trademark applications under the Trade Marks Act, 1999 and the Trade Marks Rules, 2017. It is administered by the Registrar of Trademarks under the supervision of the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks. Under the standard process, trademark applications may take several months to be examined due to backlog and administrative timelines. The expedited route allows applicants to request priority examination, significantly reducing waiting periods and enabling faster movement of the application toward publication and registration. This mechanism is particularly useful for:

- Startups launching new brands
- Businesses entering competitive markets
- Companies preparing for funding, franchising, or expansion
- E-commerce sellers requiring quick brand protection

Expedited processing does not bypass legal scrutiny. The application is still examined under the same statutory provisions (Sections 9 and 11), but it is prioritized in the queue, leading to faster issuance of the Examination Report and subsequent steps.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Faster Examination Process

Reduces waiting time for examination from months to a few weeks.

### Quick Brand Protection

Enables early legal positioning of the trademark in the market.

### Business Readiness

Supports faster product launches, marketing, and brand rollouts.

### Competitive Advantage

Helps secure trademark rights before competitors.

### Priority Handling by Registry

Application is processed on a priority basis.

### Improved Investor Confidence

Fast-tracked IP protection enhances credibility for funding.

### Early Publication Opportunity

Accelerates entry into Trademark Journal stage.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Higher Government Fees

Expedited processing involves significantly higher fees.

### No Guarantee of Approval

Application may still face objections or opposition.

### Limited Scope of Acceleration

Only speeds up examination, not entire registration lifecycle.

### Legal Complexity Remains

Applicant must still comply with all legal requirements.

### Possible Additional Costs

Professional fees may increase due to urgency.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State-wise Coverage)

Expedited Trademark Registration is governed by a central law, making it applicable uniformly across India.

### Applicable in All Regions

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands,

### Lakshadweep, Ladakh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu

### Issuing Authority

- Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks
- Processed by the Registrar of Trademarks through Trademark Registry Offices

Who Can Apply?

- Individuals
- Startups and MSMEs
- Companies and LLPs

- Foreign applicants filing in India

Expedited processing is available to all eligible applicants willing to pay the prescribed fees.

## 5) Key Features of Expedited Trademark Registration

### Priority Examination Request

Application is examined on a fast-track basis.

### Same Legal Standards

Subject to Sections 9 and 11 of the Trade Marks Act.

### Reduced Processing Time

Examination report issued significantly faster.

### Optional Facility

Applicant can choose between normal and expedited routes.

### Online Filing System

Entire process conducted through digital platform.

### Accelerated Publication

Faster movement to Trademark Journal stage.

### Applicable at Filing or Later Stage

Request can be made at the time of filing or after filing.

## 6) Documents Required

### Basic Documents

- Identity proof of applicant
- Address proof

### Trademark Documents

- Trademark representation (logo/word mark)
- Class of goods/services

### Application Documents

- Trademark application (Form TM-A)
- Expedited request (Form TM-M)

### Supporting Documents

- Proof of business (if applicable)
- MSME/Startup certificate (if claiming lower fees)

### Legal Documents

- Power of Attorney (if filed through agent)

Documentation requirements are similar to standard trademark filing with an additional expedited request.

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step-by-Step Expedited Trademark Registration Process

1. Trademark Search,  Conduct availability search to avoid conflicts.
2. Filing of Trademark Application,  Submit Form TM-A with required details.
3. Request for Expedited Processing,  File Form TM-M with prescribed fee.
4. Priority Examination by Registrar,  Application is examined on a fast-track basis.
5. Issuance of Examination Report,  Objections (if any) are communicated quickly.
6. Reply to Objection (if applicable),  Applicant submits response within timeline.
7. Hearing (if required),  Conducted if objections persist.
8. Acceptance and Publication,  Trademark is published in Journal for opposition.
9. Opposition Period,  Third parties may oppose within statutory period.
10. Registration Certificate Issued,  If no opposition or successful defense, trademark is registered.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

Expedited Trademark Registration involves higher government fees compared to standard filing.

### Government Fees (Approximate)

- Expedited Filing (Form TM-A): ₹4,500 (individual/startup/MSME)

₹9,000 (others)

- Expedited Request (Form TM-M): ₹20,000 (individual/startup/MSME)

₹40,000 (others)

### Additional Costs

- Professional/legal fees
- Trademark search and advisory
- Reply to objection or hearing fees

### Other Expenses

- Renewal fees (every 10 years)
- Opposition handling costs (if any)

### Important Notes

- Fees are subject to government revisions
- Expedited fee is non-refundable
- Total cost depends on complexity and services required

## Conclusion

Expedited Trademark Registration is a strategic solution for businesses that require rapid brand protection in a competitive environment. By prioritizing examination and reducing waiting time, it enables quicker progression through the trademark lifecycle without compromising legal scrutiny. For startups, growing businesses, and time-sensitive ventures, opting for expedited processing can provide a critical edge in securing brand identity, attracting investors, and entering the market with confidence. Proper planning, accurate filing, and timely compliance remain essential to fully leverage the benefits of this fast-track mechanism.

services.name: PSARA Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for PSARA Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# PSARA Registration

## Short overview

PSARA (Private Security Agencies Regulation Act, 2005) Registration is a mandatory license for any person or entity operating a private security agency in India, providing security guards, bouncers, security personnel, and related services. The Act regulates the security industry to ensure professional standards, character verification of guards, and accountability in private security operations.

PSARA license is granted by the State Controlling Authority (typically the Home Department or Police Commissionerate) of each state where the agency operates.

## Key features

- State-level license under Central PSARA Act
- Mandatory for all private security agencies
- 5-year validity (typically)
- Renewable

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation to operate security agency
- Eligibility for institutional security contracts
- Government and PSU tendering eligibility
- Professional credibility
- Insurance coverage facilitation

## Limitations

- State-specific licenses (multi-state operation needs multiple licenses)
- Heavy compliance, guard training, verification, uniforms
- Background verification requirements
- Renewal compliance burden
- Initial capital and infrastructure requirements

## Applicability

PSARA is mandatory in all states for any entity providing security services. Each state has its own PSARA Rules with state-specific procedures.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Form I (Application) | PSARA prescribed form |
| Affidavit | As per PSARA Rules |
| Memorandum of Association | For company applicants |
| PAN, GST | Entity tax registrations |
| Office premises proof | Lease or ownership |
| Director or proprietor verification | Police verification |
| Training arrangement proof | |
| MoU with training institute | |
| Bank guarantee | ₹25,000 to ₹1,00,000 |
| ITR (3 years) | Of entity or proprietor |
| Insurance | Liability insurance |

## Process

1. Apply to State Controlling Authority with documents.
2. Police verification of directors or proprietors.
3. Office inspection.
4. Training arrangement verification.
5. Bank guarantee submission.
6. License issuance.

Timeline: 60 to 180 days.

## Government fees

| Operating area | Approximate fee |
| --- | --- |
| Single district | ₹5,000 |
| 2 to 5 districts | ₹10,000 |
| State-wide | ₹25,000 |
| Renewal | Same as initial |

## Validity and renewal

Typically 5 years validity. Renewal application 60 days before expiry.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can I operate security in multiple states under one PSARA license?** No. Each state requires separate PSARA registration. Multi-state operations need multiple state licenses.

**2. Is training mandatory for security guards under PSARA?** Yes. All guards must undergo training (typically 100 hours) at recognised training institutes before deployment.

**3. Can ex-servicemen operate security agencies easily under PSARA?** PSARA gives some preference to ex-servicemen with reduced training requirements and facilitated registration.

**4. What is the bank guarantee for under PSARA?** The bank guarantee secures compliance and is invoked in case of violations. It's returned upon license closure with no pending liabilities.

**5. Are bouncers covered under PSARA?** Yes. Any private security personnel, guards, bouncers, security supervisors, fall under PSARA regulation.

All service content is for informational purposes. State PSARA rules and fees vary. Consult the state controlling authority and qualified advisors.

services.name: Fire License (Fire NOC)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Fire License (Fire NOC) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Fire License

## Overview

A Fire License (also referred to as a Fire NOC,  No Objection Certificate) is a mandatory clearance issued by the Fire and Emergency Services department of the state or local authority, certifying that a building, establishment, or business premises complies with fire safety standards and has adequate fire prevention, protection, and evacuation infrastructure in place. Fire NOCs are issued under state-specific Fire Prevention and Life Safety Acts and Building Bylaws, as well as the National Building Code of India (NBC) 2016 published by the Bureau of Indian Standards. While fire safety is a state subject, the National Building Code provides the overarching standards that states typically adopt. Fire Licenses / NOCs are legally required for: Industrial establishments (factories, warehouses, processing units) Commercial buildings (office complexes, malls, multiplexes) Hospitality establishments (hotels, lodges, resorts) Educational institutions (schools, colleges) Healthcare facilities (hospitals, nursing homes, clinics) Assembly occupancies (cinema halls, auditoriums, sports complexes) High-rise buildings (above a defined height threshold, typically 15 metres) Hazardous occupancies (petrol bunks, LPG storage, chemical storage) Without a valid Fire NOC, other licenses and approvals,  including trade licenses, FSSAI licenses, CMHO health NOCs, and building occupancy certificates,  may not be granted.

## Features

State-administered: Fire NOC is issued by the state Fire Services Department; each state has its own Fire Prevention Act and NOC procedures Building-type specific: Standards and requirements differ based on occupancy type (industrial, commercial, residential, assembly) and floor area Mandatory fire safety infrastructure: NOC is granted only after verification of fire extinguishers, sprinkler systems, fire alarm systems, emergency exits, hose reels, hydrants, signage, and evacuation plans Inspection-based: All Fire NOC applications require a physical inspection of the premises by Fire Department officers Annual renewal in most states: Fire NOC must be renewed annually in most states, with fresh inspection Coordinate with architect: The NBC-compliant building design must incorporate fire safety infrastructure from the design stage,  retrofitting is costly Integrated with building plan approval: In new constructions, fire safety clearance is part of the building plan approval process by local authorities

## Benefits

Legal compliance: Fire NOC is mandatory for trade license, factory license, FSSAI license, drug license, hotel classification, cinema hall license, and occupancy certificate,  without it, other licenses cannot be obtained or maintained Insurance prerequisite: Commercial property and business interruption insurance policies require a valid Fire NOC for full coverage; absence of NOC can result in claim rejection after a fire incident Employee safety and liability: Proper fire safety compliance protects employees, customers, and visitors,  and protects the business from criminal negligence liability in case of a fire incident Business continuity: Properties with valid Fire NOCs have compliant fire safety infrastructure,  reducing risk of fire incidents and ensuring faster fire response if incidents occur Regulatory inspections: Businesses with valid Fire NOCs are less likely to face closure notices from fire safety officers during surprise inspections Lender requirement: Banks and NBFCs require a valid Fire NOC for commercial property mortgages and business loans against property Tenant credibility: Commercial tenants (offices, shops) with valid Fire NOCs are more attractive to institutional landlords and corporate park operators

## Limitations

State-specific variability: Fire NOC procedures, fees, infrastructure standards, and renewal requirements vary significantly across states,  compliance in Karnataka may differ substantially from Maharashtra or UP Infrastructure cost: Achieving fire safety compliance (installing sprinklers, fire alarms, hydrants, etc.) can involve significant capital investment for buildings not designed to NBC standards Annual renewal burden: In most states, Fire NOC must be renewed annually with fresh inspection,  adding recurring compliance overhead Inspection delays: Fire Department officer availability for inspection can cause delays, especially in busy commercial districts Retrospective compliance challenges: Older buildings constructed before modern fire safety codes were enforced often struggle to retrofit required systems, leading to expensive upgrades or ongoing NOC issues Penalty for lapse: Operating without a valid Fire NOC (after expiry or before initial grant) can result in closure notice from the Fire Department or local authority

## Applicability

Occupancy Type When Fire NOC is Required Industrial (factory/warehouse) Area > 500 sq meters OR storing hazardous materials Commercial (offices/malls/shops) Area > 500 sq meters or height > 15 meters Hotels and restaurants All above a specified size (varies by state) Hospitals and nursing homes All above a specified bed count or area Schools and colleges All above a specified capacity or area Cinema halls and multiplexes All High-rise residential Above 15 meters height Petrol pumps and LPG dealerships All Event venues and auditoriums All above a specified capacity

Applicable across all states and UTs,  Karnataka (K-Fire), Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, Gujarat, Telangana, West Bengal, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, and all others. State Fire Services Departments are the issuing authorities.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Application form | State Fire Department application (online/offline) |
| Building plan (approved) | Approved building plan from local authority |
| Occupancy certificate | Or proof of building completion |
| Site plan | Showing premises location, access roads, water source |
| Fire safety system layout | Sprinkler layout, fire alarm layout, exit plan |
| Fire extinguisher details | Type, number, placement, last service date |
| Mock drill certificate | Evidence of fire drill conducted on premises |
| Electrical safety certificate | From licensed electrical inspector |
| List of hazardous materials (if any) | For industrial/storage facilities |
| Previous Fire NOC (for renewal) | Copy of existing/previous NOC |
| Certificate of Incorporation / GST / Trade License | Identity and legal existence of the business |
| AMC for fire safety equipment | Annual maintenance contract for fire safety systems |

## Process

1. Fire Safety Audit Conduct an internal fire safety audit (or engage a fire safety consultant) to assess current compliance levels and identify gaps relative to NBC and state fire safety standards.

2. Infrastructure Compliance Install or upgrade fire safety infrastructure, extinguishers, sprinkler systems, fire alarm panels, emergency lighting, exit signage, evacuation maps, and water hydrant systems, to meet applicable standards.

3. Application Submission Submit the Fire NOC application to the state Fire Department, online (in states with online portals) or physically at the Fire Station/Fire Department office. Attach all required documents.

4. Fee Payment Pay the prescribed Fire NOC application fee, varies by state, building type, and area.

5. Fire Inspector Visit Fire Department officers inspect the premises to verify that fire safety infrastructure meets standards. Address any observations (deficiencies) cited during the inspection.

6. Compliance Rectification Rectify any deficiencies noted by the Fire Inspector and submit a compliance report with photographs and certificates.

7. Grant of Fire NOC Upon satisfactory inspection, the Fire NOC is issued by the Fire Department. Display the NOC prominently at the premises.

8. Annual Renewal Renew the Fire NOC before expiry each year by filing a renewal application, paying the renewal fee, and undergoing fresh inspection. Fees State / Building Type Government NOC Fee (Approx.) Karnataka (small commercial) ₹1,000 – ₹5,000 Maharashtra (commercial/industrial) ₹2,000 – ₹25,000 Delhi (commercial) ₹1,000 – ₹10,000 Tamil Nadu (factory/warehouse) ₹2,000 – ₹15,000 Gujarat (commercial) ₹1,000 – ₹10,000


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee Fire NOC application support ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 Fire safety audit and gap assessment ₹10,000 – ₹30,000 Fire safety infrastructure planning ₹15,000 – ₹50,000 Annual renewal support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000

Government fees vary significantly by state and premises size. Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

State Typical Fire NOC Validity Most states 1 year (annual renewal) Some states (new buildings) 3–5 years for initial NOC, then annual

## Frequently asked questions

**I run a small café with 30 seats and a kitchen. Do I need a Fire NOC?** In most states, restaurants, cafes, and eateries with cooking facilities are required to obtain a Fire NOC,  especially where the kitchen involves open flames, LPG, or commercial cooking equipment. The threshold area and seating capacity varies by state. In Karnataka and Maharashtra, even small eateries with cooking facilities typically require a fire NOC or at minimum a fire safety compliance certificate from the local Fire Department. Additionally, FSSAI State License requires a valid Fire NOC or fire safety compliance document in many states.

**Our office is in a commercial complex and the building already has a Fire NOC. Do we also need a separate Fire NOC for our office unit?** It depends on the state and the specific arrangement. If the commercial complex has a building-level Fire NOC, individual tenants in the building may not need separate unit-level NOCs for standard office use. However, if your office has a specific high-risk activity (server room, laboratory, storage of flammable materials), a unit-specific fire safety assessment may be required. For trade license applications in many states, a copy of the building's Fire NOC (obtained from the building owner/landlord) is sufficient for the tenant's trade license application.

**What fire safety infrastructure is typically required for a factory to obtain a Fire NOC?** For an industrial facility, typical requirements include: adequate number of portable fire extinguishers (type based on fire hazard class,  ABC, CO2, foam), fire alarm system with detectors and manual call points, automatic sprinkler system (for certain occupancies and areas above threshold), emergency exit doors with panic bar hardware, emergency lighting and exit signage, fire hydrant system with hose reels (for larger factories), sand buckets, fire water storage tank, access roads for fire tenders, and clearly displayed evacuation plans. The exact requirements depend on the state fire code, the type of occupancy, floor area, and the nature of materials stored/used.

**Our Fire NOC expired last month. Can we continue operating until the renewal is processed?** Technically, operating with an expired Fire NOC puts you in violation of fire safety regulations and potentially affects the validity of other licenses (trade license, FSSAI) that relied on the Fire NOC. File the renewal application immediately. In practice, Fire Departments recognize that renewal processing takes time and typically do not initiate action against businesses with pending renewal applications filed before or within a short period of expiry. However, an expired Fire NOC is a significant liability,  insurance claims, inspections by other departments, and any fire incident would be seriously complicated by an expired NOC.

**Can I self-certify fire safety compliance without getting a Fire NOC?** Some states have introduced self-certification mechanisms for lower-risk occupancies (small offices, shops below a threshold area) where businesses can self-certify compliance with fire safety standards. Karnataka, for example, has introduced self-certification under SAKALA for certain categories. However, self-certification is not a substitute for the formal Fire NOC for higher-risk occupancies (hospitals, schools, cinema halls, factories, hotels). Even where self-certification is available, the business is fully liable for compliance with fire safety standards,  and false self-certification attracts serious penalties.

services.name: DPIIT Registration (Startup Recognition)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for DPIIT Registration (Startup Recognition) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Dpiit Registration

## Overview

DPIIT Registration,  also known as Startup Recognition by DPIIT,  is the government recognition accorded to eligible startups under the Startup India initiative of the Government of India, administered by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry. When a startup is recognised by DPIIT, it is officially acknowledged by the Government of India as a "Startup" under the Startup India framework. This recognition unlocks a range of regulatory benefits, tax incentives, funding access, and compliance relaxations that are available exclusively to DPIIT-recognised startups. DPIIT Recognition is the foundation of the Startup India ecosystem. It is the prerequisite for: Income tax exemption under Section 80-IAC of the Income Tax Act (3 years exemption out of 10) Capital gains tax exemption under Section 54EE DPIIT's various startup-specific regulatory benefits (IPO norms, labour law exemptions, etc.) Funding access through the Fund of Funds for Startups (FFS) managed by SIDBI Participation in government startup schemes, challenges, and procurement pilots DPIIT Recognition is obtained through the Startup India portal (startupindia.gov.in) and is free of cost.

## Features

Free recognition: DPIIT Recognition is completely free,  no government fee is charged Self-certification: The recognition process is largely self-certification based,  the startup declares its eligibility and DPIIT verifies Online process: End-to-end online on the Startup India portal,  no physical visits or document submissions Unique DPIIT Recognition Number: Each recognised startup receives a unique recognition certificate with a DPIIT recognition number Facilitates tax exemption applications: DPIIT Recognition is the gateway to applying for income tax exemption and capital gains exemption under Startup India Regulatory benefits: Recognised startups enjoy relaxations in labour and environment law self-certification, faster IPR registration, and simplified winding-up procedures Investor visibility: DPIIT-recognised startups are listed on the Startup India portal,  discoverable by investors, government departments, and ecosystem stakeholders Government procurement: Recognised startups can participate in GeM (Government e-Marketplace) as startup sellers with relaxed eligibility norms

## Benefits

Regulatory compliance relaxations: DPIIT-recognised startups can self-certify compliance under 6 labour laws (including CLRA, Maternity Benefit Act, EPF and MP Act) and 3 environmental laws for 5 years,  significantly reducing compliance burden Tax benefits gateway: Recognition is the mandatory first step for applying for 3-year income tax holiday (Section 80-IAC) and capital gains exemption (Section 54EE and 54GB) IPR fee concessions: Up to 80% rebate on patent filing fees and 50% rebate on trademark filing fees for recognised startups Fast-track patent examination: DPIIT-recognised startups get expedited patent examination under the Fast-Track scheme,  patents are examined within 30 days of request Fund of Funds access: DPIIT-recognised startups can receive funding from SEBI-registered AIFs that are funded through SIDBI's Fund of Funds,  a ₹10,000 crore government fund for startups Government procurement without turnover requirement: Recognised startups can bid for government procurement contracts without meeting the minimum turnover and prior experience criteria that apply to regular vendors Easy winding up: Under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, DPIIT-recognised startups (classified as "fast-track" companies) can be wound up within 90 days, compared to the regular process Networking and visibility: Access to the Startup India Hub, government startup events, international delegations, and investor forums

## Limitations

Eligibility caps: Companies with turnover exceeding ₹100 crore or those incorporated more than 10 years ago are not eligible,  limiting recognition to early-stage companies Innovation requirement: The company must work towards innovation, development, deployment, or commercialisation of new products, processes, or services driven by technology or intellectual property,  pure trading, manufacturing, or services without innovation do not qualify Scalability requirement: The startup must have potential to generate employment or create wealth,  passive investment companies or holding companies do not qualify Recognition ≠ tax exemption: DPIIT Recognition alone does not grant income tax exemption,  a separate application to the Inter-Ministerial Board (IMB) is required for Section 80-IAC exemption Time-bound: Recognition and its benefits are available only for 10 years from incorporation,  after which the entity is no longer considered a "startup" for DPIIT purposes No FDI facilitation: DPIIT Recognition does not directly affect FEMA/FDI compliance,  foreign investment compliance remains unchanged

## Applicability

DPIIT Recognition is available to entities that meet ALL of the following criteria: Criterion Requirement Entity type Private Limited Company, Registered Partnership Firm, or LLP Age Incorporated/registered within the last 10 years Annual turnover Less than ₹100 crore in any financial year since incorporation Nature of work Working towards innovation/development of product, process, or service; or a scalable business model with high potential for employment/wealth creation Not formed by splitting Not formed by splitting up or reconstruction of an existing business

Applicable pan-India,  startups across all states and UTs, including those in Tier 1 (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi, Hyderabad), Tier 2 (Pune, Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Kochi, Chandigarh), and Tier 3 cities can obtain DPIIT Recognition.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Certificate of Incorporation / Registration certificate | For private limited company, LLP, or partnership firm |
| PAN of the entity | Company/LLP/firm PAN |
| Brief description of innovation/business | 500-word write-up on the product/service and innovation element |
| Website URL (if any) | Company website or product link |
| Pitch deck or product demo (optional) | To support the innovation description |
| Founders' details | Name, DIN/DPIN, PAN, Aadhaar of founders/directors |
| Details of patents or IP (if any) | Patent application numbers, trademark registrations |
| Previous year's revenue (if any) | To confirm sub-₹100 crore turnover threshold |

## Process

1. Startup India Portal Registration Register on startupindia.gov.in with the company's email ID. Create a login account for the entity.

2. Entity Profile Complete the entity profile, incorporation details, type, CIN/LLPIN, PAN, and founder details.

3. Innovation Description Write a compelling, accurate description of the startup's innovation, the product/service being developed, the problem it solves, the technology/IP involved, and its scalability potential. This is the most critical part of the application.

4. Document Upload Upload the Certificate of Incorporation and other required documents.

5. Self-Declaration Submit the self-declaration certifying that all eligibility criteria are met.

6. DPIIT Review DPIIT reviews the application. For most applications, DPIIT grants recognition within 2–10 working days. If additional information is needed, DPIIT may seek clarification.

7. Recognition Certificate Upon approval, the DPIIT Recognition Certificate with unique recognition number is issued digitally on the Startup India portal.

8. Subsequent Applications Use the DPIIT Recognition Number to apply for Section 80-IAC tax exemption, IPR fee concessions, GeM startup registration, and other Startup India benefits. Fees Service Government Fee DPIIT Startup Recognition Nil (Free)


## Professional Service

Estimated Fee DPIIT Recognition application support ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Innovation write-up drafting ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 Section 80-IAC IMB application support ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 Complete Startup India compliance package ₹10,000 – ₹30,000

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Validity

DPIIT Recognition is valid until the startup reaches 10 years from its date of incorporation or its annual turnover exceeds ₹100 crore,  whichever occurs first. The recognition does not need to be periodically renewed; it remains valid as long as the startup continues to meet the eligibility criteria. The startup must update its profile on the Startup India portal with material changes (funding rounds, new products, team changes).

## Frequently asked questions

**We are a service company,  not a product or tech startup. Can we get DPIIT Recognition?** Yes. DPIIT Recognition is not limited to product or technology startups. A service company can be recognised if it is working towards innovation in service delivery, developing a new service model driven by technology or intellectual property, or has a highly scalable business model with significant employment or wealth creation potential. The key is demonstrating the innovative or scalable element. Pure conventional service businesses (traditional consulting, standard outsourcing) without any innovative element may not qualify.

**Our company was incorporated 8 years ago and just pivoted to a tech product. Can we still get DPIIT Recognition?** Yes, provided your company was incorporated within the last 10 years (from the date of application), your annual turnover has not exceeded ₹100 crore in any year, and the current business involves innovation/technology. The 10-year clock runs from incorporation date,  so if your company was incorporated 8 years ago, you have 2 more years of eligibility. Apply immediately if you qualify,  do not wait until year 10.

**Is DPIIT Recognition the same as Startup India Registration?** Yes. "DPIIT Recognition" and "Startup India Registration" refer to the same thing,  the recognition granted by DPIIT under the Startup India initiative, obtained through the startupindia.gov.in portal. The output is the DPIIT Recognition Certificate with a unique recognition number. This recognition is the foundation for all other Startup India benefits including tax exemptions, IPR concessions, and regulatory relaxations.

**What happens if our startup's turnover crosses ₹100 crore after getting DPIIT Recognition?** If a DPIIT-recognised startup's annual turnover crosses ₹100 crore in any financial year, it is no longer considered a "startup" for DPIIT purposes and ceases to be eligible for startup-specific benefits going forward. The recognition is not automatically revoked, but the entity should self-declare the change in status and is no longer entitled to claim startup-specific benefits. Tax benefits already availed (e.g., 80-IAC exemption for prior years) are not retrospectively reversed.

**Does DPIIT Recognition help in raising venture capital funding?** DPIIT Recognition itself does not directly attract or guarantee venture capital funding. However, it provides indirect facilitation: (a) it is a prerequisite for receiving funding from AIFs backed by SIDBI's Fund of Funds; (b) some institutional investors and family offices use DPIIT Recognition as a basic credibility filter; (c) the Startup India portal lists recognised startups, providing visibility to registered investors. The more significant funding impact comes from the income tax holiday (Section 80-IAC) which the recognition enables,  making the startup more attractive to investors by improving post-tax returns for founders.

services.name: CCFS scheme (Compromises, arrangements and amalgamations)
services.shortDescription: NCLT or Regional Director routes for mergers, demergers, schemes of arrangement, debt restructuring, and related corporate restructuring.
services.content:
## CCFS scheme

### Detailed overview

The Compromises, Arrangements and Amalgamations under the Companies Act, 2013, Sections 230 to 240, and the Companies (Compromises, Arrangements and Amalgamations) Rules, 2016 (CCFS Rules), provide the legal framework for corporate restructuring activities including mergers, demergers, amalgamations, debt restructuring, and other compromises and arrangements involving companies and their stakeholders (creditors, members).

The CCFS framework has streamlined corporate restructuring through fast-track mergers (between holding-subsidiary or between two small companies), formal NCLT-approved schemes for complex transactions, and structured shareholder and creditor approval processes.

### Key features

- NCLT or Regional Director routes depending on scheme type
- Class meetings, sanctioned schemes, and RoC filings to give effect
- Cross-border and tax or stamp aspects need parallel planning

## Applicability

Applicable to all companies undergoing merger, demerger, or amalgamation, companies undertaking compromise with creditors (debt restructuring), companies undertaking arrangement with members, and cross-border mergers (with prescribed jurisdictions). Pan-India applicability.

## Compliance requirements

| Scheme type | Authority |
| Fast-track merger (holding-subsidiary, two small companies) | Regional Director (RD) |
| Standard scheme (mergers, demergers) | National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) |
| Cross-border merger | NCLT and RBI approval |
| Class meetings | Of creditors and shareholders separately |

## Benefits

- Legal framework for complex restructuring
- Tax-neutral mergers under Income Tax Act (subject to conditions)
- Stamp duty relief in certain cases
- Creditor protection mechanism
- Court-approved binding effect on all stakeholders

## Risks and penalties

| Risk | Mitigation |
| Scheme rejection by NCLT | Strong commercial rationale, stakeholder consultations |
| Creditor objection | Engage major creditors early |
| Tax challenges | Pre-merger tax structuring |
| Long timelines | Plan restructuring 6 to 18 months in advance |

## Documents required

Comprehensive: scheme document, valuation reports, board resolutions, creditor lists, fairness opinion, no-objection certificates, audited financials, NCLT applications.

## Process

1. Strategic decision and structuring of scheme.
2. Valuation reports from registered valuers.
3. Board approvals.
4. Application to NCLT (or RD for fast-track).
5. NCLT directs convening of meetings of shareholders and creditors.
6. Class meetings, approval by 75% majority by value.
7. Final NCLT hearing for sanction.
8. NCLT order, filed with RoC, scheme effective.

## Government fees

NCLT fees, RoC fees, stamp duty (state-specific), significant amounts depending on transaction size.

## Timeline

6 to 18 months typically for standard schemes, 3 to 6 months for fast-track mergers.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. What is a fast-track merger?** Fast-track merger (Section 233) allows mergers between holding-subsidiary or between two small companies through a simplified process, approved by Regional Director rather than NCLT, significantly faster (3 to 6 months).

**2. Is creditor approval mandatory in all schemes?** Creditor approval (75% by value) is required when the scheme affects creditor rights, debt restructuring, mergers with significant debt restructuring component. Pure share-swap mergers may not need separate creditor approval.

**3. Can a foreign company merge with an Indian company?** Yes, cross-border mergers permitted from October 2017. Inbound merger (foreign into Indian): permitted from any country. Outbound merger (Indian into foreign): permitted only with companies from prescribed jurisdictions notified by Government.

**4. What stamp duty applies to mergers?** Stamp duty varies significantly by state, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu have different rates. NCLT order itself attracts stamp duty in some states. Pre-merger structuring should consider stamp duty implications.

**5. Are mergers tax-neutral?** Mergers complying with Section 2(1B) of the Income Tax Act are tax-neutral, no capital gains tax on transfer of assets or shares. Conditions include continuing business, shareholder continuity, and asset-share exchange ratio compliance.

All service content is for informational purposes. MCA compliance requirements, fees, and procedures are subject to change. Consult qualified company secretaries or chartered accountants for case-specific guidance based on your company's structure, status, and specific transaction needs.

services.name: Certificate of Incumbency
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Certificate of Incumbency — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Certificate Of Incumbency

## Overview

A Certificate of Incumbency (also called an Incumbency Certificate or Certificate of Good Standing in some jurisdictions) is an official document that certifies the current officers, directors, shareholders, and/or authorised signatories of a company at a specific point in time. In the context of Indian companies, it is typically a certificate issued by the Company Secretary or a recognised authority (Registrar of Companies, notary, or the company itself under seal) that confirms who the present office-holders of the company are. In India, while the term "Certificate of Incumbency" is not formally defined under the Companies Act, 2013, such certificates are routinely required by: Foreign banks and financial institutions when an Indian company opens an account or enters into contracts abroad Foreign counterparties in cross-border commercial contracts requiring proof of authority of the Indian company's signatories FEMA compliance purposes where the foreign investor/lender requires certified proof of the Indian company's management structure Due diligence in international M&A, joint venture, and partnership transactions Apostille and consular legalisation processes for use of Indian company documents overseas The certificate essentially answers the question: "Who is authorised to legally represent and sign on behalf of this company right now?",  a question that arises frequently in international business transactions.

## Features

Certifies current directors and officers: Name, designation, date of appointment, and nationality of each current director and key officer Confirms shareholding: Names and shareholding percentages of current shareholders (particularly for private limited companies) Identifies authorised signatories: Specifies who is authorised to sign banking, legal, or commercial documents on behalf of the company Point-in-time certification: The certificate is dated and certifies the company's management structure as of the date of issue Issued under company seal: Typically signed by the Company Secretary and Managing Director and bears the company's common seal Notarised and apostilled: For international use, the certificate is notarised by a Notary Public and apostilled by the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA), Government of India Customisable scope: The certificate can be tailored to include specific information required by the foreign party,  banking resolutions, specimen signatures, constitutional documents

## Benefits

International banking access: Foreign banks (especially in the US, UK, EU, Singapore, UAE) require a Certificate of Incumbency before opening corporate accounts for Indian entities,  it is a standard KYC document Cross-border contract execution: Foreign counterparties need proof that the person signing the contract on behalf of the Indian company is actually authorised to do so FEMA and RBI compliance: When Indian entities receive FDI or make ODI, the foreign investor/counterpart often requires incumbency certification as part of their KYC/AML compliance Legal proceedings: In international arbitration or litigation involving Indian companies, a Certificate of Incumbency provides certified evidence of corporate authority Anti-money laundering (AML) compliance: International financial institutions require incumbency certificates as part of their AML/CFT due diligence for Indian corporate clients Franchise and licensing agreements: International franchisors and technology licensors require incumbency certificates before executing master franchise or technology license agreements with Indian companies Investor relations: Foreign investors in Indian companies require incumbency certificates before disbursing investment tranches or executing shareholder agreements

## Limitations

Point-in-time only: The certificate is valid only as of its date of issue,  a change in directors or authorised signatories after issuance renders it potentially stale Not a statutory Indian document: Unlike a Certificate of Incorporation or Share Certificate, there is no standardised Indian statutory format for a Certificate of Incumbency,  each certificate is custom-drafted Apostille process adds time: If the certificate requires Apostille (for use in Hague Convention countries), the MEA apostille process adds 5–15 working days Foreign party-specific requirements: Different foreign banks and counterparties have different requirements for what the certificate must contain,  drafting errors or omissions cause rejections and re-issuances Notarisation costs: Notarisation fees vary across states and notaries; for urgent requirements, costs are higher Not accepted as substitute for power of attorney: A Certificate of Incumbency certifies authority within the company's constitutional structure; it does not replace a formal Power of Attorney for specific transactions

## Applicability

Indian private limited and public limited companies engaging in cross-border transactions Indian LLPs with foreign partners or entering international agreements Indian companies opening bank accounts with foreign banks (in India or overseas) Indian companies receiving foreign investment or making overseas investments Indian entities entering into technology licensing, franchise, or distribution agreements with foreign counterparties Indian companies involved in international arbitration or litigation Indian subsidiaries of foreign companies requiring certification for their parent/group transactions Applicable pan-India,  companies registered in any state (Karnataka, Maharashtra, Delhi, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, etc.) can obtain a Certificate of Incumbency as needed.

## Documents Required

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Certificate of Incorporation | From MCA,  proof of company's legal existence |
| Memorandum and Articles of Association | Constitutional documents of the company |
| Register of Directors | Current list of directors with DIN, address, date of appointment |
| Register of Members / Shareholding pattern | Current shareholding details |
| Board Resolution | Authorising issuance of the Certificate and naming authorised signatories |
| Recent MCA filings (Form DIR-12, MGT-7A) | To verify current director details with RoC records |
| PAN card of directors | KYC of current directors |
| Passport copies of directors | For international use,  especially where directors are foreign nationals |
| Specimen signatures | Signatures of directors/authorised signatories |
| Company common seal impression | If the company has a common seal |
| Notary appointment (if required) | Identification of the Notary Public for notarisation |

## Process

1. Review Foreign Party Requirements Understand exactly what information the foreign bank/counterparty requires in the Certificate, directors, shareholders, authorised signatories, banking authority, constitutional documents, etc.

2. MCA Records Verification Verify the current director and shareholder details on the MCA portal to ensure the certificate reflects accurate, current information consistent with RoC records.

3. Draft the Certificate Draft the Certificate of Incumbency in the format required by the foreign party. The certificate must be precise, comprehensive, and consistent with MCA records.

4. Board Resolution Pass a board resolution authorising the issuance of the Certificate of Incumbency and specifying who is authorised to sign on behalf of the company.

5. Execution Under Company Seal Sign the certificate by the authorised directors/Company Secretary and affix the company's common seal (if applicable).

6. Notarisation Get the certificate notarised by a Notary Public. Notarisation is typically required for international use.

7. Apostille (if required) For use in Hague Convention countries (US, UK, most EU countries, Australia, Singapore), submit the notarised certificate to the MEA or State Home Department for Apostille stamp.

8. Consular Legalisation (if required) For use in non-Hague countries, get the apostilled document further legalised by the relevant country's embassy/consulate in India.

9. Delivery Deliver the original executed, notarised, and apostilled/legalised certificate to the foreign party, either physically or via secure courier. Fees Service Component Estimated Fee Drafting Certificate of Incumbency ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Board resolution drafting ₹1,000 – ₹2,500 Notarisation charges ₹500 – ₹2,000 (per document, per notary) MEA Apostille charges ₹50 per document (government fee) + ₹500–₹2,000 (agent charges) Embassy/Consular legalisation ₹2,000 – ₹10,000 (varies by country) End-to-end Certificate of Incumbency service ₹8,000 – ₹25,000 Urgent/express service Additional ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%. Government fees (apostille, notarisation) are additional.


## Validity

A Certificate of Incumbency does not have a fixed statutory validity period. However: Most foreign banks and counterparties accept a Certificate of Incumbency that is not more than 3 to 6 months old from the date of use For ongoing banking relationships, banks may require a fresh certificate annually After any change in directors, officers, or authorised signatories, a fresh certificate must be issued

## Frequently asked questions

**Is a Certificate of Incumbency the same as a Certificate of Good Standing in India?** They are related but distinct. A Certificate of Good Standing (or Certificate of Status) confirms that a company is duly incorporated, is compliant with statutory filing requirements, and is in active status with the Registrar of Companies. A Certificate of Incumbency, on the other hand, certifies the current officers, directors, and authorised signatories of the company. In practice, for international use, many foreign parties require both,  the Certificate of Good Standing to confirm the company is legitimately incorporated and in compliance, and the Incumbency Certificate to confirm who is authorised to represent it.

**Who can issue a Certificate of Incumbency for an Indian company?** Unlike some offshore jurisdictions (British Virgin Islands, Cayman Islands) where Registered Agents issue standardised incumbency certificates, India does not have a standardised statutory format. A Certificate of Incumbency for an Indian company is typically issued by the Company Secretary (if the company has one) or by the directors themselves under the company's common seal, supported by a board resolution. For international acceptance, notarisation by a Notary Public and MEA Apostille are typically required.

**Can a Certificate of Incumbency be issued for an LLP instead of a company?** Yes. An LLP can issue an equivalent certificate,  often called a Certificate of Partnership Incumbency or Certificate of Designated Partners,  certifying the current Designated Partners (DPs) of the LLP, their authority, and the LLP's registration details. The certificate is supported by the LLP's Certificate of Incorporation, LLP Agreement, and a resolution of the Designated Partners. The process for notarisation and apostille is the same as for companies.

**Our foreign bank is asking for a Certificate of Incumbency and also a Board Resolution specifically authorising the account opening and naming the account signatories. Are these the same document?** No, they serve different purposes. The Certificate of Incumbency confirms who the current officers/directors of the company are,  it is a status certificate. The Board Resolution specifically authorises the opening of the bank account and designates the authorised signatories for that account with specimen signatures. Many foreign banks require both documents,  the Incumbency Certificate for general KYC and the Board Resolution for the specific banking transaction authorisation. Both documents may need to be notarised and apostilled.

**How quickly can a Certificate of Incumbency be arranged for an urgent cross-border transaction?** The certificate itself can be drafted and executed within 1–2 working days if all company documents and director details are readily available. Notarisation can typically be arranged within 1–2 working days. MEA Apostille, however, takes 5–15 working days through normal channels; premium/tatkaal services can reduce this to 2–5 working days at a higher cost. Consular legalisation timelines vary by country (embassy appointment availability). End-to-end for an apostilled certificate, plan for 7–15 working days under normal circumstances; 3–7 working days on an express basis.

services.name: FDI Filing (Foreign Direct Investment Reporting)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FDI Filing (Foreign Direct Investment Reporting) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# Fdi Filing

## Overview

Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) filing is a mandatory compliance requirement under the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, governed by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). Whenever a company incorporated in India receives foreign investment,  whether through equity, compulsorily convertible instruments, or other approved routes,  it must report the same to the RBI within prescribed timelines. This reporting is done through the RBI's FIRMS (Foreign Investment Reporting and Management System) portal. FDI compliance is not optional. Failure to report timely exposes Indian companies to compounding penalties, regulatory scrutiny, and restrictions on future foreign investment. Whether you are a startup receiving angel funding from an NRI, a private limited company attracting venture capital from a foreign entity, or a wholly owned subsidiary receiving share capital from its overseas parent, FDI filing obligations apply to you. Our service ensures accurate, timely, and complete FDI filing,  from Form FC-GPR to FC-TRS,  backed by expert guidance on sectoral caps, pricing guidelines, and downstream investment reporting.

## Applicability

FDI filing requirements apply to: Indian companies (private limited, public limited, LLPs with FEMA approval) receiving foreign investment Companies issuing equity shares, preference shares, or compulsorily convertible debentures/preference shares (CCDs/CCPs) to foreign investors Indian residents transferring shares to non-residents or vice versa Foreign portfolio investors (FPI) crossing reporting thresholds Downstream investments by Indian entities that have received FDI Joint ventures with foreign collaboration agreements ESOP issuances to foreign employees of group companies Applicability across India: FDI filing is a central compliance governed by RBI and FEMA, applicable uniformly across all states,  Maharashtra, Delhi, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Gujarat, West Bengal, Rajasthan, and others. State-level permissions may vary for certain sectors (e.g., real estate, retail), but the FDI reporting obligation to RBI remains pan-India.

## Key Provisions

Governing Framework: FEMA, 1999,  Primary legislation FEMA (Non-debt Instruments) Rules, 2019 RBI Master Direction on Foreign Investment in India Consolidated FDI Policy issued by DPIIT (Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade) Key Reporting Forms:

| Form | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Filing Timeline | FC-GPR |
| Reporting issue of equity instruments to foreign investor | Within 30 days of allotment |
| FC-TRS | Reporting transfer of shares between resident and non-resident |
| Within 60 days of transfer/receipt of funds | LLP-I |
| Reporting FDI received by LLPs | Within 30 days of receipt |
| LLP-II | Reporting disinvestment or transfer of profit shares in LLP |
| Within 60 days | ESOP |
| Reporting ESOP issuance to non-residents | Within 30 days of allotment |
| DI Form | Downstream investment reporting |
| Within 30 days | CN Form |
| Reporting conversion of ECB/lump sum fee into equity | Within 30 days |

Pricing Guidelines: Shares cannot be issued to foreign investors below the fair market value (FMV) determined by a SEBI-registered merchant banker or a Chartered Accountant under DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) method. Shares cannot be transferred to a non-resident below FMV; transfers from non-resident to resident cannot be above FMV. Sectoral Caps and Routes: Sector FDI Cap Route Agriculture & Animal Husbandry 100% Automatic Manufacturing 100% Automatic E-commerce (marketplace) 100% Automatic Insurance 74% Automatic (up to 49%), Government above 49% Banking (private sector) 74% Automatic (up to 49%), Government above 49% Defence 74% Automatic (up to 49%), Government above 49% Multi-brand retail 51% Government Print media 26% Government Broadcasting (FM radio) 26% Government Lottery, gambling 0% Prohibited Atomic energy 0% Prohibited

## Benefits

Regulatory compliance: Avoids compounding penalties under FEMA which can be up to 3 times the foreign exchange involved or INR 2 lakh per day of default Clean cap table: Properly filed FDI ensures a clean and auditable cap table,  essential for future fundraising rounds and M&A transactions Investor confidence: Overseas investors require proof of RBI reporting before remitting additional tranches Ease of repatriation: Future dividend repatriation, share buyback, and liquidation proceeds can only be repatriated if original investment is properly reported Legal certainty: Avoids risk of investment being treated as "illegal" under FEMA, attracting ED scrutiny Supports IPO readiness: SEBI and stock exchanges require clean FEMA compliance history during DRHP review

## Limitations

Prohibited sectors: FDI cannot be accepted in lottery, gambling, chit funds (non-Nidhis), real estate business (not real estate construction), manufacturing of tobacco products, etc. Pricing constraints: Issuing shares at a price lower than FMV is not permitted, limiting flexibility in deal structuring Downstream compliance burden: If the funded entity makes further investments in Indian companies, downstream reporting requirements add layers of compliance Government route delays: Sectors requiring government approval (FIPB route, now replaced by respective ministries) can have approval timelines of 4–8 weeks or more FEMA violations are strict: Unlike company law where compounding is straightforward, FEMA violations attract ED attention and compounding procedures before RBI are more rigorous LLP restrictions: FDI in LLPs is permitted only in sectors where 100% FDI is allowed under automatic route; LLPs cannot accept FDI under government route

## Documents Required

For FC-GPR Filing:

| Document | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Board resolution | Approving allotment of shares to foreign investor |
| Special resolution (if applicable) | For private placement under Companies Act |
| Valuation certificate | From CA/merchant banker certifying FMV under DCF or NAV method |
| KYC of foreign investor | Banker's certificate or passport/incorporation documents |
| FIRC / bank certificate | Foreign Inward Remittance Certificate evidencing receipt of funds |
| Debit authorisation | From the AD (Authorised Dealer) bank |
| Share allotment letter | Copy of allotment letter issued to investor |
| Shareholding pattern | Pre and post allotment |

Additional for FC-TRS: Share purchase/transfer agreement Consent letter from buyer and seller Board resolution approving transfer Form SH-4 (instrument of transfer under Companies Act)

## Process

1. Pre-Filing Compliance Check Review the sector of the company, identify applicable FDI cap, determine whether automatic or government route applies, and verify that the investee company is eligible to receive FDI (not in negative/restricted list).

2. Valuation Obtain FMV valuation certificate from a SEBI-registered merchant banker or CA using the DCF method (for unlisted companies) or market price (for listed entities).

3. Receive Funds and Obtain FIRC Ensure funds are received through proper banking channels. Obtain FIRC or bank certificate from the AD bank.

4. Allotment of Shares Pass board resolution, allot shares within 60 days of receipt of consideration, and file PAS-3 with MCA within 30 days of allotment.

5. FIRMS Portal Registration and Filing Register on RBI's FIRMS portal. File the relevant form (FC-GPR, FC-TRS, etc.) with all supporting documents within the prescribed timeline.

6. AD Bank Authentication The AD bank authenticates the filing on the FIRMS portal after verifying the FIRC and KYC documents.

7. Unique Identification Number (UIN) Upon successful filing, RBI issues a UIN (formerly FRN, Foreign Registration Number) which must be preserved for all future reference.

8. Post-Filing Record Keeping Maintain records of all filings for at least 10 years. Update RBI filing details in the annual FLA Return. Fees Service Component Estimated Professional Fee Pre-filing consultation and eligibility check ₹5,000 – ₹10,000 Valuation support/coordination (CA-DCF) ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 FC-GPR filing (single tranche) ₹8,000 – ₹15,000 FC-TRS filing (per transaction) ₹8,000 – ₹15,000 LLP-I / LLP-II filing ₹6,000 – ₹12,000 Downstream investment (DI Form) ₹8,000 – ₹15,000 Government route FDI application support ₹25,000 – ₹60,000 Compounding application (in case of delay) ₹30,000 – ₹75,000+ End-to-end FDI compliance package ₹25,000 – ₹75,000 Fees are exclusive of GST @18%. RBI/government filing fees are nominal or nil for most forms. Valuation fees charged separately by the valuing CA/merchant banker. Compliance Requirements Timeline adherence: FC-GPR within 30 days of allotment; FC-TRS within 60 days of transfer Annual FLA Return: Every entity that has received FDI or made ODI must file the FLA Return with RBI by July 15 each year Reporting of downstream investments: Entities funded by foreign investment must report their own investments in Indian entities Pricing compliance: Annual review recommended to ensure share transfers are within FMV bands Compounding for late filing: Delays must be compounded before RBI; delaying compounding further increases penalty exposure AD Bank intimation: The company's AD bank must be kept informed of all FDI receipts and filings No-objection from existing investors: Certain categories of share transfers require prior consent per shareholders' agreement terms


## Frequently asked questions

**What happens if I miss the FC-GPR deadline of 30 days?** Late filing constitutes a FEMA violation. You must file a compounding application before the RBI's compounding authority. The penalty is calculated based on the amount involved and the duration of delay. While compounding is available, repeated violations or large amounts can attract Enforcement Directorate (ED) scrutiny. It is strongly advisable to file proactively and seek compounding at the earliest.

**Can a startup receive FDI without a valuation certificate if the amount is small?** No. The valuation requirement under FEMA applies regardless of the quantum of investment. Every issue of shares to a foreign investor must be at or above FMV certified by a SEBI-registered merchant banker or a CA (for unlisted companies). There is no de minimis exemption under FEMA for valuation.

**Is FDI allowed in a One Person Company (OPC)?** No. OPCs are not permitted to accept FDI under FEMA. If a foreign investor wishes to invest, the OPC must first be converted into a private limited company. This is a common oversight among early-stage entrepreneurs receiving overseas angel investments.

**What is the difference between FDI and FPI for reporting purposes?** FDI involves investment of 10% or more in a company's equity and is reported through FC-GPR/FC-TRS. FPI (Foreign Portfolio Investment) is typically below 10% and handled through SEBI-registered FPI route, reported through custodians. If an FPI crosses 10% in a company, they must convert to FDI reporting within prescribed timelines.

**Is FC-TRS required for transfer of shares between two non-residents?** Yes, if the transfer involves a resident Indian company's shares (regardless of whether both buyer and seller are non-residents), FC-TRS is required within 60 days of transfer. The reporting obligation lies with the Indian company and the resident party, if any; otherwise the non-resident seller/buyer must report through their AD bank.

services.name: AOA Amendment (Articles of Association)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for AOA Amendment (Articles of Association) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## AOA amendment

### Detailed overview

The Articles of Association (AOA) is the foundational document of a company that defines the internal rules of management, governance structure, share capital provisions, director powers, meetings, dividends, transfer of shares, and operational rules. Amendment of AOA is governed by Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013, and requires shareholder approval through a special resolution (75% majority).

AOA amendments are common during company life cycle events, fundraising rounds (introducing investor protection clauses), conversion from private to public, modifying share transfer restrictions, changing borrowing limits, and altering management provisions.

### Key features

- Special resolution and MGT-14 with amended AOA attached
- Explanatory statement and 21 clear days' notice for EGMs where applicable
- Alignment of internal rules with funding, investors, and operations

## Applicability

Applicable to all companies, private limited, public limited, Section 8, OPC. Pan-India.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Special resolution | 75% shareholder approval mandatory |
| EGM or AGM | General meeting required for resolution |
| Form MGT-14 | Filed with MCA within 30 days |
| Amended AOA | New AOA copy attached |
| Notice period for EGM | 21 clear days' notice |
| Ordinary resolution | Insufficient, special resolution mandatory |

## Benefits

- Aligns governance with current business needs
- Enables fundraising flexibility
- Investor protection clauses incorporation
- Modernises legacy clauses
- Conversion enablement (private to public, etc.)

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Late MGT-14 filing | ₹100 per day |
| Insufficient resolution majority | Amendment void |
| Inadequate notice for EGM | Procedural irregularity, resolution can be challenged |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| Existing AOA | Reference |
| Proposed amended AOA | New AOA with changes marked |
| Special resolution | Passed at EGM |
| Notice of EGM | With explanatory statement |
| EGM minutes | Confirming resolution passed |
| Form MGT-14 | MCA filing |
| Director's certification | DSC signing |

## Process

1. **Board approval:** Board passes resolution approving the proposed AOA amendment and convening EGM.
2. **Notice of EGM:** Issue 21 clear days' notice to all shareholders with explanatory statement explaining the proposed changes.
3. **EGM:** Convene EGM. Special resolution passed with 75%+ majority.
4. **Amended AOA:** Prepare new AOA incorporating the amendments.
5. **File MGT-14:** Within 30 days of resolution, file Form MGT-14 with the MCA, attaching the special resolution and amended AOA.
6. **Register update:** Update company's statutory register and AOA copy at registered office.

## Government fees

| Component | Fee |
| MGT-14 | ₹200 to ₹600 (based on authorized capital) |
| Late fee | ₹100 per day |
| Stamp duty on amended AOA | State-specific (typically nominal) |

## Timeline

| Activity | Timeline |
| Board approval | Day 1 |
| EGM notice | Day 2 (21 days' notice) |
| EGM | Day 23+ |
| MGT-14 filing | Within 30 days of resolution |
| Total | 30 to 45 days |

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Can AOA be amended without shareholder approval?** No. AOA amendment requires special resolution of shareholders (75% majority). Board cannot unilaterally amend AOA, this is a fundamental governance principle.

**2. Are there any AOA clauses that cannot be amended?** Most clauses can be amended through special resolution. However, certain alterations affecting class rights of shareholders (e.g. preference shareholders) require additional approvals, class meetings and consent of the affected class.

**3. Does AOA amendment require RoC approval?** No prior approval required from RoC. AOA amendments are filed with RoC for record purposes (via MGT-14). However, certain amendments (like change of name or capital alteration) require additional MCA approvals.

**4. Can a new investor in a Series A round require AOA amendments?** Yes. Investor protection rights (anti-dilution, drag-along, tag-along, liquidation preference, board representation) are typically incorporated through AOA amendments alongside the Shareholders' Agreement (SHA). This is standard in startup fundraising.

**5. What happens if MGT-14 is not filed for an AOA amendment?** The amendment does not become legally effective until filed with the MCA. The company faces penalties and the amendment cannot be relied upon in legal proceedings or subsequent corporate actions.

services.name: XBRL Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for XBRL Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## XBRL filing

### Detailed overview

XBRL (eXtensible Business Reporting Language) is a global standard for digital business reporting that enables structured, machine-readable financial data. Under the Companies Act, 2013 and the Companies (Filing of Documents and Forms in XBRL) Rules, 2015, certain categories of companies are required to file their financial statements (AOC-4) and Cost Audit Reports in XBRL format on the MCA portal.

XBRL filing involves tagging each financial data element using a standardised taxonomy, enabling regulators, analysts, and stakeholders to extract, compare, and analyse company financials efficiently across thousands of filings.

### Key features

- Machine-readable tagged financial data with MCA taxonomy
- Validation before upload, professional certification typically required
- Pairs AOC-4 financial content with mandatory digital format for trigger companies

## Applicability

XBRL filing is mandatory in the following cases.

| Category | Trigger |
| Listed companies | All listed companies on Indian stock exchanges |
| Subsidiaries of listed companies | All Indian subsidiaries |
| Companies with paid-up capital ≥ ₹5 crore | Mandatory |
| Companies with turnover ≥ ₹100 crore | Mandatory |
| Companies preparing CFS | Required for consolidated filings |
| Cost audit applicable companies | Cost Audit Report filed in XBRL via Form CRA-4 |

It is applicable pan-India.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| AOC-4 XBRL | Annual financial statements |
| CRA-4 XBRL | Cost audit reports |
| Taxonomy | MCA prescribed XBRL taxonomy (updated periodically) |
| Tagging accuracy | Each financial element correctly tagged |
| Validation | XBRL file must pass MCA validation tool |
| Professional certification | CA, CS, or CMA certification mandatory |
| Software | XBRL preparation software (multiple vendors) |

## Benefits

- Standardised financial reporting
- Data interoperability for analysts and regulators
- Reduced manual data extraction
- Improved data quality and consistency
- Transparency for investors

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Penalty |
| Late filing | ₹100 per day (additional) |
| Tagging errors | Form rejection requiring resubmission |
| Validation failures | Cannot upload, must correct XBRL file |
| Wrong taxonomy version | Form rejection |
| Non-compliance with XBRL where mandatory | AOC-4 in non-XBRL format will be rejected |

## Documents required

Same as AOC-4 (audited financials, board report, auditor report) but converted to XBRL format using approved software and the MCA taxonomy.

## Process

1. Complete audit of financial statements.
2. Engage XBRL preparation expert or software.
3. Tag each financial data element using the current MCA taxonomy.
4. Generate the XBRL instance document.
5. Validate using the MCA validation tool.
6. Get CA, CS, or CMA certification.
7. File AOC-4 XBRL on the MCA portal.

## Government fees

Same as AOC-4, based on authorized capital. XBRL preparation software costs and professional fees are additional.

## Timeline

Same as AOC-4, within 30 days of the AGM.

## Frequently asked questions

**1. What is the MCA XBRL taxonomy and how often does it change?** The MCA XBRL taxonomy is the standardised set of tags used to mark up financial data. MCA updates the taxonomy periodically (typically annually) to reflect changes in accounting standards (Ind AS) and disclosure requirements. The latest taxonomy version must be used for the relevant FY.

**2. Can XBRL filing be done without specialised software?** Practically no. Manual XBRL preparation is extremely complex. Professional XBRL software (from vendors like IRIS Carbon, BSE XBRL, Thomson Reuters) is essential. Many CA and CS firms also provide XBRL filing services.

**3. What is the difference between AOC-4 and AOC-4 XBRL?** AOC-4 is the standard PDF-based filing. AOC-4 XBRL is the same financial information in machine-readable XBRL format with tagged data elements. XBRL enables automated data extraction, PDF is human-readable only.

**4. Is XBRL filing required for cost audit reports?** Yes. Companies subject to cost audit under Section 148 of the Companies Act must file the Cost Audit Report (CRA-3) in Form CRA-4 in XBRL format.

**5. Are subsidiaries of listed companies required to file XBRL even if they are small?** Yes. All subsidiaries of listed companies must file in XBRL format, regardless of their individual size or paid-up capital. The trigger is the parent's listed status, not the subsidiary's individual size.

services.name: MGT-7 Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for MGT-7 Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## MGT-7 filing

### Detailed overview

Form MGT-7 is the prescribed form under Section 92 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 11 of the Companies (Management and Administration) Rules, 2014, for filing the Annual Return of a company with the Registrar of Companies. The Annual Return captures comprehensive information about the company's structure, governance, capital, shareholding, debt, and changes during the financial year.

While AOC-4 captures financial statements, MGT-7 captures non-financial corporate information, making it the second pillar of a company's annual MCA compliance. The Annual Return is a critical disclosure document used by regulators, investors, and stakeholders to understand a company's governance and structural status.

For small companies and OPCs, a simplified form MGT-7A is prescribed (covered separately).

### Key features

- Annual return of corporate, governance, and capital information with RoC
- Complements AOC-4 (financials) with non-financial disclosures
- Listed and certain unlisted companies may require a practising company secretary in the loop for certification, as applicable

## Applicability

MGT-7 is applicable to all Indian companies (private limited, public limited, listed), Section 8 companies (above the small company threshold), producer companies, and foreign companies operating in India (with modifications). Exception: small companies (paid-up capital ≤ ₹4 crore and turnover ≤ ₹40 crore) and OPCs file MGT-7A instead. It is applicable across all states and UTs in India.

## Compliance requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
| Form | MGT-7 (or MGT-7A for small companies and OPCs) |
| Due date | Within 60 days of AGM |
| AGM cutoff | September 30 (FY ending March 31) |
| MGT-7 due date | November 29 (typically) |
| Pre-conditions | All other annual filings done, share transfer register updated |
| Certification | DSC of director and DSC of CS in practice (mandatory for listed and certain unlisted companies) |
| MGT-8 attachment | For companies with paid-up capital ≥ ₹10 crore or turnover ≥ ₹50 crore |

## Benefits

- Statutory annual MCA compliance
- Public record of company structure and governance
- Required for company status maintenance
- Supports M&A, IPO, and fundraising due diligence
- Director and shareholder records maintained on the MCA portal

## Risks and penalties

| Default | Consequence |
| Late filing | ₹100 per day late fee |
| Continued non-filing (3+ years) | Director disqualification under Section 164(2) for 5 years |
| Company default status | Restricts further MCA filings |
| Strike-off | RoC may strike off non-compliant companies |
| Shareholder challenges | Outdated records create disputes |

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
| List of shareholders | With shareholding details |
| List of directors | With DIN, designation, dates |
| Particulars of debentures | Debt securities issued |
| Particulars of charges | Loans secured by company assets |
| Indebtedness details | Loans payable to banks or NBFCs |
| Share transfer details | Transfers during the year |
| AGM minutes | Confirming approval of the annual return |
| Form MGT-8 | For applicable companies (CS certification) |
| DSC of director and CS | For form signing |

## Process

1. Compile all annual data: shareholders, directors, capital changes, share transfers.
2. Update statutory registers (share transfer register, members register).
3. Hold AGM and approve the annual return.
4. Prepare MGT-7 with all schedules and attachments.
5. Engage a practising CS for MGT-8 certification (if applicable).
6. DSC signing by director and certifying CS.
7. File MGT-7 on the MCA portal within 60 days of the AGM.
8. SRN generation and acknowledgement.

## Government fees

| Authorized capital | Fee |
| Up to ₹1 lakh | ₹200 |
| ₹1 lakh – ₹5 lakh | ₹300 |
| ₹5 lakh – ₹25 lakh | ₹400 |
| ₹25 lakh – ₹1 crore | ₹500 |
| Above ₹1 crore | ₹600 |

Late fee: ₹100 per day after the due date.

## Timeline

| Activity | Date |
| FY end | March 31 |
| AGM by | September 30 |
| MGT-7 due | November 29 (60 days from AGM) |

## Frequently asked questions

**1. Is MGT-7 different for small companies and large companies?** Yes. Small companies (paid-up capital ≤ ₹4 crore and turnover ≤ ₹40 crore) and OPCs file MGT-7A, a simplified version. All other companies file MGT-7. Large companies (paid-up capital ≥ ₹10 crore or turnover ≥ ₹50 crore) additionally need MGT-8 (CS certification).

**2. What is MGT-8 and when is it required?** MGT-8 is a Secretarial Audit Report attached to MGT-7 for companies with paid-up capital ≥ ₹10 crore or turnover ≥ ₹50 crore. It is certified by a Practising Company Secretary and provides assurance on annual return contents.

**3. Can MGT-7 be revised after filing?** Yes, similar to AOC-4. If errors are discovered, a revised MGT-7 can be filed. Major revisions may require board and shareholder approval depending on the nature of changes.

**4. Are foreign companies operating in India required to file MGT-7?** Foreign companies file Form FC-3 instead of MGT-7, covering similar information but adapted for the foreign company structure. Subsidiary Indian companies of foreign parents file regular MGT-7.

**5. What is the difference between AOC-4 and MGT-7?** AOC-4 captures financial statements (audited balance sheet, P&L, cash flow), MGT-7 captures non-financial corporate information (shareholders, directors, share transfers, debt, charges). Both are mandatory annual filings, AOC-4 within 30 days of AGM, MGT-7 within 60 days of AGM.
