**Q1. I have already exhausted my Rs. 1.5 lakh limit under Section 80C. Can I still contribute to NPS and get additional benefit?**

Yes, absolutely. Section 80CCD(1B) is designed precisely for this situation. Even if you have fully utilised your Rs. 1.5 lakh limit under Section 80C (through PPF, ELSS, LIC premium, etc.), you can contribute an additional Rs. 50,000 to your NPS Tier I account and claim this separately under 80CCD(1B). The two sections have independent ceilings.

**Q2. Can a self-employed person or freelancer also claim Section 80CCD(1B)?**

Yes. Section 80CCD(1B) is available to all resident individuals, including self-employed persons, consultants, and freelancers. They can open an NPS account through the eNPS portal or a bank PoP and contribute up to Rs. 50,000 per year to claim this deduction. They will also be eligible for 80CCD(1) within the Rs. 1.5 lakh limit.

**Q3. If I contribute Rs. 70,000 to NPS Tier I this year, how much of it is deductible under 80CCD(1B)?**

The maximum deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) is Rs. 50,000, regardless of the actual contribution. If you contribute Rs. 70,000, Rs. 50,000 is deductible under 80CCD(1B). The remaining Rs. 20,000 may be counted under 80CCD(1) within the Rs. 1.5 lakh limit of 80C. So the total NPS contribution of Rs. 70,000 can be fully deducted: Rs. 50,000 under 80CCD(1B) and Rs. 20,000 under 80CCD(1).

**Q4. Does my employer's NPS contribution count towards the Rs. 50,000 limit under 80CCD(1B)?**

No. The employer's contribution to NPS is deductible under a completely separate provision: Section 80CCD(2). The Rs. 50,000 limit under 80CCD(1B) applies only to the employee's own voluntary contribution. Your employer's NPS contribution does not affect or reduce your 80CCD(1B) limit in any way.

**Q5. Is there a risk of losing my NPS corpus to market fluctuations?**

NPS is a market-linked retirement product. Your contributions are invested in equity, corporate bonds, and government securities based on the scheme option chosen. The Auto Choice option adjusts the equity exposure based on your age (higher equity when young, lower as you approach 60), while Active Choice lets you decide the allocation. While market risk exists, NPS has historically delivered reasonable long-term returns. The PFRDA regulates and supervises NPS fund managers, providing an additional layer of investor protection.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80DD: Tax Relief for Families Supporting Disabled Dependents
articles.excerpt: Flat deductions for disability-dependent care, eligibility, Rs. 75,000 / Rs. 1,25,000 limits, certificate rules, example, and FAQs.
articles.content:
# Section 80DD: Tax Relief for Families Supporting Disabled Dependents

Caring for a family member with a disability is emotionally demanding and financially taxing. Section 80DD acknowledges this reality by providing a flat deduction to taxpayers who bear the medical treatment, training, or rehabilitation expenses of a dependent with a disability. Unlike most deductions, 80DD does not require you to submit actual expense receipts for the deduction amount itself; the deduction is flat and based on the severity of disability.

## 1. Overview

Section 80DD of the Income Tax Act allows individual taxpayers and HUFs to claim a deduction for expenditure incurred on a dependent who is a person with a disability. This includes costs toward nursing, training, rehabilitation, and amounts deposited in approved schemes such as LIC's Jeevan Vishwas or UTI's unit-linked insurance plans for the disabled dependent.

The key feature of this section is that the deduction is available regardless of the actual amount spent, as long as it meets or exceeds the deduction threshold. This is a significant structural advantage for caregivers.

## 2. Eligibility

Who Can Claim

Individual taxpayers who are resident Indians

Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)

Who Qualifies as a Dependent

The dependent must be a person with disability and must wholly or mainly depend on the taxpayer for support. Eligible dependents include:

Spouse

Children

Parents

Siblings (brothers/sisters)

If the disabled person himself is filing taxes and claiming deduction under Section 80U, then the caregiver cannot also claim 80DD for the same individual.

Nature of Disability

The dependent must have a disability recognized under the Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act, 1995, or the National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999. This includes:

Blindness or low vision

Hearing impairment

Locomotor disability or cerebral palsy

Mental illness or mental retardation

Autism spectrum disorder

## 3. Deduction Limits

| Disability Category | Disability Percentage | Flat Deduction (Rs.) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Disability | 40% to 79% | 75,000 |
| Severe Disability | 80% and above | 1,25,000 |

These amounts are flat deductions, not reimbursements. Even if you spent Rs. 30,000, you still get the full Rs. 75,000 deduction if the disability certificate confirms 40-79% disability. Conversely, if you spent Rs. 2,00,000, your deduction remains capped at Rs. 1,25,000.



## 4. Example Calculation

Scenario: A Parent Caring for a Child with Severe Cerebral Palsy

Deepak, a senior bank manager earning Rs. 16 lakh per year, cares for his 14-year-old daughter diagnosed with severe cerebral palsy (90% disability as certified by a government medical board). He spends approximately Rs. 1.8 lakh annually on therapy, nursing, and medication.

| Item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Gross Total Income | 16,00,000 |
| 80C deduction (EPF + PPF) | 1,50,000 |
| 80DD deduction (severe disability) | 1,25,000 |
| Taxable Income | 13,25,000 |

Tax Saved via 80DD at 30% slab

39,000 (incl. cess)

Without the 80DD deduction, Deepak would pay Rs. 39,000 more in taxes. This amounts to over three months of therapy fees for his daughter.

## 5. Optimization Tips

- Get the right certificate upfront: The disability certificate must be from a government-recognized medical authority. Ensure it clearly states the type and percentage of disability.
- Renew disability certificates timely: Some certificates are issued with an expiry date. Claiming 80DD without a valid certificate can lead to demand notices during assessment.
- Consider scheme-based deposits: Depositing in LIC or UTI schemes specifically for the disabled dependent also qualifies. These offer both tax deduction and financial security for the dependent.
- Stack with other deductions: 80DD does not conflict with 80C, 80D, or other deductions. Claim all applicable sections simultaneously to maximize your overall tax savings.
- Maintain supporting documentation: Even though the deduction is flat, keep receipts for medical expenses, insurance premiums, and therapy bills in case of tax scrutiny.

## 6. Mistakes to Avoid

- Claiming 80DD when the dependent files 80U: If the disabled individual is also a taxpayer and files their own 80U claim, the caregiver loses the right to claim 80DD for that dependent.
- Using outdated disability certificates: An expired or improperly issued certificate is grounds for disallowance of the claim during assessment.
- Assuming actual expenses determine deduction: Many taxpayers don't claim 80DD thinking they haven't spent enough. The deduction is fixed and does not depend on actual expenditure amount.
- Forgetting to specify the dependent in ITR: You must provide the dependent's details (name, relationship, disability type) in the ITR form. Leaving this blank can trigger notices.
- Claiming for a non-dependent family member: If the disabled individual is financially independent (employed or earning income), the caregiver cannot claim 80DD.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. Can I claim 80DD for both a parent and a sibling with disabilities?**

Yes, but the total deduction is capped per individual taxpayer, not per dependent. You can claim one combined deduction of Rs. 75,000 (or Rs. 1,25,000 for severe disability) for all dependents collectively, not separately for each.

**Q2. What if the disability percentage is exactly 80%? Does it qualify as severe?**

Yes. Severe disability is defined as 80% or above. A disability certified at exactly 80% qualifies for the higher deduction of Rs. 1,25,000.

**Q3. My dependent receives a disability pension from the state government. Does that affect my 80DD claim?**

Not necessarily. As long as the dependent still mainly relies on you for their daily financial needs and the pension income is modest, you may still be able to claim 80DD. However, if the dependent is fully self-sufficient from such income, the claim may be challenged.

**Q4. Can an NRI claim 80DD for a disabled dependent living in India?**

No. Section 80DD is available only to individuals who are resident Indians for tax purposes. NRIs are not eligible for this deduction.

**Q5. Is a doctor's certificate from a private hospital valid for 80DD purposes?**

No. The disability certificate must be issued by a medical authority notified under the relevant disabilities legislation, which typically means government hospitals or specified government medical boards. Private doctor certificates are not accepted for this purpose.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80GGC: Tax Deduction for Political Party Donations
articles.excerpt: 100% deduction for qualifying political donations via banking channels, eligibility, limits, example, compliance tips, and FAQs.
articles.content:
# Section 80GGC: Tax Deduction for Political Party Donations

Most taxpayers are unaware that contributions made to registered political parties or electoral trusts can be claimed as a deduction from taxable income. Section 80GGC was introduced to encourage transparent political funding by incentivizing contributions through official banking channels while making such donations tax-deductible.

## 1. Overview

Section 80GGC of the Income Tax Act, 1961 allows individual taxpayers, HUFs, firms, and other entities (except companies) to claim a 100% deduction on donations made to registered political parties or electoral trusts. This section applies only when the payment is made through legitimate banking modes and not in cash.

Its counterpart, Section 80GGB, applies specifically to companies. Section 80GGC covers everyone else. Both sections aim to promote clean, documented political financing.

## 2. Eligibility

Who Can Claim

Individual taxpayers

HUFs (Hindu Undivided Families)

Partnership firms and LLPs

Cooperative societies and other non-corporate entities

Who Cannot Claim

Companies (they must use Section 80GGB instead)

Government companies

Any taxpayer who makes the donation in cash

Recipient Must Be

A political party registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951

OR an Electoral Trust approved by the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT)

## 3. Deduction Limits

| Parameter | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Deduction Percentage | 100% of the donated amount |
| Maximum Deduction Cap | No upper limit (full donation amount deductible) |
| Payment Mode Allowed | Cheque, NEFT, RTGS, UPI, Demand Draft |
| Cash Donations | NOT eligible for deduction |
| Eligible Recipients | Registered political parties and approved electoral trusts |
| Applicable Tax Regime | Old Tax Regime only |

There is no cap on the amount you can donate and deduct, unlike many other sections. If you donate Rs. 5 lakh to a registered party by cheque, your taxable income reduces by Rs. 5 lakh.

## 4. Example Calculation

Scenario: A Small Business Owner Supporting a Political Cause

Arvind, a proprietor of a retail business, has a net taxable income of Rs. 28 lakh for FY 2024-25 under the Old Regime. He donates Rs. 2 lakh by NEFT to a registered national political party.

| Item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Gross Taxable Income | 28,00,000 |
| 80C deduction | 1,50,000 |
| 80GGC deduction (political donation) | 2,00,000 |
| Net Taxable Income | 24,50,000 |
| Tax Saved on Rs. 2L Donation at 30% | 62,400 (incl. 4% cess) |

## 5. Optimization Tips

- Always donate via banking channels: Cash donations get zero deduction. Even a Rs. 100 cash donation is ineligible. Use UPI, cheque, or bank transfer.
- Verify the party's registration: Before donating, confirm that the party is registered under the Representation of the People Act. Donations to unregistered parties do not qualify.
- Request a receipt immediately: The political party or electoral trust must provide you an official receipt. This is required for ITR documentation.
- Combine with other deductions: 80GGC can be clubbed with 80C, 80D, and other deductions for maximum tax efficiency within the Old Regime.
- Evaluate New Regime impact: If switching to the New Regime saves you more overall (despite losing 80GGC), do the math carefully before deciding.

## 6. Mistakes to Avoid

- Donating in cash and expecting a deduction: This is the most common error. Cash donations are categorically excluded, regardless of amount or the party's credibility.
- Donating to state-level unregistered groups: Only national and state parties registered under the 1951 Act qualify. Local political associations or campaign funds do not.
- Not mentioning PAN details in the donation: Providing incorrect or missing PAN details can cause the deduction to be disallowed during processing.
- Failing to report the donation in ITR: Political party donations claimed under 80GGC must be declared in Schedule VI-A of the relevant ITR form. Omitting this can lead to loss of deduction.
- Assuming electoral bond purchases qualify: Note that from February 2024, the Electoral Bond Scheme has been struck down by the Supreme Court. Consult a chartered accountant on how past or any new political donations are treated.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. Is there any limit on how much I can donate under 80GGC in a financial year?**

There is no statutory ceiling on the amount you can donate or deduct. The entire donated amount, provided it is made through banking channels, is deductible under Section 80GGC.

**Q2. Can I claim 80GGC for a donation made to a party's welfare fund rather than directly to the party?**

No. The donation must be made directly to a registered political party or CBDT-approved electoral trust. Side funds, welfare trusts, or affiliated organizations do not qualify unless they are separately registered as electoral trusts.

**Q3. My partnership firm made a political donation. Can the firm claim 80GGC?**

Yes. Partnership firms and LLPs are eligible to claim deductions under Section 80GGC, provided the donation was made through an account payee cheque or electronic transfer.

**Q4. Does contributing to a crowdfunding campaign for a political candidate qualify?**

No. Crowdfunding contributions do not qualify for 80GGC. The donation must go directly to the political party or an electoral trust registered with CBDT.

**Q5. What documents should I keep for claiming 80GGC deduction?**

You should retain the official receipt issued by the political party or electoral trust, proof of payment (bank statement, NEFT receipt, or UTR number), and the party's registration certificate number for reference during any tax assessment.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Home Loan Tax Benefits: Everything You Need to Claim in 2024-25
articles.excerpt: 80C principal, Section 24(b) interest, 80EE/80EEA, eligibility, deduction caps, joint loans, optimization, mistakes, and FAQs.
articles.content:
# Home Loan Tax Benefits: Everything You Need to Claim in 2024-25

A home loan is not just a financial commitment; it is also one of the most generous tax-saving tools available to Indian taxpayers. Between the principal repayment, the interest component, and additional provisions for first-time buyers, you could potentially claim deductions of Rs. 3.5 lakh or more every year. But most borrowers leave significant money on the table simply because they don't understand how to combine these sections strategically.

## 1. Overview

Home loan tax benefits in India are spread across multiple sections of the Income Tax Act, 1961. The primary ones are Section 80C (for principal repayment), Section 24(b) (for interest paid), and Section 80EEA (for first-time homebuyers on affordable housing). Each section has its own conditions, limits, and eligibility criteria.

Crucially, most of these benefits are available only under the Old Tax Regime. Taxpayers who have opted for the New Regime lose access to 24(b) and 80EEA, though the 80C deduction (which includes principal repayment) is also unavailable under the New Regime.

## 2. Eligibility

For Section 24(b) interest deduction:

The property must be self-occupied or let out

Loan must be taken for purchase, construction, repair, or renovation

Loan should be taken from a recognized lender (bank, HFC, cooperative society, government)

For Section 80C principal deduction:

Property must not be sold within 5 years of possession

Loan must be for purchase or construction of a residential property

For Section 80EEA (first-time homebuyer):

Stamp duty value of the property must be Rs. 45 lakh or less

No other residential property must be owned on the date of loan sanction

Loan must have been sanctioned between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022

## 3. Deduction Limits (Summary Table)



| Section | What It Covers | Max Deduction (Rs.) | Condition |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 80C | Principal repayment | 1,50,000 (within 80C limit) | Property not sold within 5 years |
| 24(b) - Self-Occupied | Interest paid on home loan | 2,00,000 | Loan for purchase/construction |
| 24(b) - Let-Out Property | Interest paid on rented property | No limit (full interest deductible) | Losses can be set off against salary up to Rs. 2L |
| 80EEA | Additional interest (first-time buyer) | 1,50,000 (over 24b limit) | Stamp duty value up to Rs. 45L; loan before Mar 2022 |
| 80EE | Interest for first-time buyer (older scheme) | 50,000 | Loan sanctioned Apr 2016 - Mar 2017; property value up to Rs. 50L |

## 4. Example Calculation

Scenario: A Salaried Employee With a Self-Occupied Flat

Meena bought a flat in Mumbai for Rs. 68 lakh with a Rs. 55 lakh home loan at 8.5% p.a. over 20 years. Her EMI is approximately Rs. 47,800 per month.

| Line item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Total EMI paid in Year 1 | 5,73,600 |
| Principal component (approx.) | 90,000 |
| Interest component (approx.) | 4,63,000 |
| Deduction claimed under 80C (principal) | 90,000 |
| Deduction claimed under 24(b) (interest, capped) | 2,00,000 |
| Total Deduction from Home Loan | 2,90,000 |
| Tax saved at 30% slab (on Rs. 2.9L) | 90,480 (incl. cess) |

Notice that although Meena actually paid Rs. 4.63 lakh in interest, she can only deduct Rs. 2 lakh under 24(b) for a self-occupied property. The remaining Rs. 2.63 lakh of interest is not deductible.

## 5. Optimization Tips

- Opt for a joint loan with your spouse: Co-borrowers who are also co-owners can each claim up to Rs. 2 lakh under 24(b) and Rs. 1.5 lakh under 80C independently, effectively doubling the household tax savings.
- Let out a second property: Interest on a let-out property has no cap under 24(b). This is useful if you have multiple properties since you can deduct the full interest paid.
- Pre-construction interest: Interest paid during the construction phase can be deducted in five equal installments starting from the year the construction is completed.
- Use 80EEA if eligible: If you are a first-time buyer who took a loan before March 31, 2022, check whether your property qualifies. This section adds Rs. 1.5 lakh more in deductions on top of 24(b).
- Do not sell within 5 years: If you sell the property before 5 years, the 80C deductions claimed on principal repayment are reversed and added back to your income in the year of sale.

## 6. Mistakes to Avoid

- Assuming full interest is always deductible: For self-occupied property, the 24(b) deduction is capped at Rs. 2 lakh. Many borrowers believe they can deduct the full interest paid, which is not the case.
- Forgetting pre-possession interest: Taxpayers often fail to track interest paid before the property was handed over, which qualifies for a 5-year phased deduction.
- Claiming as co-borrower without co-ownership: If you are a co-borrower on the loan but not a co-owner of the property, you cannot claim tax benefits. Ownership is mandatory.
- Confusing 80EE and 80EEA: These are different sections with different loan sanction periods and property value limits. Verify which one you are eligible for before filing.
- Not submitting interest certificate to employer: For salaried employees, the home loan interest certificate must be submitted to the employer each year to reflect TDS adjustments. Forgetting to do this means excess TDS deduction throughout the year.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. Can I claim both 80C and 24(b) simultaneously for the same home loan?**

Absolutely. 80C covers the principal repayment portion and 24(b) covers the interest portion. They are independent deductions and can be claimed together in the same financial year for the same loan.

**Q2. My flat is still under construction. Can I claim any tax deduction this year?**

Not yet for 24(b) deductions. However, you can begin tracking the interest paid during construction. Once possession is received, that cumulative interest gets deducted in five equal yearly installments.

**Q3. What happens to my home loan tax benefits if I rent out the property?**

If the property is rented out, the 24(b) interest deduction is unlimited (no Rs. 2 lakh cap). However, the rental income must be disclosed under 'Income from House Property', and losses can offset salary income up to Rs. 2 lakh per year.

**Q4. Does a loan taken from a friend or relative for home purchase qualify for 24(b)?**

Only partially. The interest paid on such a loan qualifies for 24(b) deduction, but the lender must charge actual interest and you need a certificate from them. Principal repayment on such informal loans does NOT qualify for 80C.

**Q5. I have two home loans simultaneously. Can I claim benefits on both?**

Yes. You can claim 24(b) for both properties, but if both are self-occupied, the combined interest deduction is still limited to Rs. 2 lakh. If one is let out, its full interest is deductible. The 80C principal deduction applies to both, within the overall Rs. 1.5 lakh cap.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Personal Loan Tax Benefits: When and How You Can Actually Save Tax
articles.excerpt: End-use based deductibility: business, renovation, education (80E), documentation, scenarios, perquisites, and FAQs.
articles.content:
# Personal Loan Tax Benefits: When and How You Can Actually Save Tax

Personal loans do not come with automatic tax benefits. Unlike home loans, there is no dedicated section of the Income Tax Act that grants a blanket deduction for personal loan repayment. However, that does not mean personal loans are always tax-neutral. The purpose for which you use the loan determines whether any deduction is available, and in certain scenarios, the interest paid is fully deductible.

## 1. Overview

A personal loan from a bank or NBFC is an unsecured credit product. The government does not provide tax relief on personal borrowings by default because such loans are used for personal consumption rather than economic productivity or asset creation. However, if you deploy the personal loan proceeds for specific purposes such as home renovation, business investment, or education, the interest paid on such loans may become deductible under the relevant sections.

The key principle: it is not the type of loan that matters for taxation; it is the end use of the borrowed funds.

## 2. Eligibility

Tax benefits on personal loan interest arise when:

Purpose of Loan

Applicable Section

Who Can Claim

Purchase, construction, or renovation of a residential property

Section 24(b)

Individual property owner

Business investment or working capital

Business expense deduction (P&L)

Business owners, self-employed professionals

Higher education (self, spouse, or children)

Section 80E

Individual taking loan for education

Purchase of shares, mutual funds for income generation

Interest cost as capital cost / investment expense

Investors

Consumer spending, travel, weddings

No deduction

Not eligible

## 3. Scenarios and Deduction Limits

| Scenario | Deductible Component | Maximum Deduction |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Personal loan for home renovation | Interest paid | Rs. 30,000 (minor repair) / Rs. 2,00,000 (construction) |
| Personal loan for business use | Full interest as business expense | Actual interest (no cap if genuine business use) |
| Personal loan for higher education | Full interest under 80E | No cap; available for 8 years from first repayment |
| Personal loan for stock market investment | Interest may offset investment income | Depends on quantum and nature of income generated |
| Personal loan for vacation or wedding | None | Zero deduction |



## 4. Example Calculations

Scenario A: Personal Loan Used for Home Renovation

Kiran takes a personal loan of Rs. 8 lakh at 14% interest for renovating her owned flat. She pays Rs. 1.12 lakh in interest in FY 2024-25.

Under Section 24(b), the renovation-related interest for a self-occupied property is deductible up to Rs. 30,000. If the renovation is treated as capital improvement for a let-out property, she may deduct the full Rs. 1.12 lakh.

| Item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Personal loan interest paid in year | 1,12,000 |
| Deduction allowed for self-occupied property (renovation) | 30,000 |
| Deduction allowed if property is let out | 1,12,000 |
| Tax saved (30% slab, let-out property) | 34,944 |
| Scenario B: Personal Loan for Business Expansion | Suresh, a proprietor of a garment business, borrows Rs. 5 lakh via personal loan to buy inventory during peak season. He pays Rs. 70,000 in interest during the year. Since the loan was genuinely used for business, this Rs. 70,000 is fully deductible as a business expense under the head 'Profits and Gains from Business or Profession'. Tax saved at 30% slab: Rs. 21,840. |



## 5. Optimization Tips

- Maintain clear documentation of end use: The only way to justify tax deductions on personal loan interest is to prove how the money was used. Keep receipts, invoices, and bank trails.
- Prefer an education loan over personal loan for studies: Education loans taken specifically for higher education already qualify for 80E with full interest deduction. A personal loan used for education may be harder to substantiate.
- Route business-use loans through a business account: If using a personal loan for business, transfer the funds into your business account and make purchases from there. This creates an audit trail.
- Inform your CA about the end use: Many taxpayers declare personal loan repayments but do not tell their CA what the loan was used for. The deduction possibility depends entirely on usage.
- Consider home improvement loans over personal loans: If your purpose is home renovation, a dedicated home improvement loan has more clearly defined tax treatment under 24(b) compared to a generic personal loan.

## 6. Mistakes to Avoid

- Assuming personal loan interest is always deductible: This is the most prevalent misconception. Loan interest is only deductible when the purpose meets the criteria of a recognized section.
- Mixing personal and business loan proceeds: If you take a personal loan for business but also use part of the funds for personal expenses, only the proportionate business-use interest is deductible.
- Claiming deduction on principal repayment: Personal loan principal repayments are never deductible, even if the loan was used for qualifying purposes. Only interest qualifies.
- Not substantiating use during ITR filing: Claiming deduction without documentation is risky. The tax department can ask for proof during scrutiny, and unsupported claims lead to additions to income.
- Treating consumer durable loans as business expenses: Buying a laptop or phone on a personal loan for 'business' use requires you to actually use it predominantly for business and depreciate it accordingly.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. I took a personal loan to buy gold for my daughter's wedding. Can I claim any deduction?**

No. Personal loans taken for weddings, gold purchases, or personal gifts do not qualify for any tax deduction under the Income Tax Act. The interest paid is a personal expense with no tax relief.

**Q2. Can I claim a deduction on my personal loan if it was used to pay for higher education abroad?**

It depends on the structure. A formal education loan taken from a recognized financial institution for higher education (including abroad) qualifies for Section 80E. A generic personal loan used informally for education may be harder to classify, though the purpose argument can still hold if documented well.

**Q3. I am self-employed and took a personal loan to pay my employees' salaries during a cash crunch. Can I deduct the interest?**

Yes. If you can demonstrate that the loan proceeds were used to meet legitimate business obligations such as salary payments, the interest is deductible as a business expense under Section 37 of the Income Tax Act.

**Q4. What proof should I maintain to claim tax benefits on a personal loan used for investment?**

Keep your loan agreement, bank statement showing the transfer to an investment account (demat account or mutual fund folio), investment statements, and a written note to your CA explaining the connection between the loan and the investment.

**Q5. My employer offers a personal loan at a subsidized interest rate. Is the interest benefit taxed as a perquisite?**

Yes. If the interest rate charged by your employer is below the Reserve Bank of India's prescribed rate (or the SBI prime lending rate for specific loan types), the difference is treated as a taxable perquisite under Section 17(2) and forms part of your salary income.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Children Education Allowance: Tax Benefits for Parents in India
articles.excerpt: CEA & hostel allowance under 10(14), tuition fees under 80C, education loan interest under 80E, limits, example couple, tips, and FAQs.
articles.content:
# Children Education Allowance: Tax Benefits for Parents in India

Education is among the highest expenses a family bears, and the tax code offers several ways to ease this burden. Whether it is an allowance from your employer, reimbursements for school fees, tuition fees paid directly, or a formal education loan, different parts of the Income Tax Act address different aspects of education spending. Understanding how these interact can save a working parent several thousand rupees each year.

## 1. Overview

Tax benefits related to children's education in India span three primary areas. First, there is the Children Education Allowance (CEA) and Hostel Allowance that employers pay as part of CTC, which are partially exempt from tax under Section 10(14). Second, tuition fees paid for children's education are deductible under Section 80C within the Rs. 1.5 lakh ceiling. Third, education loans taken for higher education qualify for full interest deduction under Section 80E.

## 2. Eligibility

Children Education Allowance under Section 10(14)

Available to salaried employees receiving this allowance from their employer

Covers up to two children per employee

Applicable for any stage of education including schooling

Tuition Fee Deduction under Section 80C

Available to both salaried and self-employed individuals under the Old Tax Regime

Applicable only for full-time education at recognized universities, colleges, or schools in India

Covers up to two children per parent

Foreign institution fees do not qualify

Education Loan Interest under Section 80E

Available to individuals (not HUFs) who have taken loans from banks or approved institutions for their own or their dependents' higher education

Covers higher education undertaken anywhere in the world

No cap on the loan amount or the interest deduction

## 3. Deduction Limits

| Benefit Type | Section | Exemption / Deduction Limit | Number of Children Covered |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Children Education Allowance (CEA) | 10(14) | Rs. 100 per month per child (Rs. 1,200/year) | Up to 2 children |
| Hostel Expenditure Allowance | 10(14) | Rs. 300 per month per child (Rs. 3,600/year) | Up to 2 children |
| Tuition Fees (school/college) | 80C | Actual fees, subject to Rs. 1.5 lakh 80C ceiling | Up to 2 children |
| Education Loan Interest (higher education) | 80E | 100% of interest paid; no ceiling | Self, spouse, children, students under legal guardianship |

Note: The CEA rate of Rs. 100/month was set decades ago and has not been revised. Government employees, however, receive a much higher revised CEA rate of Rs. 2,250 per month per child (as per the 7th Pay Commission revision).

## 4. Example Calculation

Scenario: A Working Couple With Two School-Going Children

Ramesh works at a private company and his wife Sona is a salaried professional. They have two children in school and pay Rs. 1.2 lakh per year in tuition fees for both combined. Ramesh has also taken an education loan of Rs. 15 lakh for his elder child's engineering college, on which he pays Rs. 1.3 lakh in interest during FY 2024-25.

| Line item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Tuition fees under 80C | 1,20,000 |
| Education loan interest under 80E | 1,30,000 |
| Total deduction | 2,50,000 |
| Tax saved at 20% slab (tuition under 80C) | 24,960 |
| Tax saved at 20% slab (80E interest) | 27,040 |
| Total tax saved | 52,000 (approx.) |

Additionally, Sona can claim tuition fees for both children under her own 80C filing if the fees are separately apportioned between the two parents. Each can claim up to 2 children's fees independently.

## 5. Optimization Tips

- Split tuition fees between both parents: If both spouses are working, they can each claim the tuition fees of one child under 80C. This keeps both 80C limits unaffected.
- Maximize 80E for the entire interest amount: Section 80E has no upper limit. Even if your child's education loan carries Rs. 3 lakh in interest, all of it is deductible. Do not under-report this.
- Use 80E for eight consecutive years: The 80E deduction is available from the year repayment begins and for the next seven years (eight years total). Plan loan repayment accordingly.
- Negotiate CEA from employer: While the statutory exempt limit is low (Rs. 100/month), check if your employer pays a higher allowance as part of the CTC. Even the Rs. 100 is better than nothing.
- Combine with NPS for holistic planning: If your 80C is already full with EPF and other items, prioritize 80E (no limit) and 80CCD(1B) NPS contributions for additional deductions.

## 6. Mistakes to Avoid

- Claiming fees for a third child: Both 80C tuition fees and 10(14) CEA are capped at two children. Even if you have three or more children, the deduction does not extend further.
- Including admission fees, development fees, or donations: Under 80C, only tuition fees are deductible. Development fees, school building funds, lab fees, and donations to the institution are excluded.
- Claiming foreign school fees under 80C: Fees paid to foreign schools or universities do not qualify for 80C. Section 80E covers the loan interest for foreign higher education, but not 80C tuition deductions.
- Confusing 80C tuition fees with CEA: These are separate benefits. Tuition fees are a deduction from taxable income under 80C. CEA is an employer allowance that is exempt up to a small threshold. They do not overlap.
- Missing the 80E window: Section 80E is available only for 8 years from the year repayment starts. If you miss filing during this period, you lose the benefit permanently.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. My child goes to an international school in India. Do the fees qualify for Section 80C?**

Yes, as long as the school is located in India and is recognized by the relevant Indian educational authority. The nationality or curriculum (CBSE, IB, IGCSE) does not disqualify it.

**Q2. Can I claim an 80E deduction for an education loan taken in my child's name?**

Section 80E allows the parent (or legal guardian) to claim the deduction for loans taken for the child's higher education. However, if the loan is in the child's name and they are repaying it themselves after becoming financially independent, the deduction must be claimed by the child in their own tax return.

**Q3. Can both husband and wife claim 80E for the same child's education loan?**

No. Only one parent can be the primary borrower for a particular loan, and only that person can claim the 80E deduction. If both parents are co-borrowers, typically only one can claim the benefit, and it should be declared consistently.

**Q4. I paid Rs. 2 lakh in school fees but my 80C is already full with EPF. Can I still claim tuition fees?**

Unfortunately, no. The 80C ceiling is Rs. 1,50,000 in aggregate. If your EPF, insurance premiums, and other 80C items already fill this quota, tuition fees will not provide an additional deduction. The only workaround is to plan other 80C items to make room.

**Q5. Does the education loan need to come from a bank, or will a credit union or fintech lender qualify for 80E?**

Section 80E specifies that the loan must be from a financial institution (a banking company or any institution notified by the Central Government) or an approved charitable institution. Loans from fintechs that are not recognized banks or NBFCs approved by the government may not qualify. Verify the lender's eligibility before filing.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Health Security to National Security Cess Act 2026: Everything You Need to Know
articles.excerpt: Union Budget 2026 HSNSC cess: rates for individuals and corporates, calculation, TDS and advance tax, exemptions, policy impact, and FAQs.
articles.content:
The Union Budget 2026 introduced a significant fiscal measure that has drawn attention from businesses, healthcare professionals, and policy analysts alike — the Health Security to National Security Cess (HSNSC) Act 2026. This cess represents the government's intent to channel dedicated revenue streams toward dual-purpose expenditures: strengthening India's public health infrastructure and bolstering national security frameworks. If you're a business owner, tax professional, or simply a taxpayer trying to understand how this impacts your finances, this guide breaks it all down clearly.

## What Is the Health Security to National Security Cess?

A cess in Indian taxation is a surcharge levied over and above the standard tax rate, earmarked for a specific purpose. Unlike general tax revenue that flows into the Consolidated Fund of India, cess revenue is credited to a separate fund and can only be used for the stated objective. The HSNSC Act 2026 creates a unified cess that pools funding for:

- Strengthening pandemic preparedness and bio-security infrastructure
- Funding cybersecurity and border security initiatives
- Supporting health emergency response mechanisms
- Upgrading critical national infrastructure at the intersection of health and defense
This dual mandate is what makes the cess unique — it explicitly recognizes that health security and national security are interlinked, particularly in a post-COVID world where biological threats have national security dimensions.

## Legal Framework and Enactment

The Act was passed by Parliament in the Budget Session 2026 and received Presidential assent. It amends the existing cess collection machinery under the Income Tax Act, 1961, and operates alongside other existing cesses such as:

- Health and Education Cess (4% on income tax)
- Swachh Bharat Cess (discontinued but precedent-setting)
The HSNSC Act 2026 creates a new dedicated fund under Article 270 of the Constitution, ensuring the proceeds are not divisible with states under the Finance Commission formula — a point of contention with several state governments.

## Key Provisions at a Glance

| Provision | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Name of Cess | Health Security to National Security Cess |
| Applicable On | Net income tax liability (before rebates u/s 87A) |
| Rate of Cess | 1% (for individuals), 2% (for corporates and LLPs) |
| Effective From | April 1, 2026 (AY 2026–27 onwards) |
| Applicability | All taxpayers liable to pay income tax |
| Exemptions | Persons with total income below the basic exemption limit |
| Fund Created | National Health-Security Fund (NHSF) |
| Administration | Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) |
| TDS Impact | Yes — applicable on TDS calculations |
| Advance Tax | Included in advance tax installment calculations |

## Who Is Affected?

The cess touches virtually every category of taxpayer: 

### Individuals and HUFs

Resident individuals with taxable income above the basic exemption limit (₹3 lakh for those below 60 years, ₹3.5 lakh for senior citizens) will pay 1% of their income tax liability as HSNSC. 

### Corporates and LLPs

All domestic companies, foreign companies, and LLPs will pay 2% of their computed income tax liability. This includes companies opting for the concessional tax regime under Section 115BAA. 

### Partnership Firms

Firms (other than LLPs) pay 2% on their income tax liability. 

### Non-Resident Indians (NRIs)

NRIs earning income in India above the threshold are also covered. Their HSNSC is computed on income tax on Indian-sourced income.

## How Is the Cess Calculated?

The cess is applied on the net income tax payable — after deducting any applicable rebate under Section 87A, but before any MAT/AMT credit. Formula: HSNSC = Income Tax Payable × Cess Rate (1% or 2%) Total Tax = Income Tax + Health & Education Cess (4%) + HSNSC (1% or 2%) Note that the HSNSC is calculated on the base income tax, not on the Health & Education Cess amount. These are separate surcharges computed independently.

## Practical Example: Individual Taxpayer

Rahul, a salaried employee in Mumbai, has a taxable income of ₹12,00,000 in AY 2026–27.

| Component | Amount (₹) |
| --- | --- |
| Taxable Income | 12,00,000 |
| Income Tax (as per slab) | 1,00,000 |
| Health & Education Cess (4%) | 4,000 |
| HSNSC (1% on ₹1,00,000) | 1,000 |
| Total Tax Liability | 1,05,000 |

## Practical Example: Corporate Taxpayer

XYZ Pvt. Ltd. has a taxable income of ₹1 crore. It opts for the 22% tax rate under Section 115BAA.

| Component | Amount (₹) |
| --- | --- |
| Taxable Income | 1,00,00,000 |
| Income Tax @ 22% | 22,00,000 |
| Surcharge (if applicable) | 2,20,000 |
| Health & Education Cess (4%) | 88,800 |
| HSNSC (2% on ₹22,00,000) | 44,000 |
| Total Tax | 25,52,800 |

## Impact on TDS and Advance Tax

The cess is factored into: TDS Deductions: Employers must recalculate TDS (Form 16/16A) to include HSNSC. Payroll systems need to be updated accordingly. Failure to account for HSNSC in TDS leads to short deduction and consequent interest under Section 201. Advance Tax Installments: For both individuals and corporates making advance tax payments (due on 15 June, 15 September, 15 December, 15 March), the HSNSC must be included in each installment. Underpayment triggers interest under Sections 234B and 234C.

## Exceptions and Exemptions

| Category | HSNSC Applicable? |
| --- | --- |
| Income below basic exemption limit | No |
| Agricultural income (purely) | No |
| Incomes exempt u/s 10 (PF, gratuity, etc.) | No — exempted income doesn't form part of tax base |
| Cooperative societies under special regime | Subject to CBDT clarification |
| Section 10(23C) institutions (educational) | No, if income is fully exempt |
| Political parties u/s 13A | No |

## Policy Implications and Concerns

Revenue Potential: The government estimates the HSNSC will generate approximately ₹18,000–22,000 crore annually, which will be split roughly 60:40 between health security initiatives and national security infrastructure. State Resentment: Since cess revenue does not enter the divisible pool, states receive no share. Several states have filed formal representations arguing this undermines cooperative fiscal federalism. Corporate Competitiveness: Industry bodies like FICCI and CII have raised concerns that the additional 2% cess increases India's effective corporate tax rate, potentially affecting FDI attractiveness. Compliance Burden: Every TDS deductor needs to update software, recalculate withholding, and file revised forms — a significant one-time administrative effort.

## FAQs

Q1. Is the HSNSC deductible as a business expense under the Income Tax Act? No. Like all other cesses and surcharges, HSNSC is not deductible as a business expense when computing taxable income. Section 40(a)(ii) explicitly disallows income tax and any surcharge or cess on income tax as a deduction. Q2. Will NRIs returning to India mid-year face double taxation on the HSNSC? The cess applies only on Indian-sourced income for NRIs. If an individual changes residential status mid-year, income is bifurcated — only the portion subject to Indian income tax attracts HSNSC. DTAA provisions do not typically cover cess, so there's no treaty relief available for this component. Q3. Does the HSNSC apply to income computed under the presumptive taxation scheme (Section 44AD/44ADA)? Yes. Even under presumptive schemes, the income tax computed on deemed income is subject to HSNSC at the applicable rate. The presumptive scheme only simplifies income computation — it doesn't alter the cess liability.

Q4. How will the National Health-Security Fund actually use the collected funds — is there an oversight mechanism? The Act mandates annual reporting to Parliament through a dedicated statement under the Finance Act. An inter-ministerial committee co-chaired by the Health Secretary and the National Security Advisor oversees fund allocation. However, critics note the absence of independent audit provisions beyond CAG's routine remit. Q5. If a company has a zero tax liability due to MAT credit utilization, does HSNSC still apply? Where MAT credit fully offsets regular income tax, and the company's final tax payable is nil, HSNSC would also be nil. However, if only partial credit is utilized and some income tax remains payable, HSNSC is computed on that residual amount.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Handling & Rain Fees: Taxability, Rate, and Compliance Guide
articles.excerpt: GST on handling and rain fees: SAC rates, GTA vs port trust, composite supply, RCM, ITC, port examples, and FAQs.
articles.content:
In the logistics and warehousing sector, ancillary charges like handling fees and rain fees often create significant confusion when it comes to GST treatment. These are neither pure transport charges nor standard service fees — they occupy a grey zone that has been the subject of advance rulings, trade circulars, and litigation. This article cuts through the ambiguity.

## Understanding Handling Fees and Rain Fees

Before diving into GST, let's establish what these charges actually are: Handling Fee: A charge levied by warehouse operators, port authorities, freight forwarders, or customs house agents (CHAs) for physically loading, unloading, stacking, sorting, or otherwise managing cargo. It covers the labor, equipment, and operational cost of physically moving goods. Rain Fee (also called Inclement Weather Fee / Monsoon Surcharge): A surcharge levied by ports, logistics companies, or freight operators when cargo handling is disrupted due to adverse weather — particularly monsoons or cyclone activity. It compensates for delays, additional protective measures (tarpaulins, covered sheds), and labor overtime during rainfall.

## The GST Framework Applicable

Under GST, both handling fees and rain fees are classified as supply of services. The applicable provisions depend on:

- Who is the service provider (GTA, registered logistics company, port trust, etc.)
- Who is the recipient (B2B or B2C)
- Whether the services qualify for any exemption under Notification No. 12/2017-CT
(Rate) or 9/2017-IT (Rate)

## GST Rate Applicability

| Type of Charge | HSN/SAC Code | GST Rate | Remarks |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cargo handling services | 9967 | 18% | Standard rate; no exemption |
| Port handling (by port trusts/major ports) | 9967 | 18% | Major ports are GST registered entities |
| Rain fee (by logistics company) | 9967 | 18% | Treated as ancillary/incidental to handling |
| Rain fee (by GTA) | 9965 | 5% (RCM) or 12% | Depends on GTA vs. ancillary charge classification |
| Stevedoring charges | 9967 | 18% | Loading/unloading at port |
| Wharfage fees | 9967 | 18% | Levied by port authorities |

Key Point: If the rain fee is billed as a separate line item on an invoice distinct from freight, it is independently taxed at 18%. If bundled with freight in a GTA invoice, the character of the principal supply (freight at 5%) may apply — but this requires careful documentation.

## The Composite Supply vs. Mixed Supply Question

This is where most disputes arise. Scenario A — Bundled in GTA Invoice: A goods transport agency charges ₹50,000 freight + ₹2,000 rain fee on the same invoice. If the GTA treats this as a composite supply where freight is the principal supply, the entire amount including the rain fee may attract GST at 5% under RCM (on the freight portion). This interpretation is favorable for shippers. Scenario B — Separate Invoice from Handling Agent: A logistics company issues a separate invoice for ₹2,000 as "Rain Fee / Inclement Weather Surcharge." Here, there is no composite supply argument — it's an independent taxable service under SAC 9967 attracting 18% GST. Scenario C — Port Handling with Rain Fee: Where a port trust issues a consolidated tariff that includes all handling charges plus weather surcharges, the 18% rate applies to the aggregate under the port's standard billing.

## Who Pays? Forward Charge vs. Reverse Charge

| Supplier Category | Mechanism | Who Pays GST |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Registered logistics company | Forward Charge | Supplier collects and deposits |
| GTA (freight + ancillary) | Reverse Charge (RCM) | Recipient (registered business) pays |
| Port trust / major port authority | Forward Charge | Port trust collects and deposits |
| Unregistered small logistics operator | RCM (if applicable) | Recipient registered under GST |

Under Notification No. 13/2017-CT (Rate), services by a GTA to a registered person attract RCM. If the rain fee is part of the GTA's composite charge, the recipient handles the GST under RCM.

## Practical Example 1: Importer at Mumbai Port

SteelCorp India Ltd. imports a shipment of stainless steel coils at JNPT. The charges breakdown: Charge Amount (₹) GST Rate GST (₹) Freight (GTA, ocean leg) 3,00,000 5% (RCM) 15,000 Port handling/stevedoring 40,000 18% 7,200 Rain fee (separate invoice) 5,000 18% 900 CHA professional fee 15,000 18% 2,700 Total GST

25,800 SteelCorp pays the port handling and rain fee GST directly to the service providers. For the GTA freight, it self-assesses and deposits under RCM. All of this is eligible for ITC (being used in furtherance of business).

## Practical Example 2: Agricultural Commodity Warehouse

FreshGrain Pvt. Ltd. stores paddy at a licensed warehouse. During the monsoon, the warehouse charges a rain fee for additional covering and moisture management. Here, if the warehouse is storing agricultural produce, the storage service may be exempt under Entry 54 of Notification 12/2017-CT (Rate). However, the rain fee as an independent logistical surcharge is not automatically exempt. The warehouse must evaluate:

- Is the rain fee integral to the storage (and thus covered under the exemption)?
- Or is it a separate incidental handling charge (taxable at 18%)?
Most advance rulings have held that surcharges beyond the principal exempt supply are independently taxable unless they are genuinely inseparable.

## ITC Eligibility

For businesses receiving handling and rain fee invoices, the Input Tax Credit (ITC) on 18% GST is generally available provided:

- The services are used in the course or furtherance of business
- The recipient is registered under GST
- Tax has been paid by the supplier and GSTR-2B reflects the credit
- The goods/services are not covered under Section 17(5) (blocked credits)
Importers, manufacturers, and traders can freely claim ITC on handling and rain fee GST.

## Common Errors and Compliance Issues

Wrong SAC Code: Many logistics companies erroneously use SAC 9965 (transport) instead of 9967 (cargo handling). This creates ITC mismatches. GTA Misclassification: Treating a logistics company as a GTA when it doesn't own or operate trucks — this leads to incorrect RCM application. No GST on Rain Fee: Some operators treat rain fees as a reimbursement rather than a service charge and skip GST entirely. This is incorrect unless it's a genuine cost-reimbursement with no margin. Export Transactions: For export cargo, handling at ports may qualify as a zero-rated supply (even if charged in INR) if it is an integral part of the export service chain. This requires specific documentation.

## Exceptions

- Pure labor supply contracts (where workers are contracted separately) may fall under
labor supply services at 18% but may have different classification nuances.

- Services by Port Trusts to vessels under specific exemption notifications may have
carve-outs.

- SEZ units receiving handling services may treat these as zero-rated inward supplies.
## FAQs

Q1. If a rain fee is charged by a foreign shipping line to an Indian importer, is GST applicable? Yes. Under the IGST Act, import of services is taxable. When an Indian registered importer receives a rain fee invoice from a foreign shipping line, it constitutes import of service. The importer must pay IGST under RCM (Section 5(3) of IGST Act read with Notification 10/2017-IT). The rate applicable would be 18%.

Q2. Can the handling fee be treated as part of the cost of goods for customs valuation purposes? Handling fees incurred after the goods land on Indian soil (post-importation) are not included in the customs assessable value. Only pre-importation costs (insurance, freight to Indian port) are included. Post-landing costs like stevedoring and rain fees are separate and attract only GST, not customs duty. Q3. In a warehousing agreement, if the rain fee clause is embedded as a contingency charge in the contract, must GST be charged even if the fee is waived one month? GST is payable on the actual consideration received. If the rain fee is waived for a specific month due to a commercial decision, no GST liability arises for that month. The waiver should be documented through a credit note or formal amendment to avoid scrutiny. Q4. Does the rain fee attract GST even if the consignment was damaged during rain — i.e., the fee was charged but service wasn't fully rendered? Yes, GST liability arises at the time of supply (invoice issuance or payment, whichever is earlier). The fact that the service outcome was poor doesn't negate the GST. However, if the business disputes the charge and it is reduced through negotiation, a credit note can be issued and the tax liability adjusted accordingly. Q5. How should e-commerce operators (ECOs) treat rain fees paid to third-party logistics partners through their platform? ECOs acting as aggregators for logistics services must examine whether they're liable to collect GST under Section 9(5) of the CGST Act. If the rain fee is part of the logistics service facilitated by the ECO, the TCS provisions and the ECO's own liability must be carefully evaluated. Many ECOs currently exclude ancillary charges from TCS computation — this is a legally unresolved area and businesses should seek specific advance rulings.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST Rate Cut from 18% to 5%: Understanding ITC Reversal Under Section 18(4)
articles.excerpt: Section 18(4) ITC reversal when GST rates drop: Rule 44, ITC-03, stock examples, multi-GSTIN, services, and FAQs.
articles.content:
When the GST Council reduces the tax rate on a supply — say from 18% to 5% — it's not purely good news for businesses that hold stock. The rate cut comes with a specific obligation: reversal of Input Tax Credit (ITC) already availed on inputs held in stock, work-in-progress, or capital goods. This is governed by Section 18(4) of the CGST Act, 2017.

This is one of the more complex and under-discussed provisions in GST law. Many businesses inadvertently overlook this requirement, leading to demands, penalties, and interest during audits.

## Why Does a Rate Cut Trigger ITC Reversal?

The logic is straightforward, even if the compliance is not. When goods attract 18% GST, a manufacturer or trader avails ITC on raw materials, components, and inputs — all purchased at 18%. This ITC offsets their output liability. The system is balanced. When the GST rate drops to 5%, the output tax on future sales drops dramatically. But the business has already accumulated ITC on older stock at the higher rate. If this ITC is not reversed, the business would effectively get a windfall — crediting itself more ITC than the output tax it will ever generate from that stock. Section 18(4) prevents this distortion.

## The Legal Provision: Section 18(4) Explained

Section 18(4) of the CGST Act provides: "Where any registered person who has availed of input tax credit opts to pay tax under Section 10 (composition scheme) or where the goods or services or both supplied by him become wholly exempt, he shall pay an amount, by way of debit in the electronic credit ledger or electronic cash ledger, equivalent to the credit of input tax in respect of inputs held in stock and inputs contained in semi-finished or finished goods held in stock..." The provision also applies by extension through Section 18(6) read with Rule 44 when a rate reduction effectively changes the tax treatment substantially. CBDT/GSTN clarifications have confirmed this applicability in the context of rate cuts from the taxable bracket to a lower slab. Rule 44 of the CGST Rules specifies the methodology for computing the reversal amount.

## When Does Section 18(4) Apply?

| Trigger Event | Section 18(4) Applies? |
| --- | --- |
| Goods become fully exempt from GST | Yes — full ITC reversal |
| GST rate reduced from 18% to 5% | Yes — proportionate/specific reversal required |
| Business switches to Composition Scheme | Yes — full ITC reversal of stock on hand |
| Rate reduced only on specific variants (not all goods) | Partial — only on the goods affected |
| Rate increased from 5% to 12% | No — ITC reversal not triggered; rate increase doesn't require reversal |
| Business cancels GST registration | Yes — full ITC reversal on closing stock |

## Computing the Reversal: Rule 44 Methodology

For Inputs (Raw Materials, Components): Reversal = ITC availed on such inputs proportionate to the extent they form part of stock held on the date of rate change. For Capital Goods: Residual ITC = (Original ITC × remaining useful life in months) ÷ 60 The reversal is typically computed on a last-purchase-cost basis.

## Step-by-Step Example

Situation: Paints manufacturer ABC Ltd. previously sold paints at 18% GST. The GST Council reduces the rate to 5% effective November 1, 2025. On October 31, 2025 (date of rate change), ABC Ltd. holds: Item Cost (₹) ITC Availed (18%) (₹)

Raw material (pigments, solvents) 10,00,00 0 1,80,000 Work-in-progress (partially processed) 5,00,000 90,000 Finished goods (paint tins ready to sell) 8,00,000 1,44,000 Total ITC available on stock

4,14,000 After the rate cut, the output GST on sales from this stock will only be 5%. The ITC of ₹4,14,000 availed was based on an 18% regime. Section 18(4) requires ABC Ltd. to reverse the excess credit. Reversal computation: The ITC that would have been availed at the new rate of 5% on the same purchases: Item Cost (₹) Revised ITC (5%) (₹) Excess to Reverse (₹) Raw material 10,00,00 0 50,000 1,30,000 WIP 5,00,000 25,000 65,000 Finished goods 8,00,000 40,000 1,04,000 Total

1,15,000 2,99,000 ABC Ltd. must debit its electronic credit ledger by ₹2,99,000 and file ITC-03 (or the applicable form) declaring this reversal.

## Form and Compliance Procedure

Step Action Required Step 1 Take stock count on the date immediately before the rate cut takes effect Step 2 Compute ITC reversal using Rule 44 methodology Step 3 File Form GST ITC-03 declaring the reversal Step 4 Debit electronic credit ledger (or pay via cash ledger if credit is insufficient) Step 5 Time limit — within 30 days of rate change or as notified by GSTN Failure to file ITC-03 on time attracts interest under Section 50 on the amount that should have been reversed, from the date of the rate change until actual reversal.

## Who Is Affected?

Manufacturers: Most directly affected because they hold the largest volumes of inputs and WIP. Traders and Distributors: Must reverse ITC on finished goods stock purchased at higher rates. E-commerce Sellers: Must carefully track SKU-level stock for affected products. Importers: Where imported goods face rate reduction, the IGST ITC availed on imports must also be evaluated for reversal.

## Practical Challenges

FIFO vs. Weighted Average: The law does not prescribe a specific costing method. Most businesses use the weighted average cost for ITC reversal. GSTN has not definitively clarified this. Using FIFO may be more accurate for businesses with clear lot-wise tracking. Capital Goods in Use: For machinery used to produce goods at 18% that now become 5% goods, the residual ITC on capital goods over their useful life must be proportionally reversed. This gets complex for multi-product manufacturers. Credit Ledger Insufficient: If the electronic credit ledger doesn't have enough balance for the reversal, the shortfall must be paid from the electronic cash ledger. This is a genuine cash flow concern for businesses.

## Exceptions — When Reversal May Be Reduced or Avoided

- Where goods were purchased under Bill of Supply (unregistered supplier,
composition supplier) — no ITC was availed, so nothing to reverse.

- Zero-rated supplies: Exporters may retain ITC even after rate changes since their
supply remains zero-rated.

- If the inputs were already used before the rate change (not lying in stock), no
reversal is required on those inputs.

- Transitional stock purchased after the rate change announcement (at the new rate)
naturally carries only 5% ITC — no reversal needed.

## FAQs

Q1. What if a business wasn't aware of the rate cut and didn't file ITC-03 within 30 days — is there a condonation mechanism? Currently, the CGST Act does not provide a formal condonation provision for late filing of ITC-03. However, businesses can approach the jurisdictional GST officer with a voluntary disclosure, pay the interest under Section 50, and regularize the position. Some High Courts have granted relief in bonafide cases, but this involves litigation risk. Proactive compliance is strongly recommended. Q2. If a business has multiple GST registrations (different states), does each GSTIN need to file separate ITC-03 for stock held in that state? Yes. GST registration is state-specific, and each GSTIN is an independent taxable person under GST law. Stock held in each state's warehouse must be assessed separately, and ITC-03 must be filed under each GSTIN for the stock physically located in that state. Q3. For a rate cut from 18% to 5%, is the reversal computed on the tax amount embedded in the purchase price, or on the selling price? The reversal is computed on the ITC already availed — which is based on the purchase price (inward supply value). The selling price is irrelevant for this calculation. You're reversing what you claimed as credit on inward supplies, not computing tax on future outward sales.

Q4. Does a trader who purchased goods at 18% GST and sold them before the rate change, but still holds the invoice in his books, need to reverse anything? No. Only stock physically held on the date of rate change requires ITC reversal. If goods were sold before the effective date of rate reduction, the transaction is complete and the ITC availed was validly used against 18% output tax at the time of sale. There's nothing to reverse for goods already sold. Q5. In case of rate reduction for a service (say, from 18% to 5% on restaurant services), does Section 18(4) apply for ITC reversal on furniture or kitchen equipment? Services generally don't involve "stock in hand" in the traditional sense, but capital goods (furniture, equipment) are affected. The residual ITC on such capital goods must be reversed proportionally based on remaining useful life, computed under Rule 44(6). Restaurant owners switching to a lower rate regime must carefully calculate this, especially for recently purchased kitchen equipment.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST Exemption on Sale and Purchase of Securities: A Complete Analysis
articles.excerpt: Securities and GST: Schedule III, taxable ancillary services, STT vs GST, brokerage ITC, sector notes, and FAQs.
articles.content:
The Indian financial markets involve trillions of rupees in transactions daily — equity shares, government bonds, derivatives, mutual fund units, and more. A commonly misunderstood aspect of GST is the complete exemption on the sale and purchase of securities. While the exemption itself is clear in statute, the nuances around what qualifies as a security, what services around securities are taxable, and how brokerage and ancillary fees are treated require careful understanding.

## The Statutory Exemption: Where Does It Come From?

Under the CGST Act, 2017, the definition of "goods" under Section 2(52) explicitly excludes actionable claims and money. Securities are a unique category — they are neither goods nor services under GST. More specifically:

- Section 2(101) defines "securities" to have the meaning assigned under the
Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (SCRA)

- Schedule III of the CGST Act lists activities that are neither supply of goods nor
supply of services — thereby keeping them entirely outside the GST framework Schedule III, Entry 6 reads: "Sale of land and, subject to clause (b) of paragraph 5 of Schedule II, sale of building." And crucially, Entry 7 covers transactions in money and actionable claims — within which securities fall by virtue of being treated as actionable claims in specific contexts and through the explicit carve-outs. Additionally, Notification No. 12/2017-CT (Rate), Entry 44 specifically exempts services related to buying/selling of securities on a recognized stock exchange by a member — though this applies to brokerage in specific conditions.

## What Qualifies as "Securities" for GST Exemption?

Under Section 2(h) of SCRA, securities include:

| Type of Security | GST on Transaction? |
| --- | --- |
| Listed equity shares (NSE/BSE) | Exempt |
| Unlisted equity shares | Exempt |
| Government securities (G-Secs, T-Bills) | Exempt |
| Corporate bonds and debentures | Exempt |
| Mutual fund units | Exempt (transaction; but management fees taxable) |
| Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) | Exempt |
| Derivatives (futures and options) | Exempt on underlying; services taxable |
| Rights and warrants | Exempt |
| Preference shares | Exempt |
| Commercial paper / certificate of deposit | Exempt |

The exemption covers the transaction value (the consideration for the security itself). It does NOT cover service charges, brokerage, advisory fees, or management fees that accompany these transactions.

## What IS Taxable: Services Around Securities

This is where many investors and businesses get confused. While the sale/purchase of securities is outside GST, the services facilitating these transactions are very much taxable.

| Service | GST Rate | Provider |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Stock brokerage | 18% | Broker |
| Demat account maintenance charges | 18% | Depository participant |
| Portfolio management fee | 18% | PMS manager |
| Mutual fund management fee (expense ratio) | 18% | AMC |
| Investment advisory fee | 18% | SEBI-registered IA |
| Custodian services | 18% | Custodian |
| Clearing charges | 18% | Clearing corporation |
| Exchange transaction charges | 18% | Stock exchange |
| Research report subscription | 18% | Research analyst |
| Underwriting fees | 18% | Investment bank/merchant banker |

## The Subtle GST on STT vs. GST Distinction

Many retail investors confuse Securities Transaction Tax (STT) with GST. They are entirely separate:

| Feature | STT | GST |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Nature | Direct tax on transaction | Indirect tax on services |
| Levied on | Value of securities traded | Value of services (brokerage, etc.) |
| Rate | 0.025% to 0.1% depending on transaction | 18% on service fees |
| Who pays | Buyer/seller on transaction | Service recipient |
| Deductible against tax? | Yes (for active traders) | Yes, if used in business |

GST is charged on the brokerage, not on the securities transaction itself.

## Detailed Example: Equity Trade by Retail Investor

Priya buys 100 shares of Infosys at ₹1,500 per share through her broker.

| Component | Amount (₹) | GST? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Purchase value of shares (100 × ₹1,500) | 1,50,000 | No (exempt) |
| STT (0.1% on buy) | 150 | No (direct tax) |
| Brokerage @ 0.3% | 450 | Yes — 18% GST = ₹81 |
| Exchange transaction charge | 45 | Yes — 18% GST = ₹8.10 |
| Demat/clearing charge | 15 | Yes — 18% GST = ₹2.70 |
| GST actually paid by Priya | ₹91.80 | — |

The actual securities cost (₹1,50,000) is GST-free. Only the service fees attract GST.

## Detailed Example: Fund House (Mutual Fund AMC)

Wealth Asset Management runs equity mutual funds. Its fee structure:

| Revenue Source | GST Applicable? |
| --- | --- |
| Management fee (TER — expense ratio) charged to investors | Yes — 18% (AMC is providing portfolio management service) |
| Exit load charged to investors | Partially debated — most AMCs treat this as outside GST |
| Sale/redemption of units by investors | No — mutual fund unit transactions are exempt |
| Dividend distributed to unitholders | No |

The AMC pays GST on its management fee income. Investors see this embedded in the expense ratio but cannot claim ITC unless investing through a corporate treasury (B2B).

## ITC on Securities-Related Services

For a business investing its surplus funds in securities:

- Brokerage and related service GST is blocked under Section 17(5)(d) — no ITC
available since these relate to investment activities, not the taxable supply chain. For a stock broker or SEBI-registered entity:

- GST on their own input services (office rent, software, professional fees) is claimable
as ITC.

- However, they cannot claim ITC on services used exclusively for exempt transactions
(the securities trading itself, where they act as principal).

## Impact on Specific Sectors

Banks: Banks investing in G-Secs and bonds do so in exempt territory. However, banking services themselves are partly taxable. This creates a complex ITC reversal situation under Rule 42/43 for banks.

Insurance Companies: ULIPs involve investment in securities. The investment portion is exempt; the insurance services and fund management services attract GST. Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs): Transactions by FPIs in Indian securities are exempt. However, custodian fees and banking charges incurred in India attract 18% GST. FPIs typically cannot claim ITC.

## Exceptions and Edge Cases

| Situation | GST Treatment |
| --- | --- |
| Slump sale of a business (including securities) | Exempt (treated as transfer of going concern) |
| Transfer of securities as consideration for merger | Exempt (securities transaction) |
| Repo transactions in G-Secs | Exempt on the securities leg; interest may attract GST debate |
| Option premium (F&O) | Exempt on the derivative itself; brokerage taxable |
| Foreign exchange securities (offshore funds) | Import of service; intermediary service may attract IGST |

## FAQs

Q1. If a company issues shares as Employee Stock Options (ESOPs) to employees, does GST apply on the exercise of options? No. The exercise of ESOPs involves transfer of securities (equity shares) from the employer to the employee at the exercise price. Since the sale/purchase of securities is exempt under Schedule III of the CGST Act, there is no GST on the transaction itself. However, if any third-party facilitator charges a fee for managing the ESOP platform, that service fee attracts 18% GST.

Q2. Is GST applicable on stamp duty paid during dematerialization of physical shares? Stamp duty is a state-level levy, not a GST matter. The dematerialization process itself, when facilitated by a depository participant, attracts 18% GST on the service fee. The stamp duty component is separate and outside GST. Q3. Does a holding company paying management fees to its subsidiary for managing its securities portfolio attract GST? Yes. If the subsidiary charges a management fee for portfolio management services, this is a taxable supply of services at 18%. The intra-group nature of the transaction does not exempt it. Both entities must be GST registered, and proper invoicing with GST is required. The holding company may claim ITC only if its own outputs are taxable. Q4. Are sovereign gold bonds (SGBs) issued by RBI covered under the securities exemption? Yes. SGBs are classified as Government Securities under the Government Securities Act, 2006, and are included in the definition of securities under SCRA. Therefore, the purchase and redemption of SGBs are outside the purview of GST. The interest earned on SGBs is also not subject to GST as it is a return on investment, not a supply of service. Q5. In invoice discounting platforms (where invoices are sold as financial instruments), does GST apply on the discount? This is a nuanced area. If the invoice discounting platform facilitates assignment of receivables (which have characteristics of actionable claims), the assigned receivable itself may be outside GST. However, the platform fee or facilitation charge by the fintech company is a service and attracts 18% GST. The discount or interest element may also attract GST depending on whether it constitutes a financial service or a purely money transaction — GSTN has not conclusively clarified this for all models.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Rent Agreement for GST Registration: Requirements, Pitfalls, and Best Practices
articles.excerpt: Rent proof for GST registration: mandatory clauses, registered vs notarized, co-working and home-based cases, virtual offices, and FAQs.
articles.content:
When applying for GST registration, one of the most common points of rejection or query from the GST department is the proof of principal place of business. For businesses operating from rented premises, this means submitting a valid rent or lease agreement. Getting this document right is surprisingly important — errors or mismatches here can delay registration by weeks or trigger notices down the line.

## Why Is a Rent Agreement Required for GST Registration?

Under Rule 8 of the CGST Rules, 2017, every application for GST registration must include proof of the principal place of business. The law requires this to establish that the business has a legitimate, verifiable address from which it operates. For owned premises, a property tax receipt or electricity bill suffices. For rented or leased premises, the applicant must submit:

- A copy of the valid rent/lease agreement, AND
- A supporting utility document (electricity bill, water bill, or NOC from owner)
The rent agreement establishes that the applicant has a legal right to use the premises for commercial purposes.

## Documents Required: A Comprehensive Table

| Document Category | Specific Document | Mandatory? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Rent/lease agreement | Registered or notarized agreement | Mandatory for rented premises |
| Identity of landlord | Landlord's PAN, Aadhaar (where applicable) | May be required in scrutiny |
| Utility bill of premises | Electricity/water/gas bill (last 2 months) | Mandatory (landlord's name is fine) |
| No Objection Certificate (NOC) | From landlord if agreement is unregistered | Highly recommended |
| Self-declaration | If using owner's property (family property) | Required for informal arrangements |
| Consent letter | From co-owner or partner (if shared premises) | Required if multiple parties co-own |

## What Must the Rent Agreement Contain?

Not every rent agreement passes GSTN scrutiny. The document must clearly mention: Mandatory Elements:

- Full legal name of the landlord (matching government ID)
- Full legal name of the tenant/applicant (matching PAN/Aadhaar)
- Complete address of the premises with PIN code
- Commencement date and duration of lease/rent
- Monthly rent amount (can be zero for family property, but must be stated)
- Purpose of use — must specify commercial use (residential lease agreements often
get rejected)

- Signature of both parties
### Strongly Recommended Elements:

- Landlord's PAN (GSTN officers increasingly ask for this)
- Specific mention that the property can be used as principal place of business for GST
purposes

- Clear identification of the portion being rented (if partial occupancy)
## Registered vs. Notarized vs. Unregistered Agreement — What Works?

| Type | Validity for GST | Risk Level | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Registered rent agreement (sub-registrar) | Strongest — almost always accepted | Low | Required for leases > 11 months in many states |
| Notarized agreement | Generally accepted | Low-Medium | Notary stamp adds credibility |
| Unregistered agreement (plain paper, notarized) | Usually accepted with NOC | Medium | May invite additional scrutiny |
| Unregistered, not notarized | Riskiest — may be rejected | High | Always supplement with NOC |
| Verbal arrangement (no document) | Not acceptable | Very High | Must create written agreement |

Key Rule: Under the Registration Act, 1908, any lease for more than 11 months must be compulsorily registered with the Sub-Registrar. For GST registration, even 11-month notarized agreements work — but if your operations require a longer lease, get it registered.

## Practical Scenario 1: E-Commerce Seller in a Shared Office

Vikram runs an e-commerce business from a co-working space in Bengaluru. He wants to register under GST. Challenge: Most co-working space agreements are month-to-month licenses, not traditional leases. GSTN portals sometimes reject co-working agreements that don't explicitly state the registrant's right to use the address as their principal place of business. Solution:

- Obtain a formal seat allotment letter on the co-working space's letterhead
- Get a NOC from the co-working operator stating they have no objection to Vikram
using the address for GST registration

- Submit this along with the service agreement and the co-working space's own
electricity bill Most GST officers in metro cities now accept co-working proofs, but Tier 2 and Tier 3 city jurisdictions may still be conservative.

## Practical Scenario 2: Home-Based Business

Meena runs a boutique from her residence, which is owned by her parents. She wants to use this address for GST registration. Documents Required: Document Source
