Property tax receipt or electricity bill In parents' name — acceptable Consent letter / NOC from parents Parents must sign a simple letter Meena's self-declaration Stating she operates from this address Rent agreement Not mandatory if using property with owner's consent Many GST officers accept a simple consent letter + utility bill for family property. A formal rent agreement isn't mandatory, but having one (even at zero rent) strengthens the application.

## Practical Scenario 3: Multi-State Business with Branch Offices

TechServe Pvt. Ltd. is registered in Maharashtra but opens a branch office in Delhi. They need to add Delhi as an Additional Place of Business in their GST registration (not a new registration, but an amendment to their existing Maharashtra GSTIN with a separate Delhi GSTIN if required). For Delhi registration:

- Delhi rent agreement for the branch office premises
- Utility bill for the Delhi address
- The Delhi GSTIN application will reference the Maharashtra parent entity's PAN
The rent agreement here must specifically cover the Delhi branch address, not the Maharashtra HO address.

## Common Rejection Reasons and How to Avoid Them

Rejection Reason How to Avoid Rent agreement in landlord's name only Ensure tenant's name (applicant) is clearly on the agreement

Residential use clause in agreement Get a commercial use/NOC amendment or separate commercial agreement Expired rent agreement Submit current, valid agreement; renew or extend before applying Signature mismatch with other documents Ensure signatures are consistent Address mismatch with utility bill Pin code, building name must match exactly Co-living or PG agreement submitted Not acceptable; must have formal commercial proof Foreign-owned property without Indian document Ensure property tax records or electricity account exists in India

## GST Registration for Rented Virtual Offices

Virtual offices — where a business pays for an address but has no physical presence — have become a major compliance issue. The GST department has specifically cracked down on this. The Karnataka and Delhi AAR rulings have noted that:

- A virtual office address used purely for mail forwarding is not a valid "principal place
of business" under GST

- There must be some real commercial activity at the declared address
If you're using a virtual office, ensure the space provider offers dedicated desks, meeting rooms, or storage that can be evidenced as actual use. A mere mailbox doesn't qualify.

## After GST Registration: Ongoing Compliance with the Rent Agreement

The rent agreement's role doesn't end at registration:

- If the rent agreement expires, you should update GSTN records. While the law
doesn't explicitly mandate this, a GST officer conducting premises verification will check for a valid current agreement.

- If you relocate, you must amend the principal place of business in GST registration
within 15 days under Section 25(7) read with Rule 19.

- During GST audits or survey proceedings, the officer will verify the premises
against the registered address. An expired or non-matching rent agreement can complicate things.

## Special Situations

| Situation | GST Registration Approach |
| --- | --- |
| Lease in company's name, director is individual applicant | Provide company lease + board resolution authorizing director |
| Agricultural land used as business warehouse | Registered lease + conversion certificate if required by state |
| Government premises (rented from municipal body) | Allotment letter from government body serves as proof |
| Embassy or diplomatic mission address | Special cases; CBIC has provided separate guidance |
| Newly constructed building (no utility bill yet) | Completion certificate + builder's allotment letter acceptable |

## FAQs

Q1. Can a GST registration be obtained using an agreement that hasn't yet started (future-dated lease)? Generally, GSTN requires the principal place of business to be currently available for use. A future-dated agreement that commences, say, 30 days from the application date may be rejected. The premises must be accessible and usable at the time of application. However, if the building handover is imminent and supported by an allotment

letter, some officers exercise discretion. The safer approach is to apply after the lease commencement date. Q2. If the rent is being paid in cash without a formal agreement, can a declaration by the landlord substitute the rent agreement? The GSTN portal doesn't mandate that rent be formally documented — what it requires is proof of address. An NOC or consent letter from the property owner, combined with a utility bill for the premises, can substitute for a formal rent agreement in many jurisdictions. However, for scrutiny-level applications (high-turnover businesses, fresh registrations in sensitive sectors), a formal agreement is strongly advisable. Q3. My landlord refuses to sign a commercial rent agreement — can I still get GST registration? Yes, but it becomes complicated. You can submit the residential agreement with a separate NOC letter from the landlord explicitly stating permission for commercial use and GST registration at the address. This approach has worked in practice, though it depends on the discretion of the GST officer. If the landlord is completely uncooperative, the only other option is to find an alternate registered address. Q4. Does an agreement need stamp duty payment to be valid for GST registration purposes? Stamp duty is a state-level requirement and affects the legal enforceability of the agreement in courts. However, for GST registration purposes, an unstamped or under-stamped agreement is not automatically invalid — GSTN officers look at substance over form. That said, submitting a properly stamped (and ideally registered) agreement is always the cleaner approach and reduces the risk of a technical rejection. If the agreement lacks adequate stamp duty, consider notarization as a compensating measure. Q5. If a company has a registered lease but the lessor has changed (property was sold to a new owner), what happens to GST registration? The GST registration itself is not affected as long as the physical premises remain the same. A transfer of property ownership doesn't immediately invalidate your right to use the premises under the existing lease — the new owner steps into the previous landlord's shoes regarding the lease. However, it's prudent to update your lease agreement with the new landlord and retain documentation of the succession. If the new owner asks you to vacate and you actually relocate, then you must amend your GST registration accordingly.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Difference Between TDS and TCS
articles.excerpt: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) vs Tax Collected at Source (TCS): who deducts or collects, sections, forms, thresholds, examples, and FAQs.
articles.content:
## What Are TDS and TCS? A Quick Overview

Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) and Tax Collected at Source (TCS) are two mechanisms through which the Indian government collects income tax at the point of transaction rather than at the end of the financial year. While both serve the broader goal of widening the tax net and preventing evasion, they operate very differently in terms of who bears the responsibility, when they apply, and how they are reported.

This article cuts through the confusion and gives you a clear, side-by-side understanding of both concepts, with practical examples and compliance pointers.

---

## The Core Difference: Who Deducts or Collects?

The most fundamental distinction lies in the party responsible:

- **TDS** is deducted by the payer (buyer, employer, or service recipient) at the time of making a payment.
- **TCS** is collected by the seller (or specified collector) at the time of receiving payment or raising a sale consideration.

In TDS, the burden falls on the one making the payment. In TCS, the seller adds tax on top of the sale price and collects it from the buyer.

---

## TDS vs TCS: Comprehensive Comparison Table

| Parameter | TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) | TCS (Tax Collected at Source) |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Tax Deducted at Source | Tax Collected at Source |
| Governed By | Sections 192 to 196D of Income Tax Act | Section 206C of Income Tax Act |
| Who is Responsible | Payer / Deductor | Seller / Collector |
| Who Bears the Tax | Payee / Deductee | Buyer / Collectee |
| When Applied | At the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier | At the time of sale / receipt of payment |
| Nature of Transaction | Services, salary, rent, professional fees, interest, etc. | Sale of specified goods (scrap, timber, minerals, alcohol, etc.) |
| Form for Return Filing | 24Q (salary), 26Q (non-salary), 27Q (NRI payments) | 27EQ |
| Certificate Issued | Form 16 / Form 16A | Form 27D |
| Deposit Deadline | 7th of following month (30th April for March deductions) | 7th of following month |
| Reflected in | Form 26AS / AIS | Form 26AS / AIS |
| PAN Applicability | Mandatory; higher rate if PAN absent | Mandatory; higher rate if PAN absent |

---

## Applicability: When Does Each Apply?

### TDS is applicable on:
- Salary payments (Section 192)
- Interest on securities and bank deposits (Section 193, 194A)
- Dividends (Section 194)
- Commission and brokerage (Section 194H)
- Rent (Section 194I)
- Professional and technical fees (Section 194J)
- Payments to contractors (Section 194C)
- Purchase of immovable property (Section 194IA)

### TCS is applicable on sale of:
- Scrap
- Timber obtained under a forest lease
- Tendu leaves
- Alcoholic liquor for human consumption
- Minerals (coal, lignite, iron ore)
- Motor vehicles above Rs. 10 lakhs
- Foreign remittances under Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS)
- Overseas tour packages
- Sale of goods above Rs. 50 lakhs (Section 206C(1H))

---

## Threshold Limits at a Glance

| Type | Transaction | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| TDS | Salary | Basic exemption limit |
| TDS | Professional fees (194J) | Rs. 30,000 per year |
| TDS | Rent (194I) | Rs. 2,40,000 per year |
| TDS | Contractor payments (194C) | Rs. 30,000 per transaction / Rs. 1,00,000 aggregate |
| TDS | Purchase of property (194IA) | Rs. 50 lakhs |
| TCS | Sale of goods (206C(1H)) | Rs. 50 lakhs (turnover above Rs. 10 crore) |
| TCS | Motor vehicle | Rs. 10 lakhs |
| TCS | Foreign remittance (LRS) | Rs. 7 lakhs per year (for education/medical: nil or 0.5%) |

---

## Practical Example: TDS vs TCS in Action

**Example 1 (TDS):**
Company XYZ hires a chartered accountant for audit services and agrees to pay Rs. 1,00,000. Since this exceeds the Rs. 30,000 threshold under Section 194J, XYZ must deduct TDS at 10%, i.e., Rs. 10,000. The CA receives Rs. 90,000 net and can claim credit for Rs. 10,000 in their ITR.

**Example 2 (TCS):**
A licensed alcohol manufacturer sells liquor worth Rs. 2,00,000 to a distributor. The manufacturer must collect TCS at 1% on Rs. 2,00,000 = Rs. 2,000. The distributor pays Rs. 2,02,000 in total. The Rs. 2,000 collected is deposited by the manufacturer with the government, and the distributor gets credit for it in Form 26AS.

---

## Compliance Steps for Both

### For TDS Compliance:
1. Obtain TAN (Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number)
2. Deduct tax at applicable rate at the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier
3. Deposit TDS with the government using Challan 281 by 7th of the next month
4. File quarterly TDS returns (Form 24Q / 26Q / 27Q)
5. Issue TDS certificates (Form 16 / 16A) to deductees

### For TCS Compliance:
1. Obtain TAN
2. Collect tax from buyer at the time of receipt or debiting the buyer's account
3. Deposit TCS using Challan 281 by 7th of the next month
4. File quarterly TCS returns in Form 27EQ
5. Issue Form 27D to the buyer

---

## Penalties and Consequences

| Default | TDS Consequence | TCS Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Failure to deduct / collect | Interest at 1% per month | Interest at 1% per month |
| Failure to deposit after deduction/collection | Interest at 1.5% per month | Interest at 1.5% per month |
| Late / non-filing of return | Rs. 200 per day (Section 234E) | Rs. 200 per day (Section 234E) |
| Penalty for non-compliance | Rs. 10,000 to Rs. 1,00,000 (Section 271H) | Rs. 10,000 to Rs. 1,00,000 (Section 271H) |
| Disallowance of expense (TDS) | 30% of expenditure disallowed u/s 40(a)(ia) | Not applicable |

---

## FAQs

**Q1. Can a transaction attract both TDS and TCS?**
Generally no. However, under Section 194Q (TDS on purchase of goods) and Section 206C(1H) (TCS on sale of goods), both could technically apply to the same transaction. The CBDT has clarified that if TDS is deducted under 194Q, the seller is not required to collect TCS under 206C(1H) on that transaction.

**Q2. What happens if the deductee does not have a PAN?**
Under both TDS and TCS, if the deductee or collectee does not furnish a PAN, tax must be deducted or collected at the higher of: the applicable rate, 5%, or 20% (for TCS under certain provisions). The Income Tax Department has also introduced Aadhaar-based linking as a partial remedy.

**Q3. Is TCS an additional tax burden on the buyer?**
No. TCS is a tax collected in advance and is fully adjustable against the buyer's final tax liability while filing their Income Tax Return. It reflects in their Form 26AS, from where it can be claimed as credit.

**Q4. Who is exempt from TDS deduction obligations?**
Individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are generally not required to deduct TDS unless they are subject to tax audit under Section 44AB. However, Section 194IA (property purchase) applies to all buyers regardless of audit status.

**Q5. Can TDS be avoided through a lower deduction certificate?**
Yes. A deductee can apply to the Assessing Officer under Section 197 for a certificate authorising the deductor to deduct TDS at a lower rate or nil rate. This is common for professionals and vendors with substantial business expenses that reduce their net taxable income.

articles.title: Complete Guide to GST Registration in India 2024
articles.excerpt: The Goods and Services Tax (GST), introduced on July 1, 2017, replaced over a dozen central and state taxes with a unified indirect tax structure. Every business supplying taxable goods or services above the prescribed…
articles.content:
# Complete guide to GST registration in India 2024

The Goods and Services Tax (GST), introduced on July 1, 2017, replaced over a dozen central and state taxes with a unified indirect tax structure. Every business supplying taxable goods or services above the prescribed threshold must obtain GST registration. Operating without GST registration when required is a serious legal offence, attracting penalties equal to 100% of the tax evaded (subject to a minimum of Rs. 10,000).

Beyond compliance, GST registration enables you to collect tax from customers, claim input tax credit on your purchases, and establish credibility with corporate buyers who require your GSTIN for their own ITC claims.

## Who needs to register for GST?

### Mandatory registration (threshold-based)

| Category | Threshold for mandatory registration |
| --- | --- |
| Supplier of goods (normal states) | Rs. 40 lakhs aggregate turnover |
| Supplier of services (normal states) | Rs. 20 lakhs aggregate turnover |
| Supplier of goods (special category states) | Rs. 20 lakhs aggregate turnover |
| Supplier of services (special category states) | Rs. 10 lakhs aggregate turnover |

Special Category States include Manipur, Mizoram, Nagaland, Tripura, Meghalaya, Sikkim, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, and Arunachal Pradesh.

### Mandatory registration regardless of turnover

| Situation | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Inter-state supply of goods | Any cross-state goods sale requires GST registration |
| E-commerce operators | Platforms like Amazon, Flipkart |
| Casual taxable persons | Temporary businesses in a state where they don't have a fixed place of business |
| Non-resident taxable persons | Foreign businesses supplying in India |
| Suppliers under reverse charge mechanism | When reverse charge applies |
| Input Service Distributor (ISD) | Distributing ITC across branches |
| Persons deducting TDS under GST | Government departments deducting GST TDS |
| Suppliers of online information services from outside India to India (OIDAR) | Foreign digital service providers |

## Types of GST registration

| Type | Who it is for |
| --- | --- |
| Regular registration | Standard businesses above threshold |
| Composition scheme | Small businesses below Rs. 1.5 crore (Rs. 75 lakhs for services) |
| Casual taxable person | Businesses operating temporarily in a state |
| Non-resident taxable person | Foreign businesses operating temporarily in India |
| Input Service Distributor | Head offices distributing ITC to branches |
| TDS deductor | Government entities deducting GST TDS |
| TCS collector | E-commerce operators collecting TCS on behalf of sellers |

## Composition scheme: a simpler alternative

Small businesses can opt for the Composition Scheme under Section 10 of the CGST Act.

| Type of business | Threshold | Rate of tax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Manufacturers (except notified goods) | Rs. 1.5 crore turnover | 1% (0.5% CGST plus 0.5% SGST) |
| Traders (goods) | Rs. 1.5 crore turnover | 1% |
| Restaurants not serving alcohol | Rs. 1.5 crore turnover | 5% |
| Service providers | Rs. 50 lakhs turnover | 6% |

Composition taxpayers cannot collect GST from customers, cannot issue tax invoices, and cannot claim ITC. They are suitable for purely local businesses with B2C customers who are not concerned about ITC.

## Documents required for GST registration

| Document | Individual / proprietor | Company / LLP | Partnership firm |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PAN | Owner's PAN | Company PAN | Firm PAN |
| Aadhaar | Owner's Aadhaar | Authorized signatory's Aadhaar | Partners' Aadhaar |
| Photo | Proprietor's photo | Authorized signatory's photo | Partners' photos |
| Business address proof | Utility bill / rent agreement / NOC | Same | Same |
| Bank account proof | Bank statement / cancelled cheque | Same | Same |
| Business registration proof | None (sole proprietor) | Certificate of incorporation | Partnership deed |
| Authorization letter | N/A | Board resolution authorizing signatory | Partner authorization |

## Step-by-step GST registration process

### Step 1: Access the GST portal

Visit `https://www.gst.gov.in` and click Services, Registration, New Registration.

### Step 2: Part A of Form REG-01

Enter:

- Type of taxpayer (Regular, Composition, etc.)
- State and district
- Legal name as per PAN
- PAN (auto-validated against Income Tax database)
- Email and mobile number for OTP verification

An Application Reference Number (ARN) is generated after Part A submission.

### Step 3: Part B of Form REG-01

This is the detailed application. Fill in:

- Business details (trade name, constitution of business)
- Principal place of business address
- Additional places of business (branches)
- HSN codes for goods / SAC codes for services supplied
- Bank account details
- Details of promoters, partners, or directors
- Authorized signatory details

Upload all required documents as per the entity type.

### Step 4: Aadhaar authentication

From August 2020, GST registration includes Aadhaar-based authentication. An OTP is sent to the mobile linked with Aadhaar. This step determines whether a physical verification of the business premises is required:

- If Aadhaar authentication is successful: registration approved within 3 working days (no physical verification unless risk-profiled)
- If Aadhaar authentication is skipped: physical verification of premises by GST officer within 30 days

### Step 5: Verification by GST officer

If required, a GST officer may visit the business premises and issue clarifications. The officer may issue Form GST REG-03 asking for additional information. You must respond within 7 working days using Form GST REG-04.

### Step 6: Approval and GSTIN issuance

Upon approval, the GST officer issues Form GST REG-06 containing your:

- GSTIN (15-digit GST Identification Number)
- Registration certificate

The GSTIN format: 2 digits (state code) plus 10 digits (PAN) plus 2 digits (entity number) plus 1 check digit.

## Understanding the GSTIN structure

| Position | Digits | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 to 2 | 2 | State code (for example, 27 for Maharashtra, 29 for Karnataka) |
| 3 to 12 | 10 | PAN of the taxpayer |
| 13 | 1 | Number of registrations in the state (1 = first registration) |
| 14 | 1 | Alphabet Z (default) |
| 15 | 1 | Check digit |

## Post-registration compliance

| Compliance | Frequency | Due date |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GSTR-1 (outward supplies) | Monthly (if turnover above Rs. 5 crore) | 11th of next month |
| GSTR-1 (quarterly via QRMP) | Quarterly | 13th of month after quarter |
| GSTR-3B (summary return plus payment) | Monthly | 20th of next month (20th/22nd/24th based on state) |
| GSTR-9 (annual return) | Annual | December 31 of following year |
| GSTR-9C (reconciliation, if turnover above Rs. 5 crore) | Annual | December 31 of following year |
| GSTR-4 (composition dealers) | Annual | April 30 of following year |

## GST rates: a quick reference

| Rate | Examples of goods / services |
| --- | --- |
| 0% (exempt) | Fresh vegetables, milk, eggs, unprocessed food grains, healthcare services, educational services |
| 5% | Packaged food items, economy class air travel, essential medicines |
| 12% | Processed food, business class air travel, non-AC restaurants |
| 18% | Most services (IT, consulting, banking), electronics, construction services |
| 28% | Luxury goods, automobiles, tobacco, aerated drinks, cement |
| 28% plus cess | Pan masala, tobacco, luxury cars (cess is levied over 28%) |

## Penalties for GST non-compliance

| Violation | Penalty |
| --- | --- |
| Not registering when required | 10% of tax evaded or Rs. 10,000, whichever is higher |
| Tax evasion with fraudulent intent | 100% of tax amount (Rs. 10,000 minimum) |
| Late filing of GST returns | Rs. 50 per day (Rs. 20 per day for nil returns); maximum Rs. 10,000 |
| Late payment of GST | Interest at 18% per annum on unpaid amount |
| Incorrect ITC claim | 10% of ITC claimed, subject to Rs. 10,000 minimum |
| E-way bill violations | Rs. 10,000 or tax evaded, whichever is greater |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. If my turnover is below Rs. 20 lakhs but I supply goods to another state, do I still need GST registration?**

Yes. Inter-state supply of goods mandates GST registration regardless of turnover. The threshold limits apply only to businesses that supply goods or services within a single state. The moment you make even one cross-state sale of goods, you are required to register for GST and collect IGST on that supply. Inter-state supply of services, however, is exempt from mandatory registration if turnover is below the threshold.

**Q2. Can I claim input tax credit on purchases made before GST registration?**

Yes, but with restrictions. Under Section 18(1) of the CGST Act, a newly registered taxpayer can claim ITC on inputs held in stock and inputs contained in semi-finished or finished goods on the date immediately preceding the date of registration. This claim must be made within 30 days of becoming liable to register. Capital goods purchased before registration are also eligible under certain conditions.

**Q3. My business has branches in three different states. Do I need separate GST registrations for each state?**

Yes. GST registration is state-specific. Each state where you have a fixed place of business (including branches, offices, or warehouses) requires a separate GSTIN. However, all GSTINs will be linked to your PAN. You can designate one branch as the Head Office (registered as ISD) to distribute ITC across branches for centrally procured services. A single GSTIN covering multiple states is not permitted.

**Q4. I want to cancel my GST registration because I have closed my business. What is the process?**

You can apply for cancellation through Form GST REG-16 on the GST portal. You must provide the reason for cancellation, last return filed details, and details of stock held on the date of cancellation (tax is payable on the closing stock). The GST officer processes the application and issues a cancellation order in Form GST REG-19. Even after filing the cancellation application, you must continue filing returns until the cancellation is officially approved.

**Q5. What is the difference between GST registration suspension and cancellation?**

GST registration can be suspended by the tax officer if they have reason to believe that the registration is liable to be cancelled (for example, non-filing of returns for six or more months, or the business premises is found to be non-existent). During suspension, the taxpayer cannot make taxable supplies, issue tax invoices, or claim ITC. Cancellation is the permanent ending of registration. Suspension is a provisional measure while cancellation proceedings are ongoing; the taxpayer gets an opportunity to respond before final cancellation.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Register a Private Limited Company in India
articles.excerpt: When entrepreneurs, investors, and startups think about incorporating a formal business entity in India, the Private Limited Company (Pvt. Ltd.) consistently tops the list. Its combination of limited liability protectio…
articles.content:
# How to register a private limited company in India

When entrepreneurs, investors, and startups think about incorporating a formal business entity in India, the Private Limited Company (Pvt. Ltd.) consistently tops the list. Its combination of limited liability protection, credibility with investors and banks, ability to issue equity shares, and perpetual existence makes it the default choice for serious business ventures.

This article walks you through every step of registering a private limited company in India under the Companies Act, 2013, managed through the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) portal.

## What defines a private limited company?

Under Section 2(68) of the Companies Act, 2013, a private limited company:

- Restricts the right to transfer shares
- Limits its members to a maximum of 200 (excluding current and former employees who are members)
- Prohibits any invitation to the public to subscribe to its securities

| Feature | Private limited company |
| --- | --- |
| Minimum directors | 2 |
| Maximum directors | 15 (more with special resolution) |
| Minimum shareholders | 2 |
| Maximum shareholders | 200 |
| Minimum paid-up capital | No minimum (removed by Companies Amendment Act 2015) |
| Liability of members | Limited to unpaid share capital |
| Separate legal entity | Yes |
| Perpetual succession | Yes |
| Can accept public deposits | No |
| IPO or public listing | Not directly (must convert to public company first) |

## Prerequisites before you begin

Before starting the MCA registration process, ensure you have:

| Requirement | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Directors | Minimum 2; at least 1 must be a resident Indian (stayed in India for 182 days in previous calendar year) |
| DIN | Director Identification Number required for all directors |
| DSC | Digital Signature Certificate required for the subscriber to the MoA and the proposed directors |
| Proposed name | 2 to 3 name options in order of preference |
| Registered office address | A physical address in India (can be a rented premises) |
| Share capital structure | Who owns how many shares (shareholding pattern) |

## Step-by-step registration process

### Step 1: Obtain digital signature certificates (DSC)

All proposed directors and subscribers to the Memorandum of Association (MoA) need a Class 3 DSC. DSC is obtained from certified agencies such as eMudhra, Sify Technologies, or National Informatics Centre (NIC). Documents required for DSC:

- PAN card
- Aadhaar card
- Photo and email ID verification

DSC is issued within 1 to 2 days and is valid for 2 years.

### Step 2: Apply for director identification number (DIN)

DIN can be obtained directly through the SPICe+ (Simplified Proforma for Incorporating Company Electronically Plus) form during company registration. First-time directors do not need to apply separately. Those already having a DIN from a previous directorship should use the existing one.

### Step 3: Name availability check and reservation

Use the MCA portal's RUN (Reserve Unique Name) service or the integrated SPICe+ Part A to reserve a company name. Guidelines for name approval:

- Must end with Private Limited (Pvt. Ltd.)
- Must not be identical or similar to an existing company or LLP name
- Must not use restricted words (bank, insurance, government, etc.) without regulatory approval
- Should ideally indicate the line of business

Upon approval, the name is reserved for 20 days (SPICe+ Part A) or 90 days (RUN).

### Step 4: Draft the memorandum of association (MoA) and articles of association (AoA)

The MoA contains the fundamental conditions upon which the company is registered. The AoA contains the rules for internal management.

Key clauses in MoA:

- Name clause
- Registered office clause
- Objects clause (what the company will do)
- Liability clause (limited by shares)
- Capital clause

Draft these carefully with the help of a CA or CS. The Companies Act provides Table F as a model AoA that companies can adopt wholly or with modifications.

### Step 5: File SPICe+ (INC-32) with MCA

SPICe+ is the integrated incorporation form available at `https://www.mca.gov.in`. It handles multiple applications simultaneously:

| Service | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| SPICe+ Part A | Name reservation |
| SPICe+ Part B | Company incorporation |
| AGILE-PRO-S | GST registration, EPFO, ESIC, PTAX (Maharashtra) registration |
| INC-33 | e-MoA (for up to 7 subscribers) |
| INC-34 | e-AoA |

Documents to be attached in SPICe+:

- Proof of registered office (utility bill plus NOC from owner or rent agreement)
- Identity and address proof of all directors and subscribers
- Consent to act as director (Form DIR-2)
- Self-declaration by proposed directors (Form INC-9)

### Step 6: Pay MCA registration fees

Government fee structure (as of 2024):

| Authorised capital | Registration fee |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 1,00,000 | Rs. 0 (nominal fee only for filing) |
| Rs. 1,00,001 to Rs. 5,00,000 | Rs. 4,000 |
| Rs. 5,00,001 to Rs. 50 lakhs | Slab-based |
| Above Rs. 50 lakhs | Slab-based |

Stamp duty varies by state. Most states allow e-stamping through the portal.

### Step 7: Certificate of incorporation

Once MCA approves the application (typically within 2 to 7 working days), the Registrar of Companies (RoC) issues:

- Certificate of Incorporation (CoI)
- Company Identification Number (CIN)
- PAN and TAN of the company (automatically applied for through SPICe+)

## Post-incorporation compliance

Getting incorporated is just the beginning. Private limited companies must comply with ongoing requirements:

| Requirement | Timeline |
| --- | --- |
| Open a current bank account | Within 30 days of incorporation |
| Deposit initial share capital | Within 30 days of incorporation |
| Obtain statutory registers | Immediately after incorporation |
| First board meeting | Within 30 days of incorporation |
| File INC-20A (business commencement declaration) | Within 180 days of incorporation |
| Appoint statutory auditor | At first AGM or within 30 days of incorporation |
| Annual return (MGT-7A / MGT-7) | Within 60 days of AGM |
| Financial statements (AOC-4) | Within 30 days of AGM |
| Annual general meeting | Within 6 months of financial year end |

## Cost summary for incorporation

| Item | Approximate cost |
| --- | --- |
| DSC for 2 directors | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 3,000 |
| DIN (if needed separately) | Nil (via SPICe+) |
| Government filing fee | Rs. 0 to Rs. 5,000 depending on capital |
| Stamp duty on MoA/AoA | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 10,000 (state-dependent) |
| Professional charges (CA/CS) | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 20,000 |
| Total (approx.) | Rs. 8,000 to Rs. 35,000 |

## Penalties for non-compliance

| Default | Consequence |
| --- | --- |
| Not filing INC-20A (commencement) | Rs. 50,000 for company; Rs. 1,000 per day for officers |
| Not holding AGM | Rs. 1 lakh fine plus Rs. 5,000 per day continuing default |
| Not filing annual return | Rs. 50,000 plus Rs. 100 per day |
| Not appointing auditor | Rs. 25,000 for company; Rs. 5,000 for each officer |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Can a single person start a private limited company, or is there a minimum of two people required?**

A Private Limited Company requires a minimum of two directors and two shareholders. If you want to be the sole promoter, you have two options: (1) involve a family member or trusted person as the second director or shareholder (even with 1 share), or (2) incorporate a One Person Company (OPC) instead, which allows a single member. However, OPCs have their own restrictions, including a turnover cap of Rs. 2 crores before mandatory conversion.

**Q2. Does the registered office need to be a commercial property, or can it be a residential address?**

A residential address can be used as the registered office for a private limited company. Many startups use the founder's home address initially. The only requirement is that the address is in India and documents served there will reach the company. An NOC from the property owner and a utility bill as proof are required. The company can change the registered office later as it grows.

**Q3. How long does it take to register a private limited company end-to-end?**

With all documents ready and correct, the process typically takes 7 to 15 working days from DSC application to Certificate of Incorporation. DSC takes 1 to 2 days, name reservation under SPICe+ Part A takes 1 to 5 days, and the final incorporation approval takes 2 to 7 days. Delays occur when documents are incomplete or the proposed name is rejected. Engaging a CA or CS speeds up the process considerably.

**Q4. Can a foreign national be a director or shareholder in an Indian private limited company?**

Yes. Foreign nationals can be directors (subject to visa and KYC requirements) and shareholders in an Indian private limited company. However, at least one director must be a resident Indian. Foreign shareholding must comply with the Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policy and sector-specific caps. Reporting requirements under Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) and RBI regulations also apply when foreign capital is brought in.

**Q5. What is the difference between authorized capital and paid-up capital in a private limited company?**

Authorized capital is the maximum amount of share capital a company is permitted to issue as per its MoA. Paid-up capital is the actual capital contributed by shareholders for shares already issued and paid for. For example, a company may have Rs. 10 lakh authorized capital but issue shares worth Rs. 2 lakhs initially: that is the paid-up capital. Increasing authorized capital requires an MCA filing (SH-7) and an additional fee. There is no minimum paid-up capital requirement for private limited companies in India since the 2015 amendment.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Trademark Registration in India: Complete Process & Classes
articles.excerpt: A trademark is any sign, symbol, word, phrase, logo, design, or combination thereof that distinguishes your goods or services from those of competitors. It is the visual and linguistic identity of your brand. Registerin…
articles.content:
# Trademark registration in India: complete process and classes

A trademark is any sign, symbol, word, phrase, logo, design, or combination thereof that distinguishes your goods or services from those of competitors. It is the visual and linguistic identity of your brand.

Registering a trademark in India gives you the exclusive right to use that mark across the country in relation to the goods and services for which it is registered. Without registration, your brand name or logo has limited legal protection, and anyone can replicate it without much legal recourse.

This guide covers the entire trademark registration process in India under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, including the class system, costs, timelines, and what to do after registration.

## Why register a trademark?

| Benefit | Without registration | With registration |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Legal protection | Common law rights only (limited geography) | Nationwide statutory protection |
| Right to sue for infringement | Complex and expensive | Straightforward under Sections 29 to 30 |
| Licensing | Can be licensed but enforcement is weak | Formally licensable and recordable |
| Asset value | Not a formal IP asset | Intangible asset on balance sheet |
| TM vs R symbol | Can use TM | Can use (R) after registration |
| Customs protection | Limited | Can record with Customs to block counterfeits |
| Duration | Perpetual but harder to enforce | 10 years, renewable indefinitely |

## Understanding the Nice classification system

India follows the International Classification of Goods and Services under the Nice Agreement. There are 45 classes: Classes 1 to 34 cover goods, and Classes 35 to 45 cover services.

### Selected goods classes

| Class | Description | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 5 | Pharmaceutical and veterinary products | Medicines, dietary supplements |
| 9 | Electronics and scientific instruments | Software, smartphones, apps |
| 14 | Jewellery and timepieces | Rings, watches, clocks |
| 16 | Paper goods and printed matter | Stationery, books, packaging |
| 25 | Clothing and footwear | Apparel, shoes, caps |
| 29 | Processed food (meat, dairy, etc.) | Cheese, packaged vegetables |
| 30 | Staple foods (flour, coffee, bakery) | Bread, spices, chocolates |
| 33 | Alcoholic beverages | Whisky, wine, beer |

### Selected service classes

| Class | Description | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 35 | Advertising and business services | Marketing, retail services |
| 36 | Financial and insurance services | Banking, investment advisory |
| 38 | Telecommunications | Internet services, broadcast |
| 41 | Education and entertainment | Online courses, events |
| 42 | Scientific and IT services | Software development, SaaS |
| 43 | Food and hospitality services | Restaurants, catering |
| 45 | Legal and security services | Law firms, security agencies |

> Choosing the right class is critical. If you register in the wrong class, your trademark will not protect you in the category where you actually operate. Many businesses register in multiple classes.

## Step-by-step trademark registration process in India

### Step 1: Trademark search

Before filing, conduct a comprehensive trademark search on the IP India Trademark Search portal (`https://ipindiaonline.gov.in/tmrpublicsearch/`). Search for:

- Identical marks
- Phonetically similar marks
- Visually similar logos
- Marks in the same or related classes

This step prevents filing a mark that will be rejected on grounds of similarity to an existing registered mark.

### Step 2: Determine the class(es)

Identify which Nice Classification class(es) your goods or services fall under. If your brand covers multiple categories (for example, clothing and an app), file in multiple classes.

### Step 3: Prepare the application

Gather the following:

- Applicant's name and address
- Nature of applicant (individual, company, partnership, LLP, trust)
- Representation of the mark (logo file, word mark)
- Description of goods or services
- Class(es) to be covered
- User affidavit if claiming prior use (Form TM-M)

### Step 4: File the application

File online through the IP India portal or physically at the Trade Marks Registry offices in Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata, or Ahmedabad.

**Government filing fees:**

| Applicant type | Per class (e-filing) | Per class (physical filing) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Individual, startup, MSME | Rs. 4,500 | Rs. 5,000 |
| Others (companies, LLPs, etc.) | Rs. 9,000 | Rs. 10,000 |

Upon filing, you receive an application number and a filing date. The TM symbol can now be used immediately (even before registration is complete).

### Step 5: Vienna codification (for logo marks)

If your mark includes a device or logo, the examiner applies Vienna Codes to classify the figurative elements for search purposes.

### Step 6: Examination

The Trademark Examiner reviews the application within 12 to 18 months and issues an Examination Report. The report may:

- Accept the application: proceed to publication
- Raise objections: common grounds include similarity to existing marks, descriptiveness, or deceptive marks

### Step 7: Reply to examination report (if objected)

If an objection is raised, you must file a Reply within 30 days (extendable to 2 months). If the objection persists, you can request a Hearing before the Registrar.

### Step 8: Publication in trademark journal

If accepted (after examination or after overcoming objections), the mark is published in the Trade Marks Journal. This invites the public to oppose the mark within 4 months.

### Step 9: Opposition (if any)

If a third party files an opposition, the matter enters an adversarial proceeding before the Trademark Registrar. Both parties file evidence and arguments. This can extend the timeline by 2 to 4 years in contested cases.

### Step 10: Registration

If no opposition is filed (or opposition is dismissed), the Registrar issues the Registration Certificate. The (R) symbol can now be used. The registration is effective from the date of application, not the date of registration.

## Timeline overview

| Stage | Approximate duration |
| --- | --- |
| Filing to examination report | 12 to 18 months |
| Reply to objection plus hearing | 3 to 6 months |
| Publication in journal | 2 to 3 months after acceptance |
| Opposition period | 4 months |
| Registration after no opposition | 2 to 4 months |
| Total (uncontested) | 18 to 24 months |
| Total (contested) | 3 to 5 years |

## Trademark renewal

A registered trademark is valid for 10 years from the date of registration. It can be renewed indefinitely every 10 years by filing Form TM-R with the renewal fee. Renewal must be applied for before the expiry date. A six-month grace period with a surcharge is available if the initial deadline is missed.

## Penalties for trademark infringement

| Violation | Consequence |
| --- | --- |
| Using a registered trademark without permission | Civil suit for injunction and damages |
| Counterfeiting a registered trademark | Criminal prosecution; imprisonment 6 months to 3 years plus fine Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 2 lakhs |
| Falsely applying a trademark | Imprisonment up to 3 years plus fine |
| Using similar marks to cause confusion | Injunction plus damages plus account of profits |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. Can I use the TM symbol before my trademark is officially registered?**

Yes. The TM symbol (and SM for service marks) can be used immediately upon filing the trademark application. It signals to the public that you are claiming trademark rights over the mark. The (R) symbol, however, can only be used after the Trademark Registrar issues the Certificate of Registration. Using (R) before registration is a legal offence under the Trade Marks Act.

**Q2. If someone else is using my brand name but has not registered it, can I still register the trademark?**

Yes, you can apply for registration, but it is not guaranteed. If the other party has been using the mark for a long time and has built substantial goodwill, they can oppose your application based on prior use. Indian law recognises common law rights (under the tort of passing off) for unregistered but well-known marks. To avoid a costly dispute, conduct a thorough market search before filing, not just a registry search.

**Q3. Do I need a separate trademark registration for each state in India?**

No. India has a unified national trademark registry. A single trademark registration provides protection across all states and union territories. There is no state-level trademark registration system in India. However, if you want international protection, you need to file separately under the Madrid Protocol for each target country.

**Q4. What is the difference between a wordmark and a device mark?**

A wordmark is a trademark consisting solely of text, such as the brand name Tata or Infosys in a standard font. A device mark includes a logo, stylised font, or graphical element. Many businesses file both a wordmark (for the word itself, regardless of font or design) and a device mark (for the specific logo). This provides broader protection. Filing a wordmark is generally stronger for long-term brand protection.

**Q5. My trademark registration was rejected. What are my options?**

If the Trademark Registry rejects your application after examination and hearing, you can appeal to the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB). With recent reforms, trademark appeals now go to the High Court (Commercial Courts) since the IPAB was abolished in 2021. You have three months from the date of the order to file an appeal. Alternatively, you can file a fresh application with a different or modified mark that addresses the objections raised.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: FSSAI Registration vs FSSAI License: Key Differences Explained
articles.excerpt: The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) is the regulatory body responsible for ensuring that food sold across India meets safety and quality standards. Whether you are starting a home bakery, a cloud ki…
articles.content:
# FSSAI registration vs FSSAI license: key differences explained

The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) is the regulatory body responsible for ensuring that food sold across India meets safety and quality standards. Whether you are starting a home bakery, a cloud kitchen, a restaurant chain, or importing food products, you will need FSSAI authorisation before you can legally operate.

The confusion for most food business operators is whether they need a basic registration or a state or central license. These are not interchangeable. Using the wrong category can result in penalties, cancellations, and business disruptions. This guide clarifies the differences definitively.

## The three types of FSSAI authorisation

| Type | Who it is for | Issued by | Validity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Basic registration | Very small food businesses | FSSAI directly | 1 to 5 years |
| State license | Medium-sized businesses | State Food Safety Authority | 1 to 5 years |
| Central license | Large, multi-state, import-export businesses | FSSAI Central Authority | 1 to 5 years |

## FSSAI basic registration

### Who needs basic registration?

Basic Registration (Form A) is for petty food business operators whose annual turnover does not exceed Rs. 12 lakhs.

This includes:

- Hawkers, vendors, and small retailers
- Home-based food businesses (home bakers, tiffin services, pickle makers)
- Petty manufacturers with annual turnover up to Rs. 12 lakhs
- Temporary stall owners at fairs, exhibitions, and festivals
- Small food storage operators

### What is not covered under basic registration?

- Businesses with turnover above Rs. 12 lakhs
- Businesses operating in more than one state
- Manufacturers producing more than specified quantities (for example, dairy, meat)

### Documents required for basic registration

| Document | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| Photo of the applicant | Passport-size |
| Identity proof | Aadhaar, Voter ID, Passport, or driving licence |
| Address proof of business premises | Utility bill, rent agreement |
| Form A (declaration) | Duly filled and signed |

## FSSAI state license

### Who needs a state license?

State License (Form B) is required for medium food businesses operating within one state, with annual turnover between Rs. 12 lakhs and Rs. 20 crores.

This includes:

- Restaurants, hotels, and eateries (turnover above Rs. 12 lakhs)
- Cloud kitchens and food aggregators below central license threshold
- Manufacturers with production capacity between specified limits (for example, dairy below 50,000 litres per day)
- Cold storages, warehouses, and distributors within a single state
- Caterers for events with more than 100 persons

### Turnover and capacity thresholds for state license

| Category | Threshold for state license |
| --- | --- |
| Annual turnover | Rs. 12 lakhs to Rs. 20 crores |
| Dairy units | Up to 50,000 litres per day |
| Slaughterhouses | Up to 50 large animals or 150 small animals per day |
| Vegetable oil processing | Up to 2 MT per day |
| Proprietary food units | Up to 2 MT per day |
| Hotels (star category) | Below 5-star |

## FSSAI central license

### Who needs a central license?

Central License is mandatory for:

- Food businesses with annual turnover above Rs. 20 crores
- Importers and exporters of food products
- Businesses operating in more than one state
- Food manufacturers supplying directly to government agencies or defence establishments
- E-commerce food business operators (Swiggy, Zomato, Amazon Fresh, etc.)
- Five-star hotels and above
- Food businesses at airports, ports, and railways stations

### Special cases requiring central license irrespective of turnover

- 100% Export Oriented Units (EOUs)
- Units operating under a central government ministry

## Side-by-side comparison table

| Parameter | Basic registration | State license | Central license |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Annual turnover | Up to Rs. 12 lakhs | Rs. 12 lakhs to Rs. 20 crores | Above Rs. 20 crores |
| Applicability | Very small or petty operators | Medium businesses (intra-state) | Large, multi-state, importers |
| Application form | Form A | Form B | Form B |
| Issuing authority | Registration officer | Designated state officer | Central Licensing Authority |
| FSSAI number digits | 14-digit | 14-digit | 14-digit (starts with 1) |
| Inspection before approval | Generally not required | May be required | Typically required |
| Processing time | 7 days | 30 days | 60 days |
| Fee (approx.) | Rs. 100 per year | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 5,000 per year | Rs. 7,500 per year |
| Penalty for non-compliance | Up to Rs. 25,000 | Up to Rs. 5 lakhs | Up to Rs. 10 lakhs |

## Application process

### Step 1: Determine the correct category

Use the turnover and capacity thresholds to identify whether you need basic registration, a state license, or a central license.

### Step 2: Apply on the FoSCoS portal

Visit `https://foscos.fssai.gov.in` and create an account as a Food Business Operator (FBO). Select the appropriate application type.

### Step 3: Fill in business details

Provide details of:

- Business name, type, and ownership structure
- Address and premises details
- Nature of food business activities
- Installed capacity or turnover estimates

### Step 4: Upload documents

Upload all required documents based on the license type. Central license applications require additional documents such as plant layout, water test reports, and list of food products.

### Step 5: Pay the fee

Fee is based on the type of authorization and the duration (1 to 5 years).

### Step 6: Inspection (if required)

For state and central licenses, a food safety officer may inspect the premises before issuing the license.

### Step 7: Receive the license

Upon approval, the 14-digit FSSAI number is issued. This must be displayed prominently at the business premises and printed on all food labels.

## Penalties for non-compliance

| Violation | Penalty |
| --- | --- |
| Operating without registration or license | Imprisonment up to 6 months plus fine up to Rs. 5 lakhs |
| Selling unsafe food | Up to Rs. 10 lakhs fine; life imprisonment if death results |
| Misleading advertisement | Up to Rs. 10 lakhs |
| Failure to comply with FSSAI directions | Up to Rs. 2 lakhs |
| Obstructing FSSAI officer | Up to Rs. 10,000 |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. If I run a home bakery and sell only to friends and neighbours with turnover under Rs. 12 lakhs, do I still need FSSAI registration?**

Yes. FSSAI registration is mandatory for all food business operators regardless of scale, including home-based businesses. Even if your turnover is Rs. 5,000 per month, selling food commercially without FSSAI registration is a legal violation. Basic registration (Form A) is simple, affordable, and can be obtained online within 7 working days.

**Q2. Does a restaurant on Swiggy or Zomato need a central license or a state license?**

The restaurant (cloud kitchen or physical restaurant) needs a state license based on its own turnover. The food aggregator platform (Swiggy, Zomato) needs a central license since it operates across multiple states and is classified as an e-commerce food business operator. These are two separate obligations: one for the platform and one for each listed restaurant.

**Q3. My turnover crossed Rs. 20 crores mid-year. Do I need to upgrade my license immediately?**

Yes. As soon as your turnover is projected to exceed Rs. 20 crores or your operations expand to multiple states, you must apply for a central license. Operating with a state license beyond the applicable threshold is a compliance violation. You should apply for upgrade before the threshold is crossed, not after.

**Q4. Is the FSSAI license transferable if I sell my food business?**

No. FSSAI licenses and registrations are non-transferable. When a food business changes ownership, the new owner must apply for a fresh license or registration in their own name. The previous license must be surrendered for cancellation.

**Q5. Can I display the FSSAI number on my product packaging even during the registration renewal period when the old license has expired?**

No. Operating or labelling products with an expired FSSAI license is non-compliant. You must apply for renewal at least 30 days before the expiry date. If the renewal is pending, you can continue operations if you have proof of having applied for renewal, but you cannot label products with an expired number. Always apply for renewal well in advance.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Website Maintenance
articles.excerpt: Website maintenance is the ongoing work of keeping your site secure, fast, accurate, and available. Like servicing a storefront or office, regular checks prevent outages, plug security gaps, and keep content aligned wit…
articles.content:
# Website Maintenance

## Overview

Website maintenance is the ongoing work of keeping your site **secure**, **fast**, **accurate**, and **available**. Like servicing a storefront or office, regular checks prevent outages, plug security gaps, and keep content aligned with how your business actually operates today.

> [!TIP] Why it matters
> Search engines and visitors both reward sites that load quickly, stay free of errors, and publish fresh, trustworthy information. Neglected sites accumulate broken links, outdated plugins, and invisible SEO issues.

## Core maintenance pillars

| Pillar | Typical tasks |
| --- | --- |
| **Security** | SSL renewal, malware scans, login hardening, firewall rules, dependency patches |
| **Platform** | CMS core, themes, and plugin / package updates (often via staging first) |
| **Performance** | Caching, image optimisation, script/CSS hygiene, database cleanup, CDN tuning |
| **Content & SEO** | Page updates, meta refreshes, fixing crawl errors, sitemap and Search Console hygiene |
| **Reliability** | Scheduled backups, restore drills, uptime alerts, hosting/DNS renewal tracking |

## Benefits at a glance

| Benefit | What you gain |
| --- | --- |
| **Lower breach risk** | Patches and monitoring reduce windows for exploits |
| **Better rankings** | Faster, error-free sites tend to perform better in organic search |
| **Higher trust** | Professional, up-to-date pages signal credibility |
| **Fewer emergencies** | Proactive care avoids “site down” weekend scrambles |
| **Asset protection** | Preserves the investment you already made in design and development |

## Limitations & honest trade-offs

| Topic | Reality |
| --- | --- |
| **Cost** | Maintenance is a recurring line item — skipping it often costs more later |
| **Technical risk** | Poorly executed updates can conflict; backups and staging mitigate this |
| **Third-party limits** | Hosting, DNS, payment gateways, and SaaS APIs can still cause outages outside our control |
| **Prioritisation** | Teams sometimes defer maintenance until traffic drops or an incident occurs |

## Applicability across India

Website maintenance applies to **any organisation with a public or customer-facing site** — startups, MSMEs, professionals, NGOs, and larger enterprises — across states and UTs. There is usually **no separate state licence** for “maintaining” a private commercial website; requirements arise from **hosting contracts**, **consumer/e‑commerce rules** where relevant, and **data-protection expectations**.

| Region examples | Common contexts |
| --- | --- |
| Karnataka / Telangana | SaaS, product sites, high-change marketing pages |
| Maharashtra / NCR | Corporate, finance-adjacent, heavy traffic portals |
| Tamil Nadu / Gujarat | Manufacturing, exports, catalogues and dealer portals |
| Kerala / Goa / Rajasthan | Tourism, hospitality, seasonal campaigns |

> [!NOTE] Compliance angle
> If you collect personal data (forms, logins, payments), privacy notices and handling practices should stay aligned with applicable Indian law and any sector norms — maintenance often includes policy text and cookie/consent banner updates when integrations change.

## Features we typically cover

| Feature area | Details |
| --- | --- |
| **Backups** | Scheduled full + incremental backups; off-site copies; test restores |
| **Updates** | CMS / runtime / plugins / themes on staging → controlled production rollout |
| **Monitoring** | Uptime checks; alerts on downtime or SSL expiry |
| **Performance** | Core Web Vitals–oriented fixes; asset compression; caching strategy |
| **SEO hygiene** | Broken links; redirects; index coverage basics; structured data sanity checks |
| **Forms & spam** | Honeypots / CAPTCHA where appropriate; deliverability checks |

## Information we usually need

Handing over access securely (often via vault or least-privilege accounts) speeds everything up.

| Workstream | Typical access / inputs |
| --- | --- |
| **Hosting & DNS** | Hosting panel, FTP/SFTP or CI deploy hooks, DNS registrar |
| **CMS** | Admin login; roles for editor vs administrator |
| **Analytics & search** | Google Analytics / GA4, Google Search Console (property access) |
| **Brand & content** | Latest logos, brand guidelines, service copy, team/contact changes |
| **Integrations** | CRM, payment gateway test modes, newsletter tools, booking widgets |
| **E‑commerce** | Product feeds, shipping/returns policies, tax display preferences |

## Maintenance process

| Step | What happens |
| --- | --- |
| **1. Kickoff & scope** | Align SLA (response time), channels (email/ticket/chat), and excluded items |
| **2. Baseline audit** | Technical health, security posture, performance baseline, content backlog |
| **3. Backup first** | Full backup before material changes; verify integrity |
| **4. Staging updates** | Apply updates in a safe mirror; smoke-test critical journeys |
| **5. Controlled release** | Deploy to production in a window; monitor errors and speed |
| **6. Hardening & tune** | Security tweaks, caching rules, image pipeline adjustments |
| **7. Reporting** | Monthly summary: changes made, metrics, risks, next priorities |

## Typical costs in India (indicative)

Direct government fees rarely apply to **private site upkeep** itself; you mainly budget for **SSL**, **domain**, **hosting**, and **professional time**.

| Cost head | Typical range (indicative) | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Domain renewal** | ₹800 – ₹2,500 / year | Varies by TLD (.in / .com / premium domains) |
| **Hosting** | ₹3,000 – ₹60,000+ / year | Shared → VPS → cloud / dedicated |
| **SSL** | Free (Let's Encrypt) → paid OV/EV plans | Paid certs often bundled with hosting |
| **Professional maintenance** | ₹2,000 – ₹1,00,000+ / month | Driven by traffic, stack complexity, SLA, and content velocity |

| Plan band | Usually includes | Indicative monthly band |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Lite** | Monitoring, minor updates, backups | ₹2,000 – ₹6,000 |
| **Growth** | Security + performance tuning + SEO hygiene | ₹6,000 – ₹18,000 |
| **Scale / commerce** | Heavier QA, staging workflows, incident response | ₹18,000 – ₹60,000+ |

> [!IMPORTANT] Pricing reality check
> Exact pricing depends on stack (WordPress vs headless vs custom), plugin count, multilingual setups, catalogue size, integrations, and compliance needs. We scope after a short audit.

## Frequently asked questions

**How often should updates happen?** Critical security patches should be applied quickly after vendor release notes and testing. Routine CMS/plugin cycles are commonly weekly–monthly depending on risk and traffic.

**Can maintenance prevent all hacks?** No security posture is absolute; maintenance **materially reduces** risk through patching, hardening, backups, and monitoring.

**Will updates break my site?** There is always some regression risk. That is why we prefer **backups + staging + smoke tests** before touching production.

**Do you only support WordPress?** Many SMB sites run on WordPress, but we can maintain other stacks too — scope depends on your hosting and codebase access model.

**What is included in reporting?** Typically tasks completed, uptime summary, update log, performance snapshot, and a short backlog of recommended next actions.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Track Your Shipping Bill Status on ICEGATE
articles.excerpt: Step-by-step guide to tracking a shipping bill on ICEGATE — covers the public enquiry flow, logged-in flow, every status code explained, common errors like SB001 / SB105, LEO-to-EGM sequence, and an end-to-end real example.
articles.content:
## 1) Introduction

If you are an exporter in India, the shipping bill is not just a piece of paper — it is the lifeline of your export transaction. Every GST refund, every duty drawback claim, and every DGFT incentive you are entitled to flows directly from the status of this one document. Yet thousands of exporters lose refunds, miss deadlines, and face compliance gaps simply because they do not track their shipping bill status properly or do not understand what each status actually means.

**ICEGATE** — the Indian Customs Electronic Gateway — is the official CBIC portal that allows exporters to file, track, and download shipping bills in real time. This guide walks you through the exact steps to track your shipping bill status, explains every status code you will encounter, highlights the errors that trip up most exporters, and answers the questions that come up most often.

## 2) What is a Shipping Bill?

A shipping bill is the mandatory export declaration filed with Indian Customs when goods are being sent out of the country. It records the exporter's details, description and value of goods, port of loading, destination country, and any export incentives being claimed (such as IGST refund, duty drawback, or RoDTEP).

It is filed electronically on ICEGATE — either by the exporter directly or through a Customs House Agent (CHA). Once filed, it moves through a series of approval stages before the goods can physically leave the port.

## 3) Why Tracking Matters

Missing a single status update can snowball into blocked refunds, missed incentives, and compliance penalties. Here is what is at stake if you do not track your shipping bill closely:

| Reason | What Happens If You Don't Track |
| --- | --- |
| IGST refund processing | Refund gets stuck; funds blocked |
| LEO (Let Export Order) status | Goods cannot be loaded without LEO |
| EGM filing confirmation | IGST refund not released without EGM |
| EDPMS closure | Bank realization remains unreconciled |
| DGFT benefit claims | Scheme benefits delayed or rejected |
| Early detection of errors | Errors compound and become harder to fix |

## 4) Steps to Track Shipping Bill Status on ICEGATE

There are two methods — one without login (for quick status checks) and one with login (for full document access and downloads). Both are covered below.

### Method 1: Without Login (Public Enquiry — Fastest Option)

This is the quickest way to check current status. No registration or credentials required.

#### Step 1: Go to the ICEGATE Website

Open your browser and navigate to [www.icegate.gov.in](https://www.icegate.gov.in). Use a desktop or laptop — the portal works best on Chrome or Firefox with an updated browser version. Avoid mobile browsers for this task.

#### Step 2: Navigate to Public Enquiries

From the homepage, click on **Services** in the top navigation menu. From the dropdown, select **Enquiry**, then click on **Public Enquiries 1.0**.

#### Step 3: Select Document Status → Shipping Bill

On the enquiry page, you will see a list of document types on the left menu. Click on **Document Status**, and from the options that appear, select **Shipping Bill**.

#### Step 4: Enter the Required Details

You will need three pieces of information:

| Field | What to Enter | Where to Find It |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Location (Port Code) | 6-character port code (e.g. INBOM4 for JNPT Mumbai) | Your CHA or shipping documents |
| Shipping Bill Number | The SB number assigned at filing | Your CHA confirmation / export invoice |
| Shipping Bill Date | The date the SB was filed | Your CHA confirmation |

Enter all three fields accurately. Even a single-digit error will return no results.

#### Step 5: Click Submit and Read the Status

Click the **Submit** button. The system will display the current status of your shipping bill. Take a screenshot or note down the status for your records.

### Method 2: With Login (For Full Access and PDF Download)

#### Step 1: Register on ICEGATE (First-Time Users Only)

Visit [www.icegate.gov.in](https://www.icegate.gov.in) and click **Register**. You will need:

- IEC (Import Export Code)
- PAN
- GSTIN
- Active email address and mobile number for OTP authentication

Registration is free. Once approved, your login credentials are linked to your IEC.

#### Step 2: Log In to ICEGATE

Enter your username and password on the login page. Complete OTP verification if prompted.

#### Step 3: Navigate to Services → Document Status

From the homepage after login, go to the **Services** menu. Select **Enquiry** and then **Document Status**. Click on **Shipping Bill**.

#### Step 4: Enter SB Details and Submit

Enter your Shipping Bill Number, SB Date, and Port Code. Click **Submit**.

#### Step 5: View Status and Download Document

The status screen will show the current stage of your shipping bill. To download the PDF copy, go to **Services → Document Download → Shipping Bill**, enter the same details, and click **Submit**. Your PDF will appear with a download option.

## 5) Understanding Every Shipping Bill Status Code

Knowing what each status actually means is what separates informed exporters from confused ones.

| Status Label | What It Means | What You Should Do |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SB Submitted | Filed with Customs, awaiting processing | Wait; nothing required |
| SB Under Processing | Customs is reviewing the declaration | Monitor daily; contact CHA if stuck over 3 days |
| SB Assessed | Customs has finalized duties/drawback applicable | Confirm figures with CHA |
| Under Query | Customs needs clarification or additional documents | Respond immediately through CHA |
| SB Approved | Document verification completed by Customs | Prepare for physical examination if required |
| LEO Given | Let Export Order granted — goods cleared to load | Green light; cargo can now be physically loaded |
| EGM Filed | Carrier has filed Export General Manifest confirming vessel departure | IGST refund cycle begins; verify via ICEGATE |
| Rejected / SB Cancelled | Errors found; bill rejected | Correct all flagged issues and resubmit through CHA |

> [!IMPORTANT] The LEO date is critical
> The LEO (Let Export Order) date is the official date of export recognized by GST authorities, DGFT, and banks. Your IGST refund, RoDTEP, and EDPMS closure all reference the LEO date.
> If LEO shows on ICEGATE but your refund has not moved, the EGM may not yet be filed.

> [!TIP] The LEO → EGM sequence
> IGST refunds are processed only after **both** conditions are met — (1) LEO has been granted by Customs, and (2) EGM has been filed by the carrier (shipping line or airline).
> If LEO is granted but EGM is not filed, follow up directly with your shipping line or airline — this is their obligation, not Customs'.

## 6) Real Example: Tracking a Shipment End to End

Suppose you are an exporter based in Mumbai who shipped garments from JNPT on 5 March 2026. Your CHA gives you Shipping Bill Number **5234112** filed at JNPT (Port Code: **INBOM4**).

**Public Enquiry Input**

- Location: INBOM4
- SB Number: 5234112
- SB Date: 05/03/2026

Here is how the status typically evolves over the following days:

| Day | Status | What It Means |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Day 1 | SB Under Processing | Normal. Customs is reviewing your declaration. No action needed. |
| Day 2 | SB Assessed | Customs has reviewed and assessed the drawback. Verify the drawback amount with your CHA. |
| Day 3 | Under Query | Customs has a question — e.g. mismatch between invoice value and HS code. CHA must respond immediately. |
| Day 5 | LEO Given | Customs has cleared goods for export. Garments can now be physically loaded onto the vessel. |
| Day 8 | EGM Filed | Shipping line confirms the vessel has departed. IGST refund clock officially starts. |

> [!TIP] Cross-check on day 8
> Once EGM is filed, cross-check the refund initiation on the GSTN refund dashboard. Any mismatch against your GSTR-1 data should be resolved within 48 hours to keep the refund cycle on track.

## 7) Common Errors and How to Fix Them

### Error 1: "No Records Found" After Submitting Details

**Cause:** Incorrect shipping bill number, wrong date format, or wrong port code.

**Fix:** Verify the exact SB number and date with your CHA. Double-check the port code using the CBIC port code directory (available on the CBIC website). A common format issue is the date — on ICEGATE it must be in **DD/MM/YYYY** format.

### Error 2: Error Code SB001 — "SB Validation Failed"

**Cause:** The combination of SB Number, SB Date, and Port Code in your GST invoice does not match what was filed at ICES (Indian Customs EDI System). This is a very common IGST-refund blocker.

**Fix:** Amend your GST invoice details on the GST portal using **Table 9A in GSTR-1**. Once corrected, the data will re-transmit to Customs and the error will clear. Contact the GST Helpdesk if needed.

### Error 3: Error Code SB105 — "Invalid Shipping Bill Number"

**Cause:** The SB number entered does not match the valid format. Electronic shipping bills (ESB) have **7 digits**, manual shipping bills (MSB) have **6 digits**, and courier/postal shipping bills (CRSB) have **5 digits**. Entering the wrong digit count triggers SB105.

**Fix:** Confirm the exact SB number type with your CHA and re-enter with the correct number of digits.

### Error 4: Status Stuck on "SB Under Processing" for More Than 5 Days

**Cause:** Could be a mismatch in invoice details vs. the shipping bill, incorrect HS code, IEC discrepancy, or a manual hold placed by Customs.

**Fix:** Ask your CHA to check the backend for any customs queries or hold orders. Verify that your invoice value, goods description, and HS codes exactly match what was declared in the shipping bill.

### Error 5: LEO Granted But EGM Not Filed After 7+ Days

**Cause:** The shipping line has not filed the Export General Manifest. This is the carrier's statutory obligation under **Section 41 of the Customs Act, 1962**.

**Fix:** Contact your shipping line or freight forwarder directly. Request the vessel/voyage details and ask for confirmation of EGM filing. Escalation path — contact the port's Shipping Master office if the carrier is unresponsive.

### Error 6: Shipping Bill Not Appearing on DGFT Portal

**Cause:** Either (a) LEO has not been granted yet, (b) EGM has not been filed, or (c) a temporary technical sync delay between ICEGATE and the DGFT system.

**Fix:** Confirm LEO and EGM status on ICEGATE first. If both are green, wait 48–72 hours for DGFT sync. If still not reflecting after 72 hours, contact DGFT's helpdesk with your SB number and LEO date.

### Error 7: Portal Not Loading / OTP Not Received

**Cause:** ICEGATE experiences heavy load during peak hours (9 AM–6 PM on weekdays). OTP issues often arise from outdated contact details in your ICEGATE profile.

**Fix:** Try accessing the portal during off-peak hours (early morning or late evening). Clear your browser cache. Update your registered mobile number and email in the ICEGATE profile. For persistent portal issues, email the ICEGATE helpdesk at **helpdesk@icegate.gov.in**.

### Common Mistake Summary

| Mistake | Impact | Prevention |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Wrong port code entered | "No Records Found" | Keep CBIC port code list handy |
| Invoice GSTIN doesn't match SB | SB001 error; IGST refund blocked | Verify GSTIN before filing |
| Wrong SB number format | SB105 error | Confirm ESB/MSB/CRSB type with CHA |
| Not tracking after LEO | EGM delays go unnoticed | Check ICEGATE daily for 5–7 days post-sailing |
| Ignoring "Under Query" status | SB stuck; refund delayed indefinitely | Set up daily monitoring; respond within 48 hours |
| Not registering on ICEGATE | Can only check status, cannot download | Register once; saves time permanently |

## 8) Alternative Ways to Track (Beyond the Portal)

- **Via SMS** — Send your shipping bill details to the designated ICEGATE SMS number for quick status updates (provider charges may apply).
- **Via email enquiry** — Email the ICEGATE helpdesk with your SB number, port code, and submission date. You will receive a reply with the current status.
- **Via your CHA** — Your Customs House Agent has backend access to customs systems and can often provide real-time status faster than the portal, especially for complex queries.
- **Via logistics partner portal** — Many freight forwarders and logistics platforms integrate ICEGATE data and offer dashboard-level tracking.

## 9) FAQs

### Q1. Can I track my shipping bill status without an ICEGATE login?

Yes. You can check live status — including LEO status and EGM status — without any login using the Public Enquiry facility (**Services → Enquiry → Public Enquiries 1.0 → Document Status → Shipping Bill**). However, to download the official digitally signed PDF copy of your shipping bill, you must be a registered and logged-in user on ICEGATE.

### Q2. How often is the shipping bill status updated on ICEGATE?

The ICEGATE portal typically updates status information once every 24 hours. However, for critical milestones like **LEO granted** or **EGM filed**, updates can reflect sooner — often within a few hours of the event. Do not expect real-time, minute-by-minute updates.

### Q3. My shipping bill shows LEO granted but my IGST refund has not come through. Why?

Two conditions must both be satisfied for IGST refund processing to begin — (1) LEO must be granted by Customs, and (2) EGM must be filed by the carrier. If EGM is not yet filed, your refund will not move regardless of LEO status. Check the EGM status on ICEGATE and follow up with your shipping line if needed. Also verify there are no SB001 or other validation errors against your GST filings.

### Q4. What is the difference between a Shipping Bill and a Bill of Entry?

A **Shipping Bill** is the customs document filed for **exports** — goods going out of India. A **Bill of Entry** is the customs document filed for **imports** — goods coming into India. Both are filed on ICEGATE but under separate tracking sections. They are distinct documents and should not be confused.

### Q5. Can I download shipping bills from years ago on ICEGATE?

Yes. ICEGATE provides access to older documents. You can download shipping bills filed months or even years ago, as long as you enter the correct SB number, port code, and date. The download service is free — ICEGATE does not charge for document access. Any charges would only arise if you use a CHA or third-party consultant to assist.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on SUV Cars in India 2026
articles.excerpt: Understand the complete GST and cess structure on SUV cars in India, including the three criteria that trigger the 22% cess, real-world pricing examples, and EV tax benefits.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Sport Utility Vehicles have become the dominant vehicle segment in India, accounting for over 50% of all passenger vehicle sales. Names like Mahindra Scorpio-N, Tata Safari, Hyundai Creta, and Toyota Fortuner dominate every car dealership. But owning an SUV in India comes at a steep tax price.

SUVs in India attract among the highest GST rates of any consumer product: **28% GST plus a compensation cess that can go up to 22%**, making the total tax burden as high as 50% in some categories. Understanding exactly how this works is essential before you sign on the dotted line.

## How GST on SUVs Is Calculated

GST on automobiles is levied on the ex-factory price (the price before dealer margins and other charges). The rate consists of:

- **Base GST:** 28%
- **Compensation Cess:** Varies by vehicle specifications

For SUVs specifically, the GST Council has defined a clear set of criteria that determine the cess rate.

## SUV Definition Under GST

A vehicle is classified as an SUV for the purpose of attracting 22% cess only if it meets **ALL three** of the following conditions:

1. Engine capacity exceeds 1,500cc
2. Length exceeds 4,000mm
3. Ground clearance is 170mm or above (unladen)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a vehicle fails to meet even **one** of these criteria, it falls into a different cess category.

## GST Rate Table for SUVs and Passenger Vehicles (2026)

| Vehicle Type | GST | Cess | Total Tax |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| SUV (all 3 SUV criteria met) | 28% | 22% | 50% |
| Utility vehicle not meeting all SUV criteria | 28% | 15% | 43% |
| Large / luxury cars | 28% | 20% | 48% |
| Mid-size petrol cars (>1200cc, length >4m, or hybrid) | 28% | 15% | 43% |
| Small petrol cars (<1200cc, length <4m) | 28% | 1% | 29% |
| Small diesel cars (<1500cc, length <4m) | 28% | 3% | 31% |
| Electric SUVs | 5% | Nil | 5% |
| Hybrid SUVs | 28% | 15% | 43% |

## Popular SUVs and Their Tax Classification (2026)

| Model | Engine | Length | Ground Cl. | Cess | Total GST |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Mahindra Scorpio-N | 2184cc | 4662mm | 200mm | 22% | 50% |
| Tata Safari | 1956cc | 4661mm | 205mm | 22% | 50% |
| Toyota Fortuner | 2755cc | 4795mm | 221mm | 22% | 50% |
| Hyundai Creta (1.5L) | 1493cc | 4300mm | 190mm | 15% | 43%\* |
| Kia Seltos (1.5L) | 1497cc | 4315mm | 190mm | 15% | 43%\* |
| Maruti Brezza | 1462cc | 3995mm | 198mm | 3% | 31%\*\* |
| Tata Nexon | 1497cc | 3994mm | 209mm | 3% | 31%\*\* |
| MG Hector (2.0L diesel) | 1956cc | 4720mm | 182mm | 22% | 50% |
| Jeep Meridian | 1956cc | 4763mm | 201mm | 22% | 50% |
| Tata Nexon EV | Electric | 3994mm | N/A | Nil | 5% |

> [!NOTE]
> \*Engine below 1500cc, so cess is 15% not 22%.
> \*\*Length below 4000mm, classified as small utility vehicle.
