- GST: Not applicable

## 3) Brokerage Charges

- GST: 18% Industry Impact

### Growth of Investment Culture

No GST encourages more participation.

### Rise of Fintech Platforms

Simplified tax structure supports digital trading.

### Compliance for Brokers

Service providers must manage GST carefully.

### Market Expansion

Increased accessibility for retail investors.

## Conclusion

The GST exemption on the sale and purchase of securities ensures that investment transactions remain tax-efficient and accessible. While securities themselves are not taxed, related services such as brokerage and advisory fees are subject to GST. Understanding this distinction is essential for investors and businesses to ensure proper compliance. As India’s financial markets continue to grow, GST policies will play a supportive role in maintaining efficiency and transparency.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Photography Services: Rates, SAC Codes & Compliance Guide
articles.excerpt: Complete GST guide for photographers in India: 18% rate, SAC codes for photography, videography, stock licensing, and drone services, ITC eligibility, registration threshold, and real-world examples.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Photography in India has evolved from a simple portrait service to a sophisticated, multi-crore industry spanning wedding photography, commercial shoots, wildlife photography, editorial assignments, product photography for e-commerce, and drone cinematography. Every Indian wedding today is incomplete without a team of photographers and videographers.

As the industry has professionalised, its GST obligations have become more structured and more scrutinised. Whether you are a solo freelance photographer or a large wedding photography studio, understanding GST applicability is critical to running your business compliantly and profitably.

## GST Rate on Photography Services

Photography services are classified under **SAC 998386** and are subject to GST at **18%**.

| Service Type | GST Rate | SAC Code |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Wedding photography (stills) | 18% | 998386 |
| Wedding videography | 18% | 998386 |
| Commercial / product photography | 18% | 998386 |
| Fashion / editorial photography | 18% | 998386 |
| Aerial / drone photography | 18% | 998386 |
| Wildlife / nature photography | 18% | 998386 |
| School / institutional photography | 18% | 998386 |
| Passport / ID photographs | 18% | 998386 |
| Medical / forensic photography | 18% | 998386 |
| Photo album production | 18% | 998386 / 998912 |
| Photo printing (lab services) | 18% | 998912 |
| Studio rental (photography) | 18% | 997212 |
| Photography equipment rental | 18% | 997319 |
| Online photo editing (services) | 18% | 998314 |

## SAC Code 998386: What It Covers

SAC 998386 specifically refers to *"Photography and videography and their related services."*

**This includes:**

- Taking and processing photographs
- Digitising photographs (scanning)
- Aerial photography using drones
- Video recording and post-production related to photography
- Photocopying services

**It does NOT include:**

- Filmmaking or motion picture production (different SAC under 998392)
- Broadcasting or live streaming of events (different classification)
- Sale of photographs as artworks (potentially classified differently)

## Registration Threshold for Photographers

Individual photographers and small studios frequently ask whether they need to register for GST:

- If annual turnover exceeds **Rs. 20 lakh** (Rs. 10 lakh in special category states / hill states): Mandatory GST registration.
- If providing services to clients in other states (**inter-state supply**): Mandatory GST registration regardless of turnover.
- If using an **e-commerce platform** (like social media marketplaces, stock photo sites): Registration may be required under the e-commerce operator provisions.

> [!TIP]
> A wedding photographer earning Rs. 15 lakh from local clients in the same state need not register. But if they travel to photograph a destination wedding in another state, they must register.

## Composition Scheme: Is It Available to Photographers?

Service providers (including photographers) can opt for the **Composition Scheme for Services** (QRMP-adjacent scheme), but only if:

- Their annual turnover does not exceed Rs. 50 lakh.
- They are not interstate service providers.
- They pay 6% GST (3% CGST + 3% SGST) on turnover without ITC.

For most photographers, the composition scheme is not ideal because they incur significant GST on equipment, lenses, drones, and storage, which they cannot claim as ITC under composition.

## ITC Claimable by Photographers

GST-registered photographers can claim ITC on:

- Cameras and lenses (18% GST on purchase)
- Drones (18% GST on purchase)
- Lighting equipment (18%)
- Editing software subscriptions (18%)
- Studio rent (18%)
- Photography props and sets (12-18%)
- Travel expenses (air / train / cab for shoots) (5-18%)
- Memory cards, hard drives (18%)
- Editing workstations and computers (18%)
- Albums and print materials (12-18%)

This makes GST registration financially beneficial for photographers with significant equipment expenses, as ITC offsets their output tax liability.

## Real-World Examples

**Example 1 — Freelance Wedding Photographer:** Rohan Photography charges Rs. 2,50,000 for a wedding shoot. Annual turnover: Rs. 18 lakh. Since this is below Rs. 20 lakh and all clients are local, Rohan is not required to register for GST. He does not charge or collect GST.

**Example 2 — Mid-Size Wedding Studio:** Prism Studios charges Rs. 3,50,000 for a wedding photography and videography package (annual turnover: Rs. 55 lakh). GST at 18% = Rs. 63,000. The couple pays Rs. 4,13,000. Prism Studios claims ITC on cameras (Rs. 1,20,000 camera bought in the year, Rs. 21,600 GST), editing workstation (Rs. 80,000 + Rs. 14,400 GST), etc. Net GST payable is reduced by the ITC claimed.

**Example 3 — E-Commerce Product Photography:** Pixel Perfect is a commercial photography studio that shoots products for Myntra and Amazon sellers. They charge Rs. 50,000 per brand per month. Annual turnover: Rs. 6 lakh / month = Rs. 72 lakh / year. GST at 18% = Rs. 9,000 per client per month. Clients who are GST-registered businesses can claim full ITC on this expense.

**Example 4 — Stock Photography:** A nature photographer sells images on international platforms like Shutterstock and Getty Images. Revenue received in foreign currency for images used by international clients qualifies as **export of services** (zero-rated). The photographer can claim ITC refunds on equipment if they submit proper documentation.

## GST on Photo Albums and Print Services

Many photographers bundle albums, prints, and photobooks in their packages. The GST on these products:

| Product | GST Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Photo albums (customised) | 18% |
| Photo books (printed) | 12% (printed matter, HSN 4901) |
| Photo prints | 18% |
| Frames | 12-18% |

If a photography studio sells a "packaged delivery" with stills, video, and an album, it is a composite supply where photography is the principal service. The entire package is taxed at **18%**.

## Drone Photography: Regulatory and GST Notes

Drone photography has specific layers:

- **GST on drone photography services:** 18%
- **GST on purchase of drone:** 18%
- **DGCA registration** requirement for commercial drones: Mandatory for commercial operators
- **Drone import** (DJI, etc.): Attracts customs duty + 18% IGST
- The DPIIT has made specific recommendations to reduce GST on drones to 5% to support the growing drone industry; this is under consideration but not implemented as of 2026.

## Industry Impact

The 18% GST on photography services has had the following effects.

**Positive:**

- Formalisation of the industry; clients now expect GST invoices and prefer registered studios.
- ITC availability has reduced the effective cost of expensive equipment for registered photographers.
- Clearer classification has eliminated the earlier ambiguity between service tax on photography and VAT on albums / prints.

**Challenges:**

- Price-sensitive clients (especially in smaller cities) resist the 18% GST addition.
- Many photographers operate informally below the threshold, creating an uneven competitive landscape.
- Destination wedding photographers who travel interstate face mandatory registration obligations even at moderate turnover levels.
- The composition scheme is practically not useful for photographers due to the inability to claim ITC on high-value equipment.

## Key Insights

1. Photography studios should separate the charge for services (18%) from photo prints and albums in the invoice; this allows clients to see the exact GST component and aids in ITC claims.
2. International clients (or Indian clients using images for export-linked purposes) should be carefully evaluated for zero-rating benefits.
3. Drone operators must ensure DGCA compliance independently of GST compliance; the two regulatory frameworks are separate.
4. Software subscriptions (Adobe Creative Cloud, Lightroom) used for editing photography work attract 18% GST; maintain proper invoices for ITC claims.
5. Photographers should maintain a clear turnover ledger throughout the year to know when they are approaching the Rs. 20 lakh registration threshold.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. A photographer is paid a Rs. 10,000 advance for a wedding shoot that is later cancelled. The client forfeits the advance. Is GST payable on the forfeited advance?

Yes. GST is payable on advances received for services at the time of receipt (point of taxation). When the shoot is cancelled and the advance is forfeited, the photographer has collected GST on the advance and must remit it. They cannot reverse the tax paid since the cancellation fee is treated as consideration for the service of tolerating the act of cancellation.

### Q2. If a photographer wins a prize for a photo in an international competition, is the prize money subject to GST?

Prize money received from a competition is not consideration for a supply of services. It is a reward / recognition. GST does not apply to prize money. However, if the winning photograph is subsequently licensed for commercial use, the licensing fee attracts GST.

### Q3. Does GST apply to photography tutorials sold online by a photographer?

Online photography tutorials, courses, and workshops sold via digital platforms (YouTube paid courses, Udemy, personal websites) are classified as online information and database access or retrieval services (**OIDAR**) and attract 18% GST. Photographers selling courses must register if their revenue from these exceeds the applicable threshold.

### Q4. Is there a difference in GST treatment between photos taken during a corporate event vs. personal event?

No. The GST rate (18%) is the same regardless of whether the photography is for a corporate event or a personal celebration. The difference is in ITC: a corporate client can claim ITC on the 18% GST paid to the photographer; an individual hiring a photographer for a personal occasion cannot.

### Q5. How is GST handled when a photography studio collaborates with a freelance photographer and pays them for their work?

If the freelance photographer is GST-registered and charges the studio for their sub-contracted services, the studio pays 18% GST to the freelancer and claims it as ITC. If the freelancer is unregistered (below threshold), no GST is charged between them, and the studio cannot claim ITC on that portion. The studio charges 18% to the end client on the full package regardless.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 115BAC New Tax Regime: Slabs, Deductions, Exemptions & Benefits
articles.excerpt: Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961 introduced the new tax regime to simplify income tax calculation for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs). Initially optional, it has now become the default tax regime for taxpayers,…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961 introduced the new tax regime to simplify income tax calculation for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs). Initially optional, it has now become the default tax regime for taxpayers, though individuals can still choose the old regime if beneficial. The new regime offers lower tax rates but removes most deductions and exemptions available under the old system. The idea is to:

- Simplify tax filing
- Reduce dependency on tax-saving investments
- Provide flexibility in income usage

### The regime is particularly beneficial for

- Salaried individuals without major deductions
- Young professionals
- Freelancers

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Lower Tax Rates

Reduced slab rates compared to old regime.

### Simplified Tax Filing

No need to track multiple deductions.

### Default Regime

Automatically applied unless opted out.

### Higher Rebate Benefit

Rebate under Section 87A extended up to ₹7 lakh income.

### Flexibility

No forced investments for tax saving.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### No Major Deductions

### Cannot claim

- Section 80C
- HRA
- LTA

### Not Ideal for High Deduction Taxpayers

People with home loans or investments may lose benefits.

### Limited Tax Planning

Fewer ways to reduce taxable income.

### Confusion Between Regimes

Taxpayers must compare both regimes.

### Switching Restrictions

Business income taxpayers have limits on switching.

## 4) Applicability in India (State-wise Context)

The new tax regime applies uniformly across India. Urban Taxpayers

- Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore

Prefer new regime due to higher salaries and fewer deductions Middle-Class Taxpayers

- Tier 2 cities like Jaipur, Lucknow Often compare both regimes Self-Employed Professionals
- Across all states Choose based on deduction eligibility

👉 No state-wise variation, purely central income tax.

## 5) Features

### Tax Slabs

- Up to ₹3 lakh: Nil
- ₹3–6 lakh: 5%
- ₹6–9 lakh: 10%
- ₹9–12 lakh: 15%
- ₹12–15 lakh: 20%
- Above ₹15 lakh: 30%

### Standard Deduction

₹50,000 available for salaried individuals.

### Rebate under Section 87A

No tax up to ₹7 lakh income.

### No Deductions

Most exemptions removed.

### Default Regime

Automatically applied unless opted out.

## 6) Documents List

Basic Documents

- PAN Card
- Aadhaar Card Income Documents
- Salary slips
- Form 16 Other Documents
- Bank statements
- Investment proofs (if comparing regimes)

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Calculate Income** — Determine total income from all sources.
2. **Compare Regimes** — Evaluate old vs new regime benefits.
3. **Choose Regime** — Opt for new regime or switch to old.
4. **Inform Employer** — Declare choice for TDS calculation.
5. **File ITR** — Select regime while filing return.
6. **Pay Tax** — Based on applicable slabs.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Income Tax

As per slab rates

### Surcharge

Applicable on higher incomes

### Cess

4% Health and Education cess

### Penalties

Applicable for late filing

## Conclusion

Section 115BAC has significantly transformed India’s income tax system by offering a simplified and flexible approach. While it benefits taxpayers who do not rely heavily on deductions, it may not be suitable for those with significant investments or exemptions. Choosing between the old and new regime requires careful evaluation, but for many individuals, the new regime offers a straightforward and hassle-free taxation method.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: ITR filing 2024-25: Which ITR Form Should You Use?
articles.excerpt: Filing your Income Tax Return (ITR) with the wrong form is not a minor clerical issue. The Income Tax Department treats it as a defective return under Section 139(9), which means you will receive a notice and be require…
articles.content:
# ITR filing 2024-25: which ITR form should you use?

## Why choosing the right ITR form matters

Filing your Income Tax Return (ITR) with the wrong form is not a minor clerical issue. The Income Tax Department treats it as a defective return under Section 139(9), which means you will receive a notice and be required to refile. In some cases, a defective return is treated as if it was never filed, exposing you to penalties for late filing.

With the Assessment Year 2024-25 (Financial Year 2023-24), the ITR form landscape has seen some updates in eligibility criteria and disclosures. This guide walks you through every form, its applicability, and exactly who should use which one, with examples to make the decision straightforward.

## Overview of all ITR forms (AY 2024-25)

| Form | Who can use it | Who cannot use it |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ITR-1 (Sahaj) | Resident individuals with income up to Rs. 50 lakhs from salary, one house property, other sources | Directors, shareholders in unlisted companies, NRIs, those with capital gains or business income |
| ITR-2 | Individuals and HUFs without business or professional income | Those with business or professional income |
| ITR-3 | Individuals and HUFs with income from business or profession | Those without business income (use ITR-1 or ITR-2) |
| ITR-4 (Sugam) | Individuals, HUFs, firms (not LLPs) using presumptive taxation | NRIs, directors, those with income above Rs. 50 lakhs (for individuals) |
| ITR-5 | Firms, LLPs, AOPs, BOIs, cooperatives, local authorities | Individuals, HUFs, companies |
| ITR-6 | Companies (other than those claiming Section 11 exemption) | Non-companies |
| ITR-7 | Charitable trusts, political parties, research institutions, universities, others under Section 139(4A) to 139(4F) | Others |

## Detailed breakdown: form by form

### ITR-1 (Sahaj): the simplest form

ITR-1 is designed for salaried individuals whose financial life is relatively straightforward.

**Eligible to file ITR-1 if:**

- You are a resident individual (not NRI or RNOR)
- Total income does not exceed Rs. 50 lakhs
- Income sources are: salary or pension, one house property (excluding brought-forward losses), and other sources (interest, dividends, family pension)
- Agricultural income is up to Rs. 5,000

**Cannot use ITR-1 if:**

- You are a director in any company
- You held unlisted equity shares at any time during the year
- You have capital gains of any nature
- You have income from more than one house property
- You have foreign income or foreign assets
- Tax has been deducted under Section 194N (cash withdrawals)
- You have deferred income tax on ESOPs

**Example:** Asha is a school teacher earning Rs. 6.5 lakhs in salary and Rs. 18,000 as bank interest. She owns one home (self-occupied). She should file ITR-1.

### ITR-2: for individuals with capital gains or multiple properties

ITR-2 is the most commonly used form for individuals who have sold shares, mutual funds, or property, or who have more complex income profiles.

**Eligible to file ITR-2 if:**

- You are an individual or HUF
- You have capital gains (short-term or long-term) from shares, mutual funds, property, etc.
- You own more than one house property
- You are an NRI
- You are a director in a company
- You hold unlisted equity shares
- Your income exceeds Rs. 50 lakhs (but no business income)
- You have foreign assets or foreign income

**Cannot use ITR-2 if:**

- You have income from business or profession (use ITR-3)

**Example:** Vikram is a software engineer earning Rs. 18 lakhs in salary. He sold mutual funds and made LTCG of Rs. 2.3 lakhs and STCG of Rs. 80,000. He should file ITR-2.

### ITR-3: for professionals and business owners

ITR-3 is for individuals or HUFs who run a business or practice a profession, whether under regular accounting or presumptive taxation schemes.

**Eligible if:**

- You have income from proprietary business
- You are a freelancer, consultant, or self-employed professional
- You are a partner in a firm (though the firm files ITR-5 separately)
- You have business income along with salary, capital gains, or other income

**Example:** Dr. Anand runs a private clinic and earns Rs. 25 lakhs from his practice. He also receives Rs. 4 lakhs in rent and made LTCG of Rs. 1.2 lakhs from selling mutual funds. All this is reported in ITR-3.

### ITR-4 (Sugam): presumptive taxation made easy

ITR-4 is for those opting for presumptive taxation under Sections 44AD (business), 44ADA (professionals), or 44AE (transporters).

| Scheme | Who can use | Deemed profit rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Section 44AD | Small businesses with turnover up to Rs. 3 crore (Rs. 2 crore if cash receipts exceed 5%) | 8% of turnover (6% for digital receipts) |
| Section 44ADA | Specified professionals with receipts up to Rs. 75 lakhs | 50% of gross receipts |
| Section 44AE | Goods vehicle operators (up to 10 vehicles) | Rs. 7,500 per vehicle per month |

**Cannot use ITR-4 if:**

- Turnover exceeds the applicable limit
- You are an NRI
- You have capital gains
- You have more than one house property
- You hold foreign assets
- You are a director in a company

**Example:** Ramesh runs a small grocery store with annual sales of Rs. 80 lakhs, all via digital payments. He opts for Section 44AD and declares 6% of Rs. 80 lakhs = Rs. 4.8 lakhs as profit. He files ITR-4.

### ITR-5: for firms, LLPs, and AOPs

Firms (including partnership firms), LLPs, cooperative societies, local authorities, and Associations of Persons (AOPs) must file ITR-5.

**Example:** Mehta & Shah LLP, a two-partner accounting firm, files ITR-5 to report the LLP's income, deductions, and tax payable.

### ITR-6: for companies

All companies registered under the Companies Act, except those claiming exemption under Section 11 (charitable or religious organisations), must file ITR-6.

### ITR-7: for trusts and exempt entities

Section 11 trusts, political parties, research associations, universities, hospitals, and similar entities file ITR-7.

## Quick decision chart

| Your profile | Correct form |
| --- | --- |
| Salaried, income under Rs. 50 lakhs, no capital gains | ITR-1 |
| Salaried with capital gains or multiple properties | ITR-2 |
| NRI with Indian income | ITR-2 |
| Director of a company with no business income | ITR-2 |
| Freelancer or consultant (non-presumptive) | ITR-3 |
| Small business using 44AD or professional using 44ADA | ITR-4 |
| Partnership firm or LLP | ITR-5 |
| Private or public company | ITR-6 |
| Trust, temple, political party, university | ITR-7 |

## Key dates: AY 2024-25

| Category | Due date |
| --- | --- |
| Individuals (non-audit) | July 31, 2024 |
| Businesses or professionals requiring audit | October 31, 2024 |
| Transfer pricing cases | November 30, 2024 |
| Belated or revised return | December 31, 2024 |

## Penalties for wrong or late filing

| Default | Consequence |
| --- | --- |
| Filing wrong form (defective return) | Notice under Section 139(9); must refile |
| Late filing (income above Rs. 5 lakhs) | Rs. 5,000 penalty under Section 234F |
| Late filing (income up to Rs. 5 lakhs) | Rs. 1,000 penalty under Section 234F |
| Non-filing | Prosecution under Section 276CC in severe cases |
| Missing belated return deadline | Cannot carry forward capital or business losses |

## Frequently asked questions

**Q1. I am salaried but also sold some stocks this year. Can I still file ITR-1?**

No. Any capital gains, whether from shares, mutual funds, or property, disqualify you from using ITR-1. Even if the capital gain is as small as Rs. 500, you must file ITR-2. This is a common mistake among salaried employees who invest in stocks or mutual funds and assume ITR-1 covers everything.

**Q2. I am a freelance graphic designer earning Rs. 40 lakhs. Which form do I use?**

You have two options. If your gross receipts are up to Rs. 75 lakhs and you are a specified professional (design may or may not qualify; check the CBDT list), you can opt for Section 44ADA (50% deemed profit) and file ITR-4. If you prefer regular accounting with actual expenses, you must file ITR-3. Since graphic design may not always be in the specified list, consulting a CA is advisable.

**Q3. My employer already deducted all applicable TDS. Do I still need to file an ITR?**

Yes, if your gross total income before deductions exceeds the basic exemption limit. Even if your net tax liability is zero after TDS, filing an ITR is mandatory for most individuals above the threshold. Additionally, filing an ITR is required if you want to carry forward losses, claim refunds, or apply for visa or loans that require ITR proofs.

**Q4. I am an NRI with only a fixed deposit interest income from India of Rs. 60,000. Which form applies?**

NRIs cannot use ITR-1 regardless of income level. You must use ITR-2 even if your only Indian income is interest from an NRO account. Note that TDS on NRI interest is typically deducted at 30% (or lower per DTAA), and ITR-2 allows you to claim the refund if excess TDS was deducted.

**Q5. Can I switch from old to new tax regime while filing my ITR?**

Salaried individuals who have no business income can switch between the old and new regimes every year at the time of filing their ITR. However, individuals with business income (filing ITR-3 or ITR-4) can switch out of the new regime only once in a lifetime. Once they opt out, they cannot re-enter the new regime (except by discontinuing business income). The choice of regime should be made carefully based on deductions and investments.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Which ITR Form to File: A Complete Guide to Choosing the Right Form
articles.excerpt: A complete guide to all 7 ITR forms — ITR-1 through ITR-7 — with eligibility criteria, who should file which, worked examples for salaried employees, freelancers, NRIs, and business owners, plus an elimination checklist.
articles.content:
Filing the wrong ITR form is not a minor clerical error. It results in a defective return notice from the Income Tax Department, forcing you to refile — sometimes with a late penalty attached. The Indian ITR system has seven distinct forms, each designed for a specific taxpayer profile. This guide helps you identify the right one in minutes.

## Overview of All ITR Forms

| Form | For | Cannot be used if |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ITR-1 (Sahaj) | Resident individuals: salary/pension ≤ Rs. 50L, 1 house property, other sources (interest, dividends) | You have capital gains, >1 house property, foreign assets, business income, or income >Rs. 50L |
| ITR-2 | Individuals and HUFs: income >Rs. 50L, capital gains, multiple house properties, foreign income/assets, NRIs | You have business or professional income |
| ITR-3 | Individuals and HUFs with business or professional income, partners in a firm | You opt for presumptive taxation (use ITR-4 instead) |
| ITR-4 (Sugam) | Individuals, HUFs, firms (not LLP) opting for presumptive taxation under 44AD, 44ADA, or 44AE | Income >Rs. 50L, or you have capital gains, or you are an NRI |
| ITR-5 | Firms, LLPs, AOPs, BOIs, co-operative societies | Not for individuals or companies |
| ITR-6 | Companies (other than those claiming exemption u/s 11) | Charitable trusts and NGOs |
| ITR-7 | Trusts, political parties, institutions under Sections 139(4A) to 139(4F) | Not for regular individuals or businesses |

## Detailed Eligibility: Key Decision Points

### ITR-1 (Sahaj) — Simplest Form, Most Restrictions

You can use ITR-1 if ALL of the following are true:
- You are a resident individual (not NRI or RNOR)
- Total income does not exceed Rs. 50 lakh
- Income is from salary or pension, one house property, and/or other sources (interest, family pension)
- You do not have capital gains of any kind
- You do not have foreign assets or income
- You are not a director in a company or an unlisted company shareholder

**Common mistake:** Many salaried employees with mutual fund redemptions or stock sales assume ITR-1 is fine. Any capital gain — even small STCG from one fund redemption — disqualifies you from ITR-1.

### ITR-2 — For Investors and HNIs

Use ITR-2 if you are an individual or HUF and:
- You have capital gains (equity, property, gold, mutual funds)
- You have more than one house property
- Your income exceeds Rs. 50 lakh
- You are an NRI with Indian income
- You hold foreign assets or have foreign income
- You are a company director (even if salary is below Rs. 50L)

### ITR-3 — For Business Owners and Professionals

Use ITR-3 if you are an individual or HUF with:
- Income from a proprietary business
- Professional income (doctor, architect, CA, consultant) where you are NOT opting for presumptive taxation
- You are a partner in a partnership firm (you report your share of profit here)

Also use ITR-3 if you have business/professional income in addition to salary and capital gains.

### ITR-4 (Sugam) — Presumptive Taxation Made Simple

Use ITR-4 if:
- You are an individual, HUF, or firm (not LLP) opting for presumptive taxation
- Business income under Section 44AD (turnover ≤ Rs. 3 crore): declare 8% (or 6% for digital receipts) as profit
- Professional income under Section 44ADA (receipts ≤ Rs. 75 lakh): declare 50% as profit
- Transport business under Section 44AE

Restriction: If total income exceeds Rs. 50 lakh, or if you have capital gains, you must use ITR-3 instead.

## Examples: Who Should File Which Form

**Example 1: Salaried employee with mutual fund gains**
Amit earns Rs. 12L salary and sold ELSS funds for Rs. 30,000 LTCG. He also has a savings account interest of Rs. 8,000.
→ Cannot use ITR-1 (capital gains disqualify). **Must use ITR-2.**

**Example 2: Freelance content writer**
Pooja earns Rs. 8L from freelancing and opts for 50% presumptive deduction under 44ADA.
→ Uses **ITR-4** (income under Rs. 50L, no capital gains, opts for 44ADA).

**Example 3: Small business owner**
Ravi runs a kirana store with Rs. 80L annual turnover, wants to opt for 44AD presumptive taxation.
→ Uses **ITR-4** (turnover under Rs. 3 crore, opts for 44AD).

**Example 4: Doctor with clinic + investments**
Dr. Sharma earns Rs. 35L from her clinic (does not opt for presumptive taxation) plus Rs. 1.2L in LTCG from stocks.
→ Cannot use ITR-4 (has capital gains + does not opt for presumptive). **Must use ITR-3.**

## Quick Elimination Checklist

Answer yes/no to find your form:

1. Do you have business or professional income? → If YES: ITR-3 or ITR-4
2. Do you opt for presumptive taxation (44AD/44ADA/44AE) AND income < Rs. 50L AND no capital gains? → ITR-4
3. Do you have capital gains? → If YES: ITR-2 or ITR-3 (depending on whether you also have business income)
4. Are you an NRI or hold foreign assets? → ITR-2 or ITR-3
5. Is your income only from salary/pension, ≤ Rs. 50L, no capital gains, resident individual? → ITR-1
6. Is the return for a firm, LLP, or AOP? → ITR-5
7. Is the return for a company? → ITR-6
8. Is the return for a trust, political party, or Section 8 company? → ITR-7

## Key Filing Deadlines

| Category | Due Date |
| --- | --- |
| Individuals not requiring audit | 31 July |
| Individuals requiring audit | 31 October |
| Transfer pricing cases | 30 November |
| Belated return | 31 December |

**Late filing penalty under Section 234F:**
- Rs. 1,000 if total income ≤ Rs. 5 lakh
- Rs. 5,000 if total income > Rs. 5 lakh

## Common Mistakes

1. Using ITR-1 when you have any capital gains — even small LTCG from equity funds.
2. Using ITR-4 when income exceeds Rs. 50 lakh — you must switch to ITR-3.
3. Partners in firms filing ITR-1 instead of ITR-3.
4. NRIs filing ITR-1 (NRIs must use ITR-2 or ITR-3).
5. Not reporting exempt income (like LTCG under Rs. 1 lakh) — it still needs to be disclosed even if not taxable.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. I have only salary income but also sold one mutual fund unit. Do I still need ITR-2?**
Yes. Any capital gains transaction — even for a nominal amount — disqualifies you from ITR-1. You must use ITR-2 if you have no business income.

**Q2. Can I use ITR-4 if I have both business income (44AD) and salary income?**
Yes, provided your total income does not exceed Rs. 50 lakh and you do not have capital gains or foreign income.

**Q3. What happens if I file using the wrong ITR form?**
The return will be treated as defective under Section 139(9). You will receive a notice giving you 15 days to refile with the correct form. If you miss that window, the return may be treated as invalid.

**Q4. I am an NRI. Can I use any ITR form?**
NRIs cannot use ITR-1 regardless of income level. They must use ITR-2 (if no business income) or ITR-3 (if they have business or professional income in India).

**Q5. Is there a fee to file ITR?**
No. Filing directly on the Income Tax portal (incometax.gov.in) is free. Professional fees apply only if you hire a CA or tax advisor.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: ITR Forms Download: Where to Get Them and How to Use Them
articles.excerpt: Where to download ITR forms, how to use the offline utility and prefilled JSON, and when to file online — a complete walkthrough of CBDT's e-filing utilities.
articles.content:
## Purpose

The Income Tax Return forms are official documents issued by the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) each financial year. These forms must be used to report income, claim deductions, and calculate tax liability. Knowing where to download them, which version to use, and how to prepare them correctly saves significant time and prevents filing errors.

This guide covers every method of accessing ITR forms, including the online utility, offline Java and Excel utilities, and the prefilled JSON facility on the Income Tax portal.

## Who Needs to Download ITR Forms

- Taxpayers who prefer offline preparation before uploading
- Tax professionals preparing returns for multiple clients
- Individuals who want to use Excel or Java utilities for a structured offline experience
- Those filing paper returns (where still applicable for specific categories)

> [!NOTE] Paper filing is rare
> Most individual taxpayers in India are expected to file electronically. Paper filing is now restricted to super senior citizens (above 80 years) who choose to file offline, and a very limited set of filers.

## Where to Download ITR Forms

| Source | URL | Format Available |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Income Tax e-Filing Portal | `incometax.gov.in` | Online, Excel utility, Java utility, prefilled JSON |
| CBDT Notifications | `egazette.gov.in` or `cbdt.gov.in` | Notified form in PDF (for reference) |
| Third-party tax platforms | ClearTax, TaxBuddy, TaxSpanner | Pre-loaded utilities (not blank downloads) |

The **official and recommended source** is always the Income Tax e-Filing Portal. Third-party platforms use the same form structure but with added user interfaces.

## Step-by-Step: Downloading and Using the Offline Utility

1. Visit `incometax.gov.in`.
2. Go to **Downloads**, then select **Income Tax Returns**.
3. Choose the **assessment year**. For FY 2024-25 income, the AY is 2025-26.
4. Select your ITR form (ITR-1, ITR-2, etc.).
5. Download the **offline utility** (a single Windows/Mac compatible application for all forms together).
6. Install and open the utility.
7. Enter your PAN and assessment year.
8. **Import prefilled data** if available. The portal provides a prefilled XML/JSON file that pulls your salary, TDS, advance tax, and other data from Form 26AS and AIS.
9. Fill in all relevant schedules. Verify prefilled entries against your own Form 16, Form 16A, and bank statements.
10. **Validate the form** within the utility. It checks for missing fields and computation errors.
11. Generate the **JSON file**.
12. Log in to the portal and upload the JSON under **File Return**.

## Online Filing (Without Downloading Anything)

1. Log in to `incometax.gov.in`.
2. Go to **e-File → Income Tax Returns → File Income Tax Return**.
3. Select assessment year and filing mode (**Online**).
4. The portal recommends a form based on your profile. Accept or override.
5. Prefilled data appears on screen. Verify and edit as needed.
6. Complete all schedules, review the tax computation, and submit.
7. **E-verify** using Aadhaar OTP, net banking, Demat account, or by sending a signed physical ITR-V to CPC Bengaluru within 30 days.

> [!NOTE] Online mode availability
> Online filing is currently fully available for **ITR-1 and ITR-4**. ITR-2 and ITR-3 are partially supported online, with most taxpayers using the offline utility for these.

## Summary of Available Download Formats

| Format | Best For | Compatibility |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Offline Utility (Windows / Mac) | All ITR forms, complex returns | Windows 7 and above, macOS |
| Excel Utility (older forms) | Excel-comfortable users | Microsoft Excel (older versions) |
| Prefilled JSON | Import into offline utility | Utility-based filing |
| Online mode (no download) | ITR-1 and ITR-4, simple cases | Any browser |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Downloading the utility for the wrong assessment year.** If you are filing for FY 2024-25, you need AY 2025-26 utility. Using the previous year's version causes mismatches and validation failures.
2. **Not updating the utility before filing.** CBDT releases updated versions after initial release. Always check for and install the latest version.
3. **Ignoring the prefilled data and manually entering everything.** This doubles the work and increases risk of discrepancies.
4. **Saving the partially filled utility and trying to import it to a different computer** without the correct software version.
5. **Generating JSON from an old or corrupted utility** and uploading it, which results in a schema validation error at the portal.

## Tips

1. **Clear your browser cache** before accessing the Income Tax portal if you face page loading issues.
2. **Keep your Aadhaar-linked mobile number handy.** Most utility-related interactions and e-verification require OTP.
3. **Always validate the utility** before generating JSON. The utility's internal validation catches missing fields and logical inconsistencies.
4. **If the portal is slow near July 31**, use the offline utility and upload the JSON during off-peak hours such as early morning.
5. **Once you file, download and save** the ITR-V acknowledgment and keep the filed JSON file on your system. Useful for revised returns.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Is it safe to download ITR utilities from third-party tax websites?

Always download utilities from the **official Income Tax portal** at `incometax.gov.in`. Third-party software may have their own proprietary interfaces, but official utilities from the government portal are the safest and most reliable for direct JSON upload.

### Q2. My utility shows a schema error when I upload the JSON. What should I do?

This usually means you are using an outdated version. Download the latest version, re-import your data, and generate a fresh JSON. If the error persists, check whether the form has been recently updated by CBDT.

### Q3. Can I use the same downloaded utility file for multiple taxpayers?

Yes. The offline utility allows you to file for multiple PANs on the same installed software. You simply enter a different PAN each time and generate separate JSON files.

### Q4. The portal shows my prefilled data as incomplete. What should I do?

Prefilled data depends on Form 26AS, AIS, and TIS. If your employer or bank has not filed their TDS statements yet, the data may be missing. Manually fill in those fields based on your Form 16 or bank statements.

### Q5. Can I file a paper return if I am uncomfortable with online filing?

Paper filing is only allowed for individuals **aged 80 years or above** who do not have any income chargeable to tax at higher rates. For all other individuals, electronic filing is mandatory.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax for Salary Above 10 Lakhs
articles.excerpt: Understanding the Problem First Once your salary crosses ₹10 lakh, you enter higher tax slabs under the Income Tax Act, 1961, which means a larger portion of your income gets taxed. If you don’t actively plan, you could end up paying…
articles.content:
At Rs. 10–12 lakh gross, a meaningful slice of income falls in the **20% and 30% slabs** under the old regime, while the new regime offers lower slabs but almost no investment deductions. This is the income band where **running both regimes side-by-side** stops being optional and becomes mandatory.

## What Deductions Matter Most Here

| Deduction | Typical ceiling | Why it bites at Rs. 10L |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard deduction | Rs. 50K / Rs. 75K | Baseline for salaried |
| Section 80C | Rs. 1,50,000 | Core salary saver stack |
| Section 80CCD(1B) | Rs. 50,000 | Outside 80C — efficient |
| Section 80D | Rs. 25K–75K+ | Medical — often forgotten |
| HRA / 24(b) | Variable | Rent & home loan interest |

## Side-by-Side Illustration at Rs. 10 Lakh Gross (Simplified)

Assumptions: salaried; old regime uses EPF + 80C + 80D + standard deduction; figures **illustrative** — validate with ITR utility.

| Component | Old regime (Rs.) | New regime (Rs.) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Gross salary | 10,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Standard deduction | (50,000) | (75,000) |
| Chapter VI-A (80C+80D etc.) | (2,00,000) example stack | — |
| **Approx. taxable income** | **7,50,000** | **9,25,000** |

Tax outcome depends on rebates, cess, and exact slabs — **compute both** before locking investments.

## Break-Even Thinking (Rules of Thumb)

| Your situation | Lean toward |
| --- | --- |
| Total deductions **below ~Rs. 3.75 lakh** | New regime often cheaper |
| Home loan + HRA + full 80C/80D/NPS | Old regime often cheaper |
| Rising income toward Rs. 12L+ | Re-check rebate thresholds annually |

## Four Optimization Moves

1. **Fill 80CCD(1B) before random 80C products** — Extra Rs. 50K outside the Rs. 1.5L cap (old regime).
2. **Coordinate EPF + voluntary 80C** — Do not duplicate locked investments once EPF already consumes the limit.
3. **Claim 80D even if premium is small** — Every rupee saves **20–30% + cess** at this band (old regime).
4. **Audit Form 16 vs payslips** — Employer-reported deductions must match proofs before filing.

## Worked Example: Rahul at Rs. 10 Lakh CTC

| Item | Amount (Rs.) |
| --- | --- |
| Gross | 10,00,000 |
| Employer EPF (tracked in 80C) | 72,000 |
| Additional ELSS + PPF | 78,000 → total 80C = 1,50,000 |
| NPS 80CCD(1B) | 50,000 |
| 80D (self) | 25,000 |

Under the **old regime**, this stack materially cuts taxable income versus investing nothing — then compare to **new regime** with only standard deduction.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. Is the new regime always better at Rs. 10 lakh?**
No. High renters with HRA + loan interest + insurance can still tilt old regime ahead.

**Q2. Should I buy tax-saving FDs at this income?**
Only if they fit liquidity needs — ELSS or NPS may offer better risk/reward; compare lock-ins.

**Q3. How often should I re-run regime comparison?**
At least **once every financial year** and after any loan, job change, or rent move.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80DD: Tax Relief for Families Caring for Differently-Abled Dependents
articles.excerpt: What is Section 80DD (In Simple Terms)? Section 80DD of the Income Tax Act, 1961 allows a taxpayer to claim a fixed tax deduction if they are financially supporting a dependent with a disability. This deduction is not based on how much…
articles.content:
What is Section 80DD (In Simple Terms)? Section 80DD of the Income Tax Act, 1961 allows a taxpayer to claim a fixed tax deduction if they are financially supporting a dependent with a disability. This deduction is not based on how much you spend. Instead, it’s a fixed benefit, which makes it very useful and straightforward.

Who is Eligible to Claim This Deduction? You can claim Section 80DD if: ✔ You are:

- An individual (resident in India), OR
- A Hindu Undivided Family (HUF)

### ✔ And you are supporting a dependent who is

- A person with disability
- Fully or mainly dependent on you Who Qualifies as a “Dependent”?

This is important 👇 For Individuals:

- Spouse
- Children
- Parents
- Brothers or sisters

👉 The dependent must not have claimed deduction under Section 80U themselves. For HUF:

- Any member of the HUF What is Considered a “Disability”?

As per law, disability must be certified under recognized medical guidelines. Includes:

- Blindness
- Low vision
- Hearing impairment
- Locomotor disability
- Mental retardation
- Mental illness
- Autism, cerebral palsy

👉 Certification must come from a government-approved medical authority

How Much Deduction Can You Claim? This is the best part — it’s fixed 👇

- ₹75,000 → Normal disability (40% or more)
- ₹1,25,000 → Severe disability (80% or more)

👉 No need to calculate actual expenses

What Expenses Are Covered? You don’t need to submit detailed expense proofs, but the deduction is meant for:

- Medical treatment
- Nursing care
- Rehabilitation
- Training expenses
- Insurance or schemes for dependent Important Conditions You Must Know
- The dependent should be fully dependent on you
- Disability must be certified
- Deduction is fixed, not expense-based
- Cannot be claimed if dependent claims 80U Documents Required
- Disability certificate (Form 10-IA or equivalent)
- PAN of taxpayer
- Medical documents (if needed)
- Proof of dependency (in some cases)

How to Claim Section 80DD (Step-by-Step)

1. **Get disability certificate from authorized medical authority**
2. **Confirm dependency (financial support)**
3. **Select Section 80DD while filing ITR**
4. **Enter deduction amount (₹75K or ₹1.25L)**

## Keep documents ready (no need to upload usually)

80DD vs 80U (Common Confusion) Sectio n

Who claims For whom 80DD Family member Dependent person with disability 80U Individual Self (person with disability) 👉 Both cannot be claimed for the same person

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

- Claiming without valid certificate
- Confusing 80DD with 80U
- Claiming for non-dependent family members
- Assuming it’s expense-based Quick Example

Rahul supports his father who has 85% disability. 👉 Rahul can claim ₹1,25,000 deduction 👉 Even if actual expenses are lower

## Conclusion

Section 80DD is a powerful and meaningful tax benefit designed to support families caring for dependents with disabilities. Since the deduction is fixed and straightforward, it provides both financial relief and ease of claiming. If you are eligible, don’t miss it — it can significantly reduce your taxable income while supporting a genuine need.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Personal Loan Tax Benefits: When Does a Personal Loan Qualify for a Deduction?
articles.excerpt: Personal loans are widely used for meeting financial needs such as emergencies, business expenses, or investments. Unlike specific-purpose loans such as home or education loans, personal loans are unsecured and flexible in usage. However,…
articles.content:
## Overview

Personal loans are widely used for meeting financial needs such as emergencies, business expenses, or investments. Unlike specific-purpose loans such as home or education loans, personal loans are unsecured and flexible in usage. However, when it comes to taxation under the Income Tax Act, 1961, personal loans do not automatically qualify for tax deductions. The availability of tax benefits depends entirely on the end use of the loan amount. While the principal repayment does not qualify for deduction in most cases, the interest component may be eligible under certain conditions.

## When Tax Benefits Are Available

Use for Business Purposes If a personal loan is utilized for business activities such as working capital or expansion, the interest paid on the loan can be claimed as a business expense. This reduces the taxable income under the head “Profits and Gains from Business or Profession.” Only the interest portion qualifies for deduction, provided there is clear documentation linking the loan to business use. Use for Purchase or Construction of Property When a personal loan is used for purchasing or constructing a residential property, the interest component may be claimed as a deduction under Section 24. This is similar to the treatment of home loans, although stricter documentation may be required to establish the purpose of the loan. The deduction is generally limited to ₹2 lakh per year for self-occupied properties. Use for Investment in Assets If the loan amount is invested in assets such as shares, real estate, or other investments, the interest paid can be treated as part of the cost of acquisition or as an expense while calculating capital gains. This does not provide an immediate tax benefit but helps reduce tax liability at the time of sale. Use for Rental Property In cases where the borrowed funds are used to acquire a property that is subsequently rented out, the interest paid on the loan can be deducted from rental income. This reduces the taxable income under the head “Income from House Property.”

## When Tax Benefits Are Not Available

Personal loans used for purely personal expenses do not provide any tax advantage. This includes expenditures such as travel, weddings, consumer purchases, or lifestyle expenses. Since these are considered personal in nature, neither the principal nor the interest qualifies for deduction.

## Key Considerations

The eligibility for tax benefits depends on the ability to demonstrate the purpose of the loan. Proper documentation is essential, including loan agreements, bank statements, and records showing how the funds were utilized. It is also important to note that:

- Only the interest component is eligible for deduction
- The principal repayment is generally not deductible
- Incorrect claims may lead to penalties or disallowance Illustrative Example

An individual takes a personal loan of ₹5 lakh and uses it for business purposes. If the annual interest paid is ₹50,000, this amount can be claimed as a business expense, thereby reducing taxable income. On the other hand, if the same loan is used for personal consumption, no tax benefit would be available.

## Conclusion

Tax benefits on personal loans are not inherent but conditional. The determining factor is the purpose for which the loan is used. When utilized for business, property, or investment purposes, the interest component can provide meaningful tax relief. However, loans used for personal expenses do not qualify for any deduction. A clear understanding of these rules, along with proper documentation, ensures accurate tax compliance and avoids potential disputes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194IA — TDS on Purchase of Immovable Property
articles.excerpt: Buyer obligations under Section 194IA: 1% TDS, Rs. 50 lakh threshold, stamp duty value, Form 26QB, Form 16B, penalties, and FAQs.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Buying a flat, plot, or commercial property in India comes with a lesser-known obligation: the buyer is required to deduct TDS at the time of payment to the seller. This is governed by Section 194IA of the Income Tax Act, 1961, and was introduced to curb underreporting of property transactions and bring high-value real estate deals within the tax net.

This article explains everything a property buyer needs to know to stay compliant, including rates, calculation examples, and the exact filing process.

---

## What is Section 194IA?

Section 194IA mandates that any person purchasing immovable property (other than agricultural land) from a resident seller must deduct TDS at the rate of **1%** of the total sale consideration, provided the consideration exceeds **Rs. 50 lakhs**.

The term "immovable property" here includes:
- Residential apartments and houses
- Commercial properties (offices, shops)
- Plots of land (non-agricultural)
- Under-construction properties

Agricultural land, as defined in the Income Tax Act, is specifically excluded from this provision.

---

## Rate Table: TDS Under Section 194IA

| Scenario | Rate of TDS |
|---|---|
| Sale consideration is Rs. 50 lakhs or more | 1% |
| Sale consideration is below Rs. 50 lakhs | Nil (no TDS applicable) |
| Seller does not furnish PAN | 20% |
| With valid PAN, normal rate applies | 1% |

> Note: From June 2013 onwards, this section applies to every buyer, whether an individual, company, or HUF. There is no requirement to obtain a TAN (Tax Deduction Account Number) for this specific section. A PAN suffices.

---

## Applicability

### Who Must Deduct?
Any buyer of immovable property, regardless of their tax status or size of business. This includes:
- Salaried individuals
- Self-employed professionals
- Companies and LLPs
- HUFs

### Who Is Excluded?
- If the seller is the Government or a Government-notified entity
- Agricultural land transactions
- Property valued below Rs. 50 lakhs

### Important: Stamp Duty Value vs. Consideration

As amended by Finance Act 2022, if the stamp duty value of the property exceeds the actual sale consideration, TDS must be calculated on the **higher of the two values**. This plugs the gap where buyers understate consideration to reduce TDS.

---

## Threshold Limits

| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| Minimum sale consideration for TDS | Rs. 50,00,000 |
| Properties below this | No TDS obligation |
| Agricultural land | Exempt regardless of value |

---

## Example Calculation

**Scenario:** Ramesh purchases a residential flat in Pune from Suresh for Rs. 75,00,000. The stamp duty value of the flat is Rs. 80,00,000.

**Step 1:** Determine the applicable amount for TDS.
Since stamp duty value (Rs. 80 lakhs) exceeds sale consideration (Rs. 75 lakhs), TDS is computed on Rs. 80,00,000.

**Step 2:** Apply TDS rate.
TDS = 1% of Rs. 80,00,000 = Rs. 80,000

**Step 3:** Payment to seller.
Ramesh pays Suresh: Rs. 75,00,000 - Rs. 80,000 = Rs. 74,20,000 (net of TDS deducted on 80 lakh base, adjusted as applicable).

In practice, the TDS amount is deducted from the consideration amount being paid, and the challan is filed in the government's name on behalf of the seller.

---

## Compliance Steps

### Step 1: No TAN Required
Unlike most TDS provisions, Section 194IA does not require the buyer to hold a TAN. The buyer's PAN is sufficient.

### Step 2: Deduct TDS at Payment
TDS must be deducted at the time of making payment to the seller, whether it is an instalment or a lump sum. If multiple payments are made, TDS must be deducted from each instalment.

### Step 3: Deposit TDS Using Form 26QB
The buyer must deposit TDS online using Form 26QB (discussed in detail in the next article) within **30 days from the end of the month** in which TDS was deducted. This is different from the 7th-of-next-month deadline that applies to most other TDS sections.

### Step 4: Issue Form 16B
After depositing TDS, the buyer must download and issue **Form 16B** (TDS certificate) to the seller within 15 days of the due date for furnishing Form 26QB. Form 16B can be downloaded from the TRACES portal.

---

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

| Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|
| Not deducting TDS on installments | TDS must be deducted on every payment, not just the final one |
| Deducting on consideration while ignoring stamp duty value | Deduct on higher of consideration or stamp duty value |
| Applying 1% on total property value when partial payment is made | Apply on the amount actually paid in each installment |
| Not filing 26QB for each co-buyer separately | Each buyer must file a separate 26QB for their share |

---

## Penalties Under Section 194IA

| Default | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Failure to deduct TDS | Interest at 1% per month from the date TDS was deductible |
| Failure to deposit after deduction | Interest at 1.5% per month from date of deduction to date of deposit |
| Late filing / non-filing of 26QB | Rs. 200 per day under Section 234E, subject to maximum of TDS amount |
| Penalty for non-compliance | Rs. 10,000 to Rs. 1,00,000 under Section 271H |

---

## FAQs

**Q1. If I buy a property for Rs. 48 lakhs, do I need to deduct TDS?**
No. The threshold under Section 194IA is Rs. 50 lakhs. If the sale consideration is below Rs. 50 lakhs and the stamp duty value is also below Rs. 50 lakhs, TDS is not applicable.

**Q2. What if the property has two buyers? Who files 26QB?**
Each buyer must file a separate Form 26QB for their respective share of the purchase consideration. For example, if a property is bought jointly by two individuals each paying Rs. 40 lakhs (total Rs. 80 lakhs), both must file 26QB independently since the aggregate exceeds Rs. 50 lakhs.

**Q3. Is TDS applicable on under-construction property payments to builders?**
Yes. TDS under Section 194IA applies to payments made to a builder for an under-construction property, as long as the total consideration exceeds Rs. 50 lakhs. Each instalment paid to the builder attracts 1% TDS.

**Q4. Can the seller claim refund of the TDS deducted?**
Yes. The TDS deducted reflects in the seller's Form 26AS. If the seller's total tax liability is lower than the TDS amount, they can claim a refund by filing their Income Tax Return.

**Q5. Does Section 194IA apply to NRI sellers?**
No. Section 194IA applies only when the seller is a resident Indian. If the seller is a Non-Resident Indian (NRI), TDS must be deducted under Section 195, which carries different rates and requires a TAN from the buyer.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 10IE: How to Opt for the New Tax Regime
articles.excerpt: Form 10IE is the declaration for opting into the new tax regime under Section 115BAC. Who must file it, when, and how — with slab comparison and business-income rules.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 10IE is the formal declaration form that a taxpayer submits to the Income Tax Department to opt for the **new concessional tax regime** under Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act. Without filing this form, the system defaults you to the old regime, and you cannot enjoy the lower tax rates available under the new regime.

For salaried individuals, this form is typically not a separate filing exercise since the choice is made within the ITR filing itself. However, for taxpayers with business or professional income, Form 10IE must be filed **before the due date of filing the return**, failing which the option for the new regime is lost for that year.

> [!IMPORTANT] Default regime has flipped
> From AY 2024-25 onwards, the new regime is the **default** for all taxpayers. You now need to actively opt out (using Form 10IEA) to choose the old regime, rather than actively opt in. Form 10IE remains relevant for certain specific categories, particularly business income earners wanting to formally exercise their choice.

## Who Should Use It

- Individuals and HUFs with **income from business or profession** who want to choose the new tax regime
- Taxpayers filing **ITR-3 or ITR-4** who wish to opt for Section 115BAC rates
- Salaried individuals do **not** need to file a standalone Form 10IE; they declare the regime choice within their ITR

## New Tax Regime: Rate Comparison

| Income Slab | Old Regime | New Regime (FY 2024-25) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 3,00,000 | Nil | Nil |
| Rs. 3,00,001 to Rs. 6,00,000 | 5% | 5% |
| Rs. 6,00,001 to Rs. 9,00,000 | 20% | 10% |
| Rs. 9,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000 | 20% | 15% |
| Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 15,00,000 | 30% | 20% |
| Above Rs. 15,00,000 | 30% | 30% |

Standard deduction of **Rs. 75,000** is available under the new regime from FY 2024-25.

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Determine if you have business or professional income.** If purely salaried, skip this form — you declare your regime choice within the ITR itself.
2. **Log in** to the Income Tax e-Filing Portal at `incometax.gov.in`.
3. Go to **e-File → Income Tax Forms → File Income Tax Forms**.
4. **Search for Form 10IE** under the forms list.
5. Select the relevant **assessment year**.
6. Fill in the basic details: name, PAN, address, and nature of income.
7. Make the declaration confirming your choice of Section 115BAC.
8. **Submit and e-verify** using Aadhaar OTP or other available verification methods.
9. **Download the acknowledgment** for your records.
10. File your ITR (ITR-3 or ITR-4) mentioning the Form 10IE acknowledgment number where prompted.

## Key Features and Conditions

| Feature | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Filing deadline | Before the due date of filing the ITR for that year |
| Business income taxpayers | Can opt in and opt out only **once in a lifetime** (after opting out, cannot re-enter unless business income ceases) |
| Salaried taxpayers | Can switch every year (declared within ITR, no separate form) |
| Deductions given up | 80C, 80D, 80CCD(1B), HRA, LTA, 24(b) for self-occupied, etc. |
| Deductions retained | 80CCD(2) (employer NPS), standard deduction Rs. 75,000, NPS for Agniveers |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Filing Form 10IE after the ITR due date.** The choice must be made before or at the time of filing. A late filing forfeits the new regime option for that year.
2. **Business income taxpayers trying to switch back to the new regime after opting out.** Once a business income earner opts out using Form 10IEA, they lose the ability to return to the new regime unless business income ceases entirely.
3. **Salaried employees unnecessarily searching for Form 10IE.** For salaried individuals, the regime choice is embedded in the ITR filing itself.
4. **Not downloading the acknowledgment.** The acknowledgment number must be entered in the relevant schedule of ITR-3 or ITR-4.
5. **Assuming the form eliminates all deductions.** Some deductions and exemptions are still available under the new regime.

## Tips

1. **Compare your tax liability under both regimes** before making the choice. Use the CBDT's in-built tax calculator or a reliable third-party calculator.
2. **If you are a business income earner** and have significant investments in 80C, PPF, and home loans, the old regime is likely better. Calculate before committing to Form 10IE.
3. **Keep a record of all Form 10IE filings** year-wise, especially if you have made regime switches in prior years.
4. **If your business is new and currently making losses**, the new regime might make sense now since you have limited deductions anyway. But think about future years before locking in.
5. **Consult a chartered accountant** if your income profile is complex — particularly if you have both business income and capital gains in the same year.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. If I file Form 10IE and later realise the old regime is better, can I change my mind?

For **salaried individuals**, yes. They can switch regime every year. For **business income taxpayers** who have filed Form 10IE and then filed under the new regime, switching back requires opting out using Form 10IEA, after which they cannot return to the new regime unless business income ceases.

### Q2. Is Form 10IE required every year for business income earners?

No. Once you have opted for the new regime via Form 10IE and continue to earn business income, you do not need to refile it every year. The option remains active until you choose to opt out.

### Q3. My employer deducted TDS assuming the new regime. Do I still need to file Form 10IE?

For salaried employees, the regime communicated to the employer for TDS purposes does not require a separate Form 10IE. The final regime declaration happens when you file your ITR.

### Q4. What happens if I forget to file Form 10IE before the ITR deadline as a business income earner?

If you miss filing Form 10IE before the due date, you will be defaulted to the **new regime** (since new regime is now the default from AY 2024-25), which may or may not be to your advantage. Late filing is not accepted, so planning ahead is essential.

### Q5. Can a freelancer with income under Section 44ADA file Form 10IE?

Yes. Freelancers filing ITR-4 under presumptive taxation who wish to formally confirm their new regime choice can file Form 10IE. However, since the new regime is now the default, they would only need to file **Form 10IEA** if they want the old regime.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Defective Return Under Section 139(9): Meaning, Causes, and How to Fix It
articles.excerpt: A defective return notice under Section 139(9) gives you 15 days to correct your ITR before it is treated as never filed. This guide covers the 8 most common causes, consequences of not responding, a step-by-step correction process, and an example scenario.
articles.content:
## What Is a Defective Return?

Under Section 139(9) of the Income Tax Act, the Central Board of Direct Taxes has prescribed the conditions under which a return of income may be treated as **defective**. When the Assessing Officer or the CPC detects such defects, they issue a notice asking you to rectify the return within **15 days**.

If you do not respond within the time limit, the return is treated as if it was never filed — with serious downstream consequences.

## Common Reasons Why a Return Becomes Defective

| Reason | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Incomplete return | Return filed without all required schedules |
| Missing tax payment | Tax is payable but not paid before filing |
| Wrong ITR form | Wrong form used for income type (e.g., ITR-1 filed for capital gains income) |
| No balance sheet or P&L | Business income declared without attaching financial statements |
| Mismatch in gross receipts | Gross receipts in P&L differ from those in audit report |
| Incomplete Part B of return | Part B-TI or Part B-TTI not filled |
| No TAN or irregular TDS | TDS claimed but TAN of deductor not provided |
| Audit report not uploaded | Tax audit required but Form 3CA/3CB/3CD not uploaded |

## How the Defective Return Notice Is Issued

The notice under Section 139(9) is sent to your **registered email address** and is also available on the e-filing portal under **Pending Actions** or **e-Proceedings**. It specifies:

- The defect that has been identified
- The reference number of your original return
- The deadline to respond (15 days from the date of notice, extendable on request)

## Consequences of Not Responding

> [!WARN] Your return is treated as never filed
> - Return is treated as **invalid** — as if never filed
> - You are treated as a non-filer for that year, attracting penalty under Section 234F
> - Any refund due will not be processed
> - Carry-forward of losses (business, capital) is **denied**
> - TDS credit claimed in the return will not be processed

## Example: Karan, Freelance Developer — Audit Report Not Uploaded

> [!TIP] How it was resolved
> Karan filed ITR-4 for FY 2022-23 and declared gross receipts of Rs. 28 lakh under the presumptive scheme. However, since his turnover exceeded Rs. 1 crore in FY 2021-22, a tax audit was required — but he filed without uploading Form 3CB and 3CD.
> He received a defective return notice under Section 139(9). He immediately uploaded the audit report, **accepted the defect on the portal, and re-submitted** — and his return was treated as valid with the original filing date preserved.

## How to Respond to a Defective Return Notice

1. **Log in to the income tax e-filing portal** — incometax.gov.in
2. **Go to e-Proceedings** — locate the Section 139(9) notice
3. **Read the defect description carefully**
4. **Choose "Agree"** to correct the defect, or **"Disagree" with reasons** if you believe the notice is incorrect
5. **Make the necessary corrections** — upload a revised or corrected return
6. **Submit within the deadline** — request an extension if needed
7. **Download and preserve the submission acknowledgement**

## Prevention Tips

- Use the correct ITR form based on your income sources before filing
- Ensure all taxes are paid before pressing the Submit button on your return
- If your accounts are auditable, upload the audit report **before** filing the ITR
- Cross-check that gross receipts in your return match those in your audit report
- Do not leave any mandatory schedule blank, even if the amount is zero
- Use tax filing software or engage a CA to catch errors before submission

## FAQs

### Will my original return filing date be preserved if I correct a defective return?

Yes. If you correct the return within the time allowed, the corrected return is treated as filed on the **original filing date**. This means you retain any benefits tied to filing within the due date.

### Can I disagree with the defect identified in the notice?

Yes. The portal allows you to disagree with the defect and provide your reasons. However, if the AO still considers the return defective, further correspondence may follow. Use disagreement only if you genuinely believe the notice is incorrect.

### Can I request an extension beyond the 15-day limit?

Yes. You can request a time extension from the AO through the portal. Extensions are generally granted for genuine reasons — do not wait until the last minute to make this request.

### What if I missed the deadline and my return has already been invalidated?

If the return has been treated as invalid, you may be able to file a fresh return if the time limit under Section 139(4) for belated returns has not expired. After that, you would need to appeal or seek condonation of delay.

### Is a defective return correction the same as a revised return under Section 139(5)?

No. A **revised return under Section 139(5)** is filed voluntarily to correct an error in a valid return. A **defective return correction under Section 139(9)** is filed in response to a specific notice and cures a defect the department has identified.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 26QB — TDS Return for Property Purchase
articles.excerpt: Form 26QB challan-cum-statement for Section 194IA: filing steps, instalments, corrections, penalties, and FAQs.
articles.content:
## What is Form 26QB?

Form 26QB is the challan-cum-statement used by buyers of immovable property to deposit TDS deducted under Section 194IA with the government. Unlike regular TDS returns filed quarterly, Form 26QB is transaction-specific and must be filed separately for each property purchase.

It serves a dual purpose: it is both the payment challan (like Challan 281) and the TDS return in one. After successful filing and payment, the buyer can download Form 16B from TRACES as the TDS certificate for the seller.

---

## Key Details of Form 26QB

| Parameter | Details |
|---|---|
| Form Name | Form 26QB |
| Purpose | TDS payment and return for property purchase (Section 194IA) |
| Filed By | Buyer of immovable property |
| TDS Rate | 1% of sale consideration (or stamp duty value, whichever is higher) |
| Threshold | Property value above Rs. 50 lakhs |
| Payment Deadline | Within 30 days from end of month of deduction |
| TDS Certificate | Form 16B (generated from TRACES) |
| Portal | https://www.tin-nsdl.com or https://www.incometax.gov.in |

---

## Who Must File Form 26QB?

Any buyer purchasing immovable property (excluding agricultural land) for a consideration exceeding Rs. 50 lakhs from a resident seller must file Form 26QB. This applies to:
- Individual buyers (salaried or self-employed)
- Companies and LLPs
- HUFs
- Every co-buyer in a joint purchase (separate 26QB per buyer)

---

## Threshold and Rate Summary

| Condition | TDS Rate | Form Required |
|---|---|---|
| Property value above Rs. 50 lakhs, seller has PAN | 1% | 26QB |
| Property value above Rs. 50 lakhs, seller has no PAN | 20% | 26QB |
| Property value at or below Rs. 50 lakhs | Nil | No filing required |
| Seller is NRI | As per Section 195 | Different form/process |

---

## Step-by-Step Filing Process for Form 26QB

### Step 1: Access the Portal
Visit the TIN NSDL website (https://onlineservices.tin.egov-nsdl.com) or the new Income Tax portal. Navigate to TDS on Property (Form 26QB).

### Step 2: Fill in Buyer Details
Enter:
- Buyer's PAN
- Buyer's name, address, email, and phone number
- Category of buyer (individual, company, HUF, etc.)

### Step 3: Fill in Seller Details
Enter:
- Seller's PAN
- Seller's name and address
- If multiple sellers, all must be listed

### Step 4: Property Details
Enter:
- Complete address of the property
- Date of agreement or booking
- Total sale consideration
- Stamp duty value (if higher, this becomes the base for TDS)

### Step 5: TDS Computation
- Enter the amount of TDS deducted
- The portal auto-calculates based on inputs
- Verify the figure before proceeding

### Step 6: Payment
Select the payment mode:
- Net banking (most common)
- Over-the-counter at authorised bank branches

Generate the acknowledgement number once payment is successful.

### Step 7: Download Form 16B
After payment reflects (usually within 5 working days), log into TRACES (www.tdscpc.gov.in), navigate to "Downloads > Form 16B", and download the TDS certificate for the seller.

---

## Example Calculation

**Scenario:** Priya buys a commercial shop in Chennai for Rs. 62,00,000. The stamp duty value is Rs. 65,00,000. She pays Rs. 20,00,000 as first instalment.

**TDS on first instalment:**
Since the total consideration (Rs. 62 lakhs) or stamp duty value (Rs. 65 lakhs) exceeds Rs. 50 lakhs, TDS applies.

For installment-based payments, TDS is calculated on the amount being paid in each installment:
TDS on Rs. 20,00,000 = 1% = Rs. 20,000

Priya pays the builder: Rs. 20,00,000 - Rs. 20,000 = Rs. 19,80,000
She files Form 26QB and deposits Rs. 20,000 within 30 days from the end of that month.

---

## Common Errors in Form 26QB and How to Fix Them

| Error Type | How to Correct |
|---|---|
| Wrong PAN of buyer or seller | File a correction request on TRACES |
| Incorrect property address | File a correction on TRACES |
| Wrong amount entered | File a correction on TRACES with supporting documents |
| PAN not validated | Ensure PAN is active and not inoperative; link with Aadhaar |

> Correction requests on TRACES for Form 26QB require consent from both buyer and seller (through OTP verification), so ensure all communication is smooth.

---

## Penalties for Non-Compliance

| Default | Penalty / Interest |
|---|---|
| Late deduction | Interest at 1% per month (Section 201(1A)) |
| Late deposit of TDS | Interest at 1.5% per month (Section 201(1A)) |
| Late filing of 26QB | Rs. 200 per day under Section 234E |
| Non-filing of 26QB | Penalty up to Rs. 1,00,000 under Section 271H |
| Failure to issue Form 16B | Penalty under Section 272A |

---

## FAQs

**Q1. Can Form 26QB be filed offline?**
Form 26QB is primarily an online filing. However, buyers without internet banking can visit designated bank branches (authorised by NSDL) with a filled physical form and pay over the counter. The bank generates the challan and forwards details electronically.

**Q2. What is the deadline for filing Form 26QB after each instalment payment?**
The due date is 30 days from the end of the month in which TDS was deducted. For example, if TDS is deducted in July, Form 26QB must be filed and TDS deposited by August 30.

**Q3. Do I need to file a fresh 26QB for each property payment instalment?**
Yes. A separate Form 26QB must be filed for each payment made to the seller. If a property has three instalments, three separate 26QB filings are required.

**Q4. How does the seller verify that the buyer has deposited TDS?**
The seller can log into their account on the Income Tax portal or TRACES and check Form 26AS. Once the buyer files 26QB and makes payment, it reflects in the seller's Form 26AS within a few days.

**Q5. What if the buyer and seller mutually decide not to deduct TDS?**
This does not exempt either party from the legal obligation. TDS under Section 194IA is a statutory requirement. If TDS is not deducted, the buyer becomes a defaulter under Section 201 and is liable to pay the TDS amount plus interest and penalties.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Make Tax Payment on the Income Tax Portal Without Logging In
articles.excerpt: The Income Tax Department allows taxpayers to make certain tax payments without logging into their account on the official portal. This feature is particularly useful for quick payments such as advance tax, self-assessment tax, or demand…
articles.content:
## Overview

The Income Tax Department allows taxpayers to make certain tax payments without logging into their account on the official portal. This feature is particularly useful for quick payments such as advance tax, self-assessment tax, or demand payments, especially when login credentials are not readily available. The facility is provided through the Income Tax Department portal and is designed to simplify tax compliance while ensuring secure transactions.

## When You Can Use This Facility

### You can make tax payments without logging in for

- Advance tax
- Self-assessment tax
- Regular tax payments
- Demand payments (with details)

However, certain actions like viewing past records or filing returns still require login.

## What You Need Before Starting

To make a payment without logging in, keep the following details ready:

- PAN number
- Mobile number
- Email ID
- Assessment Year
- Type of tax payment
- Bank account or payment method

Accurate entry of PAN and assessment year is critical, as errors may lead to incorrect credit. Step-by-Step Process Access the Payment Page Visit the Income Tax portal and select the option for e-Pay Tax or “Pay Without Login.” Enter PAN and Basic Details Provide:

- PAN number
- Mobile number
- OTP verification Select Payment Type Choose the applicable category:
- Advance tax
- Self-assessment tax
- Other payments Enter Tax Details Fill in:
- Amount
- Assessment year
- Relevant sections (if applicable) Choose Payment Mode Options include:
- Net banking
- Debit card
- UPI
- NEFT/RTGS Complete Payment

Proceed to payment gateway and complete the transaction. Download Challan After successful payment:

- A challan (receipt) is generated
- Save it for future reference Features

### No Login Required

Quick and accessible for all taxpayers.

### Multiple Payment Options

Supports various digital payment modes.

### Instant Confirmation

Challan generated immediately.

### Secure Process

OTP-based verification ensures safety.

### Useful for Urgent Payments

Ideal for last-minute tax payments.

## Documents / Details Required

- PAN Card
- Mobile number (linked for OTP)
- Payment details
- Bank account or UPI access Important Points to Remember
- Ensure correct Assessment Year selection
- Verify PAN before submission
- Keep challan safely for records
- Payment reflects in Form 26AS after processing Common Mistakes
- Selecting wrong assessment year
- Entering incorrect PAN
- Not saving challan
- Choosing wrong tax category Example

### A taxpayer needs to pay ₹20,000 as self-assessment tax

- Uses “Pay Without Login”
- Enters PAN and verifies OTP
- Selects self-assessment tax
- Pays via net banking
- Downloads challan as proof Conclusion

The option to make tax payments without logging in provides flexibility and convenience for taxpayers. It simplifies the process while maintaining security through OTP verification and digital payment systems. By ensuring accurate details and timely payment, taxpayers can avoid penalties and maintain smooth compliance with income tax requirements.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Taxation on Cryptocurrency in India
articles.excerpt: Cryptocurrency taxation in India has evolved significantly in recent years, with the government introducing a clear framework under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Virtual Digital Assets (VDAs), which include cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and…
articles.content:
## Overview
