If the total of all covered payments to a single partner during the financial year exceeds Rs. 20,000, TDS at 10% is applicable on the entire amount (not just the excess).

---

## Applicability

Section 194T applies to:
- Partnership firms (registered or unregistered)
- Limited Liability Partnerships (LLPs)
- Any association of persons treated as a firm for income tax purposes

It applies to payments made to:
- Working partners
- Non-working (sleeping) partners
- Managing partners

All partners, irrespective of their role, are covered if they receive any of the specified payments.

---

## Example Calculation

**Scenario:** Mehta & Associates LLP has two partners: Rajan and Seema.

Payments made during FY 2025-26:

| Partner | Salary | Interest on Capital | Commission | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rajan | Rs. 2,40,000 | Rs. 60,000 | Rs. 30,000 | Rs. 3,30,000 |
| Seema | Rs. 1,80,000 | Rs. 40,000 | Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 2,35,000 |

TDS Calculation:
- Rajan: 10% of Rs. 3,30,000 = Rs. 33,000
- Seema: 10% of Rs. 2,35,000 = Rs. 23,500

Total TDS to be deposited by the firm: Rs. 56,500

---

## Why Was Section 194T Introduced?

Previously, partner remuneration and interest payments created a significant TDS leakage. A firm would deduct these as allowable expenses under Section 40(b), reducing its taxable profits, but the partner would declare this income only when filing their ITR, often months later. Section 194T plugs this gap by ensuring tax is collected upfront.

---

## Compliance Steps

1. Identify all payments made or credited to partners that fall under the specified categories
2. Ensure each partner has furnished a valid PAN
3. If aggregate payment to any partner in the year exceeds Rs. 20,000, deduct TDS at 10%
4. Deduct at the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier
5. Deposit TDS by the 7th of the following month using Challan 281
6. File quarterly TDS return in Form 26Q (same return as other non-salary deductions)
7. Issue Form 16A to each partner quarterly

---

## Penalties

| Default | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Non-deduction | Interest at 1% per month + 30% disallowance under Section 40(a)(ia) |
| Late deposit after deduction | Interest at 1.5% per month |
| Late filing of 26Q | Rs. 200 per day under Section 234E |
| Non-filing | Penalty up to Rs. 1,00,000 under Section 271H |

---

## FAQs

**Q1. Does Section 194T apply to LLPs as well as traditional partnership firms?**
Yes. Section 194T explicitly covers both traditional partnership firms and LLPs. Any entity that pays salary, remuneration, bonus, commission, or interest on capital to its partners is covered, regardless of whether it is constituted as a traditional firm or an LLP.

**Q2. Is the Rs. 20,000 threshold applied per payment or in aggregate for the year?**
The threshold of Rs. 20,000 is the aggregate of all covered payments made to a single partner during the financial year. Once this aggregate is expected to exceed Rs. 20,000, TDS must be deducted from that point onwards. It is good practice to deduct from the first payment itself if annual payments will clearly exceed the threshold.

**Q3. Interest on capital credited in the books but not actually paid - does TDS apply?**
Yes. TDS under Section 194T applies at the earlier of payment or credit to the partner's account. If interest is credited to the partner's capital account in the firm's books, TDS must be deducted at that time, even if no cash changes hands.

**Q4. What if the firm's profits are insufficient to pay partners and the firm decides not to make any payment during the year?**
If no payment is made and nothing is credited to the partner's account during the year, TDS does not apply. However, once even a book credit is made (such as crediting interest to the partner's capital account), the TDS obligation is triggered.

**Q5. Can partners submit Form 15G to avoid TDS under Section 194T?**
No. Form 15G and Form 15H are applicable only for specific sections listed under Section 197A, which does not currently include Section 194T. As of FY 2025-26, partners cannot avoid TDS under Section 194T by submitting Form 15G or 15H. The only way to reduce TDS is through a certificate under Section 197 from the Assessing Officer.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194I — TDS on Rent
articles.excerpt: Rent for land, building, plant, machinery: rates 10%/2%, thresholds, Section 194IB, GST on rent, penalties, and FAQs.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Section 194I

Section 194I covers the deduction of TDS on rent paid by businesses and specified individuals to landlords. This provision applies not just to traditional property rentals but also to machine and equipment hire, ensuring that rental income across asset categories is brought within the TDS framework.

Given the scale of commercial real estate transactions and equipment leasing in India, Section 194I is one of the most frequently applicable TDS provisions for businesses of all sizes.

---

## Definition of Rent Under Section 194I

"Rent" under Section 194I means any payment, by whatever name called, for the use of:
- Land
- Building (including factory buildings)
- Machinery
- Plant
- Equipment
- Furniture or fittings
- Whether or not owned by the payee

This broad definition means that even informal arrangements where a business uses a third-party's assets for a recurring payment can trigger Section 194I.

---

## Rate Table: TDS Under Section 194I

| Asset Type | TDS Rate |
|---|---|
| Land and building (including factory buildings) | 10% |
| Plant and machinery | 2% |
| Equipment (computers, servers, etc.) | 2% |
| Furniture and fittings | 10% |
| Payee without PAN | 20% |

---

## Threshold Limits

| Parameter | Annual Threshold |
|---|---|
| Rent paid to a single landlord | Rs. 2,40,000 per financial year |
| Below this | No TDS required |
| Monthly equivalent | Approximately Rs. 20,000/month |

---

## Applicability

### Who Must Deduct TDS Under 194I?
- Companies and LLPs
- Firms
- HUFs and individuals liable to tax audit
- Government departments and statutory bodies

### Who Is Exempt?
- Individuals and HUFs not subject to tax audit
- Tenants paying rent for residential purposes in a personal capacity

---

## Special Provision: Section 194IB (For Individuals and HUFs)

Individuals and HUFs not subject to tax audit but paying monthly rent above Rs. 50,000 must deduct TDS under Section 194IB at 5%. This is a separate provision for individual tenants in high-value residential rentals and requires filing only once a year (not quarterly), using Form 26QC.

| Section | Applicability | Rate | Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 194I | Businesses, audit-liable individuals | 10% / 2% | Rs. 2,40,000/year |
| 194IB | Non-audit individuals / HUFs | 5% | Rs. 50,000/month |

---

## Example Calculation

**Scenario 1: Office Rent**
Nexus Technologies Pvt. Ltd. rents an office in Bangalore for Rs. 2,00,000 per month from Global Properties Ltd.

Annual rent = Rs. 24,00,000
Since this exceeds Rs. 2,40,000, TDS applies.
Monthly TDS = 10% of Rs. 2,00,000 = Rs. 20,000
Nexus pays Global Properties: Rs. 1,80,000 per month and deposits Rs. 20,000 with the government.

**Scenario 2: Machinery Hire**
A construction company hires an excavator from a plant hire company for Rs. 5,00,000 for the quarter.
TDS = 2% of Rs. 5,00,000 = Rs. 10,000
Net payment to plant hire company = Rs. 4,90,000

---

## Handling GST on Rent

TDS under Section 194I is calculated on the rent amount exclusive of GST. If the landlord raises a GST invoice separately showing rent and GST, TDS is deducted only on the rent portion. If the invoice does not separate GST, TDS may be computed on the entire amount, which creates an anomaly. It is therefore advisable to always ask landlords to issue GST-compliant invoices that clearly bifurcate the base rent and GST.

---

## Compliance Steps

1. Verify the annual rent liability to determine if it crosses Rs. 2,40,000
2. Obtain PAN of the landlord; if not available, deduct at 20%
3. Deduct TDS at 10% (land/building) or 2% (machinery/equipment) at the time of payment or credit
4. Deposit TDS using Challan 281 by the 7th of the following month
5. File quarterly TDS return in Form 26Q
6. Issue Form 16A to the landlord after each quarter

---

## Penalties

| Default | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Non-deduction of TDS on rent | Interest 1% per month + 30% disallowance of rent expense |
| Late deposit | Interest 1.5% per month |
| Late / non-filing of return | Rs. 200 per day under Section 234E |
| Non-filing | Penalty Rs. 10,000 to Rs. 1,00,000 under Section 271H |
| Non-issuance of Form 16A | Rs. 100 per day under Section 272A |

---

## FAQs

**Q1. Is TDS applicable on lease premium (one-time deposit) paid at the start of a lease?**
Generally no. A security deposit or lease premium that is refundable is not "rent" in the traditional sense and does not attract TDS under Section 194I. However, if the lease premium is a non-refundable upfront payment for the right to use property, it may be treated as advance rent and TDS would apply. The classification depends on the terms of the lease agreement.

**Q2. A company rents two separate properties from the same landlord, each at Rs. 1,50,000 per year (total Rs. 3,00,000). Does TDS apply?**
Yes. The threshold of Rs. 2,40,000 is assessed on total payments made to the same landlord during the year, regardless of whether they arise from one or multiple properties. Since the aggregate is Rs. 3,00,000, TDS applies on all payments.

**Q3. Is TDS applicable on rent paid to a State Government or Central Government entity?**
No. Payments to the Government (Central or State) are not subject to TDS since the Government cannot pay taxes to itself. However, rent paid to a Government-owned public sector undertaking (PSU) is subject to TDS, since PSUs are separate legal entities.

**Q4. What if the property is jointly owned by two people? Should TDS be split?**
Yes. If a property is jointly owned by two people (say, husband and wife), the tenant should ideally split the rental payment and deduct TDS separately, based on their ownership shares, and issue two separate Form 16A certificates. In practice, if the joint owners have not communicated their share, TDS is often deducted on the total amount against one PAN, which may need to be corrected later.

**Q5. Does Section 194I apply to Co-working space membership fees?**
This is a nuanced area. If a co-working membership gives an exclusive right to use a specific desk or office, it may qualify as rent under Section 194I. If it is a flexi-desk arrangement (no fixed desk, access to shared space), it is more likely a service and may be covered under Section 194J instead. Courts and CBDT have not issued a definitive ruling, so professional advice is recommended based on the specific agreement.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 44AB Income Tax Audit: Due Date, Criteria, Audit Report, Penalty
articles.excerpt: Tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 is a key compliance mechanism aimed at ensuring accuracy in financial reporting and proper disclosure of income. It applies to specified categories of taxpayers whose turnover,…
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 is a key compliance mechanism aimed at ensuring accuracy in financial reporting and proper disclosure of income. It applies to specified categories of taxpayers whose turnover, receipts, or income structure warrants independent verification by a chartered accountant. Beyond a procedural requirement, the tax audit framework plays a significant role in standardizing financial reporting and minimizing discrepancies in tax computation. Concept and Objective The objective of a tax audit is not merely verification of accounts but ensuring that:

- Income is correctly computed in accordance with tax provisions
- Deductions and exemptions are properly claimed
- Books of accounts reflect a true and fair view
- Compliance with various provisions of the Act is maintained

The audit culminates in the submission of prescribed audit reports, forming part of the taxpayer’s overall return filing process.

## Applicability and Threshold Criteria

Tax audit applicability varies based on the nature of activity: Business

- Turnover exceeds ₹1 crore in a financial year
- Threshold increased to ₹10 crore where cash transactions are within prescribed limits (generally ≤5%) Profession
- Gross receipts exceed ₹50 lakh Presumptive Taxation Cases Audit becomes applicable where:
- Income declared is lower than prescribed presumptive rates under Sections such as

44AD or 44ADA, and

- Total income exceeds the basic exemption limit

This makes the audit requirement not just turnover-driven but also linked to the method of income declaration. Due Date for Tax Audit The due date for furnishing the tax audit report is typically:

- 30th September of the Assessment Year
- Extended timelines may apply based on notifications issued in specific years

The audit report must be filed electronically before or along with the Income Tax Return. Audit Report Forms Tax audit reports are furnished in prescribed formats:

- Form 3CA – Where accounts are already audited under another law
- Form 3CB – Where no other statutory audit is applicable
- Form 3CD – Detailed statement of particulars forming part of the audit

Form 3CD is particularly comprehensive, requiring disclosures on:

- Depreciation
- Related party transactions
- TDS compliance
- Disallowances under various sections Process and Compliance Flow

The tax audit process follows a structured sequence:

1. **Maintenance of books of accounts** — Proper records must be maintained as per applicable provisions.
2. **Appointment of a chartered accountant** — The auditor conducts examination and verification of records.
3. **Audit execution** — Includes checking compliance, reconciliations, and reporting requirements.
4. **Preparation of audit report** — Forms 3CA/3CB and 3CD are finalized.
5. **Online submission** — The report is uploaded by the auditor and accepted by the taxpayer.
6. **ITR filing** — The return is filed referencing the audit report details. Illustrative Situations

A trading business with turnover of ₹1.8 crore falls within the audit requirement and must obtain a tax audit report before filing its return. A professional with receipts of ₹65 lakh is required to undergo audit due to exceeding the prescribed threshold. In another case, a taxpayer opting for presumptive taxation declares income lower than the prescribed percentage while having total income above the exemption limit—triggering mandatory audit.

## Penalty for Non-Compliance

Failure to comply with Section 44AB attracts penalty under Section 271B:

- 0.5% of turnover or gross receipts, subject to a maximum of ₹1,50,000

However, penalty may not be imposed if the taxpayer can demonstrate reasonable cause for non-compliance.

## Practical Considerations

### In application, tax audit compliance often hinges on

- Accurate turnover determination
- Proper classification between business and professional income
- Monitoring of cash transaction thresholds
- Timely coordination between taxpayer and auditor

Delays in initiating the audit process frequently lead to last-minute compliance risks, particularly in cases involving complex transactions or incomplete documentation.

## Conclusion

Section 44AB establishes a structured framework for ensuring transparency and accuracy in tax reporting. Its applicability extends beyond large businesses, covering professionals and certain presumptive taxation scenarios. Given its detailed reporting requirements and statutory timelines, proactive planning and disciplined record-keeping are essential. Compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 not only avoids penalties but also strengthens the integrity of financial reporting.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 15CA and Form 15CB: Compliance for International Payments
articles.excerpt: Mandatory TDS compliance forms for foreign remittances — when to file each Part of 15CA, when you need a CA certificate (15CB), DTAA considerations, and Rule 37BB exempt categories.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 15CA and Form 15CB are documents required when a resident of India makes a payment to a non-resident or foreign company. They are a compliance mechanism through which the government tracks whether tax has been correctly deducted at source on international remittances **before the money leaves India**.

The logic is straightforward: payments made to non-residents may be taxable in India depending on the nature of income and the applicable tax treaty. The government wants to ensure that TDS is correctly applied before the foreign payee takes the money abroad.

- **Form 15CA** is the declaration by the remitter (payer).
- **Form 15CB** is the certificate from a Chartered Accountant confirming that the payment is taxable and that correct TDS has been applied.

## Who Should Use These Forms

### Form 15CA is required from

- Any individual, firm, or company making a remittance to a non-resident that may be taxable in India
- This includes salary to NRI employees, consultancy fees, royalties, dividends, property purchase payments from NRIs, loan repayments, and similar transactions

### Form 15CB is required from

- A **Chartered Accountant** (the payer obtains this from their CA)
- Required only for certain categories of payments above specified thresholds where taxability is not straightforward

> [!NOTE] Exemptions exist
> Some remittances are exempt from these requirements (e.g., import payments covered under specific RBI guidelines, travel expenses, education payments abroad under personal accounts).

## Form 15CA: Four Parts Explained

| Part | When to Use |
| --- | --- |
| Part A | Payment not exceeding Rs. 5 lakh in a financial year (aggregate) and taxable |
| Part B | Order or certificate from the Assessing Officer has been obtained regarding TDS |
| Part C | Amount is taxable, exceeds Rs. 5 lakh, and Form 15CB has been obtained from CA |
| Part D | Payment is not taxable in India (covered by tax treaty or exempt) |

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Determine the nature of the payment.** Is it for services, royalty, interest, salary, dividend, or something else?
2. **Check the applicable tax treaty (DTAA)** between India and the recipient's country. India has DTAAs with over 90 countries. The treaty may reduce or eliminate Indian tax on the payment.
3. **Determine taxability.**
    - If the payment is **not taxable** in India (due to treaty or exemption): File Form 15CA Part D.
    - If taxable and payment is **below Rs. 5 lakh** in aggregate: File Form 15CA Part A.
    - If taxable and payment **exceeds Rs. 5 lakh**: Obtain Form 15CB from a CA, then file Form 15CA Part C.
4. **Engage a Chartered Accountant for Form 15CB.** The CA reviews the nature of the remittance, applicable treaty provisions, correct TDS rate, and whether TDS has been or should be deducted. The CA then issues Form 15CB certifying the above.
5. **File Form 15CA online.** Log in to `incometax.gov.in` → e-File → Income Tax Forms → File Income Tax Forms. Select Form 15CA. Enter all details about the remitter, remittee, nature of payment, country, currency, amount, and TDS deducted. Upload or reference the Form 15CB details (acknowledgment number).
6. **Generate the acknowledgment** and furnish it to your bank or authorised dealer (AD) through which the remittance will be sent.
7. **The bank processes the remittance** only after receiving the Form 15CA acknowledgment and Form 15CB (where applicable).

## Transactions Exempt from Form 15CA / 15CB

| Category | Condition |
| --- | --- |
| Import of goods | Standard commercial imports |
| Personal travel expenses abroad | Within permissible LRS limits |
| Maintenance of close relatives abroad | Under LRS |
| Studies abroad | Under LRS |
| Medical treatment abroad | Under LRS |
| Many others under Rule 37BB | Specifically listed 28 categories |

> [!NOTE] Rule 37BB
> Rule 37BB of the Income Tax Rules lists **28 specific types** of remittances that are exempt from the Form 15CA / 15CB requirement.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Not filing Form 15CA before the remittance is processed.** The form must be filed and presented to the bank before the money is sent. Filing it afterward is not compliant.
2. **Choosing the wrong Part of Form 15CA.** Selecting Part D when the payment is actually taxable (to avoid obtaining Form 15CB) is a misrepresentation with serious legal consequences.
3. **Not consulting a CA when Form 15CB is required.** Some payers try to self-assess taxability and skip Form 15CB. This is non-compliant and can expose them to penalties.
4. **Not considering the DTAA.** Payments that appear taxable under domestic law may be fully exempt or taxed at a reduced rate under an applicable treaty.
5. **Using the form for payments that are fully exempt under Rule 37BB.** While this is not a penalty-attracting mistake, it creates unnecessary compliance work and delays.

## Tips

1. **Maintain a clear paper trail** of every international payment that required Form 15CA / 15CB, including the CA certificate, the online acknowledgment, and the bank's SWIFT confirmation.
2. **If you are a small business** making regular payments to foreign consultants or software vendors, set up a standardised process with your CA firm so that Form 15CB issuance and 15CA filing happen smoothly.
3. **Track DTAA provisions** carefully for your key foreign payee countries. Some treaties require the foreign payee to provide a **Tax Residency Certificate (TRC)** for the treaty benefit to apply.
4. **Do not wait for the invoice payment due date** to initiate compliance. Start the process **5 to 7 working days** before the payment is due.
5. **Monitor changes in tax treaties.** India has renegotiated several DTAAs in recent years. A rate that was applicable last year may have changed.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I need to pay a foreign software company for a SaaS subscription. Do I need Form 15CA?

It depends on whether the payment is classified as a **royalty** (taxable in India) or a purchase of services (may not be taxable if the company has no permanent establishment in India). Many SaaS subscriptions fall under royalty in Indian tax law, making Form 15CA necessary. Consult a CA.

### Q2. Can Form 15CB be issued by a CA who is not the company's statutory auditor?

Yes. **Any practising Chartered Accountant** can issue Form 15CB. It does not need to be the same CA who audits your financial statements.

### Q3. My bank rejected my international transfer because I did not submit Form 15CA. What now?

File Form 15CA online immediately, get Form 15CB from your CA if required, and present the acknowledgments to the bank. The bank will then process the transfer.

### Q4. Is Form 15CA required for repayment of an ECB (External Commercial Borrowing)?

**Repayment of principal** of ECB is typically not taxable in India and may fall under exempt categories under Rule 37BB. **Interest payments** on ECB, however, may be taxable and would require Form 15CA. Verify with a CA based on specific loan terms and the applicable DTAA.

### Q5. What is the penalty for not filing Form 15CA before a foreign remittance?

Under **Section 271-I** of the Income Tax Act, a penalty of **Rs. 1,00,000** can be levied for failure to furnish information or for furnishing inaccurate information in Form 15CA. The bank may also refuse to process the transfer without the form.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 16: Your Complete TDS Certificate from Employer
articles.excerpt: Form 16 is the salary TDS certificate issued by your employer every year. Structure (Part A and Part B), how to use it for ITR filing, common mistakes, and cross-checks with Form 26AS.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 16 is a TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) certificate issued by employers to employees every financial year. It is one of the most important documents in the hands of a salaried taxpayer. It certifies how much tax was deducted from your salary during the year and deposited with the government, and provides a complete breakup of your salary and the deductions that were considered while computing your tax liability.

Think of Form 16 as a **pre-filled summary of everything your employer knows about your income and tax**. For most salaried taxpayers with straightforward income, Form 16 contains almost everything they need to file their income tax return.

## Who Should Use It

- Every **salaried employee** whose employer has deducted TDS from salary
- Individuals filing **ITR-1 or ITR-2** (primarily salaried filers)
- Anyone applying for a home loan, visa, or bank credit (Form 16 is commonly required as income proof)
- Tax professionals preparing returns for salaried clients

> [!NOTE] When Form 16 is not required
> If your total salary income is below the taxable threshold and no TDS was deducted, your employer is not legally required to issue Form 16. However, many employers issue it anyway as a salary summary.

## Structure of Form 16

Form 16 has two parts.

### Part A

| Detail | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| Employer's TAN and PAN | Identifying the deductor |
| Employee's PAN | Identifying the deductee |
| Employment period | Duration of employment during the year |
| Summary of TDS deducted | Quarter-wise TDS deducted and deposited |
| Challan details | Government deposit reference numbers |

### Part B

| Detail | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Total salary before any deductions |
| Allowance exemptions | HRA, LTA, and other exempt allowances |
| Net salary (taxable) | After exemptions |
| Deductions under Chapter VI-A | 80C, 80D, 80CCD(1B), etc. |
| Total taxable income | Final income after all deductions |
| Tax computation | Tax on total income, surcharge, and cess |
| Relief under Section 89 | If applicable |
| TDS deducted | Amount deducted and deposited |

> [!IMPORTANT] Part A is government-authenticated; Part B is employer-generated
> Part A is generated directly from TRACES (the TDS Reconciliation Analysis and Correction Enabling System) and carries a digital signature. Part B is generated by the employer and may vary in format.

## Step-by-Step: How to Use Form 16 for ITR Filing

1. **Collect Form 16 from your employer.** It should be issued by **June 15** following the end of the financial year (e.g., by June 15, 2025 for FY 2024-25).
2. **Check Part A carefully.** Verify your PAN, employer TAN, and the TDS amounts match what is shown in your Form 26AS or AIS on the Income Tax portal.
3. **Review Part B.** Verify that the salary figures, allowance exemptions, and deductions match your actual salary slips and investment declarations you submitted to HR.
4. **Cross-check the TDS credit in Form 26AS.** Go to the Income Tax portal, download your Form 26AS, and confirm that the TDS shown in Form 16 Part A matches what is credited under your PAN.
5. **Identify any other income not covered in Form 16.** Bank interest, rental income, capital gains, or freelance income are not in Form 16 and must be added separately in your ITR.
6. **Proceed to file your ITR**, using Form 16 data as the primary input.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Trusting Part B blindly without cross-checking.** Part B is prepared by HR or the payroll team. Errors in HRA exemption calculations, wrong investment declarations, or missed salary components are not uncommon. Always verify against your salary slips.
2. **Not checking Form 26AS for TDS credit mismatch.** If your employer deposited TDS but under a wrong PAN or there was a delay, the credit may not appear in your name. Filing without resolving this causes tax demand notices later.
3. **Assuming Form 16 covers all income.** It covers only salary. Interest income, mutual fund gains, rental income, and other sources must be added by you.
4. **Using last year's Form 16 details by mistake.** Some taxpayers file using the previous year's Form 16 values when the current year's document is delayed.
5. **Not collecting Form 16 from previous employer if you changed jobs during the year.** Both Part A documents (from each employer) are needed to report the full year's salary correctly.

## Tips

1. **If you changed employers during the year**, submit the previous employer's Form 16 or at least the salary details to your new employer. Otherwise, TDS might be computed incorrectly at the new employer, leaving you with a large tax shortfall.
2. **Cross-verify HRA exemption in Part B** with your actual rent receipts and rent agreement. Employers sometimes make errors in this calculation.
3. **Request Form 16 early**, especially if you plan to apply for a home loan. Many banks require Form 16 from the last 2 to 3 years as income proof.
4. **If your employer cannot issue Form 16** because the company shut down, use salary slips, bank statements, and your AIS to reconstruct the income data for ITR filing.
5. **Use Form 16 to verify if your employer correctly accounted for all investment proofs** you submitted. If they missed something, you can claim the deduction directly in your ITR even if it is not reflected in Form 16.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Is Form 16 mandatory for filing ITR?

Form 16 is **not legally required** for ITR filing. You can file using your salary slips, bank statements, and AIS. However, Form 16 simplifies the process significantly and is considered the authoritative salary summary from your employer.

### Q2. My employer did not deduct any TDS because my income was below the threshold. Will they issue Form 16?

If no TDS was deducted, the employer is not obligated to issue Form 16. However, they may still issue a salary certificate or Part B alone as a courtesy.

### Q3. I noticed my Form 16 has an incorrect PAN. What should I do?

Contact your employer's HR or payroll team immediately. An incorrect PAN in Form 16 means the TDS credit will not appear under your correct PAN in the tax system, and you will not be able to claim that credit. The employer must correct and reissue the certificate.

### Q4. Can I get Form 16 for a company I worked for that is now closed?

If the company has filed its TDS returns with the correct PAN, your TDS credit will still appear in your **Form 26AS**. Download your 26AS for the relevant year from the Income Tax portal to verify and use those figures for filing.

### Q5. I received a bonus in April that relates to last financial year. Will it appear in my Form 16?

Bonus is taxable in the year it is **received**, not the year it relates to. If the bonus was paid in April 2025, it will appear in the Form 16 for FY 2025-26, not FY 2024-25.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 16 vs Form 16A: Understanding the Difference
articles.excerpt: Form 16 is for salary TDS. Form 16A is for every other kind of TDS — FD interest, professional fees, rent, commission. A clear side-by-side comparison with sections, deductors, and filing steps.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Both Form 16 and Form 16A are TDS certificates. However, they serve very different purposes and are issued by completely different types of deductors. Confusing the two is one of the most common errors taxpayers make when compiling documents for ITR filing.

**Form 16 is the TDS certificate for salary income. Form 16A is the TDS certificate for all non-salary income.** Understanding this distinction helps you gather the correct documents, reconcile your TDS credits, and ensure you claim every rupee of TDS that has been deducted from your income during the year.

## Quick Comparison

| Feature | Form 16 | Form 16A |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Income type covered | Salary income only | Non-salary income (interest, rent, commission, professional fees, etc.) |
| Issued by | Employer | Banks, tenants, companies paying professional fees, others |
| Applicable TDS section | Section 192 | Sections 194A, 194H, 194I, 194J, and others |
| Frequency | Annual (once per year) | Quarterly |
| Part A source | TRACES (digitally signed) | TRACES (digitally signed) |
| Part B content | Detailed salary and deduction computation | Nature of payment, TDS amount, rates |
| Who uses it | Salaried employees | Anyone earning non-salary income with TDS |

## When Each Form Applies

### Form 16 applies when

- Your employer deducts TDS on your salary under Section 192
- You are a salaried employee, including contractual staff where TDS is deducted as salary

### Form 16A applies when

- A bank deducts TDS on FD interest under Section 194A
- A company deducts TDS on professional fees paid to a consultant under Section 194J
- A tenant deducts TDS on rent above Rs. 50,000 per month under Section 194IB
- A company deducts TDS on commission or brokerage under Section 194H
- Any other non-salary TDS situation

## Who Issues Form 16A

| Deductor | Payment Type | TDS Section |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Banks and NBFCs | FD interest, recurring deposit interest | 194A |
| Companies / individuals | Professional or technical fees | 194J |
| Tenants (paying rent above threshold) | Rent | 194IB |
| Companies | Commission and brokerage | 194H |
| Buyers of immovable property | TDS on property purchase | 194IA |
| Life insurers | Maturity amount (where TDS applies) | 194DA |

All of these are issued as Form 16A certificates. Each deductor issues a separate Form 16A **for each quarter** in which they deducted TDS.

## Step-by-Step: How to Use Both Forms for ITR

1. **Collect Form 16** from your employer for salary TDS credit.
2. **Collect Form 16A** from every deductor who deducted TDS on non-salary income: your bank (for FD interest), companies that paid you professional fees, tenants who deducted rent TDS, etc.
3. **Log in** to `incometax.gov.in` and download your Form 26AS and AIS for the financial year.
4. **Match each TDS entry in Form 26AS** against a corresponding certificate: either Form 16 (for salary TDS) or Form 16A (for non-salary TDS).
5. **If any TDS appears in 26AS but you do not have the corresponding certificate**, contact the deductor and request it. You can still claim the credit even without the physical certificate, as long as it appears in your 26AS.
6. **Add the income** corresponding to each Form 16A into the appropriate schedule of your ITR (interest under income from other sources, professional income under business schedule, etc.).
7. **Claim the TDS** from Form 16A as tax credit in **Schedule TDS** of your ITR.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Reporting only Form 16 income and ignoring Form 16A income.** The TDS shown in Form 16A corresponds to income you actually received. Not reporting that income creates an underreporting situation and can result in scrutiny.
2. **Not collecting Form 16A from the bank for FD interest.** Banks deduct TDS at 10% on FD interest exceeding Rs. 40,000 per year (Rs. 50,000 for senior citizens). Many people assume this TDS is final and skip reporting the interest income. It is not final — it must be reported.
3. **Assuming Form 16A replaces Form 16.** They are entirely separate. A salaried employee with FD income needs both.
4. **Not verifying Form 16A entries against Form 26AS.** Sometimes deductors file incorrect TAN or PAN. If a Form 16A entry does not appear in your 26AS, the credit will be denied.
5. **Filing ITR without waiting for all Form 16A certificates**, leading to omissions that are later picked up by the Compliance Management system and result in notices.

## Tips

1. **Download your Form 26AS before you start collecting Form 16 and Form 16A.** It gives you a complete map of all TDS deducted under your PAN for the year, so you know exactly how many certificates to collect.
2. **For FD interest**, log in to your bank's net banking portal instead of waiting for Form 16A in the mail. Most major banks allow you to download Form 16A directly.
3. **If your Form 16A shows TDS but you submitted Form 15G / 15H** and the bank still deducted TDS, the TDS credit still belongs to you. You can claim it in your ITR.
4. **For professional income from multiple companies**, maintain a tracker of all deductors, TDS amounts, and quarters. This prevents any certificate from slipping through.
5. **Form 16A must be verified against TRACES.** A legitimate Form 16A carries a digital signature or TRACES watermark. Verify its authenticity if you have any doubts.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I earn rent from a tenant who is a company. They gave me a Form 16A. What do I do with it?

The Form 16A shows TDS deducted on the rent. Report the full rent amount as **income from house property** in your ITR, and claim the TDS credit reflected in the Form 16A under Schedule TDS-2.

### Q2. The bank deducted TDS on my FD interest but did not send me Form 16A. Can I still claim the credit?

Yes. As long as the TDS appears in your **Form 26AS** under your PAN, you can claim the credit in your ITR regardless of whether you have the physical certificate. The 26AS is the primary source.

### Q3. What if Form 16A shows a wrong PAN?

If the PAN is wrong, the TDS credit will not appear in your Form 26AS. Contact the deductor immediately and request a correction filing with the correct PAN. Only then will the credit be available to you.

### Q4. I received professional fees from a foreign company that did not deduct TDS. Do I still need Form 16A?

No. If no TDS was deducted, there is no Form 16A. You still need to report the foreign professional income in your ITR under income from business or profession. Foreign income may also need to be reported under **Schedule FSI** for tax treaty purposes.

### Q5. Is Form 16B (for property TDS) the same as Form 16A?

No. **Form 16B** is a separate TDS certificate issued by the buyer of immovable property under Section 194IA. Form 16A is a broader category for multiple non-salary income types. A property seller receives Form 16B, not Form 16A.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 26AS: Your Complete Tax Passbook Explained
articles.excerpt: Form 26AS is the consolidated tax statement recording every rupee of TDS, advance tax, self-assessment tax, and refund against your PAN. How to download, reconcile, and use it before filing your ITR.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 26AS is the consolidated tax statement maintained by the Income Tax Department against your PAN. It records every rupee of tax paid or deducted under your PAN throughout the financial year — whether it was TDS deducted by your employer, TDS deducted by your bank, advance tax paid by you, self-assessment tax paid, or refunds received.

It is the **single most reliable document for verifying your tax credits** before filing your return. Any mismatch between what you report in your ITR and what is in your 26AS can trigger a demand notice or refund rejection.

> [!IMPORTANT] 26AS now includes AIS data
> Since 2021, 26AS has been significantly enhanced and now also includes information from the Annual Information Statement (AIS), which tracks high-value financial transactions such as mutual fund purchases, property sales, foreign remittances, and large cash deposits.

## Who Should Use It

**Every taxpayer** should review Form 26AS before filing an ITR. Specifically:

- Salaried employees verifying employer TDS credits
- Investors checking TDS on FD interest and dividend income
- Freelancers and consultants verifying TDS deducted by clients
- Property sellers checking TDS deducted by the buyer
- Anyone who has paid advance tax or self-assessment tax during the year

## What Form 26AS Contains

| Part | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Part A | TDS deducted by deductors (employers, banks, companies) |
| Part B | TDS deducted on sale of immovable property (Section 194IA) |
| Part C | Self-assessment tax and advance tax paid by you |
| Part D | Refunds received from the Income Tax Department |
| Part E | Annual Information Statement details (high-value transactions) |
| Part F | TDS on rent under Section 194IB (tenant deductions) |
| Part G | TDS under Section 194M (payment to contractors / professionals by individuals) |

## Step-by-Step: How to Download Form 26AS

1. Visit `incometax.gov.in` and log in using your PAN and password.
2. Go to **e-File → Income Tax Returns → View Form 26AS**. Alternatively, find it under the e-File menu under Income Tax Forms.
3. You will be redirected to the **TRACES portal** (TDS-CPC website) or you can access it directly via 26AS on the new portal dashboard.
4. Select the relevant assessment year.
5. Choose the format: **HTML** (for viewing) or **PDF** (for download). The PDF version requires a password — your date of birth in DDMMYYYY format.
6. **Review each part** of the document systematically.

## How to Reconcile Form 26AS with Your Records

| Document | What to Match with 26AS |
| --- | --- |
| Form 16 Part A | TDS deducted by employer under Section 192 in Part A |
| Form 16A (bank FD interest) | TDS from bank under Section 194A in Part A |
| Form 16A (professional fees) | TDS from companies under Section 194J in Part A |
| Advance tax challans | Amounts in Part C should match your challan receipts |
| Self-assessment tax challan | Entries in Part C |
| Property sale | TDS deducted by buyer appears in Part B |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Not checking 26AS at all before filing.** Many taxpayers rely entirely on Form 16 and miss TDS credits or income entries that only appear in 26AS.
2. **Ignoring Part E (high-value transactions).** Banks, brokers, and registrars report large transactions to the government. If you sold property, made large mutual fund investments, or had significant cash deposits, these appear in 26AS. Not disclosing the corresponding income is a serious compliance risk.
3. **Assuming all TDS credits in 26AS automatically appear in the ITR.** You still need to claim TDS credits explicitly in Schedule TDS of your ITR (though prefill data helps).
4. **Not resolving TDS mismatches before filing.** If Form 16 shows Rs. 80,000 TDS but 26AS shows only Rs. 60,000, the missing Rs. 20,000 has not been credited. Resolve with your employer first.
5. **Confusing 26AS with AIS.** Form 26AS focuses on taxes paid and deducted. AIS (Annual Information Statement) is broader and covers all financial transactions. Both should be reviewed together.

## Tips

1. **Download your 26AS in April or May** after year-end. By then, most employers and deductors will have filed their quarterly TDS returns for Q4, and the data will be reasonably complete.
2. **Cross-check the SFT (Specified Financial Transactions) entries** in Part E against your own records. If you sold property but the transaction is not in 26AS, check whether the buyer correctly deducted and deposited TDS.
3. **Use the AIS alongside 26AS.** AIS may show income not captured in 26AS, such as dividends, interest from small bank accounts, or crypto transactions.
4. **If you notice an incorrect entry in 26AS** (for example, a transaction attributed to your PAN that was not yours), raise a grievance on the TRACES portal or contact the erroneous deductor.
5. **Archive your annual Form 26AS every year.** Maintaining a year-wise record helps significantly if you face scrutiny or need to respond to past-year notices.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. My Form 26AS shows TDS against my PAN that I do not recognise. What should I do?

Do not ignore it. This could be a case of incorrect PAN being quoted by a deductor for someone else's transaction, which means another person's tax has been credited to your account. Report this to the Income Tax helpdesk and the deductor concerned. Filing without clarification can create issues.

### Q2. I paid advance tax but it does not appear in my Form 26AS. What went wrong?

Advance tax paid through the Income Tax portal or bank is usually reflected in Part C within a few days to weeks. If it does not appear, verify your challan details. The **CIN (Challan Identification Number)** on your payment receipt can be used to trace the deposit.

### Q3. Is Form 26AS sufficient to file ITR without Form 16?

Yes, in principle. If your TDS appears correctly in Form 26AS and you can reconstruct your income from salary slips or bank records, you can file without Form 16. However, Form 16 provides a more detailed computation, especially for exemptions and deductions.

### Q4. Can I get Form 26AS for past years?

Yes. The Income Tax portal allows you to view and download Form 26AS for **multiple past assessment years**. Log in, select the relevant year, and download accordingly.

### Q5. My employer deducted TDS in March but it does not show in my current year 26AS. Is there a problem?

TDS deducted in March is reported by the employer in their **Q4 TDS return**. If this return was filed late or after you checked your 26AS, it may not reflect yet. Check again after mid-May when Q4 returns are typically processed.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 15G and Form 15H: Stop TDS on Your Interest Income
articles.excerpt: Self-declaration forms to stop TDS on FD interest, EPF withdrawals, and other non-salary income — who can file, eligibility conditions, step-by-step process, and key differences between 15G and 15H.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 15G and Form 15H are self-declaration forms that individuals submit to financial institutions (banks, post offices, NBFCs, mutual funds) to request that **TDS not be deducted** from their income, provided their total income for the year is expected to be below the taxable threshold.

These forms exist because TDS is deducted at fixed rates regardless of a person's actual tax liability. A senior citizen or a low-income individual with FD interest may have zero actual tax liability but still face TDS deductions. Submitting Form 15G or Form 15H ensures the deductor does not deduct TDS, protecting your cash flow and sparing you from filing for refunds.

## Form 15G vs Form 15H: The Difference

| Feature | Form 15G | Form 15H |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Who files it | Individuals below 60 years, HUFs | Senior citizens (60 years and above) |
| Age condition | Below 60 years | 60 years or above |
| Tax liability condition | Tax on total estimated income must be nil | Tax on total estimated income must be nil |
| Income threshold | Total income (including interest) must be within the basic exemption limit | No condition that income be within the basic exemption limit; only that final tax liability is nil |
| HUF eligibility | Yes | No |

> [!NOTE] Key distinction
> A senior citizen with income slightly above the basic exemption limit may still file Form 15H if the deductions and senior citizen slab benefits reduce the final tax to nil. A person below 60 cannot file Form 15G unless total income is within the basic exemption limit.

## Where Form 15G and 15H Can Be Submitted

| Institution | Type of Income |
| --- | --- |
| Banks | FD interest, recurring deposit interest, savings account interest above threshold |
| Post offices | Interest on NSC, post office deposits |
| EPF office | For EPF withdrawals before 5 years of continuous service |
| Mutual fund houses | Dividend income |
| Insurance companies | Maturity proceeds where TDS applies |
| NBFC | Interest on deposits |

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Estimate your total income** for the financial year. Include all sources: salary, pension, FD interest, rental income, capital gains, and any other income.
2. **Check your eligibility.**
    - **Form 15G:** Must be below 60 years; tax on estimated income must be nil; total income (incl. FD interest) must not exceed basic exemption limit.
    - **Form 15H:** Must be 60 years or above; tax liability must be nil even if total income is above the basic exemption limit (deductions can bring it to nil).
3. **Collect the form.** Download Form 15G or 15H from the Income Tax portal or get it from your bank.
4. **Fill in your personal details:** name, PAN, residential status, and estimated income from all sources.
5. **Enter the estimated income** on which the form is being submitted (e.g., FD interest from that particular institution).
6. **Sign the declaration** (physical or digital signature as required).
7. **Submit to the institution** at the beginning of the financial year, **before any TDS is deducted**. Do not wait until interest is credited.
8. **Submit separately to each institution.** A single form does not cover all your accounts or FDs.
9. **Collect an acknowledgment** or reference number from the institution.
10. **Declare these submissions in your ITR** under the relevant schedule — including the number of forms filed and the aggregate income declared.

## TDS Rates If You Do Not Submit

| Income Type | TDS Rate Without Form 15G / 15H |
| --- | --- |
| Bank FD interest (above Rs. 40,000 / Rs. 50,000 for seniors) | 10% (20% if PAN not provided) |
| EPF withdrawal before 5 years (above Rs. 50,000) | 10% |
| Insurance maturity proceeds (taxable) | 5% |
| Dividend from mutual funds (above Rs. 5,000) | 10% |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Submitting Form 15G when income exceeds the eligibility threshold.** Filing a false declaration is a punishable offence under Section 277 of the Income Tax Act. If TDS is wrongly avoided, interest and penalties apply.
2. **Submitting only at one branch** when FDs are spread across multiple branches of the same bank. You must submit to each branch separately, or check if your bank accepts a consolidated online submission.
3. **Not renewing the form every financial year.** These forms are valid for one financial year. A new declaration must be submitted at the start of every year.
4. **Submitting without a valid PAN.** Without PAN, the TDS rate is 20% regardless of any declaration.
5. **Not disclosing these submissions in the ITR.** Skipping this creates a mismatch.

## Tips

1. **Submit through your bank's net banking or mobile banking platform** if they support online submission. Most leading banks (SBI, HDFC, ICICI, Axis, Kotak) allow online submission with immediate confirmation.
2. **Even if you submitted Form 15G, always report the full interest income in your ITR.** TDS not deducted does not mean income not taxable.
3. **Check your Form 26AS after submitting.** It shows submissions recorded by the deductor.
4. **For EPF withdrawals**, submit Form 15G to the EPF office **before** processing the withdrawal claim, not after.
5. **Senior citizens filing Form 15H should estimate income conservatively.** If actual income turns out higher and tax liability arises, the declaration becomes incorrect.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can a 59-year-old file Form 15H?

No. Form 15H is exclusively for individuals who are **60 years of age or above** during the relevant financial year. A 59-year-old must file Form 15G if eligible.

### Q2. I submitted Form 15G but the bank still deducted TDS. What should I do?

Contact the bank immediately and request a refund or reversal if the TDS was deducted in the same quarter and the return has not yet been filed by the bank. If it is too late for reversal, claim the TDS credit in your ITR and request a refund from the Income Tax Department.

### Q3. My total FD interest is Rs. 35,000. Do I still need to submit Form 15G?

If the FD interest is below the Rs. 40,000 threshold (Rs. 50,000 for senior citizens), the bank will not deduct TDS. You do not need to submit Form 15G in this case. However, you still need to report the interest income in your ITR.

### Q4. I have FDs in three different banks. Do I submit one Form 15G?

No. You must submit a **separate Form 15G / 15H to each bank or branch** where you have income subject to TDS. The total estimated income across all sources should still be within the eligible limit.

### Q5. What happens if I submit Form 15G but my actual income ends up exceeding the threshold?

If your actual income turns out higher than estimated and your tax liability is no longer nil, you must pay the tax (including interest for any shortfall in advance tax) and file your ITR accordingly. The declaration made in Form 15G would be incorrect, and while this can attract scrutiny, genuine estimation errors are generally treated differently from deliberate misrepresentation.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Is an Interest-free Loan from Employer Taxable?
articles.excerpt: Interest-free or concessional loans provided by employers are a common component of employee compensation structures. While such loans may appear to be a non-taxable financial benefit, their tax treatment is specifically addressed under…
articles.content:
Interest-free or concessional loans provided by employers are a common component of employee compensation structures. While such loans may appear to be a non-taxable financial benefit, their tax treatment is specifically addressed under the Income Tax Act,

1. **In certain cases, these loans give rise to a taxable perquisite, even though no direct** — income is received. Understanding the Tax Principle

The core issue is whether the employee derives a benefit from the employer. When a loan is provided at zero or concessional interest, the difference between the market rate and the rate actually charged is treated as a perquisite. This notional benefit is added to the employee’s salary income and taxed accordingly.

## When It Becomes Taxable

### An interest-free or low-interest loan is taxable when

- It is provided by an employer to an employee
- The interest charged is lower than the prescribed benchmark rate
- The loan exceeds specified thresholds

The valuation of this benefit is done using the State Bank of India (SBI) lending rate applicable at the beginning of the financial year for similar loans. Situations Where It Is Not Taxable Not all such loans are taxed. The law provides specific exclusions:

- Where the aggregate loan amount does not exceed ₹20,000
- Loans provided for medical treatment of specified diseases, subject to conditions
- Where interest charged is equal to or higher than the prescribed benchmark rate

These carve-outs ensure that small or necessity-driven loans are not unnecessarily taxed.

## How the Perquisite is Calculated

### The taxable value is computed as

- Interest calculated at the SBI benchmark rate, minus
- Interest actually recovered from the employee

This difference is treated as a taxable perquisite and included in salary. The calculation is typically done on a monthly outstanding balance basis, making it sensitive to repayment schedules.

## Practical Illustration

If an employer provides a loan of ₹5,00,000 at zero interest and the applicable SBI rate is 8%, the notional interest benefit becomes taxable. If the employee repays part of the loan during the year, the taxable value reduces accordingly, as the computation is based on the outstanding balance. Reporting and Compliance Employers are responsible for:

- Calculating the perquisite value
- Including it in salary income
- Deducting TDS accordingly
- Reflecting it in Form 16

Employees, in turn, should verify that the perquisite is correctly accounted for while filing their returns.

## Practical Interpretation

In substance, interest-free loans function as a non-cash benefit. Even though no income is directly received, the saving in interest cost is treated as an economic gain. However, in structured corporate environments, these loans are often planned within compensation frameworks, with tax implications already factored into payroll systems.

## Conclusion

Interest-free or concessional loans from employers are not automatically tax-free. Their taxability depends on the benefit derived in terms of interest savings, which is treated as a perquisite under salary income. Accurate valuation and reporting ensure compliance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, while also helping employees understand the real tax impact of such financial arrangements.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Joint Declaration Form EPF
articles.excerpt: The Employees’ Provident Fund (EPF) system is built on accurate member data—name, date of birth, father’s name, and other identifiers. Any mismatch between EPF records and official documents can delay withdrawals, transfers, or claim…
articles.content:
The Employees’ Provident Fund (EPF) system is built on accurate member data—name, date of birth, father’s name, and other identifiers. Any mismatch between EPF records and official documents can delay withdrawals, transfers, or claim settlements. The Joint Declaration Form is the prescribed mechanism to correct such discrepancies within the EPF framework administered under the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952.

## Why It Matters

EPF records are often created at the time of first employment and carried forward across jobs. Errors introduced at this stage—whether typographical or due to incomplete documentation—can persist for years. These mismatches become critical when:

- Filing final settlement or withdrawal claims
- Transferring EPF balances between employers
- Linking EPF with Aadhaar, PAN, or bank accounts

Without correction, claims may be delayed or rejected due to identity inconsistencies.

## What the Joint Declaration Form Is

The Joint Declaration Form is a correction request jointly signed by the employee and employer, submitted to the EPFO to update member details. It is typically used to correct:

- Name spelling errors
- Date of birth
- Father’s or spouse’s name
- Date of joining or exit
- Gender or other personal details

The “joint” aspect ensures that both employer records and EPF records are aligned.

## What Is Required Before Filing

Before submitting the form, certain elements must be in place:

- UAN (Universal Account Number) activated
- Aadhaar and PAN linked (where applicable)
- Supporting documents reflecting correct details
- Employer verification readiness

Consistency across documents is essential, as EPFO relies on documentary proof for approval. Step-by-Step Process The process can be carried out either through the employer or, in certain cases, via the EPFO portal:

1. **Identify the discrepancy** — Review EPF passbook or UAN profile to confirm incorrect details.
2. **Obtain Joint Declaration Form** — Available through employer HR or EPFO formats.
3. **Fill correct and incorrect details** — Clearly mention existing details and proposed corrections.
4. **Attach supporting documents** — Typically includes Aadhaar, PAN, or other identity proofs.
5. **Employer attestation** — The employer verifies and signs the form.
6. **Submission to EPFO** — Form is submitted physically or through employer channels.

## EPFO verification and update

Details are verified and updated in records.

## Documents Commonly Accepted

- Aadhaar Card
- PAN Card
- Passport
- Birth certificate or school leaving certificate
- Driving license (in some cases)

The document submitted must clearly support the correction being requested.

## Practical Situations

An employee whose name is recorded differently in EPF compared to Aadhaar must submit a joint declaration to align both records before initiating withdrawal. In another case, an incorrect date of birth entered at the time of joining may require correction to avoid rejection during final settlement. Such corrections are often a prerequisite for seamless EPF transactions. Operational Considerations While the process appears procedural, delays typically arise due to:

- Mismatch between submitted documents
- Lack of employer verification
- Multiple corrections requested simultaneously

In recent years, digitization and Aadhaar-based verification have reduced the need for manual intervention in some cases, but the Joint Declaration Form remains relevant where system-based updates are not feasible.

## Conclusion

The Joint Declaration Form serves as a corrective bridge between EPF records and official identity documents. Its importance lies not in routine compliance but in enabling smooth access to EPF benefits when required. Ensuring accurate and consistent data across records helps avoid delays and supports efficient processing under the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Taxation of Income Earned from Selling Shares
articles.excerpt: Income arising from the sale of shares is classified under capital gains and is taxed based on the holding period and nature of the transaction. With increasing retail participation in equity markets, understanding the tax treatment of…
articles.content:
## Overview

Income arising from the sale of shares is classified under capital gains and is taxed based on the holding period and nature of the transaction. With increasing retail participation in equity markets, understanding the tax treatment of such gains has become essential for accurate reporting and compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961. The taxation framework distinguishes between short-term and long-term gains, with different rates and conditions applicable to each. Classification of Gains The first step in determining tax liability is identifying the holding period of shares:

- Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG): Shares held for 12 months or less
- Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG): Shares held for more than 12 months

This classification directly impacts the applicable tax rate. Tax Treatment Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG)

- Applicable when listed shares are sold within 12 months
- Taxed at a special rate of 15%, provided Securities Transaction Tax (STT) is paid Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG)
- Applicable when shares are held for more than 12 months
- Gains up to ₹1,00,000 are exempt
- Gains exceeding ₹1,00,000 are taxed at 10% (without indexation)
- STT must be paid for concessional rates to apply Unlisted Shares
- Different holding period rules (generally 24 months)
- Taxed at applicable slab rates (STCG) or 20% with indexation (LTCG) How Gains Are Calculated Capital gains are computed as:
- Sale Value minus
- Cost of Acquisition minus
- Expenses related to transfer (brokerage, etc.)

For LTCG on listed shares, the concept of grandfathering (for older investments) may apply in specific cases.

## Practical Workflow

To correctly compute and report gains:

1. **Compile all buy and sell transactions**
2. **Determine holding period for each transaction**
3. **Segregate into STCG and LTCG**
4. **Calculate gains after adjusting for costs**
5. **Apply relevant tax rates**
6. **Include details in the appropriate schedule of ITR**

## Pay additional tax if required

Illustrative Examples If shares purchased in January are sold in September of the same year, the gain is classified as short-term and taxed at 15%. If shares are held for over a year and sold with a gain of ₹1,50,000, only ₹50,000 is taxable (after ₹1,00,000 exemption) at 10%. Treatment of Losses Losses from share transactions can be adjusted:

- Short-term capital loss (STCL) can be set off against both STCG and LTCG
- Long-term capital loss (LTCL) can be set off only against LTCG

Unabsorbed losses can be carried forward for 8 assessment years, subject to timely return filing. Reporting and Compliance Taxpayers must ensure:

- All transactions are accurately reported
- TDS/TCS (if any) is reconciled with Form 26AS
- Capital gains schedules in ITR are properly filled

With data increasingly captured through AIS and brokerage reports, discrepancies are more easily identified.

## Practical Interpretation

Frequent traders may need to evaluate whether their activity qualifies as business income instead of capital gains, depending on volume and intent. This classification has separate tax implications. Additionally, intra-day trading is treated differently and taxed as speculative business income.

## Conclusion

Taxation of share transactions is structured but detail-sensitive, with outcomes dependent on holding period, transaction type, and compliance accuracy. Proper classification and computation are essential to avoid errors and unintended tax exposure. With increasing digital reporting and data integration, accurate disclosure under the Income Tax Act, 1961 has become both more critical and more transparent.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Slab For Women For FY 2025-26: Tax Limit And Exemptions
articles.excerpt: The concept of separate income tax slabs for women has evolved over time. Under the current framework of the Income Tax Act, 1961, there is no separate tax slab exclusively for women. Tax rates are now uniform across individuals,…
articles.content:
## Are Tax Slabs Different for Women?

**No.** India abolished gender-based tax slabs in FY 2012-13. Women and men face identical income tax slabs under both regimes.

## Tax Slabs — FY 2025-26

### New Tax Regime (Default)

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
|---|---|
| Up to Rs. 4,00,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 4L – Rs. 8L | 5% |
| Rs. 8L – Rs. 12L | 10% |
| Rs. 12L – Rs. 16L | 15% |
| Rs. 16L – Rs. 20L | 20% |
| Rs. 20L – Rs. 24L | 25% |
| Above Rs. 24L | 30% |

> ✅ **Section 87A rebate:** Zero tax if total income ≤ ==Rs. 12,00,000== (New Regime, FY 2025-26).

### Old Tax Regime

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
|---|---|
| Up to Rs. 2,50,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 2.5L – Rs. 5L | 5% |
| Rs. 5L – Rs. 10L | 20% |
| Above Rs. 10L | 30% |

> ✅ Section 87A rebate under Old Regime: Zero tax if income ≤ ==Rs. 5,00,000==.

## Key Deductions for Women Taxpayers

| Section | Benefit | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Section 80C | EPF, PPF, ELSS, LIC, tuition fees | ==Rs. 1,50,000== |
| Section 80D | Health insurance premium | ==Rs. 25,000== (==Rs. 50,000== for senior citizen parents) |
| Section 80E | Education loan interest | Full interest, 8 years |
| Section 80CCD(1B) | NPS voluntary contribution | ==Rs. 50,000== extra |
| Section 10(13A) | HRA exemption | Actual/formula-based |
| Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana | EEE status — girl child only | Within ==Rs. 1.5L== 80C |

## Example Scenario

**Neha** (salaried, Old Regime): Gross ==Rs. 9.6 lakh==, standard deduction ==Rs. 50,000==, **80C** ==Rs. 1.5 lakh==, **80D** ==Rs. 25,000==. Taxable income falls to a lower slab band — she compares Old vs New each June using Form 12BB projections so payroll TDS matches final **ITR**.

## Planning Tips

> ✅ Co-borrowers on a home loan can each claim **Section 24(b)** interest (up to ==Rs. 2 lakh== each for self-occupied) and **80C** principal within individual caps.

> ⚠️ Stamp duty concessions for **women buyers** in some states are **not** income-tax benefits — still valuable for purchase budgeting.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q1. Are there any state-level tax benefits for women?**
Not in income tax. Some states offer reduced **stamp duty** on property registration for women buyers — check local rules.

**Q2. My husband and I jointly own a let-out property. Can I claim full deductions?**
No — each co-owner claims deductions **proportionate to ownership share** in their respective ITR.

**Q3. Does maternity pay/leave salary attract tax?**
Yes — maternity pay from a private employer is taxable as **salary**. Some statutory benefits may be exempt depending on the scheme; confirm with your employer/CA.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 234B: Interest for Non-Payment of Advance Tax
articles.excerpt: Section 234B charges 1% per month interest on shortfall in advance tax. This guide explains who must pay advance tax, the quarterly schedule, the interest formula, and the difference between Sections 234A, 234B, and 234C.
articles.content:
## What Is Section 234B?

Section 234B of the Income Tax Act, 1961 imposes interest on a taxpayer who has not paid advance tax or has paid **less than 90% of the assessed tax liability** by the end of the financial year (March 31st).

The idea behind advance tax is that the government collects tax as income is earned, not just when returns are filed. If you delay this payment, the government charges interest as compensation for the time value of money lost.

## Who Must Pay Advance Tax?

You must pay advance tax if your **total tax liability after TDS** for a financial year is **Rs. 10,000 or more**. This applies to:

- Salaried individuals with income from other sources such as interest, rent, or capital gains
- Self-employed professionals such as doctors, lawyers, and consultants
- Businesspersons with taxable profits
- Investors with substantial capital gains during the year

> [!NOTE] Senior citizens are exempt
> Senior citizens **above 60 years of age** who do not have business income are exempt from advance tax requirements.

## Advance Tax Payment Schedule

| Due Date | Minimum Cumulative Tax to Be Paid |
| --- | --- |
| By June 15 | At least 15% of estimated annual tax |
| By September 15 | At least 45% of estimated annual tax |
| By December 15 | At least 75% of estimated annual tax |
| By March 15 | At least 100% of estimated annual tax |

## How Is Section 234B Interest Calculated?

Interest is charged at **1% per month** (or part of a month) on the **shortfall** in advance tax. The shortfall is: 90% of assessed tax minus actual advance tax paid.

The interest runs from **April 1st of the assessment year** until the date of payment of tax or the date of assessment, whichever is earlier.

**Formula:** Interest = (Assessed Tax − TDS − Advance Tax Paid) × 1% × Number of Months

> [!WARN] Even a partial month counts as a full month
> If you pay on April 5, that counts as one full month. Delay another day to May 1 and it becomes two months. Pay early within each period.

## Example: Anand, Freelance Architect — Bengaluru

> [!TIP] What the interest worked out to
> Anand had a total tax liability of Rs. 3 lakh for FY 2023-24. TDS deducted: Rs. 50,000. Net advance tax required: Rs. 2.5 lakh.
> He paid Rs. 1.5 lakh by March 15 and the remaining Rs. 1 lakh when he filed his return in July 2024.
> Shortfall: Rs. 1 lakh | 234B Interest: 1% × Rs. 1 lakh × 4 months (April to July) = **Rs. 4,000**
> This is in addition to any Section 234C interest for late quarterly payments.

## Difference Between Sections 234A, 234B, and 234C

| Section | Triggered By | Interest Rate | Period |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 234A | Late filing of income tax return | 1% per month | From due date of filing to actual filing date |
| 234B | Shortfall in advance tax below 90% | 1% per month | April 1 of AY to date of payment or assessment |
| 234C | Short payment of advance tax in any instalment | 1% per month | 3 months each for first 3 instalments; 1 month for last |

## How to Respond and Rectify

1. **Pay the remaining tax** along with applicable interest before filing your return
2. **Use the self-assessment tax challan** (Challan 280) to pay the outstanding amount
3. **The ITR portal calculates 234B interest automatically** when you file — review it before submitting
4. **If interest has been wrongly computed** in an assessment order, file a rectification under Section 154

## Prevention Tips

- Estimate your total income at the beginning of the year, including expected capital gains
- Use the advance tax calculator on the income tax portal to plan quarterly payments
- If your income increases significantly mid-year due to a large capital gain or new contract, revise your estimate and pay accordingly
- Keep track of TDS deducted and reconcile with Form 26AS to ensure accurate advance tax computation
- If you are a senior citizen without business income, confirm your exemption status

## FAQs

### Can Section 234B interest be waived or reduced?

The Board has the power to waive or reduce interest in special circumstances, but this is rarely granted and requires a formal application. Paying the correct advance tax is the only reliable way to avoid this interest.

### Does Section 234B apply if all my income is from salary with TDS deducted?

If your employer deducts TDS correctly covering 90% or more of your total tax liability, Section 234B does not apply. However, if you have additional income from interest, capital gains, or freelance work on which TDS is not deducted, you need to pay advance tax on that portion.

### What if I paid advance tax on time but the AO later reassesses and increases my income?

In reassessment cases, interest under Section 234B runs from April 1 of the assessment year until the date of payment of the additional demand raised. The original advance tax paid is given credit.

### Does Section 234B apply to presumptive tax filers under Section 44AD?

Yes, but with a relaxation. Taxpayers under Section 44AD are required to pay the **entire advance tax by March 15 in a single instalment**. The graduated quarterly schedule does not apply to them.

### If I filed my return late and paid all taxes, do I pay both 234A and 234B interest?

Yes. Section 234A applies for late filing and Section 234B applies for shortfall in advance tax. They are independent charges and both apply simultaneously if applicable.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Customer Care Number: Helpline, Toll Free Number & Email ID
articles.excerpt: Navigating tax compliance often requires direct interaction with the Income Tax Department—whether for return filing issues, refund delays, or notice clarification. To streamline this, the department provides centralized support systems,…
articles.content:
Navigating tax compliance often requires direct interaction with the Income Tax Department—whether for return filing issues, refund delays, or notice clarification. To streamline this, the department provides centralized support systems, including helplines and email-based assistance, integrated with its digital infrastructure under the Income Tax Act, 1961.

## Why the Helpline Matters

With most tax processes now digitized, support queries typically relate to:

- ITR filing errors
- Login or portal access issues
- Refund status or delays
- PAN and Aadhaar linkage
- Notices and compliance requirements

The customer care system acts as a first-level resolution mechanism, reducing the need for physical visits.

## Key Helpline Numbers

The Income Tax Department provides multiple contact numbers based on the type of query:

- e-Filing Helpline: 1800-103-0025 / 1800-419-0025
- PAN/TAN Helpline: 020-27218080
- TIN (NSDL) Services: 020-27218080

These numbers are typically available during working hours, with extended support during peak filing seasons. Email Support For written queries or documentation-related issues, email support is available:

- e-Filing Support: efilingwebmanager@incometax.gov.in Email communication is useful for:
- Detailed issue descriptions
- Attaching supporting documents
- Tracking responses in a structured manner Types of Issues Handled

The support system covers a broad range of queries, including:

- Errors during ITR submission
- Problems with digital signature or OTP verification
- Refund reissue or bank account validation
- Rectification requests
- AIS/Form 26AS discrepancies

For more complex matters, escalation may be required.

## How to Use the Helpline Effectively

### Before contacting support, it is advisable to

- Keep PAN and registered mobile number ready
- Note down error messages or reference IDs
- Ensure login credentials are accessible
- Clearly define the issue

This helps in faster resolution and avoids repeated follow-ups. Alternative Support Channels Apart from helplines, the department offers:

- e-Filing portal helpdesk (ticket-based support)
- FAQs and guides available online
- Grievance redressal through CPGRAMS

These channels are particularly useful for non-urgent or documentation-heavy queries.

## Practical Context

During peak filing periods, such as July deadlines, helplines may experience high call volumes. In such cases, email or portal-based queries often provide more reliable tracking. For refund-related issues, verification through Form 26AS and bank details should be completed before raising a query.

## Conclusion

The Income Tax Department’s customer care system is an integral part of the digital tax ecosystem, providing accessible support for a wide range of compliance issues. While most queries can be resolved through these channels, effective use depends on clarity of information and proper documentation. Leveraging these resources ensures smoother interaction with the tax system and supports compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Debt Mutual Fund Taxation — The Post-2023 Landscape
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to debt mutual fund taxation post the April 2023 change: affected fund types, pre/post purchase treatment, tax-efficient alternatives like arbitrage funds, and real calculation examples.
articles.content:
## Overview

Debt mutual funds were once the poster child of tax-efficient investing for individuals in higher income brackets. The combination of indexation benefit and a 20% LTCG rate after 3 years meant that a 7% pre-tax return from a debt fund easily outpaced a 7% bank FD on a post-tax basis for anyone in the 30% bracket. **That advantage is gone for new investments.**

The Finance Act 2023 fundamentally altered the taxation of debt mutual funds effective April 1, 2023. For units purchased on or after that date, all gains, regardless of holding period, are taxed at the investor's slab rate. The distinction between STCG and LTCG no longer applies to these funds for tax computation purposes.

## The Pre vs Post April 2023 Tax Split

| Category | Units Purchased Before April 1, 2023 | Units Purchased April 1, 2023 Onwards |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Holding less than 3 years | STCG at slab rate | Slab rate (same) |
| Holding more than 3 years | 20% LTCG with indexation | Slab rate (no LTCG benefit) |
| Dividend (IDCW) payout | Slab rate at receipt | Slab rate at receipt |
| TDS on dividend | 10% if over Rs. 5,000 | 10% if over Rs. 5,000 |

## Which Funds Are Affected?

| Fund Type | Affected by April 2023 Change? |
| --- | --- |
| Liquid Funds | Yes |
| Overnight Funds | Yes |
| Ultra Short Duration Funds | Yes |
| Short Duration / Medium Duration Funds | Yes |
| Corporate Bond Funds | Yes |
| Gilt Funds | Yes |
| Dynamic Bond Funds | Yes |
| Conservative Hybrid Funds (equity less than 65%) | Yes |
| Gold ETFs / Gold Fund of Funds | Yes |
| International Fund of Funds | Yes |
| Equity Funds (equity 65% and above) | No (old rules apply) |
| Aggressive Hybrid Funds | No |

## Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Same fund, two investors with different purchase dates.

A corporate bond fund currently shows a return of 7.5% per annum.

### Investor A — Invested March 2020, redeems March 2025 (5 years)

Old rules apply. Held more than 3 years → LTCG with indexation.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Simple gain | ~Rs. 4,35,000 |
| Indexed cost (~25% inflation adjustment) | Reduces gain to ~Rs. 2,85,000 |
| Tax at 20% + cess | Rs. 59,280 |
| **Post-tax gain** | **Rs. 3,75,720** |

### Investor B — Invested May 2023, redeems May 2028 (5 years)

New rules apply. Slab rate regardless of holding period.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Simple gain | ~Rs. 4,35,000 |
| Tax at 30% + cess | Rs. 1,35,720 |
| **Post-tax gain** | **Rs. 2,99,280** |

> [!IMPORTANT] The gap is meaningful
> Difference in post-tax wealth after 5 years: **Rs. 76,440**. The old rules deliver meaningfully better outcomes for those holding pre-April 2023 units.

## Alternatives That Still Offer Tax Efficiency

| Alternative | Tax Treatment | Key Consideration |
| --- | --- | --- |
